But the actual location of this if I do control L you'll see it is forward slash root.

19
00:01:20,640 --> 00:01:26,550
So inside this you can see we have our downloads music pictures similar to the way that you'd have it

20
00:01:26,550 --> 00:01:28,290
in windows and OS x.

21
00:01:29,100 --> 00:01:34,410
We also have the time in here and in here on the right we have more controls.

22
00:01:34,410 --> 00:01:38,310
So the first thing that we have in here is the workspaces.

23
00:01:38,310 --> 00:01:42,360
So Linux comes with multiple workspaces by default.

24
00:01:42,390 --> 00:01:48,810
Basically what this allows us to do is we can have this desktop in here for example, we can open the

25
00:01:48,810 --> 00:01:53,550
places and we can for example navigate to downloads and do some work in here.

26
00:01:54,060 --> 00:02:02,040
But if we go here to workplaces and go to the second one, you can see that I have an empty desktop,

27
00:02:02,040 --> 00:02:03,660
that I can do whatever I want.

28
00:02:03,660 --> 00:02:06,420
And for example, I can launch Firefox in here.

29
00:02:07,910 --> 00:02:14,480
And then again, if I want to go back to the first workspace, click on number one and I'm back in here.

30
00:02:14,480 --> 00:02:20,810
And because I've already used up two spaces, the system is already creating a third empty space.

31
00:02:20,810 --> 00:02:22,250
So I can click on that.

32
00:02:22,250 --> 00:02:26,180
And as you can see, I'm in the third space where I can do more stuff.

33
00:02:27,010 --> 00:02:30,820
The next part of the status bar is this last part in here.

34
00:02:31,120 --> 00:02:36,100
And if you click on it, basically the first thing it will allow you to do is control the sound.

35
00:02:36,160 --> 00:02:38,860
Next you'll see your connections.

36
00:02:38,860 --> 00:02:44,230
So as you can see right now and as I showed you before, this is showing up as if we're connected to

37
00:02:44,230 --> 00:02:49,240
a wired network because I configured this network to use a Nat network.

38
00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:55,540
If you have a wireless adapter connected to this computer, you should be able to see it here.

39
00:02:55,540 --> 00:03:02,080
If you don't see it, then follow the link in the resources of this lecture to see how to properly connect

40
00:03:02,080 --> 00:03:07,120
your wireless adapter or any USB device to a virtual machine.

41
00:03:07,780 --> 00:03:16,210
Now, I should note you can only use external USB adapters in order to access Wi-Fi networks from Cali.

42
00:03:16,210 --> 00:03:23,380
You cannot use the built in Wi-Fi card, but Cali should have internet connection anyway because it

43
00:03:23,380 --> 00:03:25,420
is configured to use a Nat network.

44
00:03:25,420 --> 00:03:29,230
And as you can see in here, it's telling me my wired is connected.

45
00:03:29,410 --> 00:03:33,430
So if I actually go down to Firefox, I'll close this.

46
00:03:35,000 --> 00:03:41,660
And if I just go to google.com, you can see it is connected to the internet.

47
00:03:43,320 --> 00:03:46,020
So let's go back to where we were.

48
00:03:46,710 --> 00:03:52,560
And if we click here we also have the battery settings.

49
00:03:52,560 --> 00:03:55,200
You have this button to turn off the machine.

50
00:03:55,500 --> 00:03:57,360
This is to lock the machine.

51
00:03:57,360 --> 00:04:02,280
And this will show the preferences where you can modify the preferences of the machine.

52
00:04:02,280 --> 00:04:06,750
So you have Bluetooth Wi-Fi change the background and so on.

53
00:04:07,260 --> 00:04:10,200
So you can spend some time getting familiar with this.

54
00:04:10,230 --> 00:04:12,540
It's very easy and very intuitive.

55
00:04:12,540 --> 00:04:15,090
Same goes to the file manager in here.

56
00:04:15,090 --> 00:04:15,960
Very intuitive.

57
00:04:15,960 --> 00:04:17,310
Double click to go in.

58
00:04:17,310 --> 00:04:21,270
You can right click and archive and extract here to uncompress it.

59
00:04:21,270 --> 00:04:24,600
You can navigate throughout these files by clicking them.

60
00:04:24,600 --> 00:04:28,500
And then you have the trash here which contains the deleted files.

61
00:04:28,500 --> 00:04:33,150
Now we also have the dock in here which contains the favorite applications.

62
00:04:33,150 --> 00:04:39,390
And you can also access all the other applications by clicking on this icon right here.

63
00:04:39,390 --> 00:04:40,770
The show applications.

64
00:04:40,770 --> 00:04:44,760
And this will show you a menu with the most frequent applications.

65
00:04:44,760 --> 00:04:48,600
You can click through these arrows to load all of the applications.

66
00:04:48,600 --> 00:04:53,280
You can also use the search box to search through these applications.

67
00:04:53,280 --> 00:04:58,050
For example, if I wanted to search for this program right here nicto.

68
00:04:58,080 --> 00:05:03,510
All I have to do is type an IK and as you can see, it's filtering it for me.

69
00:05:03,600 --> 00:05:09,870
And last but not least, you can also switch between the desktops that I showed you in here through

70
00:05:09,870 --> 00:05:12,630
this menu by clicking on these desktops.

71
00:05:13,480 --> 00:05:19,150
And if you want to exit out of this, you can simply press the escape button on your keyboard or press

72
00:05:19,150 --> 00:05:22,120
the Show Apps icon in here on the left.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,240 --> 00:00:06,440
Now that we had a quick overview of Kali Linux, I want to spend some time talking about the Linux terminal.

2
00:00:06,960 --> 00:00:10,840
So you can access the terminal through this application right here.

3
00:00:10,840 --> 00:00:14,480
So if we click on that it will open the terminal window for us.

4
00:00:14,840 --> 00:00:19,360
And as you can see all it is is a black screen where you can type commands.

5
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:27,120
Now the Linux terminal is actually very very powerful because it can be used to do anything that you

6
00:00:27,120 --> 00:00:27,840
can think of.

7
00:00:27,840 --> 00:00:35,040
Really a lot of the applications in Linux that have a graphical interface where command prompt programs

8
00:00:35,040 --> 00:00:38,960
first, and then people made a graphical interface for it.

9
00:00:39,360 --> 00:00:44,960
So a lot of the time, maybe the graphical interface will be buggy or crash, and the terminal program

10
00:00:44,960 --> 00:00:45,960
will still work.

11
00:00:47,000 --> 00:00:52,560
Also, a lot of the penetration testing tools do not even have a graphical interface.

12
00:00:52,600 --> 00:00:56,040
A lot of them can only be used throughout the terminal.

13
00:00:57,000 --> 00:01:04,800
Not only that, but in many scenarios you might only have an SSH or a command prompt access to a machine.

14
00:01:05,080 --> 00:01:09,980
So you need to know how to use this command prompt in order to achieve your goals.

15
00:01:10,940 --> 00:01:14,380
So throughout the course we're going to be using the terminal a lot.

16
00:01:14,580 --> 00:01:20,860
And that's why I want to spend some time just showing you the basics of it and making sure that you're

17
00:01:20,860 --> 00:01:22,820
going to be comfortable using it.

18
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:29,740
Now the basic idea is you type a command and the result will be displayed for you on screen.

19
00:01:30,660 --> 00:01:34,300
So let's have a look on a very, very simple command which is P.

20
00:01:36,340 --> 00:01:40,500
Now this command prints the current working directory hence the name.

21
00:01:42,300 --> 00:01:49,380
So if I hit enter you can see it's printing forward slash root which means right now I am in the root

22
00:01:49,380 --> 00:01:50,180
directory.

23
00:01:50,740 --> 00:01:53,180
So basically I am in here.

24
00:01:53,340 --> 00:01:54,140
This is the root.

25
00:01:54,140 --> 00:01:54,900
It's home.

26
00:01:56,260 --> 00:02:03,100
So if I do LHS which is a command to list all the directories and files in the current working directory,

27
00:02:03,300 --> 00:02:06,740
we should get all of these directories that we see in here.

28
00:02:08,140 --> 00:02:14,720
So if I hit enter As you can see, I can see all these directories in the current working directory.

29
00:02:15,720 --> 00:02:19,040
Now another very useful command is the CD command.

30
00:02:19,440 --> 00:02:23,800
This command allows us to navigate into another directory.

31
00:02:24,280 --> 00:02:28,200
So for example let's say I want to navigate into the downloads.

32
00:02:29,080 --> 00:02:35,920
All we have to do is type CD followed by the name of the directory that I want to navigate to.

33
00:02:36,320 --> 00:02:37,960
So I'm going to type downloads.

34
00:02:39,680 --> 00:02:44,200
Now if I hit enter I should be inside the downloads now.

35
00:02:44,400 --> 00:02:51,440
So if I do PWD to see my current working directory you'll see that it's saying I'm in root forward slash

36
00:02:51,600 --> 00:02:52,320
downloads.

37
00:02:53,120 --> 00:02:58,760
So if I do LS here it should show me all the directories and files inside downloads.

38
00:02:59,080 --> 00:03:04,280
So if I do enter, as you can see I have a directory and a file in here.

39
00:03:04,480 --> 00:03:08,960
And these are the exact same files that you'll see if you double click the downloads here.

40
00:03:11,240 --> 00:03:18,380
Now if you want to go back one directory so similar to pressing the back button in here, all you have

41
00:03:18,380 --> 00:03:19,580
to do is do CD.

42
00:03:19,620 --> 00:03:23,940
Again, the command to change the working directory followed by dot dot.

43
00:03:25,220 --> 00:03:29,060
And now if I do PWD you'll see I'm back in route.

44
00:03:29,300 --> 00:03:33,340
And if I do LZ you'll see all the directories and files in route.

45
00:03:34,620 --> 00:03:40,500
Now if you are using a command and you're not sure about how this command works, you can just use the

46
00:03:40,500 --> 00:03:44,460
man command to display the manual of this command.

47
00:03:45,220 --> 00:03:50,220
For example, we've used the LS command here to list the files and directories in the current working

48
00:03:50,220 --> 00:03:50,940
directory.

49
00:03:51,420 --> 00:03:59,220
But if I do man followed by lz this basically means I'm requesting the manual of LZ.

50
00:03:59,220 --> 00:04:01,820
So I'm asking how can I use the LZ command.

51
00:04:02,780 --> 00:04:07,900
So if I hit enter you'll see I'll get a screen similar to a text file.

52
00:04:08,260 --> 00:04:13,620
And basically it's given me a lot of information on how to use the LZ command.

53
00:04:14,420 --> 00:04:19,040
So you can see that it's telling us that this command will list the directory contents.

54
00:04:19,680 --> 00:04:24,480
You can see the way it works by typing LZ, followed by the options followed by a file.

55
00:04:24,480 --> 00:04:24,960
If you.

56
00:04:24,960 --> 00:04:31,680
If you want to run it on a file, you can see a longer description of the command.

57
00:04:32,880 --> 00:04:39,240
And then you can see all the options and the arguments that we can use with this command.

58
00:04:40,480 --> 00:04:45,920
Now in Linux, most of the time the options will always follow the same syntax.

59
00:04:46,120 --> 00:04:52,320
So you either use dash letter or dash dash award to specify the argument.

60
00:04:52,680 --> 00:04:59,600
For example, in here the dash a and dash dash all will ignore entries starting with dot.

61
00:05:00,800 --> 00:05:07,160
So if you keep going down in here, you'll see all the options and arguments you can use with the LS

62
00:05:07,160 --> 00:05:07,800
command.

63
00:05:08,280 --> 00:05:09,680
We have another example here.

64
00:05:09,680 --> 00:05:17,160
We have the dash L, which means it's going to use a long listing format which will display more information

65
00:05:17,160 --> 00:05:19,960
about the files in the current working directory.

66
00:05:20,960 --> 00:05:22,120
So let's have a look on that.

67
00:05:22,120 --> 00:05:24,260
I'm going to press Q to exit this.

68
00:05:24,980 --> 00:05:27,260
And then we're going to do LZ as usual.

69
00:05:27,580 --> 00:05:33,820
And since we read the manual, we know we can do dash L to see more information about the files.

70
00:05:34,260 --> 00:05:38,780
And if I hit enter now you can see I'm still getting the same directories.

71
00:05:38,780 --> 00:05:44,140
But it's also showing me the permissions, the users, the date created and so on.

72
00:05:45,260 --> 00:05:50,260
So you can use the man command on any command you want, not only on the LZ.

73
00:05:50,540 --> 00:05:52,540
So you can use it on the PWD.

74
00:05:52,820 --> 00:05:56,300
You can use it on the CD or any other command.

75
00:05:56,420 --> 00:06:02,380
And it will show you full description or the manual page of how to use this command.

76
00:06:04,100 --> 00:06:07,460
Now I'm going to clear the screen by typing clear.

77
00:06:09,020 --> 00:06:11,660
And the next thing that I want to show you is the dash.

78
00:06:11,660 --> 00:06:12,460
Dash help.

79
00:06:12,940 --> 00:06:18,940
So this is something that you can use again in almost all commands in all programs in Linux.

80
00:06:19,180 --> 00:06:24,980
So you can just type the program name or the command name followed by dash dash help.

81
00:06:26,620 --> 00:06:32,440
As you might think, this will show you a help message telling you what this command is or what this

82
00:06:32,440 --> 00:06:39,240
program is, the arguments that it takes, how to use these arguments and examples at the bottom.

83
00:06:40,360 --> 00:06:42,400
Now another useful thing with the terminal.

84
00:06:42,400 --> 00:06:45,160
So I'm going to clear this again is the arrows.

85
00:06:45,360 --> 00:06:50,320
So you can press up to go up to see all the commands that we executed before.

86
00:06:50,480 --> 00:06:56,000
And again you can go down to navigate between the commands that you executed previously.

87
00:06:57,120 --> 00:07:00,320
You can also use the tab for autocomplete.

88
00:07:00,720 --> 00:07:03,880
So again let's do ls and you can see all the files.

89
00:07:04,040 --> 00:07:06,640
And let's say we want to go into documents.

90
00:07:07,520 --> 00:07:10,640
So we can do CD followed by documents.

91
00:07:10,640 --> 00:07:12,240
You can type documents.

92
00:07:12,520 --> 00:07:17,720
Or if you're lazy like me you can just do doc and press tab.

93
00:07:18,200 --> 00:07:23,040
And as you can see it's automatically completing the rest of the word for me.

94
00:07:24,000 --> 00:07:29,280
So this is something that comes very, very handy when you're using the terminal for a long time.

95
00:07:33,300 --> 00:07:34,660
So that's all good.

96
00:07:35,060 --> 00:07:39,220
And there's actually a huge number of commands that you can use.

97
00:07:39,220 --> 00:07:45,380
So I'm going to include a link in the resources of this lecture of all the Linux commands that you can

98
00:07:45,380 --> 00:07:45,820
use.

99
00:07:46,220 --> 00:07:47,980
You don't need to know them by heart.

100
00:07:48,020 --> 00:07:50,860
We're actually going to be using a lot of them throughout the course.

101
00:07:50,860 --> 00:07:54,980
So you're going to naturally learn them as you go through the course.

102
00:07:56,420 --> 00:08:02,100
Now, before I let you go, I just want to show you two websites that I'm going to include in the resources

103
00:08:02,100 --> 00:08:03,060
of this lecture.

104
00:08:03,620 --> 00:08:09,340
First of all, we have a website here that includes the most common Linux commands, if not all.

105
00:08:09,620 --> 00:08:12,860
So you can go through them and familiarize yourself with them.

106
00:08:12,860 --> 00:08:16,220
But keep in mind you don't need to know them all by heart.

107
00:08:16,260 --> 00:08:22,180
Like I said, as you go through my course and as you continue on with your journey, you will learn

108
00:08:22,180 --> 00:08:24,500
the commands that you need as you go.

109
00:08:25,980 --> 00:08:29,420
Another really useful resource that I'm going to include is.

110
00:08:31,500 --> 00:08:35,100
This website is designed to explain Linux commands to you.

111
00:08:35,260 --> 00:08:37,840
So all you have to do is type the commands in here.

112
00:08:37,840 --> 00:08:46,960
So for example if you just type ls and hit explain, it will explain that LS will list directory content.

113
00:08:47,280 --> 00:08:50,600
You could make it more complex and do ls la.

114
00:08:51,160 --> 00:08:57,120
And if we hit enter now, it will break all the arguments and explain every single argument.

115
00:08:57,120 --> 00:08:59,400
So again ls discontents.

116
00:08:59,400 --> 00:09:00,720
If we highlight the L.

117
00:09:00,760 --> 00:09:06,440
As you can see, it's telling us that it's going to use a long listing format, as we've seen earlier,

118
00:09:06,640 --> 00:09:13,640
and the A at the end is the same as Dash A, which is all, which means that we do not want to ignore

119
00:09:13,640 --> 00:09:15,440
entries starting with a dot.

120
00:09:15,520 --> 00:09:17,800
So we do not want to ignore hidden files.

121
00:09:18,840 --> 00:09:20,600
So you can do any command in here.

122
00:09:20,600 --> 00:09:26,480
You can do apt get install for example, and it will actually explain it to you, so it'll explain.

123
00:09:26,520 --> 00:09:31,280
APT is a package manager install can be used to install packages and so on.

124
00:09:31,600 --> 00:09:35,960
So if you ever see a Linux command that you don't understand, you can always just come in here, put

125
00:09:35,960 --> 00:09:40,640
the command and it will break it down to you and explain it really nicely.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,680 --> 00:00:06,120
Now, if you're using the custom Kali that we made for this course, you will also notice that we have

2
00:00:06,120 --> 00:00:08,000
two more terminal programs.

3
00:00:08,360 --> 00:00:10,920
The first one in here called Terminator.

4
00:00:11,120 --> 00:00:15,280
It's identical to the normal terminal that I just showed you.

5
00:00:15,320 --> 00:00:19,040
You can write commands in here and see the results as a response.

6
00:00:19,280 --> 00:00:25,080
The only difference is this terminal window can be split into many terminal windows.

7
00:00:25,560 --> 00:00:27,880
So I can right click and split horizontally.

8
00:00:27,880 --> 00:00:32,680
And I get another terminal in here when I where I can run another command and see the result.

9
00:00:32,680 --> 00:00:38,080
And then this one, I can split it for example vertically, and then split this one horizontally.

10
00:00:38,080 --> 00:00:44,840
And be able to run multiple terminal applications in all of these different terminal instances at the

11
00:00:44,840 --> 00:00:45,600
same time.

12
00:00:45,840 --> 00:00:51,080
So this will become very, very useful as we go through the course when we want to execute a number

13
00:00:51,080 --> 00:00:52,800
of commands at the same time.

14
00:00:52,800 --> 00:00:57,640
And you will see that this would be the terminal that I'll be using most of the time.

15
00:00:58,480 --> 00:01:04,400
The main point is the commands that we're using in here will work on any other Cali terminal.

16
00:01:05,040 --> 00:01:11,200
The third terminal that I want to show you is this one right here, warp, which is actually an application

17
00:01:11,200 --> 00:01:16,240
that is designed for developers to write code, but also comes in with a really nice terminal.

18
00:01:16,640 --> 00:01:20,400
Now, the main feature of this terminal is that it has AI features.

19
00:01:20,600 --> 00:01:25,360
So first of all, it's a terminal that looks identical to the terminal that I showed you before.

20
00:01:25,400 --> 00:01:26,760
You can write commands in here.

21
00:01:26,760 --> 00:01:28,160
So again I can do LZ.

22
00:01:28,600 --> 00:01:31,000
And that will list all the files and directories.

23
00:01:31,000 --> 00:01:36,240
As you can see in here I can do PWD and then see the result as well.

24
00:01:36,520 --> 00:01:38,520
With this one you can have multiple tabs.

25
00:01:38,520 --> 00:01:41,520
So we can open a new tab and execute commands in here.

26
00:01:41,640 --> 00:01:46,520
Or you could right click and again split the screen horizontally and vertically.

27
00:01:47,240 --> 00:01:51,280
The main feature of this terminal however is the AI features.

28
00:01:51,280 --> 00:01:55,480
So in here we can actually ask questions and execute commands.

29
00:01:55,480 --> 00:01:58,960
And it will automatically determine what it should do with my prompt.

30
00:01:59,320 --> 00:02:04,470
And it's going to use whatever AI model that is selected in here to answer the question.

31
00:02:04,470 --> 00:02:16,470
For example, I can ask it how do I scan the network I am connected to for vulnerabilities?

32
00:02:17,150 --> 00:02:21,670
Now you'll notice that this terminal is not the fastest because of the AI features.

33
00:02:21,670 --> 00:02:26,990
And you'll also notice that sometimes it might not answer you, but in this example it's actually answering

34
00:02:26,990 --> 00:02:29,390
you if you're asking hacking related questions.

35
00:02:29,390 --> 00:02:33,950
But I actually show how to bypass that censorship in the hacking masterclass.

36
00:02:34,550 --> 00:02:39,870
If you don't want it to automatically determine what it should do with the prompt or the command that

37
00:02:39,870 --> 00:02:43,150
you type in here, you can actually switch in here.

38
00:02:43,150 --> 00:02:48,870
So if you click the terminal icon in here, this will allow you to only execute terminal commands.

39
00:02:48,870 --> 00:02:55,230
If you click the A icon in here which is the agent mode, it will actually go in agent mode and even

40
00:02:55,230 --> 00:03:01,190
execute the commands on your behalf so you can ask it to install packages or literally go ahead and

41
00:03:01,190 --> 00:03:04,670
scan the network for you or generate backdoors and so on.

42
00:03:04,990 --> 00:03:08,950
But to be able to do this, you still need to learn the basics yourself.

43
00:03:08,950 --> 00:03:12,310
And that's why this course is still relevant and still exists.

44
00:03:12,350 --> 00:03:17,670
You need to learn everything, everything that is in this course properly so that you can direct AI

45
00:03:17,830 --> 00:03:18,950
in the right direction.

46
00:03:19,670 --> 00:03:25,030
So, for example, if I wanted to make a backdoor, which is something that you'll learn in this course

47
00:03:25,030 --> 00:03:27,790
in details, I cannot simply just tell it.

48
00:03:27,790 --> 00:03:33,350
Make me a backdoor, because there are hundreds of tools to make backdoors and there are hundreds of

49
00:03:33,350 --> 00:03:34,430
types of backdoors.

50
00:03:35,310 --> 00:03:37,510
So I have to specify the tool.

51
00:03:37,510 --> 00:03:43,390
For example, use MSF venom, and then I have to specify the type of the backdoor.

52
00:03:43,390 --> 00:03:46,910
So I want a reverse https windows executable.

53
00:03:47,470 --> 00:03:50,070
And I have to specify how it connects back to my machine.

54
00:03:50,070 --> 00:03:53,230
So I wanted a reverse connection that connects back to my machine.

55
00:03:53,230 --> 00:03:55,030
And I have to specify the port.

56
00:03:55,550 --> 00:04:00,550
Once you give it all this relevant information, you'll see that it's going to craft the correct command,

57
00:04:00,550 --> 00:04:05,420
as you can see in here, which you'll learn how to do manually because, like I said, it's very important

58
00:04:05,420 --> 00:04:07,580
to be able to prompt the AI.

59
00:04:08,100 --> 00:04:13,220
And then if we're happy with this, we can simply tell it to run it and it will create the backdoor

60
00:04:13,220 --> 00:04:13,780
for us.

61
00:04:15,580 --> 00:04:21,380
So we're able to carry out hacking actions using natural language without actually having to write any

62
00:04:21,380 --> 00:04:22,020
commands.

63
00:04:22,300 --> 00:04:25,620
And please don't be scared of all of the terminology that I used.

64
00:04:25,620 --> 00:04:29,420
You will learn all of this and much more as you go through the course.

65
00:04:29,420 --> 00:04:33,180
I just want to show you why you still need to learn hacking.

66
00:04:33,220 --> 00:04:39,340
Even though we have AI tools and AI agents like this that can carry out actions on our behalf.

67
00:04:39,900 --> 00:04:45,460
But if you think this is really cool and you want to learn more about how to use AI for hacking, then

68
00:04:45,460 --> 00:04:52,500
check out my hacking Masterclass as we have a full series within it covering how to use AI and AI agents

69
00:04:52,500 --> 00:04:55,900
and AI frameworks for hacking, bug hunting, and so on.

70
00:04:56,620 --> 00:05:02,420
You can find out more information about the Hacking masterclass in the last lecture of this course,

71
00:05:02,500 --> 00:05:03,580
the bonus Lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,420 --> 00:00:06,390
Now, before we start writing our first program, I want to spend a minute or two talking to you about

2
00:00:06,390 --> 00:00:08,580
Python two and Python three.

3
00:00:09,390 --> 00:00:15,300
Now, these are the two main versions of Python, and obviously Python three is the latest version.

4
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:19,650
So you might be thinking, which version are we going to be using throughout the course?

5
00:00:19,860 --> 00:00:22,620
Well, the quick answer to that is both.

6
00:00:22,620 --> 00:00:28,260
And by the end of the course, you're going to be able to write programs in both Python two and Python

7
00:00:28,260 --> 00:00:28,830
three.

8
00:00:29,460 --> 00:00:35,010
The reason for this is, and you probably hear this from a lot of programmers, is if you learn programming

9
00:00:35,010 --> 00:00:40,080
properly, then you're going to be able to write programs in any programming language.

10
00:00:40,470 --> 00:00:45,990
All you have to do is spend some time reading the syntax and getting familiar with the way that you

11
00:00:45,990 --> 00:00:47,520
write code for this language.

12
00:00:47,520 --> 00:00:53,070
But you're going to already know the concepts and the way of how to think of a problem, how to model

13
00:00:53,070 --> 00:00:54,000
it and solve it.

14
00:00:54,030 --> 00:00:57,630
So writing in a different language is not going to be a problem.

15
00:00:58,540 --> 00:01:04,600
Therefore, if you learn programming in Python two or in Python three, then the version number is not

16
00:01:04,600 --> 00:01:08,890
going to make a big difference to you because you're still using the same language.

17
00:01:08,890 --> 00:01:12,760
You're either using a slightly newer or a slightly older version.

18
00:01:13,480 --> 00:01:17,390
Now you might be thinking, Why are we even talking about Python two?

19
00:01:17,410 --> 00:01:21,040
If three is the latest version, well, the answer is simple.

20
00:01:21,040 --> 00:01:26,030
There is a huge number of programs that's already been written in Python two.

21
00:01:26,050 --> 00:01:32,440
So if you ever wanted to use one of these programs in your program, or if you ever wanted to read them

22
00:01:32,440 --> 00:01:36,340
and modify them, you're going to have to understand how Python two works.

23
00:01:36,880 --> 00:01:44,290
Also, there is still a lot of libraries that only work on Python two, and some of them have a Python

24
00:01:44,290 --> 00:01:45,160
three version.

25
00:01:45,160 --> 00:01:48,380
But the Python three is more buggy than Python two.

26
00:01:48,400 --> 00:01:50,710
So you might end up using Python two.

27
00:01:52,010 --> 00:01:59,840
Another reason is a lot of Linux and Unix systems still come with Python two as the default Python interpreter.

28
00:02:00,230 --> 00:02:05,900
So if you ever wanted to exploit these systems, you're going to have to write programs in Python to.

29
00:02:07,290 --> 00:02:11,010
Now don't get confused and don't worry about the two versions.

30
00:02:11,010 --> 00:02:17,100
Like I said, at the end of the day, you're writing programs in the same programming language and the

31
00:02:17,100 --> 00:02:21,360
differences are actually very small, especially when it comes to the things that we're going to be

32
00:02:21,360 --> 00:02:22,520
doing in this course.

33
00:02:22,530 --> 00:02:28,500
So they might be slight changes in the names of the libraries and the library calls or slight changes

34
00:02:28,500 --> 00:02:30,210
in some of the statements.

35
00:02:30,300 --> 00:02:34,560
So I'm actually going to be covering these changes as we go throughout the course.

36
00:02:34,560 --> 00:02:39,210
And therefore, by the end of the course, you're going to be able to write programs and understand

37
00:02:39,210 --> 00:02:42,930
programs written in both Python two and Python three.

38
00:02:43,410 --> 00:02:46,800
Now, let's go ahead and start writing our first program.

39
00:02:47,370 --> 00:02:53,880
Now, in most cases, all you need is a text editor in order to write a program.

40
00:02:54,890 --> 00:03:01,250
So now I'm in calisthenics and I'm going to go to Leaf Pad, which is a text editor that comes preinstalled

41
00:03:01,250 --> 00:03:06,710
with KDE Linux and we can start writing our Python program straight away in here.

42
00:03:07,880 --> 00:03:14,750
Now there are text editors or IDs that's designed for programming and we'll get into that in the next

43
00:03:14,750 --> 00:03:15,400
lecture.

44
00:03:15,410 --> 00:03:20,480
But for now, I'm keeping everything as simple as possible, and I'm actually going to write a very,

45
00:03:20,480 --> 00:03:24,980
very simple program that will only display a message on screen.

46
00:03:25,730 --> 00:03:32,420
Now, in the future, we will not be writing useless programs like this one, but this is just an introduction.

47
00:03:32,420 --> 00:03:37,220
So I just want you to get an idea of what a program is and how to run it.

48
00:03:38,260 --> 00:03:43,930
So in order to print something on screen in Python, we can use the print function.

49
00:03:44,260 --> 00:03:46,060
So I can just do print.

50
00:03:46,630 --> 00:03:48,220
That's the name of the function.

51
00:03:48,220 --> 00:03:55,060
And then we can use two brackets and give it the message that we want to print between these two brackets.

52
00:03:55,510 --> 00:04:01,780
Now, since the message is going to be text, we have to enclose this text between two quotation marks.

53
00:04:02,050 --> 00:04:06,910
And the message is just going to be Hello world.

54
00:04:07,480 --> 00:04:08,830
So very, very simple.

55
00:04:08,860 --> 00:04:13,490
We're calling a function, a python function that prints data on screen.

56
00:04:13,510 --> 00:04:15,220
The function is called print.

57
00:04:15,220 --> 00:04:18,850
And then we're giving it the text that we want to print in here.

58
00:04:19,510 --> 00:04:25,810
Now, this is done and you can actually run the file like this, but it's actually better to include

59
00:04:25,810 --> 00:04:30,310
the Python shebang at the start of each Python file you create.

60
00:04:31,350 --> 00:04:38,580
The shebang is a directive that tells the operating system how to handle this specific file.

61
00:04:39,210 --> 00:04:46,680
So because this is a python file, the shebang is going to be a hash followed by an escalation mark

62
00:04:46,680 --> 00:04:51,120
followed by us are bin and.

63
00:04:51,950 --> 00:04:56,180
And if you're writing a python two program you should type python.

64
00:04:56,190 --> 00:05:00,210
If you're writing a Python three program, you should type Python three.

65
00:05:01,200 --> 00:05:08,100
So like I said, this line is not mandatory, but it's a common practice and it also allows us to run

66
00:05:08,100 --> 00:05:14,460
this file as an executable so the system will automatically know that this is a python file.

67
00:05:15,420 --> 00:05:18,390
Now, once done with this, we can save this.

68
00:05:18,510 --> 00:05:26,640
So I'm going to go to file save and I'm going to put this in my root directory and I'm going to call

69
00:05:26,640 --> 00:05:29,940
this hello dot py.

70
00:05:31,130 --> 00:05:32,750
I'm going to click on Save.

71
00:05:34,140 --> 00:05:42,300
And now if I go to my files, you can see I have the file in here in my home, which is my root directory.

72
00:05:43,140 --> 00:05:48,510
So I'm going to close this and I'm going to open my terminal to run this file.

73
00:05:49,780 --> 00:05:53,930
And the terminal opens on the route directory by default.

74
00:05:53,950 --> 00:06:02,320
So if I do p wd you'll see I'm already in route and if I do ls to list all the current files and directories

75
00:06:02,320 --> 00:06:03,500
you can see i have.

76
00:06:03,500 --> 00:06:05,290
Hello dot p in here.

77
00:06:06,280 --> 00:06:10,600
Now to run this file we have to first say python.

78
00:06:11,610 --> 00:06:16,380
And then specify the Python file name that we want to run.

79
00:06:17,380 --> 00:06:24,910
The reason for this is because, as you can see in here, the code is just simple text and the computer

80
00:06:24,910 --> 00:06:27,610
does not understand this simple text.

81
00:06:28,150 --> 00:06:35,110
So what we're doing here is we're actually calling an interpreter, a python interpreter, and we're

82
00:06:35,110 --> 00:06:38,380
passing this file to the interpreter.

83
00:06:38,860 --> 00:06:46,000
You can think of the interpreter as a translator that can translate text from one language to another.

84
00:06:46,840 --> 00:06:54,460
So in this case, it's going to translate the program that we wrote in Python to machine language so

85
00:06:54,460 --> 00:06:56,560
that the computer can understand.

86
00:06:57,160 --> 00:07:00,630
This is actually why we also put this line in here.

87
00:07:00,640 --> 00:07:06,550
This line tells the system, the location of the interpreter, so that if someone tries to run this

88
00:07:06,550 --> 00:07:13,300
file without specifying Python in front of it, the system will know that this is a Python interpreter

89
00:07:13,360 --> 00:07:20,350
and pass this file to the interpreter so that it can convert it from Python code to machine code.

90
00:07:21,350 --> 00:07:25,400
So you can think of the interpreter as a person that knows two languages.

91
00:07:25,400 --> 00:07:31,610
For example, it knows English and Spanish, so you can talk to it in English and it will translate

92
00:07:31,610 --> 00:07:33,590
what you say to Spanish.

93
00:07:34,460 --> 00:07:36,080
So now if I had enter.

94
00:07:37,220 --> 00:07:42,560
You can see that the my code is getting executed in here and I can see the message that I specified

95
00:07:42,560 --> 00:07:45,070
in here being displayed on screen.

96
00:07:45,080 --> 00:07:46,490
So that's perfect.

97
00:07:47,210 --> 00:07:53,510
And if you actually want to run this in Python three, you can just type python three.

98
00:07:53,630 --> 00:07:55,110
Hello dot py.

99
00:07:55,130 --> 00:07:59,210
And again, you can see that the code getting executed exactly the same.

100
00:07:59,210 --> 00:08:04,280
And this goes back to me when I was talking about Python two and three and I said there isn't a huge

101
00:08:04,280 --> 00:08:05,540
difference between them.

102
00:08:06,320 --> 00:08:07,870
So that's it for this lecture.

103
00:08:07,880 --> 00:08:13,310
I just wanted to spend some time talking about Python two and three, and I also wanted to give you

104
00:08:13,310 --> 00:08:17,520
a basic understanding of what a program is and how to run it.

105
00:08:17,540 --> 00:08:24,020
Some people think of a program as this complicated thing, but all it is is simply a text file with

106
00:08:24,020 --> 00:08:29,180
some text in it, and when you run it, you have to run it with the interpreter of the language.

107
00:08:30,180 --> 00:08:37,350
Now I should also mention some languages such as C, they would require you to compile the code before

108
00:08:37,350 --> 00:08:38,070
running it.

109
00:08:38,610 --> 00:08:45,780
So compiling the code will actually convert the text that you have in here into another file that the

110
00:08:45,780 --> 00:08:47,460
computer can understand.

111
00:08:47,460 --> 00:08:54,450
And then instead of running this file, the source file, you will run the file that the compiler produces

112
00:08:54,900 --> 00:09:01,890
and we'll actually see how we can convert Python code, Python source code into binaries that can run

113
00:09:01,890 --> 00:09:03,570
on any operating system.

114
00:09:03,660 --> 00:09:05,010
But more on that later.

115
00:09:05,010 --> 00:09:07,110
You don't need to worry about that right now.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,120 --> 00:00:01,480
Okay.

2
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:07,840
So in the previous lecture we've seen how we can write very simple code in a normal text editor, save

3
00:00:07,840 --> 00:00:13,540
it as that P file and that file will work normally and the code will get executed.

4
00:00:14,320 --> 00:00:19,870
Now you can actually use a text editor to write even more complex Python programs.

5
00:00:20,440 --> 00:00:28,120
As the program gets bigger, it becomes more challenging to just use a normal or simple text editor.

6
00:00:28,510 --> 00:00:36,550
There are programs that are specifically made to help you with programming, testing, refactoring and

7
00:00:36,550 --> 00:00:37,240
so on.

8
00:00:37,930 --> 00:00:44,080
These are commonly known as ID ease or integrated development environment.

9
00:00:44,980 --> 00:00:50,980
So the program that we're going to use to write Python programs throughout the whole course is called

10
00:00:50,980 --> 00:00:51,850
PY Charm.

11
00:00:52,630 --> 00:00:59,110
Now I'm going to include the download link for this program in the resources of this lecture, but I

12
00:00:59,110 --> 00:01:03,000
have it in here and the professional version is paid.

13
00:01:03,010 --> 00:01:06,790
So we're going to be using the community version and it's perfect.

14
00:01:06,790 --> 00:01:09,160
You don't really need the professional anyway.

15
00:01:10,030 --> 00:01:14,920
So if you click on the download, the download will automatically start.

16
00:01:15,190 --> 00:01:21,520
Now I'm actually going to cancel it because I've already downloaded it, and when you download it,

17
00:01:21,520 --> 00:01:29,260
it's going to go in your downloads directory so you can click on this in here, or you can just go to

18
00:01:29,260 --> 00:01:33,610
your files in here and then go to downloads.

19
00:01:34,510 --> 00:01:37,600
And as you can see, I have it downloaded in here.

20
00:01:38,320 --> 00:01:40,120
Now this is compressed.

21
00:01:40,120 --> 00:01:47,440
So in order to uncompressed this archive, I'm going to right click it and click on extract here.

22
00:01:48,560 --> 00:01:54,530
Now right here I have the directory for the program and you can leave it in downloads and use it from

23
00:01:54,530 --> 00:01:55,130
here.

24
00:01:55,130 --> 00:01:59,960
But I like to put the optional programs in the opt directory.

25
00:02:00,170 --> 00:02:04,760
This is the directory that you're supposed to put all the optional programs.

26
00:02:05,660 --> 00:02:13,640
So I'm going to right click this and copy it and then I'm going to press control L to open the path

27
00:02:13,640 --> 00:02:13,940
bar.

28
00:02:13,940 --> 00:02:22,310
And here, as you can see, I'm going to put forward slash opt to go to the OPPT directory and I'm going

29
00:02:22,310 --> 00:02:24,020
to paste it in here.

30
00:02:25,160 --> 00:02:33,020
Now inside the pie chart directory, we have a bin directory and inside this directory we have a file

31
00:02:33,020 --> 00:02:35,360
called pie chart dot s h.

32
00:02:36,350 --> 00:02:41,300
This is the file that will run the program for us.

33
00:02:42,230 --> 00:02:50,330
So now in order to run this, we have to go to terminal and we have to navigate to where this file is.

34
00:02:50,330 --> 00:02:55,070
So we're going to use the CD command and it's an opt.

35
00:02:56,170 --> 00:03:00,160
Picture and you can use the tap to autocomplete, as you can see.

36
00:03:01,030 --> 00:03:07,510
And then now if we list in here, you'll see the same files that we see in here.

37
00:03:07,510 --> 00:03:10,800
And the one that we want to run is pie chart dots.

38
00:03:12,250 --> 00:03:19,660
Now, in order to run an executable in Linux, all you have to do is put dot forward slash followed

39
00:03:19,660 --> 00:03:24,320
by the name of the executable, which is pie as h.

40
00:03:25,330 --> 00:03:26,770
Now if I hit enter.

41
00:03:28,560 --> 00:03:30,930
You'll see the program is going to start loading.

42
00:03:32,410 --> 00:03:35,170
And you'll see the welcome message of the program.

43
00:03:35,620 --> 00:03:41,680
Now, I've actually run this before, so I didn't get all the initial configurations.

44
00:03:41,680 --> 00:03:46,600
So when you run it for the first time, it's going to ask you which theme you want to use.

45
00:03:46,750 --> 00:03:52,930
I picked to use the dark theme, as you can see in here, and then I kept everything else as the default.

46
00:03:54,230 --> 00:03:59,150
So let's go ahead and create a new project by clicking on this and here.

47
00:04:00,490 --> 00:04:04,750
And as you can see, it's asking us for the location for the project.

48
00:04:05,440 --> 00:04:12,550
So by default, by charm puts projects in root and in a directory called Python projects.

49
00:04:13,060 --> 00:04:15,040
So leave all of this the same.

50
00:04:15,040 --> 00:04:21,310
And the only part you want to modify is the name of the project, which is set to Untitled right now.

51
00:04:21,310 --> 00:04:23,170
And we're going to set it to.

52
00:04:23,170 --> 00:04:23,830
Hello.

53
00:04:24,930 --> 00:04:26,580
I'm going to click on Create.

54
00:04:28,450 --> 00:04:33,010
And this will create the project and open up the editor window for me.

55
00:04:34,580 --> 00:04:41,180
So right here on the left, we have the tree that shows the external libraries and we'll talk about

56
00:04:41,180 --> 00:04:45,140
what that is later on and the path for our project.

57
00:04:45,290 --> 00:04:52,010
So right now, you can see that this project in our home directory followed by Python projects, followed

58
00:04:52,010 --> 00:04:52,880
by Hello.

59
00:04:52,910 --> 00:04:55,070
So this is the name of the directory.

60
00:04:55,070 --> 00:04:58,520
And inside it, as you can see, we have no files.

61
00:04:59,360 --> 00:05:08,390
So if I actually go to my file manager and if we just go to this location, so if we go to route, which

62
00:05:08,390 --> 00:05:13,220
is home, you can see we have a new directory called Python Projects.

63
00:05:13,520 --> 00:05:20,960
So in the future, I'm always going to put all the programs and projects that we make inside this directory.

64
00:05:21,740 --> 00:05:27,560
Inside it you can see the Hello project that we just created and inside it there is nothing.

65
00:05:27,560 --> 00:05:28,790
There is no files.

66
00:05:29,450 --> 00:05:35,330
So we're going to create a new file by right clicking this and go on you.

67
00:05:35,690 --> 00:05:37,850
And we're going to create a python file.

68
00:05:39,290 --> 00:05:42,060
And it's asking us for the name of the file.

69
00:05:42,080 --> 00:05:45,380
So let's call it Hello, dot pie.

70
00:05:45,410 --> 00:05:47,390
Similar to what we did before.

71
00:05:48,460 --> 00:05:50,050
I'm going to click on OC.

72
00:05:50,860 --> 00:05:53,620
And as you can see now we have a new file here.

73
00:05:53,620 --> 00:05:59,440
And again, if we go back to the path, you can see we have a new file called Python dot API.

74
00:06:00,490 --> 00:06:06,280
Now in here, we can actually put any code that we want to write, and we're going to start by putting

75
00:06:06,280 --> 00:06:07,210
the shebang.

76
00:06:07,240 --> 00:06:11,980
So it's us are bean and python.

77
00:06:13,310 --> 00:06:19,460
And then we're going to start typing our program and we're going to write a similar program to the previous

78
00:06:19,460 --> 00:06:20,100
lecture.

79
00:06:20,120 --> 00:06:25,940
The whole point of this lecture is to show you how to install and use spy charm, not to write a program.

80
00:06:25,940 --> 00:06:27,890
So I'm just going to do print.

81
00:06:29,090 --> 00:06:35,450
And as you can see, as soon as I tie print, it's automatically shown me the function and it's showing

82
00:06:35,450 --> 00:06:37,330
me how this function can be used.

83
00:06:37,340 --> 00:06:39,740
So it takes I should open a bracket.

84
00:06:39,740 --> 00:06:44,720
And inside the bracket, as you can see now, it's telling me the values that I can put.

85
00:06:44,870 --> 00:06:52,340
And similar to what we seen in the previous lecture, we're going to put two quotation marks to enclose

86
00:06:52,340 --> 00:06:58,490
our message that we want to display and we want to display Hello World.

87
00:06:59,360 --> 00:07:04,910
Now you can see it's also using different colours for different parts of the text.

88
00:07:04,910 --> 00:07:11,030
So you can see the function name is in blue and you can see the message that I'm displaying in green.

89
00:07:11,720 --> 00:07:19,010
Now this message is being displayed in green because what's in here is known as a string in Python.

90
00:07:19,670 --> 00:07:27,980
So any sequence of characters enclosed by two quotation marks is known as a string, and we'll see what

91
00:07:27,980 --> 00:07:32,600
strings are and how to deal with them more, actually much more throughout the course.

92
00:07:33,440 --> 00:07:39,350
So right now this is done and we can actually run this code from within this project.

93
00:07:39,590 --> 00:07:46,430
So all we have to do, we can just go to run and click on the run here and we're going to tell it.

94
00:07:46,430 --> 00:07:51,110
We want to run the hello and as you can see it, run it for me.

95
00:07:51,110 --> 00:07:56,660
And it's shown me the message in here, but I actually don't like to do that.

96
00:07:56,660 --> 00:08:02,750
I like to run the file from terminal and that's why I spent a lot of time showing you where the files

97
00:08:02,750 --> 00:08:07,730
are going to be stored so that you know where to navigate what you want to run the file.

98
00:08:08,390 --> 00:08:10,790
So let's go back to our terminal.

99
00:08:11,960 --> 00:08:13,430
And I'm going to split the screen.

100
00:08:13,430 --> 00:08:16,520
So I'm going to right click and split horizontally.

101
00:08:17,600 --> 00:08:25,490
And if we want to run this file right here from the terminal, then what we need to do is navigate to

102
00:08:25,490 --> 00:08:28,250
the location where this file is stored.

103
00:08:29,090 --> 00:08:36,680
And if you remember, when we created this project, we set this project to be stored in home petrom

104
00:08:36,680 --> 00:08:37,580
projects.

105
00:08:37,610 --> 00:08:38,390
Hello.

106
00:08:38,900 --> 00:08:41,540
So we have to navigate to that location.

107
00:08:41,810 --> 00:08:48,280
So I'm going to do KD Root, which is home followed by pie charm projects.

108
00:08:48,290 --> 00:08:53,780
Again, you can use the tap to autocomplete and the name of my project is Hello.

109
00:08:54,590 --> 00:08:57,500
Now if I do ls here you'll see.

110
00:08:57,500 --> 00:08:59,930
I have the file here and again.

111
00:08:59,930 --> 00:09:03,440
In order to run this file we can just do python.

112
00:09:03,470 --> 00:09:07,790
Hello dot p y and this will execute the file for me.

113
00:09:08,990 --> 00:09:11,110
So that's it for this lecture.

114
00:09:11,120 --> 00:09:17,660
I just wanted to show you how to install pie charm, create a project, and run the code in the project

115
00:09:17,660 --> 00:09:19,790
from pie charm and from the terminal.

116
00:09:19,790 --> 00:09:25,580
So that in the future, when I create a new project or run the code, I will not cover this.

117
00:09:25,580 --> 00:09:31,400
Or I might be doing this quickly because I've already covered how to do this in this lecture.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 2: Writing a MAC Address Changer - Python Basics

1
00:00:02,140 --> 00:00:09,130
Now, like I said, the course is going to be highly practical, so I want to teach everything by example.

2
00:00:09,310 --> 00:00:15,110
Therefore, we'll literally go to start programming without even covering Python basics.

3
00:00:15,130 --> 00:00:19,450
We're going to cover the basics as we write our first program.

4
00:00:19,990 --> 00:00:25,420
Now, the first program that I want to write is going to be a mac address changer.

5
00:00:25,900 --> 00:00:29,290
Mac address stands for Media Access Control.

6
00:00:29,470 --> 00:00:37,120
It's a permanent physical and unique address assigned to network interfaces by the device manufacturer.

7
00:00:37,420 --> 00:00:44,320
So whether you have a wireless card or a Wired or Ethernet card, each one of these network cards come

8
00:00:44,320 --> 00:00:47,920
with a specific address that is unique to this card.

9
00:00:47,920 --> 00:00:54,250
So there is no two devices in the world that would have the same Mac address, and this address will

10
00:00:54,250 --> 00:00:57,630
always be the same to this specific device.

11
00:00:57,640 --> 00:01:03,460
Even if you unplug it from your computer, connect it to another computer, then this network device

12
00:01:03,460 --> 00:01:06,250
will always have the same address.

13
00:01:07,320 --> 00:01:15,240
So you might already know that the IP address is used in the internet to identify computers and communicate

14
00:01:15,240 --> 00:01:17,160
between devices on the Internet.

15
00:01:17,670 --> 00:01:25,320
The MAC address is used within the network to identify devices and transfer data between devices.

16
00:01:26,230 --> 00:01:33,760
So each piece of data or packet that is sent within the network contains a source mark and a destination

17
00:01:33,760 --> 00:01:34,360
mark.

18
00:01:34,630 --> 00:01:40,030
Therefore, this packet would flow from the source mark to the destination mark.

19
00:01:40,750 --> 00:01:48,340
So because this is a physical, unique address to each interface, to each network device, and because

20
00:01:48,340 --> 00:01:54,580
it is used to identify devices, then changing it will make you anonymous on the network.

21
00:01:55,420 --> 00:02:02,110
Not only that, but the Mac address is often used by filters to prevent or allow devices to connect

22
00:02:02,110 --> 00:02:05,700
to networks and do specific tasks on the network.

23
00:02:05,710 --> 00:02:11,740
So being able to change your Mac address to another device as Mac address will allow you to impersonate

24
00:02:11,740 --> 00:02:16,750
this device and allow you to do things that you might not be able to do.

25
00:02:16,750 --> 00:02:23,470
So you'd be able to bypass filters or connect to networks that only specific devices with specific MAC

26
00:02:23,470 --> 00:02:25,030
addresses can connect to.

27
00:02:25,030 --> 00:02:28,210
And you will also be able to hide your identity.

28
00:02:29,200 --> 00:02:32,020
Now changing the Mac address is very, very simple.

29
00:02:32,050 --> 00:02:37,960
Let me show you how to do that manually and then we'll see how we can write a program to do that.

30
00:02:38,620 --> 00:02:46,240
So first of all, we can use the if config command to list all the interfaces on the current computer.

31
00:02:46,780 --> 00:02:50,290
When I say interface, I mean a network card.

32
00:02:50,710 --> 00:02:53,710
So right here we have ETA zero.

33
00:02:54,010 --> 00:02:56,590
This is actually a virtual interface.

34
00:02:56,590 --> 00:02:57,620
It's not real.

35
00:02:57,640 --> 00:03:03,850
It is created by virtual box because we set this computer to use an ad network.

36
00:03:04,510 --> 00:03:10,720
So remember when I said that this computer thinks it is connected to a wired network and you can see

37
00:03:10,720 --> 00:03:12,340
it says Wired Connected.

38
00:03:12,670 --> 00:03:19,630
And all of this is done through a virtual network and a virtual interface in here connected to that

39
00:03:19,630 --> 00:03:20,350
network.

40
00:03:21,370 --> 00:03:25,720
You can also see the low, which is a virtual interface created by Linux.

41
00:03:25,720 --> 00:03:35,020
And I also have Land Zero, which is an external USB wireless adapter, so you might not have Land zero.

42
00:03:35,050 --> 00:03:35,800
It's okay.

43
00:03:35,800 --> 00:03:41,710
You don't really need it unless you want to run some of the attacks that we will do later on against

44
00:03:41,710 --> 00:03:43,000
our wireless network.

45
00:03:43,390 --> 00:03:44,890
So don't worry about it now.

46
00:03:44,890 --> 00:03:46,600
We'll talk more about it later.

47
00:03:46,600 --> 00:03:49,060
But it is not mandatory anyway.

48
00:03:49,970 --> 00:03:57,440
Now other than the interfaces, the IFF config command also lists information about each one of these

49
00:03:57,440 --> 00:03:58,460
interfaces.

50
00:03:59,360 --> 00:04:05,090
So you can see if this interface is connected to a network, you'll see it has an IP.

51
00:04:05,600 --> 00:04:09,230
You can see this one does not have an IP because it is not connected.

52
00:04:10,190 --> 00:04:18,230
You can also see the net mask and the broadcast for this network and you can also see the Mac address

53
00:04:18,230 --> 00:04:19,830
of this interface.

54
00:04:19,850 --> 00:04:25,430
So right now, the MAC address for ETA zero is the highlighted part right here.

55
00:04:26,340 --> 00:04:28,260
Same for ETA zero.

56
00:04:28,260 --> 00:04:30,330
We have the Mac address in here.

57
00:04:31,590 --> 00:04:35,610
Now to change this Mac address we're going to do if config.

58
00:04:37,000 --> 00:04:42,120
Followed by the name of the interface that we want to change its Mac address.

59
00:04:42,130 --> 00:04:46,990
So if you want to change the MAC address of eight zero, you do it at zero.

60
00:04:47,020 --> 00:04:51,790
If you want to change the MAC address of zero, you would do like zero.

61
00:04:52,870 --> 00:04:58,840
Now in this example, I want to do it for zero because everybody will have to zero.

62
00:04:58,870 --> 00:05:02,150
Only people who have a wireless adapter will see LAN zero.

63
00:05:02,170 --> 00:05:06,670
So I'm going to do it for zero so that everybody can follow this.

64
00:05:07,800 --> 00:05:12,270
Then I'm going to type down to disable this interface.

65
00:05:12,360 --> 00:05:13,970
So this is the first step.

66
00:05:13,980 --> 00:05:16,650
We're going to disable our interface.

67
00:05:17,600 --> 00:05:19,910
I'm going to hit enter and that's done.

68
00:05:19,940 --> 00:05:24,620
If you don't see any errors, that means the command got executed properly.

69
00:05:25,460 --> 00:05:29,960
Now that the interface is disabled, we can modify its options.

70
00:05:29,960 --> 00:05:35,090
And the option that we want to modify is the other, which is the MAC address.

71
00:05:36,080 --> 00:05:38,900
So I'm going to say if config.

72
00:05:39,660 --> 00:05:44,910
Again, put the name of the interface that you want to modify its option, which is ETA zero.

73
00:05:46,070 --> 00:05:49,970
And we're going to say, hey, w other.

74
00:05:50,760 --> 00:05:54,990
And we're going to specify the new Mac address that we want to use.

75
00:05:55,200 --> 00:05:58,120
So you can use any Mac address that you want.

76
00:05:58,140 --> 00:06:07,440
And for example, I'm just going to use 0011, two, two, three, three, four, four, five, five.

77
00:06:08,190 --> 00:06:14,430
So make sure at least 12 characters, make sure you split it using the colon and that's it.

78
00:06:15,550 --> 00:06:17,020
So I'm going to hit enter.

79
00:06:18,130 --> 00:06:19,900
And again, we don't see any errors.

80
00:06:19,900 --> 00:06:27,940
So that means the command got executed properly and now all we have to do is enable the interface because

81
00:06:27,940 --> 00:06:30,220
we disabled it using this command.

82
00:06:31,120 --> 00:06:38,620
So to enable the interface we're going to do if config interface name, which is ETA zero and I'm going

83
00:06:38,620 --> 00:06:40,840
to say up to enable it.

84
00:06:41,050 --> 00:06:45,730
So we do down to disable the interface and up to enable it.

85
00:06:46,580 --> 00:06:48,050
So I'm going to hit enter.

86
00:06:48,200 --> 00:06:50,020
And again, we don't see any errors.

87
00:06:50,240 --> 00:06:53,180
So that means the command got executed properly.

88
00:06:53,270 --> 00:07:00,470
And the last thing that I'm going to do now is do if config again, to see if the Mac address did actually

89
00:07:00,470 --> 00:07:01,460
get changed.

90
00:07:01,970 --> 00:07:03,500
So I'm going to hit Enter.

91
00:07:04,070 --> 00:07:11,360
And as you can see now, if we look at the other part of ETA zero, you can see that this has been changed

92
00:07:11,360 --> 00:07:15,560
to 00112, two, 334455.

93
00:07:16,600 --> 00:07:19,720
So as you can see, it is very easy to do that.

94
00:07:19,750 --> 00:07:24,010
And like I said, this would be useful in so many scenarios.

95
00:07:24,700 --> 00:07:30,340
Now, the main point why I'm showing you how to change it manually is because I want you to understand

96
00:07:30,340 --> 00:07:33,220
what a mac address is, how to change it.

97
00:07:33,220 --> 00:07:39,580
And then in this section, we're going to write a program that will automatically change the Mac address

98
00:07:39,580 --> 00:07:40,510
for us.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,270 --> 00:00:01,800
Okay.

2
00:00:01,840 --> 00:00:05,830
Now we know what a mac address is and how to change it.

3
00:00:06,520 --> 00:00:14,020
What we want to do now is write a Python program or script that will do this for us automatically.

4
00:00:14,350 --> 00:00:17,880
Now, I know you're thinking right now that you don't know anything about Python.

5
00:00:17,890 --> 00:00:20,590
How are you going to write a program that will do this?

6
00:00:20,740 --> 00:00:25,390
Now, like I said, learning programming can get boring if you don't have a purpose.

7
00:00:25,420 --> 00:00:28,530
That's why I want to teach this course by example.

8
00:00:28,540 --> 00:00:35,290
So we're going to start programming from the start, and as we program, I'm going to teach you programming

9
00:00:35,290 --> 00:00:38,230
concepts and how to program in general.

10
00:00:38,650 --> 00:00:43,930
So this is the first proper programming lecture that we're doing, but we're actually going to build

11
00:00:43,930 --> 00:00:47,440
a very simple script that will change the Mac address for us.

12
00:00:47,890 --> 00:00:54,320
Now, from what you know so far, all you have to do to change the Mac address is run these commands.

13
00:00:54,340 --> 00:01:00,130
So if you want to write a program that does this, the first thing that you need to do is be able to

14
00:01:00,130 --> 00:01:03,340
execute Linux commands through that program.

15
00:01:03,340 --> 00:01:09,130
We want our program to be able to execute terminal commands the same way that we are executing them

16
00:01:09,130 --> 00:01:11,560
here by typing the commands manually.

17
00:01:12,100 --> 00:01:17,560
Now, anything that you can think of, there is probably a way to do it in Python or there is probably

18
00:01:17,560 --> 00:01:19,900
a Python module that does that.

19
00:01:20,500 --> 00:01:27,370
So in order to execute system commands, we have a module called sub process in Python.

20
00:01:28,030 --> 00:01:31,170
Now sub process can execute any system commands.

21
00:01:31,180 --> 00:01:33,220
It doesn't have to be Linux commands.

22
00:01:33,250 --> 00:01:39,700
The difference is if you run the script from Windows, it will allow you to execute Windows commands.

23
00:01:39,700 --> 00:01:43,690
If you run the script from Mac, it will allow you to execute Mac commands.

24
00:01:43,690 --> 00:01:47,260
If you run it from Linux, it will allow you to run Linux commands.

25
00:01:47,290 --> 00:01:52,210
Now, since we're running our script from Linux, then we can run the commands that we learned in the

26
00:01:52,210 --> 00:01:57,340
previous lecture and be able to change our Mac address through a Python program.

27
00:01:58,090 --> 00:02:03,280
Now using the module is very simple and we're going to talk about that in a minute.

28
00:02:03,430 --> 00:02:07,990
But before I show you how to use it, I want to show you the module page.

29
00:02:08,170 --> 00:02:12,340
Now, for every Python module, there is a documentation.

30
00:02:12,340 --> 00:02:19,030
The documentation talks about what the module does, the functions included in this module, and how

31
00:02:19,030 --> 00:02:24,920
to use any of these functions and examples and even good practices and things to avoid.

32
00:02:24,940 --> 00:02:32,470
So it's a very good idea to learn how to go over the documentation and learn what modules do because

33
00:02:32,620 --> 00:02:37,360
there is no course online or anywhere that covers Python completely.

34
00:02:37,360 --> 00:02:39,850
There is no way someone can cover everything.

35
00:02:39,850 --> 00:02:47,560
So all we can do is cover Python for hacking, for example, or Python for data processing or even maybe

36
00:02:47,560 --> 00:02:48,940
a general Python course.

37
00:02:48,940 --> 00:02:50,950
But there is no way to cover everything.

38
00:02:50,950 --> 00:02:56,350
So you need to get into the habit of reading and experimenting and learning how modules work.

39
00:02:56,620 --> 00:03:02,260
Once you get comfortable with using a number of modules, using others is very easy and you'd be able

40
00:03:02,260 --> 00:03:06,580
to just read over the documentation and figure out how to use the modules.

41
00:03:06,850 --> 00:03:11,470
Now, we obviously don't expect you to know this right now, but by the end of the course it should

42
00:03:11,470 --> 00:03:12,880
be very easy for you.

43
00:03:13,210 --> 00:03:16,900
So the module that we want to use is called Sub Process.

44
00:03:17,050 --> 00:03:20,680
And like I said, this module allows us to run system commands.

45
00:03:21,340 --> 00:03:26,890
Now each module contains a number of functions that do something similar.

46
00:03:26,950 --> 00:03:33,190
For example, the sub process module, the one that we want to use, contains a number of functions,

47
00:03:33,190 --> 00:03:37,770
all of which allow us to run system commands but in different ways.

48
00:03:37,780 --> 00:03:43,120
For example, some of them will run commands in the background, some of them will run commands in the

49
00:03:43,120 --> 00:03:47,590
foreground and wait for the command to finish executing and so on.

50
00:03:48,310 --> 00:03:52,510
The function that we want to use today is called call.

51
00:03:52,690 --> 00:03:57,940
So the module name is sub process and the function is called call.

52
00:03:58,330 --> 00:04:04,330
This function allows us to run system commands, but it runs the command in the foreground, it doesn't

53
00:04:04,330 --> 00:04:09,790
run it in the background, it doesn't run it in a different thread, and it waits for the command to

54
00:04:09,790 --> 00:04:13,330
finish executing before it moves to the next line.

55
00:04:13,360 --> 00:04:19,720
This is very important because we don't want our script to exit or continue doing other stuff while

56
00:04:19,720 --> 00:04:21,910
it's still trying to change my Mac address.

57
00:04:21,910 --> 00:04:26,800
I wanted to change the Mac address, finish changing it, and then do other stuff.

58
00:04:26,800 --> 00:04:33,700
So that's why we're using the call, because it will not execute anything else until the command that

59
00:04:33,700 --> 00:04:35,890
I give it finishes executing.

60
00:04:36,640 --> 00:04:42,130
Now if we scroll down, you can see that there is two ways of using this function.

61
00:04:42,670 --> 00:04:47,680
We're going to use the second way in here and then we're going to go and use the first one in a different

62
00:04:47,680 --> 00:04:49,750
lecture and talk about the differences.

63
00:04:51,050 --> 00:04:54,980
So for the second weigh in here, I also have the example here.

64
00:04:55,010 --> 00:05:00,320
All you have to do is just type, sop, process, dot, call followed by the command that you want to

65
00:05:00,320 --> 00:05:03,320
run, followed by shell equals true.

66
00:05:03,740 --> 00:05:05,450
So it's very, very simple.

67
00:05:05,990 --> 00:05:12,320
Now let's have a very simple example of the sub process module before we actually use it to change the

68
00:05:12,320 --> 00:05:15,440
MAC address, just to see how the module works.

69
00:05:15,710 --> 00:05:25,700
So I'm just going to go to my machine and I have my pie chart in here and I have already created a project

70
00:05:25,700 --> 00:05:27,100
called Mac Changer.

71
00:05:27,110 --> 00:05:32,540
So I'm just going to create a new file and I'm creating a Python file.

72
00:05:34,870 --> 00:05:36,520
And I'm going to call it Mark.

73
00:05:36,520 --> 00:05:38,020
Change your dot pie.

74
00:05:41,820 --> 00:05:42,380
Okay.

75
00:05:42,390 --> 00:05:44,550
I'm going to add the shebang at the top.

76
00:05:47,400 --> 00:05:52,230
And then the first thing that I need to do is import the sub process module.

77
00:05:52,230 --> 00:05:55,380
So I'm going to have to say import sub process.

78
00:05:56,750 --> 00:06:03,740
So this literally tells Python that I want to use the sub process module, import all the functions

79
00:06:03,740 --> 00:06:05,780
that I can use with this module.

80
00:06:06,800 --> 00:06:11,540
Once I do that, I'm going to use the module by doing sub process.

81
00:06:12,630 --> 00:06:16,200
So this is the module name and then I'm going to put a dot.

82
00:06:16,260 --> 00:06:22,980
And you can see as soon as I put the dot pie chart automatically shows me all the functions that I can

83
00:06:22,980 --> 00:06:24,410
use with this module.

84
00:06:24,420 --> 00:06:28,140
So these are similar to the functions that you see in here.

85
00:06:28,800 --> 00:06:32,190
Now, like I said, the one that I want to use is called call.

86
00:06:32,850 --> 00:06:39,600
And again, as you can see, as soon as I type call, it shows me the arguments or the inputs that this

87
00:06:39,600 --> 00:06:40,800
function takes.

88
00:06:41,280 --> 00:06:47,130
So I'm going to hit enter to select this function and it will auto fill the brackets for me and it's

89
00:06:47,130 --> 00:06:50,700
going to give me a description on how to use this module.

90
00:06:51,210 --> 00:06:55,320
Now I know how to use it, so I'm just going to move my character to the middle.

91
00:06:56,680 --> 00:07:01,990
And in here we're going to put our command between two quotation marks.

92
00:07:02,560 --> 00:07:04,950
So this is a very simple example.

93
00:07:04,960 --> 00:07:08,020
So all we're going to do is just run if config.

94
00:07:09,250 --> 00:07:16,030
Now we know if config shows us the interfaces that are available to our current operating system.

95
00:07:16,870 --> 00:07:24,940
And finally, we're going to put a comma and add the shell argument and we're going to set that to true

96
00:07:24,940 --> 00:07:28,930
so that we can run shell commands through this function.

97
00:07:30,040 --> 00:07:31,000
And that's it.

98
00:07:31,000 --> 00:07:32,350
Our code is done.

99
00:07:32,350 --> 00:07:38,560
So as you can see, it's a very, very simple code and all it's going to do is it's going to import

100
00:07:38,560 --> 00:07:40,780
the sub process module first.

101
00:07:41,140 --> 00:07:46,000
Then we're using the sub process module and we're adding dot call.

102
00:07:46,000 --> 00:07:52,300
And this is the same way with all modules, you put the module name first followed by the function that

103
00:07:52,300 --> 00:07:55,090
you want to use that stored in this module.

104
00:07:55,360 --> 00:08:01,180
So we're using the call function and we're giving it the first argument as the command that we want

105
00:08:01,180 --> 00:08:02,170
to execute.

106
00:08:02,170 --> 00:08:05,860
And in our case right now, we run, we want to run if config.

107
00:08:06,130 --> 00:08:12,160
And secondly, we're giving it the second argument which sets the shell variable to true.

108
00:08:12,160 --> 00:08:15,310
So we can run Linux commands through this function.

109
00:08:15,850 --> 00:08:19,720
Now you can see that py charm is telling me that there is something wrong with this.

110
00:08:19,720 --> 00:08:24,940
And what's wrong in here is that shell should be a small s, not a capital S.

111
00:08:25,850 --> 00:08:28,760
And this is fine right now.

112
00:08:28,850 --> 00:08:34,340
Now, I'm going to save this by doing control s and then we're going to run it.

113
00:08:34,340 --> 00:08:39,140
You can run it from terminal or you can just go to run and run.

114
00:08:40,240 --> 00:08:43,510
And we're going to select this script, which is mark changer.

115
00:08:44,380 --> 00:08:51,760
And as you can see now, the script got executed in here and it just listed for me the result of IFF

116
00:08:51,760 --> 00:08:52,150
config.

117
00:08:52,150 --> 00:08:54,910
So you can see that it executed if config for me.

118
00:08:55,150 --> 00:09:01,750
And then we see all the interfaces that are available to the current operating system.

119
00:09:02,050 --> 00:09:04,060
So this is a very simple example.

120
00:09:04,060 --> 00:09:08,680
I just wanted to show you how to import a module and run a very simple system command.

121
00:09:08,770 --> 00:09:14,470
And then in the next lecture, this is going to make more sense because we're going to use it to write

122
00:09:14,470 --> 00:09:17,260
a script that will change the Mac address for us.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,030 --> 00:00:01,600
Okay.

2
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:07,360
So from the previous lectures, we know how to change the Mac address using these three commands by

3
00:00:07,360 --> 00:00:14,020
doing if config lan zero down if config lan zero whether followed by the MAC address that we want to

4
00:00:14,020 --> 00:00:20,620
change our MAC address to and then we do if config lan zero up to enable our interface.

5
00:00:21,460 --> 00:00:27,610
We also learned how to execute system commands from Python using the SAP process module.

6
00:00:27,730 --> 00:00:34,570
So we're going to combine these two things and write a program that will change the Mac address for

7
00:00:34,570 --> 00:00:35,080
us.

8
00:00:35,440 --> 00:00:43,450
So first of all, I'm just going to close this from here and then all we have to do right now is change

9
00:00:43,450 --> 00:00:47,710
if config in here with the commands that we executed here.

10
00:00:48,550 --> 00:00:53,080
So the first command that we want to run is going to be if config.

11
00:00:54,260 --> 00:00:56,690
Land zero down.

12
00:00:57,680 --> 00:01:03,940
Then you can actually do control DX to duplicate the line that you're currently at.

13
00:01:03,950 --> 00:01:06,790
So I'm going to do Control D right here.

14
00:01:06,800 --> 00:01:11,120
And as you can see, I have a new line exactly similar to the previous line.

15
00:01:11,600 --> 00:01:22,460
And in here we're going to do if config line 0hw ether and then input the MAC address and let's set

16
00:01:22,460 --> 00:01:24,560
it this time to zero zero.

17
00:01:25,270 --> 00:01:32,320
One, one, two, two, three, three, four, four, and we'll set it to six, six this time so we

18
00:01:32,320 --> 00:01:34,750
can differentiate it from the previous one.

19
00:01:35,530 --> 00:01:39,850
And finally, I'm going to do Control DX again because I'm lazy.

20
00:01:39,850 --> 00:01:41,870
So a duplicates the line for me.

21
00:01:41,890 --> 00:01:49,270
And the final command is going to be if config lan zero up to enable the interface and that's it.

22
00:01:49,270 --> 00:01:49,900
We're done.

23
00:01:49,930 --> 00:01:51,160
Very, very simple.

24
00:01:51,160 --> 00:01:53,530
So very similar to what we did before.

25
00:01:53,560 --> 00:01:58,990
We're importing this process module first and then we're using the same function, which is the call

26
00:01:58,990 --> 00:02:05,590
function which allows us to run system commands and we're on Linux so we can run any Linux commands.

27
00:02:05,680 --> 00:02:09,270
And we know that these commands allow us to change the Mac address.

28
00:02:09,280 --> 00:02:16,840
We put the interface down first, then we set the MAC address that we want to use by doing if config

29
00:02:16,840 --> 00:02:20,170
lan zero hash table other followed by the mac address.

30
00:02:20,440 --> 00:02:25,150
And then we do if config lan zero up to enable the MAC address.

31
00:02:25,720 --> 00:02:32,260
Now if we execute this script from terminal, the script should change the MAC address for us and we

32
00:02:32,260 --> 00:02:35,020
won't have to run these three commands to do it.

33
00:02:35,680 --> 00:02:39,580
So I can run it by going to run here and then run.

34
00:02:39,580 --> 00:02:41,020
But I actually don't want to do that.

35
00:02:41,020 --> 00:02:46,060
I want to run it from terminal because I actually like running stuff from terminal more because I can

36
00:02:46,060 --> 00:02:52,480
run other commands and so that we can run if config after it and make sure that the MAC address did

37
00:02:52,480 --> 00:02:53,740
actually change.

38
00:02:54,160 --> 00:03:00,460
So I'm going to go to my terminal and I'm going to navigate to where this project is stored.

39
00:03:00,580 --> 00:03:06,970
Now, if we go up here just for a second and hover over here, you can see that this project is stored

40
00:03:06,970 --> 00:03:08,500
in my user home.

41
00:03:08,500 --> 00:03:11,020
PY Charm mac changer.

42
00:03:11,800 --> 00:03:19,210
So I'm going to do see the pie chart projects and by the way you can just press tab as you're typing

43
00:03:19,210 --> 00:03:21,790
a command and it will autocomplete for you.

44
00:03:21,790 --> 00:03:28,720
So all I have to do is type Mac and Tab and as you can see, it automatically auto completes the mac

45
00:03:28,720 --> 00:03:29,410
changer.

46
00:03:30,370 --> 00:03:35,560
Now, if I do, LZ, here to list all the files you can see, I have my mac changer script in here.

47
00:03:36,520 --> 00:03:41,140
And to run this script, as usual, all you have to do is do python mac.

48
00:03:41,140 --> 00:03:44,440
Change your dot py hit enter.

49
00:03:45,790 --> 00:03:47,710
And this didn't show us any errors.

50
00:03:47,710 --> 00:03:48,970
So this is very good.

51
00:03:48,970 --> 00:03:51,370
This means that things did actually work.

52
00:03:51,460 --> 00:03:54,070
And now if we do if config lands zero.

53
00:03:55,950 --> 00:04:04,620
You can see that our Mac address got changed to 001122334466 instead of five five.

54
00:04:04,650 --> 00:04:12,630
So this means that our code in here worked and we managed to execute these three system commands through

55
00:04:12,630 --> 00:04:14,550
our Python script.

56
00:04:15,030 --> 00:04:16,260
Now I know what you're thinking.

57
00:04:16,260 --> 00:04:17,410
This is very simple.

58
00:04:17,430 --> 00:04:20,070
All we're doing is just running system commands.

59
00:04:20,430 --> 00:04:25,710
Doesn't really show the power of Python, but we're still at the very start of the lectures.

60
00:04:25,860 --> 00:04:32,610
I'm actually going to spend a little bit more time on this script to cover a lot of basics about Python

61
00:04:32,610 --> 00:04:33,510
programming.

62
00:04:33,900 --> 00:04:38,730
So in the next lectures, we're going to be building up on this very simple script.

63
00:04:38,730 --> 00:04:46,770
And as we do that, I'm going to teach you conditional statements, variables, regex and more.

64
00:04:46,770 --> 00:04:49,900
And this is the style I'm going to follow throughout the whole course.

65
00:04:49,920 --> 00:04:55,530
We're going to be, first of all, creating a script that does something useful, and then we'll build

66
00:04:55,530 --> 00:04:56,530
up on that.

67
00:04:56,550 --> 00:05:02,400
So this won't be the only script that we create, but we're actually spent some time working and adding

68
00:05:02,400 --> 00:05:09,000
features to this script because as I add features, I want to cover Python basics so that I can teach

69
00:05:09,000 --> 00:05:15,630
you more advanced Python programming in the next sections and build more advanced Python programs.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,440 --> 00:00:01,890
Okay.

2
00:00:01,890 --> 00:00:11,070
So now we have a script that can change the Mac address of 102001122334466.

3
00:00:12,090 --> 00:00:18,240
Now, as you can see, this is not a great script because all we can use it for is to change the Mac

4
00:00:18,240 --> 00:00:21,990
address of LAN zero to this specific Mac address.

5
00:00:23,100 --> 00:00:29,880
So we can't use it to change the Mac address of any other interface, and we can't change it to any

6
00:00:29,880 --> 00:00:36,060
other Mac address other than this one unless you come in and manually change the code here, which is

7
00:00:36,060 --> 00:00:37,440
not a good practice.

8
00:00:38,370 --> 00:00:45,180
So a better implementation would be to use variables for the places that vary.

9
00:00:45,450 --> 00:00:47,160
So we have the line zero.

10
00:00:47,160 --> 00:00:48,600
Here is a variable.

11
00:00:48,600 --> 00:00:54,470
The value of it can change depending on the interface that the person wants to use.

12
00:00:54,480 --> 00:00:58,680
So if you want to change the Mac address of to zero, you can set it to zero.

13
00:00:58,680 --> 00:01:01,080
If you want to change the MAC address of line one.

14
00:01:01,080 --> 00:01:05,010
If you have two wireless interfaces, then you can change for line one.

15
00:01:05,760 --> 00:01:11,850
Therefore, this value right here is a variable and it's better to use a variable for that.

16
00:01:12,630 --> 00:01:14,880
Same goes for the Mac address here.

17
00:01:15,300 --> 00:01:21,600
As described before, if you're actually using this in real life, you'd want to be always using a different

18
00:01:21,600 --> 00:01:26,370
Mac address, and you won't always want to use the same Mac address over and over.

19
00:01:27,300 --> 00:01:33,180
Therefore, it will make sense to use a variable for the value of the Mac address in here as well.

20
00:01:33,750 --> 00:01:41,460
And what I mean by a variable is a value that can be easily changed through the user input or through

21
00:01:41,460 --> 00:01:42,840
the program itself.

22
00:01:43,870 --> 00:01:51,010
Now variables are used everywhere in programming, in all programming languages, not only in Python,

23
00:01:51,310 --> 00:01:52,900
and they are very useful.

24
00:01:52,900 --> 00:01:55,510
You can't really write a program without a variable.

25
00:01:56,620 --> 00:02:03,880
