Now when we talk about decision making or if statements, we're talking about executing code only if

17
00:01:31,550 --> 00:01:33,140
a condition is true.

18
00:01:33,590 --> 00:01:40,670
So we're going to first check if the user entered a value for a mac address and only print an error

19
00:01:40,670 --> 00:01:44,450
message if the user did not enter a mac address.

20
00:01:44,450 --> 00:01:48,290
So we're only executing code based on a condition.

21
00:01:48,620 --> 00:01:51,500
Now there are lots of ways to come up with conditions.

22
00:01:51,500 --> 00:01:54,530
You can check if a variable is equal to something.

23
00:01:54,530 --> 00:01:57,260
You can check if something is greater smaller.

24
00:01:57,260 --> 00:02:00,440
You can check if a variable exists in the list.

25
00:02:00,440 --> 00:02:04,010
You can check if something is true or false, and so on.

26
00:02:04,640 --> 00:02:09,380
As we go through the course, we're going to be using a lot of conditional statements, and all of this

27
00:02:09,380 --> 00:02:12,500
is going to become very clear to you right now.

28
00:02:12,500 --> 00:02:19,520
I just want to give you a general idea of what decision making is and some basic examples of if statements.

29
00:02:19,520 --> 00:02:24,110
And as we go through the course, we're actually going to be using all of these statements, we're going

30
00:02:24,110 --> 00:02:25,880
to be using lots of these conditions.

31
00:02:25,880 --> 00:02:29,810
And everything is going to become clear to you by the end of the course.

32
00:02:31,440 --> 00:02:32,100
Right now.

33
00:02:32,100 --> 00:02:38,070
I just wanted to have a quick comparison between three common ways of writing an F statement.

34
00:02:38,490 --> 00:02:43,530
So here on the left, we have the most basic example of an F statement.

35
00:02:43,860 --> 00:02:46,020
So we do if condition.

36
00:02:46,020 --> 00:02:50,800
And like I said, a condition can be a lot of different things and we'll have a look on them later on.

37
00:02:50,820 --> 00:02:53,460
So let's just think of a condition as is.

38
00:02:53,910 --> 00:02:58,860
So we're doing F condition and then we have a block of code here.

39
00:02:59,040 --> 00:03:04,800
And this block of code will only be executed if this condition is true.

40
00:03:05,370 --> 00:03:12,600
Else we have another block of code and this block of code will only be executed if the above condition

41
00:03:12,600 --> 00:03:13,470
is false.

42
00:03:13,500 --> 00:03:18,140
So when you run a Python program in this example, it will check for the condition.

43
00:03:18,150 --> 00:03:20,950
If it's true, it will execute the code here.

44
00:03:20,970 --> 00:03:25,230
If it's false, it will execute the code in the false block.

45
00:03:25,770 --> 00:03:32,040
Then, once it's done with this, whether it did the code in the F block or in the else block, it will

46
00:03:32,040 --> 00:03:35,940
continue with the rest of the code executing it line by line.

47
00:03:36,660 --> 00:03:40,440
Another example is where we have multiple conditions.

48
00:03:40,440 --> 00:03:45,960
So in this example we only had one condition we had if condition otherwise do this.

49
00:03:46,710 --> 00:03:55,140
In this example we can do if condition one, we execute the code in condition one or if condition one

50
00:03:55,140 --> 00:03:59,480
is false, then we're going to check is condition two is true.

51
00:03:59,550 --> 00:04:06,600
If it is, then execute the code here and you can keep doing this on and on and on and on.

52
00:04:06,840 --> 00:04:14,700
Finally, you can use an LS statement to execute code if all of the above conditions are false.

53
00:04:15,730 --> 00:04:17,890
So let's go over this one more time.

54
00:04:18,220 --> 00:04:21,670
In the statement, we have multiple conditions.

55
00:04:22,150 --> 00:04:24,850
It will first check the first condition.

56
00:04:25,030 --> 00:04:32,000
If it's true, it will execute the code in the first condition block and it will exit everything else.

57
00:04:32,020 --> 00:04:36,400
It won't check any of this, and it will go directly to the rest of the code.

58
00:04:37,120 --> 00:04:41,710
If condition one is false, it will check its condition two true.

59
00:04:41,920 --> 00:04:45,580
If it is, it will execute the code here and exit.

60
00:04:45,580 --> 00:04:47,320
Go to the rest of the code.

61
00:04:48,070 --> 00:04:54,820
If all of the conditions are false, it will execute the code in the statement and then go to the rest

62
00:04:54,820 --> 00:04:55,780
of the code.

63
00:04:56,020 --> 00:05:02,560
So this is an example of using multiple conditions in one single if statement.

64
00:05:03,830 --> 00:05:10,010
This example brings me to the third example, which a lot of people get these two mixed up, and that's

65
00:05:10,010 --> 00:05:12,290
why I wanted to have them side by side.

66
00:05:12,320 --> 00:05:18,740
I usually like to teach you programming as a program, but I wanted to have these examples here because

67
00:05:18,740 --> 00:05:23,360
a lot of people get confused with this, and I want to make sure that you understand the difference

68
00:05:23,900 --> 00:05:25,010
in this example.

69
00:05:25,010 --> 00:05:28,370
And here we have multiple conditions as well.

70
00:05:28,640 --> 00:05:33,200
But notice we have two separate if statements.

71
00:05:33,230 --> 00:05:35,600
This is different than what we have here.

72
00:05:35,600 --> 00:05:39,550
We have an if, we have an LF and we have an LS.

73
00:05:39,560 --> 00:05:44,360
So all of this is one if statement in here.

74
00:05:44,360 --> 00:05:46,730
This is two separate if statements.

75
00:05:46,850 --> 00:05:50,210
So what happens is python checks for condition one.

76
00:05:50,240 --> 00:05:52,880
If it's true, it executes the code here.

77
00:05:53,750 --> 00:05:59,900
Even if it's true and it executes the code here, it will check condition two and if it's true, it

78
00:05:59,900 --> 00:06:02,840
will execute the code and condition two as well.

79
00:06:03,350 --> 00:06:10,580
So unlike this example, if condition one is true, Python will go to the rest of the code directly

80
00:06:10,580 --> 00:06:13,340
and want check for the rest of the conditions.

81
00:06:13,490 --> 00:06:17,150
Whereas in here these are two separate if statements.

82
00:06:17,150 --> 00:06:20,900
So if condition one is true, the code here will be executed.

83
00:06:20,900 --> 00:06:24,620
If condition two is true, the code here will be executed as well.

84
00:06:25,070 --> 00:06:27,830
You can also use ls ifs in here.

85
00:06:27,830 --> 00:06:29,450
You can use ls with them.

86
00:06:29,720 --> 00:06:34,370
The reason behind this is these are two separate if statements.

87
00:06:36,010 --> 00:06:36,340
Now.

88
00:06:36,340 --> 00:06:37,230
That's it for now.

89
00:06:37,240 --> 00:06:38,890
I don't want to make this too long.

90
00:06:38,920 --> 00:06:44,770
I just wanted to have these three examples of if statements in here so that you understand the difference

91
00:06:44,770 --> 00:06:45,700
between them.

92
00:06:45,700 --> 00:06:50,950
And in the next lecture, we're actually going to be implementing only one example because it makes

93
00:06:50,950 --> 00:06:53,170
sense in our case and we'll see why.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:01,480
Okay.

2
00:00:01,480 --> 00:00:08,200
Let's put what we learned about if statements and practice and check if the user put a value for the

3
00:00:08,200 --> 00:00:10,660
interface and for the MAC address.

4
00:00:11,080 --> 00:00:18,220
Now from before we know that the options variable and here contains the values for the interface and

5
00:00:18,220 --> 00:00:19,930
for the Mac address.

6
00:00:20,320 --> 00:00:27,880
So all we have to do is check if options dot interface and options dot new Mac contain values.

7
00:00:28,240 --> 00:00:33,640
We're going to have to do this check in here because the options is being captured in here.

8
00:00:34,270 --> 00:00:41,230
I don't really like to do that because we have a get arguments function and we should do everything

9
00:00:41,230 --> 00:00:44,530
that's related to the arguments within this function.

10
00:00:45,580 --> 00:00:51,430
That's why what I'm going to do, I'm going to revert back to something that we had before when we actually

11
00:00:51,430 --> 00:00:55,450
used to capture the arguments in here.

12
00:00:56,880 --> 00:01:00,610
And what we're doing now is very similar to what we were doing before.

13
00:01:00,630 --> 00:01:06,810
We're initializing the options and the arguments with whatever is being returned from the parser and

14
00:01:06,810 --> 00:01:07,800
parse ARGs.

15
00:01:08,040 --> 00:01:14,490
So the options is going to contain the values entered by the user and the arguments is going to contain

16
00:01:14,490 --> 00:01:16,620
the arguments entered by the user.

17
00:01:17,310 --> 00:01:20,970
Now we're going to use our if statement after that.

18
00:01:21,180 --> 00:01:28,110
So following the syntax that I showed you in the previous lecture, we have to do first do the if statement

19
00:01:28,620 --> 00:01:30,300
followed by the condition.

20
00:01:30,300 --> 00:01:34,500
And like I said, there is a large number of ways to write conditions.

21
00:01:34,500 --> 00:01:35,910
We can compare values.

22
00:01:35,910 --> 00:01:40,290
For example, if we have variables, we can check if X is equal to one.

23
00:01:40,290 --> 00:01:44,120
For example, we can check if something is greater than something.

24
00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:50,310
Something is smaller than something if a list contains an element and so on.

25
00:01:50,790 --> 00:01:56,070
In today's lecture, we're going to use something that's relevant to our goal, and we want to check

26
00:01:56,070 --> 00:02:03,150
if options contains a value for the interface and if it contains a value for the Mac address.

27
00:02:03,480 --> 00:02:09,450
So our first condition is going to be if options don't interface.

28
00:02:10,740 --> 00:02:12,010
That's all we have to do.

29
00:02:12,030 --> 00:02:19,320
So all we're saying is if options don't interface, then we put the call on and then we can execute

30
00:02:19,320 --> 00:02:26,940
code and as you can see automatically py charm has indented the space for us and it's allowing us to

31
00:02:26,940 --> 00:02:33,960
write code in here, which means this block of code will only be executed if this condition is true.

32
00:02:34,260 --> 00:02:37,620
And our condition is if options dot interface.

33
00:02:37,620 --> 00:02:41,160
So if options dot interface holds a value.

34
00:02:42,150 --> 00:02:47,580
Now if you think of it, we don't want to check if options dot interface holds a value.

35
00:02:47,610 --> 00:02:48,780
We want to check.

36
00:02:48,780 --> 00:02:52,350
If it does not hold the value, then we want to show an error.

37
00:02:52,680 --> 00:02:59,460
So what we're going to do is we're going to do, if not options dot interface, then we're going to

38
00:02:59,460 --> 00:03:03,390
write code to handle error.

39
00:03:03,420 --> 00:03:06,840
Now I'm putting this as a comment because I want to get back to it later.

40
00:03:07,970 --> 00:03:14,300
Now, the next thing that we want to do is to check if the user entered a value for the Mac address.

41
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:21,230
Now we can we can do this using the leaf or using another if statement.

42
00:03:22,370 --> 00:03:28,130
In our example, it doesn't really matter which one we use because we're going to exit in the first

43
00:03:28,130 --> 00:03:31,910
condition if the user did not put a value for the interface.

44
00:03:32,570 --> 00:03:40,070
But I'm going to go with Elif, which is short for LCF and we're going to say again if not.

45
00:03:41,080 --> 00:03:43,510
Options dot new Mac.

46
00:03:46,220 --> 00:03:49,370
And again in here, we're going to execute code.

47
00:03:50,640 --> 00:03:53,460
To handle error.

48
00:03:54,380 --> 00:03:54,880
Okay.

49
00:03:54,950 --> 00:04:00,320
Now, I didn't put any code in here because I want to have a quick look on the statement and then we'll

50
00:04:00,320 --> 00:04:01,550
put our code.

51
00:04:02,000 --> 00:04:07,280
So what we're doing now is we're checking, if not options dot interface.

52
00:04:07,280 --> 00:04:13,460
So if the user did not put a value for the interface, then we want to display an error and exit.

53
00:04:14,120 --> 00:04:20,690
If they did, then we're going to move to the next check and we'll check if they did not enter a value

54
00:04:20,690 --> 00:04:21,960
for the new Mac.

55
00:04:22,100 --> 00:04:25,970
If they didn't, then we'll also print an error and exit.

56
00:04:26,240 --> 00:04:32,120
Now you can just use a normal print statement and then use an exit statement in Python to exit.

57
00:04:32,660 --> 00:04:38,990
Or we can actually use the parser object to handle this for us.

58
00:04:39,440 --> 00:04:46,040
Because remember when we created the parser object here, we said this is an entity or a person that

59
00:04:46,040 --> 00:04:50,870
knows everything about parsing and can handle all aspects of parsing.

60
00:04:51,590 --> 00:04:54,560
So we can just do parser dot error.

61
00:04:56,830 --> 00:04:58,090
And what this will do.

62
00:04:58,090 --> 00:05:00,580
As you can see, it's already shown us how to use it.

63
00:05:00,880 --> 00:05:05,350
It will basically display the message that we put and exit.

64
00:05:05,620 --> 00:05:12,340
So the message that we're going to display to the person is going to say, please specify an interface,

65
00:05:12,340 --> 00:05:15,370
use dash, dash, help for more info.

66
00:05:16,710 --> 00:05:21,810
And again, we're going to use a similar instruction for this one.

67
00:05:21,840 --> 00:05:26,940
The only difference is we're going to say, please specify a mark.

68
00:05:27,060 --> 00:05:29,670
Use dash, dash, help for info.

69
00:05:30,300 --> 00:05:32,070
Now this is all good.

70
00:05:32,100 --> 00:05:38,110
One thing that we haven't done so far is handle the code after the if statement.

71
00:05:38,130 --> 00:05:45,870
So after this if statement, if we reach this line, line 16, that means the user put an interface

72
00:05:45,870 --> 00:05:47,210
and put a new mark.

73
00:05:47,220 --> 00:05:50,970
So we should be able to change the mark for them.

74
00:05:51,090 --> 00:05:56,340
So all we have to do at this point is return options.

75
00:05:56,910 --> 00:06:01,800
And I chose the return options because we never use the arguments in our program.

76
00:06:01,800 --> 00:06:06,480
So it makes sense to just return the options from the get arguments in here.

77
00:06:07,450 --> 00:06:13,270
And then when we're calling get arguments here, we have to capture the value of options.

78
00:06:13,270 --> 00:06:17,320
And I'm just going to create another variable and I'm going to call it options.

79
00:06:18,790 --> 00:06:19,600
And that's it.

80
00:06:19,930 --> 00:06:22,510
Now let's have a quick look at our program.

81
00:06:22,960 --> 00:06:29,860
Up to this line, we know that options and arguments will contain the arguments and the values returned

82
00:06:29,860 --> 00:06:35,920
by parser to parse ARGs, which is the values and the arguments entered by the user.

83
00:06:36,910 --> 00:06:40,660
Then we're using an if statement in the first condition.

84
00:06:40,660 --> 00:06:43,720
We'll check in if the interface is not set.

85
00:06:44,230 --> 00:06:47,500
If it's not set, then we're going to display.

86
00:06:47,500 --> 00:06:51,580
Please specify an interface and then exit the program.

87
00:06:52,060 --> 00:06:55,840
If it is set, then this condition is false.

88
00:06:55,840 --> 00:07:03,550
We're going to go to the next condition and we're going to check if the options dot new Mac is set.

89
00:07:03,880 --> 00:07:08,860
If it's not set, then we're going to display all of this and exit.

90
00:07:09,520 --> 00:07:16,750
So if we reach this line and we didn't exit the program, then that means we have an interface and we

91
00:07:16,750 --> 00:07:17,770
have a new Mac.

92
00:07:18,160 --> 00:07:23,440
The variable that holds the interface and the new Mac is the options.

93
00:07:23,440 --> 00:07:25,480
So we can just return that.

94
00:07:26,370 --> 00:07:32,070
We're capturing this in here where we are calling the get arguments and then we're using options that

95
00:07:32,070 --> 00:07:38,010
interface and options the new mark in the change mark which will execute all of this and change the

96
00:07:38,010 --> 00:07:39,210
Mac address for me.

97
00:07:40,190 --> 00:07:45,890
Now let's go and run this program and see how it's going to handle the errors.

98
00:07:46,130 --> 00:07:49,700
So, first of all, let's run it without a mac address.

99
00:07:50,240 --> 00:07:55,970
And as you can see, it's given us the usage and it's given us the error message that we specified,

100
00:07:55,970 --> 00:08:00,350
saying, please specify a new Mac, use help for more info.

101
00:08:00,890 --> 00:08:05,720
Now let's try to give it a mac, but not give it an interface.

102
00:08:08,600 --> 00:08:13,340
And again, we get an error message telling us, please specify an interface.

103
00:08:14,060 --> 00:08:20,090
Now, if we run the program properly, just to make sure that it works and this time let's change the

104
00:08:20,090 --> 00:08:23,840
Mac address to something that ends with one one hit enter.

105
00:08:23,870 --> 00:08:33,110
We get a proper print statement and if we do, if config 88 zero, you'll see that the MAC address did

106
00:08:33,110 --> 00:08:35,330
change to the MAC address that we want.

107
00:08:37,090 --> 00:08:38,380
So that's it for now.

108
00:08:38,410 --> 00:08:41,250
Quick example of using an if statement.

109
00:08:41,260 --> 00:08:48,000
I highly recommend spending some time experimenting with different types and different styles of IF

110
00:08:48,010 --> 00:08:48,910
statements.

111
00:08:48,910 --> 00:08:53,710
Something similar to what I showed you in the previous lecture just to get more comfortable with it.

112
00:08:53,830 --> 00:08:56,680
We're going to be using this a lot throughout the course.

113
00:08:56,680 --> 00:08:58,570
We're going to be using a lot of if statements.

114
00:08:58,570 --> 00:09:00,700
We're going to be using a lot of conditions.

115
00:09:00,700 --> 00:09:04,180
So using this is going to become very easy for you anyway.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 3: MAC Changer - Algorithm Design

1
00:00:01,210 --> 00:00:01,630
Okay.

2
00:00:01,660 --> 00:00:08,110
Now that our program checks and make sure that the user gives the right input, it will be nice to also

3
00:00:08,110 --> 00:00:10,720
add another check at the end of the program.

4
00:00:11,440 --> 00:00:16,780
What I'm talking about is you see me every time I run the program and you're probably doing that as

5
00:00:16,780 --> 00:00:17,240
well.

6
00:00:17,260 --> 00:00:21,400
We go and manually do if config interface name.

7
00:00:21,400 --> 00:00:27,910
So for example, ETA zero or line zero and then check and see if our Mac address got changed to the

8
00:00:27,910 --> 00:00:30,550
value that we requested from the program.

9
00:00:31,360 --> 00:00:38,230
So all I want to do, I want to add functionality to our program so that at the end of execution, it

10
00:00:38,230 --> 00:00:44,830
will check if the Mac address did actually change to what the user asked or if something went wrong.

11
00:00:44,830 --> 00:00:47,560
And then the Mac address did not get changed.

12
00:00:48,070 --> 00:00:52,330
Now, in order to do this, we need to come up with a very simple algorithm.

13
00:00:52,780 --> 00:00:58,510
What we mean by algorithm is a number of steps that will allow us to achieve our goal.

14
00:00:59,170 --> 00:01:06,610
So our goal here is to check if the Mac address got changed to the MAC address that the user requested.

15
00:01:06,640 --> 00:01:12,010
We're doing this at the end of the execution of the program and to come up with the steps that we need

16
00:01:12,010 --> 00:01:12,650
to take.

17
00:01:12,670 --> 00:01:18,970
All we have to do is just think, what do we do as humans when we try to check if the Mac address got

18
00:01:18,970 --> 00:01:19,540
changed?

19
00:01:19,570 --> 00:01:25,150
We open a terminal window, we're on F config and we check the value under the other.

20
00:01:26,110 --> 00:01:32,950
So if we want to get our program to carry out these steps, we're going to have to first execute and

21
00:01:32,950 --> 00:01:34,300
read if config.

22
00:01:34,510 --> 00:01:41,150
So so far we know how to execute system commands using Python, but we don't know how to read the output.

23
00:01:41,170 --> 00:01:44,650
So we need to be able to read the result of if config.

24
00:01:45,340 --> 00:01:49,510
We're also going to need to read the MAC address from this result.

25
00:01:49,510 --> 00:01:55,570
So once we read the whole result of config, we're going to need to filter this result to only this

26
00:01:55,570 --> 00:01:57,730
part of the result.

27
00:01:57,730 --> 00:01:59,260
So only the other part.

28
00:01:59,260 --> 00:02:01,420
The part that contains the Mac address.

29
00:02:04,070 --> 00:02:09,800
Third, we're going to need to use an if statement, so decision making to check if the Mac address

30
00:02:09,800 --> 00:02:11,590
is what the user requested.

31
00:02:11,600 --> 00:02:18,170
And finally, print an appropriate message saying either the MAC address got changed or the MAC address

32
00:02:18,170 --> 00:02:19,400
did not get changed.

33
00:02:19,940 --> 00:02:25,280
Now we're going to apply everything that we learned so far in order to achieve this, and we'll also

34
00:02:25,280 --> 00:02:28,550
learn new concepts as we go through these steps.

35
00:02:29,030 --> 00:02:31,760
We're going to tackle these steps one at a time.

36
00:02:31,760 --> 00:02:36,920
And as we carry out this step, we're going to forget about the general goal.

37
00:02:36,920 --> 00:02:42,200
We're going to forget about the fact that we want to check if the Mac address was changed.

38
00:02:42,200 --> 00:02:47,060
When we go to step one, we're just going to focus on solving that simple problem.

39
00:02:47,060 --> 00:02:50,780
We go to step two, we focus on solving that simple problem.

40
00:02:50,780 --> 00:02:56,090
And then at the end, you're going to see how everything clicks together and solve the bigger problem,

41
00:02:56,090 --> 00:02:58,820
which is check if the Mac address got changed.

42
00:02:59,120 --> 00:03:05,270
So what we're doing is we're splitting the big problem into a number of small problems which are easier

43
00:03:05,270 --> 00:03:07,790
to solve, solve these small problems.

44
00:03:07,790 --> 00:03:12,620
And at the end, once we solve all of these small problems, solving the big problem is going to be

45
00:03:12,620 --> 00:03:14,210
very simple and trivial.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:09,030
Now, the first step or the first simple problem that we need to solve is being able to execute system

2
00:00:09,030 --> 00:00:11,940
commands and read the result.

3
00:00:12,120 --> 00:00:20,040
Because so far we learned how to execute system commands using this process called function, but that

4
00:00:20,040 --> 00:00:24,090
allowed us to execute commands without seeing the result.

5
00:00:24,750 --> 00:00:31,980
Right now we're interested into running if config, but we want to see the result that if config returns

6
00:00:31,980 --> 00:00:32,790
to us.

7
00:00:33,240 --> 00:00:39,120
Now if you do a quick Google search or if you look through the manual of Python, you're going to come

8
00:00:39,120 --> 00:00:42,000
up across the sub process module again.

9
00:00:42,000 --> 00:00:47,550
And this is actually the same page that contains the sub process dot called function.

10
00:00:48,030 --> 00:00:52,650
If you literally just scroll down, you'll see sub process to check output.

11
00:00:53,280 --> 00:00:59,880
This is a function that allows us to execute system commands, just like sub process, dot call.

12
00:00:59,970 --> 00:01:06,900
The only difference is this time sub process to check output, as the name suggests, will return the

13
00:01:06,900 --> 00:01:09,390
output returned by the command.

14
00:01:09,540 --> 00:01:16,620
So when we use this function to run, if config will actually get the result that if config returns,

15
00:01:16,620 --> 00:01:22,770
we'll be able to capture that using a variable and then print it, modify it or do whatever we want

16
00:01:22,770 --> 00:01:24,660
with it in our program.

17
00:01:25,260 --> 00:01:31,620
Now you can see there is examples of how to use this function in here and you can see the way to use

18
00:01:31,620 --> 00:01:35,760
it is very similar to the way we use sub process dot call.

19
00:01:35,790 --> 00:01:37,170
You have a list.

20
00:01:37,260 --> 00:01:43,980
Each element of the list is a word in the command and you just call it by doing sub process dot check

21
00:01:43,980 --> 00:01:44,730
output.

22
00:01:45,240 --> 00:01:50,280
So let's go ahead and write this in our program and see how this is going to work.

23
00:01:50,970 --> 00:01:52,920
Now I have my program here.

24
00:01:52,920 --> 00:01:58,620
I'm actually just going to click on the minus and here beside the function name, this will allow me

25
00:01:58,620 --> 00:02:00,520
to hide the function code.

26
00:02:00,540 --> 00:02:02,490
The function code will not be deleted.

27
00:02:02,490 --> 00:02:08,310
I can just press on the plus to show it again, but it just makes it easier for us to focus on what

28
00:02:08,310 --> 00:02:09,300
we want to do.

29
00:02:09,810 --> 00:02:16,230
Now I'm going to write my code here and I'm actually before I do that, I'm going to comment this line.

30
00:02:16,260 --> 00:02:19,220
This is the line that actually changes my Mac address.

31
00:02:19,230 --> 00:02:21,330
It calls the change Mac function.

32
00:02:21,450 --> 00:02:26,850
But there is no point of change in the MAC address right now because we're just testing this new check

33
00:02:26,850 --> 00:02:28,050
output function.

34
00:02:28,170 --> 00:02:33,420
So I'm just going to press control forward slash or you can just type hash before the line.

35
00:02:33,420 --> 00:02:38,430
The line will be commented and Python will ignore this line when you run the program.

36
00:02:39,150 --> 00:02:43,260
Now, the function that we want to test is called sub process.

37
00:02:44,150 --> 00:02:44,930
DOT.

38
00:02:44,930 --> 00:02:50,060
And as you can see, when you press the dot, you'll see all the functions that you can use from the

39
00:02:50,060 --> 00:02:51,440
sub process module.

40
00:02:51,560 --> 00:02:55,340
The one we're interested in is the first one there, which is check output.

41
00:02:56,780 --> 00:03:01,430
And once you press that, you're going to get a description of how to use this function.

42
00:03:01,430 --> 00:03:06,380
But we already know that it's very similar to Suppress Call, which we are familiar with.

43
00:03:06,590 --> 00:03:13,580
All we have to do is put two square brackets to open a list, and the command that we want to run is

44
00:03:13,580 --> 00:03:16,880
if config followed by our interface name.

45
00:03:16,880 --> 00:03:23,240
So it's literally if config followed by interface name, let's say you want to run it on line zero.

46
00:03:23,480 --> 00:03:25,310
So you type line zero.

47
00:03:25,730 --> 00:03:32,780
So in our script, this is going to be if config in the first element between two quotation marks,

48
00:03:33,410 --> 00:03:38,750
put a comma to put the second element or the second word, anything that comes after a space.

49
00:03:39,140 --> 00:03:42,260
And our second element is the interface.

50
00:03:42,440 --> 00:03:49,550
Now we know in our program the interface in here is captured from the get arguments using the options

51
00:03:49,970 --> 00:03:55,880
and we use it the same way that we use it with the check mark function by doing options dot interface.

52
00:03:56,180 --> 00:04:02,300
So all I have to do is just put options that interface in here as my second element.

53
00:04:03,630 --> 00:04:08,480
Now we're not enclosing this between quotation marks because this is a variable.

54
00:04:08,490 --> 00:04:14,370
We want Python to know that this is a variable so that it substitutes the value that stored in that

55
00:04:14,370 --> 00:04:15,060
variable.

56
00:04:15,680 --> 00:04:17,260
So far so simple.

57
00:04:17,270 --> 00:04:19,010
One python will reach this line.

58
00:04:19,010 --> 00:04:25,400
It's going to execute if config followed by the interface that the person inputs as an argument.

59
00:04:26,030 --> 00:04:33,980
Now this will be executed and it's all fine, but after the execution check output will return the result

60
00:04:33,980 --> 00:04:40,100
that if config returns but this result is not being captured or used anywhere.

61
00:04:40,340 --> 00:04:43,670
So we actually have to capture that result in a variable.

62
00:04:43,910 --> 00:04:46,310
You can call the variable anything you want.

63
00:04:46,310 --> 00:04:51,470
But because I like to use meaningful names, I'm going to call it if config result.

64
00:04:52,880 --> 00:04:59,090
I'm going to say that should be equal to sub process to check output and then I'm putting the command

65
00:04:59,090 --> 00:05:01,130
that I want sub process.

66
00:05:01,130 --> 00:05:03,580
The check output will execute this command.

67
00:05:03,590 --> 00:05:05,450
It will wait for the result.

68
00:05:05,450 --> 00:05:12,050
Once it gets the result, it's going to return it to my variable, which is called if config result

69
00:05:12,620 --> 00:05:14,630
after the execution of this line.

70
00:05:14,630 --> 00:05:21,200
If config result will contain the value of the result of if config followed by the interface that the

71
00:05:21,200 --> 00:05:22,580
user enters.

72
00:05:23,060 --> 00:05:28,850
So at the end, we want to make sure that if config result contains the values that we expect it should

73
00:05:28,850 --> 00:05:29,510
contain.

74
00:05:29,510 --> 00:05:31,070
So I'm just going to do print.

75
00:05:31,900 --> 00:05:33,340
If config result.

76
00:05:33,970 --> 00:05:34,870
And that's it.

77
00:05:35,560 --> 00:05:37,930
Let's go ahead and run our python code.

78
00:05:39,440 --> 00:05:42,770
And right now we're using AI as land zero.

79
00:05:42,770 --> 00:05:44,690
So we're setting the interface to zero.

80
00:05:44,690 --> 00:05:50,390
So when we execute this, we should see the result of if config line zero hit enter.

81
00:05:50,390 --> 00:05:54,560
And as you can see, I have the result of if config lan zero.

82
00:05:55,410 --> 00:05:57,860
Now let's make sure our code is working properly.

83
00:05:57,870 --> 00:05:59,820
Let's try to give it a different interface.

84
00:05:59,850 --> 00:06:02,280
Let's give it a zero, for example.

85
00:06:03,520 --> 00:06:09,040
And again, as you can see, we're getting the result of if config it had zero.

86
00:06:09,710 --> 00:06:10,510
So that's it.

87
00:06:10,520 --> 00:06:12,730
Now, step one is done, it solved.

88
00:06:12,740 --> 00:06:19,070
We are able to execute system commands and we are able to capture the result of that command and then

89
00:06:19,070 --> 00:06:23,660
we'll be able to manipulate this result through the if config result variable.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,920 --> 00:00:08,330
Now that we have the first simple problem solved and we are able to execute and read the output of system

2
00:00:08,330 --> 00:00:16,130
commands, we can move to the next step, which is reading the Mac address from the output that we got.

3
00:00:16,520 --> 00:00:23,900
So if we go back and here and look at our program, we can see that the output that we're capturing

4
00:00:23,900 --> 00:00:32,150
in F config result is the output of if config, which is all of this text right here.

5
00:00:32,660 --> 00:00:35,740
Now, all of this is not interesting for us.

6
00:00:35,750 --> 00:00:42,170
The only part that's interesting for us is the Mac address, which is this part right here.

7
00:00:42,710 --> 00:00:50,630
So we need a way of telling our program to ignore everything and only read this part.

8
00:00:50,630 --> 00:00:54,620
So only read the highlighted part, which is my Mac address.

9
00:00:55,340 --> 00:01:01,610
Now the method that I'm going to show you can be used to filter anything you want, but we're just using

10
00:01:01,610 --> 00:01:03,080
this as an example.

11
00:01:03,080 --> 00:01:08,600
So if you are building a different program, you might want to filter something else from within a different

12
00:01:08,600 --> 00:01:09,260
text.

13
00:01:09,290 --> 00:01:10,700
It doesn't really matter.

14
00:01:10,910 --> 00:01:17,720
I'm going to show you a very powerful way of being able to search and look for interesting pieces of

15
00:01:17,720 --> 00:01:19,610
text within a large text.

16
00:01:20,370 --> 00:01:26,490
What we're going to use to do that is we're going to use what's called regex or regular expressions.

17
00:01:26,880 --> 00:01:33,030
Now, regex is available in a lot of programming languages because it's a very good way to search,

18
00:01:33,030 --> 00:01:37,650
text and find interesting pieces based on rules.

19
00:01:38,130 --> 00:01:44,940
So basically it allows us to define a rule or a pattern and then Python or whatever programming language

20
00:01:44,940 --> 00:01:50,610
you're using is going to search through that text for the pattern that you specify.

21
00:01:50,970 --> 00:01:52,410
Let me show you what I mean.

22
00:01:53,490 --> 00:01:58,410
I'm going to, first of all, copy all of the text that I'm interested in, which is the output of my

23
00:01:58,410 --> 00:01:59,640
program right now.

24
00:02:00,180 --> 00:02:05,520
I'm going to right click copy and then I'm going to go to a website called Python.

25
00:02:06,420 --> 00:02:12,000
Now, what this website does is it allows us to build regex rules.

26
00:02:12,300 --> 00:02:16,080
So first of all, we're going to put the text that we want to search for in here.

27
00:02:16,080 --> 00:02:20,880
So this is the text that contains the information that's interesting to us.

28
00:02:21,330 --> 00:02:29,280
And then we can practice or use regex rules in here and it'll highlight for us whatever our rule is

29
00:02:29,280 --> 00:02:30,030
matching.

30
00:02:30,450 --> 00:02:35,100
For example, I know it's still very vague what I'm talking about, but let me show you an example and

31
00:02:35,100 --> 00:02:42,810
you'll know the way that regex works is you give it a rule in here, and based on that rule, it will

32
00:02:42,810 --> 00:02:49,410
be able to match patterns of text within the text that you pasted or the string that you pasted.

33
00:02:50,160 --> 00:02:56,130
So you can click on this here regular expression, cheatsheet, and it will display all of this cheat

34
00:02:56,130 --> 00:02:57,150
sheet for you.

35
00:02:57,480 --> 00:03:03,300
So for example, you can see that if you put a dot as a rule, it will match any character.

36
00:03:03,300 --> 00:03:06,480
So it will basically match the whole text in here.

37
00:03:06,720 --> 00:03:07,470
Let's have a look.

38
00:03:07,470 --> 00:03:09,570
So I'm just going to put a dot in here.

39
00:03:09,960 --> 00:03:15,750
And as you can see in the match result, you can see that we matched the whole text.

40
00:03:16,980 --> 00:03:18,660
Now let's delete that.

41
00:03:19,260 --> 00:03:22,890
And let's say, for example, you you want to look for numbers.

42
00:03:22,890 --> 00:03:26,550
You want the numbers that are within this text.

43
00:03:26,970 --> 00:03:34,530
Then let's go down and see what character corresponds to numbers and you can see that backward slash

44
00:03:34,530 --> 00:03:36,870
DX corresponds to digit.

45
00:03:37,080 --> 00:03:44,940
So in my rule, if I put backward slash dx, you'll see it's going to match anywhere in the text that

46
00:03:44,940 --> 00:03:46,290
contains a digit.

47
00:03:46,680 --> 00:03:53,580
So if you are writing code that needs to use any of these digits, then you'd be able to use this rule

48
00:03:53,580 --> 00:03:55,380
to find these digits.

49
00:03:55,380 --> 00:03:59,160
Now how to implement this in Python, I'll show that to you in the next lecture.

50
00:03:59,160 --> 00:04:05,760
So for now, I just want you to have a general idea of what regex is and how to write rules for regex.

51
00:04:06,330 --> 00:04:08,760
Now, same goes for anything that you're looking for.

52
00:04:08,760 --> 00:04:15,390
For example, let's say you're looking for anything that comes after the greater and the smaller size

53
00:04:15,390 --> 00:04:16,080
in here.

54
00:04:16,590 --> 00:04:23,100
So what we're going to do is we're going to say, I'm looking for something that starts with greater

55
00:04:23,340 --> 00:04:24,900
ends with smaller.

56
00:04:25,230 --> 00:04:28,560
And between these two things, I don't care what's in there.

57
00:04:28,560 --> 00:04:31,260
I don't care whatever that's in there, show it to me.

58
00:04:31,260 --> 00:04:34,500
So like we said, Dot Match is any character.

59
00:04:34,590 --> 00:04:41,940
So if we put a dot here followed by a star because a star means zero or more of this.

60
00:04:41,940 --> 00:04:49,050
So zero of or more of a dot means zero or more characters between these two signs.

61
00:04:49,680 --> 00:04:55,980
And as you can see, it's matching this part of the string, the big string that we have.

62
00:04:57,520 --> 00:04:57,880
Now.

63
00:04:57,880 --> 00:04:59,890
None of this is interesting to us.

64
00:04:59,920 --> 00:05:05,080
What's interesting to us is the Mac address, which is this part right here.

65
00:05:05,830 --> 00:05:12,160
Now, if you look at the Mac address, you'll see that we usually have character, character, colon,

66
00:05:12,160 --> 00:05:17,620
character, character, colon, character, character, colon, and so on six times.

67
00:05:18,310 --> 00:05:23,830
Now you can see that the characters that we have, they can either be numbers or letters, so they're

68
00:05:23,830 --> 00:05:25,180
alphanumeric.

69
00:05:25,780 --> 00:05:34,720
Now let's go to our cheat sheet and you'll see that we can use the backward slash W to match an alphanumeric

70
00:05:34,720 --> 00:05:35,560
character.

71
00:05:36,100 --> 00:05:42,740
So in order to match the Mac address, all we have to do is do backward slash w.

72
00:05:42,820 --> 00:05:48,610
Now this will match all alphanumeric characters and as you can see, it's pretty much matching everything

73
00:05:48,610 --> 00:05:53,530
within our text except for the brackets, for the commas and the colons.

74
00:05:54,010 --> 00:05:58,330
But we want this to be followed by another alphanumeric character.

75
00:05:58,900 --> 00:06:02,830
And as we can see in the pattern, we have alphanumeric, alphanumeric.

76
00:06:02,830 --> 00:06:05,220
And then we have a colon for the Mac.

77
00:06:05,230 --> 00:06:06,820
So I'm going to put a colon.

78
00:06:07,390 --> 00:06:12,280
And as you can see, we have filtered all this up to the end.

79
00:06:13,120 --> 00:06:18,700
The reason why this didn't get included, because we're looking for alphanumeric.

80
00:06:18,700 --> 00:06:21,310
Alphanumeric followed by a colon.

81
00:06:21,310 --> 00:06:27,700
And as you can see there, the last element here or the last part of the Mac address follow is followed

82
00:06:27,700 --> 00:06:28,840
by a space.

83
00:06:29,380 --> 00:06:36,610
So you can actually use or a space in here, but that's going to make the regular expression a little

84
00:06:36,610 --> 00:06:37,960
bit hard to understand.

85
00:06:37,960 --> 00:06:39,190
So I'm not going to do it now.

86
00:06:39,190 --> 00:06:42,820
I'm going to keep it simple, and I'm just going to repeat this six times.

87
00:06:42,820 --> 00:06:49,990
So we're looking for alphanumeric, alphanumeric, colon, alphanumeric, alphanumeric colon, alphanumeric,

88
00:06:49,990 --> 00:06:51,370
alphanumeric, colon.

89
00:06:51,370 --> 00:06:58,030
And as you can see, as I'm typing this, this is getting updated with what I'm typing, alphanumeric,

90
00:06:58,030 --> 00:07:05,440
alphanumeric, colon, alphanumeric, colon and alphanumeric, alphanumeric.

91
00:07:05,440 --> 00:07:11,170
And as you can see now, it's highlighting the Mac address for me, which means I can use this regex

92
00:07:11,170 --> 00:07:15,550
rule in my code regardless of what programming language you're using.

93
00:07:15,610 --> 00:07:18,850
You can use this rule in your programming language.

94
00:07:18,850 --> 00:07:26,230
And if you have a text like this, this programming language will be able to only match this part of

95
00:07:26,230 --> 00:07:30,670
this text, which is what's interesting to us, which is the Mac address.

96
00:07:32,230 --> 00:07:33,760
Now, like I said, rejects.

97
00:07:33,760 --> 00:07:39,320
And a lot of the things that I'm teaching you in this course apply to all programming languages.

98
00:07:39,340 --> 00:07:45,190
The difference is going to be the syntax, but the ideas and the concepts and the way you build programs

99
00:07:45,190 --> 00:07:46,090
is the same.

100
00:07:46,090 --> 00:07:51,550
So now once you know how to use regex, you're going to be able to use it with any programming language

101
00:07:51,550 --> 00:07:52,000
you want.

102
00:07:52,030 --> 00:07:57,970
It doesn't have to be used with Python, but since this is a Python course and the next lecture, I'm

103
00:07:57,970 --> 00:08:00,460
going to show you how to implement this in our code.

104
00:08:00,460 --> 00:08:07,270
We're going to be working on our variable here, which is the if config result, and you'll learn how

105
00:08:07,270 --> 00:08:13,390
to extract the MAC address from all of this text using the rule that we just created.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:01,400
Okay.

2
00:00:01,400 --> 00:00:08,090
Now that we know what regex is and we already built a rule that will allow us to match the MAC address

3
00:00:08,090 --> 00:00:10,190
from the result of if config.

4
00:00:10,220 --> 00:00:17,510
Let's see how we can implement this in python and filter our variable right now because our variable

5
00:00:17,540 --> 00:00:21,830
here if config result gives us the full result of if config.

6
00:00:22,130 --> 00:00:28,550

00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:56,260

00:02:56,950 --> 00:02:57,730
So what?

We're going to store this in.

00:03:07,080 --> 00:03:08,280
Search.

Result equals to.

00:03:16,600 --> 00:03:26,200

47
00:03:26,680 --> 00:03:27,520
zero.

48

Because usually if there is more than one match in the string, these will be placed into a number of

49
00:03:34,600 --> 00:03:38,860
groups and you can iterate through them by saying zero, one, two, three.

50
00:03:39,160 --> 00:03:45,280
Now we're interested in the first occurrence and usually they'll only be one anyway, so we only want

51

00:03:50,110 --> 00:03:50,560
Now.

Now when we execute our Python program, it is going to it's going to get the arguments, it's going

64
00:04:15,310 --> 00:04:21,550

Mark is equal to current mark.

Now we first got the error message which which is could not read Mac address which is returned by our

98
00:06:38,920 --> 00:06:41,770
function in here or printed by our function.

00:06:42,940 --> 00:06:49,000
But we have an error in here and this error is returned by Python, not by us.

100
00:06:49,160 --> 00:06:55,540
And like I said, it's a good idea to start learning how to debug these issues and know what's happening.

101
00:06:55,810 --> 00:06:59,050
So you can see that the error is happening at 937.

102
00:06:59,530 --> 00:07:05,370
And the problem is it's telling us that cannot concatenate str and non type.

103
00:07:05,380 --> 00:07:06,850
And I'll explain this to you.

104
00:07:07,390 --> 00:07:15,400
So we have line 37 here and what's happening is it's trying to print current Mac is equal to the current

105
00:07:15,400 --> 00:07:16,420
Mac in here.

106
00:07:16,570 --> 00:07:23,260
Now this current Mac variable is capturing the result that's returned by the get current Mac function,

107
00:07:23,260 --> 00:07:24,490
which is this one.

108
00:07:26,170 --> 00:07:33,370
Now we know that when our code is being executed, we are not reaching this line because we see this

109
00:07:33,370 --> 00:07:33,760
message.

110
00:07:33,760 --> 00:07:39,040
We see this message being printed which is saying could not read Mac address, which basically means

111
00:07:39,040 --> 00:07:44,230
that whatever code we have here is not able to read the Mac address for us.

112
00:07:44,500 --> 00:07:50,470
Therefore, when this function is finished in executing, it has nothing to return.

113
00:07:50,590 --> 00:07:55,750
So you can see that if we get a mac address, we're going to return the value of the Mac address.

114
00:07:56,080 --> 00:08:01,360
But if we don't have a value for the MAC address, we're only printing that we could not read the MAC

115
00:08:01,360 --> 00:08:04,120
address and the function is exiting.

116
00:08:04,300 --> 00:08:09,790
Now, when the function exits here, we're trying to capture a variable in here in Current Mac.

117
00:08:10,270 --> 00:08:16,480
Since the function has nothing to return, it's returning an object called Non, which basically means

118
00:08:16,480 --> 00:08:17,010
nothing.

119
00:08:17,020 --> 00:08:18,100
It's a non type.

120
00:08:19,100 --> 00:08:25,130
So when we are trying to print this, when we're trying to say current mark is equal to the value stored

121
00:08:25,130 --> 00:08:29,270
in current Mac, the value stored in current Mac is not text.

122
00:08:29,270 --> 00:08:34,190
So Python does not know how to print this because it's telling me that this is a non type.

123
00:08:34,190 --> 00:08:35,120
This is nothing.

124
00:08:35,120 --> 00:08:37,460
I can't just print nothing.

125
00:08:38,440 --> 00:08:44,650
So what we need to do, we need to tell Python whatever value you get in here, I want you to treat

126
00:08:44,650 --> 00:08:45,730
it as a string.

127
00:08:45,760 --> 00:08:51,940
Even if you get nothing, if you get an object, anything that you get, I want you to print it to me

128
00:08:51,940 --> 00:08:53,890
as string, as a text.

129
00:08:54,400 --> 00:08:55,960
Now, to do that, it's very easy.

130
00:08:55,960 --> 00:09:03,460
In Python, all we have to do is just type str before the variable name and put the variable between

131
00:09:03,460 --> 00:09:04,570
two brackets.

132
00:09:05,790 --> 00:09:12,810
Now this idea of printing something as something else or treating a variable as another type is called

133
00:09:12,810 --> 00:09:14,490
casting and programming.

134
00:09:14,970 --> 00:09:20,430
So what we're doing right now is we're telling Python that whatever value that the current mike has,

135
00:09:20,430 --> 00:09:22,500
I want you to treat it as a string.

136
00:09:22,890 --> 00:09:28,380
So if this function is successful and is able to get me the Mac address, we know the MAC address is

137
00:09:28,380 --> 00:09:32,010
already going to be a string anyway, so this will work fine.

138
00:09:32,580 --> 00:09:35,130
Otherwise we're not going to get anything.

139
00:09:35,130 --> 00:09:37,650
So the function is going to return none.

140
00:09:37,650 --> 00:09:41,160
But I'm telling you, treat this non object as a string.

141
00:09:41,700 --> 00:09:47,970
Now the result when we go around our code with low, you'll see that it's going to give me, first of

142
00:09:47,970 --> 00:09:51,290
all, the message that says could not read Mac address.

143
00:09:51,300 --> 00:09:56,280
And then when we try to print the Mac address is just going to say MAC address is equals to none, it's

144
00:09:56,280 --> 00:09:57,360
equal to nothing.

145
00:09:58,020 --> 00:10:05,580
And then when we run it with ETA zero, as you can see, we have the Mac address here which is stored

146
00:10:05,580 --> 00:10:08,160
in the current Mac address variable.

147
00:10:10,020 --> 00:10:11,690
So that's it for this lecture.

148
00:10:11,700 --> 00:10:16,440
I just wanted to refactor our code so we have a function to get the current mark.

149
00:10:16,470 --> 00:10:21,870
This is very handy for us so we can use it in the future any time we need to be able to get the Mac

150
00:10:21,870 --> 00:10:22,560
address.

151
00:10:22,890 --> 00:10:29,760
Also, it makes our code prettier and easier to read, and it also allows us to use the function multiple

152
00:10:29,760 --> 00:10:30,980
times within the code.

153
00:10:30,990 --> 00:10:35,280
So you can see we're already using the function in here just to print the current Mac.

154
00:10:35,880 --> 00:10:40,710
And then later on in the next two lectures, we're actually going to use we're going to get the current

155
00:10:40,710 --> 00:10:47,790
Mac again and we're going to check if the current Mac got changed to the Mac that the user requested.

156
00:10:48,120 --> 00:10:54,060
So if we didn't do this, then we would have had to use this code twice in the program, first to just

157
00:10:54,060 --> 00:10:58,920
print the current Mac and second to use it to validate if the maggot changed.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:01,590
Okay.

2
00:00:01,600 --> 00:00:08,950
Now that we refactored and cleaned up our code, we're ready to go ahead with the next two steps, which

3
00:00:08,950 --> 00:00:15,160
is, first of all, check if the current Mac is the Mac that the user requested and then print an appropriate

4
00:00:15,160 --> 00:00:15,850
message.

5
00:00:16,630 --> 00:00:22,300
Now we're going to combine these two steps in this lecture today because they are very easy and we've

6
00:00:22,300 --> 00:00:25,330
actually used everything that I'm going to do now in the past.

7
00:00:25,330 --> 00:00:27,730
So it should be easy for you to understand.

8
00:00:28,390 --> 00:00:32,950
Now, first of all, I'm just going to hide this block right here because we don't care how we get the

9
00:00:32,950 --> 00:00:38,050
current Mac, we just know that we have a function that gives us the current Mac.

10
00:00:38,740 --> 00:00:41,800
And right here we have the code for our main program.

11
00:00:42,190 --> 00:00:49,120
And we know now this current line right here is going to get me the arguments that the user enters.

12
00:00:49,690 --> 00:00:56,470
Then the next line right here is going to get me the current Mac using the Get Current Mac function

13
00:00:56,470 --> 00:00:58,780
that we wrote in the previous lecture.

14
00:00:58,930 --> 00:01:04,570
Then we're going to print the current Mac and then this is the step where the Mac address is going to

15
00:01:04,570 --> 00:01:05,050
change.

16
00:01:05,050 --> 00:01:10,750
So I'm actually going to uncomment it so it gets executed and this is all going to be done using the

17
00:01:10,750 --> 00:01:13,240
change Mac function that we wrote before.

18
00:01:13,240 --> 00:01:16,000
So this line right here will call this function.

19
00:01:16,000 --> 00:01:21,220
It will send the options that interface and options dot new Mac, which is the interface and the Mac

20
00:01:21,250 --> 00:01:23,560
that the user enter as arguments.

21
00:01:24,280 --> 00:01:25,570
So far so good.

22
00:01:25,600 --> 00:01:31,720
So after this step, the Mac address should change to the MAC address that the user requested if everything

23
00:01:31,720 --> 00:01:32,740
goes smoothly.

24
00:01:33,040 --> 00:01:40,150
So after this line, we need to check if the current Mac is actually the same as the Mac that the user

25
00:01:40,150 --> 00:01:40,900
requested.

26
00:01:41,530 --> 00:01:45,340
Now to do this, first we need to get the current Mac again.

27
00:01:45,580 --> 00:01:52,870
So we're just going to do current Mac equals get current Mac and we're going to give it options dot

28
00:01:52,870 --> 00:01:53,740
interface.

29
00:01:55,420 --> 00:01:59,840
So this line is identical to this line, and both lines do the same thing.

30
00:01:59,860 --> 00:02:01,330
They just get the current mark.

31
00:02:02,050 --> 00:02:08,170
Keep in mind, I'm using the same variable name in here, which means that whatever value that was stored

32
00:02:08,170 --> 00:02:11,050
in the variable before will actually get changed.

33
00:02:11,050 --> 00:02:13,570
Now, if the Mac address did get changed.

34
00:02:14,050 --> 00:02:20,470
Now that we have the value of the current mark stored in the in the current Mac variable, all we have

35
00:02:20,470 --> 00:02:28,780
to do is use an if statement and we're going to check if the current MAC is equal to the Mac that the

36
00:02:28,780 --> 00:02:34,330
user requested in the arguments which is stored in options dot new Mac.

37
00:02:36,220 --> 00:02:38,800
If they are equal, then we're going to print.

38
00:02:40,740 --> 00:02:43,770
Mac address was successfully changed to.

39
00:02:45,030 --> 00:02:46,920
And I forgot an el in here.

40
00:02:48,330 --> 00:02:52,860
Now in here you can either print the options dot new Mac or current Mac.

41
00:02:52,860 --> 00:02:56,340
Doesn't really matter because it got changed otherwise.

42
00:02:56,340 --> 00:02:59,160
So we're going to do ls print.

43
00:02:59,520 --> 00:03:03,090
Mac address did not get changed.

44
00:03:03,690 --> 00:03:10,470
So one thing that I want to note here see and here I actually used equals equals instead of a single

45
00:03:10,470 --> 00:03:14,040
equal in python when you're comparing two values.

46
00:03:14,040 --> 00:03:17,550
So we're comparing the current Mac and the options dot new Mac.

47
00:03:17,790 --> 00:03:24,420
If you want to check if two values are identical or equal to each other, then you have to use equal

48
00:03:24,420 --> 00:03:26,430
equal instead of a single equal.

49
00:03:26,580 --> 00:03:33,690
Because as you see before, when we use a single equal, python thinks that we want to assign a value

50
00:03:33,690 --> 00:03:34,780
to the variable.

51
00:03:34,800 --> 00:03:41,670
For example, when we wanted to capture the value of the arguments, we said options equal to get arguments.

52
00:03:41,670 --> 00:03:46,890
What we want to capture the current Mac, which is the value returned by the get current Mac function.

53
00:03:46,890 --> 00:03:50,310
We say current mac equals get current Mac.

54
00:03:50,670 --> 00:03:56,640
So if you don't want to change the value of the variable and you just want to check if the value and

55
00:03:56,640 --> 00:04:02,820
this variable is equal to the value in this variable, then you have to use equal equal instead.

56
00:04:03,600 --> 00:04:04,410
Now that's it.

57
00:04:04,410 --> 00:04:05,520
Our code is done.

58
00:04:05,520 --> 00:04:07,080
Let's go and test it.

59
00:04:07,980 --> 00:04:15,630
So I'm going to clear all of this and I'm going to try to change the Mac address of zero to something

60
00:04:15,630 --> 00:04:18,510
that ends up with two to hit enter.

61
00:04:18,750 --> 00:04:24,190
As you can see, first of all, it's given me the current Mac, which is this, then it's given telling

62
00:04:24,190 --> 00:04:24,840
me message.

63
00:04:24,840 --> 00:04:27,630
I'm changing the Mac address of zero to this.

64
00:04:27,990 --> 00:04:30,780
And then when it succeeds, it's going to tell us.

65
00:04:30,780 --> 00:04:35,190
Mac address was successfully changed to this value right here.

66
00:04:35,910 --> 00:04:38,580
Now the program works perfectly.

67
00:04:38,610 --> 00:04:42,120
If it faces errors, it's going to print error messages for us.

68
00:04:42,240 --> 00:04:47,220
If it works well, it's going to give us a message to validate that everything works fine.

69
00:04:47,220 --> 00:04:54,690
And it also looks good and it's very reusable because we split it into a number of functions so we can

70
00:04:54,690 --> 00:04:57,990
call these functions multiple times throughout the program.

71
00:04:59,260 --> 00:05:04,480
One thing that I want you to revise again, note the value here for the current Mac.

72
00:05:04,690 --> 00:05:09,370
The first time it was printed as the actual Mac.

73
00:05:09,970 --> 00:05:16,280
And then when we use the same variable but we assigned it to the function again to get current Mac.

74
00:05:16,300 --> 00:05:23,500
Now since the Mac got changed, the value in here was overwritten with whatever this function returns.

75
00:05:23,500 --> 00:05:31,240
So if you check it again, you'll see that it's printing to us the value of the Mac after it got changed.

76
00:05:31,510 --> 00:05:38,500
So this just shows that you can overwrite the value in variables as you go along throughout the program.

77
00:05:40,450 --> 00:05:40,810
Now.

78
00:05:40,810 --> 00:05:42,190
That's it for this program.

79
00:05:42,220 --> 00:05:43,670
I'm happy enough with it.

80
00:05:43,690 --> 00:05:48,140
We can still add more features to it, but I think that's good for now.

81
00:05:48,160 --> 00:05:50,380
We learned how to use functions we use.

82
00:05:50,410 --> 00:05:52,600
We learned how to use decision making.

83
00:05:52,600 --> 00:05:54,280
We learned about projects.

84
00:05:54,280 --> 00:06:00,520
We learned how to use modules, how to execute system commands, read system output, and much more

85
00:06:00,550 --> 00:06:02,730
throughout this very simple program.

86
00:06:02,740 --> 00:06:04,420
So we're done with this.

87
00:06:04,450 --> 00:06:10,570
Next, we'll move on to another program where we're going to learn more concepts and build more advanced

88
00:06:10,570 --> 00:06:11,170
stuff.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,300 --> 00:00:06,660
Right now, we're pretty much done with this program and ready to move to the next section and start

2
00:00:06,660 --> 00:00:08,330
building new programs.

3
00:00:08,340 --> 00:00:15,000
But before doing that, at the end of each section, we're going to edit the program that we built so

4
00:00:15,000 --> 00:00:17,340
that it works with Python three.

5
00:00:18,060 --> 00:00:24,600
Now, we didn't build these programs for Python three from scratch because of a number of reasons that

6
00:00:24,600 --> 00:00:25,710
I listed earlier.

7
00:00:25,710 --> 00:00:31,950
So please go back and revise the Python two versus Python three lecture if you want to see these reasons.

8
00:00:32,070 --> 00:00:38,010
Therefore, at the end of each section, we're either going to have an extra lecture or a challenge

9
00:00:38,010 --> 00:00:46,560
to convert the program so that it works on Python three and you will see how easy it is to convert Python

10
00:00:46,560 --> 00:00:51,810
two programs to Python three programs, and how similar the end result is.

11
00:00:51,840 --> 00:00:56,130
At the end of the day, we're still programming in the same programming language.

12
00:00:56,130 --> 00:00:58,530
There are only slight differences.

13
00:00:58,920 --> 00:01:05,670
So what I usually do, if I want to convert a program, I would just run the program straight away with

14
00:01:05,670 --> 00:01:06,630
Python three.

15
00:01:06,930 --> 00:01:10,830
So we know that we can run our program with Python two like so.

16
00:01:10,830 --> 00:01:15,420
So we do Python program name and we're giving it the arguments in here.

17
00:01:15,420 --> 00:01:21,990
And if I hit enter then we just remove this be at the end, you'll see that my MC address will get changed

18
00:01:21,990 --> 00:01:22,890
successfully.

19
00:01:23,460 --> 00:01:29,430
So I'm not going to change anything in my program and I'm simply just going to run it with Python three.

20
00:01:29,430 --> 00:01:35,100
So I'm just going to type Python three followed by the exact same command and we're running the exact

21
00:01:35,100 --> 00:01:36,030
same program.

22
00:01:36,540 --> 00:01:39,360
I'm going to hit Enter and I get an error.

23
00:01:39,360 --> 00:01:45,030
Now, in many cases, the program might actually just work with Python three with no issues at all,

24
00:01:45,030 --> 00:01:45,720
and that's it.

25
00:01:45,720 --> 00:01:51,870
You can move on, or in other cases you will get an error similar to this one right here.

26
00:01:51,870 --> 00:01:58,230
And these errors are very, very useful because they give you the exact line where things are going

27
00:01:58,230 --> 00:01:58,800
wrong.

28
00:01:59,100 --> 00:02:05,520
And like I said, it's actually really important to learn how to read these errors and fix them, because

29
00:02:05,520 --> 00:02:10,290
in the future you're going to be building your own programs, not programs that I walk you through and

30
00:02:10,290 --> 00:02:11,310
you will get errors.

31
00:02:11,310 --> 00:02:16,560
So you need to understand how to read them and how to at least know where it's going wrong.

32
00:02:16,560 --> 00:02:20,820
So you can go on Google and research or discover what's going wrong.

33
00:02:21,270 --> 00:02:28,740
So if we look at the error message, we can see the main error is right here and that is Python is saying

34
00:02:28,740 --> 00:02:36,750
we have a typo error and the error itself is Python is saying we cannot use a string pattern on a byte

35
00:02:36,750 --> 00:02:37,830
like object.

36
00:02:38,400 --> 00:02:43,590
Now let's trace the error back a little bit more and we can see that the error is happening on line

37
00:02:43,590 --> 00:02:51,750
26 on the line where we are using the regex to extract the MAC address from the if config result.

38
00:02:52,170 --> 00:02:57,510
And looking at the error again, it's telling us that we cannot use a string pattern.

39
00:02:57,510 --> 00:02:59,100
This is a string pattern.

40
00:02:59,100 --> 00:03:03,810
So it's telling us that we cannot use this on a byte like object.

41
00:03:03,810 --> 00:03:06,990
So it must be referring to the if config result.

42
00:03:07,560 --> 00:03:14,040
Now this is one of the main differences between Python two and three and Python three.

43
00:03:14,040 --> 00:03:19,170
There is very clear distinction between strings and bytes.

44
00:03:19,470 --> 00:03:24,450
You can think of byte objects as machine readable strings.

45
00:03:24,450 --> 00:03:29,100
So a normal string, the strings that we know are similar to this one.

46
00:03:29,100 --> 00:03:30,390
This is a normal string.

47
00:03:30,390 --> 00:03:33,630
So it's a sequence of characters.

48
00:03:33,870 --> 00:03:37,320
A byte object is a sequence of bytes.

49
00:03:37,320 --> 00:03:45,270
So it is a string, but it's a machine readable string and the normal string is a human readable string.

50
00:03:45,990 --> 00:03:52,410
So the main issue that we have right now is in line 26 and it's in this line.

51
00:03:52,410 --> 00:03:58,410
And what Python is saying is it's saying you cannot use a string, which we said is a human readable

52
00:03:58,410 --> 00:04:03,780
string on a byte object, which is a machine readable string.

53
00:04:04,290 --> 00:04:13,110
So to fix this, we'll either have to convert this string right here to a bite object or we have to

54
00:04:13,110 --> 00:04:19,080
convert this byte object, which is a machine readable string to a human readable string.

55
00:04:19,680 --> 00:04:27,600
Now luckily we have already covered how to convert an object to a string, and we've seen that before.

56
00:04:27,600 --> 00:04:34,470
When we used it in here, when we were trying to use the current Mac, we casted it to a string by simply

57
00:04:34,620 --> 00:04:38,310
typing str and feeding the string to it.

58
00:04:38,550 --> 00:04:40,770
So we're going to do the exact same thing in here.

59
00:04:40,770 --> 00:04:47,970
We're just going to type STR and we're going to give it the if config result, which is the byte object

60
00:04:47,970 --> 00:04:50,220
that Python is complaining about.

61
00:04:50,610 --> 00:04:56,670
So if we read the error again now it's telling us that we cannot use a string pattern on a byte like

62
00:04:56,670 --> 00:04:59,280
object and that is on line 26.

63
00:04:59,280 --> 00:04:59,520
We.

64
00:04:59,810 --> 00:05:03,350
This is a string, so this must be the right object.

65
00:05:03,350 --> 00:05:05,670
And to convert an object to a string.

66
00:05:05,690 --> 00:05:10,060
All we have to do is type SDR and give it the object.

67
00:05:10,070 --> 00:05:11,450
That's all we're doing.

68
00:05:12,230 --> 00:05:18,230
So let's go ahead now and that's clear the screen and run it with Python three again.

69
00:05:18,230 --> 00:05:24,840
And let's just change the IP now and put three three at the end and we're going to hit enter and perfect.

70
00:05:24,860 --> 00:05:30,260
It works like a charm and if we do if config at zero to double check.

71
00:05:30,290 --> 00:05:35,630
As you can see, the MAC address did actually change to the one that we want.

72
00:05:36,710 --> 00:05:43,850
So this is always the methodology that I follow when I want to convert a Python to program to Python

73
00:05:43,850 --> 00:05:44,330
three.

74
00:05:44,540 --> 00:05:50,120
I run the Python two program with the Python three command just to see what errors I get.

75
00:05:50,150 --> 00:05:54,770
I go over the errors one by one and fix them based on the error result.

76
00:05:54,770 --> 00:06:01,820
And in many cases, fixing these errors is as simple as converting an object to a string or converting

77
00:06:01,820 --> 00:06:03,950
a string to a bite object.

78
00:06:03,950 --> 00:06:06,890
And we're going to see more of these as we go through the course.

79
00:06:06,920 --> 00:06:10,790
Like I said, we will be tackling this issue at the end of each section.

80
00:06:10,790 --> 00:06:16,340
We'll be converting our program to a Python three program, either through a lecture or through a challenge.

81
00:06:16,340 --> 00:06:21,680
And you'll see how easy it is and you'll be able to understand it really, really well.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 4: Programming a Network Scanner

1
00:00:00,830 --> 00:00:07,520
Information gathering is one of the most important steps when it comes to hacking or penetration testing.

2
00:00:07,820 --> 00:00:13,820
If you think of it, you can't really gain access to a system if you don't have enough information about

3
00:00:13,820 --> 00:00:14,150
it.

4
00:00:14,600 --> 00:00:20,240
So, for example, let's say you're connected to a network and one of the devices connected to this

5
00:00:20,240 --> 00:00:22,190
network is your target.

6
00:00:22,460 --> 00:00:28,430
Now, for you to hack into that target, first you need to discover all of the connected clients to

7
00:00:28,430 --> 00:00:34,400
this network, get their MAC address, their IP address, and then from there, try to maybe gather

8
00:00:34,400 --> 00:00:39,680
more information or run some attacks in order to gain access to your target.

9
00:00:40,460 --> 00:00:44,150
Now, there are a number of programs that will do this for you.

10
00:00:44,180 --> 00:00:49,130
Examples are net discover and DN map which do this job really, really well.

11
00:00:49,130 --> 00:00:53,720
And I cover both of these programs in my general ethical hacking course.

12
00:00:54,140 --> 00:00:58,250
But in this course, we're not going to be using any prebuilt tools.

13
00:00:58,250 --> 00:01:02,030
We're actually going to build our own network scanner.

14
00:01:02,690 --> 00:01:08,810
Now, there is actually an end map class that you can import in Python, and it'll allow you to do all

15
00:01:08,810 --> 00:01:10,760
of the things that you can do with end map.

16
00:01:10,760 --> 00:01:16,400
So you only need to know how a map works and you'll be able to use that and that's no fun.

17
00:01:16,400 --> 00:01:18,170
So I'm actually not going to use that.

18
00:01:18,290 --> 00:01:25,700
Instead, I want to show you how to properly implement a network scanner so that you have a deeper understanding

19
00:01:25,700 --> 00:01:31,820
on how network scanners work and have a deeper understanding on how networks work in general.

20
00:01:33,190 --> 00:01:38,260
This kind of that I'm going to implement is going to be very similar to Net Discover, which is a scanner

21
00:01:38,260 --> 00:01:40,570
that comes with Linux.

22
00:01:40,570 --> 00:01:47,110
And our scanner is going to show you all the connected devices connected to the same network, plus

23
00:01:47,110 --> 00:01:50,080
their IP addresses and their MAC address.

24
00:01:51,020 --> 00:01:54,100
Now we're going to start programming in the next lecture.

25
00:01:54,110 --> 00:02:00,770
But before we get into that, I just want to show you how this cover works so that you have a general

26
00:02:00,770 --> 00:02:04,820
idea of what I want to achieve by the end of the section.

27
00:02:04,940 --> 00:02:12,620
So I have my terminal in here and if I do a config, you'll see I have it had zero.

28
00:02:12,650 --> 00:02:14,570
It has an IP address.

29
00:02:14,570 --> 00:02:22,460
And like I said, this is the virtual interface created by VirtualBox when we set the machine to use

30
00:02:22,460 --> 00:02:23,540
an ad network.

31
00:02:24,360 --> 00:02:24,750
Now.

32
00:02:24,750 --> 00:02:30,200
I also said that this not network behaves exactly like an Internet network.

33
00:02:30,210 --> 00:02:37,320
And as far as the killing machine is concerned, it thinks that it is connected to a real wired network.

34
00:02:37,320 --> 00:02:40,770
And as you can see in here, it's telling me that wired connected.

35
00:02:41,780 --> 00:02:46,850
Now I've also installed windows as a virtual machine, and I'll show you how to do that in the next

36
00:02:46,850 --> 00:02:47,510
lecture.

37
00:02:47,510 --> 00:02:51,400
And this Windows machine is connected to the same network.

38
00:02:51,410 --> 00:02:57,560
So as far as these two computers are concerned, they think that they are connected to the same network.

39
00:02:58,170 --> 00:03:04,380
So what I want to do right now is use net, discover and see how we can use it to discover all devices

40
00:03:04,380 --> 00:03:06,420
connected to the same network.

41
00:03:07,370 --> 00:03:10,970
Now the method that I'm going to show you will work exactly the same.

42
00:03:10,970 --> 00:03:16,220
Whether you're using it against a virtual network like I'm doing right now or against real network,

43
00:03:16,220 --> 00:03:19,820
and even if your target is a wi fi or a wireless network.

44
00:03:20,690 --> 00:03:26,510
So all you have to do is type the name of the program, which is net discover and then type dash or

45
00:03:26,510 --> 00:03:30,470
to specify an IP range to search for.

46
00:03:31,160 --> 00:03:34,680
This needs to be arranged that can be accessed by you.

47
00:03:34,700 --> 00:03:42,470
So right now you can see that my IP is ten 0 to 16 and I can only access IPS on the same subnet.

48
00:03:42,980 --> 00:03:54,080
So IP is on the same subnet, start at ten 020 and they would end at ten 02254 because 254 is the last

49
00:03:54,080 --> 00:03:56,420
IP that a client can have.

50
00:03:57,370 --> 00:04:02,260
So my range is going to be ten 0 to 1.

51
00:04:02,350 --> 00:04:10,900
And I want to search for clients that might have an IP of ten 0 to 110 zero 2 to 10 023 all the way

52
00:04:10,900 --> 00:04:14,020
up to ten 02254.

53
00:04:14,680 --> 00:04:23,020
So instead of manually typing all of these IPS, I can just type over 24 and net discover will automatically

54
00:04:23,020 --> 00:04:31,570
know that I'm trying to search for all of the IPS that start at ten 0 to 1 and end at ten 0 to 2 five

55
00:04:31,570 --> 00:04:32,170
four.

56
00:04:32,380 --> 00:04:37,840
So this is a way of specifying an IP range for the whole subnet.

57
00:04:38,380 --> 00:04:39,880
So if I hit enter now.

58
00:04:41,280 --> 00:04:47,940
You'll see that Net Discover will show me all the IPS of the devices connected to the same network.

59
00:04:48,000 --> 00:04:54,120
And note that the first three parts of the IPS are always the same because they are on the same subnet.

60
00:04:54,750 --> 00:05:01,710
And I also have the MAC addresses of these clients and Net Discover is also attempting to guess the

61
00:05:01,710 --> 00:05:02,910
device vendor.

62
00:05:03,630 --> 00:05:10,680
Now if I press Q, this will quit the program and right now we have a list of all the connected clients

63
00:05:10,680 --> 00:05:12,150
to the same network.

64
00:05:13,030 --> 00:05:20,110
Now, like I said, you can also use this method to discover clients connected to the same wi fi network.

65
00:05:20,200 --> 00:05:28,690
The only thing is right now, if I do if config, you can see that my machine does not have a wireless

66
00:05:28,690 --> 00:05:29,320
adapter.

67
00:05:29,320 --> 00:05:32,080
It's not connected to a wi fi network.

68
00:05:33,030 --> 00:05:39,480
And like I said before, you cannot access the built in wireless card from a virtual machine.

69
00:05:40,220 --> 00:05:45,830
Therefore, if you want to do this or run any of the wireless attacks that we're going to see in the

70
00:05:45,830 --> 00:05:52,760
future against a real computer and a real wireless network, you're going to need to use a wireless

71
00:05:52,760 --> 00:05:53,630
adapter.

72
00:05:54,700 --> 00:06:00,040
Now I'm going to include links in the description that will help you pick a good adapter that works

73
00:06:00,040 --> 00:06:01,240
with Linux.

74
00:06:01,450 --> 00:06:07,330
But right now I actually have one and I'm just going to connect it and use it just to prove to you if

75
00:06:07,330 --> 00:06:14,290
things work on the virtual machines connected to the virtual network, they will work exactly the same

76
00:06:14,290 --> 00:06:17,080
against a real network with real machines.

77
00:06:17,290 --> 00:06:21,580
You actually do not need to buy a wireless adapter for this course.

78
00:06:21,580 --> 00:06:27,700
You can do everything in the virtual environment and I'm going to be doing pretty much everything in

79
00:06:27,700 --> 00:06:30,130
the virtual environment as well myself.

80
00:06:30,280 --> 00:06:38,050
So you do not need a wireless adapter, but if you want to run the network attacks against real computers

81
00:06:38,050 --> 00:06:42,040
connected to real networks, then you will need a wireless adapter.

82
00:06:42,940 --> 00:06:48,910
So I'm going to connect my adapter now and if I do, if config.

83
00:06:49,840 --> 00:06:51,190
It's still not showing up.

84
00:06:51,190 --> 00:07:00,940
So I'm going to connect it from my device's USB and click on the adapter name and let's see if it shows

85
00:07:00,940 --> 00:07:01,780
up now.

86
00:07:02,520 --> 00:07:03,210
Perfect.

87
00:07:03,210 --> 00:07:06,900
As you can see, I have an adapter now called Line Zero.

88
00:07:07,710 --> 00:07:15,090
And what I'm going to do is I need to connect this adapter to a wi fi network first before I can discover

89
00:07:15,090 --> 00:07:17,820
all the connected clients to this network.

90
00:07:18,630 --> 00:07:25,080
So I'm going to go to my network manager, I'm going to click in here and you want to click on Select

91
00:07:25,080 --> 00:07:26,010
Network.

92
00:07:26,430 --> 00:07:32,550
And as you can see automatically now, it actually connected to a network, but in your case, you want

93
00:07:32,550 --> 00:07:37,110
to select a network and click on Connect and then it will ask you for the password.

94
00:07:37,650 --> 00:07:46,620
So now I'm actually connected and you'll see if I do if config again right now lan zero has an IP address.

95
00:07:47,690 --> 00:07:54,020
So this means that it is connected to a network and this means that we can use it now with net discover.

96
00:07:54,890 --> 00:08:00,320
So again, I'm going to use the exact same command that I used before just to show you and prove to

97
00:08:00,320 --> 00:08:04,850
you that if this works against virtual machines, it will work against real machines.

98
00:08:05,660 --> 00:08:08,430
And the only difference is going to be the IP.

99
00:08:08,450 --> 00:08:10,420
So I'm going to remove this IP.

100
00:08:10,430 --> 00:08:15,980
And as you can see right now, my IP is 19216818.

101
00:08:16,620 --> 00:08:26,070
So therefore the range that I'm going to look for is going to start at 19216811, and I'm going to leave

102
00:08:26,070 --> 00:08:35,220
the over 24 here because this will tell net discover that I want to start at 19216811 and finish at

103
00:08:35,220 --> 00:08:37,980
192168254.

104
00:08:38,820 --> 00:08:40,830
So if I hit enter now.

105
00:08:42,670 --> 00:08:45,190
Now, this did not work, and I know why.

106
00:08:45,310 --> 00:08:50,080
In order for this to work, you actually have to disable the network.

107
00:08:50,800 --> 00:08:54,790
So to disable the net network, we're going to go on devices.

108
00:08:54,790 --> 00:09:00,130
We're going to go on network, and we're going to uncheck the Kinect network adapter.

109
00:09:01,320 --> 00:09:06,540
So now, once done with this, if we just run the exact same command again.

110
00:09:08,400 --> 00:09:15,090
As you can see, it's discovering all the connected clients, all their IP addresses, all their MAC

111
00:09:15,090 --> 00:09:18,120
addresses, and it's guessing the manufacturer.

112
00:09:18,120 --> 00:09:21,820
And you can see it's also discovering some Apple devices here.

113
00:09:21,870 --> 00:09:27,030
So as you can see, it's working perfectly using the exact same command.

114
00:09:27,850 --> 00:09:34,510
Now, I only did this just to show you that if things work against virtual machines and against virtual

115
00:09:34,510 --> 00:09:40,960
networks, then they will work against real machines, because these virtual machines and virtual networks

116
00:09:40,960 --> 00:09:43,410
are modeled over real machines.

117
00:09:43,420 --> 00:09:49,720
And as far as the machines are concerned, they actually think they are real computers and real machines.

118
00:09:50,560 --> 00:09:55,990
So like I said, you do not need a wireless adapter for anything in the course.

119
00:09:55,990 --> 00:10:02,890
But if you want to run some of the attacks against real computers and real machines, then you will

120
00:10:02,890 --> 00:10:03,580
need one.

121
00:10:03,580 --> 00:10:09,730
And I'm going to include links in the resources to help you pick a good adapter that works well with

122
00:10:10,030 --> 00:10:10,690
Linux.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,200 --> 00:00:04,160
Now to practice some of the attacks that we're going to learn in the future.

2
00:00:04,160 --> 00:00:08,720
Lectures you need to have a target machine or a victim machine.

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:15,020
So this is a computer that you're going to target and try to hack and test the attacks that we're going

4
00:00:15,020 --> 00:00:16,460
to learn against.

5
