00:00:17,100 --> 00:00:19,470
So you can do this against any machine.

6
00:00:19,470 --> 00:00:25,380
But I highly recommend installing target machines as virtual machines.

7
00:00:25,590 --> 00:00:30,660
This is great because it won't break a real computer for you if you actually break something.

8
00:00:30,690 --> 00:00:35,240
It's also very easy to fix even if you have a computer that you don't care about.

9
00:00:35,250 --> 00:00:37,440
It's very easy to fix virtual machines.

10
00:00:37,440 --> 00:00:42,060
You can just remove it and reinstall it or store a snapshot and go back to it.

11
00:00:43,270 --> 00:00:47,140
Now, as we all know, Windows is a very common operating system.

12
00:00:47,140 --> 00:00:49,490
It's used by a lot of people.

13
00:00:49,510 --> 00:00:56,260
So in this video, I'm going to show you how to install Windows as a virtual machine on all operating

14
00:00:56,260 --> 00:01:01,090
systems so that we can test the attacks that we're going to learn against it.

15
00:01:01,120 --> 00:01:07,900
If you have an Apple computer that uses an MX chip, then skip this lecture and watch the next lecture.

16
00:01:07,900 --> 00:01:12,710
That one will show you how to install windows on an Apple computer.

17
00:01:12,730 --> 00:01:18,760
For Windows Linux and Apple computers that use the Intel chip, you can continue watching this lecture

18
00:01:18,760 --> 00:01:21,130
to learn how to install windows on it.

19
00:01:21,370 --> 00:01:29,050
Now, Microsoft has released 3D virtual images for people to test windows so we can actually download

20
00:01:29,050 --> 00:01:33,160
a virtual image of Windows for free from Microsoft.

21
00:01:33,640 --> 00:01:37,630
I'm going to include the download links in the resources of this lecture.

22
00:01:37,660 --> 00:01:42,830
Now I've already done that and I already have it in here in my downloads.

23
00:01:42,830 --> 00:01:44,660
As you can see, we have it here.

24
00:01:44,780 --> 00:01:47,360
Ms. Edge Windows 10, VMware.

25
00:01:47,360 --> 00:01:51,170
And as you can see from the icon, this is a zip archive.

26
00:01:51,200 --> 00:01:57,080
Therefore, in order to use the contents inside it, we have to uncompressed this file.

27
00:01:57,260 --> 00:02:01,520
So to do that on Windows simply we're going to have to double click it.

28
00:02:02,470 --> 00:02:04,090
Copy the content.

29
00:02:04,180 --> 00:02:07,540
Go back one directory and paste it here.

30
00:02:07,690 --> 00:02:11,050
We're going to give it some time to uncompressed the content.

31
00:02:11,080 --> 00:02:13,060
On Mac you can do the same.

32
00:02:13,060 --> 00:02:14,140
Simply double clicking.

33
00:02:14,140 --> 00:02:20,500
It will uncompressed the content and on Linux you'll have to right click and click on extract here.

34
00:02:21,040 --> 00:02:27,130
Now I'm doing these steps from a Windows host computer, but you should be able to follow them on Apple,

35
00:02:27,130 --> 00:02:34,120
Mac OS and Linux because we are essentially using the same virtualization software which is VMware.

36
00:02:35,090 --> 00:02:40,820
Once this is done, we're going to have another directory in here with the contents of the virtual Windows

37
00:02:40,820 --> 00:02:41,550
image.

38
00:02:41,570 --> 00:02:45,980
To import this to VMware, we're going to, first of all, run VMware.

39
00:02:47,290 --> 00:02:53,230
We're going to click on Open Virtual Machine or go to player file open.

40
00:02:54,130 --> 00:02:58,660
We're going to navigate to the location where we have this file in here.

41
00:02:58,660 --> 00:03:07,630
So it's going to be in my downloads in edge Windows 10 VMware, and we're going to select the only file

42
00:03:07,630 --> 00:03:14,890
that we can actually select from the list we're going to click on Open and we can set the name of the

43
00:03:14,890 --> 00:03:15,880
machine in here.

44
00:03:15,880 --> 00:03:18,760
So for example, I can set it to Windows 10.

45
00:03:18,910 --> 00:03:23,590
You can change the path where it's going to be stored, but we're going to keep that the same and we're

46
00:03:23,590 --> 00:03:26,440
going to click on import to import it.

47
00:03:27,020 --> 00:03:31,610
You're going to have to give it a bit of time to import this machine into VMware.

48
00:03:33,720 --> 00:03:34,500
And perfect.

49
00:03:34,500 --> 00:03:36,990
As you can see now, it's imported in here.

50
00:03:36,990 --> 00:03:41,910
And as usual, we can simply start it by clicking on the green play button.

51
00:03:41,910 --> 00:03:46,800
But before doing that, I actually want to modify the amount of memory it has.

52
00:03:46,800 --> 00:03:50,100
So we're going to right click and go to settings.

53
00:03:50,340 --> 00:03:55,230
And as you can see by default, it's set to have four gigabytes of memory.

54
00:03:55,230 --> 00:03:57,060
I think that's a little bit too much.

55
00:03:57,060 --> 00:03:58,500
It doesn't need that much.

56
00:03:58,500 --> 00:04:03,240
So we're going to set that to two by dragging the slider down to two.

57
00:04:03,420 --> 00:04:09,330
We're going to click on okay and start it by clicking on the green play button.

58
00:04:10,580 --> 00:04:11,420
And perfect.

59
00:04:11,420 --> 00:04:13,790
As you can see now, the machine started.

60
00:04:13,820 --> 00:04:18,260
We're going to click inside and click again to log in.

61
00:04:18,740 --> 00:04:23,630
The default password in here is Capital P, a, s.

62
00:04:23,630 --> 00:04:27,470
SW0 or de escalation.

63
00:04:27,470 --> 00:04:28,070
Mark.

64
00:04:28,190 --> 00:04:29,840
I'm going to hit enter.

65
00:04:31,260 --> 00:04:32,340
And here we go.

66
00:04:32,340 --> 00:04:40,500
We have a fully working virtual Windows 10 computer installed inside our main machine.

67
00:04:40,500 --> 00:04:48,750
And we did that so we can test attacks against this virtual machine without affecting our host computer.

68
00:04:48,900 --> 00:04:53,970
Now we can go in full screen by clicking here and going into full screen.

69
00:04:54,360 --> 00:05:02,310
The screen should automatically resize if it doesn't, right click display settings and you can set

70
00:05:02,310 --> 00:05:04,900
it to the right resolution for your screen.

71
00:05:04,920 --> 00:05:11,790
I'm going to set it to three 840 by two 160 because I have a 4K screen.

72
00:05:11,820 --> 00:05:17,330
You can see that the icons are actually really small because I have such a high resolution screen.

73
00:05:17,340 --> 00:05:21,960
In your case, you'll only face this issue if you have a 4K screen.

74
00:05:21,960 --> 00:05:26,670
So you'll also probably need to set the scaling in here to 200.

75
00:05:27,030 --> 00:05:32,020
And as you can see now, the icon size has changed to the normal size.

76
00:05:32,350 --> 00:05:36,310
Now, if you face any other issues, check out the featured questions.

77
00:05:36,310 --> 00:05:42,280
And if you don't find an answer, feel free to ask us and we will respond to you within a maximum of

78
00:05:42,280 --> 00:05:43,390
15 hours.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:05,840
As mentioned in the previous lecture, installing windows on Apple computers that use the arm chips,

2
00:00:05,840 --> 00:00:07,610
the M1, M2 and so on.

3
00:00:07,610 --> 00:00:11,540
The M series is different, so the method shown in the previous lecture will not work.

4
00:00:11,540 --> 00:00:17,450
And that's why we have this lecture to show you how to install Windows 11 on Apple computers that use

5
00:00:17,450 --> 00:00:20,450
the arm chips, the M1, M2 and so on.

6
00:00:20,450 --> 00:00:25,100
If you already got windows installed following the instructions in the previous lecture, then you can

7
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:26,030
skip this lecture.

8
00:00:26,030 --> 00:00:30,980
You should only watch this lecture if you have not installed windows yet as a virtual machine because

9
00:00:30,980 --> 00:00:36,920
you have an Apple computer that uses an arm chip such as the M1, M2 and so on.

10
00:00:37,280 --> 00:00:42,740
So first of all, you're going to need to download the Windows 11 for ARM processors.

11
00:00:42,740 --> 00:00:45,470
You can download it from the resources of this lecture.

12
00:00:45,470 --> 00:00:48,080
As you can see, I already have it downloaded in here.

13
00:00:48,080 --> 00:00:51,650
It's in a seven zip format, so it's compressed.

14
00:00:51,650 --> 00:00:54,980
And in order to decompress it, we're going to double click it.

15
00:00:56,150 --> 00:00:58,370
And give it some time to decompress.

16
00:00:59,240 --> 00:01:00,020
And perfect.

17
00:01:00,050 --> 00:01:04,100
Now that the decompression is complete, we have a new file in here.

18
00:01:04,100 --> 00:01:11,780
And as you can see, the extension is VMware VM, which is basically a ready virtual machine for VMware.

19
00:01:11,810 --> 00:01:16,670
Now we've already covered how to install VMware previously, so if you don't know or if you haven't

20
00:01:16,670 --> 00:01:18,980
done it already, just go back and revise it.

21
00:01:18,980 --> 00:01:24,920
So all I'm going to do is simply double click the file to open it in VMware, click on.

22
00:01:24,920 --> 00:01:30,710
I copied it because I created this virtual machine for you and now the virtual machine is going to start.

23
00:01:31,840 --> 00:01:32,620
And perfect.

24
00:01:32,620 --> 00:01:39,040
As you can see, we are logged in into our Windows 11 computer, which is running as a virtual machine

25
00:01:39,040 --> 00:01:43,260
inside VMware player inside my Apple silicon computer.

26
00:01:43,270 --> 00:01:49,390
So if I go ahead here on the right and click on the Virtual Machine library, as you can see, I have

27
00:01:49,420 --> 00:01:56,080
Kali installed in here and now I have windows which I can run by simply clicking on Start, but right

28
00:01:56,080 --> 00:01:58,660
now it's already running, so I can't stop it.

29
00:01:59,520 --> 00:02:00,960
So I'm going to close this.

30
00:02:00,960 --> 00:02:07,350
And as you can see in here, it's saying it is not connected to the Internet because we need to install

31
00:02:07,350 --> 00:02:14,070
the VMware tools on it, which will install the drivers for the virtual interfaces so that we can access

32
00:02:14,070 --> 00:02:15,000
virtual networks.

33
00:02:15,000 --> 00:02:20,130
And it will also install the display drivers and enable a lot of usability features.

34
00:02:20,130 --> 00:02:25,410
To do that, we're going to go to the virtual machine here and we're going to click reinstall VMware

35
00:02:25,440 --> 00:02:26,160
Tools.

36
00:02:26,490 --> 00:02:28,170
We're going to click install.

37
00:02:29,070 --> 00:02:33,210
And this will basically mount a DVD drive on this computer.

38
00:02:33,210 --> 00:02:39,300
And as you can see, we have a notification in here telling us that a new DVD drive is detected.

39
00:02:39,300 --> 00:02:41,730
And it's asking me, what do I want to do?

40
00:02:42,090 --> 00:02:46,090
I'm going to click it and I'm going to say, open the folder for me, please.

41
00:02:46,110 --> 00:02:52,340
I'm going to scroll down and we're going to use the setup script in here to install the tools.

42
00:02:52,350 --> 00:02:56,610
So I'm going to right click it and choose to run it with PowerShell.

43
00:02:57,460 --> 00:03:01,750
It's going to ask you if you want to allow this application to make changes.

44
00:03:01,750 --> 00:03:07,780
We're going to say yes and we're going to give this some time in order to install VMware Tools on this

45
00:03:07,780 --> 00:03:08,830
virtual machine.

46
00:03:10,000 --> 00:03:15,010
Once the PowerShell blue screen disappears, it means that everything got installed properly.

47
00:03:15,010 --> 00:03:20,320
And if you look here at the bottom, you'll see that we have network connection through Ethernet and

48
00:03:20,320 --> 00:03:26,530
that's because this machine is configured to use the virtual network that VMware starts and I have my

49
00:03:26,530 --> 00:03:29,380
virtual machines configured to get internet access.

50
00:03:29,380 --> 00:03:34,900
And like we said before, this way, these machines think that the host machine is the router and they

51
00:03:34,900 --> 00:03:40,210
connect to the virtual network through it and get internet access through it.

52
00:03:40,210 --> 00:03:43,690
And to confirm this, we can simply open up a web browser.

53
00:03:45,620 --> 00:03:48,560
Get started and just go to Google.com.

54
00:03:50,340 --> 00:03:51,210
And perfect.

55
00:03:51,210 --> 00:03:55,230
As you can see now we have Internet access on this virtual machine.

56
00:03:56,360 --> 00:04:00,640
And we can also go into full screen by clicking this green button in here.

57
00:04:00,650 --> 00:04:06,530
The screen will adjust, but sometimes, as you can see, is not going to fully go into the right resolution.

58
00:04:06,530 --> 00:04:13,700
So to fix this, you can simply right click anywhere on the desktop, go to the display settings and

59
00:04:13,700 --> 00:04:15,590
select the resolution that you want.

60
00:04:15,590 --> 00:04:19,010
So in my case, it's the one 920 by 1080.

61
00:04:20,300 --> 00:04:21,050
And perfect.

62
00:04:21,050 --> 00:04:24,230
As you can see right now, we got the right resolution.

63
00:04:24,260 --> 00:04:29,750
If you want to exit the full screen, you can simply just take the mouse to the top and click the green

64
00:04:29,750 --> 00:04:30,380
button.

65
00:04:31,600 --> 00:04:32,860
And we are out of it.

66
00:04:32,860 --> 00:04:39,010
And as you can see right now, we have this Windows machine running as a virtual machine through VMware,

67
00:04:39,010 --> 00:04:45,370
inside my Apple silicon computer alongside my Kali machine, which we can access in here in the virtual

68
00:04:45,370 --> 00:04:46,420
machine library.

69
00:04:46,540 --> 00:04:53,140
So now we can try to hack into this Windows machine and test attacks against it using the Kali machine.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,670 --> 00:00:05,140
Now there are a number of ways to discover clients on the same network.

2
00:00:05,530 --> 00:00:12,970
The easiest is to replicate what a normal device would do to discover another device on the same network.

3
00:00:13,510 --> 00:00:16,690
So, for example, let's say we have a network here.

4
00:00:17,230 --> 00:00:19,480
We have devices A, B, C and D.

5
00:00:19,510 --> 00:00:22,060
They are all connected to the same network.

6
00:00:22,360 --> 00:00:25,030
And we have the router here for this network.

7
00:00:25,360 --> 00:00:29,020
We can see that each device has an IP and a mac address.

8
00:00:29,290 --> 00:00:34,990
And let's assume that device, A needs to communicate with device C.

9
00:00:35,650 --> 00:00:43,000
Now we're also going to assume that device A knows the IP of device C, but as we know so far, in order

10
00:00:43,000 --> 00:00:49,570
for these devices to communicate within the same network device, A needs to know the MAC address of

11
00:00:49,570 --> 00:00:56,050
device C because like we said before, the communication inside the network is carried out using the

12
00:00:56,050 --> 00:00:59,290
Mac address and not using the IP address.

13
00:00:59,740 --> 00:01:05,560
So this is a perfectly normal situation where we have a client that needs to know the Mac address of

14
00:01:05,560 --> 00:01:09,100
another client so that it can communicate with this client.

15
00:01:09,790 --> 00:01:15,790
Now, what device A would do is it will use a protocol called ARP.

16
00:01:16,420 --> 00:01:19,870
ERP stands for address resolution protocol.

17
00:01:19,870 --> 00:01:26,680
And it's a very simple protocol that allows us to link IP addresses to MAC addresses.

18
00:01:26,680 --> 00:01:32,800
So the whole point of this protocol is to aid with the situation where there is a client that needs

19
00:01:32,800 --> 00:01:34,690
to communicate with another client.

20
00:01:34,690 --> 00:01:39,730
It knows the IP of the other client, but it doesn't know the MAC address of the other client.

21
00:01:40,090 --> 00:01:44,160
So what this client does, it uses the ARP protocol.

22
00:01:44,170 --> 00:01:45,840
What do I mean by that?

23
00:01:45,850 --> 00:01:54,550
Basically it sends a broadcast message, so it sends an ARP request to a specific MAC address that's

24
00:01:54,550 --> 00:01:59,440
known in the whole network, and this is known as the Broadcast Mac address.

25
00:01:59,890 --> 00:02:07,360
When a packet is set to be sent to the Broadcast Mac address, all clients on the same network will

26
00:02:07,360 --> 00:02:08,710
receive this packet.

27
00:02:09,250 --> 00:02:17,290
So Device A will send the broadcast to all the clients on the network saying who has ten 026?

28
00:02:17,740 --> 00:02:23,950
This packet is going to be directed to the Broadcast Mac address and therefore all the clients on the

29
00:02:23,950 --> 00:02:26,050
network will receive this packet.

30
00:02:26,320 --> 00:02:29,770
So this is an example of an IP request.

31
00:02:30,400 --> 00:02:37,240
Now all of these devices will ignore this packet except the one that has this IP address, which is

32
00:02:37,240 --> 00:02:40,270
ten 026, which is device C.

33
00:02:40,810 --> 00:02:48,040
So the next step, all devices will not do anything, and the only device that will respond is device

34
00:02:48,070 --> 00:02:52,570
C sending an ARP response in this response.

35
00:02:52,570 --> 00:02:55,930
Device C is going to say I have ten 0 to 6.

36
00:02:55,930 --> 00:02:58,840
My MAC address is this Mac address.

37
00:02:59,840 --> 00:03:06,320
This way device a will have the Mac address of device C and now it will be able to communicate with

38
00:03:06,320 --> 00:03:10,670
device C and do whatever task that it wanted to do initially.

39
00:03:11,240 --> 00:03:16,840
So all of this communication is facilitated using the ARP protocol.

40
00:03:16,850 --> 00:03:20,180
Like I said, the ERP protocol is a very simple protocol.

41
00:03:20,180 --> 00:03:27,650
As you can see, all it has is requests and responses, and the whole point of it is so that we can

42
00:03:27,650 --> 00:03:35,120
link IP addresses to MAC addresses or translate IP addresses to MAC addresses so a device can send the

43
00:03:35,120 --> 00:03:37,190
request asking for a mac address.

44
00:03:37,190 --> 00:03:42,350
And then the device that has the MAC address would respond with its MAC address.

45
00:03:42,620 --> 00:03:44,300
It's as simple as that.

46
00:03:44,330 --> 00:03:47,630
It works perfectly and it does its job quickly.

47
00:03:48,990 --> 00:03:56,580
So if we wanted to build a script that will allow us to discover clients on the same network, we can

48
00:03:56,580 --> 00:04:03,780
use the AARP protocol and send broadcast requests just like the one that's sent here, but we'll send

49
00:04:03,780 --> 00:04:06,240
it for every possible IP address.

50
00:04:06,240 --> 00:04:16,350
So we're send it for ten 0 to 110 022 ten 023 ten 024 and all the way up to the end of the subnet,

51
00:04:16,350 --> 00:04:19,320
which is ten zero 2 to 5 four.

52
00:04:19,950 --> 00:04:25,650
So every time we send this broadcast, if there is a device that has that IP address, it's going to

53
00:04:25,650 --> 00:04:29,640
respond to us saying, I have that IP address, my MAC address is this.

54
00:04:30,240 --> 00:04:36,270
Otherwise we won't get a response and we'll know that there is no client at this IP address and we'll

55
00:04:36,270 --> 00:04:38,160
try the next IP address.

56
00:04:39,710 --> 00:04:45,890
Now, let me show you a very simple way of implementing this in this lecture, and then we'll dive deeper

57
00:04:45,890 --> 00:04:47,810
into it in the next lectures.

58
00:04:48,520 --> 00:04:48,910
Now.

59
00:04:48,910 --> 00:04:52,250
I've already created a new project called Network Scanner.

60
00:04:52,270 --> 00:04:56,500
I showed you how to create a new project before, so I'm not going to do that again.

61
00:04:57,420 --> 00:05:00,330
Now we're going to use a module called Skippy.

62
00:05:00,540 --> 00:05:04,440
I'm going to include its documentation in the resources of this lecture.

63
00:05:04,800 --> 00:05:12,090
And to import this module according to its documentation, we have to do import as usual and write the

64
00:05:12,090 --> 00:05:14,280
name of the module which is Skippy.

65
00:05:14,700 --> 00:05:21,150
But we have to add CPT all to implement all the functions that we can use with this module.

66
00:05:21,750 --> 00:05:27,930
Now this is perfectly correct and you can use KP now in the code and all you have to do is KP, dot,

67
00:05:27,930 --> 00:05:31,950
all dot the name of the function that you want to use.

68
00:05:32,580 --> 00:05:33,750
I don't really like that.

69
00:05:33,750 --> 00:05:39,110
So I'm going to rename Scapegoat all as Skippy.

70
00:05:39,600 --> 00:05:45,450
So every time I need to use something from Skippy module and instead of typing scapegoat all, I'll

71
00:05:45,450 --> 00:05:47,220
just have to write Skippy.

72
00:05:48,810 --> 00:05:54,300
Now we're going to make a function and we'll call this function scan because it's going to scan for

73
00:05:54,300 --> 00:05:59,130
us and we're going to make this function, take a variable for an IP address.

74
00:05:59,820 --> 00:06:05,490
And this function, we're going to use a very simple function that's in this CPP module.

75
00:06:05,730 --> 00:06:11,940
You can use this function as usual by doing Skippy, followed by the function name, which is called

76
00:06:11,940 --> 00:06:13,200
a R ping.

77
00:06:13,740 --> 00:06:17,460
And all you have to do is give the IP to this function.

78
00:06:18,470 --> 00:06:19,230
And that's it.

79
00:06:19,250 --> 00:06:21,230
It's really as simple as that.

80
00:06:21,230 --> 00:06:27,740
And like I said, this is a very, very simple example for discovering clients on the same network using

81
00:06:27,740 --> 00:06:29,120
the ERCP protocol.

82
00:06:29,150 --> 00:06:32,030
But we'll dive deeper into this in the next lectures.

83
00:06:32,030 --> 00:06:34,190
Right now, I just want to give you an overview.

84
00:06:34,760 --> 00:06:40,340
So in the main code, all we have to do is just call the function because we just didn't call the function

85
00:06:40,340 --> 00:06:41,620
anywhere in our code.

86
00:06:41,630 --> 00:06:46,160
So we're just going to do scan and give it the IP that we want to scan.

87
00:06:46,160 --> 00:06:49,190
And let's say I want to scan the IP of my router.

88
00:06:49,700 --> 00:06:53,900
So in my Linux, if I just do root NN.

89
00:06:55,190 --> 00:06:58,430
You'll see that my gateway is at ten 0 to 1.

90
00:06:58,430 --> 00:07:00,470
So this is the IP of my router.

91
00:07:00,620 --> 00:07:06,410
So I'm just going to verify that this IP exists and as a result I should get the MAC address because

92
00:07:06,410 --> 00:07:12,710
what we're going to be doing is we're going to be sending in a broadcast asking who has ten 0 to 1 in

93
00:07:12,710 --> 00:07:17,780
this case and the router is going to respond to me in this case, given me its Mac address.

94
00:07:22,920 --> 00:07:28,170
So I'm just going to do ten 0 to 1 and that's it.

95
00:07:28,170 --> 00:07:29,070
Our code is done.

96
00:07:29,070 --> 00:07:30,270
Very, very simple.

97
00:07:30,810 --> 00:07:35,160
So I'm already in the directory for my project.

98
00:07:35,160 --> 00:07:42,120
So if I do ls I have my project here which is called net scanner dot py and I can run this by doing

99
00:07:42,120 --> 00:07:44,790
python nets canada pie.

100
00:07:45,480 --> 00:07:50,130
I'm going to hit enter and as you can see, we have the result here.

101
00:07:50,370 --> 00:07:57,930
We received one packet, we got one answer, we have the IP address and we have the MAC address of my

102
00:07:57,930 --> 00:07:59,040
access point.

103
00:08:00,610 --> 00:08:06,910
Now this function right here, scapegoat ARP Ping also takes IP ranges.

104
00:08:07,210 --> 00:08:13,450
And what I mean by a range is a way of specifying multiple IPS in the same line.

105
00:08:13,780 --> 00:08:20,750
So if I wanted to scan all the IPS within this subnet, so if I wanted to scan from ten zero to 1 to

106
00:08:20,750 --> 00:08:28,720
10 0 to 2 five four, instead of typing that multiple times, I can just write over 24.

107
00:08:29,530 --> 00:08:33,430
Now, this is a common way of specifying the whole subnet and networking.

108
00:08:33,430 --> 00:08:39,580
So not only is KP, you'll find this anywhere when you're reading or in any networking program, you

109
00:08:39,580 --> 00:08:47,230
can just type over 24 to specify the range from 0 to 2 five four in the last field here in the IP.

110
00:08:47,980 --> 00:08:54,400
So if we just run this like that, it's going to scan the whole network that I'm in and it's going to

111
00:08:54,400 --> 00:08:59,920
return to me all the valid IPS with their MAC addresses, as you can see in here.

112
00:09:02,370 --> 00:09:02,730
Now.

113
00:09:02,730 --> 00:09:04,550
As shown, we didn't really do much.

114
00:09:04,560 --> 00:09:11,130
All we did is we imported the escape module and then we called a function called ARP Ping.

115
00:09:11,160 --> 00:09:15,060
We're giving it the IP or the range and it's doing everything for us.

116
00:09:15,060 --> 00:09:21,030
It's doing it's sending the broadcasts, it receiving the responses, it's parsing the responses and

117
00:09:21,030 --> 00:09:23,220
displaying the result for us on screen.

118
00:09:23,220 --> 00:09:26,250
So we're really not doing much, but that's okay.

119
00:09:26,250 --> 00:09:34,250
And this lecture, I just wanted to show you how ERP works and how to use KP with ERP to discover all

120
00:09:34,260 --> 00:09:35,940
clients on the same network.

121
00:09:36,360 --> 00:09:42,540
And the next lecture, we're going to dive deeper and rewrite this function in a more advanced way.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,930 --> 00:00:04,170
Now the script that we built so far is really cool.

2
00:00:04,680 --> 00:00:11,460
It can list all of the clients on the same network as us with their IP address and the Associated Mac

3
00:00:11,460 --> 00:00:17,640
address and doing all of this using the AAP ping function from Skype.

4
00:00:19,240 --> 00:00:28,840
We also learned that AARP achieved this by sending AARP requests, asking who has each possible IP address

5
00:00:28,840 --> 00:00:30,280
from the start of the subnet.

6
00:00:30,280 --> 00:00:38,800
So from zero to the end, which is 254, AARP ping sent these requests to the Broadcast Mac address

7
00:00:38,800 --> 00:00:43,780
so that all clients on the same network will receive these requests.

8
00:00:44,990 --> 00:00:51,500
Now, generally, clients will ignore the requests that they get unless the request is asking about

9
00:00:51,500 --> 00:00:52,970
their IP address.

10
00:00:53,090 --> 00:00:58,520
So in this case here, all the clients will ignore the requests that they're getting, except for this

11
00:00:58,520 --> 00:00:59,720
client right here.

12
00:01:00,440 --> 00:01:07,100
In this case, the client will respond with its Mac address saying, I have this IP address, my MAC

13
00:01:07,100 --> 00:01:08,300
address is this.

14
00:01:08,870 --> 00:01:13,490
Now IP ping will do this for all possible IP like I said.

15
00:01:13,490 --> 00:01:23,300
So it will start at ten 0201234 all the way up to 2548 to register all the responses that it gets at

16
00:01:23,330 --> 00:01:29,150
register the IP is that it gets back and the MAC addresses that these IPS are responding with.

17
00:01:29,180 --> 00:01:35,870
This way it will be able to build up a list of all the IPS that responded with their Associated Mac

18
00:01:35,870 --> 00:01:41,090
addresses, which will give us the list of all the clients that are connected to the same network.

19
00:01:41,780 --> 00:01:48,110
Now, because we know how this works, I'd like to build this manually, so I'd like to build a function

20
00:01:48,110 --> 00:01:53,810
that will work like ARP Ping and it will allow us to list all the clients around us because I think

21
00:01:53,810 --> 00:01:57,260
ARP Ping right here is doing too much for us.

22
00:01:57,650 --> 00:02:04,370
This being a programming course, I think we can dive deeper and learn more about Python as we do this

23
00:02:04,370 --> 00:02:10,340
and also learn more about networks and how packets and data is transferred through networks.

24
00:02:11,840 --> 00:02:17,120
So in order to achieve this, like we did before, when we have a big problem that we want to solve,

25
00:02:17,120 --> 00:02:23,600
it's better to break it down into smaller problems, solve the small problems first, and then combine

26
00:02:23,600 --> 00:02:26,450
all the small solutions to reach our big solution.

27
00:02:27,420 --> 00:02:31,350
So I came up with a very simple algorithm to achieve this.

28
00:02:31,380 --> 00:02:37,170
So our goal now in the next lectures is going to be to discover clients on the same network, so literally

29
00:02:37,170 --> 00:02:41,760
achieve what we have so far, but by building the function ourselves.

30
00:02:42,570 --> 00:02:48,150
Now in order to come up with the steps to do this, I relied on what we learned so far.

31
00:02:48,570 --> 00:02:55,710
So the first step is going to be to create an ARP request directed to the Broadcast Mac address asking

32
00:02:55,710 --> 00:02:56,980
for an IP.

33
00:02:57,000 --> 00:03:03,000
So basically it's just this step we're going to need to create a packet that asks who has a specific

34
00:03:03,000 --> 00:03:03,660
IP.

35
00:03:03,870 --> 00:03:10,430
This is going to be an IP request and it needs to be directed to the Broadcast Mac address so that all

36
00:03:10,440 --> 00:03:11,730
clients receive it.

37
00:03:14,280 --> 00:03:20,760
Then we need to be able to send this packet that we created and wait for the response so that when a

38
00:03:20,760 --> 00:03:26,820
client responds with their Mac address, we'll be able to capture that response and analyze it.

39
00:03:28,260 --> 00:03:34,860
Finally, we'll need to be able to analyze the responses that we get from all the clients and then print

40
00:03:34,860 --> 00:03:36,300
the result on screen.

41
00:03:36,720 --> 00:03:42,370
So like we did before, we're going to forget about the big or the larger goal, which is discover all

42
00:03:42,390 --> 00:03:43,890
clients on the same network.

43
00:03:43,890 --> 00:03:50,100
And in the next lecture we're going to tackle each of these problems one at a time, solve the small

44
00:03:50,100 --> 00:03:53,820
problems, and then at the end will combine everything together.

45
00:03:53,820 --> 00:03:59,790
And combining it is just going to be trivial and simple and we'll be able to achieve the bigger goal.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:01,390
Okay.

2
00:00:01,390 --> 00:00:07,990
So the first step of our algorithm or the first simple problem that we need to solve is being able to

3
00:00:07,990 --> 00:00:09,250
create packets.

4
00:00:09,580 --> 00:00:14,180
So the packet that we need to create has two main features.

5
00:00:14,200 --> 00:00:21,730
The first thing that we need to do is we need to use ARP to ask who has a specific IP.

6
00:00:22,660 --> 00:00:30,220
And then we need to direct this packet to the Broadcast Mac address so that it gets delivered to all

7
00:00:30,220 --> 00:00:32,080
the clients on the same network.

8
00:00:33,330 --> 00:00:40,340
So let's first of all see how we can create an ARP request packet and ask about a specific IP.

9
00:00:40,350 --> 00:00:42,930
Ask who has a specific IP.

10
00:00:43,140 --> 00:00:49,110
So we're literally going to be building something that looks like this packet right here, literally

11
00:00:49,110 --> 00:00:52,590
a packet that asks who has a specific IP.

12
00:00:53,730 --> 00:00:59,340
Now we're going to go to my script and I'm going to remove this line which uses ARP Ping because we

13
00:00:59,340 --> 00:01:05,850
want to build our own way of doing this and we'll try to create our ARP request.

14
00:01:06,330 --> 00:01:10,920
So first of all, I'm going to use a variable and I'm going to call it a ARP request.

15
00:01:12,710 --> 00:01:17,630
And I'm going to make this request equals to and AARP packet.

16
00:01:17,660 --> 00:01:21,860
Now we're going to use Skippy to create an AARP packet object.

17
00:01:21,860 --> 00:01:26,320
So you can think of this similar to the way that we created a parser object.

18
00:01:26,330 --> 00:01:31,250
This time you're creating an object that represents an ARP packet.

19
00:01:31,280 --> 00:01:34,100
Now Skippy is the module that allows us to do that.

20
00:01:34,100 --> 00:01:35,720
So we're going to do Skippy.

21
00:01:36,490 --> 00:01:38,080
Dot AARP.

22
00:01:38,950 --> 00:01:39,880
And that's it.

23
00:01:39,910 --> 00:01:48,660
Now we have a variable called R p request and it contains an instance of an ARP packet object made by

24
00:01:48,670 --> 00:01:49,390
escapee.

25
00:01:49,900 --> 00:01:54,850
Now I know this might be a little bit confusing, but let's go through this lecture and the next lecture

26
00:01:54,850 --> 00:01:56,770
and you'll see it'll all make sense.

27
00:01:57,400 --> 00:02:04,480
So first of all, this CPT ARP class allows us to print a summary of the current object that we just

28
00:02:04,480 --> 00:02:05,200
created.

29
00:02:05,740 --> 00:02:11,530
So we can just do a print a arp request dot summary.

30
00:02:13,030 --> 00:02:20,140
So this is a method and the ARP class implemented by Skippy and it literally gives us a summary of what

31
00:02:20,140 --> 00:02:21,580
we created right here.

32
00:02:22,870 --> 00:02:27,040
So I'm going to clear this and we'll just run our script again.

33
00:02:27,610 --> 00:02:33,250
And as you can see, this ARP request, the summary of it basically says that this will use the ERP

34
00:02:33,250 --> 00:02:37,990
protocol and it's going to ask who has this specific IP?

35
00:02:38,590 --> 00:02:45,940
And it's also saying return the result to ten 028, which is my IP address.

36
00:02:46,420 --> 00:02:47,590
So this is perfect.

37
00:02:47,590 --> 00:02:48,700
This is really cool.

38
00:02:48,730 --> 00:02:58,220
The only thing is the way we want this, if we look back, is to ask who has a specific IP, not 0.0.0.0.

39
00:02:58,240 --> 00:03:04,720
So we actually need to have a variable that represents the IP in here and you can guess it.

40
00:03:04,720 --> 00:03:07,270
It's the variable that we're passing when we're calling.

41
00:03:07,270 --> 00:03:10,990
This can function in here, which is the IP variable here.

42
00:03:11,680 --> 00:03:15,170
So we need we need this to say who has IP.

43
00:03:15,190 --> 00:03:18,190
Whatever IP we put here, we want this to say it.

44
00:03:18,790 --> 00:03:27,070
So we need a way to modify this object right here and set the value for the IP to any value that we

45
00:03:27,070 --> 00:03:27,670
want.

46
00:03:28,150 --> 00:03:34,210
Now, we don't know how to do that because we don't know how to set the fields and what these fields

47
00:03:34,210 --> 00:03:36,340
are called inside this object.

48
00:03:36,520 --> 00:03:42,730
So to get the names of the fields that we can set or the names of the variables that we can set, we

49
00:03:42,730 --> 00:03:50,770
can use another function voice shape, which is the RLS function, so we can just do KP dot lrs.

50
00:03:51,460 --> 00:03:57,130
And between the brackets, put the name of the class that you want to learn, the names of the variables

51
00:03:57,130 --> 00:03:59,360
that you can set for it, or the fields.

52
00:03:59,380 --> 00:04:02,110
So in our case, this is the ARP class.

53
00:04:02,110 --> 00:04:05,800
So we're going to say Skip Dot ARP.

54
00:04:07,810 --> 00:04:15,430
Now, if we run our program again, you'll see that it's given us a list of all the fields that we can

55
00:04:15,430 --> 00:04:16,090
set.

56
00:04:16,300 --> 00:04:22,120
It's also given us a description of what each of these fields mean and the default value.

57
00:04:22,720 --> 00:04:27,030
Now you can see that the field that we're interested in is the IP field right here.

58
00:04:27,040 --> 00:04:30,220
So in order to set this, you'd use p dest.

59
00:04:30,910 --> 00:04:34,000
And as you can see, the default value of this is zero.

60
00:04:34,000 --> 00:04:36,280
So 0.0.0.0.

61
00:04:36,280 --> 00:04:38,920
And it is what we are seeing right here.

62
00:04:39,430 --> 00:04:41,500
Now, we can set this in two ways.

63
00:04:41,500 --> 00:04:49,820
We can either in here, we can just do before we print this, we can just do a ARP request dot.

64
00:04:49,870 --> 00:04:56,110
The name of the field that we want to set, which is p DST and set the value.

65
00:04:56,110 --> 00:05:03,650
So we are setting this to the value of the IP that gets passed to us when the function is called.

66
00:05:03,670 --> 00:05:07,120
So we're going to set this to IP so this will work.

67
00:05:07,120 --> 00:05:09,070
So if we just run this again.

68
00:05:10,420 --> 00:05:16,360
You can see in the summary, it's asking who has the IP that we are passing in here?

69
00:05:17,700 --> 00:05:19,930
Alternatively, which is what I like.

70
00:05:19,950 --> 00:05:23,130
You can set this value when you create the object.

71
00:05:23,130 --> 00:05:24,930
So we're creating the object here.

72
00:05:24,930 --> 00:05:28,620
We're saying ARP request equals scapegoat ARP.

73
00:05:28,920 --> 00:05:32,460
And between the brackets you can set this value in here.

74
00:05:32,460 --> 00:05:38,460
So you can just say again, P DST, which is the name of the field that you want to set and make it

75
00:05:38,460 --> 00:05:40,650
equal to the value that you want to set.

76
00:05:40,650 --> 00:05:46,350
And again, we're setting it to the IP because the function takes IP as input and we're given the IP

77
00:05:46,350 --> 00:05:48,270
in here when we're calling the function.

78
00:05:48,930 --> 00:05:51,960
So with that, we can remove this line right here.

79
00:05:53,430 --> 00:05:58,620
And I'm also going to remove the RLS line which lists all the fields that we can set because we are

80
00:05:58,620 --> 00:06:02,760
happy with what we have right now and run this again.

81
00:06:03,750 --> 00:06:10,710
And as you can see now, our r.p is saying, who has this IP, which is the IP that we're passing?

82
00:06:10,710 --> 00:06:16,740
And it's saying return the result to ten 028, which is exactly what we want.

83
00:06:18,090 --> 00:06:21,660
So now the first part of our request is done.

84
00:06:21,660 --> 00:06:24,890
As you can see, if we go back here, we have achieved this.

85
00:06:24,900 --> 00:06:29,400
We have a packet that says who has the IP that we passed to it.

86
00:06:29,400 --> 00:06:32,370
So we're using a variable for the IP, which is perfect.

87
00:06:32,490 --> 00:06:37,800
Now in the next lecture, we're going to tackle the next part of the packet that we need to create,

88
00:06:37,800 --> 00:06:45,270
which is make sure that the destination of this packet is the broadcast mark so that it gets delivered

89
00:06:45,270 --> 00:06:47,760
to all the clients on the same network.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,720 --> 00:00:01,140
Okay.

2
00:00:01,140 --> 00:00:07,710
So we're still at the first step of our algorithm, which is creating an ARP request that asks for a

3
00:00:07,710 --> 00:00:12,390
specific IP and is sent to the Broadcast Mac address.

4
00:00:12,690 --> 00:00:15,870
Now we've broken this down to even smaller parts.

5
00:00:15,870 --> 00:00:22,890
So the first part was to create an IP request asking about a specific IP, and we achieved this in the

6
00:00:22,890 --> 00:00:28,740
previous lecture and we already have that stored in a variable called IP request.

7
00:00:28,950 --> 00:00:34,170
We verified it has the right result by printing the summary of this packet.

8
00:00:34,710 --> 00:00:40,800
The next thing that we need to do is to set the destination mark to the Broadcast Mac address to make

9
00:00:40,800 --> 00:00:45,690
sure that this will be sent to all the clients on the same network.

10
00:00:46,080 --> 00:00:52,980
Now, in order to do this, we need to use an Ethernet frame because like I said, data in networks

11
00:00:52,980 --> 00:00:56,860
is sent using the MAC address, not the IP address.

12
00:00:56,880 --> 00:01:05,100
The MAC address is a physical address that is engraved on each network card and the source Mac and the

13
00:01:05,100 --> 00:01:10,950
destination Mac is set in the Ethernet part of each packet, not in any other part.

14
00:01:11,250 --> 00:01:18,180
Therefore, we need to create an ethernet frame and then append our IP request to it later on.

15
00:01:19,160 --> 00:01:24,140
So for this lecture, as we usually do, we're going to forget about everything and we're going to focus

16
00:01:24,140 --> 00:01:29,930
on creating an Internet frame that will be sent to the Broadcast Mac address.

17
00:01:30,990 --> 00:01:35,640
Now creating this is very similar to the way that we created the ARP request.

18
00:01:35,850 --> 00:01:37,950
So I'm going to delete this line.

19
00:01:38,820 --> 00:01:42,060
And in here we're going to say broadcast.

20
00:01:43,610 --> 00:01:44,860
Is equal to.

21
00:01:44,870 --> 00:01:52,100
And we're going to say, Skippy, that either to create an ethernet frame and put to brackets.

22
00:01:52,430 --> 00:01:56,150
So very similar to the way we were creating an ARP packet.

23
00:01:56,150 --> 00:01:58,850
All we're doing is we're saying scapegoat either.

24
00:01:58,850 --> 00:02:06,980
This will create an Ethernet object for us from Skype using a class that's implemented by Skippy and

25
00:02:06,980 --> 00:02:12,770
it's going to store an instance of that in broadcast in a variable called broadcast.

26
00:02:12,920 --> 00:02:15,650
Again, you can call these variables whatever you want.

27
00:02:15,680 --> 00:02:19,010
I'm just using these names because I think they're meaningful.

28
00:02:19,010 --> 00:02:25,670
They make sense to me now, just like we had to set the IP in the previous lecture when we were creating

29
00:02:25,670 --> 00:02:32,660
the ARP, and here we need to set the MAC address to the broadcast MAC address the whole point of using

30
00:02:32,660 --> 00:02:40,190
either and here is to make sure that the packet that will be sending will be sent to the Broadcast Mac

31
00:02:40,190 --> 00:02:42,680
address and not to only one device.

32
00:02:43,460 --> 00:02:49,610
So we need to set the destination mark and here to the mark of the broadcast.

33
00:02:49,970 --> 00:02:55,460
Now, again, we don't know how to do this because we don't know the field name that's used for the

34
00:02:55,460 --> 00:02:57,170
Destination Mac address.

35
00:02:57,440 --> 00:03:03,620
Therefore, just like we did before, in order to get the names of all the fields that we can set for

36
00:03:03,620 --> 00:03:08,510
the other class or for the other object, we can use this couples function.

37
00:03:09,020 --> 00:03:14,300
And in here we're going to put the name of the class that we want to get information about.

38
00:03:14,300 --> 00:03:17,480
So in the previous lecture we put KP ARP.

39
00:03:17,510 --> 00:03:19,970
Right now we're going to put CPT other.

40
00:03:21,340 --> 00:03:23,290
And then we'll run our code.

41
00:03:24,960 --> 00:03:30,270
And as you can see, it's given us the list of all the fields that we can set and the description of

42
00:03:30,270 --> 00:03:30,720
them.

43
00:03:31,440 --> 00:03:37,710
So as we know, we need to set the destination Mac to the MAC address of the broadcast.

44
00:03:37,920 --> 00:03:41,220
So to do this, we're going to need to use the dest.

45
00:03:41,220 --> 00:03:45,090
And here again, you can use that in two ways.

46
00:03:45,090 --> 00:03:46,950
You can either do broadcast.

47
00:03:48,020 --> 00:03:56,180
Dot DST equals and put the Mac address or you can do it the same way that we did it in here.

48
00:03:56,180 --> 00:04:01,670
Just copy all of this and set the dest field when you create the object.

49
00:04:01,670 --> 00:04:05,600
So you just put that in here between the two brackets.

50
00:04:06,050 --> 00:04:11,960
Now the broadcast Mac address, like I said, it's actually a virtual Mac address is something that

51
00:04:11,960 --> 00:04:13,520
doesn't exist really.

52
00:04:13,520 --> 00:04:18,230
But when you send something to it, all clients will receive it in the network.

53
00:04:18,440 --> 00:04:21,620
So the value for this is six FS.

54
00:04:23,660 --> 00:04:26,720
So it's one, two, three, four, five, six.

55
00:04:26,870 --> 00:04:28,010
And that's it.

56
00:04:29,370 --> 00:04:30,600
Now, like we did before.

57
00:04:30,600 --> 00:04:34,350
We can do a print broadcast summary.

58
00:04:36,700 --> 00:04:40,190
You clear all of this and run our code again.

59
00:04:40,490 --> 00:04:43,840
And as you can see, it's going to be an Internet packet.

60
00:04:43,850 --> 00:04:49,110
It's going to be sent from this Mac address, which is the Mac address of my computer right now of 88

61
00:04:49,190 --> 00:04:49,820
zero.

62
00:04:50,030 --> 00:04:55,550
And it's going to be sent to this Mac address, which is the MAC address of the broadcast.

63
00:04:56,860 --> 00:05:03,300
Now, if we go back to our algorithm and look at the first step, we need to use ARP to ask who has

64
00:05:03,310 --> 00:05:10,810
specific IP and we already have that in our ARP request variable using the ARP class or the object from

65
00:05:10,840 --> 00:05:11,590
Skype.

66
00:05:11,950 --> 00:05:16,420
And the next step is to set the destination mark to the broadcast mark.

67
00:05:16,420 --> 00:05:23,290
And again, we just implemented that and the frame that does that is stored in the broadcast variable

68
00:05:23,290 --> 00:05:24,010
in here.

69
00:05:24,550 --> 00:05:31,600
So now all we have to do is just combine these two packets into one packet and we'll have our first

70
00:05:31,600 --> 00:05:32,470
step done.

71
00:05:32,890 --> 00:05:37,360
Now to do this in Skippy, you can just use the forward slash.

72
00:05:37,420 --> 00:05:40,720
So we're going to create a final packet which will contain both.

73
00:05:40,720 --> 00:05:45,850
So we're going to call this ARP request broadcast.

74
00:05:45,850 --> 00:05:47,650
And again, this is just a variable name.

75
00:05:47,650 --> 00:05:49,390
You can call it whatever you want.

76
00:05:49,540 --> 00:05:53,710
And this is going to be the combination of the two that we created so far.

77
00:05:53,890 --> 00:05:56,470
So this will be our broadcast.

78
00:05:57,720 --> 00:06:05,640
And then I'm appending the AARP part to it using the forward slash and we're adding the AARP request

79
00:06:05,640 --> 00:06:08,010
packet that we created in here.

80
00:06:08,490 --> 00:06:13,770
So again, all we're doing now is we're just creating a new packet, which is the combination of the

81
00:06:13,770 --> 00:06:16,530
two that we created in the previous two lectures.

82
00:06:16,530 --> 00:06:21,690
And we're combining them using the forward slash because CP allows us to do that.

83
00:06:22,900 --> 00:06:25,960
Now we can print this summary of this.

84
00:06:31,160 --> 00:06:36,140
And you'll see that this has an either part and it has an AARP part.

85
00:06:36,140 --> 00:06:42,830
And in the AARP part, it's asking who has this IP address, which is the IP that we're passing here?

86
00:06:42,830 --> 00:06:47,630
And it's saying return the result to this IP, which is my IP.

87
00:06:48,110 --> 00:06:55,670
Now we can also print something useful by doing the name of the packet, which is AARP request broadcast

88
00:06:55,670 --> 00:06:56,990
dot show.

89
00:06:57,380 --> 00:07:02,780
And what this will do, it will show us more details about the contents of this packet.

90
00:07:03,170 --> 00:07:05,630
So let's do this and see what we get.

91
00:07:05,660 --> 00:07:14,930
Now we can actually use the show method on all the other packets so we can actually just do a r.p request

92
00:07:15,200 --> 00:07:16,280
dot show.

93
00:07:17,450 --> 00:07:21,260
And we will do our broadcast or show.

94
00:07:23,190 --> 00:07:27,650
And we have already our IRP request broadcast on show.

95
00:07:27,660 --> 00:07:32,940
And what this will do, it will show us the details of each of these packets.

96
00:07:33,060 --> 00:07:39,600
So let's see what the difference is between these two and what happens when we combine them in the last

97
00:07:39,600 --> 00:07:40,800
statement in here.

98
00:07:41,280 --> 00:07:42,780
So let's run this.

99
00:07:44,740 --> 00:07:46,390
And let's analyze that.

100
00:07:46,660 --> 00:07:51,930
So the first result here is this ARP request.

101
00:07:51,940 --> 00:07:54,610
So as you can see, it's an ARP request.

102
00:07:54,610 --> 00:08:05,560
It's asking who has it's been sent from our Mac address, from our IP, and it's directed to ten .0.2.1

103
00:08:05,560 --> 00:08:11,680
over 24, which means all the IPS or the possible IPS and our subnet.

104
00:08:12,380 --> 00:08:18,080
Now note the IP here is the same as the IP that we requested when we called the function.

105
00:08:19,070 --> 00:08:21,950
Then the next show is the show in here.

106
00:08:21,950 --> 00:08:24,020
So this is this one right here.

107
00:08:24,860 --> 00:08:28,010
And as you can see, this contains Ethernet data.

108
00:08:28,130 --> 00:08:30,940
The destination is the Broadcast Mac address.

109
00:08:30,950 --> 00:08:32,990
Again, it's what we set in here.

110
00:08:32,990 --> 00:08:36,860
And the source is the MAC address of my ETA zero.

111
00:08:38,080 --> 00:08:44,950
And finally, the last show in here is the show of the AARP request broadcast, which is the combination

112
00:08:44,950 --> 00:08:45,940
of the two.

113
00:08:46,900 --> 00:08:48,880
And if we look at that.

114
00:08:50,850 --> 00:08:54,740
You can see that this packet right now contains two parts.

115
00:08:54,750 --> 00:08:56,070
Let me make this bigger.

116
00:08:57,730 --> 00:09:05,140
So it has an Ethernet part which will make sure that this packet is directed to the Broadcast Mac address

117
00:09:05,710 --> 00:09:14,500
and it also has an ARP part which will ask who has and it will ask about the IP in the subnet, which

118
00:09:14,500 --> 00:09:17,200
are the IP that we pass to the function.

119
00:09:17,560 --> 00:09:25,360
So as you can see, this is a combination of the two variables or the two objects that we created before.

120
00:09:27,050 --> 00:09:30,350
Now in the next lecture, we're going to continue through the algorithm.

121
00:09:30,350 --> 00:09:36,290
We'll see how we can send this packet, receive the response, pass the response and discover all clients

122
00:09:36,290 --> 00:09:37,550
on the same network.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,880 --> 00:00:04,040
Now we have the first step of our algorithm done.

2
00:00:04,060 --> 00:00:10,510
We created a packet that will be directed to the Broadcast Mac address, so it's going to be delivered

3
00:00:10,510 --> 00:00:17,020
to all clients or computers on the same network, and it's going to ask for a specific IP, whatever

4
00:00:17,020 --> 00:00:19,630
IP that we use as the IP variable.

5
00:00:20,230 --> 00:00:27,040
Now it's time to move to the next step in which we will try to send this packet into the network and

6
00:00:27,040 --> 00:00:28,600
wait for the response.

7
00:00:28,960 --> 00:00:32,080
So to do this, we're going to use a Skype module.

8
00:00:32,650 --> 00:00:38,050
But before we do that, I'm actually going to remove all the print statements and the show functions

9
00:00:38,050 --> 00:00:44,920
in here because they just show stuff on screen and we were just using them to understand how Skype works.

10
00:00:45,280 --> 00:00:48,820
So I'm just going to remove this and this.

11
00:00:50,910 --> 00:00:55,380
And we're going to try to send the ARP request broadcast packet in here.

12
00:00:56,010 --> 00:01:01,500
Again, we're sending this packet because this packet contains the combination of the broadcast and

13
00:01:01,500 --> 00:01:08,190
the AARP request, so it will automatically go to the broadcast MAC address asking for who has the IP

14
00:01:08,190 --> 00:01:11,250
that we pass as the IP as a variable.

15
00:01:13,210 --> 00:01:18,750
So the function that we'll use to send this packet is called SRP.

16
00:01:18,760 --> 00:01:21,700
So we're going to do Skippy dot SRP.

17
00:01:22,620 --> 00:01:28,290
And we'll put the name of the packet that we want to send, which is a ARP request broadcast.

18
00:01:29,070 --> 00:01:35,020
Now Skype has a function called ASR, which basically stands for send and receive.

19
00:01:35,040 --> 00:01:39,600
But we're using a different variation of this function, which is called XRP.

20
00:01:39,660 --> 00:01:46,160
And the only difference is the SRP allows us to send packets with a custom either part.

21
00:01:46,170 --> 00:01:53,370
So, as you know, previously we created an either layer or an either part to and we used it in the

22
00:01:53,370 --> 00:01:54,840
packet that we're sending.

23
00:01:55,140 --> 00:02:01,530
Therefore, because we're using a custom, either we're using the SRP instead of the SR function.

24
00:02:02,010 --> 00:02:07,890
Now, as the name suggests, this will send the packet that we give it and receive the response.

25
00:02:08,220 --> 00:02:13,590
But when this line will be executed, it's going to send this packet right here.

26
00:02:13,680 --> 00:02:16,860
But the response is not being captured anywhere.

27
00:02:17,130 --> 00:02:23,340
So like we were using before, because this function returns a value, we need to capture the value

28
00:02:23,340 --> 00:02:25,920
that the function returns and a variable.

29
00:02:25,950 --> 00:02:28,620
Now, this is not any other simple function.

30
00:02:28,620 --> 00:02:31,260
It actually returns more than one value.

31
00:02:31,980 --> 00:02:38,610
Now, looking at the documentation, you will learn that this function will return a couple of two lists.

32
00:02:38,610 --> 00:02:41,520
So basically it will return to values.

33
00:02:41,790 --> 00:02:43,290
Forget about the list part.

34
00:02:43,290 --> 00:02:44,880
We'll talk about that later.

35
00:02:44,880 --> 00:02:49,920
But for now, we're going to focus on the fact that it's going to return to us two values.

36
00:02:49,920 --> 00:02:51,330
So two variables.

37
00:02:51,690 --> 00:02:54,750
One of them will represent the answered packets.

38
00:02:54,750 --> 00:03:00,810
So the answers that we got and the other one will represent the unanswered packets.

39
00:03:01,200 --> 00:03:06,810
So in order to capture these two values that are returned by this variable, we're going to have to

40
00:03:06,810 --> 00:03:08,190
use two variables.

41
00:03:08,490 --> 00:03:15,080
And because I like to use meaningful variable names, we're going to call them answered, put a comma,

42
00:03:15,090 --> 00:03:21,870
name the other variable, which we will call unanswered, and we're going to make that equal to our

43
00:03:21,870 --> 00:03:22,560
function.

44
00:03:23,070 --> 00:03:29,070
Now, looking at this line again one more time, we're going to see that this will use the XRP function

45
00:03:29,070 --> 00:03:35,070
to send the packet that we created in the previous lectures and wait for the response.

46
00:03:35,070 --> 00:03:39,390
When it gets the responses, it's going to return two sets of values.

47
00:03:39,390 --> 00:03:43,770
One of them is the asset packets, and the other one is the unanswered.

48
00:03:43,800 --> 00:03:47,030
Therefore, we're capturing it in two variables.

49
00:03:47,040 --> 00:03:52,080
One of them will call an answered, and the other one will call it unanswered.

50
00:03:52,380 --> 00:03:54,540
Now, again, these are just variable names.

51
00:03:54,540 --> 00:03:56,670
You can use any names that you want.

52
00:03:57,120 --> 00:04:03,300
One more thing I'd like to highlight when we are using this function note that we are not giving it

53
00:04:03,300 --> 00:04:04,320
a destination.

54
00:04:04,320 --> 00:04:07,200
We just tell Skippy to send this packet.

55
00:04:07,200 --> 00:04:09,990
We don't tell the function where to send the packet.

56
00:04:10,020 --> 00:04:17,400
The packet is going to go in the right direction because we added another layer to it and in that layer

57
00:04:17,400 --> 00:04:19,890
we set the destination Mac address.

58
00:04:20,310 --> 00:04:25,830
So if you didn't want to send this packet to the broadcast, you can just use any Mac address on the

59
00:04:25,830 --> 00:04:30,630
network here and this packet will be delivered to the location that you want.

60
00:04:31,050 --> 00:04:35,630
So this is the way to use the SRP or the SR functions in Skippy.

61
00:04:35,640 --> 00:04:40,590
Even if you are building a completely different program, all you do is you just give the packet to

62
00:04:40,590 --> 00:04:46,800
the function and before that you have to configure your packet to go in the right direction.

63
00:04:46,800 --> 00:04:52,350
Again, you can look at all the fields by doing LS, followed by the class name that you're using like

64
00:04:52,350 --> 00:04:53,550
I showed you before.

65
00:04:54,030 --> 00:04:59,010
Now there is one more thing that I want to add to this function, which is a timeout.

66
00:04:59,100 --> 00:05:04,170
Basically, when we set a timeout we are saying Wait for this amount of seconds.

67
00:05:04,170 --> 00:05:06,570
If you don't get any response, move on.

68
00:05:06,570 --> 00:05:07,890
Don't keep waiting.

69
00:05:08,190 --> 00:05:10,500
So I'm going to set the timeout to one.

70
00:05:10,920 --> 00:05:16,800
And what this will do is whenever this is working, if it sends a request and gets no response, then

71
00:05:16,800 --> 00:05:19,590
it will only wait for 1/2 and move on.

72
00:05:19,830 --> 00:05:25,470
If you don't add this timeout, you'll be stuck because if you don't get a response, you will never

73
00:05:25,470 --> 00:05:27,090
exit out to the program.

74
00:05:27,630 --> 00:05:32,940
Finally, now that we have all of this done, let's print the answer summary.

75
00:05:33,030 --> 00:05:34,860
So we're just going to do a print.

76
00:05:35,340 --> 00:05:36,330
Answer it.

77
00:05:37,430 --> 00:05:38,540
Dot summary.

78
00:05:40,120 --> 00:05:44,170
Now, like I said, the answered will contain the answered packets.

79
00:05:44,170 --> 00:05:48,160
So we're only printing a summary of the answers that we got.

80
00:05:48,880 --> 00:05:50,500
Let's go and run our code.

81
00:05:54,100 --> 00:06:00,640
And as you can see in the summary, we can see the first part, which is the request that we sent.

82
00:06:00,670 --> 00:06:07,600
So for example, in this request we were asking who has ten 0 to 1 tell ten, 0 to 8?

83
00:06:07,600 --> 00:06:13,840
And then the response came from this Mac address saying, I have that IP again.

84
00:06:13,840 --> 00:06:17,160
And here we're asking who has ten 0 to 2?

85
00:06:17,260 --> 00:06:25,060
The response came from ten 0 to 2, given us their Mac address, and same happened for ten 0 to 3 and

86
00:06:25,060 --> 00:06:26,650
ten, 0 to 7.

87
00:06:27,370 --> 00:06:29,560
Now, if we print the unanswered.

88
00:06:35,200 --> 00:06:38,440
You'll see that we have much more entries in here.

89
00:06:38,710 --> 00:06:43,990
And as you can see, we're asking who has this IP address and we're getting non-response.

90
00:06:43,990 --> 00:06:47,620
We're asking who has this IP address and we're getting no response.

91
00:06:47,980 --> 00:06:53,980
So as you can see, this tells us that these IPS are not used by any clients on the network, which

92
00:06:53,980 --> 00:06:56,680
means that there are no clients using them.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,930 --> 00:00:01,500
Okay.

2
00:00:01,500 --> 00:00:07,920
Now that we have the second small problem or the second step solved and we are able to send packets

3
00:00:07,920 --> 00:00:13,920
asking for a specific Mac address and we are able to capture the response in a variable and we call

4
00:00:13,920 --> 00:00:15,720
this variable answered.

5
00:00:15,720 --> 00:00:21,000
Now it's time to move to the next step, which is parsing the response that we got.

6
00:00:21,570 --> 00:00:29,280
So from the previous lectures, you know that the response that we're getting is being stored in a variable

7
00:00:29,280 --> 00:00:30,630
called answered.

8
00:00:31,110 --> 00:00:35,520
Now all we did is we printed answered the summary.

9
00:00:35,580 --> 00:00:42,690
Now summary is a method that's implemented by escapee that prints the summary of the content stored

10
00:00:42,690 --> 00:00:44,610
in the answered variable.

11
00:00:44,730 --> 00:00:52,020
And we see that that will print for us all the requests and the response that we got for that request.

12
00:00:52,500 --> 00:00:58,710
Now, this is very useful for us because we know what we're looking for, but if we wanted to present

13
00:00:58,710 --> 00:01:05,100
this to a user or if we wanted to use this further in a program to do something else with the information

14
00:01:05,100 --> 00:01:05,730
that we got.

15
00:01:05,730 --> 00:01:12,240
So if we wanted to actually use the Mac address here to do further attacks, then we need to be able

16
00:01:12,240 --> 00:01:18,840
to access this MAC address variable on its own, because what we're doing right now is we're printing

17
00:01:18,840 --> 00:01:23,340
all the responses and the requests that triggered these responses.

18
00:01:23,340 --> 00:01:30,120
So there is a lot of data that is not useful for us, combined with the data that's useful to us.

19
00:01:30,600 --> 00:01:37,260
So what we really want is we need to be able to isolate the MAC addresses and the IPS so that we can,

20
00:01:37,260 --> 00:01:42,540
first of all, print them to a user who is not interested in to know, knowing what requests we send

21
00:01:42,540 --> 00:01:44,700
or any of this clutter right here.

22
00:01:44,940 --> 00:01:51,540
And we'll also be able to use it in this program and in other programs in the future to run further

23
00:01:51,540 --> 00:01:53,910
attacks or carry further tasks.

24
00:01:54,480 --> 00:02:01,230
So our objective now in the first step, which is the third smaller problem, is to be able to pass

25
00:02:01,230 --> 00:02:07,620
this mysterious variable answered and see how we can extract the useful information out of it.

26
00:02:08,950 --> 00:02:15,550
Now, if we look at the documentation of KP, you'll see that I tell us the send and response function

27
00:02:15,550 --> 00:02:17,530
returns to us to lists.

28
00:02:17,860 --> 00:02:22,900
So one of them is for the answer and the other one is for the unanswered.

29
00:02:23,320 --> 00:02:28,900
And as you can see in here, we're capturing this by saying answered and unanswered.

30
00:02:29,730 --> 00:02:35,820
Now, since we know these variables are lists now, it will be useful to name them something meaningful

31
00:02:35,820 --> 00:02:37,670
since we're doing that from the start.

32
00:02:37,680 --> 00:02:44,460
So I'm just going to call this answer list and in here we'll call this unanswered list.

33
00:02:45,340 --> 00:02:49,090
And again, I'll just update this and we'll call it answered list.

34
00:02:49,690 --> 00:02:55,960
Now I'm just doing this so that my code is more readable and so that it's easier even for me to understand.

35
00:02:56,680 --> 00:03:00,730
Now we know this is a list, but we never talked about lists.

36
00:03:00,730 --> 00:03:06,130
We don't know what lists are, and we don't know how they work, how to deal with them and how to use

37
00:03:06,130 --> 00:03:06,580
them.

38
00:03:06,970 --> 00:03:10,480
So right now, I'm going to give you an overview on lists.

39
00:03:10,480 --> 00:03:15,580
And then in the next lecture, we'll come back to this and we'll see how we can pass the answered list

40
00:03:15,580 --> 00:03:18,280
and extract the useful information from it.

41
00:03:20,590 --> 00:03:23,080
So lists are very, very useful.

42
00:03:23,290 --> 00:03:26,140
You might have heard of them in other programming languages.

43
00:03:26,140 --> 00:03:30,850
They are called arrays, but in Python we refer to arrays as lists.

44
00:03:32,130 --> 00:03:39,480
What we mean by a list or an array is it's a list of values or elements that are kind of related to

45
00:03:39,480 --> 00:03:45,690
each other so they can refer to the same thing or are used in a similar problem, and we can access

46
00:03:45,690 --> 00:03:48,480
them based on only one variable name.

47
00:03:48,750 --> 00:03:51,870
So I have a quick example here that shows in Python.

48
00:03:51,870 --> 00:03:56,970
If you wanted to create a list, first of all, you have to name it something, a variable name, the

49
00:03:56,970 --> 00:03:59,040
same way that we use to use variable names.

50
00:03:59,040 --> 00:04:01,800
So it will be handy to use something meaningful.

51
00:04:01,830 --> 00:04:07,860
For example, if we wanted to create a list of lucky numbers we're creating here, we're saying lucky

52
00:04:07,860 --> 00:04:12,360
numbers list equals to three, seven, eight, 17, 24.

53
00:04:12,360 --> 00:04:16,740
Now to me, these are my lucky numbers could be different to you or to any other person.

54
00:04:16,740 --> 00:04:18,150
This is just an example.

55
00:04:18,780 --> 00:04:26,550
Now when you store this as a list, the way Python interpret this is think of this table right here

56
00:04:26,550 --> 00:04:28,170
as the lucky numbers list.

57
00:04:28,470 --> 00:04:33,480
So you have the values here, which is three seven, eight, 17, 24.

58
00:04:33,480 --> 00:04:36,450
So it's the same as the values that we specified here.

59
00:04:37,020 --> 00:04:40,110
And to each value there is an index.

60
00:04:40,200 --> 00:04:46,260
So the index starts at zero and then moves on to one, two, three, four.

61
00:04:46,650 --> 00:04:54,570
So in the future, if we wanted to access element zero of our lucky numbers list, we can just do lucky

62
00:04:54,570 --> 00:04:58,650
numbers list and put zero between two square brackets.

63
00:04:59,310 --> 00:05:04,200
If we wanted to access the second element, then we put one between two square brackets.

64
00:05:04,200 --> 00:05:08,430
If we want to access the second element, we put three and so on.

65
00:05:08,820 --> 00:05:15,450
So basically the way to access any element in the list, you put the variable name followed by the index

66
00:05:15,450 --> 00:05:18,210
of that element between square brackets.

67
00:05:18,750 --> 00:05:21,690
It's very, very simple and it's very powerful.

68
00:05:21,690 --> 00:05:24,570
And we're actually going to be using it more during this course.

69
00:05:24,570 --> 00:05:29,970
And I highly recommend you do some reading about lists in Python because they're very powerful and they're

70
00:05:29,970 --> 00:05:30,900
used a lot.

71
00:05:30,900 --> 00:05:33,840
But I don't want to spend too much time talking about it right now.

72
00:05:33,840 --> 00:05:38,310
We're going to move through our programs, as we usually do, and learn by doing.

73
00:05:38,760 --> 00:05:44,310
Now, I want to show you a very quick example before we move to our program in the next lecture.

74
00:05:44,460 --> 00:05:49,860
So I'm just going to go to my terminal and in the terminal I'm going to type Python.

75
00:05:50,460 --> 00:05:57,000
This will open my Python interpreter for me and it will allow me to run Python code and see the result

76
00:05:57,000 --> 00:05:57,840
directly.

77
00:05:58,290 --> 00:06:03,330
So first of all, I'm just going to create a list and we're going to call this Lucky Numbers list.

78
00:06:04,860 --> 00:06:08,100
And we're going to make that equals to my lucky numbers.

79
00:06:08,100 --> 00:06:13,200
And again, remember, you're going to put the values between two square brackets and separate them

80
00:06:13,200 --> 00:06:14,010
by comma.

81
00:06:14,280 --> 00:06:22,290
So the numbers I'm going to put are three, seven, eight, 17 and 24.

82
00:06:23,070 --> 00:06:31,830
Now, let's try to access the first element of this list so we can just do print lucky numbers list

83
00:06:31,890 --> 00:06:38,400
and then put zero between two square brackets to access the first element of the list, which is three.

84
00:06:39,090 --> 00:06:41,310
Now, if I hit enter, you'll see.

85
00:06:41,310 --> 00:06:42,690
I'll get number three.

86
00:06:44,670 --> 00:06:48,030
Now you can actually just put the variable name and you'll get number three.

87
00:06:48,030 --> 00:06:52,950
But I just want to show you an example similar to the way that we use it, if we were writing it in

88
00:06:52,950 --> 00:06:53,700
a script.

89
00:06:54,090 --> 00:06:59,370
Now, again, if we wanted to access the second element, which is seven, we can just put one and this

90
00:06:59,370 --> 00:07:00,420
will give me seven.

91
00:07:00,600 --> 00:07:06,360
If we want to access element number three, we can put two and this will give me eight.

92
00:07:06,990 --> 00:07:12,450
And if we want to access, for example, element one, two, three, four, five, which is this one

93
00:07:12,450 --> 00:07:16,320
we're going to put number four because the index starts at zero.

94
00:07:16,320 --> 00:07:19,380
So zero, one, two, three, four.

95
00:07:19,500 --> 00:07:23,310
Then if we put number four, we'll get number 24.

96
00:07:24,030 --> 00:07:26,490
You can also access lists backwards.

97
00:07:26,490 --> 00:07:33,540
So if I put the list name for input minus one in my square bracket, I will access the last element

98
00:07:33,540 --> 00:07:35,820
of the list, which is 24.

99
00:07:37,530 --> 00:07:37,860
Now.

100
00:07:37,860 --> 00:07:38,610
That's it for now.

101
00:07:38,610 --> 00:07:40,010
I don't want to keep this too long.

102
00:07:40,020 --> 00:07:44,700
There is a lot of ways that you can deal with lists and all that, and we'll cover that later on.

103
00:07:44,700 --> 00:07:48,810
But I just wanted to give you a quick overview of what lists are.

104
00:07:48,810 --> 00:07:52,080
And in the next lecture, we're going to continue with our algorithm.

105
00:07:52,080 --> 00:07:59,760
We're going to continue with our code and learn how to access the useful information from the list of

106
00:07:59,760 --> 00:08:01,710
the answered packets that we got.

107
00:08:03,320 --> 00:08:08,660
Now once done with using Python interpreter like this, you can just do exit.

108
00:08:09,500 --> 00:08:14,510
Two brackets, which is actually a function that you can use in Python to exit your program.

109
00:08:14,510 --> 00:08:20,150
But when you use it in the interpreter like this, it will exit you out of the interpreter.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,530 --> 00:00:07,280
Now that we have a basic idea of what lists are and how to use lists, let's go back to our code and

2
00:00:07,280 --> 00:00:09,350
put what we learned to practice.

3
00:00:09,890 --> 00:00:13,430
So the first thing that I want to modify is this line.

4
00:00:13,700 --> 00:00:16,370
Now we're going back to the ZP function.

5
00:00:16,370 --> 00:00:23,570
And again, if we go to the documentation page of KP, you'll see that this tells us that this function

6
00:00:23,570 --> 00:00:26,150
gives us two lists.

7
00:00:26,420 --> 00:00:31,940
So we have an answered list and an unanswered list.

8
00:00:32,360 --> 00:00:38,660
Now, as you can see in our code or only interested in the answered list, we're not interested in the

9
00:00:38,660 --> 00:00:39,920
unanswered list.

10
00:00:40,280 --> 00:00:45,890
So there is no point of just using a variable here that we'll never actually use in the code.

11
00:00:46,370 --> 00:00:49,040
So I'm going to remove this variable here.

12
00:00:49,130 --> 00:00:57,560
And then in order to only capture the answer list from the return result of this function, we're just

13
00:00:57,560 --> 00:01:01,760
going to put a zero between two square brackets at the end.

14
00:01:02,390 --> 00:01:08,930
So what this will do is instead of giving me two elements, element zero for the answered list and element

15
00:01:08,930 --> 00:01:15,440
one for the unanswered list, I'm only telling it to give me element zero because I'm only interested

16
00:01:15,440 --> 00:01:22,550
in the first element of this list, which, as we know, first element has an index of zero, and this

17
00:01:22,550 --> 00:01:25,250
element is the answered list element.

18
00:01:25,580 --> 00:01:30,770
So now if I go back to my code, it should give me the same result just to make sure that we didn't

19
00:01:30,770 --> 00:01:31,820
break the program.

20
00:01:32,600 --> 00:01:38,810
And as you can see, we're still able to capture the answer list, and we have all the answers in here.

21
00:01:39,500 --> 00:01:45,500
Now, the next thing that we want to do is to be able to extract the values for the Mac address and

22
00:01:45,500 --> 00:01:48,200
for the IP from the answer list.

23
00:01:48,200 --> 00:01:53,870
Because like I explained before, all we're doing right now is calling a method that's called summary

24
00:01:53,870 --> 00:01:57,140
that is already programmed in this copy module.

25
00:01:57,140 --> 00:02:02,030
And this method is just giving us the summary of all of the data in the answered list.

26
00:02:02,660 --> 00:02:04,850
So we're going to remove this line.

27
00:02:05,790 --> 00:02:13,410
And we're going to try to dissect or break the answered list and assess its elements individually.

28
00:02:14,410 --> 00:02:21,430
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to use a for loop to iterate over the values or the elements in

29
00:02:21,430 --> 00:02:22,720
the answered list.

30
00:02:23,080 --> 00:02:26,980
Now, I know you might be getting confused right now, but let me write it down.

31
00:02:26,980 --> 00:02:30,340
And actually when I write the code, it will just make sense to you.

32
00:02:30,910 --> 00:02:35,910
So what we're going to say, we're going to say four, which is a name for a loop.

33
00:02:35,920 --> 00:02:41,560
So we're going to say for each element and the answer list.

34
00:02:43,100 --> 00:02:46,490
I want you to print the element.

35
00:02:47,920 --> 00:02:48,400
Okay.

36
00:02:48,400 --> 00:02:50,600
So very, very simple code.

37
00:02:50,620 --> 00:02:53,770
Now, this is actually an example of using loops.

38
00:02:53,980 --> 00:02:59,290
There is a lot of ways to write loops in Python and there is a lot of uses for loops.

39
00:02:59,320 --> 00:03:06,280
Right now we're having an example of using a loop in order to iterate over a list, so to access all

40
00:03:06,280 --> 00:03:07,420
this elements.

41
00:03:07,810 --> 00:03:13,750
So what we're doing is we're saying for each element and this right here is just a variable name.

42
00:03:13,750 --> 00:03:15,970
You can use any name you want in here.

43
00:03:15,970 --> 00:03:18,940
I'm just using element because I think it makes sense.

44
00:03:19,480 --> 00:03:26,470
So we're saying for each element in the answered list and automatically when Python sees that you put

45
00:03:26,470 --> 00:03:32,860
a variable for a list after the end, it will automatically know that what you want to do is you want

46
00:03:32,860 --> 00:03:40,330
to access all list elements one by one individually, so it will automatically iterate over that list.

47
00:03:40,600 --> 00:03:47,110
So the code in here will be executed on each element in the list, one by one.

48
00:03:47,680 --> 00:03:54,940
So if you were doing this for our list in here of the lucky numbers, it will execute the print statement

49
00:03:54,940 --> 00:03:59,910
on three, on seven, on eight, on 17 and on 24.

50
00:03:59,920 --> 00:04:06,760
So it will literally print three, seven, eight, 17, 24 each on a separate line.

51
00:04:07,510 --> 00:04:10,290
Now what we're doing in here is a little bit different.

52
00:04:10,300 --> 00:04:16,720
We're actually accessing a special list of objects, and we're saying for each element in this list,

53
00:04:16,720 --> 00:04:18,460
print this element for me.

54
00:04:18,790 --> 00:04:21,490
Let's have an example and see what will this print for us?

55
00:04:24,340 --> 00:04:25,540
Now this is perfect.

56
00:04:25,540 --> 00:04:29,500
It's printing all of the data that we want in the answered list.

57
00:04:29,500 --> 00:04:34,600
And as you can see, there is actually more data now than what we seen in the summary, because the

58
00:04:34,600 --> 00:04:40,110
summary only prints a summary of what's in the packets or what's stored in the object.

59
00:04:40,120 --> 00:04:45,700
But what we're doing right now, we're literally printing all of the data stored in that variable.

60
00:04:46,060 --> 00:04:47,800
Now, this is really hard to read.

61
00:04:47,800 --> 00:04:53,410
So what I'm going to do is I'm actually going to add another print statement and I'm just going to print

62
00:04:53,410 --> 00:04:55,300
a line to separate entries.

63
00:04:55,960 --> 00:04:59,540
So what this is doing now, you can see how this loop works.

64
00:04:59,560 --> 00:05:05,440
It's going to, first of all, print the element, print a line, and then go back for the next element

65
00:05:05,440 --> 00:05:06,300
in the list.

66
00:05:06,310 --> 00:05:11,140
Print the element, print a line, go up again for the next element in the list.

67
00:05:11,140 --> 00:05:15,200
Print the element, print a line all the way up to the end of the list.

68
00:05:15,220 --> 00:05:17,320
No matter how long your list is.

69
00:05:17,830 --> 00:05:23,920
So going back, I'm going to do control to clear the screen and run Python again.

70
00:05:25,470 --> 00:05:30,030
And as you can see now, the result is a little bit more readable and it's a little bit nicer.

71
00:05:30,420 --> 00:05:34,930
And as you can see, we have in here the first element in our list.

72
00:05:34,950 --> 00:05:37,410
Then we have the second element in the list.

73
00:05:37,410 --> 00:05:39,870
We have third element and fourth element.

74
00:05:40,700 --> 00:05:46,730
Now let's go back to this KP module and understand how the answer list is structured.

75
00:05:47,150 --> 00:05:53,510
So going back in here on the documentation, you can see that it's telling us the first element is a

76
00:05:53,510 --> 00:05:54,800
list of couples.

77
00:05:54,800 --> 00:05:56,900
So this is our answered list.

78
00:05:57,110 --> 00:06:00,580
And in these couples, we have two more elements.

79
00:06:00,590 --> 00:06:07,620
The first element is the package sent and the next element is the answer for that packet.

80
00:06:07,640 --> 00:06:13,370
So the answer list itself contains a smaller list or a couple.

81
00:06:13,400 --> 00:06:19,430
The first element in that is the packet sent, so it's the packet that triggered that answer.

82
00:06:19,430 --> 00:06:22,040
