And the next element is the answer.

83
00:06:23,290 --> 00:06:28,810
So if we look back at this, you'll see that we have a comma in each entry.

84
00:06:28,810 --> 00:06:33,160
So we have a comma here, a comma here, comma here, and come here.

85
00:06:33,310 --> 00:06:39,130
And this comma separates the two smaller elements in the bigger answered list.

86
00:06:40,090 --> 00:06:46,720
And if you notice in here, just as stated in the documentation you have, the first element here is

87
00:06:46,720 --> 00:06:52,810
the request sent, and you can see that we always have the same request, so we're always sending a

88
00:06:52,810 --> 00:06:55,960
request to the Broadcast Mac address.

89
00:06:56,170 --> 00:07:00,310
But the difference is we're asking about different Mac addresses.

90
00:07:00,310 --> 00:07:03,490
So in here we were asking who has ten 0 to 1?

91
00:07:03,490 --> 00:07:08,950
And here we're asking who has ten zero to 2 to 3 and to seven in here.

92
00:07:09,850 --> 00:07:15,370
Then the second part of this smaller list is the answer.

93
00:07:15,370 --> 00:07:20,980
So in here we have the client ten 0 to 1 replying with its Mac address.

94
00:07:21,250 --> 00:07:26,290
Same in here we have the client at ten zero two to reply, replying with its Mac address.

95
00:07:26,290 --> 00:07:28,480
Same here and same here.

96
00:07:29,680 --> 00:07:33,790
Now, the only part that we're interested in, again, it's the answer.

97
00:07:34,060 --> 00:07:37,510
So I just want to be able to print this on its own.

98
00:07:37,990 --> 00:07:40,660
So it's the element of one.

99
00:07:40,660 --> 00:07:46,780
So it's element, and we're putting one between two square brackets because that is the second value

100
00:07:46,780 --> 00:07:50,470
in the element or the second element in the bigger element.

101
00:07:50,470 --> 00:07:54,820
So we're actually dealing with a nested loop or a couple inside the loop.

102
00:07:55,180 --> 00:08:02,500
So if we use Element Square bracket to one, it will actually print this packet right here.

103
00:08:02,500 --> 00:08:04,060
So let's see what happens.

104
00:08:06,580 --> 00:08:07,390
And perfect.

105
00:08:07,390 --> 00:08:13,540
As you can see, we actually see the packet itself being printed and as you can see, it actually doesn't

106
00:08:13,540 --> 00:08:18,730
make any sense for us because we see the raw packet printed for us in here.

107
00:08:19,180 --> 00:08:26,080
Now, just like we learned before, if we have a packet object, we can just do the show to see all

108
00:08:26,080 --> 00:08:28,950
of the fields in that packet.

109
00:08:28,960 --> 00:08:32,440
So again, if we go back now and print that.

110
00:08:33,340 --> 00:08:36,710
You'll see that each one of these packets have two layers.

111
00:08:36,730 --> 00:08:44,620
It has an Ethernet layer and an ARP layer, and we can see the fields that correspond to each of these

112
00:08:44,620 --> 00:08:45,370
layers.

113
00:08:45,730 --> 00:08:53,140
Now, looking at this, we know what's interesting for us is the P Z, which is the IP, which is the

114
00:08:53,140 --> 00:08:55,930
source IP that the packet was sent from.

115
00:08:55,930 --> 00:09:06,130
So this is the IP of the client that sent the packet to us and the W s RC, which is the hardware source,

116
00:09:06,130 --> 00:09:10,960
which is the MAC address of the client that sent the packet back to us.

117
00:09:11,440 --> 00:09:17,530
So to access this, all we have to do is just do we're already doing a print and we're going to print

118
00:09:17,530 --> 00:09:23,140
the P as RC to print the IP address of the source.

119
00:09:24,070 --> 00:09:32,530
And I'm going to do control D to try to duplicate the line and we're going to print the W as RC to print

120
00:09:32,530 --> 00:09:34,660
the MAC address of the client.

121
00:09:35,470 --> 00:09:37,450
Let's run this and see what happens.

122
00:09:38,550 --> 00:09:39,360
And perfect.

123
00:09:39,360 --> 00:09:42,840
As you can see, we got the eyepiece of the target.

124
00:09:45,070 --> 00:09:47,560
And their addresses right here.

125
00:09:48,580 --> 00:09:51,640
So that brings us to the end of step three.

126
00:09:51,640 --> 00:09:58,420
So we managed to pass the the values that are being captured in the answered list.

127
00:09:58,420 --> 00:10:05,350
We learned how to use loops to iterate over lists, and we got more comfortable with using lists, analyzing

128
00:10:05,350 --> 00:10:08,440
them and getting what we want from the list.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:07,240
And the last lecture we finished the third step of our algorithm and we were able to extract the useful

2
00:00:07,240 --> 00:00:09,940
data from the answered packets list.

3
00:00:10,720 --> 00:00:13,210
We actually even kind of did step forward.

4
00:00:13,240 --> 00:00:20,020
So we already are printing the results were already printing the MAC address and the IP on the screen.

5
00:00:20,200 --> 00:00:27,070
But in this lecture, I actually want to spend some time improving this print statement to print the

6
00:00:27,070 --> 00:00:28,770
results in a nice table.

7
00:00:28,780 --> 00:00:35,260
And as we do that, I think you'll get more comfortable with lists, with loops, and with dealing with

8
00:00:35,260 --> 00:00:38,470
strings and variables and printing them on screen.

9
00:00:39,130 --> 00:00:40,810
So let's go and do this.

10
00:00:40,810 --> 00:00:42,550
It's going to be an easy task.

11
00:00:42,580 --> 00:00:46,420
Basically, our script right now prints the results like this.

12
00:00:46,420 --> 00:00:52,540
So you can see we have the IP, the Mac address followed by a line, and then it goes ahead.

13
00:00:52,540 --> 00:00:55,540
It prints IP Mac address line and keeps going.

14
00:00:55,540 --> 00:00:58,110
And this is obviously done through this loop.

15
00:00:58,120 --> 00:01:05,710
So for each element, we print the IP, we print the Mac, we print a line, and then we go to the start

16
00:01:05,710 --> 00:01:06,580
of the loop.

17
00:01:07,060 --> 00:01:11,050
Now what I want to achieve, I want to achieve something similar to this.

18
00:01:11,050 --> 00:01:18,250
So this is a program in Carly called Net Discover and it allows us to list all the IP addresses and

19
00:01:18,250 --> 00:01:21,580
their associated Mac addresses in the current network.

20
00:01:21,850 --> 00:01:25,060
So it's something similar to what we built so far.

21
00:01:25,090 --> 00:01:30,580
It just prints the results in a way that's much nicer than the way that we do in our program.

22
00:01:30,940 --> 00:01:34,090
So let's try to achieve something similar to this.

23
00:01:34,540 --> 00:01:39,610
So the first thing that we need to do is we need to hide some of the clutter in here.

24
00:01:39,610 --> 00:01:44,290
So I don't want to show this finish to send 256 packets.

25
00:01:44,290 --> 00:01:45,920
I don't want to show this.

26
00:01:45,940 --> 00:01:49,150
All I want to show is the result in here.

27
00:01:49,840 --> 00:01:54,790
So all of this is actually being displayed by the SRP function.

28
00:01:55,390 --> 00:02:02,080
And to hide this data, we can instruct the ZP function to be less verbose.

29
00:02:02,140 --> 00:02:09,640
So all we have to do in here is just add an argument called verbose and set this to false.

30
00:02:11,470 --> 00:02:13,270
Now let's run our program.

31
00:02:14,980 --> 00:02:17,590
And as you can see, we see nothing on top.

32
00:02:17,590 --> 00:02:24,070
And then when the function finishes execution, we'll see the results that were printing in the loop.

33
00:02:24,550 --> 00:02:26,200
So this is pretty good.

34
00:02:26,230 --> 00:02:30,940
Now, the next thing that we want to do is we want to print a header.

35
00:02:30,940 --> 00:02:32,980
So we want to have something like this.

36
00:02:33,130 --> 00:02:38,620
So we want to print IP Mac address, and then after that we'll be printing the values.

37
00:02:38,890 --> 00:02:41,200
So at the start, let's just print the header.

38
00:02:41,440 --> 00:02:49,180
So we're going to print this outside of our loop because if we print this inside the loop, our print

39
00:02:49,180 --> 00:02:52,810
statement will be executed for each element in the loop.

40
00:02:52,810 --> 00:02:58,750
So the header will be printed four times or five times, depending on how many elements or how many

41
00:02:58,750 --> 00:03:01,420
responses we get in the answered list.

42
00:03:01,900 --> 00:03:08,740
So what I'm going to do, I'm going to print in here and I'm just going to print IP to show the IP,

43
00:03:09,040 --> 00:03:15,790
and then I want to print the MAC address, but I don't want to leave a space between them.

44
00:03:15,790 --> 00:03:18,460
I actually want to leave a number of spaces.

45
00:03:18,460 --> 00:03:20,950
So something like two or three tabs.

46
00:03:21,400 --> 00:03:28,090
So in order to add a top character in the print, you can do a backward slash followed by T to add a

47
00:03:28,090 --> 00:03:28,660
tab.

48
00:03:29,170 --> 00:03:35,620
So we're going to add four of these and then we're going to print Mac address.

49
00:03:36,190 --> 00:03:42,370
So the result of this is going to be IP for Tab's Mac address.

50
00:03:42,880 --> 00:03:45,700
So let's go and run this again.

51
00:03:46,600 --> 00:03:47,080
Sorry.

52
00:03:47,080 --> 00:03:47,920
In here.

53
00:03:48,900 --> 00:03:53,730
And as you can see, we have the IP first and then we have the MAC address in here.

54
00:03:54,150 --> 00:03:56,940
Now, I think four tabs is a little bit too much.

55
00:03:56,940 --> 00:03:57,960
So let's do this.

56
00:03:57,960 --> 00:03:59,370
Let's make it three tabs.

57
00:04:01,380 --> 00:04:02,550
Print it again.

58
00:04:04,240 --> 00:04:05,410
And yeah, this is good enough.

59
00:04:05,410 --> 00:04:08,320
So we have the IP, we have the MAC address.

60
00:04:09,280 --> 00:04:17,500
Now let's print a line so you can either put a backward slash end to go down a line and then print a

61
00:04:17,500 --> 00:04:18,640
line like this.

62
00:04:20,280 --> 00:04:23,190
Or you could put that in a different print statement.

63
00:04:23,190 --> 00:04:29,520
But I'm just going to do it in here just to show you the use of the slash T and slash N so the slash

64
00:04:29,520 --> 00:04:32,070
de can be used to print a tab.

65
00:04:32,100 --> 00:04:35,070
The slash end can be used to go to the next line.

66
00:04:35,400 --> 00:04:37,140
So the result of this.

67
00:04:38,110 --> 00:04:42,460
Is going to be IP Mac address and then we have a line.

68
00:04:42,460 --> 00:04:48,730
So even though we put this all in the same line since we added a slash and this will be printed on the

69
00:04:48,730 --> 00:04:52,860
next line, now that we're done with this, we're going to enter the loop.

70
00:04:52,870 --> 00:04:58,000
So the first thing that's going to be printed is going to be our Mac address, which is this.

71
00:04:58,750 --> 00:05:04,000
Then if we look at our table, we don't want to print the Mac address under it.

72
00:05:04,030 --> 00:05:08,140
We want to add three tabs and then print the MAC address in here.

73
00:05:08,530 --> 00:05:10,240
So we're going to go up.

74
00:05:11,470 --> 00:05:14,650
And we'll do something similar to this print statement.

75
00:05:14,680 --> 00:05:18,430
So first of all, we're going to append some string.

76
00:05:18,460 --> 00:05:23,620
And inside this string we're going to put two tabs actually, because we already have some text for

77
00:05:23,620 --> 00:05:28,660
the IP and followed by that we're going to put the MAC address.

78
00:05:28,670 --> 00:05:30,190
So I'm just going to cut this.

79
00:05:31,570 --> 00:05:32,650
Based it here.

80
00:05:34,310 --> 00:05:36,650
And remove these two print statements.

81
00:05:37,280 --> 00:05:44,750
So for each element, we're going to print the element IP, put two tabs, print the MAC address, end

82
00:05:44,750 --> 00:05:51,320
of our for loop, go to the next element to the same until we reach the end of the answer list.

83
00:05:51,980 --> 00:05:53,660
Let's see what we're going to get.

84
00:05:56,750 --> 00:05:57,560
And perfect.

85
00:05:57,560 --> 00:05:58,250
As you can see.

86
00:05:58,250 --> 00:06:03,230
Now, we're printing a table that has two columns under the I.P. column.

87
00:06:03,230 --> 00:06:10,130
We have the IPS and under the MAC address column, we have the MAC addresses that correspond to these

88
00:06:10,130 --> 00:06:11,390
IPS right here.

89
00:06:12,840 --> 00:06:17,460
Now that's the end of this lecture and it's actually the end of our algorithm.

90
00:06:17,460 --> 00:06:22,770
So we managed to achieve our goal, which is discover all clients on the same network.

91
00:06:22,770 --> 00:06:25,920
And the final stage was to print the results.

92
00:06:25,920 --> 00:06:29,160
And we are printing the results in a nice way right here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,120 --> 00:00:01,500
Och.

2
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:03,140
Now our script is done.

3
00:00:03,160 --> 00:00:04,360
It's doing its job.

4
00:00:04,360 --> 00:00:08,950
It's printing the IP addresses and the marks of the clients on the same network.

5
00:00:08,950 --> 00:00:11,200
It's doing this using a manual way.

6
00:00:11,200 --> 00:00:13,810
We're actually crafting the packet that we want to send.

7
00:00:13,810 --> 00:00:19,000
We're sending it ourselves, we're receiving the response and passing everything ourselves and then

8
00:00:19,000 --> 00:00:20,260
printing it on screen.

9
00:00:20,260 --> 00:00:21,610
So it's pretty cool.

10
00:00:21,970 --> 00:00:28,000
It's also using a function called scan where we're passing a variable of the IP that we want to scan

11
00:00:28,000 --> 00:00:30,820
to it, and it's doing this card based on our variable.

12
00:00:30,820 --> 00:00:32,410
So that's all good.

13
00:00:32,650 --> 00:00:39,100
You can use what you learned in the previous section to get this script, to accept user arguments and

14
00:00:39,100 --> 00:00:45,370
take the IP as an argument, and you can implement that exactly the same way as I covered it before.

15
00:00:45,370 --> 00:00:48,400
So it will actually be a good practice for you to do that.

16
00:00:48,910 --> 00:00:54,730
What I want to do though, I want to improve the code in here so the result of the program is going

17
00:00:54,730 --> 00:00:55,630
to be the same.

18
00:00:55,630 --> 00:00:58,420
But the code in here, I don't think it's good enough.

19
00:00:58,420 --> 00:01:01,240
I think we can do better as we do that.

20
00:01:01,240 --> 00:01:06,790
As usual, I'm going to be introducing you to new concepts and you're going to be learning more about

21
00:01:06,790 --> 00:01:07,510
Python.

22
00:01:08,050 --> 00:01:15,220
So what I want to do is I'd like to have a function that will do the scanning for me, return some kind

23
00:01:15,220 --> 00:01:22,210
of a variable or a data structure that contains the result of the scan and then have a completely separate

24
00:01:22,210 --> 00:01:24,610
function that will print the result.

25
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:25,480
Why?

26
00:01:25,480 --> 00:01:32,140
I'd like to do that because it's good to have functions that only do one task and do that task really

27
00:01:32,140 --> 00:01:32,740
well.

28
00:01:33,160 --> 00:01:38,890
The reason behind this is maybe in the future I'd want a function that will do the scanning for me,

29
00:01:39,010 --> 00:01:40,630
but not print a result.

30
00:01:40,630 --> 00:01:46,840
So maybe I'm using this in a big program and I've already printed the results before, or I'm doing

31
00:01:46,840 --> 00:01:52,540
multiple scans, so I only want to print this scan one time and then I don't want to print it in the

32
00:01:52,540 --> 00:01:53,410
other ones.

33
00:01:53,620 --> 00:01:59,080
So there's a lot of reasons that you might want to do this, and it's always when you're programming,

34
00:01:59,080 --> 00:02:04,990
it's always a good idea to break your program into a number of smaller functions with each function

35
00:02:04,990 --> 00:02:09,460
doing only one task instead of doing a number of tasks like we have in here.

36
00:02:09,460 --> 00:02:13,420
So we're doing the scanning and then we're doing the printing and one function.

37
00:02:13,870 --> 00:02:21,130
Now in order to achieve this, we need to return the answered list, captured whatever we're calling

38
00:02:21,130 --> 00:02:26,380
the program, and then pass this variable to the function that's going to do the printing.

39
00:02:26,830 --> 00:02:32,830
But as we know, the answered list contains a lot of information that's not useful for us.

40
00:02:32,830 --> 00:02:37,630
So we want this list to only contain the MAC address and the IP address.

41
00:02:38,080 --> 00:02:45,520
Therefore, I think it's a better idea to construct our own variable, our own list, and in that list

42
00:02:45,520 --> 00:02:47,830
only put the stuff that's useful to us.

43
00:02:47,830 --> 00:02:51,820
So only put the MAC address and the IP address.

44
00:02:52,030 --> 00:02:57,940
So in order to do that, we can use this for loop right here, which iterates over each element in the

45
00:02:57,940 --> 00:03:04,270
list and then store the IP address and the MAC address in the list so that the first element of the

46
00:03:04,270 --> 00:03:06,010
list is the IP address.

47
00:03:06,010 --> 00:03:09,160
And the second element of the list is the MAC address.

48
00:03:09,550 --> 00:03:15,910
Now this will work and it's perfectly fine, but first of all, you're going to end up with nested lists,

49
00:03:15,910 --> 00:03:17,980
so you're going to have one big list.

50
00:03:18,130 --> 00:03:24,340
And then each element of this list is a smaller list in which the first element is the IP address,

51
00:03:24,340 --> 00:03:26,500
and the second element is the MAC address.

52
00:03:27,370 --> 00:03:34,120
Alternatively, you can use a dictionary and I think using dictionaries is a better implementation of

53
00:03:34,120 --> 00:03:34,720
this.

54
00:03:35,140 --> 00:03:42,430
Not only that, but it'll also give me an opportunity to introduce you to dictionaries and get you using

55
00:03:42,430 --> 00:03:44,980
them in a practical, useful example.

56
00:03:46,080 --> 00:03:49,560
Basically you can think of dictionaries as lists.

57
00:03:49,680 --> 00:03:56,400
They're very similar to lists in a way that you have a variable, and that variable will correspond

58
00:03:56,400 --> 00:04:02,820
to a number of elements and you will be able to access all of these elements using this variable.

59
00:04:02,910 --> 00:04:05,550
The main difference is with lists.

60
00:04:05,550 --> 00:04:11,010
As you remember, we used to access the elements in the list based on their index.

61
00:04:11,010 --> 00:04:14,680
So if you wanted to access the first element, we use zero.

62
00:04:14,700 --> 00:04:19,560
If we want to access the third element, we use two, for example, and so on.

63
00:04:19,800 --> 00:04:25,830
So we access elements based on their location in the list and a dictionary.

64
00:04:25,830 --> 00:04:33,480
We define a key for each element, for each value, and we access the values based on the key.

65
00:04:33,810 --> 00:04:37,650
So first of all, when creating a dictionary, you use the curly brackets.

66
00:04:38,010 --> 00:04:42,850
And in this example, we're we're creating a dictionary called Target Client.

67
00:04:42,870 --> 00:04:48,660
So in this example, I'm just making a dictionary similar to what we might need to use in our program

68
00:04:48,960 --> 00:04:50,730
and the values in here.

69
00:04:50,730 --> 00:04:56,490
We have the MAC address, the IP address and the operating system of the client.

70
00:04:56,910 --> 00:05:00,870
And as you can see, for each value, we're defining a key.

71
00:05:01,080 --> 00:05:02,920
So we define a key.

72
00:05:02,940 --> 00:05:04,890
For example, here the key is Mac.

73
00:05:04,890 --> 00:05:08,970
We put a column and then we put the value, which is the Mac address.

74
00:05:09,390 --> 00:05:11,400
Then we can define an IP.

75
00:05:11,430 --> 00:05:18,150
We put a colon and we put the value for the IP and same goes for the operating system.

76
00:05:18,660 --> 00:05:26,640
So the interpretation of this is we have you can think of this as the variable target client and the

77
00:05:26,640 --> 00:05:30,210
keys in this dictionary are Mac IP OS.

78
00:05:30,420 --> 00:05:36,180
And the values that correspond to these keys are the MAC address, the IP address and the operating

79
00:05:36,180 --> 00:05:39,270
system of the specific target client.

80
00:05:40,820 --> 00:05:46,520
So in order to access the value for the Mac address, all we have to do is just type the name of the

81
00:05:46,520 --> 00:05:53,900
variable which is target client and between the two brackets, instead of specifying the index or the

82
00:05:53,900 --> 00:05:58,620
location of this value, which is what you would do if it was a list.

83
00:05:58,640 --> 00:06:03,680
But since this is a dictionary, you specify the key for this value, which is Mac.

84
00:06:04,400 --> 00:06:07,940
Again, if you want to access the IP, you just put the IP.

85
00:06:07,940 --> 00:06:11,300
And if you want to access the operating system, you just put OS.

86
00:06:12,630 --> 00:06:16,680
Now let's have a quick example of this in the Python interpreter.

87
00:06:16,890 --> 00:06:19,410
So I'm just going to type Python here.

88
00:06:20,300 --> 00:06:21,890
And we're going to create a variable.

89
00:06:21,890 --> 00:06:24,350
We'll create a variable similar to what we did there.

90
00:06:24,350 --> 00:06:29,540
So we're just going to call it target client and this variable is going to be a list.

91
00:06:29,540 --> 00:06:34,640
So I'm using curly brackets and we're going to define the first key.

92
00:06:34,640 --> 00:06:42,860
And this is going to be a mac address and we'll set the value, which is going to be 001122, three

93
00:06:42,860 --> 00:06:45,410
three, four, four, five five.

94
00:06:45,890 --> 00:06:53,030
Now, keep in mind, I'm using keys and values as strings, and therefore I'm enclosing them with the

95
00:06:53,030 --> 00:06:54,710
quotation marks in here.

96
00:06:55,340 --> 00:07:05,240
Now, the next key, we're going to set that to the IP and we'll set that to ten, 0 to 1, put a comma

97
00:07:05,240 --> 00:07:10,220
and define the next element, which is going to be my operating system.

98
00:07:10,220 --> 00:07:14,510
This is the key for the next element and then the value for the next element.

99
00:07:14,510 --> 00:07:16,340
We're just going to set it to Windows.

100
00:07:16,910 --> 00:07:18,500
So very, very simple.

101
00:07:18,500 --> 00:07:19,970
We have a variable name.

102
00:07:19,970 --> 00:07:22,010
We're making that equal to something.

103
00:07:22,070 --> 00:07:29,510
We created a dictionary because we enclose our elements between curly brackets this time, not square

104
00:07:29,510 --> 00:07:30,260
brackets.

105
00:07:30,290 --> 00:07:36,620
Remember, we were using square brackets for lists, but we use curly brackets for dictionaries.

106
00:07:37,070 --> 00:07:40,370
As you can see, we have three elements separated by comma.

107
00:07:40,940 --> 00:07:43,820
And the first part, we define the key.

108
00:07:43,850 --> 00:07:47,810
We put a colon and then we put the value for this key.

109
00:07:48,320 --> 00:07:53,180
So I'm going to hit Enter and then Python understand this with no problems because we don't see any

110
00:07:53,180 --> 00:07:53,900
errors.

111
00:07:54,260 --> 00:08:01,490
Now if we want to access values in this dictionary, I'm going to do print and in here I'm going to

112
00:08:01,490 --> 00:08:04,160
say I want to print the target client.

113
00:08:05,300 --> 00:08:08,870
And I'm actually just going to print it as is for now just to show you.

114
00:08:09,620 --> 00:08:11,810
So this is how Python looks at this.

115
00:08:11,810 --> 00:08:15,380
And as you can see, it's very similar to the way that I defined it.

116
00:08:15,890 --> 00:08:21,350
Now, let's say that we only want to access the IP value for this target client.

117
00:08:21,410 --> 00:08:25,760
So think of this as one element in the list that we're getting.

118
00:08:25,760 --> 00:08:28,490
So we're getting a number of clients in our code.

119
00:08:28,490 --> 00:08:29,840
In here we have four.

120
00:08:29,870 --> 00:08:32,870
So think of this as the first result.

121
00:08:33,140 --> 00:08:37,800
So let's say that I only want to access the IP address of this result.

122
00:08:37,820 --> 00:08:43,670
So all we're going to do is we're going to say, I want the target client and between the square brackets

123
00:08:43,700 --> 00:08:49,070
instead of put in the index, which is what we do, if it was a list, we're going to put the key,

124
00:08:49,070 --> 00:08:50,600
which is IP.

125
00:08:50,810 --> 00:08:52,070
I'm going to hit enter.

126
00:08:52,070 --> 00:08:55,490
And as you can see, I get the IP same.

127
00:08:55,490 --> 00:09:00,200
Let's say that we wanted to access the operating system for this specific client.

128
00:09:00,260 --> 00:09:06,740
Again, instead of using its index or its location, we're going to use the key, which is AWS and that'll

129
00:09:06,740 --> 00:09:08,330
return windows for me.

130
00:09:09,340 --> 00:09:12,520
Again, if you want to exit out of this, we just do exit.

131
00:09:12,520 --> 00:09:14,890
And that will take me out of the interpreter.

132
00:09:15,460 --> 00:09:17,500
And that's the end of this lecture.

133
00:09:17,530 --> 00:09:23,590
I just wanted to give you a quick overview of what dictionaries are, how to create one, how to use

134
00:09:23,590 --> 00:09:24,080
one.

135
00:09:24,100 --> 00:09:30,250
I know this is kind of a boring example because it's not really part of a useful program, but we'll

136
00:09:30,250 --> 00:09:35,710
put this in practice in the next lecture and you'll see how we can use this with the network scanner

137
00:09:35,710 --> 00:09:36,820
that we created.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,080 --> 00:00:01,530
Okay.

2
00:00:01,530 --> 00:00:07,440
Now that we know what dictionaries are and how to use them, let's put what we learned in practice.

3
00:00:07,770 --> 00:00:15,450
So from what I said in the previous lecture, my goal now is to have this function return a variable,

4
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:18,780
and that variable is going to contain a list.

5
00:00:19,200 --> 00:00:27,750
Each element in that list is going to contain an IP address of a client and a mac address of a client.

6
00:00:28,380 --> 00:00:36,240
So each element in this list is not a single value, it's not a single string, it's actually another

7
00:00:36,240 --> 00:00:37,020
structure.

8
00:00:37,020 --> 00:00:39,930
And that structure can be another list, like I said.

9
00:00:39,930 --> 00:00:42,420
But I said, that's not going to be very pretty.

10
00:00:42,420 --> 00:00:44,730
It's nicer to use a dictionary.

11
00:00:44,880 --> 00:00:51,660
So what we're going to create, we're going to create a list in which each element is a dictionary.

12
00:00:51,960 --> 00:00:55,140
So we're creating a list of dictionaries.

13
00:00:55,500 --> 00:00:57,000
Let me show you what I mean.

14
00:00:57,000 --> 00:01:01,530
I've made a quick visual representation of what I want to achieve.

15
00:01:01,920 --> 00:01:07,260
So right here you can think of this big table right here as a list.

16
00:01:07,650 --> 00:01:13,080
So as you can see, this has an index and we can access its element based on their index.

17
00:01:13,080 --> 00:01:17,310
So we can access list zero, list one and list two.

18
00:01:17,910 --> 00:01:20,310
Now each element in this list.

19
00:01:20,310 --> 00:01:26,490
So each value in this list is another structure and the structure is a dictionary.

20
00:01:26,490 --> 00:01:30,600
Now, so as you can see, it has a key and it has a value.

21
00:01:30,840 --> 00:01:38,970
So the zero element of the list has a dictionary, and this dictionary has values of MAC address and

22
00:01:38,970 --> 00:01:40,140
IP address.

23
00:01:40,200 --> 00:01:45,090
And for each of these values there is a key called Mac and IP.

24
00:01:45,540 --> 00:01:52,890
So, for example, to access the Mac address of the first element in the list, what we do is we do

25
00:01:52,890 --> 00:01:56,250
a list zero to select the first element.

26
00:01:56,250 --> 00:01:57,930
So literally select an on.

27
00:01:57,930 --> 00:02:02,280
If you actually print list zero, it will print all of this dictionary.

28
00:02:02,520 --> 00:02:04,710
But we're only interested in the Mac.

29
00:02:04,710 --> 00:02:08,850
So we put another square bracket and we specify the Mac.

30
00:02:09,390 --> 00:02:12,810
Now I'm going to cover accessing the elements in the next lecture.

31
00:02:12,900 --> 00:02:20,100
So for now, what we want to do is we want to create a big list in which each element is a dictionary,

32
00:02:20,490 --> 00:02:26,460
and this dictionary is going to contain the values for the MAC address and the IP address of the client.

33
00:02:26,940 --> 00:02:28,620
So let's see how we do this.

34
00:02:29,100 --> 00:02:35,910
So in our code we know the answer list is the list that contains all of the raw information.

35
00:02:35,910 --> 00:02:41,730
So it contains the information that we need and a lot of information that is not useful to us.

36
00:02:41,730 --> 00:02:45,090
So this is where we want to extract our information.

37
00:02:45,330 --> 00:02:52,410
And we also know that this list will iterate over each element in the answer list and we can capture

38
00:02:52,410 --> 00:02:54,240
the interesting stuff in here.

39
00:02:54,240 --> 00:02:57,240
We capture the IP and capture the MAC address.

40
00:02:57,870 --> 00:03:04,350
So to create the big list, we're going to have to create the big list outside of this loop because

41
00:03:04,350 --> 00:03:10,320
if we create it inside, we're going to be creating a list for every element in this variable in the

42
00:03:10,320 --> 00:03:13,110
answered list, and we don't want to do that.

43
00:03:13,590 --> 00:03:22,020
So I'm going to create my list outside in here, and I'm just going to call this client list and I'm

44
00:03:22,020 --> 00:03:28,800
going to make this equal to an empty list because I don't have any elements to add to it right now.

45
00:03:28,920 --> 00:03:32,160
So literally just saying I want to create a new variable.

46
00:03:32,160 --> 00:03:36,690
This variable is going to be called client list and it's going to be a list.

47
00:03:36,690 --> 00:03:42,930
But right now I have nothing to add to this list, so just create an empty list for me and I'm using

48
00:03:42,930 --> 00:03:46,890
square brackets in here because I want to use a list.

49
00:03:46,890 --> 00:03:50,940
If I was creating a dictionary, I'd use curly brackets.

50
00:03:51,720 --> 00:04:00,090
Now looking back at our diagram here we have the big list created and then for each element in this

51
00:04:00,090 --> 00:04:00,420
list.

52
00:04:00,420 --> 00:04:06,900
So for the zero, for the one, for the two, for each element, we want to create a dictionary.

53
00:04:07,110 --> 00:04:09,960
So as you can see here, for the zero, we have a dictionary.

54
00:04:09,960 --> 00:04:13,170
It has two keys, same for one, same for two.

55
00:04:13,590 --> 00:04:16,620
So we're going to do this inside our loop.

56
00:04:16,620 --> 00:04:21,720
So we have a loop that iterates over each element in our answers list.

57
00:04:21,720 --> 00:04:28,920
So the list that contains the useful information and the not useful information in this loop we're going

58
00:04:28,920 --> 00:04:30,870
to do for each element.

59
00:04:30,870 --> 00:04:38,850
I want to create a dictionary, so we're going to call this client dict to say that this is a dictionary

60
00:04:39,240 --> 00:04:45,150
and we're going to say that this is going to be equal to and we'll use curly brackets this time because

61
00:04:45,150 --> 00:04:49,230
we are creating a dictionary, just like I showed you in the previous lecture.

62
00:04:49,680 --> 00:04:53,760
And then we're going to set the key of the first element in this dictionary.

63
00:04:53,880 --> 00:04:57,420
Again, this is very similar to what we did in the previous lecture.

64
00:04:57,420 --> 00:04:59,700
So we're going to set a key, we're going to call it.

65
00:04:59,830 --> 00:05:00,460
IP.

66
00:05:01,240 --> 00:05:05,620
And the value for this is going to be the IP address in here.

67
00:05:05,800 --> 00:05:08,980
So it is what we used to print in our print statement.

68
00:05:08,980 --> 00:05:13,630
And that's why printing is very useful, because it allows us to make sure that we're going to use the

69
00:05:13,630 --> 00:05:17,830
right values in our data structures or in our code in general.

70
00:05:18,280 --> 00:05:20,320
So I'm going to paste that in here.

71
00:05:20,470 --> 00:05:24,580
And this is going to be the value for the IP key.

72
00:05:25,300 --> 00:05:31,750
Then I'm going to put a comma to give the next element or the second element in my client dictionary.

73
00:05:31,870 --> 00:05:37,690
And the next element is going to be the Mac address again, just like we did in the previous lecture.

74
00:05:38,320 --> 00:05:40,420
Then give it the value of the Mac.

75
00:05:40,420 --> 00:05:45,010
And again, we can get the value of the Mac using this variable right here.

76
00:05:45,960 --> 00:05:47,190
And we are done.

77
00:05:48,000 --> 00:05:54,840
So what we're doing right now is for each element and our big list, we're going to create a dictionary.

78
00:05:54,930 --> 00:06:00,300
This dictionary is going to contain a key for the IP and a key called Mac.

79
00:06:00,570 --> 00:06:06,270
And then we're given the values for the IP and for the Mac for this specific element.

80
00:06:07,260 --> 00:06:13,950
And finally, we want to add this as an element for the big list that we created in here.

81
00:06:14,340 --> 00:06:22,410
So what we're going to do, we're going to do client list, dot, append, client dict.

82
00:06:23,690 --> 00:06:27,470
So all we're doing in this line is we're adding the client dict.

83
00:06:27,470 --> 00:06:34,910
So basically we're adding this dictionary right here as an element to the big list and that's it.

84
00:06:34,910 --> 00:06:35,630
We're done.

85
00:06:35,690 --> 00:06:37,340
So what's going to happen now?

86
00:06:37,370 --> 00:06:39,080
We're going to reach this line.

87
00:06:39,140 --> 00:06:45,170
The answer list contains all the packets that contain useful information for us.

88
00:06:45,290 --> 00:06:51,080
So what's going to happen is in the for loop, we're going to go for each element in this big list that

89
00:06:51,080 --> 00:06:53,900
contains useful and not so useful data.

90
00:06:54,290 --> 00:06:56,270
We're going to create a dictionary.

91
00:06:56,540 --> 00:06:58,520
The dictionary will have two keys.

92
00:06:58,520 --> 00:07:01,550
It's going to have an IP key and a key.

93
00:07:01,580 --> 00:07:08,720
We're going to give the values, the IP and the Mac, and then we're going to add this dictionary as

94
00:07:08,720 --> 00:07:12,380
an element to the big list in here, the clients list.

95
00:07:13,160 --> 00:07:18,320
Now, once done with all of this, with appending our data, let's print our list.

96
00:07:18,320 --> 00:07:25,550
So I'm just going to do print in here and notice I put the print statement outside of the block of the

97
00:07:25,550 --> 00:07:26,060
four.

98
00:07:26,060 --> 00:07:31,160
So it's one tab back so that it doesn't get executed as part of the loop.

99
00:07:31,160 --> 00:07:35,990
So any code I put here will be executed after the loop is finished.

100
00:07:35,990 --> 00:07:41,600
So after the loop finishes iterating over all the elements in the answered list.

101
00:07:42,290 --> 00:07:49,130
So what I want to print in here, I want to print the clients list and let's see what values it has.

102
00:07:50,260 --> 00:07:52,720
I'm going to clear this and run my code.

103
00:07:53,820 --> 00:07:55,830
And this is all old code.

104
00:07:55,860 --> 00:07:57,520
This is what we got so far.

105
00:07:57,540 --> 00:07:59,730
So this is the new code that we added.

106
00:08:00,580 --> 00:08:07,210
So as you can see, we have only one big list that starts with the square bracket in here and ends with

107
00:08:07,210 --> 00:08:09,890
this square bracket inside it.

108
00:08:09,910 --> 00:08:13,840
We have a number of dictionaries, so this is our first dictionary.

109
00:08:14,200 --> 00:08:22,600
As you can see, it has an IP key and then a value for this IP key, a mark and a value for this key.

110
00:08:22,870 --> 00:08:25,270
This is the first element of the list.

111
00:08:25,450 --> 00:08:27,550
Then we have the next element.

112
00:08:28,300 --> 00:08:35,770
Again, it has an IP and a mark inside a dictionary, and same goes for all elements in this list.

113
00:08:36,460 --> 00:08:39,090
Now, this is exactly what I wanted to achieve.

114
00:08:39,100 --> 00:08:42,040
So it's exactly what you see here in this table.

115
00:08:42,070 --> 00:08:46,810
It's a very nice way of storing the useful data in a data structure.

116
00:08:47,200 --> 00:08:52,960
And in the next lecture, we'll see how we can iterate over this, how we can access the useful data

117
00:08:52,960 --> 00:08:54,580
and print it on screen.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,850 --> 00:00:07,120
Right now that we have our data stored in a nice list of dictionaries, let's see how we can access

2
00:00:07,120 --> 00:00:09,400
this data and print it on screen.

3
00:00:10,060 --> 00:00:17,020
Now, in the last lecture, I quickly touched on how you'd be able to access data within such structure,

4
00:00:17,140 --> 00:00:19,120
and you do that intuitively.

5
00:00:19,120 --> 00:00:23,350
So the first thing you want to do is we know that the big structure is a list.

6
00:00:23,350 --> 00:00:28,630
So you do list zero to access the zero element, and that's perfectly fine.

7
00:00:28,630 --> 00:00:34,450
You can actually just use this first element, this dictionary encode by doing list zero.

8
00:00:34,840 --> 00:00:40,720
Now if you want to access a specific part of this element, you would have to use the key because this

9
00:00:40,720 --> 00:00:42,340
element is a dictionary.

10
00:00:42,340 --> 00:00:49,420
So we'll do a list zero and follow that by mark or the name of the key that you want to access.

11
00:00:50,050 --> 00:00:51,880
Now, I'm actually not going to use that.

12
00:00:51,880 --> 00:00:54,550
You can test that on your own time and see how it works.

13
00:00:54,550 --> 00:01:01,420
What I'm going to do, I'm going to iterate over the list and the dictionary is using a for loop, so

14
00:01:01,420 --> 00:01:01,840
I'll show you.

15
00:01:01,840 --> 00:01:03,100
How am I going to do that?

16
00:01:03,610 --> 00:01:06,880
First of all, let's tidy up our code.

17
00:01:06,880 --> 00:01:12,850
The main reason why we're going through the trouble of storing everything in a list of dictionaries

18
00:01:12,850 --> 00:01:18,700
is so that we can have ID code, because I want to have a function that will do the scanning for me

19
00:01:18,700 --> 00:01:25,030
and return a list of dictionaries that contain the data and then have another function that will do

20
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:25,840
the printing.

21
00:01:25,840 --> 00:01:31,360
This will make our code nicer and it will also make it easier to re-use in the future.

22
00:01:31,990 --> 00:01:36,970
So the first thing that I'm going to do is remove this print statement because we're going to handle

23
00:01:36,970 --> 00:01:39,940
the printing in a different function.

24
00:01:40,720 --> 00:01:46,060
Also, we want this function to return the clients list not printed.

25
00:01:46,060 --> 00:01:48,310
So we're going to do return.

26
00:01:49,040 --> 00:01:50,270
Client list.

27
00:01:50,420 --> 00:01:56,630
And this way I'll be able to capture this variable whenever this function finishes execution and then

28
00:01:56,630 --> 00:01:58,850
pass it to my print function.

29
00:01:59,990 --> 00:02:06,470
So let's create our print function and we'll call it print result.

30
00:02:07,730 --> 00:02:14,030
And as you might already think, this is going to take a variable and we're going to call the variable

31
00:02:14,030 --> 00:02:15,530
results list.

32
00:02:17,480 --> 00:02:23,540
And the first thing that we'll do in this function is print this line, which will just print the IP

33
00:02:23,540 --> 00:02:26,220
and Mac address and put a line after it.

34
00:02:26,240 --> 00:02:29,950
Just the header that we used to print at the start of our print.

35
00:02:29,960 --> 00:02:34,250
So literally just this part of the output.

36
00:02:35,640 --> 00:02:40,260
Next we want to iterate over the list that we got.

37
00:02:40,260 --> 00:02:46,170
So the list that we're going to get is going to be pretty much this list right here that's created here.

38
00:02:46,290 --> 00:02:51,000
So we're going to want to iterate over each element in that list.

39
00:02:51,240 --> 00:02:57,510
So we're going to use a for loop just like we did before, and we're going to define a variable name.

40
00:02:57,510 --> 00:03:03,840
And here we call the element, we can call it element here, but just to make the code more readable

41
00:03:03,840 --> 00:03:08,580
and to make this easier to understand for you, I'm going to call this client.

42
00:03:09,150 --> 00:03:13,530
So we're saying for each client in my results list.

43
00:03:14,380 --> 00:03:17,290
I want to print this client.

44
00:03:18,970 --> 00:03:23,290
Now I want you to guess what we will get when we print this.

45
00:03:23,800 --> 00:03:30,850
Now, going back to our diagram, what we're doing is we're saying for each element in the results list.

46
00:03:30,850 --> 00:03:34,840
So this is the results list and we're saying for each element.

47
00:03:34,840 --> 00:03:36,160
So this is an element.

48
00:03:36,160 --> 00:03:42,850
This is an element and this is an element we're saying for each element, I want to print that element.

49
00:03:43,000 --> 00:03:48,430
So we should get dictionaries because each one of these elements is a dictionary.

50
00:03:48,430 --> 00:03:54,280
So we should get the dictionaries along with their keys and values printed on screen.

51
00:03:54,730 --> 00:03:56,560
So let's do that.

52
00:03:56,740 --> 00:04:03,100
Now in our main code, we're calling the scan function, which is this, but we modified this function

53
00:04:03,100 --> 00:04:04,810
to return a list.

54
00:04:05,110 --> 00:04:10,270
So what we're going to do, we're going to capture the value of the list that's being returned from

55
00:04:10,270 --> 00:04:14,500
this function in a variable called scan result.

56
00:04:16,480 --> 00:04:21,010
And then in the next line, we're going to call our print function.

57
00:04:21,010 --> 00:04:23,260
So we're going to say print result.

58
00:04:23,920 --> 00:04:27,040
And what I want to print is this can result.

59
00:04:28,390 --> 00:04:29,290
And that's it.

60
00:04:29,410 --> 00:04:31,060
So we're calling the scan.

61
00:04:31,090 --> 00:04:35,020
The scan will do the scan for us, return a list of dictionaries.

62
00:04:35,050 --> 00:04:37,360
Each element represents a client.

63
00:04:37,390 --> 00:04:38,590
We're capturing that.

64
00:04:38,590 --> 00:04:45,490
We're storing it in a variable called scan result, and then we're calling the print result, given

65
00:04:45,490 --> 00:04:47,950
it the scan result that we got.

66
00:04:48,430 --> 00:04:55,990
This function will iterate over each element in the list and it will print that element for us.

67
00:04:55,990 --> 00:05:00,820
This is not going to be the end result, but it's going to be something and then we'll work from there.

68
00:05:01,330 --> 00:05:03,100
So let's run our code.

69
00:05:04,910 --> 00:05:05,750
And perfect.

70
00:05:05,750 --> 00:05:09,740
As you can see, we're getting a dictionary for each element.

71
00:05:10,340 --> 00:05:16,670
And the first part of the dictionary, we can see the keys in here, the values, the keys again, and

72
00:05:16,670 --> 00:05:17,510
the values.

73
00:05:17,960 --> 00:05:20,600
Now, accessing these is going to be trivial.

74
00:05:20,630 --> 00:05:23,120
All we have to do is just print.

75
00:05:23,120 --> 00:05:30,140
Instead of saying client, we're going to say, I want to print the client and say IP between two square

76
00:05:30,140 --> 00:05:35,720
brackets to specify that I want the IP value of this dictionary.

77
00:05:37,170 --> 00:05:42,270
Then, just like we did before, I'm going to do a plus and put two tabs.

78
00:05:43,100 --> 00:05:48,830
Put another plus to append string and then we're going to print again the client.

79
00:05:48,830 --> 00:05:55,250
So it's a dictionary, but this time we want to print a different element in this dictionary and we

80
00:05:55,250 --> 00:05:59,570
want to use the key that is called Mark to access this element.

81
00:05:59,570 --> 00:06:02,480
And this will print the next element of my dictionary.

82
00:06:02,480 --> 00:06:04,730
So it's these values right here.

83
00:06:05,540 --> 00:06:11,480
Now we're going to save this, run the code and perfect.

84
00:06:11,480 --> 00:06:14,120
As you can see, we're getting our result.

85
00:06:14,120 --> 00:06:20,150
We have the IPS here, we have the clients here, and we managed to achieve that using two separate

86
00:06:20,150 --> 00:06:20,900
functions.

87
00:06:20,900 --> 00:06:26,690
And one of them does the scanning and return a list of dictionaries, and the other one will iterate

88
00:06:26,690 --> 00:06:29,300
over the result and print it on screen.

89
00:06:31,060 --> 00:06:32,730
So that's it for this lecture.

90
00:06:32,740 --> 00:06:38,920
I just wanted to, first of all, introduce you to dictionaries and then show you more examples of iterating

91
00:06:38,920 --> 00:06:45,070
and accessing values and lists in dictionaries or in lists of dictionaries and so on.

92
00:06:45,640 --> 00:06:50,560
Now, again, what you learn throughout this lecture, you can adapt it to anything you learn in the

93
00:06:50,560 --> 00:06:51,240
future.

94
00:06:51,250 --> 00:06:57,340
For example, the same ways that I showed you of accessing the elements and iterating and all of that,

95
00:06:57,340 --> 00:06:58,120
they apply.

96
00:06:58,120 --> 00:07:03,580
If you are using different data types, so you can use lists of other data types, you can use a list

97
00:07:03,580 --> 00:07:10,120
of any other objects that you want or use objects as values inside dictionaries and so on.

98
00:07:10,120 --> 00:07:16,090
And you'll be able to use them and access them and iterate over them exactly the same way that I'm showing

99
00:07:16,090 --> 00:07:17,080
you right here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,670 --> 00:00:06,520
Now, if you finish the exercise, you should have a function like this that returns the arguments that

2
00:00:06,520 --> 00:00:07,690
the user enters.

3
00:00:07,690 --> 00:00:13,600
And the main argument, or the only argument that it takes is an argument for the target.

4
00:00:14,110 --> 00:00:20,650
Now, I went one step further and I called the function in here in my main program exactly the way that

5
00:00:20,650 --> 00:00:25,450
I called it in the Mac changer example when we did it in the previous section.

6
00:00:25,450 --> 00:00:30,940
So I just have a variable called options that will capture the value for options when get arguments

7
00:00:30,940 --> 00:00:37,630
is called and then I'll pass passing options dot target to my scan function, which is the function

8
00:00:37,640 --> 00:00:43,210
that does the scan and that takes an IP as an argument or as an input.

9
00:00:43,600 --> 00:00:51,100
So I ended up with a program that I can run like, so I can just do Python program name, which is network

10
00:00:51,100 --> 00:00:59,590
scanner, and then I can do dash T or dash dash target, and after that I can give the IP that I want

11
00:00:59,590 --> 00:01:00,350
to scan.

12
00:01:00,350 --> 00:01:07,430
And in this example I'm going to give a range ten 0 to 1 over 24 to scan my whole subnet.

13
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:11,340
And as you can see, I'll get the results in here.

14
00:01:12,310 --> 00:01:14,500
Now this all works and it's all good.

15
00:01:14,530 --> 00:01:19,330
The only thing is the opt parse module in here is deprecated.

16
00:01:19,630 --> 00:01:22,960
What we mean by deprecated is it still works.

17
00:01:22,960 --> 00:01:24,990
It even works with Python three.

18
00:01:25,000 --> 00:01:31,630
But if the python developers wanted to add new features, they're not going to add it to this module.

19
00:01:31,630 --> 00:01:35,740
They're going to add it to a different module that came after this.

20
00:01:36,340 --> 00:01:41,950
Now, the reason why I showed this module is because I'm actually very used on using it and I've been

21
00:01:41,950 --> 00:01:45,310
using it even before they created the new module.

22
00:01:45,310 --> 00:01:49,340
And since this still works, I'm just used to always use this.

23
00:01:49,360 --> 00:01:51,820
Now I'm not the only programmer that does that.

24
00:01:51,820 --> 00:01:59,050
There are lots of programs and scripts online that still use the opt parse, hence why Python made sure

25
00:01:59,050 --> 00:02:04,480
that it still works even in Python three and hence why I taught it to you because you might be looking

26
00:02:04,480 --> 00:02:09,220
at some code written by some other programmer and they might be using optparse.

27
00:02:09,490 --> 00:02:16,090
Now if you want to use the successor of Optparse, it's called Argparse and I'm going to include its

28
00:02:16,090 --> 00:02:18,760
manual in the resources of this lecture.

29
00:02:18,760 --> 00:02:25,570
But using it is actually very, very simple and very, very similar to using Optparse and you'll see

30
00:02:25,570 --> 00:02:26,380
why now.

31
00:02:26,950 --> 00:02:32,410
So in order to use Argparse instead of importing Optparse, we're going to import Argparse.

32
00:02:33,600 --> 00:02:39,240
And then when you're creating the parser here, instead of doing Optparse, obviously we're going to

33
00:02:39,240 --> 00:02:41,790
say Argparse, which is the name of our module.

34
00:02:42,570 --> 00:02:49,140
And instead of saying option parser, the name of the class in this case is called Argument parser.

35
00:02:49,980 --> 00:02:51,990
Again, you can see the similarities.

36
00:02:51,990 --> 00:02:53,130
It's identical.

37
00:02:53,130 --> 00:02:57,150
The only thing we're doing is just changing the module name and the class name.

38
00:02:57,570 --> 00:03:03,930
Next, when you want to add an option, instead of saying dot add option, we're going to say dot add

39
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:04,740
argument.

40
00:03:06,150 --> 00:03:06,780
Again.

41
00:03:06,780 --> 00:03:08,430
Everything in here stays the same.

42
00:03:08,430 --> 00:03:10,140
We don't need to change any of that.

43
00:03:10,260 --> 00:03:16,260
And then in here, when we're calling parse args to parse the arguments again, this is the same.

44
00:03:16,260 --> 00:03:17,910
We don't need to change any of that.

45
00:03:17,940 --> 00:03:22,250
The only difference is in this case it only returns the options.

46
00:03:22,260 --> 00:03:24,210
It doesn't return the arguments.

47
00:03:24,210 --> 00:03:26,640
So we can remove this in here.

48
00:03:28,030 --> 00:03:30,700
And just return the options at the end.

49
00:03:30,940 --> 00:03:34,930
Now, this code will work, so if we just go down and run it.

50
00:03:36,380 --> 00:03:40,910
Again, as you can see, it's still working and it works exactly the same.

51
00:03:40,910 --> 00:03:43,550
The methods are still called exactly the same.

52
00:03:43,550 --> 00:03:46,310
The only difference is they're called different names.

53
00:03:46,310 --> 00:03:52,790
And again, the only difference between this module and the Optparse is that Optparse is deprecated.

54
00:03:52,790 --> 00:03:57,920
So whenever Python developers want to add new features, they're going to be adding it to Argparse,

55
00:03:57,950 --> 00:03:59,210
not Optparse.

56
00:03:59,210 --> 00:04:04,190
But Optparse works with both Python two and even Python three.

57
00:04:04,190 --> 00:04:06,230
So that's very important to note.

58
00:04:06,820 --> 00:04:10,540
Now you can actually use our code exactly the same way it is.

59
00:04:10,570 --> 00:04:15,860
So the program that we wrote will work on both Python two and Python three by default.

60
00:04:15,880 --> 00:04:18,340
You actually don't need to modify anything.

61
00:04:18,370 --> 00:04:24,460
So if I just type Python three here to run my program with Python three, you'll see that the program

62
00:04:24,460 --> 00:04:28,930
will work exactly the same way that it used to work with Python 2.7.

63
00:04:30,140 --> 00:04:31,910
So that's it for this lecture.

64
00:04:32,060 --> 00:04:34,280
I just wanted to give you two quick notes.

65
00:04:34,280 --> 00:04:37,430
And as we did this, we learned a few useful things.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 5: Writing an ARP Spoofer

1
00:00:01,390 --> 00:00:09,100
In the previous section, we learned what AARP is, how it works, and how we can use it to discover

2
00:00:09,100 --> 00:00:12,490
all the connected clients on the same network.

3
00:00:13,800 --> 00:00:19,410
In this section, I want to focus on building an ARP spoofing program.

4
00:00:20,340 --> 00:00:28,500
Now ARP spoofing allows us to redirect the flow of packets, so instead of it flowing as shown in this

5
00:00:28,500 --> 00:00:32,910
diagram, it would flow through my own computer.

6
00:00:33,510 --> 00:00:42,420
So any requests sent and any responses received by the target computer will have to flow through the

7
00:00:42,420 --> 00:00:43,650
hacker computer.

8
00:00:44,340 --> 00:00:52,830
This means that any messages, any websites and images and usernames and passwords entered by the target

9
00:00:52,830 --> 00:00:55,290
will have to flow through my computer.

10
00:00:55,950 --> 00:01:00,690
This allows me to read this information, modify it, or drop it.

11
00:01:01,560 --> 00:01:06,240
So as you can see, this is a very serious and very powerful attack.

12
00:01:06,360 --> 00:01:13,170
And the reason why it is possible is because r.p is not very secure.

13
00:01:14,010 --> 00:01:21,000
Now, for us to understand how this works, you need to have a basic understanding of what R.P. is.

14
00:01:21,000 --> 00:01:25,880
And I covered that at the start of the network scanner section.

15
00:01:25,890 --> 00:01:31,020
So if you don't remember how ERP works, please go back and revise that lecture.

16
00:01:31,560 --> 00:01:40,620
But basically, ERP is used so that clients can identify other connected clients on the same network

17
00:01:40,620 --> 00:01:42,450
and get their MAC address.

18
00:01:42,450 --> 00:01:49,950
Because, as I said before, clients connect with each other on the same network using the Mac address.

19
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:59,790
So each computer have an ARP table which links IP addresses on the same network to their MAC addresses.

20
00:02:00,780 --> 00:02:07,770
So if I go on the machine and do RPA, you can see my ARP table here.

21
00:02:07,770 --> 00:02:12,840
And as you can see, it's linking the Router's IP to the Router's Mac address.

22
00:02:13,880 --> 00:02:14,330
Now.

23
00:02:14,330 --> 00:02:19,070
Same if I go to the Windows machine and run my CMD.

24
00:02:20,420 --> 00:02:28,430
And do AARP a you'll see again, it's linking the Router's IP to its MAC address.

25
00:02:29,060 --> 00:02:36,140
So this machine, any time it needs to send any request to the Internet, it will direct that request

26
00:02:36,140 --> 00:02:43,250
to this MAC address, to the MAC address that's associated with the IP of the router, which is ten

27
00:02:43,250 --> 00:02:44,360
0 to 1.

28
00:02:45,560 --> 00:02:53,660
Now this value in here can be easily modified by exploiting the ARP protocol.

29
00:02:54,260 --> 00:03:02,420
So let me go back to my diagrams and right here we have a diagram of a typical network and you can see

30
00:03:02,420 --> 00:03:09,080
that normally any device that's connected to the network, if it wants to send the requests, it will

31
00:03:09,080 --> 00:03:10,460
send them to the router.

32
00:03:10,460 --> 00:03:16,400
The router will go and send that request to the internet, wait for the response, and then forward

33
00:03:16,400 --> 00:03:19,100
the response to the device that requested it.

34
00:03:19,400 --> 00:03:25,760
So if the hacker or the victim or any other computer on the network wanted to send a request, they

35
00:03:25,760 --> 00:03:29,630
will send that request directly to the router.

36
00:03:30,560 --> 00:03:40,430
Now, what we can do is we can exploit the ARP protocol and send two ARP responses one to the gateway

37
00:03:40,430 --> 00:03:42,440
and one to the victim.

38
00:03:43,160 --> 00:03:47,810
We're going to tell the gateway that I am at the IP of the victim.

39
00:03:48,290 --> 00:03:56,480
So the access point will update its ARP table and it will associate the IP of the target with my MAC

40
00:03:56,480 --> 00:03:57,200
address.

41
00:03:58,120 --> 00:04:02,780
We'll do the same with the victim, so we'll send it an ARP response.

42
00:04:02,780 --> 00:04:11,240
We're going to tell it that I am at ten 0 to 1, so it's going to update its ARP table and associate

43
00:04:11,240 --> 00:04:16,370
the IP of ten 0 to 1 with my own MAC address.

44
00:04:16,820 --> 00:04:23,510
So the result of this, the victim is going to think that I am the router and the router is going to

45
00:04:23,510 --> 00:04:25,310
think that I am the victim.

46
00:04:26,030 --> 00:04:32,960
So any time the victim wants to send any requests, the requests will have to flow through my computer

47
00:04:32,960 --> 00:04:35,390
and I'm going to forward them to thereafter.

48
00:04:35,750 --> 00:04:42,680
And then any time the access point or the router wants to send responses, they're going to go to my

49
00:04:42,680 --> 00:04:49,790
machine because it thinks that I am the victim and then I'm going to forward it to the victim.

50
00:04:50,600 --> 00:04:56,990
So as you can see, this puts me in the middle of the connection and it gives me so much power and we'll

51
00:04:56,990 --> 00:05:00,830
see all the things that we can do once we become the man in the middle.

52
00:05:03,060 --> 00:05:12,600
Now, the main reason why we can do all of this is because AARP is not secure, because, first of all,

53
00:05:12,630 --> 00:05:17,640
clients can accept responses even if they did not send the request.

54
00:05:17,820 --> 00:05:23,160
So, as I said before, we're going to send the response to the access point and the response to the

55
00:05:23,160 --> 00:05:30,450
victim telling them that I am at a specific IP without them asking, who am I or without them asking

56
00:05:30,450 --> 00:05:31,510
for this IP?

57
00:05:31,530 --> 00:05:35,880
I'm just going to send a response and they're going to accept that response anyway.

58
00:05:36,780 --> 00:05:41,490
Not only that, but they're also not going to verify who I am.

59
00:05:41,580 --> 00:05:49,500
So when I say that I am at ten 027, I am clearly not at that IP because this computer is at this IP.

60
00:05:49,620 --> 00:05:56,550
But the access point will trust this and it will actually update its AARP table based on the information

61
00:05:56,550 --> 00:05:57,450
that I send.

62
00:05:58,200 --> 00:05:59,750
Same goes to the victim.

63
00:05:59,760 --> 00:06:03,000
I'm going to tell it that I am at ten 0 to 1.

64
00:06:03,030 --> 00:06:09,570
It's going to trust and believe this, even though I am clearly not at this IP because the access point

65
00:06:09,570 --> 00:06:10,710
is at this IP.

66
00:06:11,730 --> 00:06:20,070
So these are the two main weaknesses with AARP protocol that allow us to run AARP spoofing attacks.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,420 --> 00:00:07,620
Now that we understand the theory behind ARP spoofing attacks, it should be easy enough for us to implement

2
00:00:07,620 --> 00:00:09,030
it using Python.

3
00:00:09,480 --> 00:00:16,980
But before we get into writing our own ARP spoofing tool, I want to spend just one lecture showing

4
00:00:16,980 --> 00:00:20,890
you how to practically run an AARP spoofing attack.

5
00:00:20,910 --> 00:00:24,630
Just to give you an idea of what we want to achieve.

6
00:00:25,470 --> 00:00:31,650
Now Kali Linux comes with a number of tools that allow us to run an ARP spoofing attack.

7
00:00:32,520 --> 00:00:37,960
There is a tool called ARP spoof, and using this tool is very easy.

8
00:00:37,980 --> 00:00:43,380
All we have to do is specify the interface that we want to run this attack through.

9
00:00:43,380 --> 00:00:50,050
And in my case, it's ETA zero because that's the interface that's connected to the network.

10
00:00:50,070 --> 00:00:58,560
So if I do, if config, you'll see ITI zero is the interface that's connected and we're going to do

11
00:00:58,920 --> 00:01:01,410
T to specify the target.

12
00:01:01,410 --> 00:01:06,450
And my target is at ten 0 to 7.

13
00:01:07,400 --> 00:01:10,700
As you can see in here, that's the IPO of my target.

14
00:01:11,750 --> 00:01:20,030
And I'm going to have to give it the IP of the gateway, which is at ten 0 to 1, as you can see in

15
00:01:20,030 --> 00:01:20,600
here.

16
00:01:21,470 --> 00:01:26,060
Now, this will spoof the target, telling him that I am the Raptor.

17
00:01:26,660 --> 00:01:30,440
We will also need to run this command once more in here.

18
00:01:30,530 --> 00:01:32,540
So I'm going to clear the screen.

19
00:01:32,540 --> 00:01:38,870
And again, I'm going to do IRP spoof eight zero.

20
00:01:38,870 --> 00:01:46,400
And the target this time is going to be ten, zero, two, one and ten 027.

21
00:01:47,270 --> 00:01:52,670
So right now, we're going to be telling the Raptor that I am the victim.

22
00:01:52,880 --> 00:01:55,120
So the first one will fool the victim.

23
00:01:55,130 --> 00:01:57,200
The second one will fool the Raptor.

24
00:01:58,040 --> 00:02:05,540
Now, keep in mind, this attack will work against both Ethernet and wi fi or wireless networks.

25
00:02:05,720 --> 00:02:12,350
I'm running it right now against the virtual network, which acts as an Ethernet or a wired network.

26
00:02:12,350 --> 00:02:18,050
But the attack can be executed exactly the same against wireless networks.

27
00:02:18,080 --> 00:02:24,170
All you have to do is connect a wireless adapter to the machine, connect the adapter to the target

28
00:02:24,170 --> 00:02:25,770
network and use it.

29
00:02:25,790 --> 00:02:29,070
So the same concept as the network scanner applies.

30
00:02:29,090 --> 00:02:34,700
You need to have a wireless adapter that works well with Kelly and you need to have that adapter connected

31
00:02:34,700 --> 00:02:37,400
to Kelly and connect it to the Target network.

32
00:02:38,430 --> 00:02:43,200
So I'm going to hit enter here and I'm going to hit enter here.

33
00:02:43,950 --> 00:02:52,890
And now if we go to the target machine and run the same command, RPA, you're going to see that the

34
00:02:52,890 --> 00:02:57,240
Mac address now for the router is different than what it was.

35
00:02:58,400 --> 00:03:01,760
And this is actually the Mac address of the Carli machine.

36
00:03:02,090 --> 00:03:09,170
So right now, this Windows machine thinks the router is at this Mac address, and every time it needs

37
00:03:09,170 --> 00:03:15,560
to send requests, it will send it to this MAC address, which means that they will be sent to this

38
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:17,120
computer right here.

39
00:03:18,170 --> 00:03:20,840
Now this computer is not a raptor.

40
00:03:20,840 --> 00:03:26,490
So when it gets requests, it's actually going to stop them from flowing and going to the router.

41
00:03:26,510 --> 00:03:33,500
This is a security feature in Linux, so you need to enable port forwarding so that this computer would

42
00:03:33,500 --> 00:03:37,010
allow packets to flow through it, just like a router.

43
00:03:37,670 --> 00:03:42,110
Now to enable port forwarding, we're going to do echo one.

44
00:03:43,110 --> 00:03:44,740
To proxies.

45
00:03:44,760 --> 00:03:48,180
Net IP version for IP forward.

46
00:03:49,200 --> 00:03:52,860
And as you can see, this command gets executed with no issues.

47
00:03:52,860 --> 00:03:57,060
And right now this computer will still have its Internet access.

48
00:03:57,060 --> 00:04:00,390
So we can go and browse any website we want.

49
00:04:01,080 --> 00:04:09,720
But all these requests are not going directly to the router, but they are going to this computer first

50
00:04:09,720 --> 00:04:16,620
and then this computer is forwarding them to the router as shown in this diagram.

51
00:04:16,620 --> 00:04:22,050
And then when the responses come back, they're going to go to the hacker first and then they go to

52
00:04:22,050 --> 00:04:22,860
the victim.

53
00:04:23,910 --> 00:04:30,840
Now, ARP spoof is a prebuilt tool that comes with Kali, so we will not be using it in this course.

54
00:04:31,020 --> 00:04:38,340
What I want to do in this section is build a program that will run this attack for us exactly like ARP

55
00:04:38,340 --> 00:04:45,660
spoof did right here, so that we can redirect the flow of packets and fool this computer into thinking

56
00:04:45,660 --> 00:04:47,100
that we are the router.

57
00:04:48,000 --> 00:04:52,560
Once we're done with this program, we're actually going to build more on it and you're going to see

58
00:04:52,560 --> 00:05:00,300
how we can inject code in the browser of the target, how we can still usernames, passwords, see all

59
00:05:00,300 --> 00:05:06,990
the information that the person sends and receives, replace downloaded files with Trojans and much,

60
00:05:06,990 --> 00:05:07,800
much more.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,640 --> 00:00:06,850
So from the previous lecture, we know in order to become the man in the middle, as shown in this diagram,

2
00:00:06,850 --> 00:00:14,080
and exploit the ARP protocol and redirect the flow of packets so that it flows through our computer,

3
00:00:14,200 --> 00:00:21,070
we need to send ARP responses to the router and to the target computer.

4
00:00:21,430 --> 00:00:26,410
When we send it to the target computer, we pretend to be the router and we tell the router that we

5
00:00:26,410 --> 00:00:27,880
are the target computer.

6
00:00:28,240 --> 00:00:34,300
So in this lecture we're going to learn how to create an ARP response using Python and send it to the

7
00:00:34,300 --> 00:00:40,510
target so that we can poison their ARP table, which will put us in the middle of the connection.

8
00:00:41,350 --> 00:00:45,490
So as you might already guess, we're going to be using Skype.

9
00:00:45,580 --> 00:00:52,390
Now, I've already created a new project called ARP Spoof and I'm going to import Skippy exactly the

10
00:00:52,390 --> 00:00:54,910
same way that we did it in the previous program.

11
00:00:54,910 --> 00:00:59,050
So we're just going to do import CPT all as Skippy.

12
00:01:00,260 --> 00:01:03,200
And then we're going to create our IRP packet.

13
00:01:03,770 --> 00:01:13,400
So we're going to say packet is equals to escapee dot arp and between the two brackets we're going to

14
00:01:13,400 --> 00:01:16,910
set the fields or the options for this packet.

15
00:01:17,240 --> 00:01:22,850
Now, like I showed you before, when you're not sure about the fields that you can set, we can just

16
00:01:22,850 --> 00:01:25,040
go to our python interpreter.

17
00:01:25,040 --> 00:01:30,440
So just in terminal, we type python and then we're going to import Skippy in here the same way that

18
00:01:30,440 --> 00:01:31,940
we import it in scripts.

19
00:01:31,940 --> 00:01:35,540
So we're going to say import CPT all as copy.

20
00:01:37,300 --> 00:01:44,890
And now to get a list of all the fields that we can set for an AARP packet, we can just say Skippy,

21
00:01:45,460 --> 00:01:48,250
the dot, liz, which is short for list.

22
00:01:48,580 --> 00:01:53,980
And between the two brackets we're going to say skippy dot arp.

23
00:01:54,340 --> 00:02:01,780
So basically saying we want to list all the fields that we can set for skippy AARP class, which is

24
00:02:01,780 --> 00:02:04,780
the class that we're going to use to create the packet.

25
00:02:05,410 --> 00:02:10,570
So if I hit enter now, you'll see I get a list of all the fields that I can set.

26
00:02:11,110 --> 00:02:13,540
So let me, first of all, exit out of this.

27
00:02:15,160 --> 00:02:18,310
And the first thing that I want to set is the o.P.

28
00:02:18,880 --> 00:02:25,750
Now this is set to one by default, and basically this means by default when you create an IP packet,

29
00:02:25,750 --> 00:02:28,870
you're going to be creating an ARP request.

30
00:02:29,170 --> 00:02:35,530
But as explained in the previous lecture, we need to create an ARP response, not a request.

31
00:02:35,530 --> 00:02:38,260
So we need to set the value of this to two.

32
00:02:38,740 --> 00:02:45,280
So I'm going to go up to my script and in here I'm going to set the o.P to two, I'm going to put a

33
00:02:45,280 --> 00:02:45,910
comma.

34
00:02:46,330 --> 00:02:56,170
And the next option that I want to set is the P DST, which is the IP field, which is the IP of the

35
00:02:56,170 --> 00:02:57,640
target computer.

36
00:02:58,210 --> 00:03:03,490
So in this example, I'm going to be targeting this one DDoS machine which is connected to the same

37
00:03:03,490 --> 00:03:05,410
network as the Cally machine.

38
00:03:05,410 --> 00:03:13,420
Therefore, I need to use its IP in the IP field now in order to get its IP, we can actually use the

39
00:03:13,420 --> 00:03:16,450
network scanner that we created in the previous section.

40
00:03:16,750 --> 00:03:22,360
So I'm already in the directory where the network scanner is stored, so all I need to do is just do

41
00:03:22,360 --> 00:03:30,850
Python Networks Canada Spy and set the target to my subnet over 24 to scan the whole network for the

42
00:03:30,850 --> 00:03:32,110
connected clients.

43
00:03:34,180 --> 00:03:39,910
Now, as you can see, I get a list of all the connected clients and I actually know that the Windows

44
00:03:39,910 --> 00:03:41,800
Machine is this one right here.

45
00:03:42,430 --> 00:03:44,230
So I'm going to copy this.

46
00:03:45,070 --> 00:03:46,970
And I'm going to set the P.

47
00:03:47,240 --> 00:03:51,910
S T to this IP and I'm going to make sure that this is a string.

48
00:03:51,910 --> 00:03:54,790
So I'm going to put it between two quotation marks.

49
00:03:55,750 --> 00:03:59,680
The next thing that I need to set is the Target Mac address.

50
00:03:59,680 --> 00:04:02,170
So the MAC address of the target computer.

51
00:04:02,500 --> 00:04:07,720
Now this can be set using the DST, which is the MAC field.

52
00:04:07,720 --> 00:04:12,400
And again, I have it in here using the program that we created in the previous lecture.

53
00:04:13,650 --> 00:04:20,340
So I'm going to say h w d estes equal to and put it between quotation marks as well.

54
00:04:20,340 --> 00:04:21,540
So it's a string.

55
00:04:22,260 --> 00:04:27,030
And finally, we're going to set the source that this packet is coming from.

56
00:04:27,030 --> 00:04:28,770
So we're actually forging this.

57
00:04:28,770 --> 00:04:34,650
This is going to be false information because this packet is coming from my own computer, but I'm going

58
00:04:34,650 --> 00:04:38,370
to tell the target computer that this is coming from the router.

59
00:04:38,370 --> 00:04:40,410
So I'm going to set the source field.

60
00:04:40,410 --> 00:04:45,540
And if we go down and look for the source field, you'll see it right here.

61
00:04:45,780 --> 00:04:48,750
We're going to set that to the IP of the router.

62
00:04:48,960 --> 00:04:54,960
Now we have the IP of the router here or you can run route RN to get it.

63
00:04:55,740 --> 00:04:58,800
And as you can see, it's right here, ten 0 to 1.

64
00:04:59,900 --> 00:05:07,850
So we're going to set the PSC to the IP of the router, which is ten 0 to 1.

65
00:05:09,010 --> 00:05:09,690
And that's it.

66
00:05:09,700 --> 00:05:11,470
Our packet is done.

67
00:05:12,010 --> 00:05:14,740
So let's go over this one more time.

68
00:05:14,950 --> 00:05:23,170
So first of all, we are creating an ARP packet here using CPT ARP and restoring the value or the contents

69
00:05:23,170 --> 00:05:26,020
of this packet in a variable called packet.

70
00:05:26,680 --> 00:05:34,330
Now we're setting the fields of this packet in a way so that it can be used to redirect the flow of

71
00:05:34,330 --> 00:05:36,250
packets through our computer.

72
00:05:36,760 --> 00:05:42,610
So what we're doing in this particular line is we're basically sending a packet to the victim saying,

73
00:05:42,610 --> 00:05:44,620
I have the Router's Mac address.

74
00:05:45,280 --> 00:05:51,370
We're doing this by, first of all, setting the op field to two so that this will be sent as an ARP

75
00:05:51,370 --> 00:05:53,080
response, not a request.

76
00:05:53,650 --> 00:05:58,630
We're set in the destination IP, which is the IP of the target windows machine.

77
00:05:58,960 --> 00:06:05,200
We're set in the hardware destination, which again is the MAC address of the target machine.

78
00:06:05,860 --> 00:06:10,690
And finally, we're setting the source IP to the IP of the router.

79
00:06:10,900 --> 00:06:17,440
So whenever this packet is sent and received by the target computer, they'll see that it's coming from

80
00:06:17,440 --> 00:06:23,590
the Mac address of the machine, but they'll think this is sent by this IP, which is the router is

81
00:06:23,590 --> 00:06:31,450
IP, and therefore in its ARP table it will associate this IP, which is the router IP with the MAC

82
00:06:31,450 --> 00:06:34,540
address of the killing machine of the attacker.

83
00:06:34,930 --> 00:06:38,410
And this will place the attacker in the middle of the connection.

84
00:06:38,410 --> 00:06:44,860
So every time that the target computer wants to send anything to the router, it will use the Mac address

85
00:06:44,860 --> 00:06:47,980
that's associated with the routers IP.

86
00:06:47,980 --> 00:06:51,670
And that MAC address is going to be the machine's MAC address.

87
00:06:52,840 --> 00:06:58,900
Now this packet is ready and if we send it, it'll actually fool the target into thinking that we are

88
00:06:58,900 --> 00:06:59,680
the router.

89
00:07:01,190 --> 00:07:03,670
And the next lecture, I'll show you how to do that.

90
00:07:03,680 --> 00:07:09,800
And we'll also have a closer look on the contents of this packet so that we have a better understanding

91
00:07:09,800 --> 00:07:11,240
on how this will work.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,080 --> 00:00:01,610
Okay.

2
00:00:01,680 --> 00:00:04,140
Now we're ready to send this package in the air.

3
00:00:04,140 --> 00:00:09,540
And if it's sent, it'll fool the target computer into thinking that we are the router.

4
00:00:10,570 --> 00:00:16,870
But before we do that, let's have a closer look on the contents of the packet to understand how it's

5
00:00:16,870 --> 00:00:20,320
going to fool the target into thinking that we are the router.

6
00:00:20,770 --> 00:00:25,810
So I'm going to print packet the show and packet dot summary.

7
00:00:26,170 --> 00:00:31,420
We've seen these two functions before and we've seen how they show us the details and the summary of

8
00:00:31,420 --> 00:00:32,120
the packet.

9
00:00:32,140 --> 00:00:36,490
So let's go and run this script and see what we'll get.

10
00:00:37,580 --> 00:00:39,530
So I'm going to clear all of this.

11
00:00:40,950 --> 00:00:47,250
And first of all, I need to navigate to the location where my script is stored and it's located in

12
00:00:47,250 --> 00:00:52,260
one directory back and it's called ARP Spoof File List.

13
00:00:52,260 --> 00:00:58,260
Here you'll see my script and I can run it by the one Python ARP spoof dot API.

14
00:00:59,040 --> 00:01:04,590
And as you can see, we have the first part here is the result of the show function and you can see

15
00:01:04,590 --> 00:01:11,820
that this is the type of this packet is and is ad so it says that a certain device is at a certain location.

16
00:01:12,390 --> 00:01:17,490
It source Mac address is this one which is the Mac address of ETA zero.

17
00:01:17,490 --> 00:01:25,200
So if I do if config ETA zero, you'll see that this is the same mac address, but the source IP is

18
00:01:25,200 --> 00:01:27,150
set to the IP of the router.

19
00:01:27,390 --> 00:01:33,450
So this is how the target or the device that's going to receive this packet will associate the Mac address

20
00:01:33,450 --> 00:01:40,710
of ETA zero with the IP of the router, with the IP that we set here and the RC field.

21
00:01:41,250 --> 00:01:47,370
And you can also see that the destination is set to ten 027, which is our target device.

22
00:01:47,370 --> 00:01:53,130
And all of this information is taken from the fields that we set in this particular line.

23
00:01:53,730 --> 00:01:58,140
Now, if we go back down, you can see the result of the summary in here.

24
00:01:58,500 --> 00:02:04,110
And basically what this says is this is an IP packet, it's a type is AD.

25
00:02:04,110 --> 00:02:12,300
So what it's saying is it says that ten 0 to 1 is at this Mac address, which is the MAC address of

26
00:02:12,300 --> 00:02:13,380
ETA zero.

27
00:02:14,670 --> 00:02:19,540
Therefore, when we send this to the Windows machine, it will accept it.

28
00:02:19,560 --> 00:02:21,210
It's not going to verify the result.

29
00:02:21,210 --> 00:02:22,530
It will just trust it.

30
00:02:22,530 --> 00:02:32,220
And then it will update its ARP table and associate the IP of the router with the MAC address of CALLY

31
00:02:32,220 --> 00:02:33,960
or of ETA zero.

32
00:02:34,020 --> 00:02:39,030
And this way, every time it needs to communicate with the router, it will communicate with us, which

33
00:02:39,030 --> 00:02:44,550
will allow us to spy on the target and redirect the flow of packets through our computer.

34
00:02:45,060 --> 00:02:50,280
Now let's go and send this packet, because all we're doing now is we're creating the packet and we're

35
00:02:50,280 --> 00:02:54,510
printing its content to get an idea of what the packet will do.

36
00:02:54,810 --> 00:02:57,060
So I'm actually going to delete all of this.

37
00:02:57,930 --> 00:03:05,880
