And in order to send this packet, all we have to do is just say KP, dot, send and put the packet

38
00:03:05,880 --> 00:03:09,480
that we want to send, which is called packet, and that's it.

39
00:03:09,510 --> 00:03:15,000
Now when we execute this script, this will create the packet for us, store it in the packet variable

40
00:03:15,000 --> 00:03:18,060
and scapegoat send will send this packet for us.

41
00:03:18,480 --> 00:03:21,740
So let me show you what's going to happen and you're going to understand it more.

42
00:03:21,750 --> 00:03:28,680
So if I do RPA here, this will show me the ARP table that is used by the Windows machine.

43
00:03:28,680 --> 00:03:35,250
And as you can see right now, it's associating the IP address of the router with the Routers Mac address.

44
00:03:35,670 --> 00:03:42,360
Now once we run the attack, it should associate the IP address of the router with the MAC address of

45
00:03:42,360 --> 00:03:43,590
the machine.

46
00:03:44,190 --> 00:03:48,360
So let's go and run this very simple script and see what happens.

47
00:03:50,230 --> 00:03:53,860
And as you can see, this is saying that it sent one packet for us.

48
00:03:53,890 --> 00:03:57,760
Let's go to the Windows Machine and run RPA.

49
00:03:58,360 --> 00:04:06,040
As you can see, the value for the Mac address for the router has changed to the MAC address of the

50
00:04:06,340 --> 00:04:07,030
machine.

51
00:04:07,030 --> 00:04:13,180
So in here you can see that the router is IP address is associated with this MAC address.

52
00:04:13,600 --> 00:04:21,700
After we run the attack, this computer received the packet that said that it came from this IP address.

53
00:04:23,030 --> 00:04:27,530
Because we said the source IP address here to the IP address of the router.

54
00:04:28,010 --> 00:04:34,490
So therefore whenever this machine received that packet, it thought that it came from this IP and it

55
00:04:34,490 --> 00:04:37,820
automatically stored the MAC address that it got it from.

56
00:04:37,820 --> 00:04:41,690
And that MAC address was the Machine's Mac address.

57
00:04:42,380 --> 00:04:47,630
So now this computer thinks that the machine is the router.

58
00:04:47,630 --> 00:04:53,450
So any time that this computer wants to access a website or do anything on the Internet, it will actually

59
00:04:53,450 --> 00:04:59,270
send its requests to the machine thinking that the machine is the router.

60
00:04:59,720 --> 00:05:05,840
Now, just to confirm this to you, if I go back here and just do if config it hit zero.

61
00:05:06,860 --> 00:05:12,710
As you can see, this is the Mac address of Carly and this is the Mac address that you're seeing in

62
00:05:12,710 --> 00:05:17,000
here associated with the IP of the router.

63
00:05:17,570 --> 00:05:19,610
Now, this is one step of the attack.

64
00:05:19,610 --> 00:05:22,790
We also need to tell the router that we are the victim.

65
00:05:22,790 --> 00:05:27,140
We can do that using the same function and using the same idea that I just did.

66
00:05:27,140 --> 00:05:32,600
I'll show you how to do that in the next lecture, and we'll continue working on this script to make

67
00:05:32,600 --> 00:05:33,560
it work better.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,680 --> 00:00:06,920
Now the code that we wrote so far can be used to fool one target that we are someone else.

2
00:00:08,100 --> 00:00:14,580
But our goal is to fool the target, telling them that we are the router and to fool the router, telling

3
00:00:14,580 --> 00:00:18,630
them that we are the target so that we can become the man in the middle.

4
00:00:19,200 --> 00:00:25,740
So in order to do that, we can use the same two lines, just copy and paste them here and change the

5
00:00:25,740 --> 00:00:27,330
values for the fields.

6
00:00:27,810 --> 00:00:31,740
Or we can make a function out of this so that we can reuse it.

7
00:00:31,950 --> 00:00:34,620
So let's do that because that's better.

8
00:00:34,890 --> 00:00:39,090
So I'm going to define a function and I'm going to call it spoof.

9
00:00:40,210 --> 00:00:44,050
And I'm going to make this function take to arguments or to inputs.

10
00:00:44,050 --> 00:00:47,950
The first input is going to be the target IP.

11
00:00:49,490 --> 00:00:53,290
And the next input is going to be the spoof IP.

12
00:00:53,300 --> 00:00:59,870
So this is the IP that we're pretending to be the IP of the machine that we want to tell the target

13
00:00:59,870 --> 00:01:01,610
that we are that machine.

14
00:01:02,360 --> 00:01:07,790
Then I'm going to highlight all of this and tab it so that it's part of this function.

15
00:01:09,140 --> 00:01:11,290
And I'm going to use the values in here.

16
00:01:11,300 --> 00:01:19,880
So the P test is going to be the target IP and the P source is going to be the spoof IP.

17
00:01:20,420 --> 00:01:22,700
So it's the IP that we're pretending to be.

18
00:01:23,940 --> 00:01:31,710
Now we have one problem, which is the WD East, which is the hardware destination, which is the MAC

19
00:01:31,710 --> 00:01:33,990
address of the target computer.

20
00:01:33,990 --> 00:01:38,130
So it's the MAC address that's associated with this IP.

21
00:01:38,580 --> 00:01:44,790
Now in the previous lecture, we actually inputted this manually in the code and we can make this function,

22
00:01:44,790 --> 00:01:46,500
take it manually as well.

23
00:01:46,800 --> 00:01:53,550
But I think we can do better because from what we learned before, we know that we can get the Mac address

24
00:01:53,550 --> 00:01:57,210
of any computer on the same network using their IP.

25
00:01:57,240 --> 00:02:00,960
This is exactly what our network scanner does.

26
00:02:01,680 --> 00:02:05,120
So let's have a look at the network scanner that we wrote before.

27
00:02:05,130 --> 00:02:15,030
So I'm just going to go to file open and my network scanner is stored in route pie and projects and

28
00:02:15,030 --> 00:02:16,950
it's called network scanner.

29
00:02:17,730 --> 00:02:23,640
I'm going to open that and I'm going to open it in a new window so it doesn't close my current project.

30
00:02:24,890 --> 00:02:32,420
And in here we know the scan function, which is this one is able to scan an IP address and return its

31
00:02:32,420 --> 00:02:33,440
MAC address.

32
00:02:33,950 --> 00:02:38,420
So I'm going to copy this code as is and then I'll modify it once we paste it.

33
00:02:38,540 --> 00:02:43,730
So I'm going to close this and I'm going to paste this as a function in here.

34
00:02:44,890 --> 00:02:45,560
Okay.

35
00:02:45,560 --> 00:02:52,190
So first of all, I'm going to rename this function and I'm going to call it Get Mac.

36
00:02:52,730 --> 00:02:58,460
So we're going to have a function that will give me the Mac address of the IP that I give it.

37
00:02:58,970 --> 00:03:01,610
So we're giving it the IP in here.

38
00:03:01,640 --> 00:03:05,210
It's going to create a broadcast asking who has this IP?

39
00:03:05,420 --> 00:03:11,300
It's going to send it using the ZP function like we explained before, and it's going to get a list

40
00:03:11,300 --> 00:03:12,350
of answers.

41
00:03:12,650 --> 00:03:15,620
Now, we're actually not interested in the whole list.

42
00:03:15,650 --> 00:03:20,960
We're interested in the first element of this list because we're actually going to get only one answer,

43
00:03:20,960 --> 00:03:22,950
because we're going to ask about one IP.

44
00:03:22,970 --> 00:03:25,400
We're not going to be asking about a range.

45
00:03:25,610 --> 00:03:32,480
So this is right here is equivalent to the element because we have this loop here which says for each

46
00:03:32,480 --> 00:03:38,130
element in the answered list, but we're selecting the first element, the zero element.

47
00:03:38,150 --> 00:03:42,860
So basically this is equivalent to the element that we say in here.

48
00:03:43,280 --> 00:03:51,020
And then we know and here we can access the IP by doing element square brackets, one dot PS or C,

49
00:03:51,110 --> 00:03:58,370
and we can access the Mac by doing element square brackets one dot H, W As or C.

50
00:03:58,730 --> 00:04:05,510
And since all of this is equivalent to the element, then we should be able to access the Mac address

51
00:04:05,510 --> 00:04:12,720
by doing answer list zero square brackets one dot h, w.

52
00:04:12,740 --> 00:04:13,910
S or C.

53
00:04:15,680 --> 00:04:21,350
I know it might be confusing how I got this, but if you go back to the lecture where I explain this

54
00:04:21,350 --> 00:04:26,840
loop, we said This loop will iterate over each element in the answered list.

55
00:04:26,840 --> 00:04:29,870
So we're doing four elements in the answer list.

56
00:04:29,870 --> 00:04:36,110
And then for each element in there, we're printing elements, square brackets, one dot piece RC,

57
00:04:36,110 --> 00:04:43,070
which is the IP and then H.W. is RC, which is the Mac, and I explained how I was able to come up with

58
00:04:43,070 --> 00:04:43,610
this.

59
00:04:44,150 --> 00:04:49,040
So what we're doing right now, we're doing answered list, square brackets, zero.

60
00:04:49,160 --> 00:04:55,730
So all of this in blue is actually equivalent to the element, because when we do this, we're actually

61
00:04:55,730 --> 00:05:00,440
selecting the first element and we iterate over elements in here.

62
00:05:00,440 --> 00:05:08,450
We're selecting one element at a time, therefore we're only appending square brackets one dot W as

63
00:05:08,450 --> 00:05:11,270
RC to select the MAC address.

64
00:05:11,720 --> 00:05:16,910
Now I could be wrong, so let's make sure that I'm doing this right and let's just print that.

65
00:05:18,190 --> 00:05:20,830
And then I'm going to remove all of this code.

66
00:05:21,760 --> 00:05:26,290
And in my main function, I'm just going to call get Mark.

67
00:05:26,990 --> 00:05:28,460
And give it the IP.

68
00:05:28,460 --> 00:05:33,290
And I'm just going to set it to the IP of the router, which is ten 0 to 1.

69
00:05:34,500 --> 00:05:35,940
Let's run this program.

70
00:05:37,220 --> 00:05:44,060
And as you can see, it's given me the Mac address of the router, so our code is working perfectly.

71
00:05:44,690 --> 00:05:49,580
Now, instead of printing this, I'm actually going to get this function to return this value.

72
00:05:50,870 --> 00:05:57,380
And this way, whenever this function is executed, if everything goes as expected at the end of the

73
00:05:57,380 --> 00:06:02,540
function, it will return the value of the Mac address of the IP that we gave it.

74
00:06:02,540 --> 00:06:05,030
So it will basically give us the MAC address.

75
00:06:05,730 --> 00:06:12,330
So we can use this in our spoof code and we can say Target Mac.

76
00:06:13,630 --> 00:06:15,610
Is equal to get Mark.

77
00:06:16,450 --> 00:06:18,130
For the target IP.

78
00:06:18,970 --> 00:06:24,820
And then and here, instead of given the match manually, we're going to use Target Mac.

79
00:06:26,080 --> 00:06:28,660
Now our spoof function looks really good.

80
00:06:28,670 --> 00:06:36,760
It takes a target IP and a spoof IP and then it will craft an appropriate r.p packet and send it to

81
00:06:36,760 --> 00:06:42,040
spoof the target IP and make it think that we are the IP that we want to spoof.

82
00:06:42,760 --> 00:06:46,300
Now in the next lecture, we're going to use this in our main code in here.

83
00:06:46,300 --> 00:06:52,420
We're going to implement this function and we'll talk more about how we can use that to spoof two computers

84
00:06:52,420 --> 00:06:58,390
and properly redirect the flow of packets in the network and make us become the man in the middle.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,630 --> 00:00:06,930
Now that we have a function that we can use to fool any computer in the network, let's use this function

2
00:00:06,930 --> 00:00:07,680
twice.

3
00:00:07,680 --> 00:00:13,590
So we tell the victim that we are the router, we tell the router that we are the victim, and this

4
00:00:13,590 --> 00:00:20,280
will effectively put us in the middle of the connection redirect in all packets sent or received by

5
00:00:20,280 --> 00:00:23,460
the target victim to go through our computer.

6
00:00:24,270 --> 00:00:29,940
So this is going to be very simple because we've actually done all of the difficult stuff already.

7
00:00:30,240 --> 00:00:36,180
So I'm going to remove the get mark function in here and my main code and all I'm going to do is call

8
00:00:36,180 --> 00:00:41,610
my spoof function the first time I'm going to be spoofing the target.

9
00:00:41,610 --> 00:00:45,810
So the target IP is ten 027.

10
00:00:46,780 --> 00:00:49,750
And I'm going to tell this computer that I am the Raptor.

11
00:00:49,750 --> 00:00:57,010
So I'm going to tell him that I'm ten 0 to 1, and then I'm going to do this again.

12
00:00:57,820 --> 00:01:03,340
Instead, this time, I'm going to tell the router that I am the target computer.

13
00:01:03,880 --> 00:01:07,630
So effectively, I'm sending these two messages right here.

14
00:01:07,630 --> 00:01:12,420
I'm telling the target that I am the router, telling the router that I am the victim.

15
00:01:12,430 --> 00:01:15,100
This will put me in the middle of the connection.

16
00:01:16,120 --> 00:01:19,300
Now I got the IPS using my scanner app.

17
00:01:19,750 --> 00:01:27,130
So as you can see, if we just run our network scanner, we're able to see the router and the target

18
00:01:27,400 --> 00:01:30,700
and we can also confirm the router IP by doing route.

19
00:01:30,700 --> 00:01:37,600
And as I showed you before, now I'm going to save this and before I run this, I'm going to go to my

20
00:01:37,600 --> 00:01:43,720
windows machine and clear this and then do RPA again.

21
00:01:44,290 --> 00:01:47,950
And as you can see right now, the IRP table is looking normal.

22
00:01:47,950 --> 00:01:55,060
We have the writer's IP address associated with the right MAC address, which is the Routers Mac address.

23
00:01:55,990 --> 00:02:02,530
After running my command, the Routers Mac address should change to the MAC address of the machine,

24
00:02:02,530 --> 00:02:03,670
which is this one.

25
00:02:04,670 --> 00:02:09,020
So let's run our program by doing python arp spoof dot py.

26
00:02:10,730 --> 00:02:13,460
And as you can see now, it's saying it's sent to packets.

27
00:02:13,460 --> 00:02:18,380
So the first packet was going to the client, the second packet was going to the router.

28
00:02:18,770 --> 00:02:27,080
Now, if you run RPA again, as you can see, the Mac address that's associated with the router is changed

29
00:02:27,080 --> 00:02:29,990
to the Mac address of the machine.

30
00:02:30,470 --> 00:02:37,010
So now this computer thinks that I am the router and the router thinks that I am this computer.

31
00:02:37,130 --> 00:02:42,800
Therefore, anything that this computer will send if it's trying to go to a website or do anything on

32
00:02:42,800 --> 00:02:49,160
the internet, will be sent to the machine where we'll be able to read it, edit it, modify it to whatever

33
00:02:49,160 --> 00:02:50,150
we want with it.

34
00:02:50,150 --> 00:02:55,250
And then it will be going to the Internet or to the router again.

35
00:02:55,250 --> 00:02:58,960
When the router sends any responses, they'll be going back to us.

36
00:02:58,970 --> 00:03:03,440
We'll be able to analyze them and then we'll forward them to the victim.

37
00:03:03,920 --> 00:03:06,080
Now, we'll go through all of that later on.

38
00:03:06,260 --> 00:03:08,930
But I want to show you something else right now.

39
00:03:09,110 --> 00:03:12,050
Now, if I just go and open my browser.

40
00:03:13,620 --> 00:03:16,500
And let's say I just wanted to go to CNN.com.

41
00:03:17,880 --> 00:03:19,980
Now you'll see everything will work fine.

42
00:03:20,190 --> 00:03:23,220
But if we go back and do RPA.

43
00:03:24,580 --> 00:03:30,100
You'll see that the Mac address went back to the right one to the default, one to the right Mac address

44
00:03:30,100 --> 00:03:31,030
of the router.

45
00:03:31,210 --> 00:03:33,910
So we're not the man in the middle anymore.

46
00:03:34,390 --> 00:03:38,320
Now, this is happening because we are only doing this one time.

47
00:03:38,320 --> 00:03:42,610
We only sent one packet fooling both the router and the client.

48
00:03:42,970 --> 00:03:49,660
If we actually want to stay the man in the middle and maintain fooling both the router and the client,

49
00:03:49,660 --> 00:03:54,070
we need to keep sending these packets for the period that we want to fool them.

50
00:03:54,160 --> 00:03:56,070
So one packet is not enough.

51
00:03:56,080 --> 00:04:02,290
We actually need to keep sending these packets continuously until we want to stop the attack.

52
00:04:03,280 --> 00:04:11,140
Therefore, we need to modify our code in a way so that it will keep executing these two lines repeatedly.

53
00:04:12,210 --> 00:04:15,660
So to do that, we're going to use a while loop.

54
00:04:15,810 --> 00:04:22,500
Now we had an example of a four loop before and we seen how we can use a for loop to iterate over lists.

55
00:04:22,680 --> 00:04:26,610
In this lecture, we're going to have a simple example of using a while loop.

56
00:04:26,700 --> 00:04:29,550
Now again, you can use loops to do so many things.

57
00:04:29,550 --> 00:04:32,310
So this is just a small other example.

58
00:04:32,580 --> 00:04:38,670
So we're going to say, while true, I want you to do these lines.

59
00:04:41,260 --> 00:04:46,120
Now, the way that a while loop works is, first of all, it takes a condition.

60
00:04:46,120 --> 00:04:49,090
And in our case, we're given the condition as true.

61
00:04:49,750 --> 00:04:56,070
And then for as long as this condition is true, it's going to execute the loop body.

62
00:04:56,080 --> 00:04:58,060
So this is the loop body right here.

63
00:04:58,060 --> 00:04:59,740
As you can see, it's stopped.

64
00:05:00,010 --> 00:05:05,170
It's following the same syntax that we usually use when we're using functions or if statements.

65
00:05:05,680 --> 00:05:11,350
So for as long as this condition is true, it's going to execute these two lines.

66
00:05:11,980 --> 00:05:15,310
Now, this condition can be anything that Python accepts.

67
00:05:15,310 --> 00:05:17,650
So we can be comparing to variables.

68
00:05:17,650 --> 00:05:22,240
We can be checking if something is greater than something, if something is smaller than something,

69
00:05:22,240 --> 00:05:28,960
if something is equal to something, if a list contains something and so on, and then the loop will

70
00:05:28,960 --> 00:05:31,990
run for as long as this condition is true.

71
00:05:32,380 --> 00:05:35,350
Now, in our example, we're actually not using a condition.

72
00:05:35,350 --> 00:05:40,050
We're literally just saying, well, true, I want to execute these two lines.

73
00:05:40,060 --> 00:05:47,020
So basically these two lines will always will keep executing for as long as our program is working.

74
00:05:47,650 --> 00:05:53,890
Now, just so that we don't send too many packets on the network, I'm actually going to add the delay

75
00:05:53,890 --> 00:05:55,030
to this loop.

76
00:05:55,480 --> 00:06:01,900
So I'm going to say time dot sleep and I'm going to put two to sleep for 2 seconds.

77
00:06:02,560 --> 00:06:07,840
So now what's going to happen is as soon as we execute our program, we're going to hit this while loop

78
00:06:07,840 --> 00:06:10,060
and the condition here is true.

79
00:06:10,060 --> 00:06:12,580
So this loop will keep executing.

80
00:06:13,540 --> 00:06:19,540
So first of all, it's going to call the spoof function with these two values spoofing the client that

81
00:06:19,540 --> 00:06:26,140
we are the router, then it's going to call the spoof function with these two values spoofing the router

82
00:06:26,140 --> 00:06:31,900
that we are the client, then the python will stop executing for 2 seconds.

83
00:06:31,900 --> 00:06:34,270
So it's literally going to sleep for 2 seconds.

84
00:06:34,270 --> 00:06:35,560
Nothing's going to happen.

85
00:06:35,560 --> 00:06:37,060
It won't do any work.

86
00:06:37,300 --> 00:06:40,150
Then it's going to go back up to the start of the loop.

87
00:06:40,180 --> 00:06:42,550
It's going to check my condition, which is true.

88
00:06:42,550 --> 00:06:43,870
It's always going to be true.

89
00:06:43,900 --> 00:06:48,730
It will execute these two lines, sleep for 2 seconds, go up, check.

90
00:06:48,730 --> 00:06:50,860
The condition again is true.

91
00:06:50,890 --> 00:06:54,660
Execute two lines, sleep for 2 seconds and so on.

92
00:06:54,670 --> 00:06:58,090
This will keep executing until I press control.

93
00:06:58,120 --> 00:07:05,140
See, therefore I'll be able to fool the client and the router and redirect the flow of packets for

94
00:07:05,140 --> 00:07:10,030
as long as the program is running until I press control C on my terminal.

95
00:07:10,720 --> 00:07:12,490
So let's see this in action.

96
00:07:15,030 --> 00:07:19,740
And I'm getting this error because I forgot to import the time module.

97
00:07:19,950 --> 00:07:25,290
So I need to go in here and just say Import time.

98
00:07:27,120 --> 00:07:29,100
And then run this again.

99
00:07:29,580 --> 00:07:34,770
And as you can see now, it's sent in two packets, sleeping for 2 seconds, send in two packets, sleeping

100
00:07:34,770 --> 00:07:36,960
for 2 seconds, and so on.

101
00:07:37,500 --> 00:07:42,900
So now if we go on our Windows machine, we run RPA thereafter.

102
00:07:42,900 --> 00:07:46,080
Is IP is associated with Carly's Mac address.

103
00:07:47,450 --> 00:07:52,190
Now if we go to Firefox and let's say we just go to test.

104
00:07:54,400 --> 00:07:59,920
Now the target lost their Internet connection because the data is actually going through the machine,

105
00:07:59,920 --> 00:08:03,910
but the machine needs to allow packets to flow through it.

106
00:08:04,030 --> 00:08:06,580
So we actually this is nothing to do with Python.

107
00:08:06,580 --> 00:08:13,450
We actually need to enable IP forwarding to allow packets to flow through my computer without dropping

108
00:08:13,450 --> 00:08:16,030
them and therefore work as a router.

109
00:08:16,060 --> 00:08:28,900
So to fix this, we just need to do echo one to proc sis net ip virgin for IP forward.

110
00:08:29,920 --> 00:08:36,790
And now if we go back to the Windows machine and just go to Bing, as you can see, it works perfectly.

111
00:08:37,330 --> 00:08:43,890
And if we check our RPA, you can see that we are still the man in the middle.

112
00:08:43,900 --> 00:08:51,010
The router is IP address is still associated with Carly's Mac address and everything is properly flowing

113
00:08:51,010 --> 00:08:52,600
through the machine.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,440 --> 00:00:01,750
Okay.

2
00:00:01,830 --> 00:00:06,320
So, you know, when we run our program, we're going to get outputs like so.

3
00:00:06,330 --> 00:00:13,290
So every time we send a packet, we'll get an output from the send function saying send one packet sent

4
00:00:13,290 --> 00:00:14,250
to unpack it.

5
00:00:14,460 --> 00:00:19,380
Every time the output is displayed on screen, we get two lines.

6
00:00:19,380 --> 00:00:21,810
So one for each packet we're sending.

7
00:00:21,810 --> 00:00:28,200
Because if you go up here, you'll see that we're calling the spoof function twice and every time we

8
00:00:28,200 --> 00:00:35,700
call the spoof function, it calls the send function and this output is basically being printed from

9
00:00:35,700 --> 00:00:36,900
the send function.

10
00:00:37,830 --> 00:00:39,570
So I actually don't like this.

11
00:00:39,570 --> 00:00:45,180
As you can see now, my terminal screen is full of this and I can't really see or do anything else.

12
00:00:45,180 --> 00:00:46,770
I don't think it's elegant.

13
00:00:46,770 --> 00:00:51,540
So in this lecture I want to show you how to display nicer output on screen.

14
00:00:51,750 --> 00:00:55,740
And as we do that, you're going to learn more about variable types.

15
00:00:55,740 --> 00:01:01,680
We're going to have a look on integers, and I think you'll have better understanding of why loops and

16
00:01:01,680 --> 00:01:02,670
how to use them.

17
00:01:03,270 --> 00:01:06,780
So first of all, I'm going to do control C to quit this.

18
00:01:06,870 --> 00:01:10,050
I'm going to clear the screen, I'm going to go up.

19
00:01:10,710 --> 00:01:17,520
And the first thing that I want to do is I want to stop this function from printing its output.

20
00:01:17,730 --> 00:01:24,030
So I don't want it to print this output right here, and then I want to write my own print statements.

21
00:01:24,390 --> 00:01:30,330
So the first thing that we will do, because this is a copy function, just like we did before when

22
00:01:30,330 --> 00:01:33,000
we didn't want it to produce output on screen.

23
00:01:33,000 --> 00:01:36,480
All we have to do is just pass an argument to this function.

24
00:01:36,480 --> 00:01:41,070
The argument is called verbose and we're going to set this to false.

25
00:01:41,550 --> 00:01:48,990
So similar to what we did with the SR function before and now if we go down and run this, you'll see

26
00:01:48,990 --> 00:01:52,890
that this will run in the background, but you won't see anything on screen.

27
00:01:53,430 --> 00:01:59,130
Now this is slightly better, but it's not good enough because if a user is running this, they're not

28
00:01:59,130 --> 00:02:05,610
sure if the program is actually doing what it's supposed to do or if it's just stuck at some point.

29
00:02:05,610 --> 00:02:11,190
So it will be nice to produce some output to the user so that they know what's happening in the background.

30
00:02:11,460 --> 00:02:17,610
So I'm going to do Control C again and we're going to try to print output in here.

31
00:02:17,610 --> 00:02:20,910
Every time we send the packets, we're going to print.

32
00:02:22,160 --> 00:02:23,900
Sent two packets.

33
00:02:25,060 --> 00:02:25,650
Okay.

34
00:02:25,660 --> 00:02:31,900
Now, if we run this, we should see one print statement for every time this loop runs and it should

35
00:02:31,900 --> 00:02:33,700
say send to packets.

36
00:02:34,180 --> 00:02:37,270
So we're going to go down, run this again.

37
00:02:37,450 --> 00:02:42,940
And as you can see, we get sent to packets 2 seconds, the loop gets executed again and we get the

38
00:02:42,940 --> 00:02:45,490
next one and the next one and so on.

39
00:02:46,300 --> 00:02:51,280
Now what I'd really like is if we can print the number of packets that have been sent.

40
00:02:52,180 --> 00:02:54,460
So let's see how we can do that.

41
00:02:54,730 --> 00:02:56,900
Now, as we know, this is a loop.

42
00:02:56,920 --> 00:03:02,770
Once Python gets into this loop, it will never quit it because the condition is set to true.

43
00:03:03,160 --> 00:03:08,350
So what I'm going to do, I'm going to, first of all, create a variable in here and I'm going to call

44
00:03:08,350 --> 00:03:10,900
this variable send packets count.

45
00:03:12,360 --> 00:03:19,680
And I'm going to set this to zero because at this point in the program, we still haven't sent any packets.

46
00:03:20,370 --> 00:03:27,510
And note, I put the zero here as is I didn't put it between two quotation marks because I don't want

47
00:03:27,510 --> 00:03:28,820
this to be a string.

48
00:03:28,830 --> 00:03:30,900
I want this to be an integer.

49
00:03:30,900 --> 00:03:33,180
So I want it to be a number.

50
00:03:33,630 --> 00:03:36,480
So we actually never had a look on integer variables.

51
00:03:36,480 --> 00:03:39,630
That's why I thought, this is a good example for you.

52
00:03:40,230 --> 00:03:45,240
Now I want to use this variable and print it in here in my print statement.

53
00:03:45,240 --> 00:03:49,830
So I'm going to change this print statement and I'm going to call it packet sent.

54
00:03:51,740 --> 00:03:58,100
And then we're going to append our variable here and this is called send packets count.

55
00:03:58,520 --> 00:04:02,090
So at the moment, this is set to zero and we're going to print this.

56
00:04:02,090 --> 00:04:07,700
This should print zero in here, but it's actually going to give us an error.

57
00:04:07,700 --> 00:04:11,540
But I'm going to I want you to see the error first before before I fix it.

58
00:04:12,110 --> 00:04:13,670
So I'm going to run the code.

59
00:04:14,240 --> 00:04:16,340
And as you can see, we get a python error.

60
00:04:16,340 --> 00:04:19,460
And we actually had a look on something similar to this.

61
00:04:19,790 --> 00:04:24,740
Now, like I said, I want you to get comfortable with getting errors and debugging them so you can

62
00:04:24,740 --> 00:04:28,070
see that this is telling us we're getting an error on line 26.

63
00:04:28,070 --> 00:04:31,730
So if we go up and look at line 26, this is the line.

64
00:04:33,480 --> 00:04:36,120
And it's telling us that the error is a type error.

65
00:04:36,120 --> 00:04:39,030
So the type of the variable is causing this error.

66
00:04:39,090 --> 00:04:44,190
And the problem is we cannot contaminate a string and an integer.

67
00:04:44,880 --> 00:04:50,850
So if we go up again and have a look, you can see that we're printing a string and then we're saying,

68
00:04:50,850 --> 00:04:54,810
I want to print this string, which is enclosed between two quotation marks.

69
00:04:54,810 --> 00:04:59,430
And I'm saying plus, and then I'm putting an integer, so I'll put in a number.

70
00:04:59,460 --> 00:05:02,040
This is not a string, this is a number.

71
00:05:02,040 --> 00:05:06,990
So if we put this between two quotation marks, this print statement will work fine.

72
00:05:07,170 --> 00:05:13,260
But because we are using a number in here without quotation marks, Python is getting confused.

73
00:05:13,350 --> 00:05:19,170
Are these two values that I need to add, or are these two strings that I need to print so it doesn't

74
00:05:19,170 --> 00:05:20,640
know what to do with it?

75
00:05:20,910 --> 00:05:26,430
Therefore, I need to tell Python that I want you to print this and treat this number as a string.

76
00:05:26,520 --> 00:05:29,220
So I'm going to type string before it.

77
00:05:31,120 --> 00:05:38,560
And now if we go down and print this, you'll see that it's given us cents zero packets since zero packets

78
00:05:38,560 --> 00:05:40,030
sends zero packets.

79
00:05:40,030 --> 00:05:41,590
So that's fine.

80
00:05:41,740 --> 00:05:48,330
But what we actually wanted to do is we want this number to be incremented every time the loop runs.

81
00:05:48,340 --> 00:05:53,500
So we know the first time we send two packets and then we send another two and then we send another

82
00:05:53,500 --> 00:05:53,890
two.

83
00:05:53,890 --> 00:05:57,070
So at this line, this number should be equal to six.

84
00:05:57,640 --> 00:05:59,590
So this is very easy to do.

85
00:05:59,620 --> 00:06:03,800
All we have to do is just increment this variable by two.

86
00:06:03,820 --> 00:06:05,740
Every time this loop runs.

87
00:06:06,520 --> 00:06:14,890
So to do that, we're going to say send packets count is equal to send packet counts to the same variable

88
00:06:15,160 --> 00:06:16,120
plus two.

89
00:06:16,690 --> 00:06:18,930
So let's go over what's going to happen here.

90
00:06:18,940 --> 00:06:24,130
First of all, the value of this variable is going to be set to zero before we enter the loop.

91
00:06:24,670 --> 00:06:29,170
Then we're going to enter a loop that will keep executing until we press control.

92
00:06:29,170 --> 00:06:32,020
C Because we're setting the condition to true.

93
00:06:32,680 --> 00:06:37,480
So the first line will spoof the client, the next line is going to spoof the router.

94
00:06:37,930 --> 00:06:43,330
Then we are saying zero is equal to zero plus two.

95
00:06:44,120 --> 00:06:50,060
So we're saying the value that stored in this variable is equal to whatever value is stored in the same

96
00:06:50,060 --> 00:06:51,860
variable plus two.

97
00:06:52,520 --> 00:06:54,070
We're going to print that.

98
00:06:54,080 --> 00:06:58,580
We're going to sleep for 2 seconds and then we're going to go up again.

99
00:06:58,760 --> 00:07:01,050
It will send these proofing packets.

100
00:07:01,070 --> 00:07:04,750
Then this variable now contains a value of two.

101
00:07:04,760 --> 00:07:08,720
So this equation is going to be two is equal to two plus two.

102
00:07:09,200 --> 00:07:10,340
We're going to print four.

103
00:07:10,340 --> 00:07:12,320
And here again, we go up.

104
00:07:12,320 --> 00:07:13,460
We do the spoofing.

105
00:07:13,490 --> 00:07:18,410
The value here is four, four plus two is equal to six.

106
00:07:18,440 --> 00:07:21,170
We're going to print six and so on.

107
00:07:21,680 --> 00:07:23,810
So let's go down and have a look on this.

108
00:07:24,050 --> 00:07:26,870
I'm going to clear the screen and run it again.

109
00:07:27,530 --> 00:07:33,650
So as you can see now, it's given us packets that are two, four, six and so on.

110
00:07:34,640 --> 00:07:35,060
Now.

111
00:07:35,060 --> 00:07:36,320
This is really cool.

112
00:07:36,350 --> 00:07:40,150
It's given us useful output, but we still have a problem.

113
00:07:40,160 --> 00:07:42,680
It's still going to overflow our screen.

114
00:07:42,680 --> 00:07:48,380
So this is going to keep going until our screen is full and then it's just not going to look that nice.

115
00:07:49,230 --> 00:07:54,570
So in the next lecture, we're going to address this issue and you'll learn how to print dynamic print

116
00:07:54,570 --> 00:07:55,440
statements.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,860 --> 00:00:06,920
Now in the previous lecture, we improved our program so that it prints custom print statements, not

2
00:00:06,920 --> 00:00:09,740
the default ones that's produced by Skype.

3
00:00:10,250 --> 00:00:16,340
We also managed to get it print the number of packets that have been sent, but the print statement,

4
00:00:16,340 --> 00:00:17,830
it's still not very nice.

5
00:00:17,840 --> 00:00:20,210
It's still going to overflow our terminal.

6
00:00:20,600 --> 00:00:28,310
So what we'd like to achieve in this lecture is to use dynamic printing so that we only print one line

7
00:00:28,310 --> 00:00:35,000
which will say packet sent, and then the number of packets will be updated on the same line.

8
00:00:35,000 --> 00:00:38,660
And instead of printing a new line every time the loop runs.

9
00:00:39,690 --> 00:00:46,410
Now, the reason why the program is actually printing a new line for every time the loop runs is because

10
00:00:46,410 --> 00:00:53,190
whenever we call the print function right here, it automatically appends a new line character at the

11
00:00:53,190 --> 00:00:53,680
end.

12
00:00:53,700 --> 00:01:00,630
Even though we never put a new line character which is backward slash PN in our print statement, it

13
00:01:00,630 --> 00:01:03,900
will automatically append it to the end of the line.

14
00:01:04,440 --> 00:01:11,280
So if we want to print stuff on the same line in Python, we need to tell the print function not to

15
00:01:11,280 --> 00:01:14,430
append a print character at the end of the line.

16
00:01:14,940 --> 00:01:20,760
And to do that, all we have to do is just put a comma after the function.

17
00:01:20,760 --> 00:01:27,600
So literally a comma, even after the bracket of the function at the end of the print line.

18
00:01:28,290 --> 00:01:35,100
Now, if you just do this as is and go down and run the program, you'll see that we'll actually get

19
00:01:35,100 --> 00:01:38,070
stuck and we won't see anything on screen.

20
00:01:38,400 --> 00:01:45,270
The reason for this is because Python now is going to put everything that's being printed in a buffer.

21
00:01:45,480 --> 00:01:51,750
And then when we quit the program, when we exit out of the loop, you'll see everything is printed

22
00:01:51,750 --> 00:01:53,010
for us in here.

23
00:01:53,490 --> 00:01:56,760
And as you can see, our print statement is actually working.

24
00:01:56,760 --> 00:02:02,160
So it's instead of putting everything in separate lines, it's putting it in the same line.

25
00:02:02,310 --> 00:02:07,830
But the problem is, it won't print it until we quit the program or exit the loop.

26
00:02:08,310 --> 00:02:13,350
Now, like I said, this is happening because Python is putting all the values that we want to print

27
00:02:13,350 --> 00:02:14,280
in a buffer.

28
00:02:14,700 --> 00:02:20,180
And in order to fix this, to tell Python that I want I don't want you to put stuff in a buffer.

29
00:02:20,190 --> 00:02:23,400
I want you to print it for me on screen straight away.

30
00:02:23,460 --> 00:02:26,490
We need to tell Python to flush this buffer.

31
00:02:26,880 --> 00:02:33,810
And to do that, we're going to do this dot steady output, dot flush.

32
00:02:34,620 --> 00:02:39,480
So what this says basically tells Python to flush the standard output.

33
00:02:39,480 --> 00:02:44,730
So whatever print statements that you have in there, I want you to print it for me instantly.

34
00:02:44,730 --> 00:02:47,670
And don't wait until the program quits.

35
00:02:48,270 --> 00:02:56,010
Now we're using a library called SIS, so we need to import this library in here and we are good to

36
00:02:56,010 --> 00:02:56,580
go.

37
00:02:56,970 --> 00:03:02,520
So now every time we're printing, we're saying we want to print this without the new line character.

38
00:03:02,520 --> 00:03:08,250
And then we're telling Python, don't store this in your buffer, flush your buffer and print that for

39
00:03:08,250 --> 00:03:09,240
me on screen.

40
00:03:09,690 --> 00:03:13,740
So let's go down, clear and run it.

41
00:03:15,700 --> 00:03:18,550
And as you can see now, it's printing it as the loop runs.

42
00:03:18,550 --> 00:03:25,090
So it's not storing everything in a buffer and everything is being printed on the same line.

43
00:03:25,900 --> 00:03:33,030
Now we're getting closer to our goal, but this is not quite what we want because this is still ugly.

44
00:03:33,040 --> 00:03:38,470
It's still going to fill my terminal horizontally and then it's going to go down and even uglier than

45
00:03:38,470 --> 00:03:40,360
print in one thing, in one line.

46
00:03:40,990 --> 00:03:42,900
So what would be perfect?

47
00:03:42,910 --> 00:03:44,170
What would actually achieve?

48
00:03:44,170 --> 00:03:50,130
Our goal is if we can move this print statement to the start of the line.

49
00:03:50,140 --> 00:03:55,990
So first of all, it's going to print for me packet sent to and then whenever it needs to print this,

50
00:03:55,990 --> 00:04:01,960
I want this to be printed at the start of the line overwriting what's being printed in here.

51
00:04:02,770 --> 00:04:09,400
Then when we reach this print statement, I want you again to start at the start of the line overwriting

52
00:04:09,400 --> 00:04:11,350
the previous print statement.

53
00:04:11,920 --> 00:04:17,410
Now, to do this, we're going to use the backward slash or string literal.

54
00:04:17,440 --> 00:04:24,190
So we had the look on backwards slash DN or the end string literal, which will go down in one line.

55
00:04:24,190 --> 00:04:31,300
We had the look on the tab which was backwards slash T and today we're going to have a look on backwards

56
00:04:31,300 --> 00:04:37,840
slash or and what this says basically it tells Python that I don't want you to print from where you

57
00:04:37,840 --> 00:04:38,770
are right now.

58
00:04:38,770 --> 00:04:43,480
I want you to always print the statement from the start of the line.

59
00:04:43,990 --> 00:04:50,050
So, for example, when we reach this part and instead of printing it from where the cursor is, I want

60
00:04:50,050 --> 00:04:53,920
you to go all the way back and start from the start of the line.

61
00:04:54,670 --> 00:05:02,170
If we do this for every print, we will be overwriting the previous print and printing the current print

62
00:05:02,170 --> 00:05:04,030
or printing the current statement.

63
00:05:04,660 --> 00:05:06,940
So let's go down and have a look on this.

64
00:05:08,430 --> 00:05:11,850
And as you can see now, we have only one line.

65
00:05:11,850 --> 00:05:19,110
And this one line is dynamically presenting the value that stored in our account variable, which is

66
00:05:19,110 --> 00:05:21,150
called sent packets count.

67
00:05:21,950 --> 00:05:28,700
So the print right now is useful and elegant, and it won't overflow our terminal with useless data.

68
00:05:29,570 --> 00:05:29,870
Now.

69
00:05:29,870 --> 00:05:33,740
This solution will only work with Python 2.7 and below.

70
00:05:33,770 --> 00:05:36,050
It won't work with Python three.

71
00:05:36,710 --> 00:05:40,390
If you want to achieve this in Python three, it's actually easier.

72
00:05:40,400 --> 00:05:42,590
So let me show you how you do this.

73
00:05:42,620 --> 00:05:45,680
First of all, you're not going to need to import the this.

74
00:05:46,730 --> 00:05:50,030
Because you won't need to do the standard output flush.

75
00:05:50,390 --> 00:05:58,040
And if you want to print stuff on the same line in Python three without appending the new line character,

76
00:05:58,070 --> 00:06:00,440
then you don't need to use the comma here.

77
00:06:00,440 --> 00:06:01,790
So I'm going to remove that.

78
00:06:02,090 --> 00:06:08,270
But instead we're going to put a comma inside and use an argument called end.

79
00:06:08,540 --> 00:06:15,560
And basically this argument specifies what character do you want to add to the end of the print statement?

80
00:06:15,560 --> 00:06:19,700
And we're going to set this to blank so that we won't add any characters.

81
00:06:20,330 --> 00:06:22,660
So with this, it's actually much easier.

82
00:06:22,670 --> 00:06:28,820
All you have to do is just use the R literal to start printing at the start of the line and then use

83
00:06:28,820 --> 00:06:35,450
an argument called end with the print statement and set this argument to nothing so that Python will

84
00:06:35,450 --> 00:06:38,450
not add anything to the end of the print statement.

85
00:06:39,140 --> 00:06:42,200
So if we go down and use this with Python three.

86
00:06:44,610 --> 00:06:52,200
Again, as you can see, we are able to dynamically print the result that is being dynamically incremented

87
00:06:52,200 --> 00:06:53,790
in our wild loop.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,630 --> 00:00:08,400
Now we know when we run our program, the program will continue executing until we press control, see,

2
00:00:08,400 --> 00:00:13,200
because we're literally trapping the program in this wild loop right here.

3
00:00:13,890 --> 00:00:17,910
And when we press control C, the program will quit.

4
00:00:17,910 --> 00:00:21,810
But it will also display this ugly error message.

5
00:00:22,230 --> 00:00:27,930
Now, this message is actually very helpful when something is going wrong, because sometimes when you're

6
00:00:27,930 --> 00:00:32,580
doing when you're programming, your program might crash in the middle of execution.

7
00:00:32,580 --> 00:00:38,520
So you'll actually get a message similar to this one telling you what went wrong so that you can fix

8
00:00:38,520 --> 00:00:38,880
it.

9
00:00:39,570 --> 00:00:45,240
Now, in our case, nothing went wrong and the program crashed because the user entered control.

10
00:00:45,240 --> 00:00:51,930
C And you can also see from this message it's telling you that the reason why all of this happened or

11
00:00:51,930 --> 00:00:57,540
the reason why the program quit execution is because of a keyboard interrupt.

12
00:00:57,990 --> 00:01:03,720
So this is a type of an exception that can happen during the execution of the program.

13
00:01:04,080 --> 00:01:09,300
Now, like I said, there are many types of exceptions, and sometimes you're going to need to be able

14
00:01:09,300 --> 00:01:15,960
to handle this exception in a certain way so that your program continues executing or so that it can

15
00:01:15,960 --> 00:01:18,330
adapt to the exception that happened.

16
00:01:19,020 --> 00:01:24,120
Now, in this lecture, I'm going to show you a very simple example of handling this exception.

17
00:01:24,120 --> 00:01:30,570
So whenever the user enters control C, we're going to be able to capture this exception and do something.

18
00:01:31,140 --> 00:01:36,540
In today's example, we're only going to print a statement and hide this ugly error message.

19
00:01:36,540 --> 00:01:42,350
But the same method can be used to execute any type of code when the exception happens.

20
00:01:42,360 --> 00:01:47,310
And I'm actually going to show you an example of that in the next lectures where we'll be restoring

21
00:01:47,310 --> 00:01:49,800
the ARP values to the way they were.

22
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:54,090
But first, let's learn how we can handle exceptions in Python.

23
00:01:54,690 --> 00:01:59,580
The way to do that, first of all, you want to see where your exception might happen.

24
00:01:59,970 --> 00:02:04,620
So we know that our exception is happening in this wild loop right here.

25
00:02:05,280 --> 00:02:13,380
So what we're going to do is we're going to say, I want you to try to go and execute this wild loop.

26
00:02:13,980 --> 00:02:20,340
Now, the try statement is similar to the wild and to the if statements so that it takes a block of

27
00:02:20,340 --> 00:02:22,050
code to execute.

28
00:02:22,470 --> 00:02:28,440
So the block of code that we want to execute in this try statement is this wild loop.

29
00:02:28,710 --> 00:02:35,610
So basically we're telling Python that I want you to try to execute this code right here, but at the

30
00:02:35,610 --> 00:02:37,830
same time, I want you to accept.

31
00:02:38,930 --> 00:02:47,120
A keyboard interrupt and I basically got this keyboard interrupt or the name of the exception from the

32
00:02:47,120 --> 00:02:47,420
error.

33
00:02:47,420 --> 00:02:52,100
So you can see that it's given us the name in here, which is keyboard interrupt.

34
00:02:52,910 --> 00:02:58,160
So what we're saying is we're saying that python, please, I want you to try to execute this while

35
00:02:58,160 --> 00:03:03,620
loop and I want you to expect or accept this kind of error.

36
00:03:04,040 --> 00:03:08,930
If this error happens, I'm going to give you a block of code to execute as well.

37
00:03:09,620 --> 00:03:12,080
And in here, you can use a function.

38
00:03:12,080 --> 00:03:14,660
You can use any type of code that you want.

39
00:03:14,660 --> 00:03:18,290
But in this very simple example, we're just going to print.

40
00:03:19,730 --> 00:03:23,900
And we're going to say detected control Z quitting.

41
00:03:25,090 --> 00:03:25,930
And that's it.

42
00:03:26,140 --> 00:03:29,180
So let's have one very quick look on the code.

43
00:03:29,200 --> 00:03:32,350
We're using a try and accept statement.

44
00:03:32,380 --> 00:03:36,250
The try statement takes a block of code to execute.

45
00:03:36,250 --> 00:03:40,690
So this is what's going to happen when Python interpreter reaches this part.

46
00:03:40,810 --> 00:03:46,240
It will never execute the exception part unless this exception happens.

47
00:03:46,240 --> 00:03:48,040
So the loop is going to keep running.

48
00:03:48,040 --> 00:03:54,700
Or the block of code that you use in the try statement will keep running until this error happens.

49
00:03:55,000 --> 00:03:57,760
So like I said, this error can be any type of error.

50
00:03:57,760 --> 00:04:02,830
For example, in the previous lecture, when we use the integer in the print statement here, you can

51
00:04:02,830 --> 00:04:04,570
use a try to detect that.

52
00:04:04,810 --> 00:04:10,420
In our example, we're waiting for a keyboard interrupt because that's what's what happened in here.

53
00:04:10,750 --> 00:04:18,010
So this code in the try block will keep executing until Python detects a keyboard interrupt or someone

54
00:04:18,010 --> 00:04:19,000
presses control.

55
00:04:19,000 --> 00:04:21,370
See if someone presses control.

56
00:04:21,400 --> 00:04:24,070
See, we're going to print this statement right here.

57
00:04:24,430 --> 00:04:26,560
So let's run our code again.

58
00:04:27,370 --> 00:04:30,070
And as you can see now, the code is running normally.

59
00:04:30,100 --> 00:04:31,900
Now I'm going to press control.

60
00:04:31,930 --> 00:04:32,620
See?

61
00:04:33,760 --> 00:04:39,580
And as you can see, we got a message saying detected controlled C quitting.

62
00:04:39,580 --> 00:04:42,850
So we didn't get this error message in here.

63
00:04:44,420 --> 00:04:50,240
Now this is actually very handy and you'll see the power of it as we write more complex programs and

64
00:04:50,240 --> 00:04:52,760
as you start learning more about Python.

65
00:04:53,150 --> 00:04:57,920
Right now, we're using it in a very simple way because I want to give you an introduction of it, but

66
00:04:57,920 --> 00:05:03,890
you'll see in the next lectures we're actually going to be implementing more code in here so that when

67
00:05:03,890 --> 00:05:11,060
the person presses control C, we will actually fix the network and restore the ARP tables to the way

68
00:05:11,060 --> 00:05:15,620
that they should be set or to the way that they were before running our attack.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:07,910
Now we created an ARP spoof or that works perfectly and can be used to redirect the flow of packets

2
00:00:07,910 --> 00:00:16,550
so that we can target a computer and intercept the requests sent and the responses received by this

3
00:00:16,550 --> 00:00:22,070
computer, which will effectively put us in the middle of the connection between this computer and the

4
00:00:22,070 --> 00:00:28,340
router or the gateway, allowing us to spy on that computer, read passwords, usernames, or anything

5
00:00:28,340 --> 00:00:31,640
they do on the internet, and even modify that.

6
00:00:31,940 --> 00:00:38,750
Now we can verify that we are becoming the man in the middle by looking at the ARP tables of the target

7
00:00:38,750 --> 00:00:39,590
computer.

8
00:00:39,710 --> 00:00:42,350
So if I just go and run my program.

9
00:00:44,290 --> 00:00:52,060
Then go to the Windows machine and you can see I've already executed RPA and you can see that the Mac

10
00:00:52,060 --> 00:00:56,620
address of the router in here associated with the router is IP.

11
00:00:56,800 --> 00:01:02,200
And if I just do IP again, you'll see the mac address associated with the routers.

12
00:01:02,200 --> 00:01:10,390
IP has changed to the MAC address of the attacker, which means that this computer thinks that Carly

13
00:01:10,390 --> 00:01:15,760
or my attacker computer is the router, therefore placing me in the middle of the connection.

14
00:01:16,420 --> 00:01:21,130
Now we also know if I press control, see here my program will quit.

15
00:01:21,520 --> 00:01:32,170
But if I go back to the Windows Machine and run RPA again and again and again, as you can see, the

16
00:01:32,170 --> 00:01:40,870
Mac address that's associated with the routers IP did not change back to the actual Mac address of the

17
00:01:40,870 --> 00:01:41,530
router.

18
00:01:41,680 --> 00:01:47,860
So this computer is still being fooled into thinking that Carly is the router.

19
00:01:48,010 --> 00:01:55,090
So even though we quit our program, the flow of packets is still following this diagram.

20
00:01:55,090 --> 00:02:01,780
So the victim computer or the target computer is still sending any requests that they want to send to

21
00:02:01,780 --> 00:02:02,290
the router.

22
00:02:02,290 --> 00:02:06,970
They're sending it to us and the router still thinking that we are the target.

23
00:02:06,970 --> 00:02:10,630
So any time it needs to send the responses, it sent it to us.

24
00:02:10,630 --> 00:02:13,090
So we are still the man in the middle.

25
00:02:13,420 --> 00:02:19,840
Now this is not good because in many cases you want to be able to quit the program and make everything

26
00:02:19,840 --> 00:02:21,250
go back to normal.

27
00:02:21,790 --> 00:02:28,540
So in order to achieve that, we're going to have to send two ARP responses the same way that we did

28
00:02:28,540 --> 00:02:31,270
when we were fooling the target and the router.

29
00:02:31,270 --> 00:02:36,220
But this time we will actually be nice and give them the right addresses.

30
00:02:36,460 --> 00:02:43,510
So we'll send an IP response to the victim telling it that the router is at this MAC address.

31
00:02:43,510 --> 00:02:47,650
So basically correcting the victim ARP table.

32
00:02:48,460 --> 00:02:55,570
We'll also do the same with the router, so we'll send it an ARP response saying this IP should be associated

33
00:02:55,570 --> 00:03:03,400
with this MAC address and we're only doing this so that we can restore the ARP tables in both the router

34
00:03:03,400 --> 00:03:07,780
and the target so that the traffic will go back to flowing normally.

35
00:03:07,780 --> 00:03:13,240
So whenever the victim or the target want to do anything, they'll send it to the router and whenever

36
00:03:13,240 --> 00:03:17,470
the router wants to communicate with the target, they'll send it back to the victim.

37
00:03:18,010 --> 00:03:24,100
This way we'll be restoring everything back to normal and our attack will be properly finished.

38
00:03:24,670 --> 00:03:30,070
Now, I should note, if you actually give it some time and let the target go and browse the internet,

39
00:03:30,070 --> 00:03:33,760
their ARP tables will go back to normal by default.

40
00:03:33,760 --> 00:03:40,540
But it's nice or it's better to actually restore everything to the way it was when we quit the program.

41
00:03:41,230 --> 00:03:48,430
So in order to do this, we're going to implement a function and we'll call this function Restore.

42
00:03:50,840 --> 00:03:57,290
Now similar to the spoof function here, this function is going to take two IP addresses, the IP of

43
00:03:57,290 --> 00:03:59,810
the target and IP of the router.

44
00:04:00,230 --> 00:04:06,800
Now I'm going to use generic variable names so we can use this function to fix the IP tables for the

45
00:04:06,800 --> 00:04:08,770
router and for the target.

46
00:04:08,780 --> 00:04:12,050
So I'm going to call my variables destination IP.

47
00:04:14,350 --> 00:04:15,730
And source IP.

48
00:04:16,120 --> 00:04:18,670
So as usual, these are just variable names.

49
00:04:18,670 --> 00:04:23,470
You can use any names that you think are suitable and easy for you to follow.

50
00:04:24,100 --> 00:04:31,780
Now, similar to the spoof function, we're going to need to create an ARP packet and we're going to

51
00:04:31,780 --> 00:04:34,150
need to store this packet in a packet variable.

52
00:04:34,150 --> 00:04:39,460
So I'm just going to do packet is equal to scapegoat ARP.

53
00:04:39,490 --> 00:04:46,210
So it's all similar to this right here and we're going to give the fields that we need to set.

54
00:04:46,450 --> 00:04:52,360
Now, when I created this packet in there, I actually spent a lot of time going and showing how we

55
00:04:52,360 --> 00:04:56,110
discovered the name of the fields and why we set it that way.

56
00:04:56,530 --> 00:05:01,060
So right now, I'm not going to show you how to see all the possible fields for ARP.

57
00:05:01,090 --> 00:05:06,610
I covered that previously, so if you don't remember how to do that, please go back and revise that.

58
00:05:07,000 --> 00:05:12,970
So we're going to set the LP to two and like I said, this will tell Skippy that this is going to be

59
00:05:12,970 --> 00:05:15,910
an ARP response, not an ARP request.

60
00:05:15,910 --> 00:05:23,290
If we set it to one, it's going to be an ARP request and we're going to set the PDC, which is the

61
00:05:23,290 --> 00:05:24,970
destination IP.

62
00:05:24,970 --> 00:05:29,410
So this is going to be the destination IP because we're taking this as a variable.

63
00:05:29,410 --> 00:05:38,260
And here we're also going to set the WD SD, which is the hardware address of the destination or the

64
00:05:38,260 --> 00:05:40,360
MAC address of the destination.

65
00:05:40,780 --> 00:05:48,160
Now we created a function in here that get us the Mac of an IP and we already have the destination IP

66
00:05:48,370 --> 00:05:52,990
so we can get the destination MAC address using the get Mac function.

67
00:05:53,530 --> 00:05:56,050
So I'm actually going to write this in here.

68
00:05:56,050 --> 00:06:01,150
I'm going to create a new variable and we're going to call this destination Mac.

69
00:06:03,190 --> 00:06:10,210
And this is going to be equal to get mark of the destination IP and then we're going to use the destination

70
00:06:10,210 --> 00:06:11,230
mark in here.

71
00:06:13,150 --> 00:06:20,770
Now, the third field that we need to set again, as shown in here, is going to be the P, Z and this

72
00:06:20,770 --> 00:06:22,180
is the source IP.

73
00:06:22,570 --> 00:06:26,830
And again, we're taking this as a variable as an input to the function.

74
00:06:26,830 --> 00:06:29,500
So we're just going to set this to source IP.

75
00:06:30,160 --> 00:06:33,970
Now, here is the difference between this line and this line.

76
00:06:33,970 --> 00:06:35,320
In this function.

77
00:06:35,320 --> 00:06:40,570
So far they are identical and using the same fields and the same variables.

78
00:06:40,930 --> 00:06:44,830
The only difference that's going to happen now is in this line.

79
00:06:44,830 --> 00:06:47,980
We didn't specify a source Mac address.

80
00:06:48,610 --> 00:06:55,540
The reason for that is Skippy will automatically put my Mac address as the source mac address.

81
00:06:56,750 --> 00:06:58,160
Now in this line.

82
00:06:58,160 --> 00:07:05,480
If we don't specify the source Mac address, Skippy again will automatically put my Mac address as the

83
00:07:05,480 --> 00:07:06,830
source Mac address.

84
00:07:07,250 --> 00:07:11,960
And if it does that, then this will not fix the ARP tables.

85
00:07:11,960 --> 00:07:15,260
Because what we want to say if we go back here again.

86
00:07:16,350 --> 00:07:23,370
We want to say that, for example, the router, which is ten 0 to 1, is at this Mac address.

87
00:07:23,640 --> 00:07:30,300
So if we leave out the Mac address, the message is going to be the router is at our MAC address, so

88
00:07:30,300 --> 00:07:33,960
we'll be spoofing the victim again and we don't want to do that.

89
00:07:34,560 --> 00:07:42,120
Therefore, we're going to manually set the h w as RC, which is the source Mac address.

90
00:07:42,900 --> 00:07:48,150
And we're going to have to set that to the Mac address of the source IP in here.

91
00:07:48,720 --> 00:07:53,100
So again, we'll just create a variable and we're going to call it source.

92
00:07:55,160 --> 00:07:59,900
And we're going to set that to get Mac of the sauce IP.

93
00:08:01,140 --> 00:08:02,430
And we'll use the source.

94
00:08:02,430 --> 00:08:03,570
Mac in here.

95
00:08:04,950 --> 00:08:05,720
And that's it.

96
00:08:05,730 --> 00:08:06,360
We're done.

97
00:08:07,050 --> 00:08:09,870
So let's go over this line one more time.

98
00:08:10,200 --> 00:08:16,380
Basically, what we are doing right here is we are creating an RFP response because we're setting the

99
00:08:16,380 --> 00:08:17,510
O.P. to two.

100
00:08:17,940 --> 00:08:24,480
We are setting the destination IP to the IP of the first argument that we get here.

101
00:08:24,510 --> 00:08:30,750
We're setting the destination Mac address to the destination Mac and we're getting that using the get

102
00:08:30,750 --> 00:08:33,390
Mac function that we created before.

103
00:08:33,840 --> 00:08:38,880
Then we're setting the source IP to the IP stored in the second variable.

104
00:08:39,270 --> 00:08:45,120
So all of this so far is identical to the IRP packet that does the spoofing.

105
00:08:45,360 --> 00:08:52,800
And like I said, if we omit the last field in here, Skip, you will automatically set the Mac address

106
00:08:52,800 --> 00:08:56,980
in this packet to my Mac address to Carly's Mac address.

107
00:08:57,000 --> 00:09:06,570
Therefore, we manually set the W as RC, which is the source Mac, and we set that to the mac address

108
00:09:06,570 --> 00:09:09,030
of the source IP.

109
00:09:09,450 --> 00:09:16,500
And again, we're getting the source Mac using the get mac function in here and we are giving it the

110
00:09:16,500 --> 00:09:17,390
source IP.

111
00:09:17,400 --> 00:09:20,760
So this is going automatically and getting me the MAC address.

112
00:09:21,510 --> 00:09:27,240
Now that this packet is done, we are ready to send it and we can send it using the send function in

113
00:09:27,240 --> 00:09:27,810
here.

114
00:09:28,110 --> 00:09:32,850
But before I do that, I just want to make sure that you understand what this packet is going to do.

115
00:09:32,880 --> 00:09:36,750
So as usual, we're going to print the packet.

116
00:09:37,520 --> 00:09:41,000
The show and the packet dot summary.

117
00:09:43,290 --> 00:09:46,170
And we will just call this at the start of the program.

118
00:09:46,170 --> 00:09:51,780
It's okay even before doing the ARP spoofing, because this is actually not going to send the packet.

119
00:09:51,780 --> 00:09:57,750
It will just print out the summary and the details of the packet so that we see what's going to what

120
00:09:57,750 --> 00:09:58,860
it's going to look like.

121
00:09:59,160 --> 00:10:03,810
So I'm just going to call the restore here from my main program, and I'm going to give it the first

122
00:10:03,810 --> 00:10:13,800
IP or the destination IP as the IP of the Windows machine and give it the source IP as the router.

123
00:10:14,400 --> 00:10:19,980
So effectively, this should be giving the Windows machine the right Mac address for the router.

124
00:10:20,610 --> 00:10:23,310
So let's go down and run our program.

125
00:10:24,850 --> 00:10:28,720
And I'm going to quit it straightaway because this is the first thing that's going to print and this

126
00:10:28,720 --> 00:10:30,310
is what I'm interested in.

127
00:10:31,240 --> 00:10:35,650
So looking at the details, you can see that this is an exact IRP packet.

128
00:10:35,650 --> 00:10:43,920
So it's a response saying that the device with this IP, so with this source IP, which is ten 0 to

129
00:10:43,920 --> 00:10:51,610
1, which is the IP of the router, can be found at this Mac address, which is the correct MAC address

130
00:10:51,610 --> 00:10:52,630
of the router.

131
00:10:52,630 --> 00:10:55,560
And this is the Mac address that we manually set.

132
00:10:55,570 --> 00:11:02,770
And here again, if we didn't set this, this would have been the Mac address of the Cally machine and

133
00:11:02,770 --> 00:11:06,940
therefore this would have fooled the target that Carly is the router.

134
00:11:07,540 --> 00:11:14,260
And you can also see that the destination is the Windows Machine Mac address and the destination IP

135
00:11:14,260 --> 00:11:16,030
is the Windows Machine IP.

136
00:11:16,780 --> 00:11:21,160
If you look at the summary here, you'll see that this is an IP packet.

137
00:11:21,190 --> 00:11:28,600
The type of this packet is is AD and it's basically saying that this IP address can be found at this

138
00:11:28,600 --> 00:11:29,560
MAC address.

139
00:11:29,950 --> 00:11:33,010
Again, this is the correct information in the network.

140
00:11:33,010 --> 00:11:39,610
And when this packet is sent, it will give the Windows machine the correct physical address or the

141
00:11:39,610 --> 00:11:42,160
correct MAC address of the router.

142
00:11:42,160 --> 00:11:47,950
Therefore, the Windows machine will be able to directly communicate with the router and it won't be

143
00:11:47,950 --> 00:11:49,180
spoofed anymore.

144
00:11:49,540 --> 00:11:54,490
Now in the next lecture we're going to implement this properly and we'll see it in action.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,850 --> 00:00:08,790
Now that we understand how the restore function works, let's go ahead and change the restore in here.

2
00:00:08,800 --> 00:00:13,450
Remove the print statements and send the packet that we created.

3
00:00:13,450 --> 00:00:19,330
So we're just going to do Skippy the send and we'll send our packet.

4
00:00:20,820 --> 00:00:28,170
I will also add one more argument to this, which is called count, and I'm going to set this to four.

5
00:00:28,170 --> 00:00:34,380
So we actually send this packet four times just to make sure that the target machine will receive it

6
00:00:34,380 --> 00:00:36,690
and correct its ARP table.

7
00:00:37,590 --> 00:00:46,560
I will also set the verbose to false so that it doesn't show me any information when it sends this packet.

8
00:00:46,560 --> 00:00:49,170
Something similar to what I was doing here.

9
00:00:49,170 --> 00:00:51,780
And then I'll print this statement in here.

10
00:00:52,980 --> 00:00:54,360
So this function is done.

11
00:00:54,360 --> 00:00:58,680
So I'm going to minimize it and I'm going to minimize this as well.

12
00:00:59,820 --> 00:01:06,360
And what we're going to do is we're going to call the restore from our accept block.

13
00:01:07,140 --> 00:01:09,270
So what happens when we reach this?

14
00:01:09,270 --> 00:01:15,870
As I explained before, when we use a try statement, it will execute the block of code that we have

15
00:01:15,870 --> 00:01:16,920
in the try.

16
00:01:17,400 --> 00:01:23,460
This will run the ARP spoof or it will spoof the targets, putting us in the middle of the connection.

17
00:01:23,850 --> 00:01:28,860
Then it's going to expect the user to interrupt this using control.

18
00:01:28,890 --> 00:01:31,860
See if the user enters control.

19
00:01:31,890 --> 00:01:39,300
See, we're going to print detected control C and then we are going to fix the IRP tables.

20
00:01:39,300 --> 00:01:41,370
So we're going to call the restore function.

21
00:01:41,850 --> 00:01:50,160
Now, instead of saying quitting in here, I'm actually going to say resetting IRP tables.

22
00:01:51,770 --> 00:01:52,850
Please wait.

23
00:01:54,720 --> 00:02:02,040
And then we have our function here, which will reset the device at ten 027 with the correct values

24
00:02:02,040 --> 00:02:06,510
for the IP that is at ten 021, which is the router.

25
00:02:06,990 --> 00:02:11,460
Now we are using way too many variables as constants in here.

26
00:02:11,670 --> 00:02:17,850
So I'm just going to do this quickly and I'm going to define two variables in here.

27
00:02:17,880 --> 00:02:20,880
One of them we will call Target IP.

28
00:02:21,750 --> 00:02:25,200
And this is going to be the IP of the Windows machine, as you know.

29
00:02:27,110 --> 00:02:30,290
And the next one is going to be the gateway IP.

30
00:02:32,040 --> 00:02:34,710
And this is going to be the IP of the router.

31
00:02:35,770 --> 00:02:42,280
Now you can use any of the ways that I showed you before to get this from the user using the input function

32
00:02:42,280 --> 00:02:43,960
or using the arguments.

33
00:02:44,320 --> 00:02:47,980
For now, we're keeping this simple and we're setting it in the code.

34
00:02:48,550 --> 00:02:50,860
So we're just going to modify this.

35
00:02:50,860 --> 00:02:54,160
We're going to change this with the target IP here.

36
00:02:55,790 --> 00:02:58,280
And this will be the gateway IP.

37
00:03:00,090 --> 00:03:01,380
Same here.

38
00:03:01,560 --> 00:03:07,110
But this time, the gateway is going to be here because we're spoofing the gateway, telling it that

39
00:03:07,110 --> 00:03:08,580
we are the target.

40
00:03:10,620 --> 00:03:15,820
And when we want to restore this, we're actually going to have to do the restore twice as well.

41
00:03:15,840 --> 00:03:20,610
So, first of all, we're going to restore the ARP tables of the target.

42
00:03:22,120 --> 00:03:24,730
Giving it the correct values for the gateway.

43
00:03:25,870 --> 00:03:33,160
And we'll have to do this one more time, giving the gateway the correct Mac address of the target.

44
00:03:35,450 --> 00:03:36,250
And this is it.

45
00:03:36,260 --> 00:03:42,800
So basically what I did right now is whenever the user hits control, see, the following block of code

46
00:03:42,800 --> 00:03:44,330
is going to be executed.

47
00:03:44,360 --> 00:03:51,380
The first call to the restore function will give the target the right MAC address of the router.

48
00:03:51,380 --> 00:03:57,350
And the second time we're going to give the Gateway the correct Mac address of the target.

49
00:03:58,100 --> 00:04:04,370
So what we're doing is we're basically sending this message right here and this message right here,

50
00:04:04,370 --> 00:04:07,520
fixing the ARP tables of both clients.

51
00:04:08,210 --> 00:04:14,390
And this will bring my network back to the default settings and it's going to look like this so that

52
00:04:14,390 --> 00:04:18,320
the victim and the router will be directly communicating with each other.

53
00:04:19,700 --> 00:04:21,020
So let's go and test this.

54
00:04:21,020 --> 00:04:22,340
I'm going to save this.

55
00:04:22,370 --> 00:04:26,180
Go down here, clear the screen and run my code.

56
00:04:27,570 --> 00:04:30,270
Now this is AARP spoofing my target.

57
00:04:30,360 --> 00:04:36,270
If we go to the Windows machine, clear this and run an RPA.

58
00:04:36,330 --> 00:04:42,990
And as expected, the Router's Mac address now is associated with the Mac address of the machine.

59
00:04:43,230 --> 00:04:46,770
So now my machine is in the middle of the connection.

60
00:04:47,220 --> 00:04:53,220
If we go back, press control, see, we're going to get a nice print statement telling us that the

61
00:04:53,220 --> 00:04:58,680
ARP tables are going to be reset to the way they were and then the program will quit.

62
00:04:58,740 --> 00:05:08,400
But if we come here, run RPA, you will see that the Mac address associated with the router is the

63
00:05:08,400 --> 00:05:10,570
actual router is Mac address.

64
00:05:10,590 --> 00:05:15,690
So everything has been restored back to the default to the right way that it should be.

65
00:05:15,780 --> 00:05:21,060
And now the router and the target are directly communicating with each other.

66
00:05:21,630 --> 00:05:27,990
This way our program is more complete and it actually works like the proper IP spoofing programs like

67
00:05:27,990 --> 00:05:32,220
ITER, cab ARP Spoof Man and the Middle F and so on.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 6: Writing a Packet Sniffer

1
00:00:00,680 --> 00:00:06,890
In the previous section, we built a program that will allow us to run an ARP spoofing attack, which

2
00:00:06,890 --> 00:00:12,770
will allow us to redirect the flow of packets in the network and place us in the middle of the connection

3
00:00:12,770 --> 00:00:16,250
between any clients in the network and the router.

4
00:00:16,880 --> 00:00:23,540
This means that anything that this client sends or receive will go through our own computer, allowing

5
00:00:23,540 --> 00:00:30,290
us to see all the passwords, all the usernames, the URLs, the images, the messages that they send

6
00:00:30,290 --> 00:00:31,310
and receive.

7
00:00:32,650 --> 00:00:34,390
So this is very, very cool.

8
00:00:35,020 --> 00:00:40,750
Right now we have a program that will put us in the middle of the connection, but we still don't know

9
00:00:40,750 --> 00:00:42,460
how to read this information.

10
00:00:42,460 --> 00:00:49,000
So this information is flowing through our computer, but right now, we're still not reading this information

11
00:00:49,000 --> 00:00:51,880
and we have no way of reading this information.

12
00:00:52,180 --> 00:00:58,480
Now, if this information flows as packets and in order to read this information, you'll need a packet

13
00:00:58,480 --> 00:00:59,170
sniffer.

14
00:00:59,710 --> 00:01:05,740
Now, again, Kleenex contains a number of tools that allow you to sniff packets and read this data.

15
00:01:05,740 --> 00:01:11,440
And I actually cover these tools in my other courses and my general ethical hacking course and in my

16
00:01:11,440 --> 00:01:13,300
advanced network hacking course.

17
00:01:13,300 --> 00:01:17,680
But as you know, in this course we don't use any pre built tools.

18
00:01:17,680 --> 00:01:24,820
So in this section I want to build our own packet sniffer so that whenever we are the man in the middle,

19
00:01:24,820 --> 00:01:30,730
we'll be able to read all the information that flows through our computer and we'll be able to see all

20
00:01:30,730 --> 00:01:37,600
the usernames, passwords, URLs and so on that any target computer sends or receives.

21
00:01:38,050 --> 00:01:44,950
Now the idea of the packet sniffer is it's basically a program that reads packets or data that flow

22
00:01:44,950 --> 00:01:46,210
through an interface.

23
00:01:46,450 --> 00:01:51,730
So at the start of the section, we're actually going to build a program that will allow us to read

24
00:01:51,730 --> 00:01:54,820
the data that flow through a certain interface.

25
00:01:55,300 --> 00:02:01,750
Then once we use the tool that we built previously to become the man in the middle, the data will flow

26
00:02:01,750 --> 00:02:05,740
through our interface by default because we'll become the man in the middle.

27
00:02:05,740 --> 00:02:11,800
And you'll see at the end of this section how we'll be able to read the usernames and passwords entered

28
00:02:11,800 --> 00:02:15,730
by a remote computer that is connected to the same network.

29
00:02:16,700 --> 00:02:21,590
Now, before we jump into programming, I want to show you the program that we're going to build in

30
00:02:21,590 --> 00:02:22,220
action.

31
00:02:22,220 --> 00:02:23,970
So this is just a teaser.

32
00:02:23,990 --> 00:02:25,700
Don't worry about how this works.

33
00:02:25,700 --> 00:02:27,640
We're going to cover that in this section.

34
00:02:27,650 --> 00:02:31,460
I just want you to have an idea of how this is going to work.

35
00:02:33,410 --> 00:02:39,980
So right here I have the AAP pooper and the packet sniffer that we're going to write in this section

36
00:02:39,980 --> 00:02:41,930
running at the same time.

37
00:02:43,110 --> 00:02:46,700
So let's go to the Windows Machine and just go to any website.

38
00:02:46,710 --> 00:02:48,480
So let's go to CNN.com.

39
00:02:49,380 --> 00:02:55,410
And now if we come back, as you can see, first of all, we can see Bill.com Dotcom has been registered

40
00:02:55,410 --> 00:02:57,750
as a website that the target visited.

41
00:02:58,080 --> 00:03:01,140
We can also see all the files that has been loaded.

42
00:03:01,140 --> 00:03:05,730
So the web page automatically loaded some JavaScript and we can see it here.

43
00:03:05,970 --> 00:03:08,910
We can also see the images that have been loaded.

44
00:03:09,270 --> 00:03:15,690
And like I said, anything that the user is going to load through the browser will actually be captured

45
00:03:15,690 --> 00:03:16,800
in our sniffer.

46
00:03:17,580 --> 00:03:21,090
Now let me show you an example of logging in to our log in page.

47
00:03:22,050 --> 00:03:27,430
So I'm back at the Windows Machine here and I'm at a website called Dictionary.com.

48
00:03:27,450 --> 00:03:29,580
So if we go to the login page.

49
00:03:31,390 --> 00:03:33,100
And put some fake information.

50
00:03:33,100 --> 00:03:38,800
So we'll just put an email address test at gmail.com and then put any password.

51
00:03:39,690 --> 00:03:40,950
We're going to hit enter.

52
00:03:42,210 --> 00:03:47,940
And now if we go back and scroll up in our sniffer.

53
00:03:49,490 --> 00:03:56,000
As you can see, it's telling me that there is a possible username password combination and it's also

54
00:03:56,000 --> 00:04:03,020
showing me the username which was test at gmail.com and the password that I entered which was one,

55
00:04:03,020 --> 00:04:05,210
two, three, four, five, six, seven.

56
00:04:06,480 --> 00:04:09,660
So as you can see, the sniffer is pretty useful.

57
00:04:09,690 --> 00:04:16,380
It builds up on what we did so far on the ARP spoof program, and it's going to allow us to see all

58
00:04:16,380 --> 00:04:22,440
the passwords, username URLs or anything that any user does on our network.

59
00:04:22,470 --> 00:04:28,470
Of course, after we become the man in the middle again, don't worry about how this works.

60
00:04:28,470 --> 00:04:32,790
We will build this program from scratch in this section.

61
00:04:32,790 --> 00:04:38,730
So this is just a teaser to show you what we want to achieve by the end of the section.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,790 --> 00:00:08,740
Now in order to sniff or capture data that is being sent to or from one of our interfaces on our computer,

2
00:00:08,830 --> 00:00:13,420
we can use a function called sniff that comes in with Skippy.

3
00:00:13,900 --> 00:00:19,330
So in this lecture, I'm going to give you a basic example on sniffing data through an interface on

4
00:00:19,330 --> 00:00:24,670
our computer, and then as usual, will start building on that as we go through the course.

5
00:00:25,390 --> 00:00:31,780
Now I've already created a project and it's called Packet Sniffer, and I've already imported Skippy,

6
00:00:31,780 --> 00:00:33,160
as we have been doing.

7
00:00:33,760 --> 00:00:38,740
So now what I want to create is a function and I'm going to call this function sniff.

8
00:00:39,670 --> 00:00:45,010
I'm going to make this function, take a variable called interface, and this will be the interface

9
00:00:45,010 --> 00:00:48,160
that we will be sniffing or capturing data from.

10
00:00:48,790 --> 00:00:55,420
Now, inside this function, I'm actually going to call escape function and the name of this function

11
00:00:55,420 --> 00:00:56,980
is sniff as well.

12
00:00:58,310 --> 00:01:01,460
Now this function can take a number of arguments.

13
00:01:01,670 --> 00:01:05,060
The ones I want to use is, first of all, the interface.

14
00:01:05,060 --> 00:01:06,900
So it's called face.

15
00:01:07,250 --> 00:01:12,500
And this basically specifies the interface that you want this function to sniff on.

16
00:01:12,560 --> 00:01:18,470
And because we're taking the interface as an argument for our own function, we'll just pass that on

17
00:01:18,470 --> 00:01:19,310
to here.

18
00:01:19,610 --> 00:01:21,770
So we're going to give this interface.

19
00:01:22,860 --> 00:01:26,880
The next argument that I want to use is an argument called Store.

20
00:01:27,180 --> 00:01:33,810
And what this argument does, it basically tells KP not to store packets in memory so that it doesn't

21
00:01:33,810 --> 00:01:36,210
cause too much pressure on our computer.

22
00:01:36,360 --> 00:01:39,720
