So we'll be printing packets and doing stuff with it later on.

23
00:01:39,720 --> 00:01:45,540
But I'm telling you that I don't want you to store anything for me, so we're going to set this to false.

24
00:01:46,020 --> 00:01:50,370
And the last argument that I want to use is the p r and argument.

25
00:01:51,210 --> 00:01:55,380
Now, this argument allows us to specify a callback function.

26
00:01:55,950 --> 00:02:02,820
What we mean by a callback function is a function that will be called every time this function captures

27
00:02:02,820 --> 00:02:03,630
a packet.

28
00:02:03,750 --> 00:02:09,360
So for each packet that we capture, this will execute another function for us.

29
00:02:09,690 --> 00:02:12,480
So let me implement this and it will become clearer.

30
00:02:13,260 --> 00:02:19,620
So first of all, I have to give the name of my callback function in here for the PRN argument and let's

31
00:02:19,620 --> 00:02:22,770
call this function process snipped packet.

32
00:02:24,390 --> 00:02:26,400
Now you can see that pie chart.

33
00:02:26,520 --> 00:02:33,240
It tells me that this is an unresolved reference because I have nothing called process sniff packet.

34
00:02:33,390 --> 00:02:39,600
So I'm basically saying for each packet that you capture in here, I want you to execute this function.

35
00:02:39,930 --> 00:02:43,920
But I haven't defined this function in my program.

36
00:02:44,220 --> 00:02:51,300
So what I need to do is I need to just go down here and create another function and we're going to call

37
00:02:51,300 --> 00:02:56,340
this function the same name that we used in here, which is processed, sniffed packet.

38
00:02:57,090 --> 00:03:00,150
And here this function is going to have to take a packet.

39
00:03:00,150 --> 00:03:03,600
So this is the packet that we actually received or sniffed.

40
00:03:04,230 --> 00:03:09,930
And in here you can do whatever you want, you can filter this packet, you can modify it, you can

41
00:03:09,930 --> 00:03:11,610
do a lot of cool stuff.

42
00:03:12,150 --> 00:03:17,700
So what I want to do right now is simply just print the packet that we snipped and later on we'll be

43
00:03:17,700 --> 00:03:19,470
doing more complex stuff.

44
00:03:19,860 --> 00:03:23,070
So right now all I'm going to do is just print packet.

45
00:03:24,330 --> 00:03:29,880
Now, finally, in my main program, all I'm going to do is just I'm going to call my sniff function

46
00:03:30,390 --> 00:03:34,230
and I'm going to give it my interface, which is ETA zero.

47
00:03:34,770 --> 00:03:40,590
Now I'm using ETA zero because this is the interface that is connected to the Internet.

48
00:03:40,620 --> 00:03:44,400
It is the interface that is connected to the network that I'm targeting.

49
00:03:44,790 --> 00:03:49,560
So if you're targeting a wi fi network and you have an interface called Line Zero, then that's the

50
00:03:49,560 --> 00:03:50,910
one that you should use.

51
00:03:50,940 --> 00:03:57,720
In my case, when I do if config, you'll see that we have been targeting the network that has IPS on

52
00:03:57,720 --> 00:04:02,640
this subnet, which is ten zero two, and therefore this is the interface that I'm using.

53
00:04:03,730 --> 00:04:09,280
So let's have a quick look now on the code that we wrote so far, and then we'll execute it and see

54
00:04:09,280 --> 00:04:10,180
how it works.

55
00:04:10,450 --> 00:04:15,550
Now, as you can see, the main function that I defined in this lecture was the snip function, which

56
00:04:15,550 --> 00:04:20,920
takes an interface which refers to the interface that we want to capture data from.

57
00:04:21,490 --> 00:04:27,280
In this function, we're actually calling another function, which is a function made by KP, and this

58
00:04:27,280 --> 00:04:32,860
function will do the hard work for us, the sniffing data that is flowing through the interface that

59
00:04:32,860 --> 00:04:35,500
we give as the efface argument.

60
00:04:36,070 --> 00:04:41,290
We are also telling this function, Please don't store anything in memory so that it doesn't cause too

61
00:04:41,290 --> 00:04:43,120
much load on my computer.

62
00:04:43,570 --> 00:04:50,530
And finally, I'm telling this function that for every packet that you sniff or for every piece of data

63
00:04:50,530 --> 00:04:56,230
that you sniff, I want you to call another function called process sniff packet.

64
00:04:56,950 --> 00:05:00,910
Then I define this other function, which is called process sniff packet.

65
00:05:00,940 --> 00:05:06,850
This function will take a packet, which is the packet that we have sniffed in here, and then we're

66
00:05:06,850 --> 00:05:11,500
literally just printing this packet on screen to make sure that everything is working properly.

67
00:05:11,980 --> 00:05:18,280
And then in my main program I call the sniff function passing ITI had zero to it because this is the

68
00:05:18,280 --> 00:05:21,910
interface that is connected to the network that we're targeting.

69
00:05:22,600 --> 00:05:28,360
Now in this lecture, I'm actually not going to become the man in the middle, because right now in

70
00:05:28,360 --> 00:05:34,480
these few lectures, while we are building the sniffer, it's much easier to test it on our own computer.

71
00:05:34,480 --> 00:05:39,220
So we're literally going to be using the browser here to generate traffic.

72
00:05:39,220 --> 00:05:45,010
We're going to test our sniffer here, and if it works here, it will automatically work when we become

73
00:05:45,010 --> 00:05:46,090
the man in the middle.

74
00:05:46,090 --> 00:05:52,150
Because when we become the man in the middle, the ARP spoof or the program that put us in the middle

75
00:05:52,150 --> 00:05:58,050
will do the hard work and it will automatically make the data flow through to zero.

76
00:05:58,060 --> 00:06:00,190
So the sniffer doesn't have to do anything.

77
00:06:00,190 --> 00:06:06,880
All it's doing is it's going to analyze and read the data that flows through to zero and the AAP spoof

78
00:06:06,880 --> 00:06:12,010
or the Man in the Middle program that you'll use will actually put us in the middle of the connection,

79
00:06:12,010 --> 00:06:16,720
forcing data from other computers to flow through to zero.

80
00:06:18,000 --> 00:06:24,390
So right now I'm just going to clear this and I'm already in the working directory of my program, so

81
00:06:24,390 --> 00:06:32,460
all I'm going to do is just do python packet sniffer dot py I'm going to hit enter and then I'm simply

82
00:06:32,460 --> 00:06:35,220
going to generate some traffic here on my computer.

83
00:06:35,220 --> 00:06:37,380
And I'm just going to go to BBC.com.

84
00:06:39,630 --> 00:06:46,650
And as you can see now, anything that we do in here is actually flowing through or being captured through

85
00:06:46,650 --> 00:06:49,500
our program and being displayed on screen.

86
00:06:49,950 --> 00:06:55,290
Now, like I said, if you are the man in the middle, this data will automatically go through it zero.

87
00:06:55,290 --> 00:07:00,540
So whenever your target tries to go somewhere or do anything, it will flow through to zero.

88
00:07:00,540 --> 00:07:05,580
And then the program will be able to read it and we'll have examples of that in a few lectures from

89
00:07:05,580 --> 00:07:06,090
now.

90
00:07:06,600 --> 00:07:07,830
Now, I know what you're thinking.

91
00:07:07,830 --> 00:07:11,400
You're thinking this is all gibberish and it has nothing useful to us.

92
00:07:11,400 --> 00:07:17,340
But in the next lecture you'll see how we can dissect these packets and extract useful data such as

93
00:07:17,340 --> 00:07:19,380
usernames and passwords from them.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,870 --> 00:00:07,440
Now, as you can see, we're able to capture or sniff packets that go through a certain interface on

2
00:00:07,440 --> 00:00:08,520
our computer.

3
00:00:09,180 --> 00:00:15,430
But as you can see, what we're capturing is kind of useless because it is very hard to read.

4
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:17,010
It looks like it's gibberish.

5
00:00:17,550 --> 00:00:24,960
Now, the reason why it looks like this, because this is all the packets, all the data that is flowing

6
00:00:24,960 --> 00:00:26,370
through the interface.

7
00:00:27,120 --> 00:00:34,050
So if we want to read the interesting stuff, we're going to have to filter this and find the interesting

8
00:00:34,050 --> 00:00:36,540
stuff within all of this gibberish.

9
00:00:37,110 --> 00:00:42,210
So in this lecture, we're going to have a look on some basics about filtering this data.

10
00:00:42,210 --> 00:00:45,870
And then again, we'll build up on that as we go through the course.

11
00:00:46,440 --> 00:00:53,640
So the easiest way to filter packets is using the filter argument in this nip function in here.

12
00:00:54,390 --> 00:01:00,630
So right now we're passing three arguments and we can also pass another argument which is called filter.

13
00:01:02,300 --> 00:01:10,190
Now this argument allows us to filter packets using the BPF or Berkeley packet filter syntax.

14
00:01:10,700 --> 00:01:16,130
I'm going to include a link in the description for all the filters that you can use, but using it is

15
00:01:16,130 --> 00:01:17,180
pretty simple.

16
00:01:17,210 --> 00:01:22,170
Basically, let's say you're looking for packets that are being sent over UDP.

17
00:01:22,190 --> 00:01:25,070
Then all you have to do is just type UDP.

18
00:01:25,860 --> 00:01:34,040
Now UDP is a protocol that's usually used to send videos, audio plays, phone calls and such because

19
00:01:34,040 --> 00:01:36,680
it's much quicker than the normal TCP.

20
00:01:37,820 --> 00:01:38,690
Another example.

21
00:01:38,690 --> 00:01:44,210
Let's say you're only looking for ARP packets, then you can just type in ARP here.

22
00:01:44,960 --> 00:01:49,340
If you're looking for TCP packets, then you type in TCP.

23
00:01:50,000 --> 00:01:52,220
You can also filter based on ports.

24
00:01:52,220 --> 00:01:57,980
For example, let's say you're looking to capture data that is used on a certain service or a certain

25
00:01:57,980 --> 00:01:58,820
protocol.

26
00:01:58,850 --> 00:02:03,350
For example, let's say your targeting, you're looking for FTP passwords.

27
00:02:03,350 --> 00:02:06,980
Now we know FTP is used on Port 21.

28
00:02:06,980 --> 00:02:14,660
So you can just say Port 21 in here to capture data that's only being sent to Port 21.

29
00:02:15,260 --> 00:02:17,900
Again, you can use any type of port that you want in here.

30
00:02:17,900 --> 00:02:23,990
For example, you can use 80 to capture data sent to Web servers because web servers and websites are

31
00:02:23,990 --> 00:02:28,250
run on Port 80 by default and so on.

32
00:02:28,760 --> 00:02:34,040
Now, like I said, there is a large number of ways to write filters with this, and the way to do it

33
00:02:34,040 --> 00:02:39,380
is using the BPF syntax and I will include the link of that in the description.

34
00:02:40,160 --> 00:02:47,900
The problem here is our goal is to be able to capture usernames, passwords or URLs that the target

35
00:02:47,900 --> 00:02:49,790
is entering on their browser.

36
00:02:50,420 --> 00:02:58,610
Unfortunately, the BPF syntax or the filter argument in here does not allow us to filter packets that's

37
00:02:58,610 --> 00:03:00,830
being sent over http.

38
00:03:00,860 --> 00:03:04,520
So the packets that are being sent to websites or from websites.

39
00:03:05,650 --> 00:03:12,490
Therefore, we're going to have to install a third party module to be able to filter HTTP packets.

40
00:03:12,850 --> 00:03:15,310
So first of all, I'm going to delete all of this.

41
00:03:16,690 --> 00:03:20,980
And then I'm going to go on my terminal and clear all of this.

42
00:03:21,550 --> 00:03:23,380
And in here I'm going to use Pip.

43
00:03:23,380 --> 00:03:29,170
And we had a look on Pip before and we said, this is the tool that we use to install third party modules

44
00:03:29,170 --> 00:03:36,190
on Python and the module name that we want to install is Skippy http.

45
00:03:36,520 --> 00:03:40,840
So we're saying pip install skippy http.

46
00:03:42,090 --> 00:03:43,770
Now I've already installed that.

47
00:03:43,770 --> 00:03:46,710
So as you can see, it's telling me that it's already installed.

48
00:03:46,710 --> 00:03:49,740
But if it's not installed for you, it should install it.

49
00:03:51,310 --> 00:03:56,500
Now I'm going to include a link for the documentation of this module and the resources.

50
00:03:56,770 --> 00:04:04,000
But according to the documentation, we can import this by saying from scapegoat players import HTTP.

51
00:04:04,880 --> 00:04:08,060
Now that's it imported and we are ready to use it.

52
00:04:08,390 --> 00:04:12,950
So we know for each packet that we sniff will execute this function.

53
00:04:12,950 --> 00:04:16,820
And right now this function only prints the packet that we sniff.

54
00:04:17,510 --> 00:04:21,690
Our goal right now is to print only HTTP packets.

55
00:04:21,710 --> 00:04:28,820
So what we're going to do is I'm going to, first of all, remove this and I'm going to use an if statement

56
00:04:29,180 --> 00:04:30,980
to check if the packet.

57
00:04:31,750 --> 00:04:33,700
Has a HTTP layer.

58
00:04:33,700 --> 00:04:40,720
So we're going to say packet dot has layer, http dot http request.

59
00:04:42,420 --> 00:04:48,990
So basically what we're saying is if our packet has a layer and the layer that we're asking if it has,

60
00:04:48,990 --> 00:04:51,630
it is a HTTP request.

61
00:04:51,840 --> 00:04:56,430
So if my packet has that layer, I want you to print this packet for me.

62
00:04:57,500 --> 00:05:02,360
So instead of printing all packets, we're using a simple if statement.

63
00:05:02,780 --> 00:05:07,310
In this simple statement, we're checking if the packet has a layer.

64
00:05:07,310 --> 00:05:16,070
So has there is a method that's implemented by Skippy that checks if this packet has data that is being

65
00:05:16,070 --> 00:05:21,440
sent over the layer that we specify in here so we can give any layer we want.

66
00:05:21,440 --> 00:05:27,230
For example, we can check if the packet has a TCP layer, if the packet has an Ethernet layer.

67
00:05:27,230 --> 00:05:32,000
But in our example, we're checking if the packet has HTTP layer.

68
00:05:32,690 --> 00:05:39,620
The reason for that is because URLs, images, videos or passwords are pretty much everything that is

69
00:05:39,620 --> 00:05:44,090
sent using a web browser is sent using the HTTP layer.

70
00:05:44,330 --> 00:05:47,720
So this is what contains interesting stuff for us.

71
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:53,120
So we're saying if the packet has a HTTP layer, I want you to print this packet for me.

72
00:05:54,080 --> 00:05:56,630
Now, let's go and run our program.

73
00:05:59,100 --> 00:06:01,500
And let's just search for anything in here.

74
00:06:03,550 --> 00:06:09,820
Now, as you can see, we're still getting a lot of data in here, but this data is actually much more

75
00:06:09,820 --> 00:06:12,520
useful than what we were getting before.

76
00:06:12,580 --> 00:06:18,310
So you can see we can see the referral URL, we can see the HTTP requests.

77
00:06:18,310 --> 00:06:23,110
For example, we have a request here requesting this particular URL.

78
00:06:23,350 --> 00:06:30,340
You'll also be able to see the images, the URLs and the passwords if the user enters a password.

79
00:06:30,880 --> 00:06:35,080
So this is much better than what we did before, but it's still not great.

80
00:06:35,080 --> 00:06:40,870
It's not gibberish, it's useful stuff, but it's still hidden behind a lot of other data.

81
00:06:41,380 --> 00:06:47,950
So in the next lecture, we're continue building on this and we'll see how we can extract only the useful

82
00:06:47,950 --> 00:06:49,420
parts that we want.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,720 --> 00:00:01,160
Okay.

2
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:08,070
Now, like I said, our sniffer is able to capture anything that is sent over HTTP.

3
00:00:08,160 --> 00:00:16,320
And as an example, we've seen how to filter the URLs, visited using the browser in this lecture and

4
00:00:16,320 --> 00:00:23,910
the next two lectures I want to show you how to filter the login information such as usernames and passwords

5
00:00:23,910 --> 00:00:25,860
entered through the browser.

6
00:00:27,380 --> 00:00:33,350
Now we're going to be testing this against a HTTP login page, not a HTTPS.

7
00:00:33,620 --> 00:00:38,000
And I know most websites use HTTPS these days.

8
00:00:38,270 --> 00:00:44,720
The only thing is whenever you're building a program and the program is still not ready, it's best

9
00:00:44,720 --> 00:00:48,470
to focus on the simplest form of your problem.

10
00:00:48,590 --> 00:00:58,460
So the simplest form of our problem is to filter data from HTTP login pages because dealing with https

11
00:00:58,460 --> 00:01:01,280
will add an extra layer of complexity.

12
00:01:01,910 --> 00:01:07,130
So we'll try to get our program to work with a normal HTTP login page.

13
00:01:07,130 --> 00:01:14,690
And once it's working well, we'll see how we can build on our program and improve it so that we can

14
00:01:14,690 --> 00:01:22,520
sniff data from login pages that use HTTPS such as Hotmail, Apple and so on.

15
00:01:24,090 --> 00:01:30,790
So I'm going to be testing this against a HTTP login page on a website called Dictionary.com.

16
00:01:30,810 --> 00:01:37,290
But you won't be able to test against this website because they removed their login functionality.

17
00:01:37,440 --> 00:01:45,130
But you can test this against any other login page that uses http, not https.

18
00:01:45,150 --> 00:01:49,650
So you can use Google to look for login pages that use HTTP.

19
00:01:49,680 --> 00:01:55,740
Or you can use one of the websites that I'm going to include in the resources of this lecture.

20
00:01:55,740 --> 00:02:00,240
So you can access the resources from the top left of the lecture.

21
00:02:01,590 --> 00:02:03,990
And in here I'm just going to put a test email.

22
00:02:03,990 --> 00:02:12,480
So we're just going to put this as test at Google or gmail.com and then I'll put the password as test,

23
00:02:12,510 --> 00:02:15,810
test, test, test, just something big.

24
00:02:15,810 --> 00:02:21,330
So we'll be able to spot it easy in here because as you can see, we're getting a lot of information

25
00:02:21,330 --> 00:02:21,990
in here.

26
00:02:22,770 --> 00:02:24,180
So I'm going to hit Enter.

27
00:02:25,930 --> 00:02:27,340
And I'm going to do control.

28
00:02:27,340 --> 00:02:31,390
See here and let's go up and try to find our password.

29
00:02:33,610 --> 00:02:36,230
Now, as you can see, we're able to capture it here.

30
00:02:36,250 --> 00:02:42,940
The username is test at gmail.com and the password is test, test, test, test.

31
00:02:43,480 --> 00:02:51,610
So like I said, everything is being captured through Skype and we're able to display it in here.

32
00:02:51,820 --> 00:02:58,270
Now, the same idea you can use to capture FTP packets if you just set the port to 21 like I showed

33
00:02:58,270 --> 00:03:02,050
you before using the filter argument and so on.

34
00:03:02,800 --> 00:03:04,450
Now I still don't like this.

35
00:03:04,450 --> 00:03:06,160
There's still a lot of information.

36
00:03:06,160 --> 00:03:11,110
And if you think of it, if you're actually doing this against the proper computer, it will be very

37
00:03:11,110 --> 00:03:13,570
hard for you to spot the username and password.

38
00:03:13,570 --> 00:03:19,860
We're able to see it now because we know exactly when we clicked the log in and then looked for it.

39
00:03:19,870 --> 00:03:23,980
But in a real life scenario it will be very hard for you to spot it.

40
00:03:24,550 --> 00:03:32,650
So what I want to do is I want to add some kind of filter to my program so that I can only see usernames

41
00:03:32,650 --> 00:03:33,670
and passwords.

42
00:03:34,360 --> 00:03:38,200
So let's first of all, clear the screen here and go up.

43
00:03:39,190 --> 00:03:44,950
And instead of just printing the packet, I'm going to print packet dot show.

44
00:03:45,400 --> 00:03:51,970
Now we know from the previous lectures this will show me all the layers in the packet and the fields

45
00:03:51,970 --> 00:03:54,460
that are set in this packet.

46
00:03:55,000 --> 00:04:01,840
The reason why I'm doing this, because I want to see which layer and which field is being used to send

47
00:04:01,840 --> 00:04:02,710
the password.

48
00:04:02,920 --> 00:04:09,340
If I know which layer and which field, then I can program my program here so that it only prints that

49
00:04:09,340 --> 00:04:10,990
layer and that field.

50
00:04:11,680 --> 00:04:14,950
So let's go down and run our program.

51
00:04:15,370 --> 00:04:21,640
Now, this will actually display even more data than what we were getting before because we're printing

52
00:04:21,640 --> 00:04:24,100
the show of each packet.

53
00:04:24,610 --> 00:04:33,070
So I'm going to configure my terminal here so that it has infinite scrolling, because by default the

54
00:04:33,070 --> 00:04:37,110
terminal will only allow you to go back 500 lines.

55
00:04:37,120 --> 00:04:43,720
So because this will show us a lot of information, the username and password might be beyond these

56
00:04:43,720 --> 00:04:44,950
500 lines.

57
00:04:45,040 --> 00:04:49,540
So what I'm going to do, I'm going to go right click preferences.

58
00:04:50,330 --> 00:04:51,440
Profile.

59
00:04:52,420 --> 00:04:55,900
Scrolling and set this to Infinite.

60
00:04:55,900 --> 00:04:56,830
Scroll back.

61
00:04:57,430 --> 00:05:04,210
So what this means is if this gets filled up with a lot of data, I'll always be able to go back and

62
00:05:04,210 --> 00:05:11,170
read it, even if it's more than 500 lines, because by default it's limited to 500 lines.

63
00:05:11,860 --> 00:05:15,970
So I'm going to run my program now and I'm going to log in.

64
00:05:18,100 --> 00:05:19,720
And I'm going to do control.

65
00:05:19,720 --> 00:05:20,770
See here.

66
00:05:22,210 --> 00:05:28,240
Now, as you can see, similar to what we got before, when we use the show function, you can see that

67
00:05:28,240 --> 00:05:33,110
for each packet it's actually showing us the layers inside this packet.

68
00:05:33,130 --> 00:05:38,590
So you can see, for example, for this packet right here we have the Ethernet layer and this is the

69
00:05:38,590 --> 00:05:40,210
information stored in it.

70
00:05:40,210 --> 00:05:48,670
We have the IP layer with the information in it and we have the TCP layer and the HTTP layer.

71
00:05:49,180 --> 00:05:51,790
You can also see for each layer we have the fields.

72
00:05:51,790 --> 00:05:59,080
So before we used to actually play with the DST, the destination and the source fields, you can see

73
00:05:59,080 --> 00:06:03,370
for HTTP layer, we actually have more fields in there.

74
00:06:03,370 --> 00:06:04,510
Kind of interesting.

75
00:06:04,510 --> 00:06:07,270
You can see we have the HTTP method.

76
00:06:07,810 --> 00:06:12,820
So for example, this packet is using HTTP get and it's request.

77
00:06:14,210 --> 00:06:18,290
And you can also see the headers and your URLs and so on.

78
00:06:19,070 --> 00:06:25,790
Now, because we sent the password using a form in here, these passwords are usually sent using post.

79
00:06:25,790 --> 00:06:29,360
So in the method we need to see something called post.

80
00:06:29,660 --> 00:06:34,670
So this is what I'm going to look for and here to make it easier for me to see the password.

81
00:06:34,670 --> 00:06:39,110
So I'm going to look for a HTTP layer and that has post in here.

82
00:06:40,690 --> 00:06:47,140
So scrolling up, as you can see in here, I have a packet that has a HTTP layer.

83
00:06:47,230 --> 00:06:49,840
The method in this layer is post.

84
00:06:49,840 --> 00:06:53,170
So if I scroll down, I'll probably see my password.

85
00:06:55,140 --> 00:07:04,020
And as you can see in here, we can see that the username is being sent in here as test at gmail.com

86
00:07:04,020 --> 00:07:07,050
and the password is test, test, test, test.

87
00:07:07,680 --> 00:07:15,690
Now the main reason I used packet dot show in my code in here is so that I know which layer contains

88
00:07:15,690 --> 00:07:18,690
the information that I'm looking for.

89
00:07:18,990 --> 00:07:26,070
So if I go down here, you can see that the layer that contains the useful information is called raw.

90
00:07:26,760 --> 00:07:30,060
So I can add another filter in here.

91
00:07:30,510 --> 00:07:33,900
So we're checking if the packet has a HTTP layer.

92
00:07:34,020 --> 00:07:40,590
And then I can use another check and check if the packet has a layer.

93
00:07:40,590 --> 00:07:45,720
And this time instead of chicken for HTTP layer, we're going to check for raw.

94
00:07:45,960 --> 00:07:49,200
And to do that, we're going to say KP, dot raw.

95
00:07:49,920 --> 00:07:53,220
If you find that, I want you to print the packet.

96
00:07:54,200 --> 00:08:01,910
So you can see from these two examples and here you can check if a packet contains any layer using the

97
00:08:01,920 --> 00:08:03,140
hash layer method.

98
00:08:03,170 --> 00:08:08,780
So all you have to do is just say packet dot has layer and put the layer that you're looking for.

99
00:08:08,780 --> 00:08:13,190
And here in the above example, we're looking for HTTP layer.

100
00:08:13,190 --> 00:08:16,400
So we use http, http request.

101
00:08:16,580 --> 00:08:23,360
And in this example we're using Scapegoat Raw because we're looking for the raw layer now for all the

102
00:08:23,360 --> 00:08:30,290
other layers such as either net r.p and so on, you'd always use KP dot layer name.

103
00:08:30,860 --> 00:08:40,070
The only reason why we used http http request here because KP doesn't have a HTTP filter by default.

104
00:08:40,070 --> 00:08:47,360
And as you remember, we had to install the HTTP to be able to filter based on the HTTP layer.

105
00:08:47,900 --> 00:08:53,690
So what I'm trying to say is, in the view this is just an example and in the future, if you are looking

106
00:08:53,690 --> 00:09:00,530
for data inside a different layer, then all you have to do is just type packet, use the hash layer

107
00:09:00,530 --> 00:09:03,800
method and then put the layer name that you're looking for.

108
00:09:04,610 --> 00:09:09,920
Now we're checking if a packet has a HTTP layer, then we're going to check if the packet has a role

109
00:09:09,920 --> 00:09:10,550
layer.

110
00:09:10,790 --> 00:09:15,170
Then instead of printing the show, we don't really need to use the show anymore.

111
00:09:15,200 --> 00:09:19,610
We were using it just to see all the fields inside our packets.

112
00:09:19,670 --> 00:09:25,370
So now instead of printing the whole packet, which will again give us the result that we were seeing

113
00:09:25,370 --> 00:09:30,800
before, so it will contain the password plus other information that's not useful for us.

114
00:09:30,800 --> 00:09:35,450
We only want to print the raw layer of the packet.

115
00:09:35,600 --> 00:09:42,140
So what we're going to say is square brackets and put the name of the layer that we're interested in

116
00:09:42,140 --> 00:09:45,440
and in our case is shaped to throw.

117
00:09:48,410 --> 00:09:52,040
So what will this do to omit all the other layers?

118
00:09:52,040 --> 00:09:54,620
The one print, either net one print IP.

119
00:09:54,620 --> 00:09:56,960
It will only print the raw layer.

120
00:09:56,960 --> 00:09:57,710
And here.

121
00:09:58,990 --> 00:10:03,670
Now inside this layer, you can see we have a field called load.

122
00:10:04,120 --> 00:10:11,080
So this is just the field name similar to the fields in here, the DST and the Z that we used before.

123
00:10:11,800 --> 00:10:14,890
So we actually only want this field.

124
00:10:14,890 --> 00:10:22,390
So in order to only print this field, we're just going to do that load and that's it.

125
00:10:22,810 --> 00:10:26,680
And again, like I said before, this is just an example.

126
00:10:26,680 --> 00:10:29,230
If you want to print a different layer.

127
00:10:29,260 --> 00:10:35,080
All you have to do is put the packet name and then the layer that you're interested in between the square

128
00:10:35,080 --> 00:10:35,890
brackets.

129
00:10:36,310 --> 00:10:42,670
And if you want to access any field within that layer, you can just do dot field name.

130
00:10:43,330 --> 00:10:49,510
So if you want to check if a packet has a specific layer, you do packet, dot has layer and put the

131
00:10:49,510 --> 00:10:50,470
layer name.

132
00:10:50,500 --> 00:10:56,010
If you want to print that layer, you're going to do pocket square brackets, layer name.

133
00:10:56,020 --> 00:11:01,240
And then if you're looking for a specific field, then you do dot the name of the field.

134
00:11:01,300 --> 00:11:06,880
So this applies for all the packets that you're going to capture, not only for HTTP packets.

135
00:11:07,240 --> 00:11:13,750
So if you understand this, you'll be able to adopt this to any situation that you want or to any goal

136
00:11:13,750 --> 00:11:15,220
that you want to achieve.

137
00:11:15,940 --> 00:11:18,580
So let's save this and test it.

138
00:11:19,530 --> 00:11:21,480
So I'm going to clear this.

139
00:11:21,630 --> 00:11:24,720
Run my code and let's log in.

140
00:11:27,150 --> 00:11:33,300
And as you can see now, my program is perfect because it doesn't show any other useless information,

141
00:11:33,450 --> 00:11:40,470
even in the useful pocket we seen before that there is a lot of useless information inside the same

142
00:11:40,470 --> 00:11:41,480
useless packet.

143
00:11:41,490 --> 00:11:48,090
But right now we're only printing the useful information, so we're only printing the raw, the load

144
00:11:48,090 --> 00:11:49,100
inside the raw.

145
00:11:49,110 --> 00:11:53,850
And as you can see, we can see the username and we can see the password.

146
00:11:54,690 --> 00:11:59,070
Now I know I kind of covered a lot of things in this lecture, but they're all related.

147
00:11:59,070 --> 00:12:01,410
That's why I wanted to put them in the same lecture.

148
00:12:01,860 --> 00:12:05,640
And even if it looks a little bit difficult, don't worry about that.

149
00:12:05,640 --> 00:12:09,300
We're going to be using this more in the next few lectures.

150
00:12:09,300 --> 00:12:12,390
So we're actually going to be accessing more fields and more layers.

151
00:12:12,390 --> 00:12:16,770
And like I said, the way to access these fields and these layers is the same.

152
00:12:16,770 --> 00:12:21,150
So you'll be able to understand it more as you go through the course.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,980 --> 00:00:03,460
Now our program is pretty good.

2
00:00:03,470 --> 00:00:11,060
It's able to capture all the packets that are flowing through our interface, and we coded it in a way

3
00:00:11,060 --> 00:00:16,340
so that it only shows the interesting part, which is usernames and passwords.

4
00:00:16,340 --> 00:00:21,170
So we can see here, it's only showing me the username here and the password.

5
00:00:21,950 --> 00:00:30,110
And the way we achieve this is by only printing a specific layer and a specific field of all the data

6
00:00:30,110 --> 00:00:31,250
that we're sniffing.

7
00:00:31,310 --> 00:00:38,030
And the specific layer that we're printing is the zero layer, and we're only printing the load field

8
00:00:38,030 --> 00:00:39,230
of that layer.

9
00:00:40,220 --> 00:00:44,990
Now, when we're looking at this, we can see that it's only showing the interesting stuff.

10
00:00:44,990 --> 00:00:51,500
But the problem is some websites still send data in the load field.

11
00:00:51,500 --> 00:00:53,960
That is not usernames and passwords.

12
00:00:54,380 --> 00:00:55,880
So let me show you an example.

13
00:00:55,880 --> 00:00:57,440
Let's just go on Bing.

14
00:00:57,620 --> 00:00:59,990
And we're already searching for something.

15
00:00:59,990 --> 00:01:02,630
So let's just go to a different page, for example.

16
00:01:03,800 --> 00:01:08,540
And as you can see, again, we're still getting a lot of useless data.

17
00:01:08,960 --> 00:01:10,980
Now, this is still much better than before.

18
00:01:11,000 --> 00:01:12,830
You can see it's not filling my screen.

19
00:01:12,830 --> 00:01:18,380
I still can see my username and password here, but it's still not perfect.

20
00:01:18,530 --> 00:01:21,630
This doesn't mean that's what we did so far is bad.

21
00:01:21,650 --> 00:01:23,170
It's actually really good.

22
00:01:23,180 --> 00:01:28,220
It's only showing the part that contains the usernames and passwords.

23
00:01:28,220 --> 00:01:36,080
But the problem is this exact same part is used by some other websites to send other stuff that is not

24
00:01:36,080 --> 00:01:37,250
useful to us.

25
00:01:37,580 --> 00:01:45,230
So we just need to add a slight modification to our code to make it only list usernames and passwords.

26
00:01:46,010 --> 00:01:50,780
And the way we're going to do that is let me just go up to where my username and password is.

27
00:01:51,650 --> 00:01:57,980
So the way we're going to do this is we're going to check if the string that we're going to print contains

28
00:01:58,010 --> 00:02:00,320
a keyword, if it contains a username.

29
00:02:00,770 --> 00:02:07,220
Now, this is a great opportunity for me to show you a way to check if a substring is contained within

30
00:02:07,220 --> 00:02:08,210
a big string.

31
00:02:08,420 --> 00:02:13,150
And if you learn this right now, you'll be able to use it in a large number of programs.

32
00:02:13,160 --> 00:02:15,500
It doesn't even have to be a sniffer.

33
00:02:16,160 --> 00:02:18,140
So let's see how we can do that.

34
00:02:18,800 --> 00:02:24,020
Now, the variable that we're printing on screen that contains the useful stuff is this one.

35
00:02:24,020 --> 00:02:27,050
So it's the load field of the packet.

36
00:02:27,680 --> 00:02:32,720
So I'm going to cut this, I'm going to remove the print statement and I'm just going to store that

37
00:02:32,720 --> 00:02:36,470
in a variable and let's call this variable load.

38
00:02:38,480 --> 00:02:39,090
Okay.

39
00:02:39,110 --> 00:02:46,580
Now, what we need to do is check if this variable that's called load contains the word username.

40
00:02:47,000 --> 00:02:51,470
Because as we can see here, the load variable contains all of this.

41
00:02:51,770 --> 00:02:55,190
So we're going to say if it contains a username, print it for me.

42
00:02:55,220 --> 00:02:56,630
Otherwise don't.

43
00:02:56,750 --> 00:02:58,850
This should result into the program.

44
00:02:58,850 --> 00:03:02,450
Print in this line, for example, and it won't print this line.

45
00:03:02,900 --> 00:03:04,340
So let's go up.

46
00:03:05,180 --> 00:03:07,940
And all we have to do is use an if statement.

47
00:03:08,300 --> 00:03:14,060
And what we're going to say, we're going to say if username is in load.

48
00:03:14,990 --> 00:03:18,590
If it is, then I want you to print load for me.

49
00:03:19,410 --> 00:03:20,840
So very, very simple.

50
00:03:20,850 --> 00:03:22,680
It doesn't get any simpler than that.

51
00:03:22,830 --> 00:03:29,370
The substring that we're looking for is username, and all we're saying is if load, if this bigger

52
00:03:29,370 --> 00:03:34,680
string, if this bigger variable contains this string, this smaller string.

53
00:03:35,340 --> 00:03:37,860
So if we go down and run our code.

54
00:03:39,700 --> 00:03:45,490
Now you'll see here if we literally just go and browse, you'll see nothing coming up on screen.

55
00:03:45,730 --> 00:03:48,640
But if we go and log in in here.

56
00:03:49,700 --> 00:03:50,390
You'll see.

57
00:03:50,390 --> 00:03:52,550
It'll show me the username and password.

58
00:03:53,360 --> 00:04:00,140
So now, no matter what we do on the Internet, no matter what websites we go for, even if these websites

59
00:04:00,140 --> 00:04:07,550
use the load field to send useless information, this information will not be printed to us unless it

60
00:04:07,550 --> 00:04:09,470
contains the word username.

61
00:04:09,920 --> 00:04:13,040
Now we have one problem with the code that we wrote so far.

62
00:04:13,340 --> 00:04:20,570
The reason why you see username in here is because the person that programmed this page decided to send

63
00:04:20,570 --> 00:04:23,210
the username in the field called username.

64
00:04:23,900 --> 00:04:30,380
So you might have another website and the programmer, for example, decides to call this field login.

65
00:04:30,860 --> 00:04:39,980
Or maybe they decided to call it email so it would be useful instead of only checking for one name in

66
00:04:39,980 --> 00:04:44,680
here to check for a list of names or a number of names.

67
00:04:44,690 --> 00:04:51,560
If any of these names occurs in the bigger string in here, in the load, then we're going to print

68
00:04:51,560 --> 00:04:51,920
it.

69
00:04:51,950 --> 00:04:53,600
Otherwise we won't.

70
00:04:54,170 --> 00:05:00,770
Again, this is a very good opportunity for me to show you how to check if an element in a list is in

71
00:05:00,770 --> 00:05:01,720
a bigger string.

72
00:05:01,730 --> 00:05:06,410
So what I'm going to show you right now, you can use it in many, many scenarios.

73
00:05:07,010 --> 00:05:12,980
So instead of just checking for one word in here, what I'm going to do is I'm going to create a list

74
00:05:13,310 --> 00:05:15,920
and this list, I'm going to call it keywords.

75
00:05:17,390 --> 00:05:23,860
And you're going to add the names that you think programmers might use as interesting fields.

76
00:05:23,870 --> 00:05:30,260
So for example, the interesting fields in here are username and password, but we're going to add anything

77
00:05:30,260 --> 00:05:34,430
that we think a programmer might use instead of username and password.

78
00:05:34,430 --> 00:05:38,450
So let's first of all add username as an element.

79
00:05:39,050 --> 00:05:41,180
We'll also add user.

80
00:05:42,480 --> 00:05:44,520
Let's also add log in.

81
00:05:46,020 --> 00:05:48,750
Password and pass.

82
00:05:48,930 --> 00:05:50,660
Now, this is just a normal list.

83
00:05:50,670 --> 00:05:55,590
We spoke about lists initializing them and using them before, so I'm not going to talk about that.

84
00:05:55,710 --> 00:06:02,250
And basically all I'm doing is I'm creating a list of keywords that I'm going to use to trigger my print

85
00:06:02,250 --> 00:06:03,090
statement.

86
00:06:03,690 --> 00:06:09,870
Now, in order to do that, first of all, I'm going to use a for loop to iterate over each element

87
00:06:09,870 --> 00:06:10,920
in this list.

88
00:06:11,220 --> 00:06:14,820
Now again, we covered that before, so I'm going to do it a little bit quickly.

89
00:06:14,820 --> 00:06:17,340
So we're going to say for each keyword.

90
00:06:18,080 --> 00:06:21,560
In my keywords, so in my keywords list.

91
00:06:22,730 --> 00:06:24,530
I want to run a check.

92
00:06:25,010 --> 00:06:30,710
The check is going to say if the keyword is in my load.

93
00:06:31,310 --> 00:06:33,380
Then I want to print the load.

94
00:06:35,020 --> 00:06:35,680
And that's it.

95
00:06:35,710 --> 00:06:37,540
Now, we don't need this code anymore.

96
00:06:38,810 --> 00:06:43,700
Now, if you look at this code, it's actually very nice and it's easy to understand because you can

97
00:06:43,700 --> 00:06:46,790
pretty much read it just like you're reading normal English.

98
00:06:47,210 --> 00:06:54,500
So we know the load contains the interesting stuff for us, but sometimes some websites might put stuff

99
00:06:54,500 --> 00:06:56,300
that's not interesting in it.

100
00:06:56,450 --> 00:07:02,780
So what we're doing is we're creating a list of keywords that would refer to the interesting stuff that

101
00:07:02,780 --> 00:07:03,800
we're looking for.

102
00:07:04,280 --> 00:07:10,630
Then we're saying for each word in these keywords I want to check, is that keyword in my load?

103
00:07:10,640 --> 00:07:12,290
Is it in that variable?

104
00:07:12,980 --> 00:07:14,920
If it is, then print it.

105
00:07:14,930 --> 00:07:16,760
Otherwise don't do anything.

106
00:07:17,600 --> 00:07:25,130
Now, the result of this will only print data that is sent in the row layer in the load field and that

107
00:07:25,130 --> 00:07:28,130
contain one or more of these words.

108
00:07:28,580 --> 00:07:30,440
So let's go and run our code.

109
00:07:32,130 --> 00:07:37,920
Now this will obviously work against this example because it already has username, but the whole idea

110
00:07:37,920 --> 00:07:42,480
is it should work against other websites that might use different field names.

111
00:07:42,960 --> 00:07:48,120
So if we log in again, you can see the data is being printed in here.

112
00:07:49,080 --> 00:07:54,810
Now you can see that the same line is being printed one, two, three, four, five times.

113
00:07:55,170 --> 00:08:04,080
Now, the reason for this is if you look at my loop and here I'm looping over each element in this list.

114
00:08:04,350 --> 00:08:08,190
So first of all, we're checking if username is in load.

115
00:08:08,190 --> 00:08:09,810
Yes, it is printing it.

116
00:08:09,810 --> 00:08:13,980
Then we're checking if user, we're checking if login and so on.

117
00:08:14,610 --> 00:08:22,290
Now if you look at my load in here in this particular example, you'll see that the output in here contains

118
00:08:22,290 --> 00:08:28,980
all the keywords, so it contains username, it contains user login, password and pass.

119
00:08:29,400 --> 00:08:33,720
That's why when the loop is getting executed, it's checking.

120
00:08:33,720 --> 00:08:37,490
If username is in it, it is its print in it, then it's chicken.

121
00:08:37,500 --> 00:08:40,890
If user is in it, it is, it's printing it and so on.

122
00:08:40,890 --> 00:08:48,090
So the same code is being repeated five times now for me, for this example, if I print the packet,

123
00:08:48,090 --> 00:08:48,810
that's it.

124
00:08:48,810 --> 00:08:49,410
It's enough.

125
00:08:49,410 --> 00:08:52,140
I need to quit and just go to the next packet.

126
00:08:52,140 --> 00:08:56,970
There is no point of checking the next four because I've already printed it.

127
00:08:57,540 --> 00:09:01,500
So we need a way to exit out of this loop.

128
00:09:01,680 --> 00:09:04,080
If we execute this line in here.

129
00:09:05,080 --> 00:09:09,940
So basically we want this line to be executed once at most.

130
00:09:10,510 --> 00:09:15,760
Therefore, all we're going to do is we're going to add we're going to say break after this.

131
00:09:16,270 --> 00:09:23,380
So what this will do is if a keyword is in the load, it will print the load and it will break out of

132
00:09:23,380 --> 00:09:24,130
the loop.

133
00:09:24,490 --> 00:09:31,240
So the break keyword can be used to break out of the current loop without checking for condition, without

134
00:09:31,240 --> 00:09:32,600
checking for anything.

135
00:09:32,620 --> 00:09:39,850
If you use it anywhere within a loop, it will exit you out of the loop and continue the program execution

136
00:09:39,850 --> 00:09:41,740
straight after the loop.

137
00:09:41,830 --> 00:09:44,350
Now, in this example, we have nothing after the loop.

138
00:09:44,350 --> 00:09:50,080
So it literally exit this function and go back to the sniff, which will continue sniffing.

139
00:09:50,860 --> 00:10:01,030
So now if we go down and clear this, run the code again and log in, you'll see that we'll only print

140
00:10:01,030 --> 00:10:09,360
the line once because we use the break in here, which it will check if a username is in the load.

141
00:10:09,370 --> 00:10:15,390
It's going to print it and break and exit out of the loop without it like we seen before.

142
00:10:15,400 --> 00:10:19,090
We continue checking all the other elements in the list.

143
00:10:20,560 --> 00:10:20,890
Now.

144
00:10:20,890 --> 00:10:22,360
That's it for this lecture.

145
00:10:22,390 --> 00:10:24,680
Our program now runs perfectly.

146
00:10:24,700 --> 00:10:31,180
It literally only shows usernames and passwords entered on websites through HTTP.

147
00:10:31,570 --> 00:10:35,620
And we manage to do this by adding a little bit more filters.

148
00:10:35,920 --> 00:10:40,270
As we did this, we learned more about loops and how to break out of loops.

149
00:10:40,270 --> 00:10:46,360
We learned more about lists, and we learned more about conditional statements and how to check for

150
00:10:46,360 --> 00:10:48,160
some strings and strings.

151
00:10:48,640 --> 00:10:54,340
And again, like I said before, everything that I show when we're programming is generic.

152
00:10:54,340 --> 00:11:00,220
So the methods that I showed here for exiting out of a loop or for checking for a substring within a

153
00:11:00,220 --> 00:11:04,810
string can be used in any other Python program that you're writing.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:01,410
Okay.

2
00:00:01,440 --> 00:00:09,960
So our program now can be used to sniff data and only display usernames and passwords entered on a web

3
00:00:09,960 --> 00:00:11,700
page through HTTP.

4
00:00:12,120 --> 00:00:18,720
Now, another useful thing that we might want our program to display is the URLs that the target is

5
00:00:18,720 --> 00:00:19,530
accessing.

6
00:00:20,040 --> 00:00:25,680
So we're actually going to be using stuff that we learned before, but this will actually give you a

7
00:00:25,680 --> 00:00:32,970
better understanding on how to dissect and analyze packets and display the useful stuff on screen.

8
00:00:33,540 --> 00:00:40,710
So first of all, like we did before, after I check that the packet has a HTTP layer, I'm going to

9
00:00:40,710 --> 00:00:49,920
print the packet dot show and I'm going to do that to see which part of the packet contains the interesting

10
00:00:49,920 --> 00:00:50,560
stuff.

11
00:00:50,580 --> 00:00:55,320
And in this example is the URLs that the target person is visiting.

12
00:00:55,770 --> 00:00:58,830
So I'm going to go down, run my program.

13
00:01:00,150 --> 00:01:01,860
And then let's just search for something.

14
00:01:01,860 --> 00:01:03,510
Let's just search for test.

15
00:01:05,370 --> 00:01:10,710
And I'm actually just going to go on images because it's easier to see their URLs.

16
00:01:12,910 --> 00:01:15,370
I'm just going to click on any image in here.

17
00:01:17,220 --> 00:01:24,360
And let's click on View Image and I'm actually going to press refresh to make sure that the last thing

18
00:01:24,360 --> 00:01:27,660
I capture is the packet that's loading this image.

19
00:01:28,110 --> 00:01:33,750
Now, the reason why I'm doing this is because it's easy to see the URL of the image.

20
00:01:33,750 --> 00:01:38,070
So the URL in this example is this URL right here.

21
00:01:38,520 --> 00:01:44,250
And if we look in here, you can see that we have the URL in here.

22
00:01:44,940 --> 00:01:47,160
Now this is to the computer.

23
00:01:47,160 --> 00:01:48,630
This is just a URL.

24
00:01:48,630 --> 00:01:55,800
So it doesn't matter if it's an image or if it's a HTML or PHP and MP three, it doesn't really matter

25
00:01:55,800 --> 00:01:57,720
as long as it's a URL.

26
00:01:57,720 --> 00:02:01,560
And here it's going to be sent as a packet.

27
00:02:02,220 --> 00:02:10,860
And as you can see, it's being sent in the HTTP layer and the field that contains this is called path.

28
00:02:11,900 --> 00:02:19,910
Now notice the path field only contains the path after the domain name, so it only contains this path

29
00:02:19,910 --> 00:02:20,780
right here.

30
00:02:21,230 --> 00:02:29,150
If you look closely in here, you'll also see we have a field called Host and this field contains the

31
00:02:29,150 --> 00:02:31,970
domain name, so it contains the data in here.

32
00:02:32,510 --> 00:02:40,310
So if we wanted to print the full URL, we'll need to first of all access the HTTP layer and then in

33
00:02:40,310 --> 00:02:45,080
that layer, print the host and print the path fields.

34
00:02:46,510 --> 00:02:54,490
Now, based on what we learned before, we know to access a certain layer, all we have to do is do

35
00:02:54,500 --> 00:02:59,620
packet and put the layer name between square brackets.

36
00:02:59,620 --> 00:03:06,370
So the layer name, http dot, http request.

37
00:03:07,300 --> 00:03:11,380
And the fields that I'm looking for are the path and the host.

38
00:03:11,560 --> 00:03:18,670
So to access the host field, we will do the host and to access the path field, I'll have to do the

39
00:03:18,670 --> 00:03:20,140
exact same thing.

40
00:03:21,750 --> 00:03:24,330
But put path instead of host.

41
00:03:25,950 --> 00:03:29,690
Now, this should actually be a capital H, not a small hedge.

42
00:03:30,410 --> 00:03:37,310
Now, these are two strings, and I want to append them because again, as we see here, the host is

43
00:03:37,310 --> 00:03:41,860
the first part of the URL and the path is the rest of the URL.

44
00:03:41,870 --> 00:03:49,190
So I literally want to append these two strings and we know from before, from previous lectures to

45
00:03:49,190 --> 00:03:54,650
append to strings, all we have to do is just put a plus between them and I'm going to store the value

46
00:03:54,650 --> 00:03:58,010
of all of this in a variable called URL.

47
00:03:58,850 --> 00:04:02,480
So we're saying the URL is equal to all of this.

48
00:04:03,140 --> 00:04:09,860
Finally, I'm going to print my URL in here, so I'm just going to do print your URL.

49
00:04:11,050 --> 00:04:11,860
And that's it.

50
00:04:12,430 --> 00:04:19,030
Now, I'm also going to remove my print here that was showing me the show of the packet because I was

51
00:04:19,030 --> 00:04:22,240
only using that to see all the fields in the packets.

52
00:04:22,930 --> 00:04:29,170
So all we did now is we first of all checked which layer contains the useful information.

53
00:04:29,170 --> 00:04:36,700
And using the show we were able to see that the HTTP layer is contains useful information, which is

54
00:04:36,700 --> 00:04:38,230
the URL in our case.

55
00:04:38,530 --> 00:04:46,480
We also see that in the path we see the rest of the URL at the start of the URL is included in the host

56
00:04:46,480 --> 00:04:47,110
field.

57
00:04:47,980 --> 00:04:53,920
Now we see in previous lecture we can access the layer by putting the layer between square brackets

58
00:04:53,920 --> 00:04:57,520
and putting the field after the dot.

59
00:04:58,060 --> 00:05:01,210
So this is similar to when we were accessing the load.

60
00:05:01,210 --> 00:05:03,520
We used to say the packet.

61
00:05:03,520 --> 00:05:06,910
We get the raw layer and we get the load field.

62
00:05:07,840 --> 00:05:14,110
And this is what I meant when I said the method that I showed you can be used to filter any data from

63
00:05:14,110 --> 00:05:16,420
any layer and from any field.

64
00:05:17,200 --> 00:05:21,370
So if we save this and go down and run my program.

65
00:05:23,210 --> 00:05:24,410
Go back to being.

66
00:05:28,340 --> 00:05:32,810
You can see that it's giving me an error because I misspelled HTTP request.

67

It should be http capital and the request is small.

Like so.

69

So I'm just going to copy that pasted here and here and run the code again.

87
00:06:50,390 --> 00:06:55,370
Now again, if I was the man in the middle and a remote computer was doing this, will still be able

And if we go back and just log in, our program will still filter the log in.

00:07:04,400 --> 00:07:10,790

91
00:07:10,790 --> 00:07:12,610
So it's not filled by useless stuff.

92
00:07:12,620 --> 00:07:15,020
It's still being filled with useful stuff.

93
00:07:15,350 --> 00:07:18,440
And you can see the username and the password in here.

94
00:07:19,680 --> 00:07:20,010
Now.

95
00:07:20,010 --> 00:07:27,870
I just wanted to spend more time to show you how to filter stuff, because I know this is a vital step

96
00:07:27,870 --> 00:07:30,300
when it comes to sniffing or spying.

97
00:07:30,330 --> 00:07:35,990
We did all the hard work with becoming the man in the middle and all that, so it wouldn't hurt to spend

98
00:07:35,990 --> 00:07:43,470
the next lecture or two showing how to properly filter data and access data regardless of what type

99
00:07:43,470 --> 00:07:44,940
of data you're looking for.

100
00:07:45,120 --> 00:07:51,300
So right now, we should be able to adopt what I showed you to capture data from any layers, from any

101
00:07:51,300 --> 00:07:59,790
field, whether it's been sent through ICMP as an email or if it's sent to FTP, HTTP and so on.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:06,930
So far in the previous two sections, I've been bragging about building a tool to become the man in

2
00:00:06,930 --> 00:00:09,030
the middle using ARP spoofing.

3
00:00:09,030 --> 00:00:14,820
And I said, that allows me to pretty much see anything that any person does on the same network.

4
00:00:14,910 --> 00:00:23,580
And then I showed you how to create a sniffer to see the URLs and usernames and passwords sent or received

5
00:00:23,580 --> 00:00:25,650
by a specific interface.

6
00:00:26,100 --> 00:00:30,900
I also said if this works on my local machine, it will work if I become the man in the middle.

7
00:00:30,990 --> 00:00:38,340
So all of the testing that we did was against our own local machine or against the computer, but we

8
00:00:38,340 --> 00:00:41,550
didn't really run any real life scenario.

9
00:00:42,240 --> 00:00:49,290
So what I want to do in this lecture is use a combination of the ARP spoofing tool that we created to

10
00:00:49,290 --> 00:00:55,920
redirect the flow of packets so that packets sent or received by this Windows machine will have to flow

11
00:00:55,920 --> 00:00:56,760
through me.

12
00:00:57,060 --> 00:01:04,500
Then I'll also use my sniffer that I created so that I can see the URLs that this person accesses and

13
00:01:04,500 --> 00:01:10,290
the usernames and passwords that they send if they log in to any service online.

14
00:01:11,070 --> 00:01:17,250
Now, before I do any of that, because this is an easy lecture, we're literally just testing our tools

15
00:01:17,250 --> 00:01:20,220
and they should work because they work locally.

16
00:01:20,370 --> 00:01:27,060
I want to spend a minute or two refactoring this code and just making it function and look better.

17
00:01:27,870 --> 00:01:31,870
So the first thing that I want to do is modify my print statements.

18
00:01:31,890 --> 00:01:36,760
So the first print statement is here and this statement prints the URL.

19
00:01:36,780 --> 00:01:46,860
So I just want to say, hey, http request and then give the request and then I put a plus to append

20
00:01:46,860 --> 00:01:47,370
this.

21
00:01:48,090 --> 00:01:54,900
The next thing is I want to modify this print statement, which will print a line that possibly contains

22
00:01:54,900 --> 00:01:56,610
a username and password.

23
00:01:56,880 --> 00:02:00,100
So I want to make this stand out more.

24
00:02:00,120 --> 00:02:06,510
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to, first of all, say possible username, password.

25
00:02:08,150 --> 00:02:12,460
And I'm also going to put two new line characters before it.

26
00:02:12,470 --> 00:02:20,150
So we're doing backward slash and backward slash and and I'm going to put two new line characters after

27
00:02:20,150 --> 00:02:25,280
it as well, so that it will stand out when it's being printed on screen.

28
00:02:25,640 --> 00:02:30,590
Now, we spoke about these characters and we spoke about appending strings before, so I'm not going

29
00:02:30,590 --> 00:02:32,540
to spend too much time on it right now.

30
00:02:33,290 --> 00:02:38,930
The next thing that I want to do is I want to move all of this into a function.

31
00:02:38,930 --> 00:02:45,680
So we know that this literally constructs my URL, but I don't like the way that we have a lot of code

32
00:02:45,680 --> 00:02:46,280
here.

33
00:02:46,280 --> 00:02:56,030
So if I just have a function here and call this function, get your URL and this takes a packet and

34
00:02:56,030 --> 00:03:01,070
this function returns all of this, which is the URL.

35
00:03:01,550 --> 00:03:08,720
So that here I can just say your URL is equal to get your URL for my packet.

36
00:03:09,440 --> 00:03:14,090
So this is much cleaner and then I can close this and forget about how this works.

37
00:03:14,990 --> 00:03:18,680
The next thing that I want to do is copy all of this.

38
00:03:19,900 --> 00:03:21,820
Into a function as well.

39
00:03:22,720 --> 00:03:24,730
So I'm going to create a function.

40
00:03:26,110 --> 00:03:29,380
And we're going to call this function get login.

41
00:03:30,420 --> 00:03:31,320
Info.

42
00:03:31,800 --> 00:03:38,130
This will also take a packet and we'll paste everything in here.

43
00:03:39,310 --> 00:03:44,020
And in here, instead of printing the load, we're going to return.

44
00:03:45,330 --> 00:03:46,170
The load.

45
00:03:47,040 --> 00:03:49,590
Now because we are returning in here.

46
00:03:49,590 --> 00:03:52,590
This has a similar effect of using brake.

47
00:03:52,590 --> 00:03:56,760
So this will actually exit out of the loop and out of the whole function.

48
00:03:56,770 --> 00:04:00,000
So there is no need for using the break here.

49
00:04:00,300 --> 00:04:04,650
So I'm going to cut this line and remove the break.

50
00:04:05,610 --> 00:04:07,170
And this will work fine.

51
00:04:07,170 --> 00:04:09,630
So again, I can forget about how it works.

52
00:04:09,630 --> 00:04:14,730
I can just minimize it and all I have to do is call it in here.

53
00:04:15,120 --> 00:04:23,700
So we're going to say login info is equal to get login info for my packet.

54
00:04:24,890 --> 00:04:31,150
And we're going to do a check because this will not always we will not always have log in info.

55
00:04:31,160 --> 00:04:36,830
As you know, some packets will be just normal HTTP packets that has no login info.

56
00:04:36,980 --> 00:04:45,290
So we're going to say if we have login info, then we want to use our print statement which will print

57
00:04:45,290 --> 00:04:46,040
the load.

58
00:04:46,040 --> 00:04:53,870
It used to print the load, but now we want it to print login info because that is the variable that

59
00:04:53,870 --> 00:04:55,790
has the login info right now.

60
00:04:56,300 --> 00:04:58,280
So now the code is much prettier.

61
00:04:58,280 --> 00:04:59,870
It's already refactored.

62
00:04:59,870 --> 00:05:04,250
Let's save it and test it against the remote windows computer.

63
00:05:05,940 --> 00:05:09,090
So I'm already in the working directory of my sniffer.

64
00:05:09,090 --> 00:05:13,110
But before I start the sniffer, I'm going to split the screen.

65
00:05:13,320 --> 00:05:17,910
I'm going to go back one directory and open my ARP spool for.

66
00:05:19,430 --> 00:05:22,670
And use it to poison my windows machine.

67
00:05:24,110 --> 00:05:27,740
So we're running it exactly the same way that we used to run it before.

68
00:05:27,980 --> 00:05:32,120
And then I'm going to run my sniffer by doing python sniff dot py.

69
00:05:32,870 --> 00:05:40,130
Now the ARP spoof is running an ARP spoofing attack, fooling the router that I am the client and fooling

70
00:05:40,130 --> 00:05:41,840
the client that I am the router.

71
00:05:41,870 --> 00:05:47,780
Therefore placing me in the middle of the connection and therefore anything that the client, the Windows

72
00:05:47,780 --> 00:05:55,880
machine will do will flow through ETA zero and my sniffer will be able to capture that and filter it

73
00:05:55,880 --> 00:06:01,400
exactly the same way that it was doing when we were testing it against our local machine.

74
00:06:02,270 --> 00:06:07,220
So let's go to the Windows Machine and let's just go to Broadcom.

75
00:06:09,540 --> 00:06:10,530
If we go back.

76

You can see we're able to capture the URLs that the user is requesting.

77

You can see the JavaScript files.

78

You can see some images being loaded in here.

79

Now let's go and log in.

80

So we're going to look for dictionary login.

Clicking on the first result.

82

Then we're going to go to log in.

83

Put some fake information.

84
00:06:36,600 --> 00:06:43,110
So we're just going to go test at gmail.com and put the password as one, two, three, four, five,

85

six, seven, eight.

86
00:06:44,490 --> 00:06:45,720
Hit enter.

87
00:06:46,140 --> 00:06:47,280
Go back.

88
00:06:48,630 --> 00:06:50,500
And I'm going to control see this.

89
00:06:50,550 --> 00:06:56,700
And if we go up looking for the filter that we use for password, so we should we should be able to

90

see it because we put two new lines before and two new lines after.

91
00:07:01,470 --> 00:07:04,680
And as you can see, this is very easy to see in here.

92
00:07:04,950 --> 00:07:08,370
And it's telling us that there is a possible username and password.

93
00:07:08,880 --> 00:07:15,480
We can see that the username in here, which is test at gmail.com and the password is one, two, three,

94
00:07:15,480 --> 00:07:17,250
four, five, six, seven, eight.

95
00:07:18,000 --> 00:07:19,530
So this is perfect.

96
00:07:19,680 --> 00:07:26,700
As you can see now, we manually built our own tools to run an ARP spoofing attack and to sniff data

97
00:07:26,700 --> 00:07:29,310
from any computer in our network.

98
00:07:29,610 --> 00:07:34,500
Now, the methods that I showed you when we were sniffing data and filtering them are generic.

99
00:07:34,500 --> 00:07:37,560
And like I said, you can use it to filter any type of data.

100
00:07:37,560 --> 00:07:43,590
So if you're looking for FTP passwords, if you're looking to just get the images, download the images

101

or do whatever you want, really, you can use the same way that I showed you with filtering, with

103

104
