━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,080 --> 00:00:01,580
Okay.

2
00:00:01,590 --> 00:00:08,880
Now that we have a packet sniffer that works perfectly with Python two as usual at the end of the section,

3
00:00:08,880 --> 00:00:13,680
let's go ahead and try to run it with Python three and see if it works.

4
00:00:13,680 --> 00:00:16,710
If it doesn't and if it doesn't, we'll see how we can fix it.

5
00:00:16,950 --> 00:00:23,010
So I'm going to be testing this locally because as we've seen before, if it works locally, it's going

6
00:00:23,010 --> 00:00:25,800
to work remotely once you become the man in the middle.

7
00:00:25,800 --> 00:00:29,370
So there is no point of increasing the complexity of this.

8
00:00:29,370 --> 00:00:35,430
Let's keep it simple so we know what exactly is going wrong and we can fix the things that are going

9
00:00:35,430 --> 00:00:36,000
wrong.

10
00:00:36,270 --> 00:00:41,910
So I'm just going to run the program with Python three instead of Python, and I'm going to type the

11
00:00:41,910 --> 00:00:48,510
program name, which is packet sniffer, dot pie, as usual, hit enter programs running.

12
00:00:48,510 --> 00:00:52,080
And let's go ahead and just browse any website.

13
00:00:52,080 --> 00:00:57,720
And in this lecture I'm going to be using a website that uses simple HTTP.

14
00:00:57,750 --> 00:01:02,940
So it's really good for testing, sniffing and it is one web dot com.

15
00:01:03,780 --> 00:01:10,530
Now as you can see, as soon as we run the website, we got an error here and it's the famous Python

16
00:01:10,530 --> 00:01:18,240
three error that complains about strings and bites because as we said before, one of the major changes

17
00:01:18,240 --> 00:01:23,190
in Python three is the clear separation between strings and bytes.

18
00:01:23,190 --> 00:01:30,060
And as we said, strings are a sequence of characters that are human readable, whereas you can think

19
00:01:30,060 --> 00:01:37,110
of bytes as sequences of characters, but they're machine readable, they're byte objects, they're

20
00:01:37,110 --> 00:01:38,940
sequence of bytes.

21
00:01:39,540 --> 00:01:41,640
So we have the error online.

22
00:01:41,640 --> 00:01:49,200
22 So let's go ahead to line 22 and our code, this line right here and you can see here, the problem

23
00:01:49,200 --> 00:01:56,610
is we have a string which is human readable, followed by a URL variable that contains a byte object,

24
00:01:56,610 --> 00:01:59,160
which is a series of bytes.

25
00:01:59,160 --> 00:02:07,470
So we have to either convert this string to a bite object or convert this byte object to a string.

26
00:02:07,770 --> 00:02:16,140
Now we see before all we have to do to convert this to a string, we just do a str and put two brackets

27
00:02:16,140 --> 00:02:22,110
around the variable that we want to convert to a string and this will work with no issues at all.

28
00:02:22,800 --> 00:02:31,410
Now another way of doing the exact same as this is to use the decode method that will also convert the

29
00:02:31,410 --> 00:02:33,900
byte object variable here to a string.

30
00:02:33,930 --> 00:02:41,010
It's just a different way of doing it, and it's the more common way of doing it across the community.

31
00:02:41,010 --> 00:02:45,390
So you can use the program like this and it will work with no issues at all.

32
00:02:45,390 --> 00:02:51,480
But just to show you another way of doing it, in case you're reading code on a GitHub or written by

33
00:02:51,480 --> 00:02:57,270
someone else, and just for you to have another way of doing this, I'm going to undo this change.

34
00:02:57,360 --> 00:03:02,740
And instead of using the star cast, we're actually going to do that.

35
00:03:02,790 --> 00:03:03,780
Decode.

36
00:03:05,740 --> 00:03:11,140
So this is just another way of converting a byte object to a string.

37
00:03:11,170 --> 00:03:15,610
It's exactly like using the string brackets.

38
00:03:16,420 --> 00:03:18,550
So let's go ahead and test this.

39
00:03:18,550 --> 00:03:24,280
So I'm going to clear the screen, run the program, and in here we're just going to load the website

40
00:03:24,280 --> 00:03:24,940
again.

41
00:03:24,940 --> 00:03:27,940
And as you can see, perfect will get in the URLs here.

42
00:03:27,970 --> 00:03:29,230
No issues at all.

43
00:03:29,230 --> 00:03:36,130
And if we click on one of them again, it'll load and we're getting all the URLs and all the HTTP requests

44
00:03:36,130 --> 00:03:37,800
exactly as we expected.

45
00:03:37,810 --> 00:03:39,160
So that's perfect.

46
00:03:39,250 --> 00:03:44,180
And let's go ahead and log in to see if we can capture the username and the password.

47
00:03:44,200 --> 00:03:50,050
So I'm going to keep the username to admin and let's set the password to one, two, three, four,

48
00:03:50,050 --> 00:03:52,420
five, six, hit enter.

49
00:03:52,570 --> 00:03:57,430
And again, we have another problem, but it's pretty much the same problem.

50
00:03:57,430 --> 00:04:05,860
We have a type error and we're feeding a string and a place where we should be feeding and a byte like

51
00:04:05,860 --> 00:04:06,610
object.

52
00:04:06,850 --> 00:04:08,980
And this is happening online.

53
00:04:08,980 --> 00:04:09,890
16.

54
00:04:09,910 --> 00:04:14,380
So let's go ahead and we have line 16 right here.

55
00:04:14,380 --> 00:04:20,810
And the main problem in here is we're comparing the keyword in here, which is a string.

56
00:04:20,830 --> 00:04:27,340
Each one of these is a keyword and we're comparing it with a bite like object, which is the load.

57
00:04:27,880 --> 00:04:36,070
So the load is initialized in here and all we have to do, again, just convert this variable to a string.

58
00:04:36,370 --> 00:04:38,440
So you can just do it in here.

59
00:04:38,440 --> 00:04:46,600
You can create another variable and convert it to a string, or you can simply wrap this, all of this

60
00:04:46,600 --> 00:04:50,050
with the STR like we've seen before.

61
00:04:50,140 --> 00:04:51,690
So that's what I'm going to do.

62
00:04:51,700 --> 00:04:54,970
I'm going to do a str open my bracket.

63
00:04:55,060 --> 00:04:56,470
Close the bracket.

64
00:04:57,380 --> 00:04:58,900
And what's going to happen now.

65
00:04:58,910 --> 00:05:05,510
Whatever value that is going to be returned in here, that's going to be a bite like object.

66
00:05:05,510 --> 00:05:12,590
But because we're using the STR that will convert it to a string, so the load now will contain a string.

67
00:05:12,590 --> 00:05:17,660
And then anywhere we use the load throughout the code, it's going to be a string and that's going to

68
00:05:17,660 --> 00:05:22,940
work fine because if you think of it in Python two, our load was a string.

69
00:05:22,940 --> 00:05:27,200
So our code already expects the load to be a string.

70
00:05:27,200 --> 00:05:32,900
So once we convert this string in here the first time that we initialize it or the first time that we

71
00:05:32,930 --> 00:05:37,160
capture it, the rest of the code should work with no issues at all.

72
00:05:37,790 --> 00:05:39,590
So let's go ahead and see that.

73
00:05:41,130 --> 00:05:47,550
Run the code again and let's just log out of this because it logged me in as admin again.

74
00:05:47,550 --> 00:05:50,850
As you can see, URLs are loading with no issues at all.

75
00:05:50,850 --> 00:05:53,400
And then let's go ahead and log in.

76
00:05:55,220 --> 00:05:57,590
One, two, three, four, five, six.

77
00:05:57,590 --> 00:06:01,640
Again, click on log in and perfect.

78
00:06:01,640 --> 00:06:08,750
As you can see, it's telling us that it has a possible username and password and the username is admin

79
00:06:08,750 --> 00:06:12,050
and the password is one, two, three, four, five, six.

80
00:06:12,050 --> 00:06:15,200
So we managed to capture the username and the password.

81
00:06:15,200 --> 00:06:22,010
We're also able to capture the URLs and this code now is working perfectly with Python three.

82
00:06:22,370 --> 00:06:25,850
So if you think of it, it was actually very, very simple.

83
00:06:25,880 --> 00:06:31,640
As usual, all we had to do is run the code as is with Python three and trace the errors.

84
00:06:31,640 --> 00:06:38,540
The first thing we had to do is convert this variable and here to a string instead of a byte object,

85
00:06:38,540 --> 00:06:42,500
we can use the STR to brackets like we did here.

86
00:06:42,500 --> 00:06:48,860
But I just use the decode method in here just to show you another way of achieving the same result.

87
00:06:49,710 --> 00:06:51,980
We ran the code, we got another error.

88
00:06:51,990 --> 00:06:54,300
It was caused by the load in here.

89
00:06:54,300 --> 00:06:59,730
And instead of just converting this load to a string and converting this load to a string, again,

90
00:06:59,730 --> 00:07:06,150
we simply decided to convert the load to a string the first time we initialize it, which is at this

91
00:07:06,150 --> 00:07:11,730
line and this way, because our code already expects a string in the load.

92
00:07:11,730 --> 00:07:15,180
We don't have to convert it anymore throughout the rest of the code.

93
00:07:15,180 --> 00:07:18,450
And as you can see, the code is working perfectly.

94
00:07:18,450 --> 00:07:19,230
And here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 7: Writing a DNS Spoofer

1
00:00:00,450 --> 00:00:08,130
Now in this lecture and the next few lectures, I want to focus on intercepting and modifying packets.

2
00:00:08,340 --> 00:00:15,450
So previously we actually wrote a program that can place us in the middle of the connection in any network,

3
00:00:15,450 --> 00:00:22,440
so that any requests and responses sent from a device will be flowing through our computer.

4
00:00:22,830 --> 00:00:28,680
Then we also use KP to write another program, which was a sniffer, and we were able to read these

5
00:00:28,680 --> 00:00:30,500
requests and responses.

6
00:00:30,510 --> 00:00:38,160
So you can think of Skippy as this length right here, allowing us to read the data sent and received,

7
00:00:38,160 --> 00:00:42,240
and we seen how we can read the usernames, passwords and URLs.

8
00:00:42,780 --> 00:00:47,280
Now we can actually use KP to also modify this data.

9
00:00:47,700 --> 00:00:54,510
So, for example, let's say we want to redirect any request made by our target to some other place,

10
00:00:54,510 --> 00:01:00,120
to a fake website or to a website that would ask them to download a backdoor and so on.

11
00:01:00,660 --> 00:01:02,550
So we can do that using Skype.

12
00:01:02,580 --> 00:01:10,440
Once we get the packet in our computer, we can create another packet which will be the modified packet

13
00:01:10,560 --> 00:01:13,830
and then send that to its destination.

14
00:01:14,430 --> 00:01:21,420
The problem with this implementation is KP cannot be used to intercept or drop packets.

15
00:01:21,540 --> 00:01:24,500
So what we're doing here is we're receiving the request.

16
00:01:24,510 --> 00:01:31,790
We're creating a copy of that request, a modified request, and then both will be sent to the target.

17
00:01:31,800 --> 00:01:37,260
So we can't actually stop the original request from being sent to the target.

18
00:01:37,860 --> 00:01:45,360
So the result is the target is going to receive two requests, the original one and the modified one,

19
00:01:45,690 --> 00:01:49,230
and then it will decide which one it will execute.

20
00:01:49,680 --> 00:01:56,280
Now, it usually executes the one that it receives first, and since modifying a request might take

21
00:01:56,280 --> 00:02:04,290
some time or split seconds, then chances are the target will never execute our modified request because

22
00:02:04,290 --> 00:02:07,470
it will always receive the original request first.

23
00:02:08,310 --> 00:02:16,740
Now, a better implementation is to create a queue in our hacker machine and trap packets inside that

24
00:02:16,740 --> 00:02:17,390
queue.

25
00:02:17,400 --> 00:02:23,460
So whenever we get a request, we'll put it in that queue and never send it to its target.

26
00:02:24,180 --> 00:02:31,080
Then we'll access this queue from our Python program and modify the packets as we want.

27
00:02:31,170 --> 00:02:35,580
And after we finish modifying the packet, we will send the request.

28
00:02:36,120 --> 00:02:42,930
So the result is the target will only receive one packet, which will be the modified packet or the

29
00:02:42,930 --> 00:02:44,280
modified request.

30
00:02:44,970 --> 00:02:51,090
Now we can use the same way to modify responses, so whenever we get a response we'll trap it in a queue.

31
00:02:51,120 --> 00:02:58,330
We access the queue from a Python program and only then, only after modifying the packet will actually

32
00:02:58,330 --> 00:03:00,840
be forwarded to its destination.

33
00:03:01,470 --> 00:03:04,890
Now, as I said, Skippy does not allow us to do this.

34
00:03:04,890 --> 00:03:10,770
So in this lecture, I want to show you a very basic setup of setting up the queue and a very simple

35
00:03:10,770 --> 00:03:13,980
Python program to access the queue and forward packets.

36
00:03:14,130 --> 00:03:19,080
And then we'll work on that as usual and build more complex programs.

37
00:03:19,920 --> 00:03:22,620
So first, let's go to the machine.

38
00:03:23,190 --> 00:03:29,760
And the first thing that I want to do is redirect any packets that we receive on this computer to this

39
00:03:29,760 --> 00:03:34,440
queue, to the queue that we said will trap all the requests, all the responses.

40
00:03:34,440 --> 00:03:39,320
And to do that, we're going to use a program called IP Tables.

41
00:03:39,330 --> 00:03:46,380
This is a program that is installed on UNIX computers that allow us to modify routes on the computer.

42
00:03:46,890 --> 00:03:53,550
Now, IP tables can be used to do so many things and one of them is modifying routing rules.

43
00:03:53,970 --> 00:04:02,220
So we're going to type in IP tables, we're going to use the capital I argument to specify a chain that

44
00:04:02,220 --> 00:04:03,570
we want to modify.

45
00:04:03,660 --> 00:04:11,100
So the change that we want to modify is the forward chain, because this is the chain or the place where

46
00:04:11,100 --> 00:04:15,840
packets that come to our computer are placed in by default.

47
00:04:16,320 --> 00:04:22,950
So you can think of this as the place where these packets are kept by default.

48
00:04:24,790 --> 00:04:31,120
We're going to say that I want to put this in an NEF queue, which is a short for net filter.

49
00:04:31,120 --> 00:04:34,810
Q And we're going to specify the Q number.

50
00:04:37,140 --> 00:04:39,370
And you can use any number you want.

51
00:04:39,390 --> 00:04:45,570
So I'm just going to use number zero to identify my queue, which is the queue that I'm going to trap

52
00:04:45,570 --> 00:04:47,100
all the packets in.

53
00:04:47,610 --> 00:04:49,500
So it's a very simple command.

54
00:04:49,500 --> 00:04:56,250
We're using IP tables, which is the program that's going to allow us to trap all the packets in a queue.

55
00:04:56,700 --> 00:05:04,290
We're saying that I want to trap all the packets that usually go to the forward chain and an NF queue

56
00:05:04,290 --> 00:05:05,820
or a net filter queue.

57
00:05:05,820 --> 00:05:09,810
And then I'm giving the queue number, which is zero.

58
00:05:09,840 --> 00:05:12,280
Now again, you can use any queue number you want.

59
00:05:12,300 --> 00:05:14,550
I'm just using zero in this example.

60
00:05:14,940 --> 00:05:18,730
Now I'm going to hit enter and I misspelled queue.

61
00:05:18,750 --> 00:05:20,700
It's a queue, not a G in here.

62
00:05:21,670 --> 00:05:26,980
And as you can see now, this is running with no errors, which means that the command got executed

63
00:05:26,980 --> 00:05:27,790
properly.

64
00:05:28,270 --> 00:05:34,000
Now, keep in mind, this is a simple Linux command, so you can actually use the sub process module

65
00:05:34,000 --> 00:05:36,670
in our program to execute this command.

66
00:05:36,880 --> 00:05:41,130
But I'm just doing it in here to show you that this is nothing to do with Python.

67
00:05:41,140 --> 00:05:43,960
This is something to do with the system itself.

68
00:05:44,350 --> 00:05:50,410
So right now what we did is we created the queue and you can think of what's going to happen now if

69
00:05:50,410 --> 00:05:56,050
we are the man in the middle and your requests that we're going to get or responses will be trapped

70
00:05:56,050 --> 00:05:57,820
in a queue like this one.

71
00:05:58,450 --> 00:06:03,760
The next thing that we need to do is access this queue from our Python program.

72
00:06:04,570 --> 00:06:09,880
To do this, we're going to need to, first of all, install a module called net filter queue.

73
00:06:10,210 --> 00:06:12,550
So we're going to use PIP as usual.

74
00:06:12,820 --> 00:06:17,440
So we're going to do PIP install net filter queue.

75
00:06:21,390 --> 00:06:23,160
Now I've already installed this.

76
00:06:23,160 --> 00:06:26,700
So as you can see, it's still in me that it's already installed.

77
00:06:26,970 --> 00:06:29,730
Next, we're going to import this in our program.

78
00:06:29,730 --> 00:06:32,850
So we're just going to do import net filter queue.

79
00:06:33,910 --> 00:06:40,480
Now I've already created a project and I've named it net cut because by the end of this lecture you'll

80
00:06:40,480 --> 00:06:46,690
automatically learn how to create a program that can be used to cut the Internet connection from any

81
00:06:46,690 --> 00:06:48,370
client in the network.

82
00:06:48,760 --> 00:06:53,710
Now we're going to use this module to create an object to interact with.

83
00:06:53,710 --> 00:06:54,820
Q Number zero.

84
00:06:54,820 --> 00:06:59,590
So the Q that we created in this command, which is this queue right here.

85
00:07:00,610 --> 00:07:05,290
So first of all, I'm going to create a variable and we're I'm going to call this queue.

86
00:07:06,070 --> 00:07:11,860
We're going to make that equal to net filter queue dot to create the object.

87
00:07:11,860 --> 00:07:15,550
Usually object names start with a capital letter.

88
00:07:15,560 --> 00:07:19,510
So this one is called net filter.

89
00:07:19,540 --> 00:07:20,350
Q.

90
00:07:22,070 --> 00:07:29,660
So basically what we're doing is we're using we're creating an instance of a net filter Q object, and

91
00:07:29,660 --> 00:07:33,110
we're literally placing that in a variable called queue.

92
00:07:33,650 --> 00:07:38,300
So right now, this queue is still not interacting with the Q that we made here.

93
00:07:39,460 --> 00:07:45,580
In the next step, we're going to say Q which is the Q that we just created, and we're going to put

94
00:07:45,580 --> 00:07:49,240
a dot to invoke a method on this object on the net filter.

95
00:07:49,270 --> 00:07:50,380
Q Object.

96
00:07:50,470 --> 00:07:52,870
And this method is called bind.

97
00:07:53,500 --> 00:08:01,630
And basically what this method allows us to do is to connect or bind this Q to the Q that we created

98
00:08:01,630 --> 00:08:02,170
here.

99
00:08:02,890 --> 00:08:07,330
Now, in order for this to bind with this Q, we have to give it the Q number.

100
00:08:07,540 --> 00:08:10,920
And as you can see, I set the Q number here to zero.

101
00:08:10,930 --> 00:08:13,540
So we're going to set that to zero in here as well.

102
00:08:14,470 --> 00:08:17,950
And then we're going to give it a callback function.

103
00:08:18,010 --> 00:08:23,340
So this function will be executed on each packet that is in this.

104
00:08:23,350 --> 00:08:31,030
Q So you can think of this function as something similar to the callback function we used with Skippy

105
00:08:31,030 --> 00:08:34,000
that we passed to the PR an argument.

106
00:08:34,540 --> 00:08:37,180
So I'm going to call this function process packet.

107
00:08:38,830 --> 00:08:45,700
And basically what's going to happen is this function will be executed on each packet that is trapped

108
00:08:45,700 --> 00:08:47,260
in this queue right there.

109
00:08:48,640 --> 00:08:56,740
Now finally we're going to run the queue by doing queue run and that's it, we're done.

110
00:08:56,890 --> 00:09:02,200
So basically we created an instance of a net filter queue object.

111
00:09:02,440 --> 00:09:09,160
We connected this object with the queue that we created in here using the same ID.

112
00:09:09,190 --> 00:09:12,190
So we use zero here because we use zero here.

113
00:09:12,370 --> 00:09:20,770
And then we gave a callback function to execute on each packet that will be trapped in this queue right

114
00:09:20,770 --> 00:09:21,280
here.

115
00:09:22,640 --> 00:09:30,890
And finally we used cue dot run and I forgot the brackets in here to run this queue that we created.

116
00:09:31,340 --> 00:09:33,020
Now there is one thing missing.

117
00:09:33,020 --> 00:09:41,000
We haven't implemented the process packet callback function, so I'm just going to go in here and define

118
00:09:41,000 --> 00:09:41,480
it.

119
00:09:43,090 --> 00:09:49,360
Now similar to the callback function of the sniff of Skippy, this is going to take a packet as an input.

120
00:09:50,200 --> 00:09:53,860
And in here we're literally just going to print the packet.

121
00:09:55,050 --> 00:09:56,950
Now let's test our program.

122
00:09:56,970 --> 00:10:02,250
So, first of all, I'm going to become the man in the middle using my ARP subwoofer, so I'm just going

123
00:10:02,250 --> 00:10:07,710
to do python arp spoof dot py note I'm already in it's working directory.

124
00:10:08,860 --> 00:10:15,370
And then I'm going to run my net cut program and again, I'm already in it's working directory right

125
00:10:15,370 --> 00:10:15,850
here.

126
00:10:15,850 --> 00:10:19,840
So I'm just going to do python net dot pie.

127
00:10:21,520 --> 00:10:24,760
Now you can see packets are already coming in here.

128
00:10:24,790 --> 00:10:29,800
Now, let's go to my target machine, which is my windows machine, and let's just try to go to any

129
00:10:29,800 --> 00:10:30,550
website.

130
00:10:32,330 --> 00:10:39,680
Now you'll see that the browser is trying to load this website and we're receiving the packets in here,

131
00:10:39,860 --> 00:10:42,050
but the website is not loading.

132
00:10:42,050 --> 00:10:44,420
So as you can see, it's trapped.

133
00:10:44,750 --> 00:10:50,020
And the reason behind this is because all the packets are coming in here.

134
00:10:50,030 --> 00:10:52,580
They are getting trapped in this queue.

135
00:10:52,610 --> 00:10:59,030
They're being accessed by my Python program, which is printing the packets for me in here, but we're

136
00:10:59,030 --> 00:11:03,620
not forwarding the package to the target, so the target is not receiving these packets.

137
00:11:04,250 --> 00:11:05,870
So now we have two options.

138
00:11:05,870 --> 00:11:07,700
I'm just going to do control C here.

139
00:11:08,600 --> 00:11:16,820
The first option is to accept the packet so we can just do packet dot accept and this will basically

140
00:11:16,820 --> 00:11:19,760
forward the packet to its destination.

141
00:11:19,880 --> 00:11:22,640
So if we just run the program again.

142
00:11:24,020 --> 00:11:26,480
And I'm going to click on Try Again.

143
00:11:27,630 --> 00:11:31,300
And as you can see, the website loads, no problem at all.

144
00:11:31,320 --> 00:11:35,340
And this way the packets are being put in this queue.

145
00:11:35,370 --> 00:11:40,890
The Python program is able to access them and we'll see how we can modify them in the future.

146
00:11:40,890 --> 00:11:45,840
And then the Python program is forwarding these packets to its destination.

147
00:11:46,530 --> 00:11:54,000
The second option that we have is to drop the packet, which will actually allow us to achieve the goal

148
00:11:54,000 --> 00:11:58,740
of our program, which is to cut the Internet connection of the target client.

149
00:11:59,280 --> 00:12:06,000
So now if I run the program again and go here, let's try to go somewhere else.

150
00:12:06,000 --> 00:12:07,710
So let's go to Google.com.

151
00:12:09,440 --> 00:12:15,620
You'll see the page will not load because we are dropping the packets in here so we're not allowing

152
00:12:15,620 --> 00:12:17,540
them to reach their destination.

153
00:12:17,960 --> 00:12:23,840
So basically what's happening now is the packets are getting into the table, they're being accessed

154
00:12:23,840 --> 00:12:29,430
by the Python program, but the Python program is not forwarding them to their destination.

155
00:12:29,450 --> 00:12:31,520
They're being trapped in here.

156
00:12:33,510 --> 00:12:39,990
So now we know how to intercept packets using Python and in the future we'll see how we can modify these

157
00:12:39,990 --> 00:12:45,270
packets before we actually accept them and forward them to their destination.

158
00:12:46,020 --> 00:12:52,560
Now, once done with all of this, make sure that you actually delete the IP tables that you created

159
00:12:52,560 --> 00:12:53,490
at the start.

160
00:12:53,940 --> 00:12:58,680
So to do that, we're just going to do IP tables flush.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,050 --> 00:00:01,700
Okay.

2
00:00:01,710 --> 00:00:10,110
Now that we know how to trap packets in a queue and access this queue and control it from a Python program,

3
00:00:10,230 --> 00:00:18,390
the next step is to learn how to use this Python program to modify packets before forwarding them to

4
00:00:18,390 --> 00:00:19,560
their destination.

5
00:00:20,400 --> 00:00:25,410
As an example, I want to create a DNS spool for now.

6
00:00:25,410 --> 00:00:32,430
In the next lecture, I'll explain to you what I mean by DNS spoofing and how to exactly achieve that.

7
00:00:32,640 --> 00:00:39,180
But before we do that, because that's going to be a slightly complex program, I want to spend one

8
00:00:39,180 --> 00:00:46,230
more lecture to show you how to properly set up a testing environment so that in the future you learn

9
00:00:46,230 --> 00:00:53,760
how to properly analyze what happens when a certain request is sent, how to display that, how to modify

10
00:00:53,760 --> 00:00:59,040
it, and then properly achieve your goal or execute your attack.

11
00:00:59,970 --> 00:01:06,120
So first of all, because that's going to be, like I said, a little bit more complex than a simple

12
00:01:06,120 --> 00:01:07,530
net cut program.

13
00:01:07,860 --> 00:01:15,840
It will be handy if we can actually test this program on our local computer first and then run it on

14
00:01:15,840 --> 00:01:17,310
a remote computer.

15
00:01:17,910 --> 00:01:25,470
So basically what I want to achieve is I want to be able to use my program and test it by targeting

16
00:01:25,470 --> 00:01:26,910
my own computer.

17
00:01:26,910 --> 00:01:33,270
And then if this works, as we know from previous lectures, we'll be able to easily adapt it on a remote

18
00:01:33,270 --> 00:01:34,080
computer.

19
00:01:35,010 --> 00:01:42,000
Now, in the next lecture, we learned in order to trap packets in our queue, we had to run an IP table's

20
00:01:42,000 --> 00:01:49,110
command that will trap any packets going to the forward chain into a net filter queue.

21
00:01:49,110 --> 00:01:51,570
And then we gave the queue number in here.

22
00:01:52,290 --> 00:01:59,010
Now if you want to test this against your own local machine, then packets will not be going into the

23
00:01:59,010 --> 00:02:00,180
forward chain.

24
00:02:00,180 --> 00:02:04,290
They only go into this chain if they're coming from a different computer.

25
00:02:04,860 --> 00:02:11,310
So because we want to be able to test on our local computer, we're going to have to change this rule

26
00:02:11,310 --> 00:02:17,970
and say that the chain that I want to trap its packets is the output chain.

27
00:02:18,330 --> 00:02:23,730
So this is the chain where packets leaving my computer go through.

28
00:02:24,270 --> 00:02:27,750
So I'm going to hit enter and that's going to run with no errors.

29
00:02:28,320 --> 00:02:34,950
The next thing that I want to do is run the exact same command again, but this time I'm going to run

30
00:02:34,950 --> 00:02:36,840
it on the input chain.

31
00:02:37,730 --> 00:02:41,780
So these are the packets coming to my computer.

32
00:02:42,350 --> 00:02:51,440
Now, using these two commands by redirecting the output and the input chain to my Q and here all the

33
00:02:51,440 --> 00:02:59,150
requests and responses sent to my computer will be trapped into this queue and then I'll be able to

34
00:02:59,150 --> 00:03:02,290
use my Python program exactly the same way.

35
00:03:02,300 --> 00:03:09,590
So the only thing that's going to differ when testing on a local machine or against a remote machine

36
00:03:09,590 --> 00:03:13,640
with ARP spoofing is the IP tables rules.

37
00:03:13,640 --> 00:03:19,880
If you're testing against a remote computer or if you're targeting a remote computer, then you should

38
00:03:19,880 --> 00:03:23,840
redirect the forward chain to your queue.

39
00:03:24,080 --> 00:03:30,170
If you're testing on your local machine, then you want to redirect the output and the input chains.

40
00:03:31,030 --> 00:03:34,300
So now let's just go and run our program quickly.

41
00:03:34,300 --> 00:03:36,820
And I'm just going to modify this to accept.

42
00:03:38,560 --> 00:03:43,090
Now, if you notice, I've actually created a new project called DNS Spoof.

43
00:03:43,540 --> 00:03:49,330
Again, I'll explain what DNS spoofing for you in the next lecture and we'll talk about that in details.

44
00:03:49,360 --> 00:03:54,970
For now, this actually has the exact same code that we used in the net cut program.

45
00:03:55,540 --> 00:04:01,990
So I'm going to go down and I'm actually just going to go to the DNS spoof directory.

46
00:04:04,220 --> 00:04:07,670
And run it by doing Python dance spoof.

47
00:04:08,610 --> 00:04:12,210
Now this should simply just print the packets on the console.

48
00:04:12,210 --> 00:04:14,760
So if we just go to Booking.com.

49
00:04:15,900 --> 00:04:18,600
You'll see the packets being printed in here.

50
00:04:19,650 --> 00:04:21,390
So this is very good.

51
00:04:22,300 --> 00:04:28,600
The next thing that I want to show you in this lecture is how to get more details out of the packets.

52
00:04:28,870 --> 00:04:32,690
So you can see right here, it's shown us that we are capturing packets.

53
00:04:32,710 --> 00:04:36,040
It's showing us the protocol and the packet size.

54
00:04:36,040 --> 00:04:37,020
But that's it.

55
00:04:37,030 --> 00:04:38,980
There is nothing useful in here.

56
00:04:39,160 --> 00:04:46,000
Now we want to be able to read the data that's actually being sent inside the packet so that we can

57
00:04:46,000 --> 00:04:46,930
modify it.

58
00:04:46,960 --> 00:04:50,530
We can't just randomly modify packets like this.

59
00:04:51,130 --> 00:05:00,280
So in order to print more details in this packet, we can just say print packet, dot, get payload.

60
00:05:02,200 --> 00:05:05,830
So basically we're using a method called get payload.

61
00:05:05,830 --> 00:05:13,780
And what this method will do is it will show me the actual contents inside the packet itself.

62
00:05:14,410 --> 00:05:19,600
So let's go down and run our program again and let's search for something simple.

63
00:05:19,600 --> 00:05:21,580
For example, let's search for test.

64
00:05:23,340 --> 00:05:26,310
And as you can see, we're getting more useful data.

65
00:05:26,310 --> 00:05:32,910
And this data is similar to what we'd actually get in the raw layer when we were using Skippy.

66
00:05:33,690 --> 00:05:40,080
Now this is all good when you want to modify stuff in the raw layer, but we can't see anything else

67
00:05:40,080 --> 00:05:41,310
in other layers.

68
00:05:41,700 --> 00:05:47,700
So if you remember when we used to use Skippy, we were able to see all the layers, all the fields,

69
00:05:47,700 --> 00:05:52,320
and then we were able to actually even modify these fields and play around with them.

70
00:05:52,980 --> 00:06:00,450
So since in the next lectures we actually want to start modifying data, it will be handy if we can

71
00:06:00,450 --> 00:06:06,240
convert this packet right here to escape packet.

72
00:06:06,480 --> 00:06:11,190
If we do that, we'll be able to use all this stuff that we learned with Skippy.

73
00:06:11,190 --> 00:06:18,720
So we'll be able to use the Dojo method to see more information about the packet will be able to access

74
00:06:18,720 --> 00:06:20,040
its specific layers.

75
00:06:20,040 --> 00:06:27,450
Using the way I showed you before, we'll be able to modify specific fields in specific layers again,

76
00:06:27,450 --> 00:06:29,430
exactly as we seen before.

77
00:06:30,120 --> 00:06:38,040
Now, converting this packet to escape packet is very easy to do that we're just going to create a new

78
00:06:38,040 --> 00:06:40,980
variable and let's call this Skippy packet.

79
00:06:43,060 --> 00:06:54,040
And we're going to say that this Skippy packet is going to be equal to Skippy dot IP and then we're

80
00:06:54,040 --> 00:06:58,960
going to give the payload of the packet as an input.

81
00:06:59,500 --> 00:07:02,470
So I'm going to cut this and paste it here.

82
00:07:03,220 --> 00:07:11,860
So basically what I'm doing is I'm wrapping the payload of this packet with a Skippy IP layer.

83
00:07:12,430 --> 00:07:19,900
When I do that, Skippy will automatically convert this packet into a Skippy packet and then I'll be

84
00:07:19,900 --> 00:07:27,160
able to interact with this Skippy packet exactly the same way that I used to interact with packets snipped

85
00:07:27,160 --> 00:07:28,090
with Skippy.

86
00:07:28,120 --> 00:07:34,030
So like I said, I'll be able to access its layers, modify its fields, and we'll see how to do that

87
00:07:34,030 --> 00:07:35,230
in the next lecture.

88
00:07:35,740 --> 00:07:43,090
Right now let's just print this Skippy packet and we'll use a method that we can only use on Skippy

89
00:07:43,090 --> 00:07:49,600
packets that will show all the layers and all the fields, which is the DOT show method.

90
00:07:50,050 --> 00:07:55,900
Now we actually use this method a lot before and using it here in the print statement will show you

91
00:07:55,900 --> 00:08:02,830
that this packet got actually converted to a proper Skippy packet and that we can use all the things

92
00:08:02,830 --> 00:08:07,000
that we learned with Skippy before now in our program.

93
00:08:07,690 --> 00:08:12,460
Now I've actually not imported Skippy, so I'm going to do import.

94
00:08:13,320 --> 00:08:20,160
Skippy got all Skippy exactly the same way that we used to do it before.

95
00:08:20,490 --> 00:08:22,530
And we are good to go.

96
00:08:23,400 --> 00:08:27,030
So let's go down, run our program.

97
00:08:27,830 --> 00:08:30,360
And let's just go again to Buy.com.

98
00:08:30,410 --> 00:08:37,940
And actually, even before doing that, you can see that the packets are being printed with all of their

99
00:08:37,940 --> 00:08:44,270
layers because we're using the DOT show method and you can see that the output is identical to what

100
00:08:44,270 --> 00:08:51,200
we used to get with the Dutch show when we were using it on packets snipped with KP itself.

101
00:08:51,830 --> 00:08:58,610
Now this is very handy because like I said, we'll be able to access all the layers, not only the payload

102
00:08:58,610 --> 00:08:59,570
or the load.

103
00:08:59,660 --> 00:09:06,320
We'll be able to modify these layers based on their fields, and we'll see actually how powerful that

104
00:09:06,320 --> 00:09:08,330
is in the next lecture.

105
00:09:09,020 --> 00:09:16,760
Now, don't forget, once done with using this, remove the IP tables rules that you added by doing

106
00:09:16,760 --> 00:09:19,160
IP tables flush.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:06,310
Now in the previous lecture, I said My goal is to build a dense spoof.

2
00:00:06,940 --> 00:00:13,480
I even created a program called Dance Spoof, but I never explained what this program is supposed to

3
00:00:13,480 --> 00:00:14,050
do.

4
00:00:14,680 --> 00:00:21,880
Now, before we talk about our program, let's talk about dance and understand what it is and what it's

5
00:00:21,880 --> 00:00:22,840
used for.

6
00:00:23,380 --> 00:00:29,650
Now, I've actually spoke about dance and explain dance spoofing in my other lectures.

7
00:00:29,650 --> 00:00:34,930
So if you already know what it is, then you can skip this lecture and jump into the programming part

8
00:00:34,930 --> 00:00:36,100
in the next lecture.

9
00:00:36,670 --> 00:00:43,870
If you don't know what dance or dance spoofing is, or if you just want a refresh, then follow me in

10
00:00:43,870 --> 00:00:44,680
this lecture.

11
00:00:45,340 --> 00:00:49,390
So right here, I made a very simple diagram to show a user.

12
00:00:49,660 --> 00:00:53,110
And in here we have the cloud or the internet.

13
00:00:53,830 --> 00:00:59,230
Now, on the cloud, you can see we have a number of servers, so you have 1 to 3 servers.

14
00:00:59,680 --> 00:01:03,760
Now you can think of the servers as just computers.

15
00:01:03,760 --> 00:01:09,220
They are computers that work exactly like the computer that you're watching this video from.

16
00:01:09,460 --> 00:01:16,120
The only difference is it has more resources and it's tuned in a way to work as a server.

17
00:01:17,010 --> 00:01:24,570
So you can think of websites such as bing dot com and facebook.com as a bunch of files installed on

18
00:01:24,570 --> 00:01:25,470
a computer.

19
00:01:25,500 --> 00:01:29,170
This computer has a special program called a web server.

20
00:01:29,190 --> 00:01:35,550
Therefore, it's able to display the contents of the website for you when you go to big dotcom or to

21
00:01:35,550 --> 00:01:36,750
Facebook.com.

22
00:01:37,350 --> 00:01:44,280
Now, just to convey this idea, let me go here to my Kelly machine and show you how to actually use

23
00:01:44,280 --> 00:01:47,250
Carly as a web server and as a website.

24
00:01:47,640 --> 00:01:55,170
So Kelly comes in with a web server pre-installed in it, and all you have to do to start it is do service

25
00:01:55,620 --> 00:01:57,030
Apache to start.

26
00:01:58,970 --> 00:02:04,670
Now this will start my web server and it will allow me to access the website that's installed on this

27
00:02:04,670 --> 00:02:09,290
web server using the IP of the current computer of the machine.

28
00:02:09,560 --> 00:02:15,830
So to get the IP of Kali, I'm going to do if config and this is the IP of my machine.

29
00:02:15,950 --> 00:02:21,080
So if I literally just copy and paste this IP in a web browser.

30
00:02:23,000 --> 00:02:28,370
You'll see, I'm able to access the default website that's installed on the web server.

31
00:02:28,610 --> 00:02:33,470
Now, this is an actual proper web server that is used on many famous websites.

32
00:02:34,220 --> 00:02:40,100
Now the files that are loaded by this web server are actually stored on this current computer.

33
00:02:40,190 --> 00:02:42,620
So if I just go to places.

34
00:02:44,730 --> 00:02:53,850
And if we just go to var w w w h html, you'll see the page that is being loaded in here, which is

35
00:02:53,850 --> 00:02:55,900
called indexed HTML.

36
00:02:56,070 --> 00:03:03,090
So this is literally a file that is being loaded by a special program that is called a web server.

37
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:09,750
Now also, if we just go to a Windows machine, so this is another computer completely separate than

38
00:03:09,750 --> 00:03:10,350
Kelly.

39
00:03:10,350 --> 00:03:13,530
And if I just paste the IP of Kelly in here.

40
00:03:14,980 --> 00:03:17,770
Again, you'll see the website loading in here.

41
00:03:18,250 --> 00:03:25,060
So basically what I'm trying to say right now is a website is literally a bunch of files installed on

42
00:03:25,060 --> 00:03:25,960
a computer.

43
00:03:25,960 --> 00:03:30,670
And these files are loaded through a special program that we call a web server.

44
00:03:31,180 --> 00:03:36,840
Now, the way to access any computer on the Internet is using the IP of that computer.

45
00:03:36,850 --> 00:03:43,060
So even when we try to hack into a computer, we have to either connect to that computer using its IP

46
00:03:43,060 --> 00:03:47,750
or get that computer to connect back to us again using our IP.

47
00:03:47,770 --> 00:03:53,500
So the communication on the Internet is always facilitated using the IP address.

48
00:03:54,670 --> 00:04:00,230
Now, if you think of it, when we actually try to go to a website, we never use the IP.

49
00:04:00,250 --> 00:04:07,090
So when I want to go to big dotcom, I just typing dot com, I don't put the IP of bing dot com.

50
00:04:07,510 --> 00:04:11,710
And here is where DNS servers are used.

51
00:04:11,950 --> 00:04:15,310
So DNS stands for domain name server.

52
00:04:15,760 --> 00:04:23,320
And basically let's go back to our diagram and let's say this user right here wants to go to CNN.com.

53
00:04:23,680 --> 00:04:25,980
So they open their Web browser like I did.

54
00:04:25,990 --> 00:04:31,810
They type ping dot com in the browser, but the computer doesn't know where Bing is.

55
00:04:31,810 --> 00:04:34,300
It doesn't know that Bing has this IP.

56
00:04:34,300 --> 00:04:38,830
And the only way for this computer to access Bing is using its IP.

57
00:04:39,310 --> 00:04:45,040
So what will the computer do is it will send a request to a special server.

58
00:04:45,040 --> 00:04:51,220
So this is just another computer, again, is tuned in a certain way and has certain programs installed

59
00:04:51,220 --> 00:04:57,010
on it, and it's designed to resolve domain names into IP addresses.

60
00:04:57,460 --> 00:05:05,590
So when the user sends a request to the DNS server asking for dot com, the DNS server will have a big

61
00:05:05,590 --> 00:05:09,850
table like this and it contains a number of domains.

62
00:05:09,850 --> 00:05:14,680
As you can see here, each one of them might have multiple records.

63
00:05:14,680 --> 00:05:20,230
So right here we have an example of an A record and basically it's going to look where it's bing dot

64
00:05:20,230 --> 00:05:25,030
com, bing dot com is here and it's IP is right here.

65
00:05:25,630 --> 00:05:34,690
Therefore it's going to send a DNS response to the user telling the user that Bing Dotcom actually is

66
00:05:34,690 --> 00:05:38,140
located at a computer with this IP address.

67
00:05:38,440 --> 00:05:45,520
Then the computer knows where bing dot com is and it will communicate with bing dot com and bing will

68
00:05:45,520 --> 00:05:49,810
send the responses and then the user will be able to use that website.

69
00:05:51,370 --> 00:05:59,440
So basically the whole point of using DNS is to translate domain names such as Booking.com or Facebook.com

70
00:05:59,440 --> 00:06:04,450
to the IP addresses of the servers hosting these websites.

71
00:06:05,810 --> 00:06:10,730
Now things get interesting when a hacker manages to become the man in the middle.

72
00:06:10,970 --> 00:06:17,060
We know that when a hacker manages to become the man in the middle, regardless of how they achieve

73
00:06:17,060 --> 00:06:23,180
that, all the requests sent from the user will have to flow through the hacker's computer.

74
00:06:23,210 --> 00:06:26,450
The hacker will then forward that to its destination.

75
00:06:26,480 --> 00:06:28,330
Same goes for the responses.

76
00:06:28,340 --> 00:06:32,480
They'll flow through the hacker and the hacker will forward them to the user.

77
00:06:33,320 --> 00:06:39,470
Now, as packets are flowing through the hacker's computer, the hacker can control the direction and

78
00:06:39,470 --> 00:06:41,330
the content of these packets.

79
00:06:41,900 --> 00:06:47,960
Now, going back to the example that we're talking about, let's say the user wants to go to CNN.com.

80
00:06:48,320 --> 00:06:50,780
The hacker is going to receive this request.

81
00:06:50,840 --> 00:06:58,970
And at this stage, the hacker has a number of ways to serve the IP of the hacker's web server, which

82
00:06:58,970 --> 00:07:00,650
is ten, 0 to 16.

83
00:07:00,650 --> 00:07:09,110
And instead of the IP of Booking.com, this is very dangerous because we'll be able to hijack and spoof

84
00:07:09,140 --> 00:07:14,160
any DNS request made by the user and serve the user.

85
00:07:14,180 --> 00:07:19,670
Fake websites, fake login pages, fake updates, and so on.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:08,570
Now, if you're the man in the middle and you get a DNS request from a user of a domain name, then

2
00:00:08,570 --> 00:00:13,740
you can easily spoof this request and serve the user any website you want.

3
00:00:13,760 --> 00:00:17,960
For example, it can serve them your website, your own fake website.

4
00:00:18,410 --> 00:00:20,610
Now you have a number of options to do this.

5
00:00:20,630 --> 00:00:26,900
The easiest is to install a DNS server, so an application similar to the one installed on this web

6
00:00:26,900 --> 00:00:34,760
server on your machine and configure that server to return whatever website you want for whatever request

7
00:00:34,760 --> 00:00:36,170
that the user enters.

8
00:00:36,770 --> 00:00:42,470
Now, this does not involve a lot of programming, and since this is a programming course, we're not

9
00:00:42,470 --> 00:00:43,310
going to cover this.

10
00:00:43,310 --> 00:00:45,410
I actually cover it in my other courses.

11
00:00:45,830 --> 00:00:53,450
The next option is to craft a DNS response in the hacker computer, in this computer right here and

12
00:00:53,450 --> 00:00:58,550
send it back to the user, given them this IP instead of the actual IP for Bing.

13
00:00:59,120 --> 00:01:05,930
Now, this would require you to have extensive knowledge of how DNS works and how network layers work

14
00:01:05,930 --> 00:01:06,800
in general.

15
00:01:07,160 --> 00:01:12,530
And even with that, you might miss small things and then the attack will just not work.

16
00:01:13,310 --> 00:01:20,420
The third option is to actually forward the request that the user made to the to the right DNS server.

17
00:01:20,990 --> 00:01:27,980
Wait for the response and once we get the response at the hacker machine will modify this response will

18
00:01:27,980 --> 00:01:36,440
only modify the IP part and instead of sending the right IP, we will send the IP that we want and this

19
00:01:36,440 --> 00:01:37,520
is what we'll do.

20
00:01:38,210 --> 00:01:42,320
So I'm going to go to the Python program that we wrote before.

21
00:01:42,410 --> 00:01:44,720
I still didn't modify anything in it.

22
00:01:44,720 --> 00:01:50,720
It's still doing the exact same thing, just converting the packet to escape packet and printing the

23
00:01:50,720 --> 00:01:52,280
show of this packet.

24
00:01:52,820 --> 00:02:00,440
Now before I run this code, because we're interested in packets that has DNS responses, I'm going

25
00:02:00,440 --> 00:02:08,420
to add an if statement and I'm going to use the hash layer method just like we've seen before, to check

26
00:02:08,450 --> 00:02:15,080
if Skippy packet has a DNS response layer.

27
00:02:15,080 --> 00:02:17,840
So we put the layer name between the two brackets.

28
00:02:17,840 --> 00:02:24,230
And because we're looking for a DNS response, we're going to say Skippy dot DNS.

29
00:02:24,230 --> 00:02:31,880
Or if you're looking for a DNS request, you do our Q but I'm looking for a DNS response, so I'm going

30
00:02:31,880 --> 00:02:37,460
to say DNS or and I'm going to tap this print statement.

31
00:02:37,460 --> 00:02:39,620
So that's inside the if statement.

32
00:02:40,010 --> 00:02:45,080
And all this is going to do is it's going to check if the packet has a DNS response.

33
00:02:45,110 --> 00:02:47,870
It's going to print the show of this packet.

34
00:02:49,310 --> 00:02:57,020
Now let's go ahead and generate a DNS request and see what the response looks like in here.

35
00:02:57,050 --> 00:03:03,140
Now I'm doing all of this because I don't know what a DNS response looks like, so I want to see what

36
00:03:03,140 --> 00:03:10,310
it looks like first, and then I'll try to modify that in order to achieve my goal and spoof the response

37
00:03:10,310 --> 00:03:11,660
to whatever I want.

38
00:03:12,250 --> 00:03:14,140
So I'm going to run my program here.

39
00:03:15,370 --> 00:03:18,490
And I'm going to generate a DNS request here.

40
00:03:18,490 --> 00:03:26,170
But before I do that, I need to redirect all the input and output packets in my computer to the Q that's

41
00:03:26,170 --> 00:03:27,250
used in here.

42
00:03:27,520 --> 00:03:33,010
Now, I covered why we do this and how to do this before, so I'm going to do that quickly using IP

43
00:03:33,010 --> 00:03:33,790
tables.

44
00:03:34,000 --> 00:03:40,180
So we're going to redirect the output using this command and redirect the input using this command.

45
00:03:40,980 --> 00:03:47,070
And finally, I'm going to generate a DNS request so that I can see the response in here.

46
00:03:47,970 --> 00:03:53,010
Now, the easiest way to generate a DNS request is to use the pin command.

47
00:03:53,760 --> 00:03:59,250
The PIN Command can be used to send PIN requests to any website you want.

48
00:03:59,550 --> 00:04:04,650
So I'm going to say I want to send only one pin request using the dash key argument.

49
00:04:05,860 --> 00:04:09,260
And then I'm going to put the website that I want to send the request to.

50
00:04:09,280 --> 00:04:11,950
And as an example, I'm going to use bing dot com.

51
00:04:13,160 --> 00:04:18,740
So this is literally going to send only one pink request to dot com.

52
00:04:19,370 --> 00:04:20,750
Now if I hit enter.

53
00:04:21,980 --> 00:04:26,130
You'll see this will actually give me the IP of Bing.

54
00:04:26,150 --> 00:04:30,800
So this is a very easy and quick way to get the IP of any website you want.

55
00:04:30,800 --> 00:04:35,690
And we usually use this a lot in hacking when we want to get the IP of a website quickly.

56
00:04:35,930 --> 00:04:42,470
Now this ping request generated a DNS request and for that DNS request we got a response.

57
00:04:42,470 --> 00:04:46,160
And in the response we got the IP of the website.

58
00:04:46,970 --> 00:04:50,570
Now this is shown in more details in here in my program.

59
00:04:50,720 --> 00:04:52,370
So I'm going to do control, see.

60
00:04:53,470 --> 00:04:55,900
And let's have a look on what we have.

61
00:04:57,480 --> 00:05:00,610
And as you can see, we have the response here.

62
00:05:00,630 --> 00:05:03,330
It's made of a number of layers.

63
00:05:03,450 --> 00:05:11,100
And therefore, you can see trying to spoof this is not as simple as just modifying the IP.

64
00:05:11,130 --> 00:05:17,760
So this diagram right here simplifies things, but this is not the way that things happen in real life.

65
00:05:17,760 --> 00:05:24,650
And that's why we needed to first analyze what happens when a request is sent and a response is received.

66
00:05:24,660 --> 00:05:30,780
And then from here we'll try to make sense of this and modify it so that we can achieve our goal.

67
00:05:31,320 --> 00:05:35,670
So so I could have actually just made a quick lecture showing you how to write the program.

68
00:05:35,670 --> 00:05:41,730
You'll see the program is actually just a few number of lines, but knowing what to modify is key.

69
00:05:41,730 --> 00:05:48,600
And I wanted to show you the proper way of trying to think and analyze and then build the program.

70
00:05:49,880 --> 00:05:51,310
So so far this is great.

71
00:05:51,320 --> 00:05:56,450
We are able to see that the actual packet contains a number of layers.

72
00:05:56,450 --> 00:06:03,440
We have an IP layer, we have a UDP layer and we have a dense layer which is the one that we want to

73
00:06:03,440 --> 00:06:04,850
play with the most.

74
00:06:05,450 --> 00:06:14,390
Now in the next lecture, we'll try to dissect this, understand it more, and build our DNS spoof and

75
00:06:14,390 --> 00:06:20,720
see how we can modify this packet and forward it to the target so that we can redirect them whenever

76
00:06:20,720 --> 00:06:22,280
they look for big dot com.

77
00:06:22,280 --> 00:06:25,400
We can redirect them to any other website we want.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,720 --> 00:00:05,860
Right now that we have a complete DNS response in here.

2
00:00:05,880 --> 00:00:11,190
Let's try to understand what's in it so that we can modify it and achieve our goal.

3
00:00:11,700 --> 00:00:17,820
Now, let's forget about these main fields here at the start, and let's look at the first main part

4
00:00:17,820 --> 00:00:22,080
of the response, which is the DNS question record.

5
00:00:22,710 --> 00:00:26,670
Remember I said, if you want to check for a request, you do DNS.

6
00:00:26,700 --> 00:00:32,640
Q Ah, so it's short for DNS question record and we're looking for DNS responses.

7
00:00:32,640 --> 00:00:36,930
We do DNS or which is what we used in here.

8
00:00:37,560 --> 00:00:42,510
So you can see there are two main parts of this layer, which is the question.

9
00:00:42,690 --> 00:00:47,940
And that question was asking for a website called W-w-what dot com.

10
00:00:48,720 --> 00:00:51,810
You can see the type which is a which is an A record.

11
00:00:51,810 --> 00:00:56,760
And like I said, there is a number of DNS records, but the one that we're interested in, the one

12
00:00:56,760 --> 00:01:03,630
that converts domain names to IP is is the A and you can see in here we have the answer.

13
00:01:03,630 --> 00:01:08,220
So it's dot an is the short or DNS resource record.

14
00:01:08,880 --> 00:01:12,540
And you can see we actually got a number of answers in here.

15
00:01:13,410 --> 00:01:18,410
Now, again, the main answer that we'd be interested in is the one to the A record.

16
00:01:18,420 --> 00:01:27,840
So if we look down, you'll see we have an answer in here and you can see the our data field is the

17
00:01:27,840 --> 00:01:34,890
field that contains the IP, which is the same IP that we received when we pinged bing dot com.

18
00:01:35,830 --> 00:01:39,880
So this is the field that we actually want to modify first.

19
00:01:41,590 --> 00:01:48,730
But before we modify this field, we only want to modify this field whenever the user tries to go to

20
00:01:48,970 --> 00:01:49,500
dot com.

21
00:01:49,510 --> 00:01:53,770
We don't want to modify this field every time the user wants to go to a website.

22
00:01:53,800 --> 00:01:58,000
We only want to do that when the user wants to go to a specific website.

23
00:01:58,000 --> 00:02:01,900
And in our example, this specific website is being dot com.

24
00:02:02,350 --> 00:02:08,200
So the first thing that we need to check is if the user is going to the Target website.

25
00:02:08,530 --> 00:02:15,460
And to do that, if you look up, you'll see in the question again, this is where our target website

26
00:02:15,460 --> 00:02:16,300
is listed.

27
00:02:16,900 --> 00:02:20,500
So we're going to use another if statement in here.

28
00:02:20,620 --> 00:02:27,040
After checking that this packet contains a response, we're going to check if the question, which is

29
00:02:27,040 --> 00:02:30,820
this part right here is looking for our target website.

30
00:02:31,360 --> 00:02:37,270
So first of all, I'm going to save this field in a variable in my program because I'm going to need

31
00:02:37,270 --> 00:02:38,950
to use it multiple times.

32
00:02:39,880 --> 00:02:45,760
So to access this field, we're going to access it exactly the same way that we would access any other

33
00:02:45,760 --> 00:02:47,020
field in KP.

34
00:02:47,020 --> 00:02:52,540
So that's why when I was showing you stuff before, I was always saying you can access fields the same

35
00:02:52,540 --> 00:02:55,270
way regardless of what you're trying to achieve.

36
00:02:55,720 --> 00:03:03,790
So to access the Q&amp;A, all we have to do is put the packet name, which is CP packet, and put the layer

37
00:03:03,790 --> 00:03:05,440
name between square brackets.

38
00:03:05,440 --> 00:03:13,030
And in my case it's the DNS, QR and the field name that I'm interested in is Q Name.

39
00:03:13,510 --> 00:03:16,990
Now, I'm not going to spend too much time talking about how I got this again.

40
00:03:16,990 --> 00:03:22,570
I explained this in details in a number of lectures before, so I'm just going to continue and what

41
00:03:22,570 --> 00:03:28,600
I want to do with this, I want to store this in a variable and I'll call this variable Q name.

42
00:03:29,290 --> 00:03:32,740
So we have Q&amp;A is equal to the Q name field.

43
00:03:32,740 --> 00:03:35,470
So right now it should be equal to com.

44
00:03:36,350 --> 00:03:37,880
Next, we're going to check.

45
00:03:38,120 --> 00:03:39,770
We're going to say if.

46
00:03:41,020 --> 00:03:46,630
W w w dot pink dot com is in my Q&amp;A.

47
00:03:47,410 --> 00:03:50,980
Then we want to modify this packet in here.

48
00:03:52,200 --> 00:03:56,160
And I'm going to remove my print statement because we're not going to be printing that.

49
00:03:56,250 --> 00:04:01,950
And actually, let's print a nice print statement saying spoofing target.

50
00:04:02,920 --> 00:04:07,930
Now you can put this in a variable and say in spoofing dot com, but I don't want to spend too much

51
00:04:07,930 --> 00:04:08,530
time on that.

52
00:04:08,530 --> 00:04:12,640
I want to focus on the fact that we want to do a spoofing attack.

53
00:04:13,210 --> 00:04:20,260
So now that we're able to check if a packet is a DNS response and if that DNS response is a response

54
00:04:20,260 --> 00:04:26,110
to a website that we want to target, that we want to spoof and we want to redirect to somewhere else.

55
00:04:26,140 --> 00:04:32,410
Then in here we can put our code that we'll use to redirect the user to somewhere else.

56
00:04:32,740 --> 00:04:39,040
So just like I said before, if we look at all the records that were sent in, the answer, the one

57
00:04:39,040 --> 00:04:41,290
that we're interested in is the A record.

58
00:04:42,040 --> 00:04:46,480
And you can see that the IP is stored in the our data field.

59
00:04:46,990 --> 00:04:52,780
So let's use KP to create a DNS response just like this one.

60
00:04:52,780 --> 00:04:58,510
And instead of using this IP in the our data, we're going to use any IP we want.

61
00:04:59,240 --> 00:05:06,020
So let's go here in our code and we're going to say, I'm just going to create a variable called answer

62
00:05:06,410 --> 00:05:10,820
and I'm going to make that equal to, say, p dot.

63
00:05:11,420 --> 00:05:15,470
Dense or because we want to create a dense response.

64
00:05:16,250 --> 00:05:22,340
And in this response, we want to first of all, we want to put the following fields in here so you

65
00:05:22,340 --> 00:05:26,690
can literally just copy and paste all of these fields in here.

66
00:05:27,350 --> 00:05:33,980
But you don't actually have to do that because Skippy will automatically calculate and fill any missing

67
00:05:33,980 --> 00:05:34,700
fields.

68
00:05:34,700 --> 00:05:41,240
So you only need to specify the ones that you actually want to change or the main fields that that cannot

69
00:05:41,240 --> 00:05:42,860
be determined by.

70
00:05:42,950 --> 00:05:44,140
KP Itself.

71
00:05:44,150 --> 00:05:48,290
So the main ones are the RR name and the our data.

72
00:05:48,830 --> 00:05:57,140
So the RR name is actually going to be the same as the Q name because we want to send this for whenever

73
00:05:57,140 --> 00:06:04,070
the person looks for big dot com and we already calculated the Q name using this variable in here.

74
00:06:04,610 --> 00:06:10,850
So this is done and the next thing that we want to do is specify the our data.

75
00:06:11,150 --> 00:06:17,870
And as you can see in here, this is the field that contains the IP that is sent as a response whenever

76
00:06:17,870 --> 00:06:19,760
a DNS request is sent.

77
00:06:20,510 --> 00:06:25,520
So we want to set this to any IP you want to redirect your target to.

78
00:06:25,940 --> 00:06:32,150
And in our example, let's redirect them to a web server that is hosted on my machine.

79
00:06:32,150 --> 00:06:38,600
Now again, you can use any IP you want, but I'm just going to use case IP because I already have a

80
00:06:38,600 --> 00:06:43,880
web server running on it and this will work great as an example, and that's it.

81
00:06:43,880 --> 00:06:51,320
So all we did is we used KP to create a dense response and we only specify two fields, the only two

82
00:06:51,320 --> 00:06:58,280
fields that KP cannot determine by itself, because like I said, we can omit a lot of fields and KP

83
00:06:58,280 --> 00:07:04,250
will automatically fill them in, such as the our class, the title and so on.

84
00:07:04,910 --> 00:07:11,990
So all we did is we specify the RR name, which is the website that the user requested, and that is

85
00:07:11,990 --> 00:07:14,840
the same as the Q&amp;A, which is bing dot com.

86
00:07:15,500 --> 00:07:23,540
And the next thing that we specified is the R data, which is the IP returned as the IP of the requested

87
00:07:23,540 --> 00:07:24,260
domain.

88
00:07:24,620 --> 00:07:31,520
And again, we are able to determine all of this by looking at a proper DNS response.

89
00:07:31,520 --> 00:07:34,430
So we didn't just wake up and know how to do this.

90
00:07:35,000 --> 00:07:36,080
Now this is great.

91
00:07:36,080 --> 00:07:42,290
We've made our response in here, but we still haven't used this response in the packet.

92
00:07:42,290 --> 00:07:48,440
So in the next lecture I'll show you how to inject this response instead of the actual response that

93
00:07:48,440 --> 00:07:50,360
is being sent to the target.

94
00:07:50,360 --> 00:07:55,130
And we'll see how to modify a few other fields to get this to work properly.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,850 --> 00:00:01,500
Okay.

2
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:08,170
Now that we created a spoofed answer, we still haven't used it anywhere, so it's still not even being

3
00:00:08,170 --> 00:00:08,990
sent.

4
00:00:09,010 --> 00:00:11,650
So there is no way we can send it to the user.

5
00:00:12,070 --> 00:00:20,860
So let's modify the answer field of Skippy packet and make that equal to the answer that we just created.

6
00:00:21,280 --> 00:00:26,420
So to access a field, again, I've showed this multiple times before.

7
00:00:26,440 --> 00:00:30,940
All we have to do is just put the packet name, which is CP packet.

8
00:00:31,000 --> 00:00:36,700
Put the layer between square brackets and that's going to be the DNS layer.

9
00:00:36,880 --> 00:00:44,440
And what we want to modify is if we go back, you'll see that we want to modify the answer part.

10
00:00:44,440 --> 00:00:45,610
So this part.

11
00:00:45,730 --> 00:00:53,650
So again, we'll do that again because this can be looked at as a field as well instead of DNS or.

12
00:00:54,610 --> 00:00:57,910
And we'll make that equal to my answer.

13
00:00:59,110 --> 00:01:01,660
So he could have really just put this line in here.

14
00:01:01,660 --> 00:01:07,420
But I just wanted to make things simpler as I'm writing the program now, I've actually forgot to put

15
00:01:07,450 --> 00:01:11,290
Skippy in here before the layer name, so that's fine.

16
00:01:11,830 --> 00:01:16,750
And now we actually modified this answer in here.

17
00:01:16,750 --> 00:01:19,660
So everything on top is still the same.

18
00:01:19,660 --> 00:01:27,190
And everything in here that is being stored after the answer has been modified with what we created.

19
00:01:28,340 --> 00:01:36,500
Now, if you look at this, you'll see that there is one, two, three, four answers being sent as

20
00:01:36,500 --> 00:01:37,580
a response.

21
00:01:37,910 --> 00:01:40,880
But we only created one answer.

22
00:01:41,390 --> 00:01:48,590
Now, if you look up in the other fields that I told you to forget about, for now, you'll see we have

23
00:01:48,590 --> 00:01:51,260
a field called a n count.

24
00:01:51,410 --> 00:01:57,410
So this is the number of answers that were sent as a response to the question.

25
00:01:58,160 --> 00:02:00,640
So, again, this number is four.

26
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:04,060
It corresponds to the number of answers that are being sent here.

27
00:02:04,070 --> 00:02:07,700
But we only created one answer, one response.

28
00:02:08,180 --> 00:02:14,750
Therefore, we need to modify this field so that it corresponds to the number of answers that we're

29
00:02:14,750 --> 00:02:16,310
sending in our code.

30
00:02:16,850 --> 00:02:23,360
So to modify this field again, exactly the same as modifying any other field, you're going to do,

31
00:02:23,360 --> 00:02:29,480
say P, followed by the layer name, followed by the field name and the field name is A and count.

32
00:02:30,290 --> 00:02:34,700
And we're going to set that to one, and that's perfect.

33
00:02:35,330 --> 00:02:42,650
Now, the last thing that I want to talk about is the length fields in the IP layer and here and in

34
00:02:42,650 --> 00:02:45,890
the UTP layer and the checksum layers.

35
00:02:45,890 --> 00:02:55,190
And here and in here now the length layer corresponds to the length of or the size of the layer, and

36
00:02:55,190 --> 00:03:00,320
the checksum is used to make sure that the packet has not been modified.

37
00:03:00,590 --> 00:03:07,820
So we have a length layer in the IP layer and in the UDP layer and we have a checksum here and a checksum

38
00:03:07,820 --> 00:03:08,360
here.

39
00:03:08,930 --> 00:03:15,740
Now, to make sure that these fields will not corrupt our packet, we're going to remove them from our

40
00:03:15,740 --> 00:03:23,300
packet and then when we send them, Skippy will automatically recalculate them based on the values that

41
00:03:23,300 --> 00:03:24,350
we modified.

42
00:03:24,620 --> 00:03:28,510
So this is actually something that's great about Skippy.

43
00:03:28,670 --> 00:03:35,360
So all we have to do is just delete these fields and to delete a field, all you have to do is type

44
00:03:35,360 --> 00:03:35,900
delete.

45
00:03:35,900 --> 00:03:41,090
So the L followed by the field name and again to access a field.

46
00:03:41,090 --> 00:03:44,480
You'll do this exactly the same way that we've been doing it before.

47
00:03:44,480 --> 00:03:49,340
So you put the packet name, which is CP packet followed by the layer.

48
00:03:49,340 --> 00:03:52,700
So we're going to do this for the IP layer first.

49
00:03:52,700 --> 00:03:58,340
So we'll do scapegoat IP and we want to remove the length.

50
00:03:59,030 --> 00:04:03,890
I'm going to do control D to duplicate this line and remove the checksum.

51
00:04:05,000 --> 00:04:10,220
And then we want to do exactly the same as this for the player.

52
00:04:10,550 --> 00:04:11,840
So I'm going to do control.

53
00:04:11,840 --> 00:04:15,290
DH And we're looking for you.

54
00:04:15,290 --> 00:04:21,260
DP Removing the checksum and do this again and remove the length.

55
00:04:21,950 --> 00:04:28,400
So again, this will remove the length and the checksum layers before sending this packet, so that

56
00:04:28,400 --> 00:04:34,160
when KP sends them, it will recalculate them based on the values that we modified.

57
00:04:34,760 --> 00:04:35,630
And that's it.

58
00:04:35,630 --> 00:04:37,070
Our code is done.

59
00:04:37,370 --> 00:04:41,840
So first of all, we're checking if our packet contains a DNS response.

60
00:04:41,840 --> 00:04:49,160
We're checking if the question that caused this response is a domain that we want to target.

61
00:04:49,160 --> 00:04:51,440
And in our example, it's dot com.

62
00:04:52,220 --> 00:04:55,580
If it is, we're going to create a spoofed answer.

63
00:04:56,000 --> 00:05:02,210
We're going to modify our escape packet so that it uses this answer.

64
00:05:02,210 --> 00:05:09,080
And then we modified a number of fields to make sure that this packet will work and it will not be corrupt

65
00:05:09,080 --> 00:05:11,450
after we modified this answer.

66
00:05:13,180 --> 00:05:20,350
Now all of the modification has been done on Skippy packet on a variable called Skippy packet which

67
00:05:20,350 --> 00:05:27,880
is derived from the packet that is being sniffed, that is being trapped in the queue, in the queue.

68
00:05:28,300 --> 00:05:34,810
Now if you look at the end and if you remember the first time we spoke about this queue, we said we

69
00:05:34,810 --> 00:05:40,150
have to do packet dot except to actually forward this packet to the target.

70
00:05:40,510 --> 00:05:47,740
So with all of the modification that I did in here, this modified packet, Skippy packet is not being

71
00:05:47,740 --> 00:05:54,400
sent to the target because packet, the one that we're receiving is never getting modified through our

72
00:05:54,400 --> 00:05:54,890
code.

73
00:05:54,910 --> 00:05:56,580
We are receiving it here.

74
00:05:56,590 --> 00:06:03,760
We're making Skippy packet equal to it and then we're carrying out all of the operations on Skippy packet.

75
00:06:04,090 --> 00:06:10,000
Then the packet is actually being sent properly without modifying anything in it.

76
00:06:10,690 --> 00:06:16,600
So the last step in here after modifying Skippy packet and doing all of the cool stuff that we did,

77
00:06:16,750 --> 00:06:23,890
we need to set the payload of this packet, of the packet that we snipped to escape the packet to the

78
00:06:23,890 --> 00:06:29,710
modified packet to the packet that we've been working on throughout all of the previous lectures.

79
00:06:30,160 --> 00:06:32,410
Now to do that is very simple.

80
00:06:32,440 --> 00:06:38,980
All we have to do is put the packet that we want to modify and we're going to say we want to set the

81
00:06:38,980 --> 00:06:46,360
payload of this packet to the string of my Skippy packet.

82
00:06:47,200 --> 00:06:52,960
Now the reason why I use this string here can be explained if you actually go back and watch.

83
00:06:52,960 --> 00:07:00,610
The first time that I converted this packet to Skippy packet because if you actually print this packet,

84
00:07:00,610 --> 00:07:04,900
you will just get a string of the data inside this packet.

85
00:07:04,900 --> 00:07:10,210
You will not be able to access the layers nicely the same way that we do with Skippy.

86
00:07:10,630 --> 00:07:15,610
That's why the first thing that we did is we converted packet to Skippy packet.

87
00:07:15,640 --> 00:07:23,200
We play around with it, we modify it all the way we want, and then at the end we convert the Skippy

88
00:07:23,200 --> 00:07:30,220
packet to a normal string, and then we give that string to my packet, to my original packet in here.

89
00:07:30,220 --> 00:07:33,190
And finally we accept this packet.

90
00:07:33,580 --> 00:07:40,690
Invoking the accept method will forward this packet to its target and then it should work the same way

91
00:07:40,690 --> 00:07:41,740
that we wanted.

92
00:07:42,430 --> 00:07:45,100
Now, in the next lecture, I'm going to test this.

93
00:07:45,130 --> 00:07:49,480
We're going to test our DNS buffer against our local machine on the machine.

94
00:07:49,480 --> 00:07:55,240
And we're also going to test it against a remote Windows computer and see how we can use that with an

95
00:07:55,240 --> 00:07:56,860
ARP spoofing attack.

I'm going to do Python Dance Proof Dot Pi.

00:00:19,560 --> 00:00:27,150
I've already run my IP tables that will redirect everything to cue number zero, which is the one that

7
00:00:27,150 --> 00:00:28,780
is trapping packets here.

8
00:00:28,800 --> 00:00:38,280
So now if I do ping c one being dot com and instead of getting the IP of bing like we were getting previously

9
00:00:38,280 --> 00:00:44,400
this one, we should get the IP that I set in here which is ten 0 to 16.

10
00:00:45,350 --> 00:00:47,540
So let's run this.

11
00:00:48,260 --> 00:00:49,070
And perfect.

12
00:00:49,070 --> 00:00:49,920
It's working.

13
00:00:49,940 --> 00:00:56,410
We got our print statement here and we got the IP ten 0 to 16.

14
00:00:56,420 --> 00:00:58,190
I'm actually really happy right now.

15
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:07,400
So the target is requesting to go to Buy.com and the DNS, which is supposed to give the target the

16
00:01:07,400 --> 00:01:11,930
actual IP of bing dot com returned a spoofed IP.

17
00:01:11,930 --> 00:01:15,200
And this IP is being set by us, which is the hacker.

18
00:01:15,410 --> 00:01:20,150
Now, let me show you just a quick example of how this can be used.

19
00:01:20,630 --> 00:01:29,030
So if I just go to my file manager and go to the location where my web server files are stored, and

20
00:01:29,030 --> 00:01:36,240
this is enviar w w w h html you will see a file called Indexed HTML.

21
00:01:36,290 --> 00:01:40,430
This is the file that gets loaded by default in web servers.

22
00:01:40,700 --> 00:01:42,980
So I'm just going to modify this very quickly.

23
00:01:42,980 --> 00:01:44,750
I'm not really doing a proper attack.

24
00:01:44,750 --> 00:01:48,490
I just want to give you a good idea of what this is.

25
00:01:48,500 --> 00:01:52,760
So I'm just going to open this file and leave pad.

26
00:01:54,830 --> 00:01:56,810
And I'm going to delete everything here.

27
00:01:57,260 --> 00:02:00,280
I'm just going to say, ha ha ha ha.

28
00:02:00,290 --> 00:02:01,700
Nothing really important.

29
00:02:01,700 --> 00:02:04,310
But this is what's going to load in my website.

30
00:02:04,700 --> 00:02:09,090
I'm going to quit this and run my web server that comes with Kali.

31
00:02:09,110 --> 00:02:14,330
So all you have to do is just service Apache to start.

32
00:02:15,820 --> 00:02:18,610
And now if I go to my web browser.

33
00:02:19,890 --> 00:02:22,110
And try to go to BBC.com.

34
00:02:25,510 --> 00:02:31,750
You'll see that instead of getting big dot com, we will actually get a website that is configured by

35
00:02:31,750 --> 00:02:32,470
the hacker.

36
00:02:32,470 --> 00:02:36,190
And you can see on the top it still says big dot com.

37
00:02:36,880 --> 00:02:43,160
So this opens a wide array of attacks that we can do after becoming the man in the middle.

38
00:02:43,180 --> 00:02:47,670
You can give your target fake login pages when they try to log into a website.

39
00:02:47,680 --> 00:02:52,980
You can give them fake websites and ask them to load or to execute some kind of code.

40
00:02:52,990 --> 00:02:55,570
You can give them even fake updates.

41
00:02:55,570 --> 00:03:00,820
And I cover a lot of that in my other courses, such as the social engineering and the General Ethical

42
00:03:00,820 --> 00:03:01,810
Hacking Course.

43
00:03:01,900 --> 00:03:07,090
But this is kind of drifting away from programming, so I won't be covering that in this lecture.

44
00:03:07,630 --> 00:03:13,900
What I also want to show you is how to run this against a remote Windows computer.

45
00:03:14,530 --> 00:03:16,150
So I'm going to close this.

46
00:03:16,390 --> 00:03:23,230
And the only difference is we'll need to first flush our IP tables, so remove the IP tables that we

47
00:03:23,230 --> 00:03:23,760
have.

48
00:03:23,770 --> 00:03:26,890
And to do that, we'll do IP tables flush.

49
00:03:27,100 --> 00:03:34,750
Again, I covered this before and we'll use an IP table rule that'll redirect packets coming from other

50
00:03:34,750 --> 00:03:39,640
computers into the queue that is being used in this program.

51
00:03:39,940 --> 00:03:45,220
Again, I covered this before and I showed the exact command, so I'm just going to run it quickly here.

52
00:03:45,910 --> 00:03:50,620
And it's literally, instead of the input in here, we're going to say forward.

53
00:03:51,100 --> 00:03:56,830
So forward is the name of the chain that packets go through when they're coming from other computers,

54
00:03:56,830 --> 00:03:59,140
when we are the man in the middle.

55
00:03:59,530 --> 00:04:00,720
So I'm going to hit enter.

56
00:04:00,730 --> 00:04:04,360
This runs with no errors, which means it got executed properly.

57
00:04:04,480 --> 00:04:08,110
And the last thing that you want to do is become the man in the middle.

58
00:04:08,110 --> 00:04:12,940
And we'll do that using our ARP spoof program that we wrote before.

59
00:04:13,390 --> 00:04:21,490
Now, I'm already in the directory of this program so all I have to do is python erp spoof dot py.

60
00:04:23,010 --> 00:04:28,140
And now let's go to the Windows Machine and go to Bing dot com.

61
00:04:31,600 --> 00:04:39,340
And as you can see, instead of getting the actual page four being dot com, we got redirected to the

62
00:04:39,340 --> 00:04:47,260
web server that is running on the machine and we see the content that we put in the indexed HTML.

63
00:04:47,830 --> 00:04:54,970
Now obviously this is a very simple content, but the attack that we were building so far is working

64
00:04:54,970 --> 00:05:01,810
perfectly and we can redirect any request to any domain to any other server.

65
00:05:02,050 --> 00:05:09,310
So this can be used to serve fake web pages, fake login pages, fake downloads, fake updates, and

66
00:05:09,310 --> 00:05:11,830
so many other cool attacks.

67
00:05:12,490 --> 00:05:17,980
If you want to learn more about what you could do with DNS spoofing, I highly suggest you have a look

68
00:05:17,980 --> 00:05:25,480
on my other courses as I use that to run a large number of cool attacks to gain full control over computer

69
00:05:25,480 --> 00:05:26,230
systems.

70
00:05:27,010 --> 00:05:32,530
As you know, this is a programming course, so we're interested in the programming aspect.

71
00:05:32,530 --> 00:05:37,570
And to run these attacks that I'm talking about, you don't really need to program anything you'll be

72
00:05:37,570 --> 00:05:40,390
using and already existing programs.

73
00:05:40,390 --> 00:05:44,230
Therefore, I will not be included in them in this course.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
