# Section 8: Writing a File Interceptor

1
00:00:00,980 --> 00:00:08,810
Now in the previous section, we wrote a program that can modify packets as they flow through our computer,

2
00:00:08,810 --> 00:00:16,510
allowing us to spoof DNS requests and redirect any requests to a new website to another destination.

3
00:00:16,520 --> 00:00:19,580
And we spoke about how dangerous that is.

4
00:00:19,850 --> 00:00:26,900
In this section, I want to build yet another program that can be used to modify data as it flows through

5
00:00:26,900 --> 00:00:28,040
our computer.

6
00:00:28,040 --> 00:00:32,780
But previously we were modifying data in the DNS layer.

7
00:00:32,810 --> 00:00:39,170
Now in this section we're going to be modifying data in the HTTP layer.

8
00:00:39,170 --> 00:00:45,680
So the HTTP layer is the layer that data gets sent through when you browse websites.

9
00:00:45,680 --> 00:00:49,250
So you see the HTTP dot to forward slashes.

10
00:00:49,250 --> 00:00:55,850
So pretty much everything that you see through your browser is loaded over HTTP.

11
00:00:56,060 --> 00:01:02,900
So we're going to have an example of modifying HTTP data and by the end of the section you're going

12
00:01:02,900 --> 00:01:08,300
to be able to adopt this to modify anything that sent over HTTP.

13
00:01:10,150 --> 00:01:14,080
Then this example in this program, we're going to be modifying downloads.

14
00:01:14,080 --> 00:01:19,810
So when our target tries to download something from the Internet, whether they're downloading a program

15
00:01:19,810 --> 00:01:27,580
or a PDF, for example, we're going to intercept that download and replace that download with a completely

16
00:01:27,580 --> 00:01:28,570
different file.

17
00:01:29,690 --> 00:01:35,990
Now, this is very, very dangerous because we can intercept downloads that the target person is downloading.

18
00:01:35,990 --> 00:01:41,960
And whenever they want to download the program, for example, instead of them getting a program, they

19
00:01:41,960 --> 00:01:49,280
will actually get a back door or a credential harvester or a virus or any other file that you want.

20
00:01:49,940 --> 00:01:54,260
So as usual, let me show you a teaser of this program.

21
00:01:54,440 --> 00:01:57,090
Don't worry about how this program works for now.

22
00:01:57,110 --> 00:01:59,150
We're going to discuss this in details.

23
00:01:59,180 --> 00:02:02,770
For now, just enjoy and see how this is going to work.

24
00:02:02,780 --> 00:02:08,330
And in the next lecture, we're going to write this program using Python and we'll cover how it works

25
00:02:08,330 --> 00:02:09,200
in details.

26
00:02:10,390 --> 00:02:16,210
So right here I have the Replace Downloads program, which is the file interceptor that we're going

27
00:02:16,210 --> 00:02:23,380
to build in this section, running alongside my ARP spoofing program that we built before.

28
00:02:24,230 --> 00:02:31,430
So let's go to the target windows machine and I'm going to go to AVG dot com, which is a website for

29
00:02:31,430 --> 00:02:34,460
the famous antivirus program Avg.

30
00:02:34,790 --> 00:02:41,180
So if I click on the free download to download this antivirus to protect my computer, you will see.

31
00:02:41,180 --> 00:02:49,050
Instead of getting the actual installer for AVG, we are getting a file called Evil dot x.

32
00:02:49,220 --> 00:02:49,490
E.

33
00:02:49,700 --> 00:02:57,020
Now this is happening because we are running the file interceptor and our ARP spoofing programs at the

34
00:02:57,350 --> 00:02:58,070
machine.

35
00:02:58,910 --> 00:03:05,930
So the ARP spoofing program is putting us in the middle of the connection and the file interceptor is

36
00:03:05,930 --> 00:03:12,770
replacing the file that the target person should get with a file that the hacker picked, which is in

37
00:03:12,770 --> 00:03:15,890
this case the evil dot exe file.

38
00:03:16,640 --> 00:03:21,550
So if we click on save now this will actually download it for me.

39
00:03:21,560 --> 00:03:25,640
So if I go to my downloads, you will see it right here.

40
00:03:26,210 --> 00:03:27,980
Now, this file can be anything.

41
00:03:27,980 --> 00:03:32,000
It can be a backdoor, a virus or anything that you pick.

42
00:03:32,810 --> 00:03:38,780
And we'll learn how to program our own backdoors, keyloggers and our own malware later on.

43
00:03:39,290 --> 00:03:44,870
Not only that, but you can also see that this file still looks suspicious, so it doesn't really have

44
00:03:44,870 --> 00:03:45,590
an icon.

45
00:03:45,620 --> 00:03:47,780
It's called evil, dirty, sexy.

46
00:03:47,810 --> 00:03:50,330
But don't worry about this again.

47
00:03:50,330 --> 00:03:56,090
Later on, I'm going to show you how to convert our malware to files that look like any other file,

48
00:03:56,090 --> 00:03:57,950
like an image or a PDF.

49
00:03:58,370 --> 00:04:05,030
So for now, for this section, our goal is going to be trying to build a program that will replace

50
00:04:05,030 --> 00:04:12,050
downloads requested by any computer on the same network as us with any other file that we want.

51
00:04:12,080 --> 00:04:18,260
So as we've seen in this example, the target might go to iTunes or to any other website trying to download

52
00:04:18,260 --> 00:04:25,220
a normal program, and instead of them getting the normal program or book, they will get a file that

53
00:04:25,220 --> 00:04:26,060
we choose.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,190 --> 00:00:01,640
Okay.

2
00:00:01,640 --> 00:00:09,680
So my goal now is to write a program that can detect when a user requests to download a certain file.

3
00:00:10,040 --> 00:00:14,940
And when we detect that request, we will serve a different file.

4
00:00:14,960 --> 00:00:19,700
This different file can be a backdoor or a keylogger or any other file that you want.

5
00:00:20,090 --> 00:00:28,280
Now, as a template, I'm using the DNS spoof code that we wrote in the previous section, didn't modify

6
00:00:28,280 --> 00:00:29,120
anything at all.

7
00:00:29,120 --> 00:00:29,780
And here.

8
00:00:30,050 --> 00:00:35,390
And what I'm going to do right now, I'm going to delete all of this because all of this will actually

9
00:00:35,390 --> 00:00:39,710
work to spoof a certain website to a different location.

10
00:00:39,710 --> 00:00:41,720
So that's not what we want to do.

11
00:00:42,290 --> 00:00:46,970
I'm going to keep the first line which will convert the packet to escape packet because we're going

12
00:00:46,970 --> 00:00:49,220
to need to obviously modify this packet.

13
00:00:49,640 --> 00:00:57,020
And then the next line here, you can see that this line checks if the current packet has a DNS response

14
00:00:57,020 --> 00:00:57,650
layer.

15
00:00:58,190 --> 00:01:04,970
Now when someone downloads a file through the browser, the request and the response is sent through

16
00:01:04,970 --> 00:01:08,540
the HTTP layer, not through the DNS layer.

17
00:01:09,230 --> 00:01:16,400
So the check in here, we need to check if the current packet contains a HTTP layer.

18
00:01:16,850 --> 00:01:21,170
Now you can use this KB HTTP library that I showed you before.

19
00:01:21,740 --> 00:01:29,210
This library works great when you want to analyze and print data on screen, but when it comes to modifying

20
00:01:29,210 --> 00:01:33,500
the data, it will actually make our code much more complex.

21
00:01:33,740 --> 00:01:40,910
Therefore, in this lecture, I'm going to show you how to manually filter packets that contain a HTTP

22
00:01:40,910 --> 00:01:48,650
layer and categorize these packets as requests and responses so that we can further analyze these packets

23
00:01:48,650 --> 00:01:53,840
and then use this information to modify the packets and achieve our goal.

24
00:01:54,320 --> 00:01:59,720
So the first thing that I want to do in this check right here, and instead of checking for a DNS response,

25
00:01:59,720 --> 00:02:04,220
I want to check if this packet contains a HTTP layer.

26
00:02:04,220 --> 00:02:11,810
I'm still not sure if it's a HTTP response or request, but if a packet contains a HTTP layer with useful

27
00:02:11,810 --> 00:02:15,380
data, it will contain that in the raw layer.

28
00:02:15,890 --> 00:02:23,210
So basically the way that KP works, it has the main layers, which are the IP, the TCP, the UDP.

29
00:02:23,210 --> 00:02:31,370
And then finally at the end it appends the raw data such as the HTTP data in the raw layer.

30
00:02:32,090 --> 00:02:38,210
So to check if a packet contains this layer, all we have to do is just type raw instead of the DNS

31
00:02:38,210 --> 00:02:45,860
or R, and then we can print our packets so we can print KP, dot sho.

32
00:02:46,700 --> 00:02:53,420
And what will this do right now is print any packet that contains a raw layer, and this will print

33
00:02:53,420 --> 00:02:57,290
all the packets that contain useful HTTP data.

34
00:02:57,290 --> 00:03:04,400
Because like I said, it's KP data sent in the HTTP layer are placed in the raw layer.

35
00:03:05,030 --> 00:03:12,620
So let's go ahead and run Python, run our program and let's just go to Bill.com.

36
00:03:12,770 --> 00:03:20,000
Now, I've actually run IP tables in here to redirect the requests and the responses to my program.

37
00:03:20,000 --> 00:03:25,730
I showed how to do this before, so I'm not going to do it and I'm just going to go to Big Dotcom in

38
00:03:25,730 --> 00:03:26,480
here.

39
00:03:28,010 --> 00:03:35,680
And as you can see, it's printing the packets that contain HTTP layer with the useful data.

40
00:03:35,690 --> 00:03:39,530
And as you can see, the useful data is actually in the raw layer.

41
00:03:39,710 --> 00:03:42,080
Now in this packet, it's actually not so useful.

42
00:03:42,080 --> 00:03:43,160
It's compressed.

43
00:03:43,190 --> 00:03:44,840
We'll talk about that later.

44
00:03:44,930 --> 00:03:47,390
But let's go all the way to the top.

45
00:03:48,170 --> 00:03:52,940
And as you can see right here, we have a packet so you can see the layers in here.

46
00:03:52,940 --> 00:03:56,150
It has an IP layer, it has a TCP layer.

47
00:03:56,330 --> 00:04:02,960
And then in the raw layer, again, we have the useful data which we can read in plain text.

48
00:04:03,530 --> 00:04:04,670
Now this is great.

49
00:04:04,670 --> 00:04:11,210
Now we're able to filter only packets that contain useful HTTP data.

50
00:04:11,480 --> 00:04:17,690
Now I want to take this one step further, because right now we're not sure which packets are requests

51
00:04:17,690 --> 00:04:19,880
and which packets are responses.

52
00:04:19,880 --> 00:04:26,300
So I want to be able to differentiate between the responses and the requests so that I can understand

53
00:04:26,300 --> 00:04:31,970
what's happening when we click on a link or when we download the file, and then from there we'll be

54
00:04:31,970 --> 00:04:34,010
able to build our attack.

55
00:04:34,520 --> 00:04:40,670
So we know that this is a request because we can see that this is a get request to big dot com.

56
00:04:41,120 --> 00:04:50,210
And if you look at the TCP layer and look at the de port field, you'll see that it says this is HTTP.

57
00:04:50,810 --> 00:04:58,910
Now the port stands for destination port and this means that the destination port of this packet is

58
00:04:58,910 --> 00:05:00,620
a HTTP port.

59
00:05:00,830 --> 00:05:03,050
Now, if you scroll down a little bit.

60
00:05:04,350 --> 00:05:08,490
You'll see that we have the response for the above requests.

61
00:05:08,490 --> 00:05:10,230
So there are other requests here.

62
00:05:10,410 --> 00:05:11,310
You'll see.

63
00:05:11,310 --> 00:05:16,890
Notice the response is also sent in the load in the load field of the row.

64
00:05:17,460 --> 00:05:21,810
And I note the response because this is a HTTP response.

65
00:05:22,350 --> 00:05:31,290
And what also makes me know 100% that this is a response is if I look at the export, so the export

66
00:05:31,290 --> 00:05:37,650
stands for the source port and this time the export is set to HTTP.

67
00:05:37,650 --> 00:05:43,470
So the source port of this current packet is a HTTP port.

68
00:05:43,860 --> 00:05:52,560
So using this the export and the port fields of the TCP layer, we can determine whether this is a response

69
00:05:52,560 --> 00:05:57,660
or a request if the port is set to HTTP like this one.

70
00:05:57,660 --> 00:06:02,100
And here this means that the destination is a port.

71
00:06:02,100 --> 00:06:04,500
So this packet is a request.

72
00:06:04,500 --> 00:06:07,950
It's leaving us, it's going to a HTTP port.

73
00:06:08,490 --> 00:06:18,660
But if the port is set to http, that means that the packet left from a HTTP port coming to us, which

74
00:06:18,660 --> 00:06:20,520
means that this is a response.

75
00:06:20,940 --> 00:06:28,290
So we can use a simple if statement in our code in here and say if KP packet.

76
00:06:29,520 --> 00:06:34,160
And notice that the export and the deposit are in the TCP layer.

77
00:06:34,260 --> 00:06:43,290
So we need to first access this layer by doing KP, dot, TCP and the field name that we're looking

78
00:06:43,290 --> 00:06:44,940
for is the port.

79
00:06:45,510 --> 00:06:51,150
And we're going to say if this field is equal to 80, which is HTTP.

80
00:06:51,180 --> 00:06:55,320
So when you look in here, you'll actually see HTTP instead of 80.

81
00:06:55,320 --> 00:07:01,440
But the actual port number that is used for HTTP by default is 80.

82
00:07:01,470 --> 00:07:05,910
So in our code, we're going to be using 80 instead of HTTP.

83
00:07:06,450 --> 00:07:15,420
So if the destination port in the TCP layer is set to 80, then this means that this is a packet leaving

84
00:07:15,450 --> 00:07:21,470
our computer to a destination port of 80, which means that it is a request.

85
00:07:21,480 --> 00:07:27,120
So we're going to print this is a HTTP request.

86
00:07:27,910 --> 00:07:31,020
And then we'll print the packet show.

87
00:07:31,960 --> 00:07:35,530
Now we can add another condition by doing LF.

88
00:07:36,430 --> 00:07:40,780
And the second condition that we're going to use is we're going to check again.

89
00:07:40,780 --> 00:07:46,710
The KP packet will access the TCP layer by doing Skippy, the TCP.

90
00:07:47,500 --> 00:07:55,840
And this time we're going to check if the SW port so the source port is set to 80, then this is a packet

91
00:07:55,840 --> 00:08:05,200
leaving from a HTTP port coming back to us, which means that this is a HTTP response.

92
00:08:06,800 --> 00:08:11,090
And then I'm going to print the packet dot show and I'm done.

93
00:08:11,600 --> 00:08:13,670
So basically what this will do.

94
00:08:13,700 --> 00:08:17,450
It will separate the HTTP packets that were capturing.

95
00:08:17,720 --> 00:08:21,890
And if it's a request, it will tell us that this is a request printed.

96
00:08:21,920 --> 00:08:25,670
If it's a response, it will tell us this is a response and print it.

97
00:08:26,530 --> 00:08:33,250
So right now we built a program that is actually very useful when it comes to analyzing HTTP traffic

98
00:08:33,250 --> 00:08:36,960
will be able to separate the responses and the requests.

99
00:08:36,970 --> 00:08:44,320
And based on that, we'll be able to build any program that we want that will rely on HTTP requests

100
00:08:44,320 --> 00:08:45,490
and responses.

101
00:08:46,270 --> 00:08:52,000
Now, I could have literally wrote this program straight away, but I wanted to start from the basic

102
00:08:52,000 --> 00:08:57,970
and print the show before we do any of that, so that you see the process of how you should analyze

103
00:08:57,970 --> 00:09:02,770
packets and use the fields to extract the useful stuff to you.

104
00:09:04,380 --> 00:09:05,710
So that's it for now.

105
00:09:05,730 --> 00:09:11,220
In the next lecture, we're going to run this program, see what happens when someone downloads a file

106
00:09:11,220 --> 00:09:17,730
and see how this program will be very useful to analyze this data and build our target program, which

107
00:09:17,730 --> 00:09:21,810
will replace the downloaded file with any other file that we want.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,270 --> 00:00:01,570
Okay.

2
00:00:01,690 --> 00:00:09,220
So now that we have a program that can filter HTTP requests and responses, let's use it to analyze

3
00:00:09,220 --> 00:00:15,970
and see what happens when a user clicks on a download link and see how we can use this information to

4
00:00:15,970 --> 00:00:21,010
hijack this download and serve the user any other file that we want.

5
00:00:21,820 --> 00:00:23,860
So I'm going to go down.

6
00:00:23,980 --> 00:00:29,260
I've already run my IP tables rules and I'm going to run my programming here.

7
00:00:29,830 --> 00:00:34,210
And before I download the link, I'm actually going to delete my history.

8
00:00:37,120 --> 00:00:39,430
And then I'm going to click on the link.

9
00:00:39,790 --> 00:00:46,330
Also notice before I click on the link, this is a HTTP website, not a HTTPS website.

10
00:00:46,810 --> 00:00:49,120
This will only work against HTTP.

11
00:00:49,150 --> 00:00:53,350
For now, we'll talk about HTTPS later on in the course.

12
00:00:54,130 --> 00:01:03,310
So I'm going to click on download and then I'm going to do control C, cancel this and stop it.

13
00:01:04,090 --> 00:01:09,380
And let's go all the way up to see what's the first request that was sent.

14
00:01:09,400 --> 00:01:14,710
So the first request should be the download request, because that was the only request that I made

15
00:01:14,710 --> 00:01:16,450
while the program was running.

16
00:01:18,300 --> 00:01:22,830
So you can see that the first request is the HTTP request.

17
00:01:23,310 --> 00:01:26,820
And if you go down, you'll see the TCP layer.

18
00:01:26,820 --> 00:01:32,610
And the layer that really interests us is the row and specifically the load.

19
00:01:33,000 --> 00:01:42,150
And you can see in here that this was a get request to download a file that is specified in here.

20
00:01:42,990 --> 00:01:44,910
Now, let me copy all of this.

21
00:01:46,390 --> 00:01:52,090
And I'm just going to put it in a text file so that we can analyze it better.

22
00:01:53,380 --> 00:01:58,500
So you can see that all the useful information is in the load, like I said.

23
00:01:58,510 --> 00:02:06,160
And basically what's happening in here, it's specifying the host, which is the domain where the file

24
00:02:06,160 --> 00:02:09,400
is going to be downloaded from, which is this one in here.

25
00:02:10,310 --> 00:02:17,540
And then it's also specifying the file or the location of the file on this domain in here.

26
00:02:18,050 --> 00:02:24,860
So basically, if you combine the two, so if you put the domain and then put this followed by it.

27
00:02:25,460 --> 00:02:30,350
This is actually the URL where WinZip is going to be downloaded from.

28
00:02:30,350 --> 00:02:35,960
So you can literally copy this, paste it in the browser and you'll be able to download it.

29
00:02:36,560 --> 00:02:45,530
So for us now to detect if the user is downloading a file that we want to hijack, we can use the value

30
00:02:45,530 --> 00:02:52,100
in here in the load field and check if the load contains anything with a dot x.

31
00:02:53,060 --> 00:02:59,450
So I'm going to go back to my code and I'm going to go to where I'm detecting the request and I'm going

32
00:02:59,450 --> 00:03:01,220
to add another if statement.

33
00:03:01,730 --> 00:03:11,600
And in this if statement, I'm going to say if dot x is in my skip packet and access the load by doing

34
00:03:11,640 --> 00:03:13,940
Skippy dot raw first.

35
00:03:14,510 --> 00:03:18,410
So we're accessing the raw layer and then we're accessing the load.

36
00:03:19,190 --> 00:03:28,460
So basically what we're saying is if X is in the load field of the raw layer, which is basically in

37
00:03:28,460 --> 00:03:37,820
here, if you see a dot x, then that means I want to hijack this, then I want to run my code in this

38
00:03:37,820 --> 00:03:38,630
F statement.

39
00:03:38,630 --> 00:03:41,660
BLOCK So, first of all, we're going to print.

40
00:03:43,320 --> 00:03:52,020
X e request, so someone requested an x and then after it we can do a packet dot show to get more information

41
00:03:52,020 --> 00:03:57,000
or we can put our code that will actually replace the file.

42
00:03:57,090 --> 00:04:03,330
So basically using the x e as a keyword to trigger our code to hijack the files.

43
00:04:03,450 --> 00:04:10,410
So if you're targeting if you want to replace PDFs, then you change the X here to a PDF.

44
00:04:10,410 --> 00:04:14,280
If you want to replace images, you'd use the image extension.

45
00:04:14,280 --> 00:04:18,780
If you want to replace any other file, just use the file extension in here.

46
00:04:19,170 --> 00:04:23,640
And you can also trigger this code based on anything else that you see in the load.

47
00:04:23,640 --> 00:04:28,560
So it all depends on how you're going to analyze the packets and depends on your goal.

48
00:04:29,130 --> 00:04:30,180
So this is perfect.

49
00:04:30,180 --> 00:04:38,460
Now we're able to detect whenever the user tries to download an X, we can execute any code we want.

50
00:04:38,730 --> 00:04:46,020
So right now you might think all we have to do is replace the host in here to any host that we want

51
00:04:46,020 --> 00:04:53,010
and then replace the URL in here to any URL that we want and then we'll be able to replace the file.

52
00:04:54,150 --> 00:04:57,180
Unfortunately, it's not as simple as this.

53
00:04:57,540 --> 00:04:59,100
You can do this.

54
00:04:59,100 --> 00:05:07,150
But before you can modify requests or send requests, you're going to have to manually initiate a TCP

55
00:05:07,150 --> 00:05:08,520
IP handshake.

56
00:05:08,910 --> 00:05:10,980
So you're going to have to do that first.

57
00:05:10,980 --> 00:05:15,750
And then you can serve the target other files that you want.

58
00:05:16,570 --> 00:05:22,480
There is a simpler way, though, now that we are able to catch the requests and the responses.

59
00:05:22,480 --> 00:05:28,030
And as you can see, we're catching the request for the x, e or for the target file.

60
00:05:28,420 --> 00:05:34,030
We can wait for the response and modify this response this way.

61
00:05:34,030 --> 00:05:41,770
The handshake has already been established and therefore we can modify it without creating or establishing

62
00:05:41,770 --> 00:05:43,060
a new handshake.

63
00:05:43,420 --> 00:05:48,970
And the next lecture, I'll show you how we're going to modify the response and successfully replace

64
00:05:48,970 --> 00:05:54,190
downloads downloaded from any computer that is connected to our network.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:00,630
Okay.

2
00:00:00,630 --> 00:00:07,050
So so far, our program is able to intercept downloads of a specific file type.

3
00:00:07,500 --> 00:00:13,890
What we're doing so far is we're printing the download request and the response on screen.

4
00:00:14,370 --> 00:00:20,700
Now, in this lecture, I want to show you how we're going to successfully modify the response so that

5
00:00:20,700 --> 00:00:27,360
when the target requests to download a specific file type, we will serve them a completely different

6
00:00:27,360 --> 00:00:28,020
file.

7
00:00:28,500 --> 00:00:34,710
So this can be a Trojan, a backdoor, a keylogger or anything that you want.

8
00:00:35,310 --> 00:00:42,780
So going back open here, we can see that we have the request in here that requested the exe and then

9
00:00:42,780 --> 00:00:47,880
you can see that the next HTTP packet was a HTTP response.

10
00:00:48,540 --> 00:00:52,380
And let's copy this response actually before we do anything.

11
00:00:52,380 --> 00:00:54,150
So I'm just going to copy all of it.

12
00:00:57,340 --> 00:01:02,590
And you can see in this response it's saying in the load, which is the part that's useful for us as

13
00:01:02,590 --> 00:01:03,280
usual.

14
00:01:03,460 --> 00:01:10,790
It's saying it's given at 200 OC, which is a code that means basically I can serve you the request.

15
00:01:10,810 --> 00:01:17,770
So the request was the x, e and here the download the winzip x and basically the server is saying,

16
00:01:17,770 --> 00:01:19,630
okay, I can serve you this.

17
00:01:19,660 --> 00:01:27,820
You'll see some HTTP headers after that and then you'll see you'll just have literally hex code in here,

18
00:01:27,820 --> 00:01:30,250
which is the content of the X.

19
00:01:30,430 --> 00:01:36,070
This is literally the content of the WinZip file that's going to be downloaded to the target.

20
00:01:37,030 --> 00:01:44,470
So if you pay close attention in here on the response, you'll see that there is nothing in here that

21
00:01:44,470 --> 00:01:52,270
tells us that this is a response that contains WinZip to take C or it's a response for an x e file.

22
00:01:52,540 --> 00:01:58,510
So how can we know that this response is made for this request right here?

23
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:05,380
Because as we know, there are lots and lots of requests and responses sent on the network.

24
00:02:05,380 --> 00:02:11,230
So sometimes maybe you click on one link, but there is a lot of requests and responses are being sent

25
00:02:11,230 --> 00:02:13,960
in the background to serve you that link.

26
00:02:14,590 --> 00:02:21,730
So we need a way to know that this response right here corresponds to this request.

27
00:02:22,480 --> 00:02:29,590
Now, in order to do this, you're going to need to pay close attention to all the fields in here and

28
00:02:29,590 --> 00:02:30,340
in here.

29
00:02:30,970 --> 00:02:36,670
You can pause the lecture right now and try to determine it yourself, and it will be a nice challenge

30
00:02:36,670 --> 00:02:37,390
for you.

31
00:02:38,170 --> 00:02:43,990
Now, if you look at the action here in the request, so I'm going to put a few stars beside it.

32
00:02:44,980 --> 00:02:48,370
And if you look at the sequence in here.

33
00:02:50,030 --> 00:02:57,380
You'll see that these are identical and this is how packets are associated with each other.

34
00:02:57,620 --> 00:03:06,380
So when this request was sent, it has this ACC number, and then when the server responds and the response

35
00:03:06,380 --> 00:03:13,250
in the TCP layer, it's going to create a field called sequence and it will put the same number that

36
00:03:13,250 --> 00:03:17,030
it received in the ACC as the sequence number.

37
00:03:17,750 --> 00:03:23,420
So this is how the system knows that this response corresponds to this request.

38
00:03:23,450 --> 00:03:28,070
It checks if the sequence corresponds to the ACC in here.

39
00:03:29,060 --> 00:03:36,830
So we can use this same mechanism to check if the response corresponds to this request.

40
00:03:37,430 --> 00:03:45,560
So I'm going to go back to my code and I'm going to create a list in here and I'm going to call this

41
00:03:45,710 --> 00:03:50,300
ACC list, and I'm going to keep this list empty.

42
00:03:50,300 --> 00:03:53,000
So I'm just going to put two square brackets.

43
00:03:53,450 --> 00:03:59,030
Now you'll understand why I'm using this list in a minute, but let me go through it and it'll become

44
00:03:59,030 --> 00:03:59,840
more clear.

45
00:04:00,350 --> 00:04:07,700
So the reason why I'm creating the list here and not inside my function in here, because as you know,

46
00:04:07,700 --> 00:04:14,600
this function, the process packet function gets called every time we snip a packet.

47
00:04:15,050 --> 00:04:21,620
So if you declare a variable like this one in here or initialize it, this variable will be created

48
00:04:21,620 --> 00:04:28,320
and initialized every time this function gets executed, which means every time we sniff a packet.

49
00:04:28,340 --> 00:04:35,810
But if you create it in here, this will only be created and initialized once and we'll be able to use

50
00:04:35,810 --> 00:04:37,160
it throughout the program.

51
00:04:37,430 --> 00:04:45,800
So I'm creating an empty list in here and my code already detects whenever I have a HTTP request and

52
00:04:45,800 --> 00:04:52,700
it also checks if the request is requesting a dot x a file that has a dot x extension.

53
00:04:53,420 --> 00:05:01,910
So once I detect that I'm going to print, I have an XY request and then I'm going to append to my list

54
00:05:01,910 --> 00:05:07,820
so you can add elements to lists by putting the list name followed by dot append.

55
00:05:08,240 --> 00:05:12,350
So this is a method that allows us to add elements to a list.

56
00:05:12,440 --> 00:05:18,920
The list name is a list and all we have to do now is put the element that we want to add to the list

57
00:05:18,920 --> 00:05:25,520
between the brackets and the element that we want to add in here is the arc of this packet, because

58
00:05:25,520 --> 00:05:26,600
this is a request.

59
00:05:27,140 --> 00:05:30,380
So we basically want to add this right here.

60
00:05:31,100 --> 00:05:36,350
So to add that, we have to access the TCP layer and access this field.

61
00:05:37,910 --> 00:05:45,680
So we're going to put the packet name, which is CP packet, followed by the layer name, which is Skippy

62
00:05:45,980 --> 00:05:46,880
TCP.

63
00:05:47,120 --> 00:05:49,570
And the field name is ACH.

64
00:05:50,490 --> 00:05:59,100
So now every time there is a request that contains dot x, the act of this request is going to be stored

65
00:05:59,100 --> 00:06:02,280
in my list because we are using the append method.

66
00:06:03,060 --> 00:06:08,280
Then in the next if statement that we already have that checks for responses.

67
00:06:08,340 --> 00:06:13,440
What we're going to do, we're going to use another if statement.

68
00:06:14,310 --> 00:06:19,650
And in this if statement, we're going to check if the sequence.

69
00:06:19,650 --> 00:06:25,560
So we're going to check if this field right here is contained in my list.

70
00:06:26,010 --> 00:06:29,460
Again, note the sequence here is the same as the IHC here.

71
00:06:29,580 --> 00:06:36,930
So if the sequence here is contained in my list, then this means that this is a response to a request

72
00:06:36,930 --> 00:06:42,990
that I'm interested in, to a request that was captured in this if statement in here.

73
00:06:43,680 --> 00:06:49,770
So again, we're going to have to access the escape packet, the TCP layer of this.

74
00:06:49,770 --> 00:06:52,860
So skip the TCP.

75
00:06:53,130 --> 00:07:02,280
And this time we're looking for the sequence and we're going to say, if this is in my list, then I'm

76
00:07:02,280 --> 00:07:06,270
going to print this statement saying replacing file.

77
00:07:07,500 --> 00:07:14,070
And then we can put any code that we want in here that we'll use to replace the download.

78
00:07:14,580 --> 00:07:22,260
So now I don't need this print statement right here and I don't need this one in here either.

79
00:07:23,330 --> 00:07:24,400
And we're done.

80
00:07:24,410 --> 00:07:31,760
So all we're doing is whenever we detect a request that that's interesting to us, we're going to store

81
00:07:31,790 --> 00:07:36,290
it's a field and a list that's called a list.

82
00:07:37,160 --> 00:07:44,750
And then in our response method or in our response block code, we're going to check if the sequence

83
00:07:44,750 --> 00:07:48,290
of the current response is in my list.

84
00:07:48,990 --> 00:07:54,330
If it is, then we're going to replace it and we're going to put all the code that we're going to use

85
00:07:54,330 --> 00:07:56,280
to replace the file in here.

86
00:07:57,060 --> 00:08:04,290
Now, you should also and here, since we have detected that the sequence is in the act list and we're

87
00:08:04,290 --> 00:08:05,700
going to replace it in here.

88
00:08:05,700 --> 00:08:10,680
So we should actually remove this sequence from our list because we're not going to use it anymore.

89
00:08:11,070 --> 00:08:16,810
So in order to remove an element from the list, we use the append to add to the list.

90
00:08:16,830 --> 00:08:22,140
If we want to remove something from the list again, we're just going to put the list name followed

91
00:08:22,140 --> 00:08:23,440
by remove.

92
00:08:23,460 --> 00:08:25,890
So that's the method that we can use to remove.

93
00:08:26,010 --> 00:08:29,300
And then we're going to put the element that we want to remove.

94
00:08:29,310 --> 00:08:35,400
And in my case, it is exactly as this one, which is the one that we're checking for.

95
00:08:36,900 --> 00:08:43,380
So two new methods that we're actually using in today's code, we're using the list name followed by

96
00:08:43,410 --> 00:08:46,590
Dot append to add an element to the list.

97
00:08:46,770 --> 00:08:53,920
And then we're using the list name to remove to remove an element from the list and that's it.

98
00:08:53,940 --> 00:09:01,620
Our code now can detect if the target is downloading a certain file, and then we can also capture the

99
00:09:01,620 --> 00:09:05,670
response that corresponds to this request.

100
00:09:06,210 --> 00:09:08,610
So now let's go and test this code.

101
00:09:09,090 --> 00:09:11,310
So I'm going to clear all of this.

102
00:09:12,120 --> 00:09:14,040
And run my program.

103
00:09:14,280 --> 00:09:17,160
And I'm also going to remove my history.

104
00:09:20,580 --> 00:09:26,530
And you'll see now, if I refresh the page, you won't really see anything not useful to us.

105
00:09:26,550 --> 00:09:31,560
We're only going to be displaying if a user is downloading something, we're going to display a request

106
00:09:31,560 --> 00:09:36,960
and whenever the response is sent to the user will see the DOT show of the response.

107
00:09:37,500 --> 00:09:40,800
So let's click on download and that's perfect.

108
00:09:40,830 --> 00:09:42,450
Now Control seen here.

109
00:09:42,540 --> 00:09:45,300
Cancel this and stop.

110
00:09:45,900 --> 00:09:52,410
And if I scroll up now, you'll see we don't have too much data like we used to get before.

111
00:09:52,680 --> 00:09:56,770
You can see that it's telling us that there was an X request.

112
00:09:56,790 --> 00:10:04,020
We're seeing the show of the request and then we can see that it's still replacing the file and we can

113
00:10:04,020 --> 00:10:06,900
see the result of this response.

114
00:10:07,620 --> 00:10:14,610
Now, in the next lecture, we're going to analyze this response and see how we can modify it so that

115
00:10:14,610 --> 00:10:18,720
we can replace this file with any other file that we want.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,050 --> 00:00:01,490
Okay.

2
00:00:01,500 --> 00:00:09,360
Now our program is able to detect requests that request to download an X-ray, and it's also able to

3
00:00:09,360 --> 00:00:16,770
detect the responses that correspond to these requests and printing everything for us on screen.

4
00:00:17,130 --> 00:00:24,180
So now if I just run the program and download a file, so I just cancel this.

5
00:00:25,450 --> 00:00:34,480
You'll see that I'm able to first detect the request in here that is requesting the x e and then I'm

6
00:00:34,480 --> 00:00:41,020
getting the response, which is telling me it's replacing the file and I have the contents of the response

7
00:00:41,020 --> 00:00:41,740
packet.

8
00:00:42,700 --> 00:00:46,120
So I'm going to copy this and paste it in here.

9
00:00:47,090 --> 00:00:50,000
And I'm going to maximize this so we can focus on it.

10
00:00:50,660 --> 00:00:58,940
And our goal now is to modify this response so that instead of serving the file that the user is getting,

11
00:00:58,940 --> 00:01:02,180
we're going to serve them any other file that we want.

12
00:01:02,840 --> 00:01:10,280
Now, as we've seen and spoke about in the previous lectures, we said the part where the HTTP data

13
00:01:10,280 --> 00:01:16,600
is stored or is transmitted is in the load and this is the response.

14
00:01:16,610 --> 00:01:20,930
And in the load we can see that this is a 200 OC.

15
00:01:21,890 --> 00:01:27,650
So the 200 K in here is actually a HTTP status code.

16
00:01:27,710 --> 00:01:32,630
So there is a number of these codes that can be returned as a response.

17
00:01:32,780 --> 00:01:40,160
And in order to manipulate this packet, we need to learn a little bit more about HTTP responses.

18
00:01:40,790 --> 00:01:47,750
So I'm going to include a link in the resources to a Wikipedia page that explain this really, really

19
00:01:47,750 --> 00:01:48,290
good.

20
00:01:48,680 --> 00:01:55,670
So this page shows the list of HTTP status codes and you can see that the code that we're getting right

21
00:01:55,670 --> 00:02:02,150
now is the 200 OC, which basically means that I can give you the thing that you requested.

22
00:02:02,150 --> 00:02:10,640
And then as you can see in here, we actually have the contents of the file in here transmitted in Hex.

23
00:02:11,390 --> 00:02:16,340
Now what we want to do, we don't want to say that everything is okay.

24
00:02:16,340 --> 00:02:22,430
We actually want to give a completely different file than the file than that the target requested.

25
00:02:22,970 --> 00:02:30,620
So if you go and spend some time looking through the status codes that we can use, if you scroll down,

26
00:02:30,950 --> 00:02:34,790
you'll see we have the 300 or the 3xx codes.

27
00:02:35,240 --> 00:02:42,470
They correspond to redirection codes, so they allow us to redirect a request to somewhere else.

28
00:02:42,470 --> 00:02:49,880
So if someone if the client requests something from a certain website, we can use a 300 status code

29
00:02:49,880 --> 00:02:52,910
to redirect this client to somewhere else.

30
00:02:53,420 --> 00:02:59,420
And the one that we want to use is the 301 moved permanently status code.

31
00:02:59,900 --> 00:03:02,720
So you can see the description of the status code.

32
00:03:03,140 --> 00:03:08,600
And if you actually click on it, it will take you to its page which will give you more information,

33
00:03:08,600 --> 00:03:12,620
plus an example so it doesn't get any simpler than this.

34
00:03:13,310 --> 00:03:17,870
So you can see in this response we're going to use 301 instead of 200.

35
00:03:18,170 --> 00:03:21,320
The name of the status code is moved permanently.

36
00:03:21,950 --> 00:03:28,400
Then we're going to specify the location which contains the file or the link that we want to redirect

37
00:03:28,400 --> 00:03:29,450
the client to.

38
00:03:30,550 --> 00:03:32,800
So I'm going to copy all of this.

39
00:03:35,300 --> 00:03:36,950
Go to my program.

40
00:03:37,310 --> 00:03:43,280
And basically all I have to do is change the load to the code that I just copied.

41
00:03:43,790 --> 00:03:50,480
So I'm going to go to my program and I'm going to do this in here because this is where we're capturing

42
00:03:50,480 --> 00:03:52,730
the packet and this is where we're printing it.

43
00:03:52,910 --> 00:03:56,930
So I'm actually going to remove the print statement because we don't need that anymore.

44
00:03:58,510 --> 00:04:00,190
I'm going to remove this one as well.

45
00:04:01,150 --> 00:04:05,970
And in here, we're going to have to first access the raw layer of the packet.

46
00:04:05,980 --> 00:04:14,830
So we're going to say Skippy packet, Skippy dot raw, and we want to modify the load of this packet

47
00:04:15,340 --> 00:04:19,570
and we're going to say that's equal to whatever I just copied.

48
00:04:20,540 --> 00:04:27,410
Now note that I can do this because the load of the RO layer is actually a string.

49
00:04:27,410 --> 00:04:33,290
So I can use all the methods that we can use with strings, or I can just make it equal to a string.

50
00:04:33,890 --> 00:04:40,850
Now keep in mind that the way that this response should be sent is a line, new line, and then the

51
00:04:40,850 --> 00:04:41,660
location.

52
00:04:42,230 --> 00:04:47,600
So in order to do this, I'm going to put this all in one line.

53
00:04:49,030 --> 00:04:55,240
And put the new line character which is backward slash NW before the location.

54
00:04:55,480 --> 00:05:01,990
So this will basically mean I have this is my first line in here and then I have the new line character

55
00:05:01,990 --> 00:05:06,700
which will basically mean I'm going to go down one line and then I have the location.

56
00:05:07,210 --> 00:05:12,160
Now in this location, you can put any URL that you want to redirect your client to.

57
00:05:12,610 --> 00:05:18,310
In our example, we want to replace the X that the client is downloading with another X.

58
00:05:19,000 --> 00:05:24,640
So again, you can use a backdoor, you can use a keylogger or credential harvester or anything that

59
00:05:24,640 --> 00:05:25,000
you want.

60
00:05:25,000 --> 00:05:25,630
Really?

61
00:05:26,290 --> 00:05:29,050
I'm going to show you something cooler in the next lecture.

62
00:05:29,350 --> 00:05:34,420
But for now, because we're still focusing on the programming, I'm just going to use a link from the

63
00:05:34,420 --> 00:05:34,930
Internet.

64
00:05:34,930 --> 00:05:41,200
So I have a link here from our lab, which is which is another program, and I'm just going to copy

65
00:05:41,200 --> 00:05:41,890
the link.

66
00:05:44,380 --> 00:05:45,970
And paste it in here.

67
00:05:47,910 --> 00:05:54,720
So basically what's going to happen is whenever our target tries to download an x ray, they will get

68
00:05:54,720 --> 00:05:56,100
this x instead.

69
00:05:56,100 --> 00:06:02,580
So if they try to download WinZip or any other x e, they will get this x e instead.

70
00:06:03,590 --> 00:06:04,790
Now, that's perfect.

71
00:06:04,820 --> 00:06:10,640
We've modified the relevant part of the package, which is the part that contain the file.

72
00:06:11,090 --> 00:06:17,390
But just like I did in the DNS spoofing lecture, we need to remove the length or the length field,

73
00:06:17,420 --> 00:06:22,970
the checksum in the IP and the checksum and the TCP layer.

74
00:06:23,510 --> 00:06:29,510
The reason for that is because we modified the packet, so the values in here should change.

75
00:06:29,750 --> 00:06:36,500
We can calculate the manually, but we can just remove them and Skippy will automatically calculate

76
00:06:36,500 --> 00:06:38,720
the values in here for us.

77
00:06:39,440 --> 00:06:46,010
So this is very similar to what I did when we were doing the DNS spoofing lecture, and you'd want to

78
00:06:46,010 --> 00:06:48,380
do this every time you modify packets.

79
00:06:48,920 --> 00:06:59,060
So we're going to do a deal Skippy packet and access the IP layer and we're removing the LAN duplicate

80
00:06:59,060 --> 00:07:04,730
this line and we're going to remove the checksum and duplicate this line.

81
00:07:04,730 --> 00:07:10,450
And this time we'll be removing the checksum from the TCP and that's it.

82
00:07:10,460 --> 00:07:11,180
We're done now.

83
00:07:11,190 --> 00:07:17,780
We removed all the fields that need to be recalculated and Skippy will automatically recalculate these

84
00:07:17,780 --> 00:07:18,530
for us.

85
00:07:19,070 --> 00:07:25,400
And finally, we've we've modified the packet and we've done all the cool stuff, but we still didn't

86
00:07:25,400 --> 00:07:29,600
modify the actual packet that will be sent to the target.

87
00:07:29,600 --> 00:07:34,970
So we're doing all of this in Skippy packet, which is literally just a variable that's used in this

88
00:07:34,970 --> 00:07:35,450
function.

89
00:07:35,450 --> 00:07:36,560
It's not sent.

90
00:07:36,860 --> 00:07:41,720
So again, this is exactly like what we did with the DNS spoof program.

91
00:07:41,720 --> 00:07:45,710
We're going to do packet dot, set payload.

92
00:07:47,250 --> 00:07:52,560
And we're going to set the payload to the string of my escape packet.

93
00:07:53,220 --> 00:07:59,100
So I actually explained this in my Das spoof lecture, but I'm going to go over it quickly.

94
00:07:59,130 --> 00:08:06,030
Basically, we are converting the packet that we're getting in here to escape packet in this line.

95
00:08:06,720 --> 00:08:09,900
We're going to analyze it, modify it the way we want.

96
00:08:09,900 --> 00:08:15,690
And finally, when we're done modifying, we're going to, first of all, convert this escape packet

97
00:08:15,690 --> 00:08:19,830
to a string because this method expects a string.

98
00:08:20,280 --> 00:08:26,670
And then we're saying packet, dot, set payload and we're passing to it the packet that we modified.

99
00:08:27,090 --> 00:08:31,770
Now I'm going to save this and let's go and test our code.

100
00:08:31,860 --> 00:08:33,990
So I'm going to run the program.

101
00:08:34,620 --> 00:08:36,570
I'm going to delete my history.

102
00:08:38,620 --> 00:08:40,240
And download the file.

103
00:08:41,800 --> 00:08:43,810
And this didn't work.

104
00:08:44,050 --> 00:08:49,330
If you look in here, we actually did get redirected to the length that we want.

105
00:08:49,420 --> 00:08:52,030
But there is an extra quotation mark.

106
00:08:52,180 --> 00:08:55,540
And to be honest, I'm not sure where is this coming from.

107
00:08:55,870 --> 00:09:03,220
So what I'm going to do, what I suspect is I suspect whenever we're modifying our field in here, whenever

108
00:09:03,220 --> 00:09:10,780
we're modifying the load and we're setting that to all of this in here, I think there is something

109
00:09:10,780 --> 00:09:15,670
in the packet itself that's been appended straight after the X.

110
00:09:16,810 --> 00:09:21,700
So what I'm going to do now, I'm actually going to put two new line characters to make sure if there

111
00:09:21,700 --> 00:09:28,030
is anything, any clutter, make sure that they'll come after my HTTP response to make sure that my

112
00:09:28,030 --> 00:09:34,020
HTTP response looks properly and it will be translated properly when it's sent.

113
00:09:34,030 --> 00:09:35,980
So I'm going to go down again.

114
00:09:37,090 --> 00:09:39,580
And control C out of this.

115
00:09:39,940 --> 00:09:41,650
Run it again.

116
00:09:42,650 --> 00:09:47,300
And then I'm going to go back and remove my history.

117
00:09:49,290 --> 00:09:52,260
And finally, I'm going to download it again.

118
00:09:53,840 --> 00:09:58,850
And as you can see right now, it's not letting me download WinZip.

119
00:09:58,880 --> 00:10:06,710
It's given me a file that's called W Ra dot x e, which is the file that we're using in this link right

120
00:10:06,710 --> 00:10:07,250
here.

121
00:10:09,030 --> 00:10:15,120
So even though we're still in Wednesday website, even though we click the link from WinZip, it's allowing

122

123
00:10:18,720 --> 00:10:24,690
So if I save this, this will get downloaded and I can access it in my downloads.

124
00:10:25,230 --> 00:10:31,760
Now I can't run this file because I'm on Linux, but this just shows that our attack works.

125
00:10:31,770 --> 00:10:35,980
So you can use this and we'll see this in the next lecture.

126

Against any computer, once you become the man in the middle and you'll be able to replace any file

127

00:00:01,520 --> 00:00:06,110
Now that we built and tested our program against our local machine.

3

computer and will replace the file that they download with something that's actually useful will replace

6

So we'll have to run ARP spoof first, get in the middle, run the IP tables rule that we use when we

8

want to redirect packets coming from remote computers, not local packets, and then run our script

9
00:00:42,320 --> 00:00:43,100
right here.

00:00:43,700 --> 00:00:48,560
So it's very simple, but it will show you how useful this program is.

Now, before I do that, because that's very simple, I want to spend a minute or two refactoring this

12

code, just making it look prettier, because I think in here they were doing a lot of stuff and that

13

can be placed in a function.

14
00:01:03,710 --> 00:01:10,160
Now I think the code will look much nicer if we have a function, for example, that's called set load.

15
00:01:11,660 --> 00:01:17,570
That I can pass this CP packet to it and pass this load right here.

16
00:01:17,570 --> 00:01:22,370
So whatever I want to set the load to, which is all of this string.

17
00:01:24,730 --> 00:01:27,460
And then it will do everything for me.

18
00:01:27,940 --> 00:01:32,260
So now I call the function, but I've actually not defined the function.

19
00:01:32,260 --> 00:01:35,560
So it's telling me that this is an unresolved reference.

20
00:01:35,830 --> 00:01:43,130
So I'm going to copy all of the code that does the modification for me, and I'm going to create a function

21
00:01:43,130 --> 00:01:50,010
in here and we'll call this set load just like we're calling it in here.

22
00:01:50,020 --> 00:01:52,420
This function will take a packet.

23
00:01:53,500 --> 00:01:54,640
And allowed.

24
00:01:56,020 --> 00:01:57,430
And in its body.

25
00:01:57,430 --> 00:02:02,680
We're going to put all of the code that we wrote before that will modify the package for me.

26
00:02:03,520 --> 00:02:09,490
So first of all, the packet in this function is actually called packet, not copy packet.

27
00:02:10,030 --> 00:02:14,500
So I need to replace all the occurrences of Skippy packet here with packet.

28
00:02:15,430 --> 00:02:19,270
So I'm just going to copy this and paste it here.

29
00:02:21,400 --> 00:02:27,630
And finally in here, we're supposed to set this to the load variable that's been passed in here.

30
00:02:27,700 --> 00:02:30,280
It's the same that what we're passing in here.

31
00:02:30,430 --> 00:02:33,130
So we're setting this to the load.

32
00:02:34,180 --> 00:02:35,980
And this function is perfect.

33
00:02:35,980 --> 00:02:39,940
Now it's going to take a packet and a string.

34
00:02:40,030 --> 00:02:46,420
It will delete the LAN, the check sums from it, and it will set the load part of the packet that it

35
00:02:46,420 --> 00:02:48,730
took to the load that's passed to it.

36
00:02:49,210 --> 00:02:52,360
And finally, we actually need to return this packet.

37
00:02:52,360 --> 00:02:54,880
So we'll do return packet.

38
00:02:56,950 --> 00:02:59,050
Then we'll capture this in here.

39
00:02:59,050 --> 00:03:01,990
So we're going to say modified.

40
00:03:02,830 --> 00:03:09,340
Packet is equal to set load for this CP packet, and this is the load that I want to use.

41
00:03:09,880 --> 00:03:16,510
And then since we're since the modified packet now is stored in this variable, we're going to use that

42
00:03:16,510 --> 00:03:18,460
in our set payload method.

43
00:03:19,360 --> 00:03:21,640
So I haven't done anything fancy right now.

44

I just created a new function just to make things look prettier.

with it.

00:03:34,290 --> 00:03:39,420

So first of all, I want to modify this link in here and I want to serve a completely different file.

00:04:27,310 --> 00:04:31,690
So I have that already stored in my webroot.

So remember I said Kelly comes with a web server and if I go to var w w w hi html.

This is the web root, the location where the files of your web server are stored.

And I created a directory called Evil Files and my evil file is called Evil dot x, so we can access

In order to be able to actually use the web server here, you need to start the web server.

That's why it worked for me.

00:05:41,760 --> 00:05:50,130
But if yours didn't work, then you need to do Service Apache to start and this will start it for you.

74
00:05:50,310 --> 00:05:59,430
Then you need to run the IP tables rule that we use to redirect data coming from remote computers.

75
00:05:59,550 --> 00:06:06,480
So first of all, we're going to do IP tables flush to remove everything from our IP tables and then

76
00:06:06,480 --> 00:06:09,750
we usually run the output and the input rule.

77
00:06:09,750 --> 00:06:16,460
So we redirect data coming on the input and the output chain when running this on a remote computer.

78
00:06:16,470 --> 00:06:20,460
As I explained before, we do this for the exception.

79
00:06:21,770 --> 00:06:24,250
And as usual, I always make this mistake.

00:06:24,260 --> 00:06:26,720

81
00:06:28,240 --> 00:06:29,830
Och, this is done.

00:06:29,860 --> 00:06:32,440
Now we need to become the man in the middle.

There is a number of ways to become the man in the middle.

I actually explain a few of them in my advanced network hacking course.

00:06:39,130 --> 00:06:44,440
In this, we're talking about becoming the man in the middle using ARP spoofing, and we built a tool

86
00:06:44,440 --> 00:06:45,840
that allows us to do that.

87
00:06:45,850 --> 00:06:52,120
So I'm just going to navigate to where my tool is stored, which is backward directory, and that's

00:06:54,490 --> 00:06:59,290
And in here I'm just going to do Python a ARP spoof dot py.

90
00:06:59,320 --> 00:07:06,070
This will put me in the middle of the connection so now I can run my DNS spoof for or run my sniffer

91
00:07:06,070 --> 00:07:10,180
or on any other tool that I want to use once I'm the man in the middle.

92
00:07:10,960 --> 00:07:17,590
In this lecture, we're testing our replace downloads, so we're going to call this tool which is replace

93
00:07:17,590 --> 00:07:20,500
downloads dot py and that's it.

94
00:07:21,910 --> 00:07:28,360
Now the report that I'm going to get from my evil file will be sent to my Gmail account.

95
00:07:29,710 --> 00:07:36,550

96
00:07:36,580 --> 00:07:40,030
Net IP version for IP forward.

97
00:07:40,810 --> 00:07:47,530
Now we do this to allow packets to flow through the Linux machine because by default this is set to

98

zero and it will prevent packets from flowing through the machine and basically will disable the internet

99
00:07:55,480 --> 00:07:56,590
connection in here.

100
00:07:56,980 --> 00:07:59,300
I'm going to remove all my browsing data.

101
00:07:59,320 --> 00:08:06,550
As usual, it's good for testing and I'm not going to test this against WinZip because WinZip uses https

102
00:08:06,550 --> 00:08:09,240
now, so this method will not work against it.

103
00:08:09,250 --> 00:08:14,720
I will teach you how to use this program with HTTPS websites, including WinZip.

104
00:08:14,740 --> 00:08:16,090
Later on in the course.

105

But as usual, it's always best to test against the simplest case and then build up on that to cover

106
00:08:22,360 --> 00:08:23,830
more complex scenarios.

107
00:08:23,830 --> 00:08:31,600
So let's test against a website that uses simple HTTP because as we know, HTTPS encrypts data, which

108
00:08:31,600 --> 00:08:34,360
makes it a little bit more challenging to deal with.

109
00:08:34,390 --> 00:08:38,230
So I'm going to test it against speed with dot com.

00:08:40,330 --> 00:08:46,660
As you can see, this website loads over normal http by default and we're simply going to click on download

111
00:08:46,660 --> 00:08:49,720
to download speed bit, which is a download accelerator.
