113
00:08:57,580 --> 00:09:04,360
This is because our code, our file interceptor has intercepted that download and replaced it with this

114
00:09:04,360 --> 00:09:05,350
evil file.

115
00:09:05,380 --> 00:09:09,730
Now I know this might look a little bit suspicious, but don't worry about that.

116
00:09:09,730 --> 00:09:16,660
I will teach you how to create Trojans later on in the course, so you'll be able to embed evil code

117
00:09:16,660 --> 00:09:23,470
and evil files within any type of file, like an image, a PDF or an actual installer as well.

118
00:09:23,770 --> 00:09:27,160
But right now we're focusing on how to replace downloads.

119
00:09:27,160 --> 00:09:30,790
And as you can see, our program is doing that successfully.

120
00:09:31,530 --> 00:09:33,460
So let's go ahead and test this.

121
00:09:33,480 --> 00:09:36,990
We're just going to click on the downloads and click on the file.

122
00:09:38,230 --> 00:09:39,760
And we're going to run it.

123
00:09:40,300 --> 00:09:42,910
And let's go to my carry machine.

124
00:09:43,390 --> 00:09:47,020
And as you can see, I got a new message in my inbox.

125
00:09:47,590 --> 00:09:55,180
And if I open this message, it's showing me a username and a password that are stored in that computer

126
00:09:55,180 --> 00:10:00,340
for an email that they that is security dot org and it's relevant password.

127
00:10:01,120 --> 00:10:04,640
So we managed to do this without sending the target anything.

128
00:10:04,660 --> 00:10:11,290
The target willingly went and try to download a program and once they did, they got this file right

129
00:10:11,290 --> 00:10:11,830
here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 9: Writing a Code Injector

1
00:00:01,060 --> 00:00:07,600
In the last section, we built a program that we can use to intercept downloads and replace any file

2
00:00:07,600 --> 00:00:13,000
downloaded by any computer connected to our network with any other file.

3
00:00:13,330 --> 00:00:19,480
Now, this was an example of modifying HTTP packets as they flow through our computer.

4
00:00:20,230 --> 00:00:26,740
Now in this section, I want to give you another example on modifying HTTP packets as they flow through

5
00:00:26,740 --> 00:00:27,880
our computer.

6
00:00:28,000 --> 00:00:34,900
But in this example, instead of creating a new response like we did in the previous section, we are

7
00:00:34,900 --> 00:00:38,890
going to modify the data sent in the raw layer.

8
00:00:39,400 --> 00:00:45,550
So by the end of the section, you're going to understand how to modify anything that gets sent in zero

9
00:00:45,550 --> 00:00:46,150
layer.

10
00:00:46,360 --> 00:00:50,500
As an example, we're going to be modifying the HTML code.

11
00:00:51,010 --> 00:00:57,880
One of the most important and useful things that can be sent through the raw layer is the HTML code

12
00:00:57,880 --> 00:01:01,630
for any web page loaded by the target client.

13
00:01:01,840 --> 00:01:09,460
So we're going to build a program that can be used to modify any part of any web page loaded by our

14
00:01:09,460 --> 00:01:10,000
target.

15
00:01:10,000 --> 00:01:17,020
And as an example, we're going to be injecting JavaScript code in the Target web page, and we'll see

16
00:01:17,020 --> 00:01:20,350
why this is dangerous and how useful this can be.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,130 --> 00:00:01,750
Okay.

2
00:00:01,760 --> 00:00:08,210
Now, if you think of it, the way that this program is going to work is similar to the way that the

3
00:00:08,210 --> 00:00:11,990
previous program, the Replace Downloads program, would work.

4
00:00:12,200 --> 00:00:16,340
So it will need to be able to see the requests and the responses.

5
00:00:16,460 --> 00:00:22,940
And instead of creating a new response like we were doing in the previous program, we're going to modify

6
00:00:22,940 --> 00:00:24,710
parts of the response.

7
00:00:24,710 --> 00:00:30,050
So we want to modify parts of the HTML code that will be sent to the target.

8
00:00:30,890 --> 00:00:38,420
Therefore, I'm starting with a new project, but the code in this new project is copied from the replace

9
00:00:38,420 --> 00:00:42,150
download script, so I haven't modified anything in here.

10
00:00:42,170 --> 00:00:48,740
The only difference is this is a new project called Code Injector and I have a new file called Code

11
00:00:48,740 --> 00:00:50,300
Injector Dot API.

12
00:00:51,050 --> 00:00:58,910
And before we actually start programming anything, I want to bring this back one step and modify the

13
00:00:58,910 --> 00:00:59,360
code.

14
00:00:59,360 --> 00:01:05,870
So it only shows me the requests and the responses so that I can see what happens when someone literally

15
00:01:05,870 --> 00:01:07,440
loads a website.

16
00:01:07,460 --> 00:01:12,290
So this is similar to what we did before, when our target was to replace a download.

17
00:01:12,320 --> 00:01:17,690
The first thing that we did is we analyzed what happens when someone clicks on a download link.

18
00:01:18,050 --> 00:01:24,440
Now we want to replace parts of the HTML code of the page that the target person loads.

19
00:01:24,440 --> 00:01:28,460
So let's just see what happens once someone loads a web page.

20
00:01:28,910 --> 00:01:35,840
So first of all, I'm going to remove this line which creates my list that we were using, and I'm going

21
00:01:35,840 --> 00:01:43,100
to remove all the code in here because that's the code that checks if there is an X and modifies the

22
00:01:43,100 --> 00:01:43,840
request.

23
00:01:43,850 --> 00:01:49,970
But right now all I want to do is print that this is a request.

24
00:01:51,450 --> 00:01:52,960
And then print the packet.

25
00:01:52,960 --> 00:01:53,790
Don't show.

26
00:01:57,320 --> 00:02:03,230
And I'm going to do the same here because we know that anything that comes in here is going to be a

27
00:02:03,230 --> 00:02:04,100
response.

28
00:02:04,100 --> 00:02:06,140
So I'm going to print.

29
00:02:07,490 --> 00:02:10,880
Response and then print the packet to show.

30
00:02:11,060 --> 00:02:17,810
Now we actually built the script before and we explained how this works and how it's able to filter

31
00:02:17,810 --> 00:02:19,430
the requests and the responses.

32
00:02:19,430 --> 00:02:21,080
So I'm not going to go into that.

33
00:02:21,080 --> 00:02:26,630
If you don't remember how this works, please go back to the start of the replace download script.

34
00:02:27,260 --> 00:02:35,120
Right now I'm just going to go down and I've already run my IP table rules so that the packets are redirected

35
00:02:35,120 --> 00:02:41,810
to my ndphc and I'm already in the working directory of my code injector of my new program.

36
00:02:41,810 --> 00:02:49,010
So I'm just going to run it and I'm going to scroll up a little bit so that whenever there is packets

37
00:02:49,010 --> 00:02:53,540
coming up, the terminal will not automatically go all the way down.

38
00:02:53,540 --> 00:02:58,940
So it will stick in here and I'll be able to trace the requests and the responses from the start.

39
00:02:59,510 --> 00:03:06,500
And then I'm just going to go to my browser and I'm going to go to any HTTP website like bing dot com.

40
00:03:08,930 --> 00:03:12,920
Now, as usual, we're going to be able to capture the requests here.

41
00:03:12,920 --> 00:03:20,930
So as you can see, we have a request here and the request is the get request and it's requesting bing

42
00:03:20,930 --> 00:03:21,710
dot com.

43
00:03:22,490 --> 00:03:27,410
If you go down, you'll see that we have the response or we can say the first response.

44
00:03:27,560 --> 00:03:31,880
And in this first response we can see the HTTP header is first.

45
00:03:32,060 --> 00:03:41,480
And then if you keep scrolling down, you'll see the HTML code of the page being sent in here and notice

46
00:03:41,480 --> 00:03:44,630
this is actually sent in the load field of the raw.

47
00:03:44,630 --> 00:03:49,490
So as usual, this is the field that contains the useful information for us.

48
00:03:50,060 --> 00:03:57,290
But if you know anything about HTML, you'll know directly that this code is not HTML code.

49
00:03:57,650 --> 00:04:04,730
So if I just right click this and view the page source, which is the HTML code of the page, you'll

50
00:04:04,730 --> 00:04:10,340
see that the HTML code looks something like this and not like this.

51
00:04:11,210 --> 00:04:12,770
Why is this happening?

52
00:04:12,860 --> 00:04:18,110
So there has to be something that's going wrong or we must be missing something.

53
00:04:18,560 --> 00:04:25,190
And the answer is we are missing something if we go up in here and look for the request.

54
00:04:25,310 --> 00:04:27,350
So this is our first request.

55
00:04:27,710 --> 00:04:34,640
And if we look at the load of this request, because as usual, the load is what contains the important

56
00:04:34,640 --> 00:04:44,330
stuff, we can see that we have a field called accept encoding and the value for this field is gsib

57
00:04:44,330 --> 00:04:45,560
and deflate.

58
00:04:46,250 --> 00:04:52,820
So basically in the request, we first of all were saying that we want a website called big dot com

59
00:04:53,120 --> 00:05:01,010
and in this same request we're saying that or the browser is saying that I can accept GCP encoding.

60
00:05:01,610 --> 00:05:08,210
So the server is going to receive this and it's going to see that the browser which is us are able to

61
00:05:08,210 --> 00:05:14,990
understand GZIP encoding, which is an encoding that's used to compress HTML code.

62
00:05:15,560 --> 00:05:22,820
Therefore, when the browser wants to send us the response, it will first compress the response to

63
00:05:22,820 --> 00:05:29,120
a GZIP format, send it to us, which is what we are seeing in here.

64
00:05:30,890 --> 00:05:36,020
And then our browser will decode this to HTML code.

65
00:05:36,470 --> 00:05:43,040
So basically all the gibberish that you see in here is actually the HTML code that we have in here.

66
00:05:43,280 --> 00:05:50,810
But because we're telling the server that I can understand GCP, the server is coding this HTML, compressing

67
00:05:50,810 --> 00:05:58,130
it into GCP format, sending it to us, which is what we're capturing here in the raw layer.

68
00:05:58,130 --> 00:06:05,030
And then the browser is decoding this to the normal HTML code that it can understand.

69
00:06:05,660 --> 00:06:12,510
Now this is a problem because we can't really inject anything in here unless we can read the code.

70
00:06:12,530 --> 00:06:14,420
How could we replace the images?

71
00:06:14,420 --> 00:06:16,790
How could we replace the links?

72
00:06:16,790 --> 00:06:21,230
How could we inject JavaScript such as the JavaScript code in here?

73
00:06:21,230 --> 00:06:25,840
If we can't read the code itself, we're not going to know where we're going to inject stuff.

74
00:06:25,850 --> 00:06:27,920
We're not going to know where we remove stuff.

75
00:06:28,280 --> 00:06:36,350
Therefore, the first step is going to be decoding this and being able to receive the HTML code and

76
00:06:36,350 --> 00:06:41,240
reading it in plain text instead of reading it in JSP format.

77
00:06:41,720 --> 00:06:46,760
Now there are a number of ways to achieve this, and I'm going to show you the easiest and simplest

78
00:06:46,760 --> 00:06:48,410
one in the next lecture.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,970 --> 00:00:10,450
Now in this lecture our goal is to be able to convert this GZIP HTML code to a plain text HTML code

2
00:00:10,450 --> 00:00:14,590
like this one here so we can read it and then modify it.

3
00:00:15,580 --> 00:00:20,860
And in the previous lecture, when I was talking about this, I hinted on the request.

4
00:00:20,870 --> 00:00:28,300
So the first thing that I did is I showed you the contents of the request, and I showed you that one

5
00:00:28,300 --> 00:00:35,290
of the parts of the header is in the request was the accept encoding, which is basically the browser's

6
00:00:35,290 --> 00:00:42,670
way of telling the web server that I can understand GZIP encoding, and that's why the server assumes

7
00:00:42,670 --> 00:00:48,310
that we can understand it and then encodes the HTML code into GZIP format.

8
00:00:48,730 --> 00:00:58,000
So if we modify the request and remove this header right here, the accept encoding header, the server

9
00:00:58,000 --> 00:01:06,760
will think that we cannot understand GZIP encoding and therefore it will send us the response in plain

10
00:01:07,030 --> 00:01:08,920
HTML as text.

11
00:01:09,190 --> 00:01:18,700
So if we can modify this load in here and literally remove this field, the accept encoding field from

12
00:01:18,700 --> 00:01:22,390
the start all the way down to the slash r slash.

13
00:01:22,390 --> 00:01:29,170
And then the browser will think that we cannot understand GZIP format and therefore it will send us

14
00:01:29,170 --> 00:01:32,860
the data in plain text, which is the HTML code.

15
00:01:33,760 --> 00:01:42,250
So in order to do this, we can use a very simple Python method called dot replace and replace all of

16
00:01:42,250 --> 00:01:43,780
this text with nothing.

17
00:01:44,290 --> 00:01:50,710
But that's not the best way to do it because this request right here might be a little bit different.

18
00:01:50,710 --> 00:01:58,570
For example, it might just say accept encoding GZIP on its own without deflate, or it might contain

19
00:01:58,570 --> 00:02:00,670
other encoding types.

20
00:02:01,120 --> 00:02:04,780
So the best way to do this is to use regex.

21
00:02:05,080 --> 00:02:11,350
Now we covered how we can find text based on regex, but in this lecture we'll see how we can replace

22
00:02:11,350 --> 00:02:13,420
text based on regex.

23
00:02:13,960 --> 00:02:20,440
So first of all, I'm going to copy all of the text that contains the subtext that we want to replace.

24
00:02:22,450 --> 00:02:27,820
I'm going to go to Pith X, which is a website that I showed you before when we spoke about regex.

25
00:02:28,840 --> 00:02:34,210
And I'm going to open the cheat sheet and we're going to write our regex code in here.

26
00:02:34,660 --> 00:02:43,030
Now, as we know, the main thing that we're looking for is this part right here, which is except encoding.

27
00:02:43,690 --> 00:02:53,890
So I'm going to type, accept encoding, put the call on and then, like I said, I want to have a rule

28
00:02:53,890 --> 00:03:01,270
that will match everything that comes after the except encoding and all the way to the first slash or

29
00:03:01,270 --> 00:03:02,350
slash DN.

30
00:03:02,620 --> 00:03:09,280
Again, the reason why I want to do this instead of matching the whole string in here is just in case

31
00:03:09,280 --> 00:03:13,850
that the value for the accept encoding field is different.

32
00:03:13,870 --> 00:03:20,290
So the accepting coding is the name of the field and it will never be different, but the value of it

33
00:03:20,290 --> 00:03:21,580
could be different.

34
00:03:21,910 --> 00:03:28,090
Therefore, we are using the accepting coding as a keyword to start the matching algorithm.

35
00:03:28,390 --> 00:03:34,120
Then we're going to say we're going to put a dot to say that I can accept any character after that,

36
00:03:34,450 --> 00:03:42,640
and then I'm going to put a star to say I can accept any number of characters after that and the end

37
00:03:42,640 --> 00:03:44,180
of the string that I want to match.

38
00:03:44,200 --> 00:03:48,220
As you can see in here is slash or slash DN.

39
00:03:48,940 --> 00:03:56,230
But when you are using regex, you can't really use backward slash because as you can see in here,

40
00:03:56,230 --> 00:04:00,460
the backward slash can be used to escape special characters.

41
00:04:00,460 --> 00:04:04,750
So it has a specific role when you are writing regex.

42
00:04:05,170 --> 00:04:10,660
Therefore, if you want to write a normal backward slash, you'll have to type it twice.

43
00:04:11,170 --> 00:04:18,340
And what we're looking for is a slash or so I'll put the R and then I'm going to type this twice again

44
00:04:18,340 --> 00:04:25,120
and put the DN because I'm looking for a slash and as well now you can see that this is matching the

45
00:04:25,120 --> 00:04:33,130
whole string from the start, which is accepting coding and all the way down to the last slash or slash.

46
00:04:34,120 --> 00:04:35,500
Now I don't want that.

47
00:04:35,500 --> 00:04:41,230
I want it to match all the string from the start, which is accepting coding, but all the way down

48
00:04:41,230 --> 00:04:46,540
to the first slash, R slash and not to the last slash or slash n.

49
00:04:47,080 --> 00:04:55,390
So to tell the regex that I only want you to match string up to the first occurrence of the end string,

50
00:04:55,540 --> 00:04:59,440
we're going to have to specify that this rule is non greedy.

51
00:05:00,040 --> 00:05:04,840
And to do that, we're going to put a question mark right in here.

52
00:05:05,410 --> 00:05:11,530
And now, as you can see, the regex engine is going to start with the start of the string that we put

53
00:05:11,530 --> 00:05:13,930
in here, which is accepting coding.

54
00:05:14,290 --> 00:05:21,310
Then we're saying that I can accept any number of characters by putting Dot Star, and then I'm saying

55
00:05:21,310 --> 00:05:23,440
that I want this to be non greedy.

56
00:05:23,440 --> 00:05:32,110
So I want you to stop at the first occurrence of the slash r, slash n and I'm using double slash in

57
00:05:32,110 --> 00:05:38,980
here and in here because a single slash has a specific role when we are writing regex rules.

58
00:05:38,980 --> 00:05:43,540
So we use the double slash to tell regex that this is a normal slash.

59
00:05:43,540 --> 00:05:46,600
And it's not me trying to tell you something else.

60
00:05:47,320 --> 00:05:53,980
Now, of course, I've spoke about regex before and I spoke about the dot star rule before.

61
00:05:53,980 --> 00:05:58,780
So if you don't remember this, please go back to the lecture when I first spoke about regex.

62
00:05:58,900 --> 00:06:04,330
And you can also see a cheat sheet in here which will allow you to build regex rules.

63
00:06:05,110 --> 00:06:12,190
Now, basically, the rule that we have will match the highlighted string in here in any string.

64
00:06:12,190 --> 00:06:14,500
So let me show you an example.

65
00:06:14,500 --> 00:06:20,920
Let's say the string that we had only had GZIP and it didn't have the flight in it.

66
00:06:22,370 --> 00:06:25,850
As you can see, our rule will still match that.

67
00:06:26,000 --> 00:06:29,360
Let's say that we had some kind of unknown encoding.

68
00:06:30,200 --> 00:06:37,340
Again, the rule will match that and this will work perfect in our case because we don't care what comes

69
00:06:37,340 --> 00:06:39,140
after the except encoding.

70
00:06:39,320 --> 00:06:44,780
We want to remove the accepting coding and everything that comes after it.

71
00:06:45,350 --> 00:06:52,820
So this rule will allow us to match anything that starts with except encoding colon and ends with a

72
00:06:52,820 --> 00:06:54,590
slash or slash DN.

73
00:06:55,400 --> 00:07:01,850
Anything that comes between can be variable and can be anything, and it will still be matched with

74
00:07:01,850 --> 00:07:02,720
our rule.

75
00:07:04,420 --> 00:07:10,600
Now in the next lecture, we're going to use this rule in our python code and we'll see how we can use

76
00:07:10,600 --> 00:07:14,560
regex to replace a substring within a string.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:01,610
Okay.

2
00:00:01,620 --> 00:00:08,130
Now that we have a rule that can match any substring that starts with accepting coding and ends with

3
00:00:08,130 --> 00:00:14,160
a slash or slash n such as this string right here within any bigger string like this one.

4
00:00:14,160 --> 00:00:21,930
And here in this lecture, I want to show you how we can use regex to basically remove the accepting

5
00:00:21,930 --> 00:00:26,490
coding and its value from any load that we have.

6
00:00:26,760 --> 00:00:33,510
So this is going to be an example of using regex to replace a substring within a bigger string.

7
00:00:33,510 --> 00:00:37,530
And you can use this knowledge with any scenario that you have.

8
00:00:37,530 --> 00:00:42,290
So you might want to replace this within a file or within any other program.

9
00:00:42,300 --> 00:00:47,880
It doesn't really matter as long as you build up your rule and here and use the function that I'm going

10
00:00:47,880 --> 00:00:49,140
to show you right now.

11
00:00:49,770 --> 00:00:55,140
So first of all, I'm going to go to my machine and I'm going to import.

12
00:00:55,780 --> 00:00:59,200
The RI module, which is the regex module in Python.

13
00:01:00,110 --> 00:01:05,540
Next, I'm going to remove this, which is the part that prints my request.

14
00:01:06,410 --> 00:01:08,540
And in here, first of all, we're printing that.

15
00:01:08,540 --> 00:01:17,360
We got a request and then I'm going to do re dot sub, which is short for substitute and you can automatically

16
00:01:17,360 --> 00:01:21,050
see py charm showing us how to use this sub module.

17
00:01:21,680 --> 00:01:24,860
So the first thing that we need to give is the pattern.

18
00:01:24,860 --> 00:01:31,460
So this is the regex pattern that will be used to match the substring within the bigger string.

19
00:01:31,610 --> 00:01:35,870
So we've already built our pattern in here, which is this one.

20
00:01:36,020 --> 00:01:39,380
So I'm going to copy it and paste it here.

21
00:01:40,570 --> 00:01:45,580
The next thing that we need to specify is what we want to replace.

22
00:01:45,580 --> 00:01:52,870
The text that will be matched with this pattern with for example, we have this text is being matched

23
00:01:52,870 --> 00:01:58,810
within the pattern that we have and then we have to tell the function what we want this highlighted

24
00:01:58,810 --> 00:02:00,790
text to be replaced with.

25
00:02:00,910 --> 00:02:07,390
So like I said, depending on your scenario, you might want to replace this with a specific text.

26
00:02:07,390 --> 00:02:13,570
For example, you might want to say accept encoding, deflate, for example, instead of GZIP.

27
00:02:14,080 --> 00:02:21,490
In our example, we actually want to remove all of the highlighted text so that the web server thinks

28
00:02:21,490 --> 00:02:28,390
that we cannot understand any type of encoding and therefore send us the HTML page without encoding

29
00:02:28,390 --> 00:02:28,780
it.

30
00:02:29,320 --> 00:02:31,930
So we basically want to remove all of this.

31
00:02:31,930 --> 00:02:35,590
So what we're going to say is we want to replace it with nothing.

32
00:02:35,590 --> 00:02:40,420
So I'm going to put two empty quotation marks or an empty string.

33
00:02:41,230 --> 00:02:49,570
The final part of this function is the bigger string that we want to match this substring width that

34
00:02:49,570 --> 00:02:53,230
we want to apply this regex rule to.

35
00:02:53,470 --> 00:03:00,820
So it's basically the all of this string right here that we used, the one that we used in the test

36
00:03:00,820 --> 00:03:05,980
string and we took all of this from the result of the load.

37
00:03:05,980 --> 00:03:08,890
So that's going to be an escape packet.

38
00:03:10,010 --> 00:03:15,170
Skippy the draw and in the load field.

39
00:03:15,560 --> 00:03:23,030
So basically, all all we are saying is I want you to match any substring that matches this rule and

40
00:03:23,030 --> 00:03:28,790
the bigger string, which is the load that is in the row layer of this CP packet.

41
00:03:29,060 --> 00:03:31,670
And I want you to replace that with nothing.

42
00:03:32,450 --> 00:03:37,650
Now, the result of executing this function will be returned as a string.

43
00:03:37,670 --> 00:03:43,580
So we have to capture that, and I'm going to capture it in a string called Modified Load.

44
00:03:45,790 --> 00:03:52,420
Now we can use the set load function that we have in here that we built in the replace download script.

45
00:03:52,870 --> 00:03:58,720
So we know that this function takes a packet and a load and it'll return a packet with the modified

46
00:03:58,720 --> 00:03:59,320
load.

47
00:03:59,560 --> 00:04:02,020
So we're going to use that in here.

48
00:04:02,020 --> 00:04:08,290
So all I'm going to do is I'm going to say new packet is equal to set load.

49
00:04:09,620 --> 00:04:17,900
To my escapee packet and the load that I want to use this time is the modified load, and that will

50
00:04:17,900 --> 00:04:25,220
give me a new packet that is identical to this CP packet, but with the modified load that is returned

51
00:04:25,220 --> 00:04:29,030
in this function because it's stored in the modified load variable.

52
00:04:29,330 --> 00:04:34,370
Now I covered how to write this function right here and the replace downloads video.

53
00:04:34,370 --> 00:04:39,140
So if you don't remember how this is done or how this works, please go back to that lecture.

54
00:04:39,740 --> 00:04:45,740
And finally, just like we did with the replaced downloads, the last thing that we need to do is set

55
00:04:45,740 --> 00:04:48,050
the payload of the actual packet.

56
00:04:48,050 --> 00:04:55,190
So the first thing that we do is we convert our packet to escape packet so that we can read it, analyze

57
00:04:55,190 --> 00:04:56,150
it, modify it.

58
00:04:56,150 --> 00:04:59,840
And the last thing that we do is accept this packet.

59
00:05:00,440 --> 00:05:06,410
But since we modified the packet in here, we need to change the payload of the packet that we will

60
00:05:06,410 --> 00:05:10,040
accept to the new payload of the new packet.

61
00:05:10,580 --> 00:05:13,400
So all we have to do is do packet.

62
00:05:14,500 --> 00:05:21,670
That set payload and set this payload to the string of the new packet.

63
00:05:22,360 --> 00:05:28,300
Now again, I covered why we do this and how we do this in more details in the previous lecture of the

64
00:05:28,300 --> 00:05:29,440
replace downloads.

65
00:05:29,440 --> 00:05:34,720
And that's why I'm doing this kind of quickly now, if you kind of don't understand what's happening,

66
00:05:34,720 --> 00:05:36,880
please go back and revise that lecture.

67
00:05:37,630 --> 00:05:39,190
Now we're done with this.

68
00:05:39,190 --> 00:05:47,020
And basically what this should do now is whenever it gets a request that contains accept encoding in

69
00:05:47,020 --> 00:05:50,590
its load, it'll remove all of this.

70
00:05:50,920 --> 00:05:58,060
And the result of that is the web server is going to think that we do not understand GZIP encoding or

71
00:05:58,060 --> 00:06:04,870
any other encoding and therefore it will send us back the HTML code in plain text.

72
00:06:05,530 --> 00:06:08,320
So let's see, we're going to save this.

73
00:06:08,350 --> 00:06:11,470
Go down, clear all of this.

And run my program again.

75
00:06:15,250 --> 00:06:24,050
Going to scroll up a little bit so that I don't have to scroll again and I'll close this and let me

76
00:06:24,050 --> 00:06:26,570
just go to Bing Dot Dotcom again.

77
00:06:29,640 --> 00:06:33,240
And you can see that the requests and the responses are being sent here.

78
00:06:33,240 --> 00:06:35,580
And let's go down and have a look.

79
00:06:35,700 --> 00:06:40,740
Now, this time I'm only printing the show of the responses, so you can see when I get a request it

80
00:06:40,740 --> 00:06:45,390
just says that there is a request and I'm only printing the responses.

81
00:06:45,810 --> 00:06:47,910
And let's go down a little bit.

82
00:06:48,030 --> 00:06:54,630
And as you can see from the start from here, we can see that we have the HTML code, so we have it

83
00:06:54,630 --> 00:06:55,350
in here.

84
00:06:55,920 --> 00:07:01,830
If we keep going down, you'll see we have HTML code in here as well.

85
00:07:02,310 --> 00:07:09,990
And if you keep going down, you'll see we have the style of the web page being sent first and keep

86
00:07:09,990 --> 00:07:11,280
scrolling down.

87
00:07:12,090 --> 00:07:19,710
You'll see we have the markup of the page, the divs, the lies, the tags, which are the tags that

88
00:07:19,710 --> 00:07:22,200
are responsible for links.

89
00:07:22,200 --> 00:07:25,500
You'll see the images, you'll see everything.

90
00:07:25,500 --> 00:07:32,850
Literally everything that is being loaded on this page is going to be sent in here in plain text.

91
00:07:33,450 --> 00:07:39,150
So now you're only limited by your knowledge of HTML and JavaScript.

92
00:07:39,150 --> 00:07:45,870
If you know any of this, you'll be able to inject HTML code and the web page that is being loaded in

93
00:07:45,870 --> 00:07:46,380
here.

94
00:07:46,380 --> 00:07:53,040
You'll be able to inject JavaScript code in the page that is being loaded in here as well.

95
00:07:53,280 --> 00:07:58,950
And only by knowing these two things you'll be able to achieve so much you'll be able to replace the

96
00:07:58,950 --> 00:08:03,570
images, for example, just for fun, you'll be able to replace the URLs.

97
00:08:03,570 --> 00:08:09,030
So the links on the pages that are getting loaded, for example, you can make the news, take you to

98
00:08:09,030 --> 00:08:12,660
somewhere else other than the right place that it should take you to.

99
00:08:12,780 --> 00:08:15,150
You can change the way when you submit a form.

100
00:08:15,150 --> 00:08:20,220
For example, when you search for something here, you can make it so that the user will go somewhere

101
00:08:20,220 --> 00:08:20,700
else.

102
00:08:20,700 --> 00:08:27,690
Instead of having the search query executed on the target website, you can inject JavaScript code,

103
00:08:27,690 --> 00:08:32,970
which is what I'm going to show you in the next lecture, which is very, very powerful because it will

104
00:08:32,970 --> 00:08:39,090
allow you to use frameworks like VIF, which will allow you to do so many things on the target, such

105
00:08:39,090 --> 00:08:46,350
as showing them fake updates, taking screenshots of their web browser, showing them fake notification

106
00:08:46,350 --> 00:08:51,360
bars, fake login dialogs, and so many more cool attacks.

107
00:08:52,790 --> 00:08:53,810
So that's it.

108
00:08:53,810 --> 00:09:01,730
For this lecture, we managed to write a script that will modify the HTTP requests so that we tell the

109
00:09:01,730 --> 00:09:08,660
server that we don't understand the GZIP encoding so that we get the HTML code in plain text.

110
00:09:08,660 --> 00:09:15,860
And in the next lecture we'll see how we can modify the plaintext HTML code that we're getting in here

111
00:09:15,860 --> 00:09:19,490

112
00:09:19,940 --> 00:09:27,140
Now, everything that I showed you so far from modifying the headers to using Python to use this sub

113
00:09:27,140 --> 00:09:31,940
in here to replace a substring within a bigger string based on regex.

All of this is generic and you can use it in so many other scenarios.

I just like to use these functions in examples so that it's easier and more fun to learn them.

116

But that doesn't mean that they are limited to what I'm using them for.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,040 --> 00:00:01,520
Okay.

2
00:00:01,520 --> 00:00:09,230
Now, using our script, we're able to see the requests and the responses and we're even able to see

3
00:00:09,230 --> 00:00:15,440
the plain text of the HTML code of the pages that the person is loading.

4
00:00:15,920 --> 00:00:24,410
And as expected, we can see that the HTML code is being sent in the load field of the raw layer of

5
00:00:24,410 --> 00:00:26,030
each response packet.

6
00:00:26,360 --> 00:00:33,770
Now we know that the load is just a string and basically using Python we can replace any part of this

7
00:00:33,770 --> 00:00:38,830
string or inject more string or more text inside the string.

8
00:00:38,840 --> 00:00:45,800
So you literally only limited by your knowledge of HTML and JavaScript with what you can do with this.

9
00:00:46,130 --> 00:00:53,540
Now what I want to do in this example is inject a JavaScript code that will only show an alert on screen

10
00:00:53,540 --> 00:00:58,310
saying test just to make sure that what I'm doing is correct and that it's working.

11
00:00:59,150 --> 00:01:04,520
And then in the next lecture, we're going to inject more useful JavaScript codes.

12
00:01:05,300 --> 00:01:12,110
Now, JavaScript is a client side programming language, which basically means that this language gets

13
00:01:12,110 --> 00:01:16,580
executed on the browser itself, not on the server.

14
00:01:16,580 --> 00:01:24,560
So it's similar to HTML, but HTML is a markup language, whereas JavaScript is a programming language

15
00:01:24,560 --> 00:01:27,680
allowing us to do so much more than HTML.

16
00:01:28,250 --> 00:01:33,440
So the script that will show the alert that I want to use is basically just alert.

17
00:01:33,440 --> 00:01:40,760
And then I'm going to put the alert text, which is test and that's it, that's the whole script.

18
00:01:40,760 --> 00:01:47,210
So alert is a function in JavaScript that will display a message to the user.

19
00:01:47,210 --> 00:01:50,330
And the message that I want to display is called test.

20
00:01:51,170 --> 00:01:59,570
Now if you want to run a JavaScript code in the HTML page, you have to wrap this with a script tag.

21
00:01:59,720 --> 00:02:02,810
So you have to say script before it.

22
00:02:04,350 --> 00:02:08,460
And at the end of that, you have to do slash script.

23
00:02:10,030 --> 00:02:10,510
Okay.

24
00:02:10,570 --> 00:02:11,770
Now, that's perfect.

25
00:02:12,040 --> 00:02:20,920
And all we want to do now is put this text somewhere in our Web page, and here now you can put it anywhere

26
00:02:20,920 --> 00:02:21,550
you want.

27
00:02:21,550 --> 00:02:28,480
So you can literally replace this slash div with another slash div followed by the script.

28
00:02:29,420 --> 00:02:30,270
Or what?

29
00:02:30,290 --> 00:02:37,790
What is much better is to look for the last tag of the page, which is a tag that should exist in every

30
00:02:37,790 --> 00:02:38,900
single page.

31
00:02:39,200 --> 00:02:46,820
So if I just right click this and view the page source, you can see that the page ends with a slash

32
00:02:46,820 --> 00:02:47,420
body.

33
00:02:47,930 --> 00:02:55,460
Now, this is usually the last tag of the page that indicates this is the end of the body of the page.

34
00:02:55,790 --> 00:03:02,390
There is only one of this unlike other tags that you might see they repeat, and if you try to inject

35
00:03:02,390 --> 00:03:07,640
stuff after it, then you'll end up with injecting your code multiple times in the page.

36
00:03:08,090 --> 00:03:15,050
So the body tag is a really good tag to use as an anchor when injecting stuff on the page.

37
00:03:15,680 --> 00:03:22,220
It's also a good idea to inject your JavaScript in here so that the page gets loaded first and then

38
00:03:22,220 --> 00:03:29,210
your JavaScript code gets executed so that it won't delay the page and the user will not get suspicious.

39
00:03:29,210 --> 00:03:35,720
Because if you inject your code, for example, here at the head at the start of the page, then if

40
00:03:35,720 --> 00:03:42,920
your code takes some time to execute, the page will not load until your code is executed or loaded.

41
00:03:43,100 --> 00:03:46,880
Whereas when we put it at the bottom, it will get loaded at the bottom.

42
00:03:46,880 --> 00:03:52,940
And even if there is an error with your code, it won't really affect the way that the website looks

43
00:03:52,940 --> 00:03:55,040
because we're putting it at the end.

44
00:03:55,940 --> 00:04:03,890
So what I want to do is I want to replace any occurrence of the slash body with another slash body followed

45
00:04:03,890 --> 00:04:05,030
by my script.

46
00:04:05,630 --> 00:04:13,040
To do this, we're going to use a replace method that can be invoked on any string in Python.

47
00:04:13,730 --> 00:04:19,400
So you can see that the HTML code is literally coming up in the load as a string.

48
00:04:19,640 --> 00:04:25,730
And since this is a string, we can use any methods that can be used on Python strings and therefore

49
00:04:25,730 --> 00:04:27,710
we can use the dot replace method.

50
00:04:28,730 --> 00:04:34,310
So basically what I'm going to say in my response, so we know that the response is coming here.

51
00:04:34,340 --> 00:04:40,910
I'm going to remove the print statement here and I'm going to say the modified load.

52
00:04:42,330 --> 00:04:47,910
Is equal to Skippy packet and we want to access the raw layer.

53
00:04:47,910 --> 00:04:55,320
So we're going to do KP Dot raw and we can access the load field because this is the field that contains

54
00:04:55,320 --> 00:04:57,030
the HTML code.

55
00:04:57,150 --> 00:05:01,680
And for all of this I want to do dot replace.

56
00:05:02,100 --> 00:05:06,030
So I want to invoke a Python method that is called the replace.

57
00:05:06,360 --> 00:05:13,320
And this method basically allows me to replace a certain string with another string.

58
00:05:14,280 --> 00:05:19,650
So the first input to this argument is the string that I want to replace.

59
00:05:19,650 --> 00:05:27,120
And what I want to replace is every time I want Python to see a slash body tag, which is the last tag

60
00:05:27,120 --> 00:05:32,430
that I just showed you, I want it to be replaced with my JavaScript code.

61
00:05:32,640 --> 00:05:35,040
So it's this code in here.

62
00:05:35,040 --> 00:05:36,180
I'm going to copy it.

63
00:05:38,930 --> 00:05:42,500
And then we're going to append this large body.

64
00:05:42,860 --> 00:05:50,180
So basically what we're saying is I want you to, first of all, read the load field of the raw layer

65
00:05:50,180 --> 00:05:51,590
of every packet.

66
00:05:51,710 --> 00:05:54,290
I want you to look for slash body.

67
00:05:54,650 --> 00:05:58,310
And I want you to replace that with my JavaScript code.

68
00:05:59,440 --> 00:06:01,300
Followed by slash body.

69
00:06:02,910 --> 00:06:06,700
Now you can use this replace function on any string.

70
00:06:06,720 --> 00:06:09,020
It doesn't have to be a load field of a packet.

71
00:06:09,030 --> 00:06:16,080
Like I said, everything that I show is generic, so this method can be invoked or on any string and

72
00:06:16,080 --> 00:06:20,940
it will allow you to replace a certain substring with another substring.

73
00:06:22,030 --> 00:06:28,930
And based on what we are able to see in here, you'll see that you'll be able to replace images, download

74
00:06:28,930 --> 00:06:36,280
links, JavaScript codes or anything that is loaded in the HTML page in here.

75
00:06:37,060 --> 00:06:44,190
Now let's go back to our example, and now our modified load contains the load that has our script.

76
00:06:44,200 --> 00:06:48,610
So now all we have to do is literally do what we were doing in here.

77
00:06:48,610 --> 00:06:51,400
So we're going to say our new packet.

78
00:06:53,010 --> 00:06:55,620
Is equal to set payload.

79
00:06:55,620 --> 00:07:00,480
So we're invoking the set payload function and we're going to give it this KP packet.

80
00:07:00,480 --> 00:07:04,470
And we wanted to set this with the modified load.

81
00:07:04,920 --> 00:07:12,510
And finally we want to set the payload of the packet to the string of the new packet.

82
00:07:13,670 --> 00:07:14,320
And that's it.

83
00:07:14,330 --> 00:07:15,020
We're done.

84
00:07:15,470 --> 00:07:17,600
So let's save this.

85
00:07:17,840 --> 00:07:20,240
Go down, Control.

86
00:07:20,240 --> 00:07:21,320
See this?

87
00:07:21,320 --> 00:07:23,030
Run our code again.

88
00:07:23,510 --> 00:07:29,870
And I'm just going to close everything and remove my history first.

89
00:07:30,590 --> 00:07:32,960
And then I'm going to go to Bing dot com.

90
00:07:34,230 --> 00:07:41,190
And as you can see, as soon as the page loads we are getting an alert or a message saying test.

91
00:07:41,490 --> 00:07:48,150
So this means that our JavaScript code that we injected is getting executed on the target page.

92
00:07:48,720 --> 00:07:49,880
So that's perfect.

93
00:07:49,890 --> 00:07:56,820
Now, as you can see, this is not really a very useful JavaScript code, but in the next lecture I'm

94
00:07:56,820 --> 00:08:02,310
going to show you a more useful JavaScript code that you could run that will allow you to do so many

95
00:08:02,310 --> 00:08:03,840
cool things on the target.

96
00:08:04,500 --> 00:08:11,100
But basically for now, this is the end of the lecture, and you can use what I showed you so far to

97
00:08:11,100 --> 00:08:16,950
replace any part in any HTML page so it doesn't have to be injecting JavaScript.

98
00:08:16,950 --> 00:08:22,080
You can replace the links, you can replace the images, and like I said, you're only limited by your

99
00:08:22,080 --> 00:08:24,870
knowledge of JavaScript and HTML.

100
00:08:25,470 --> 00:08:27,990
Also the methods that I showed you so far.

101
00:08:27,990 --> 00:08:34,110
So the normal replace method that can be invoked on strings, and the three dot sub method that replace

102
00:08:34,110 --> 00:08:39,420
a substring and a bigger string using regex, you can use them on any string.

103
00:08:39,450 --> 00:08:42,360

00:00:00,890 --> 00:00:07,430
Now before I show you how to use useful code and inject it into a remote browser, I want to spend a

00:00:14,150 --> 00:00:19,790
the code after we write it, and you'll notice that when you do this, you'll see that there is a lot

6

00:00:33,080 --> 00:00:39,650

your dot load a lot in my code.

14
00:01:10,790 --> 00:01:17,000
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to copy this and in here on the top, whenever I check if this

15
00:01:17,000 --> 00:01:23,090
packet is interesting for me, I'm going to create a new variable and I'm going to call it load and

16
00:01:23,090 --> 00:01:28,550
I'm going to make the load is equal to the load field of the row layer in this copy packet.

17
00:01:29,000 --> 00:01:33,440
Then every time I need to use the load, all I have to do is just type load.

18
00:01:33,440 --> 00:01:35,780
So here I can remove all of this.

19
00:01:37,010 --> 00:01:40,640
And in here, I can remove all of this.

20
00:01:42,010 --> 00:01:48,190
Now, the next thing that you'll probably notice right away is the use of the modified load.

21
00:01:48,220 --> 00:01:55,090
Now, this is a redundant variable that we don't really need because in here I can just say the load.

22
00:01:55,810 --> 00:01:58,930
Is equal to the load after we modify it.

23
00:01:59,600 --> 00:02:05,240
Then whenever I'm using it here, I can use my new load variable or basically the only load variable

24
00:02:05,240 --> 00:02:06,110
that we have.

25
00:02:06,800 --> 00:02:08,450
And same goes in here.

26
00:02:08,450 --> 00:02:14,360
So whenever I'm replacing the load, instead of putting that in a new variable called modified load,

27
00:02:14,360 --> 00:02:18,800
we can just say our load is going to be equal to this.

28
00:02:20,190 --> 00:02:21,360
And same goes here.

29
00:02:21,360 --> 00:02:27,510
When we when we change the load in the packet, we're just going to use the load variable that we always

30
00:02:27,510 --> 00:02:28,740
use in the code.

31
00:02:29,550 --> 00:02:35,700
So this actually makes it very easy for us in the future when we try to modify the code, because before

32
00:02:35,700 --> 00:02:42,000
we had two variables, we had a modified load and an original load and it might get hard to track once

33
00:02:42,000 --> 00:02:48,020
the code gets bigger, but now we only have one variable for the load and that's literally called load,

34
00:02:48,030 --> 00:02:53,940
so we can modify it, check its value and do whatever we want with it throughout the code.

35
00:02:53,940 --> 00:02:58,550
And we know that this is the only variable that holds the value for the load.

36
00:02:58,560 --> 00:03:04,110
So if we modify the load, this is the only way to modify it and this is the only variable that we need

37
00:03:04,110 --> 00:03:07,590
to check if we ever needed to check the value of the load.

38
00:03:08,400 --> 00:03:13,980
Now, another thing that you probably noticed is these two lines in here, which is where we set the

39
00:03:13,980 --> 00:03:19,380
new packet and then we set the payload of the original packet are repeated.

40
00:03:19,380 --> 00:03:24,270
So we have them in here and we have the exact same two lines in here.

41
00:03:24,900 --> 00:03:32,040
Now as you know, this code works perfectly, but let's say, for example, in a month time from now

42
00:03:32,040 --> 00:03:36,060
there was a problem or there was an update to the set payload method.

43
00:03:36,420 --> 00:03:41,970
So if you wanted to modify your code, you'll have to modify it in here and in here.

44
00:03:42,360 --> 00:03:46,050
That's why repeated code is considered to be bad practice.

45
00:03:46,050 --> 00:03:51,120
When it comes to programming, you should not repeat code that does the same function.

46
00:03:51,330 --> 00:03:57,870
So our goal now, instead of repeating these two lines in here, we should only use them once.

47
00:03:58,900 --> 00:04:05,410
Now we know these two lines set the payload of the original packet, which is the packet that we get

48
00:04:05,410 --> 00:04:11,020
as an input to the load that we modify if we modify the load.

49
00:04:11,770 --> 00:04:14,110
So that's the same case in both scenarios.

50
00:04:14,110 --> 00:04:19,740
We only do this, we only execute these two lines if we actually modify the load.

51
00:04:19,750 --> 00:04:26,320
And you can see that in in this statement, we modified the load here and in this statement we modify

52
00:04:26,320 --> 00:04:27,220
the load here.

53
00:04:27,460 --> 00:04:33,880
So if the load is not modified, we don't need to run these two lines and we only need to run them if

54
00:04:33,880 --> 00:04:35,410
the load gets modified.

55
00:04:36,070 --> 00:04:43,300
So if we want to run this once throughout our code, all we have to do is check if the load got modified

56
00:04:43,300 --> 00:04:44,230
in any of these.

57
00:04:44,230 --> 00:04:49,360
If statements then set the payload of my packet to the new load.

58
00:04:49,630 --> 00:04:51,760
Otherwise don't run this code.

59
00:04:52,270 --> 00:04:57,670
So after the two if statements, I'm going to add another new if statement.

60
00:04:57,670 --> 00:04:59,140
This is not going to be a branch.

61
00:04:59,140 --> 00:05:00,580
It's not going to be an LF.

62
00:05:00,580 --> 00:05:02,360
It's going to be another if.

63
00:05:02,380 --> 00:05:09,760
Because as we know, whenever one of these conditions get executed, the other will not get executed.

64
00:05:09,880 --> 00:05:16,990
So if I add this as a branch, it will never get executed if any of the top two branches get executed.

65
00:05:17,440 --> 00:05:25,360
But I'm going to add a new statement so that this statement will always be processed by Python regardless

66
00:05:25,360 --> 00:05:26,860
of what happened before it.

67
00:05:27,430 --> 00:05:36,370
So in my statement, I'm going to check if the load is not equal to my skippy packet.

68
00:05:36,370 --> 00:05:38,500
Skip your dot load.

69
00:05:38,500 --> 00:05:44,890
So if it's not equal to the load field of the wrong layer of this CP packet.

70
00:05:45,580 --> 00:05:51,220
Now if this is the case, we're going to need to execute these two lines in here.

71
00:05:51,220 --> 00:05:52,600
So I'm going to cut them.

72
00:05:54,150 --> 00:05:58,560
Pace them here and I'm going to remove them from here as well.

73
00:06:00,190 --> 00:06:02,530
Let's have a quick look on what we did so far.

74
00:06:02,680 --> 00:06:09,790
So we are creating a new variable here where are calling this variable load and we're setting the value

75
00:06:09,790 --> 00:06:14,920
of this variable to the load field of the row layer of this CP packet.

76
00:06:15,430 --> 00:06:20,100
Then we have an if statement that checks whether this is a request or response.

77
00:06:20,140 --> 00:06:24,040
And based on that, it might modify the load.

78
00:06:24,040 --> 00:06:26,320
So we're not sure if the load gets modified.

79
00:06:26,650 --> 00:06:33,970
Then at the bottom here we have an if statement and notice that this if statement uses the same variables

80
00:06:33,970 --> 00:06:35,020
used in here.

81
00:06:35,080 --> 00:06:39,040
So it's basically checking if the load has changed.

82
00:06:39,340 --> 00:06:46,120
So we're using the escalation equal to say if this is not the same as this.

83
00:06:46,120 --> 00:06:53,140
So we're saying if the load now is not the same as the load field in the raw layer of this CPP packet.

84
00:06:54,010 --> 00:06:59,560
So at the start, we set the value of the load to the value of the load in the row field.

85
00:06:59,740 --> 00:07:05,620
We execute that if statement that might change the value of the load and then we check if the value

86
00:07:05,620 --> 00:07:06,520
got changed.

87
00:07:07,210 --> 00:07:14,890
If if it did get changed, then I want to set the load of my packet to the new load that I just modified.

88
00:07:15,630 --> 00:07:16,630
And that's it.

89
00:07:16,650 --> 00:07:19,050
So you can see now I'm not reusing my code.

90
00:07:19,050 --> 00:07:24,140
And if I ever wanted to modify the logic in here, I won't have to modify it twice.

91
00:07:24,150 --> 00:07:26,220
I'll only have to modify it once.

92
00:07:27,510 --> 00:07:33,240
Now I'm also going to remove this print statement from here because I'm really done debugging the request

93
00:07:33,240 --> 00:07:39,480
and I'm going to keep the print statement in here and the response because I still want to debug the

94
00:07:39,480 --> 00:07:45,180
response because as I remember as you remember, I said that this code is not perfect and it still doesn't

95
00:07:45,180 --> 00:07:47,130
work against all websites.

96
00:07:48,000 --> 00:07:54,930
Now let's go ahead and test my code and make sure that whatever I modified so far did not break my code.

97
00:07:55,440 --> 00:07:58,560
So I'm just going to run my program.

98
00:07:58,890 --> 00:08:04,440
I've already run my IP tables rules and let's just go to being dot com.

99
00:08:07,130 --> 00:08:14,170
And as you can see, code is working perfect and whatever I modify did not make my code less effective.

100
00:08:14,180 --> 00:08:15,980
Still working as expected.

101
00:08:16,490 --> 00:08:18,190
So that's it for this lecture.

102
00:08:18,200 --> 00:08:23,810
Just wanted to show you how to kind of tidy up this code and make it look better because we're actually

103
00:08:23,810 --> 00:08:28,640
going to spend a little bit more time on this and you'll see why in the next lectures.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,750 --> 00:00:07,830
So now we have a pretty good program that we can use to inject code into any website that a person might

2
00:00:07,830 --> 00:00:10,710
load while we are the man in the middle.

3
00:00:11,370 --> 00:00:13,590
Now there is a problem with this code.

4
00:00:13,950 --> 00:00:17,370
That problem is it wouldn't work with every website.

5
00:00:17,730 --> 00:00:19,260
Let me show you an example.

6
00:00:19,350 --> 00:00:24,000
So we know now if we load big dotcom, so I'm just going to do a refresh here.

7
00:00:24,420 --> 00:00:30,470
You'll see that my JavaScript code will get injected and the code will get executed.

8
00:00:30,480 --> 00:00:35,340
And since we're using an alert code, we're seeing this dialog message saying test.

9
00:00:36,060 --> 00:00:43,530
Now the reason why this code is getting executed is because using our code in here, we are replacing

10
00:00:43,530 --> 00:00:48,540
anybody tag with our script followed by the body tag.

11
00:00:49,290 --> 00:00:56,910
So if I go down on this page that we just loaded and right click and view page source, this will show

12
00:00:56,910 --> 00:01:00,660
me the code of the page that's being loaded in the browser.

13
00:01:00,810 --> 00:01:09,510
And if I zoom in you'll see at the end we have the body tag and the code that we used, which is the

14
00:01:09,510 --> 00:01:10,400
script code.

15
00:01:10,410 --> 00:01:13,680
The alert code is being injected right here.

16
00:01:14,190 --> 00:01:17,800
Now all of this is perfect and we're happy with the way it's working.

17
00:01:17,820 --> 00:01:23,940
And like I said, we can use this method to inject anything within this page and to inject cooler stuff

18
00:01:23,950 --> 00:01:25,380
than than the alert code.

19
00:01:25,380 --> 00:01:27,660
And I'll show you that later on in the course.

20
00:01:28,560 --> 00:01:32,340
The problem now is that we just highlight where I am now.

21
00:01:32,340 --> 00:01:33,240
So I know.

22
00:01:34,020 --> 00:01:39,420
And let me go to another website and let's go to WinZip Dotcom.

23
00:01:39,570 --> 00:01:47,250
Now keep in mind I'm only visiting HTTP websites because this will not work against HTTPS and I'll address

24
00:01:47,250 --> 00:01:50,370
https later on in the course as well.

25
00:01:50,730 --> 00:01:56,550
Now as you can see, this page loaded, but our code didn't get executed.

26
00:01:57,090 --> 00:02:03,240
Now, I'm 100% sure that this page does have a slash body at the end.

27
00:02:03,240 --> 00:02:08,100
So it definitely has this tag because all pages have to have it.

28
00:02:08,790 --> 00:02:11,850
So let's have a look on the source of this page.

29
00:02:13,090 --> 00:02:15,100
And see why this didn't work.

30
00:02:15,780 --> 00:02:20,490
So I'm going to scroll all the way down because the slash body is always at the end of the page.

31
00:02:20,910 --> 00:02:23,010
And I'm going to zoom in for you.

32
00:02:24,530 --> 00:02:30,680
And if you look at here at the bottom, you'll see that we have the start of our script, we have the

33
00:02:30,680 --> 00:02:35,590
alert and we have the first letter, which is the T and then there is nothing.

34
00:02:35,600 --> 00:02:41,690
There is no end, there is no slash body, there is no slash HTML, which is again, it's essential

35
00:02:41,690 --> 00:02:44,060
for the end of the HTML page.

36
00:02:44,870 --> 00:02:51,920
So basically what's happening is when the browser is requesting this page, the page is loading, it's

37
00:02:51,920 --> 00:02:58,190
receiving all the markup, all the HTML up to the point where we inject our code.

38
00:02:58,670 --> 00:03:04,130
And once we inject our code, some of the code is getting injected and the rest isn't.

39
00:03:04,130 --> 00:03:10,790
So basically the connection is getting stopped and terminated and the rest of the HTML code is not getting

40
00:03:10,790 --> 00:03:11,450
loaded.

41
00:03:12,290 --> 00:03:18,410
So basically something is going wrong in the communication between the browser and the web server and

42
00:03:18,410 --> 00:03:21,530
that's stopping the code from being received.

43
00:03:22,010 --> 00:03:27,800
Now, in order to understand what's happening, let's have a look on the response that we get after

44
00:03:27,800 --> 00:03:29,690
we request winzip dot com.

45
00:03:30,760 --> 00:03:37,120
Now this is the first response to the request of loading the homepage of WinZip Dotcom.

46
00:03:37,330 --> 00:03:40,990
You can see that it's a 200 K, which means that it can.

47
00:03:40,990 --> 00:03:46,930
The server is still in the client or the web browser here that I can respond to your request.

48
00:03:47,080 --> 00:03:49,360
It's given us a HTML page.

49
00:03:50,230 --> 00:03:56,800
And if you look in here and one of the headers, we have something called content length and you can

50
00:03:56,800 --> 00:03:58,720
see that this has a value.

51
00:03:59,410 --> 00:04:06,910
Now the use of the content length in the HTTP header is to specify the size of the response.

52
00:04:06,910 --> 00:04:14,710
So it's basically the size of the HTML page that we're going to receive, which is this page right here.

53
00:04:15,190 --> 00:04:21,250
Now, the problem is the first thing that the server is telling us is I'm going to send you a page of

54
00:04:21,250 --> 00:04:22,180
this size.

55
00:04:22,780 --> 00:04:29,740
And I'm saying, okay, and I'm receiving all the page and everything is fine all the way down until

56
00:04:29,740 --> 00:04:32,410
something gets injected in this page.

57
00:04:32,620 --> 00:04:37,750
Now, when this gets injected in the page, this will modify the size of the page.

58
00:04:37,750 --> 00:04:43,240
And because the size of the page is getting modified, the browser said, Oh, wait, something's going

59
00:04:43,240 --> 00:04:43,690
wrong.

60
00:04:43,720 --> 00:04:47,250
This is not the same size that you told me that you're going to send me.

61
00:04:47,260 --> 00:04:51,190
Then I'm going to cut the connection and not load the page anymore.

62
00:04:52,000 --> 00:04:55,150
So basically the main problem is the web server.

63
00:04:55,150 --> 00:05:01,750
Before it sends the web page, it's telling the web browser or still in the client that I'm going to

64
00:05:01,750 --> 00:05:07,780
send you a page of a specific size, and it's given the exact size of the page.

65
00:05:08,440 --> 00:05:14,110
Now, because we're injecting code in this page, then the size of the page is changing, it's going

66
00:05:14,110 --> 00:05:20,410
to increase and the browser is going to detect that the size of the page is not the same size that set

67
00:05:20,410 --> 00:05:24,460
in the content length and therefore terminate the connection.

68
00:05:25,210 --> 00:05:32,230
So in order to solve this problem, we'll have to modify the value in the content length in here and

69
00:05:32,230 --> 00:05:36,260
make sure that this value corresponds to the size of the page.

70
00:05:36,280 --> 00:05:38,890
After we inject our code.

71
00:05:39,670 --> 00:05:44,590
So in the next lecture, I'm going to show you how to, first of all, detect this value, how to read

72
00:05:44,590 --> 00:05:51,190
this value using our python code, how to calculate the size of the page after we inject the code in

73
00:05:51,190 --> 00:05:59,230
it, and how to set this value to the new value and inject code even if the content length is set in

74
00:05:59,230 --> 00:06:00,910
the HTTP response.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,030 --> 00:00:01,510
Okay.

2
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:08,230
So now our algorithm is going to be, first of all, read the value of the content length, calculate

3
00:00:08,230 --> 00:00:12,160
the new value, and then set the content length to the new value.

4
00:00:12,730 --> 00:00:19,780
So the first goal now is to be able to read this value right here throughout our Python code.

5
00:00:20,230 --> 00:00:22,960
And to do this, you've probably already guessed it.

6
00:00:22,990 --> 00:00:24,730
We're going to use regex.

7
00:00:25,300 --> 00:00:31,180
And as we do this, I'm going to cover something new about regex that we haven't covered before.

8
00:00:31,720 --> 00:00:37,360
So, first of all, I'm going to select all of this and copy it.

9
00:00:38,960 --> 00:00:40,890
And we're going to go to politics.

10
00:00:40,910 --> 00:00:48,710
We're going to paste all of this in my string, and I'll just remove this from the top in here, and

11
00:00:48,710 --> 00:00:52,850
then we'll try to build our regular expression.

12
00:00:53,390 --> 00:00:58,910
So the first thing that we know is before the interesting number, this is the number that we're interested

13
00:00:58,910 --> 00:00:59,480
in.

14
00:00:59,480 --> 00:01:04,340
And we know this number will be preceded by this string right here.

15
00:01:04,340 --> 00:01:07,190
So it will be preceded by content length.

16
00:01:07,820 --> 00:01:14,780
So I'm going to copy this and paste it in my regex and this will straight away highlight content length

17
00:01:14,780 --> 00:01:17,030
for me, so it will match this for me.

18
00:01:17,750 --> 00:01:24,230
We also know that this will be followed by a space so we can use backward slash se to highlight a space.

19
00:01:24,680 --> 00:01:29,600
And then we know that this will be followed by a number of digits.

20
00:01:29,870 --> 00:01:35,780
So if you look at the cheat sheet at the bottom, you'll see to match a digit, you're going to use

21
00:01:35,780 --> 00:01:37,940
a backward slash dx.

22
00:01:39,400 --> 00:01:46,750
And as you can see now, we're matching the first digit of the content length, but we actually want

23
00:01:46,750 --> 00:01:48,480
the whole number in here.

24
00:01:48,490 --> 00:01:52,560
So we're going to say that I can have any number of digits after that.

25
00:01:52,570 --> 00:01:55,060
So we're using this start as a wild card.

26
00:01:55,060 --> 00:02:02,080
And as you can see now, it's match in content length followed by the whole digits in here, which is

27
00:02:02,080 --> 00:02:05,260
the actual value that we're interested in.

28
00:02:05,980 --> 00:02:14,020
So I'm going to copy all of this and I'm going to go to my python code and I'm going to search for the

29
00:02:14,020 --> 00:02:19,180
content length by, first of all, saying content length.

30
00:02:19,900 --> 00:02:24,730
Search is equal to re dot search.

31
00:02:24,730 --> 00:02:26,680
And this is something that I covered before.

32
00:02:26,680 --> 00:02:31,690
So if I'm going to click, if you don't understand what I'm doing, then please go back and revise the

33
00:02:31,690 --> 00:02:38,350
first time that we use regex before because this is basic regex stuff and the first input to this is

34
00:02:38,350 --> 00:02:43,060
going to be my regex rule, which is the rule that I just copied.

35
00:02:43,990 --> 00:02:49,000
Followed by the strength to search RN and in my case, it's the load.

36
00:02:50,780 --> 00:02:56,960
Now the next thing that we'll do, because some responses will not contain a content length, we need

37
00:02:56,960 --> 00:03:01,550
to make sure that this search did actually return a result.

38
00:03:01,700 --> 00:03:09,170
So we're going to say if we have a content length search, so if the search was successful, then I'm

39
00:03:09,170 --> 00:03:12,950
going to create a new variable and I'm going to call this content length.

40
00:03:14,550 --> 00:03:22,830
And this variable is going to be equal to the content length search dot group zero, which means that

41
00:03:22,830 --> 00:03:27,300
it's the first thing that you match out of the whole string.

42
00:03:27,690 --> 00:03:30,000
Now, I covered all of this before.

43
00:03:30,030 --> 00:03:36,660
I haven't done anything new right now, and let's just print the content length.

44
00:03:37,980 --> 00:03:44,100
And what I'm actually going to do now, I'm going to remove this or I'll just comment it just so that

45
00:03:44,100 --> 00:03:46,620
we don't have too much output on the screen.

46
00:03:47,310 --> 00:03:53,190
And basically what we're doing now is we're using a regex rule to look for the content length.

47
00:03:53,220 --> 00:03:56,220
This will match all of this right here.

48
00:03:56,550 --> 00:03:58,950
And then we're going to print it on screen.

49
00:03:59,670 --> 00:04:01,290
So let's go down.

50
00:04:01,590 --> 00:04:04,320
Let's cancel this, clear it.

51
00:04:04,740 --> 00:04:06,390
Run my code again.

52
00:04:06,480 --> 00:04:10,590
And let's just go and refresh the Web page in here.

53
00:04:12,070 --> 00:04:13,090
Okay, perfect.

54
00:04:13,090 --> 00:04:19,720
So you can see every time we have a content length and the response, it's being printed on screen in

55
00:04:19,720 --> 00:04:23,110
here and basically all of this right here.

56
00:04:23,110 --> 00:04:27,120
So content length call on 166.

57
00:04:27,130 --> 00:04:30,730
All of this is stored in my content length.

58
00:04:32,090 --> 00:04:40,880
Now, as I said, my goal now is to match the number and then recalculate the new content length.

59
00:04:40,880 --> 00:04:45,930
So what I actually want to match is only this number right here.

60
00:04:45,950 --> 00:04:53,330
I don't want to match the content length at the start of the regular expression, so I only want to

61
00:04:53,330 --> 00:04:57,770
be able to store this value in my variable and I'm not interested in this.

62
00:04:57,770 --> 00:05:04,340
I don't need this in my variable because if I have this, then I can't use this value to add or subtract

63
00:05:04,340 --> 00:05:04,970
stuff.

64
00:05:05,390 --> 00:05:08,180
And this is where things will be a little bit different.

65
00:05:08,180 --> 00:05:13,640
And this is what I meant when I said that I'm going to cover something new and regex in this lecture.

66
00:05:14,300 --> 00:05:20,900
So what I want to do is I want to be able to access the value that is being matched by this part of

67
00:05:20,900 --> 00:05:25,850
the regular expression and not by the whole regular expression in here.

68
00:05:26,450 --> 00:05:30,380
And to do this, we're going to use a non matching word.

69
00:05:31,440 --> 00:05:33,600
So let's go to politics.

70
00:05:33,870 --> 00:05:41,670
And if we look at the cheat sheet, you'll see that we can mark a group as non capturing.

71
00:05:42,030 --> 00:05:48,900
And basically what it means is that this group will be used to locate the interesting part, but it

72
00:05:48,900 --> 00:05:52,620
will not be part of the regular expression that will capture.

73
00:05:52,740 --> 00:05:54,750
And this is exactly what we want.

74
00:05:54,780 --> 00:06:01,980
We want the content length to be used to locate the actual value of the content length, but we don't

75
00:06:01,980 --> 00:06:05,670
want it to be part of the string that gets captured.

76
00:06:06,210 --> 00:06:13,110
So to do this, all we have to do is highlight the content length as a non capturing part of the regular

77
00:06:13,110 --> 00:06:19,080
expression, and you can highlight it by adding a question mark followed by a colon.

78
00:06:19,620 --> 00:06:27,030
So let's go back and the first thing that I'm going to do to my regular expression is separated into

79
00:06:27,030 --> 00:06:27,960
two groups.

80
00:06:27,960 --> 00:06:35,100
So I have the first group, which is the group that is going to be used to locate my value but will

81
00:06:35,100 --> 00:06:37,560
not be part of the value that I capture.

82
00:06:37,560 --> 00:06:44,100
And this is going to be my content length colon space, and then I'm going to close this group.

83
00:06:44,100 --> 00:06:48,240
So this is going to be my first group that is going to be non capturing.

84
00:06:48,240 --> 00:06:50,490
That is not important for me.

85
00:06:51,150 --> 00:06:58,980
And what I'm interested in is the series of digits that follow this group and this is being highlighted

86
00:06:58,980 --> 00:07:01,920
here and the backward slash DX star.

87
00:07:01,920 --> 00:07:06,510
So any number of digits that come after this non capturing part.

88
00:07:06,870 --> 00:07:12,510
And again, I'm going to separate this into another group by surrounding it with brackets.

89
00:07:13,200 --> 00:07:20,790
So now all I did is I separated my regular expression into two groups, Group one and Group two.

90
00:07:21,300 --> 00:07:25,860
Now I want to tell Python that the first group is a non capturing group.

91
00:07:25,860 --> 00:07:30,360
You can use this group to locate my string, the string that's important for me.

92
00:07:30,360 --> 00:07:35,100
But this group, I don't want it to be part of the value that you capture.

93
00:07:35,340 --> 00:07:42,420
So to do that as shown in the cheat sheet, all I have to do is put a question mark followed by a colon.

94
00:07:43,260 --> 00:07:49,080
This will tell Python that look for something that's called content strength, followed by a column

95
00:07:49,080 --> 00:07:55,260
followed by space, but don't include that in the regular expression that you capture.

96
00:07:55,350 --> 00:08:02,130
If this is followed by a series of digits, then the series of digits or the second group is the part

97
00:08:02,130 --> 00:08:03,420
that you should capture.

98
00:08:04,260 --> 00:08:11,640
And we'll be able to capture or access this second group by saying Group one instead of group zero.

99
00:08:11,760 --> 00:08:15,570
So Group zero will actually return everything, just like we've seen before.

100
00:08:15,600 --> 00:08:20,550
Group one will only return the number, the content length.

101
00:08:21,180 --> 00:08:24,840
So I'm going to save this and let's go around my code.

102
00:08:25,960 --> 00:08:27,210
And refresh the page.

103
00:08:27,220 --> 00:08:34,990
Now remember, the content length was returned as content length followed by the integer or by the number.

104
00:08:35,470 --> 00:08:41,350
But now, as you can see, even without me refreshing the page, or if I refresh it right here, you'll

105
00:08:41,350 --> 00:08:45,490
see I'll get the content length on its own as a number.

106
00:08:46,330 --> 00:08:53,290
Now, this is very, very important because in the next steps I want to be able to use this number in

107
00:08:53,290 --> 00:08:56,620
order to recalculate the new content length.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,060 --> 00:00:01,740
Okay.

2
00:00:01,750 --> 00:00:09,460
Now that we know the content length variable right here contains the actual value of the size of the

3
00:00:09,460 --> 00:00:14,290
page or the value that's being sent as the content length in the response.

4
00:00:14,620 --> 00:00:22,630
Now what we want to do is we want to recalculate the content length or the size of the page after we

5
00:00:22,630 --> 00:00:25,210
inject this code in the page.

6
00:00:25,900 --> 00:00:32,110
So first of all, I'm actually going to cut this and I'm going to put this in a variable, and let's

7
00:00:32,110 --> 00:00:35,380
call this variable injection code.

8
00:00:36,790 --> 00:00:40,360
And we'll make this equal to whatever we just copied.

9
00:00:40,720 --> 00:00:47,350
And then when we're doing the replace, this will be the injection code, plus the body.

10
00:00:48,280 --> 00:00:55,930
Now, the reason why I did this is because the new size of the page is going to be whatever value we

11
00:00:55,930 --> 00:01:00,850
have in the content length plus the size of the injection code.

12
00:01:01,360 --> 00:01:08,410
So to calculate this, it's going to be very easy going to remove this and we're going to say the new

13
00:01:08,410 --> 00:01:09,580
content length.

14
00:01:11,890 --> 00:01:20,470
Is going to be equal to my content length, so to the current content length plus the size of my injection

15
00:01:20,470 --> 00:01:21,140
code.

16
00:01:21,160 --> 00:01:29,080
And to get the size of the injection code or the size of any string in Python, then you do land and

17
00:01:29,080 --> 00:01:33,190
put the variable that you want to get the size of between two brackets.

18
00:01:33,190 --> 00:01:38,320
And the variable that I want to get the size of is the injection code.

19
00:01:39,580 --> 00:01:46,090
Now, one thing you need to keep in mind is the way that we're getting the content length, we are getting

20
00:01:46,090 --> 00:01:51,290
it from a string, so we're getting it from a series of characters.

21
00:01:51,310 --> 00:01:58,900
Therefore, Python still thinks that if we look at it in here, it still thinks that this is a series

22
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:02,740
of characters and it doesn't think that this is a number.

23
00:02:02,890 --> 00:02:09,700
So I need to specifically tell Python to treat this variable as a number, as an integer.

24
00:02:10,150 --> 00:02:17,710
So as I showed you before, we have to say end and put the variable between two brackets.

25
00:02:18,960 --> 00:02:26,040
And this will tell Python that I want you to treat whatever value stored in this variable as an integer,

26
00:02:26,040 --> 00:02:26,940
as a number.

27
00:02:27,690 --> 00:02:34,080
So basically all we're saying so far is we're saying the new content length is equal to the original

28
00:02:34,080 --> 00:02:38,250
content length plus the length of the injection code.

29
00:02:38,310 --> 00:02:43,260
And I'm set in my injection code in here to the script that I'm injecting.

30
00:02:43,980 --> 00:02:50,280
Now, all we have to do is change the load to account for the new content length.

31
00:02:50,310 --> 00:02:54,540
So if I just go back to Python, I still have the load in here.

32
00:02:54,540 --> 00:03:01,140
And as you remember, the content length is set in the headers using the content length header followed

33
00:03:01,140 --> 00:03:03,510
by this value right here.

34
00:03:04,200 --> 00:03:13,410
So you can use regex to replace this or an even easier way is we can just say the load.

35
00:03:14,390 --> 00:03:21,440
Is equal to the load that replace and I want to replace the content length.

36
00:03:21,440 --> 00:03:29,180
So remember the content length now only refers to the value of the content length, so it's literally

37
00:03:29,180 --> 00:03:30,950
just the number on its own.

38
00:03:31,550 --> 00:03:36,050
So I want to replace this number with the new content length.

39
00:03:36,980 --> 00:03:37,640
And that's it.

40
00:03:37,640 --> 00:03:43,400
I don't need to reset the packet or set the payload of the packet because this if statement that we

41
00:03:43,400 --> 00:03:47,510
wrote before will automatically detect that the load has been modified.

42
00:03:47,510 --> 00:03:54,980
And since the load has been modified, it'll set the payload in my packet to the new load and everything

43
00:03:54,980 --> 00:03:56,540
should work as expected.

44
00:03:57,560 --> 00:04:05,390
Now, one thing that we need to revisit actually is the new content length in here is an integer.

45
00:04:05,480 --> 00:04:13,850
Because remember, we converted the string in here to an integer to a number and the value returned

46
00:04:13,850 --> 00:04:19,100
by the length function in here is going to be a number and we're adding two numbers together.

47
00:04:19,100 --> 00:04:25,760
So the value in the new content length variable is going to be a number or an integer type.

48
00:04:26,180 --> 00:04:28,640
So we can't really use an integer.

49
00:04:28,640 --> 00:04:36,170
And here in my replace because as we know, the replace replaces a substring or a small string within

50
00:04:36,170 --> 00:04:37,160
a bigger string.

51
00:04:37,160 --> 00:04:40,400
So it replaces a small text within a bigger text.

52
00:04:40,640 --> 00:04:47,600
And because the new content length is a number, I need to tell Python to treat this number as a string

53
00:04:47,600 --> 00:04:49,850
or as characters or text.

54
00:04:50,610 --> 00:05:01,290
So again, with Python to do this, you have to type a star and surround this by brackets and we are

55
00:05:01,290 --> 00:05:01,920
done.

56
00:05:02,370 --> 00:05:08,850
So just to revisit the conversions here, because we did quite a lot of converting between strings and

57
00:05:08,850 --> 00:05:15,570
integers, we know strings are series of characters or text, and we know integers are numbers.

58
00:05:15,900 --> 00:05:21,750
So whenever we're doing our search in here, when we're searching for the content length, we're searching

59
00:05:21,750 --> 00:05:24,070
in the load, which is a string.

60
00:05:24,090 --> 00:05:31,050
So whatever value we get in the search result, even if the value is numbers, Python will think this

61
00:05:31,050 --> 00:05:31,980
is a string.

62
00:05:32,010 --> 00:05:33,870
It will think that this is text.

63
00:05:34,530 --> 00:05:39,990
So because we're going to use this in a mathematical equation here, because we're going to be adding

64
00:05:39,990 --> 00:05:46,740
it or summing it with another number, we have to tell Python to treat this variable as a number.

65
00:05:47,010 --> 00:05:51,480
And since we're adding two numbers, the result is going to be a number.

66
00:05:51,720 --> 00:05:58,920
Then I want to use this number inside a string, inside a series of characters, inside the text.

67
00:05:58,920 --> 00:06:05,790
So I need to tell Python to treat this number as a string or as a series of characters.

68
00:06:05,790 --> 00:06:10,260
And I'm doing this by surrounding the variable with a star.

69
00:06:11,070 --> 00:06:12,550
Now this is all done.

70
00:06:12,570 --> 00:06:15,450
Let's go down and test our code.

71
00:06:15,990 --> 00:06:20,940
So I'm going to close this and close this and delete my history.

72
00:06:21,150 --> 00:06:22,740
And let's go to WinZip.

73
00:06:26,100 --> 00:06:26,940
And perfect.

74
00:06:26,940 --> 00:06:27,840
As you can see.

75
00:06:27,840 --> 00:06:34,380
Now we're able to inject code in WinZip, which is a website that we couldn't inject code in before

76
00:06:34,560 --> 00:06:38,280
due to the use of the content length in this website.

77
00:06:38,280 --> 00:06:39,840
And this is just one example.

78
00:06:39,840 --> 00:06:46,380
So a lot of websites use the content length and our code wouldn't have worked for these websites without

79
00:06:46,380 --> 00:06:48,870
recalculating the content length.

80
00:06:49,680 --> 00:06:51,930
So we're going to click on Okay.

81
00:06:51,930 --> 00:06:55,530
And let's actually look at the source very quickly.

82
00:06:58,300 --> 00:06:59,920
And go to the bottom.

83
00:07:00,070 --> 00:07:02,140
And as you can see in here.

84
00:07:03,140 --> 00:07:08,360
The code is getting injected right before the body tag at the correct place.

85
00:07:08,360 --> 00:07:15,110
And if you recall, when the code wasn't working, it was being injected up to here and then the connection

86
00:07:15,110 --> 00:07:21,830
was being terminated because the size of the page is different than the size that was being set in the

87
00:07:21,830 --> 00:07:22,910
content length.

88
00:07:23,540 --> 00:07:28,310
Now let's also make sure that this code still works against Bing and other websites.

89
00:07:28,310 --> 00:07:37,580
So if I go to Booking.com, you'll see that it actually doesn't work and it's given us a bad request.

90
00:07:38,300 --> 00:07:44,300
Now, I actually know what the reason for this is, but I wanted to show you so that you actually go

91
00:07:44,300 --> 00:07:48,320
through the same experience that I went through when I wrote the script.

92
00:07:48,980 --> 00:07:54,200
So if we just go back and look at the response that we were getting and I have this stored here in my

93
00:07:54,200 --> 00:07:59,720
python x, you'll see that we are replacing the content length with the new content length.

94
00:08:00,170 --> 00:08:07,130
And the reason why we're doing this is because we're injecting HTML pages with my script.

95
00:08:07,610 --> 00:08:16,940
Now, the problem is the content length in here is not only used in responses for HTML pages, for example,

96
