00:08:16,940 --> 00:08:26,150
the server might be sending a text or a JavaScript or a CSS or an image with a content length header.

97
00:08:26,570 --> 00:08:33,980
Now, because this is an image, it will not have a body tag and therefore my JavaScript will not be

98
00:08:33,980 --> 00:08:35,030
injected.

99
00:08:35,030 --> 00:08:42,230
And then I'm going to have incorrect content length because my code in here would recalculate the content

100
00:08:42,230 --> 00:08:45,710
length, assuming that this code will be injected.

101
00:08:46,040 --> 00:08:54,110
But this code will only be injected if a body tag is found in the response and in images and CSS files

102
00:08:54,110 --> 00:08:57,260
and JavaScript files, there is no body tag.

103
00:08:57,260 --> 00:09:03,560
So this code, the JavaScript code will not be injected, but I'm still going to modify the content

104
00:09:03,560 --> 00:09:07,520
length which will cause this error that I'm seeing right here.

105
00:09:08,060 --> 00:09:15,110
So what I want to do, I need to make sure that this code right here, which is the code that re calculates

106
00:09:15,110 --> 00:09:22,790
the content length, is only executed if the response contains a HTML page.

107
00:09:23,360 --> 00:09:29,510
Now, if we go back and look at the response, you'll see that this is very easy to check for because

108
00:09:29,750 --> 00:09:36,440
HTML responses will actually say that the content type is text HTML.

109
00:09:36,920 --> 00:09:42,740
So all I need to do is literally copy this and go to my if statement in here.

110
00:09:42,740 --> 00:09:51,620
So I'm checking if there is a content length in the response and I'm also going to check if text HTML

111
00:09:51,980 --> 00:09:53,630
is in my load.

112
00:09:55,130 --> 00:10:02,540
Therefore, if the load does not contain text or HTML, if it does not contain this string, then I

113
00:10:02,540 --> 00:10:05,480
will not recalculate the content length.

114
00:10:06,170 --> 00:10:08,930
So let's go down and test this again.

115
00:10:10,960 --> 00:10:16,240
Delete my history and go to dot com and this will work.

116
00:10:16,240 --> 00:10:23,140
That's perfect because now we're not calculating the content length for any other files other than HTML

117
00:10:23,140 --> 00:10:23,830
files.

118
00:10:23,830 --> 00:10:29,260
Bing doesn't even send the content length, so none of my new code is really getting executed.

119
00:10:30,130 --> 00:10:36,880
Now if we go to WinZip, which actually sends the content length for HTML pages, my code should calculate

120
00:10:36,880 --> 00:10:41,020
the content length because the HTML page is sent in this content length.

121
00:10:41,020 --> 00:10:47,380
And as you can see, the JavaScript code is getting executed successfully on the Target web page.

122
00:10:48,910 --> 00:10:52,360
And this brings us to the end of the lecture.

123
00:10:52,450 --> 00:10:58,930
Now our code is able to inject code in any HTML page that gets loaded.

124
00:10:59,470 --> 00:11:07,000
We managed to do this by recalculating the content size only for HTTP pages.

125
00:11:07,090 --> 00:11:10,290
And as we did this, we learned more about regex.

126
00:11:10,300 --> 00:11:15,100
We learned how to divide it into groups and use a non capturing group.

127
00:11:15,310 --> 00:11:23,200
We learned more about HTTP requests and the content size and more about Python and organizing our code

128
00:11:23,200 --> 00:11:24,040
in general.

129
00:11:24,700 --> 00:11:29,290
Now, everything we did so far works against HTTP pages only.

130
00:11:29,290 --> 00:11:33,610
So make sure if you are testing, you test against HTTP pages.

131
00:11:33,790 --> 00:11:37,570
I will address https later on in the course.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,320 --> 00:00:06,780
In this lecture, I'd like to introduce you to a really cool framework called Beef.

2
00:00:07,290 --> 00:00:11,100
Beef is short for the browser exploitation framework.

3
00:00:11,370 --> 00:00:18,600
It allows us to run a number of attacks on hooked browsers that would allow us to further exploit the

4
00:00:18,600 --> 00:00:25,410
target system to steal passwords or even gain full access or full control over the system.

5
00:00:26,220 --> 00:00:33,060
Now let's see how to install it, and then we'll talk about how to hook targets to it and run attacks

6
00:00:33,060 --> 00:00:34,290
on these targets.

7
00:00:34,680 --> 00:00:41,250
So right here I'm at my card machine and as usual, before installing anything, we're going to update

8
00:00:41,250 --> 00:00:42,030
our sources.

9
00:00:42,030 --> 00:00:44,010
So I'm going to do apt get update.

10
00:00:47,450 --> 00:00:48,890
And to install a package.

11
00:00:48,890 --> 00:00:56,450
Again, as usual, we do have to get installed followed by the package name and in my case I want to

12
00:00:56,450 --> 00:00:59,490
install a package called Beef X.

13
00:01:02,140 --> 00:01:04,590
Now it's asking us, do we really want to do this?

14
00:01:04,600 --> 00:01:05,890
I'm going to say yes.

15
00:01:06,400 --> 00:01:12,490
And this will install all the dependencies, all the libraries needed by B and install and configure

16
00:01:12,490 --> 00:01:15,280
B for us and perfect.

17
00:01:15,310 --> 00:01:17,050
Now we see no errors on screen.

18
00:01:17,050 --> 00:01:19,540
This means that it got installed properly.

19
00:01:19,540 --> 00:01:22,630
So now we can go ahead and start Vive.

20
00:01:22,780 --> 00:01:26,590
And to do that I'm going to click on all my applications.

21
00:01:26,920 --> 00:01:28,540
I'm going to type B.

22
00:01:29,750 --> 00:01:32,630
And I'm going to click on the first icon in here.

23
00:01:32,660 --> 00:01:33,950
Beef Start.

24
00:01:34,070 --> 00:01:36,140
You can use the beef stop to stop it.

25
00:01:36,140 --> 00:01:38,750
But right now we just want to start the framework.

26
00:01:38,750 --> 00:01:45,140
So we're going to click on the start now because this is the first time we run the framework.

27
00:01:45,140 --> 00:01:49,040
It's asking us to set a password for the default user.

28
00:01:49,160 --> 00:01:52,100
So go ahead and pick any password you want.

29
00:01:52,370 --> 00:01:57,230
And note, when you type the password, you will not see the characters on screen.

30
00:01:57,260 --> 00:02:02,650
This is a security feature so that the people around you will not see your password.

31
00:02:02,660 --> 00:02:07,430
So right now I actually typed my password, but you can't see anything on screen.

32
00:02:07,520 --> 00:02:14,480
I'm just going to hit enter and then it will automatically start the beef framework for me and it will

33
00:02:14,480 --> 00:02:22,670
automatically start Firefox and go to the web interface that we can use to access beef and control the

34
00:02:22,670 --> 00:02:23,750
hooked browsers.

35
00:02:24,710 --> 00:02:31,280
As you can see, the first thing that will ask us is a username and password to log in to this web interface.

36
00:02:31,460 --> 00:02:39,740
The default user is B, B, E, F, and the password is the password that we just said in terminal.

37
00:02:41,080 --> 00:02:42,970
I'm going to hit enter to log in.

38
00:02:43,450 --> 00:02:48,730
And as you can see, we have the main user interface of beef.

39
00:02:49,930 --> 00:02:54,790
The main thing you can see here on the left is the online and the offline browsers.

40
00:02:54,970 --> 00:03:01,000
In the online browsers, you'll see the browsers that are hooked to beef right now that you can control.

41
00:03:01,000 --> 00:03:07,030
And in the offline browsers, you'll see the browsers that you previously were able to control.

42
00:03:07,030 --> 00:03:10,630
So browsers that you previously hooked to beef.

43
00:03:11,500 --> 00:03:18,700
Now, in order to hook a browser to beef and be able to control it and run commands on it, you have

44
00:03:18,700 --> 00:03:23,500
to get that browser to execute a specific JavaScript code.

45
00:03:23,680 --> 00:03:31,000
So if we go back to the terminal window here that started before us and just scroll up, you'll see

46
00:03:31,000 --> 00:03:36,790
that it's given us the JavaScript code that we need to use to hook browsers to beef.

47
00:03:37,780 --> 00:03:43,690
As soon as this piece of code gets executed on a web browser, you will see that web browser here in

48
00:03:43,690 --> 00:03:49,810
the online browsers, and you'll be able to execute a lot of really cool commands that will allow you

49
00:03:49,810 --> 00:03:51,670
to show fake login pages.

50
00:03:51,700 --> 00:03:57,280
It will allow you to show fake updates and even gain full control over the target system.

51
00:03:57,820 --> 00:04:05,650
Now, because beef uses JavaScript, it will work on any web browser that supports JavaScript, regardless

52
00:04:05,650 --> 00:04:08,990
of what device this web browser is running on.

53
00:04:09,010 --> 00:04:12,160
So it will basically work on all modern browsers.

54
00:04:12,160 --> 00:04:20,350
So it will work against phones, tablets, smart TVs, all operating systems used on PCs such as Linux,

55
00:04:20,350 --> 00:04:23,020
Windows, OSX and so on.

56
00:04:23,050 --> 00:04:29,200
Literally any browser that can run JavaScript will be able to run this code and therefore will be able

57
00:04:29,200 --> 00:04:34,840
to hook that browser to beef and do all of the cool things that you will learn in the next lectures.

58
00:04:35,650 --> 00:04:41,500
So right now, it really depends on you and your imagination on how you're going to get this piece of

59
00:04:41,500 --> 00:04:43,990
code to run on a web browser.

60
00:04:44,500 --> 00:04:51,070
If you're able to become the man in the middle, you can do DNS spoofing and redirect requests to a

61
00:04:51,070 --> 00:04:53,410
page that contains this hook code.

62
00:04:53,920 --> 00:05:00,910
Or you can inject this JavaScript in any web page that the target loads using the JavaScript injection

63
00:05:00,910 --> 00:05:02,220
methods that we learned.

64
00:05:02,230 --> 00:05:09,910
Once you become the man in the middle, you can also exploit an XSS vulnerability and inject this code

65
00:05:09,910 --> 00:05:14,950
in a vulnerable web page and you will learn how to do that in the website hacking section.

66
00:05:15,040 --> 00:05:22,120
Or you can use social engineering and social engineer at Target into loading a web page that contains

67
00:05:22,120 --> 00:05:23,320
our hook code.

68
00:05:23,680 --> 00:05:31,600
So all you'll need to do is think of a way to get a target to load a page that contains the Hook JavaScript

69
00:05:31,600 --> 00:05:32,170
code.

70
00:05:32,830 --> 00:05:38,410
Now in this lecture, I'm going to show you a very basic hook method that is really good for us to make

71
00:05:38,410 --> 00:05:41,470
sure that our system is working as expected.

72
00:05:41,470 --> 00:05:47,620
And this very page can also be used with method number one and method number four, because we're going

73
00:05:47,620 --> 00:05:50,440
to have a HTML page that contains the hook code.

74
00:05:50,440 --> 00:05:56,500
Therefore, you can either spoof targets to this page or social engineer them to this page.

75
00:05:56,500 --> 00:06:01,900
And once they load this page, the hook code will be executed on their browser and therefore they will

76
00:06:01,900 --> 00:06:07,060
be hooked to beef and will be able to run all of the commands that we've allow us to run.

77
00:06:07,540 --> 00:06:09,370
So let's go back to beef.

78
00:06:09,370 --> 00:06:15,040
And like I said, this is the code that we want our target browsers to load or execute.

79
00:06:15,040 --> 00:06:23,110
So I'm going to copy it and I'm going to go and open my file manager and I want to go to my web route.

80
00:06:23,110 --> 00:06:29,650
So you can either press control EL from your keyboard or click in here at the Path bar and just press

81
00:06:29,650 --> 00:06:30,850
the forward slash.

82
00:06:31,240 --> 00:06:36,190
This will open a path text box where you can type the path that you want to go to.

83
00:06:36,190 --> 00:06:41,230
And I want to go to var w w w h html.

84
00:06:42,100 --> 00:06:48,520
Now, as we learned before, this is the location where the files for your web server are stored and

85
00:06:48,520 --> 00:06:56,410
the indexed HTML is the file that gets loaded by default when someone loads your IP in their web browser.

86
00:06:56,980 --> 00:07:04,090
So I'm going to right click the indexed HTML and I'm going to choose to open it with a text editor.

87
00:07:07,740 --> 00:07:12,840
And I'm simply going to remove all of this and paste the hook code in here.

88
00:07:13,140 --> 00:07:17,250
There is one thing that I want to modify is the IP in here.

89
00:07:17,520 --> 00:07:22,080
I'm going to replace this IP with the IP of my daily machine.

90
00:07:22,350 --> 00:07:25,950
As we learned before, you can get the IP using the IFF config command.

91
00:07:25,950 --> 00:07:27,460
So I'm not going to do it right now.

92
00:07:27,480 --> 00:07:29,220
You should already know this by now.

93
00:07:29,220 --> 00:07:36,130
So I know my IP is ten 2014 to 0 seven and that's it.

94
00:07:36,150 --> 00:07:37,210
We're ready to go.

95
00:07:37,230 --> 00:07:45,330
So if anybody now loads my IP address, which is ten 2014 207, I will have a web server working on

96
00:07:45,330 --> 00:07:48,660
it which will load the index HTML by default.

97
00:07:48,660 --> 00:07:55,230
And when this file is loaded it'll load the beef hook code and it will hook that browser to beef, which

98
00:07:55,230 --> 00:07:58,680
will allow me to run a number of really cool commands.

99
00:07:59,410 --> 00:08:05,170
Now before loading this page, we need to start our web server and as we learned before, we can do

100
00:08:05,170 --> 00:08:07,870
this by doing service.

101
00:08:08,410 --> 00:08:17,530
Apache to start and now my Apache web server is running and if I go to my Windows machine in here and

102
00:08:17,530 --> 00:08:26,680
simply just go to the address of my web server, which is ten 2014 to 0 seven.

103
00:08:27,910 --> 00:08:30,310
Now, as you can see, we'll get a blank page.

104
00:08:30,310 --> 00:08:34,240
That's fine, because our page contains nothing but the code.

105
00:08:34,240 --> 00:08:39,560
You could actually have a proper HTML page and just put the hook code at the end or at the start.

106
00:08:39,580 --> 00:08:42,310
But this will do for testing for now.

107
00:08:42,760 --> 00:08:51,520
And if we go back here and go to the beef interface, if we look at the online browsers, you'll see

108
00:08:51,520 --> 00:08:56,050
we have the IP address of my target, which is ten 2014 to oh six.

109
00:08:56,050 --> 00:08:59,590
This is the IP address of the Windows machine.

110
00:08:59,680 --> 00:09:07,150
And right now if I click on it, you'll see I'll have a number of tabs in here which will allow us to

111
00:09:07,150 --> 00:09:09,940
do various things in the home.

112
00:09:09,940 --> 00:09:14,980
In the details, you'll see we have information about the browser of the target.

113
00:09:15,220 --> 00:09:21,580
For example, you can see that it doesn't have a VLC plugin installed, it supports web socket.

114
00:09:21,700 --> 00:09:26,200
If you scroll down, you can see the user agent, which is Mozilla five.

115
00:09:26,230 --> 00:09:30,460
It's running on Windows anti 64 bit architecture.

116
00:09:30,820 --> 00:09:38,770
Again, if you keep going down, you can see the cookies, you can see the host name, you can see the

117
00:09:38,770 --> 00:09:47,140
screen size and a lot of other information that can be useful when it comes to exploiting a target at

118
00:09:47,140 --> 00:09:53,410
the logs tab in here you'll see logs of all the events that happened in the browser and if you execute

119
00:09:53,410 --> 00:09:56,560
commands, you'll see logs in here for these commands.

120
00:09:58,020 --> 00:10:03,000
The command stop is the one that we're going to be using the most in the next lectures.

121
00:10:03,390 --> 00:10:06,430
This will allow you to run various commands on the target.

122
00:10:06,450 --> 00:10:10,050
Like I said, you can run information gathering commands.

123
00:10:10,050 --> 00:10:16,230
You can run commands to inject other JavaScript codes, you can run commands to steal passwords, use

124
00:10:16,350 --> 00:10:22,980
fake logins, you can run fake updates and get your target to download the backdoor and hack their computer.

125
00:10:23,010 --> 00:10:28,920
There are a lot of really cool things that you can do and I will walk you through some of the best ones

126
00:10:28,920 --> 00:10:30,240
in the next lectures.

127
00:10:31,500 --> 00:10:37,590
In the proxy tab, you can configure and use a hooked browser as a proxy.

128
00:10:38,190 --> 00:10:46,920
The x rays will show you if the hook page contains any XSS vulnerabilities and the network tab will

129
00:10:46,920 --> 00:10:49,830
give you an overview of the current network.

130
00:10:49,830 --> 00:10:53,430
So right now you can see we have the target running windows.

131
00:10:53,430 --> 00:11:00,330
We have his browser which is hooked to beef to us, and then from here we'll be able to, like I said,

132
00:11:00,330 --> 00:11:06,750
launch a number of really cool commands that can be used to do so many things and further exploit the

133
00:11:06,750 --> 00:11:07,800
target system.

134
00:11:08,740 --> 00:11:16,810
Once done with using beef, you can click on logout to log out and then go to your all applications.

135
00:11:17,410 --> 00:11:20,890
Look for beef and click on beef.

136
00:11:20,890 --> 00:11:24,700
Stop to stop the service from running in the background.

137
00:11:25,680 --> 00:11:27,150
So that's it for this lecture.

138
00:11:27,150 --> 00:11:31,260
I just wanted to give you a quick overview on beef.

139
00:11:31,260 --> 00:11:36,900
And in the next lectures we'll see how we can use it to steal credentials, get screenshots, gather

140
00:11:36,900 --> 00:11:41,610
information, and even gain full control over hooked browsers.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,270 --> 00:00:08,230
In the previous lecture, I gave you a simple introduction to beef, and we seen how to hook a target

2
00:00:08,230 --> 00:00:11,620
to beef using a very basic hook method.

3
00:00:12,400 --> 00:00:18,580
Once the computer is hooked, we can run a number of really cool commands and we'll have a look on these

4
00:00:18,580 --> 00:00:20,530
commands in the next lectures.

5
00:00:21,670 --> 00:00:27,940
Basically, the main reason why I showed you the simple hook method is so that you can test beef and

6
00:00:27,940 --> 00:00:30,040
make sure it's working properly.

7
00:00:30,190 --> 00:00:36,700
But it's not really useful in a real life scenario because we have to get the target person to load

8
00:00:36,700 --> 00:00:39,940
our web server, which has this index page.

9
00:00:40,570 --> 00:00:47,560
So in this lecture, I want to show you a more realistic way of hooking targets to beef.

10
00:00:48,100 --> 00:00:53,410
Now, as you can see, and as we learned from the previous lecture, in order to hook a target or a

11
00:00:53,410 --> 00:01:00,340
computer to beef, all we have to do is execute this JavaScript code and their browser.

12
00:01:00,940 --> 00:01:03,220
So there is a number of ways to achieve this.

13
00:01:03,220 --> 00:01:08,830
You can achieve this using X vulnerabilities, and we'll cover that later on in the course.

14
00:01:08,920 --> 00:01:14,440
You can achieve it using social engineering, and I cover this in my social engineering course.

15
00:01:14,560 --> 00:01:22,720
Or if you're the man in the middle and you have a cool program that can inject code in the HTML pages

16
00:01:22,720 --> 00:01:29,710
loaded by any computer in the same network, then you can inject this code in the page is loaded.

17
00:01:29,710 --> 00:01:36,100
The code will be executed when the page loads and this will hook any computer that is connected to your

18
00:01:36,100 --> 00:01:40,060
network to beef automatically without them knowing.

19
00:01:40,570 --> 00:01:47,830
So I think this will work as a great example to use it with our code injector tool that we have right

20
00:01:47,830 --> 00:01:48,400
here.

21
00:01:49,270 --> 00:01:55,840
Now, as we were testing this tool, we were always injecting a very simple JavaScript code that will

22
00:01:55,840 --> 00:01:58,930
literally just display a message, say, in test.

23
00:01:59,710 --> 00:02:07,000
So instead of injecting this code, I'm going to copy the code that I have right here in the index HTML

24
00:02:07,000 --> 00:02:09,370
that we used in the basic hook method.

25
00:02:10,330 --> 00:02:16,090
And I'm going to paste it in here instead of my injection code.

26
00:02:16,660 --> 00:02:23,650
Now what I'm going to do, I'm just going to use single quotes for the string variable, because inside

27
00:02:23,650 --> 00:02:28,210
my code right now I'm using double quotes and that's it.

28
00:02:28,210 --> 00:02:29,230
We're actually done.

29
00:02:29,920 --> 00:02:33,100
Now we already programmed this tool and we already know how it works.

30
00:02:33,100 --> 00:02:40,870
And basically what it's going to do is it's going to inject this code into any HTML page loaded by the

31
00:02:40,870 --> 00:02:41,500
target.

32
00:02:42,280 --> 00:02:48,430
I'm also going to test this against a remote computer, against this Windows machine, because we didn't

33
00:02:48,430 --> 00:02:52,330
have an example of testing the code injector against a remote computer.

34
00:02:52,330 --> 00:02:55,240
We were always doing it on our local machine.

35
00:02:56,390 --> 00:03:04,210
Now to run this against a remote computer, we will have to change the IP table rule that we use.

36
00:03:04,220 --> 00:03:10,100
So exactly like we did with the replaced downloads when we tested it against a remote computer.

37
00:03:10,580 --> 00:03:15,980
So first of all, I'm going to clear my IP tables by doing IP tables flush.

38
00:03:16,990 --> 00:03:25,210
Then I'm going to redirect the packets that come to my computer to my Q using this IP tables command

39
00:03:25,210 --> 00:03:27,280
and we use this before and explained it.

40
00:03:27,280 --> 00:03:29,620
That's why I'm doing this a little bit fast.

41
00:03:30,100 --> 00:03:33,610
And finally, I'm going to enable IP forwarding.

42
00:03:33,610 --> 00:03:38,500
You don't always have to do this, but I'm just making sure that it's set to one so that the packets

43
00:03:38,500 --> 00:03:41,320
will not be dropped when they flow through my computer.

44
00:03:42,040 --> 00:03:43,170
And now I'm ready.

45
00:03:43,180 --> 00:03:48,910
So the first thing that I'm going to do is run a ARP spoof to put me in the middle of the connection

46
00:03:49,000 --> 00:03:54,400
and redirect the flow of packets that this computer sent to my Carli machine.

47
00:03:54,700 --> 00:04:02,890
And then I'm going to run my code injector by doing python code injector dot spi I'm going to hit enter.

48
00:04:02,890 --> 00:04:07,630
This will work in the background and let's go to the windows machine and test this.

49
00:04:08,320 --> 00:04:13,630
Now one thing I want you to note for now, always test against HTTP pages.

50
00:04:13,630 --> 00:04:16,420
This will not work against HTTPS.

51
00:04:16,750 --> 00:04:21,970
I will show you how to get this to work against HTTPS in the next section.

52
00:04:21,970 --> 00:04:27,460
But for now, since we're testing, I want to keep everything as simple as possible, so I'm only going

53
00:04:27,460 --> 00:04:31,300
to test it against http pages now.

54
00:04:31,300 --> 00:04:36,280
Bing started using https literally while I was recording this course.

55
00:04:36,280 --> 00:04:42,220
So instead of testing this against Bing, I'm going to go to WinZip dot com.

56
00:04:43,190 --> 00:04:43,580
Now.

57
00:04:43,580 --> 00:04:45,550
WinZip does not use https.

58
00:04:45,590 --> 00:04:47,510
It uses simple http.

59
00:04:47,600 --> 00:04:53,870
Right now if you are testing this and winzip uses HTTPS by default, please test it against a normal

60
00:04:53,870 --> 00:04:58,490
HTTP page and I will show you how to bypass https later on.

61
00:04:59,330 --> 00:05:08,060
Now as you can see, the page is loading normally and if we go back and go to beef, you will see I

62
00:05:08,060 --> 00:05:15,680
have a hooked browser in here so I can connect to this by clicking on it and I can go on my commands

63
00:05:15,680 --> 00:05:20,720
and execute all of the cool commands that beef allows me to execute.

64
00:05:21,540 --> 00:05:26,220
Now, so far we still haven't seen these commands and we haven't seen how powerful they are.

65
00:05:26,250 --> 00:05:32,010
In the next lectures, I'm going to highlight the most useful commands in my opinion, but I highly

66
00:05:32,010 --> 00:05:36,900
recommend spending some time with this and experimenting with the rest of the commands.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,120 --> 00:00:08,020
So now that we have our browser or Target hooked, we can go on the commands and start executing commands

2
00:00:08,020 --> 00:00:09,130
on that target.

3
00:00:09,640 --> 00:00:14,530
You can use the search to filter looking for a certain command if you know what you're looking for.

4
00:00:14,650 --> 00:00:20,860
Or you can use the categories and look for command suitable to what you want to do on the target computer.

5
00:00:21,250 --> 00:00:23,820
Some of these commands are information gathering commands.

6
00:00:23,830 --> 00:00:25,750
Some of them are social engineering.

7
00:00:25,750 --> 00:00:29,530
Some of them will even give you full control over the target computer.

8
00:00:29,560 --> 00:00:33,100
There is a lot of commands, so I won't be able to go over all of them.

9
00:00:33,100 --> 00:00:38,110
But I'll be showing you some of the most important commands and examples of simple ones as well.

10
00:00:38,110 --> 00:00:42,040
So you know how to experiment and run the other commands.

11
00:00:42,900 --> 00:00:44,180
So we're starting.

12
00:00:44,190 --> 00:00:48,840
If you go to the browser, you'll see commands related to stuff that you can do inside the browser.

13
00:00:48,840 --> 00:00:53,910
So you can see things that will allow you to, for example, get a screenshot and you'll be able to

14
00:00:53,910 --> 00:01:01,470
try and gain and turn on the webcam and see if it works and basically open the webcam on the target.

15
00:01:01,500 --> 00:01:08,970
You can gather information and if you go here on the exploits, you'll see a number of exploits that

16
00:01:08,970 --> 00:01:10,440
you can run again.

17
00:01:10,440 --> 00:01:14,190
Depending on what's running on the target computer, you can run them.

18
00:01:15,040 --> 00:01:19,570
All they have to do is just click on the module that you want to run and click on execute.

19
00:01:20,020 --> 00:01:25,600
Sometimes the modules need to send some options to be set up and we'll have examples of that as well.

20
00:01:26,200 --> 00:01:31,180
And the social engineering, again, there is some really cool stuff that you can do and you can show

21
00:01:31,180 --> 00:01:35,290
fake updates, fake notification bars and stuff like that.

22
00:01:36,400 --> 00:01:39,180
So let's have an example of a very simple command.

23
00:01:39,190 --> 00:01:42,400
So again, we're going to do just an alert to show an alert box.

24
00:01:44,240 --> 00:01:46,550
So I'm just using this search to filter.

25
00:01:47,000 --> 00:01:53,080
And you can see here, this will just create an alert dialog and it's going to say beef alert dialog.

26
00:01:53,090 --> 00:01:55,330
You can modify this and type in anything you want.

27
00:01:55,340 --> 00:02:00,560
For example, I'm going to type in test and then when you hit execute.

28
00:02:01,810 --> 00:02:06,650
Go on to the target and you'll see that the target got a message saying test.

29
00:02:06,670 --> 00:02:09,700
So this has been injected into the target browser.

30
00:02:11,620 --> 00:02:15,040
Another cool thing that you can do is the raw JavaScript.

31
00:02:17,750 --> 00:02:21,410
And this will allow you to execute any JavaScript you want.

32
00:02:21,440 --> 00:02:26,270
So again, you can look for Google for a useful JavaScript code, for example, a keylogger or whatever

33
00:02:26,270 --> 00:02:27,050
you want to do.

34
00:02:27,440 --> 00:02:29,750
Or you can write your own if you know, JavaScript.

35
00:02:29,750 --> 00:02:33,770
And basically whatever you write here will be executed on the target.

36
00:02:33,800 --> 00:02:40,100
Again, we're only saying an alert and this is going to say beef, raw JavaScript, I'm going to execute.

37
00:02:42,840 --> 00:02:43,890
And here we go again.

38
00:02:43,890 --> 00:02:46,680
We've got a dialogue saying before our JavaScript.

39
00:02:49,460 --> 00:02:55,160
And let's see if we can get a screenshot of the target computer and we're going to use a plugin called

40
00:02:55,160 --> 00:02:57,340
Spy Spider, I think.

41
00:02:57,350 --> 00:02:57,620
Yeah.

42
00:02:57,650 --> 00:02:58,430
Spider, I.

43
00:03:00,010 --> 00:03:01,780
So again, click on it.

44
00:03:01,810 --> 00:03:02,080
Hit.

45
00:03:02,080 --> 00:03:02,890
Execute.

46
00:03:04,230 --> 00:03:05,510
Give it a second.

47
00:03:05,520 --> 00:03:07,470
Then we're going to click on the command here.

48
00:03:09,280 --> 00:03:11,040
Looks like this time didn't work properly.

49
00:03:11,050 --> 00:03:12,600
Let's just do it again.

50
00:03:22,920 --> 00:03:23,660
And here we go.

51
00:03:23,670 --> 00:03:27,690
As you can see, we've got a screenshot of what the target person is looking at.

52
00:03:29,400 --> 00:03:34,080
Another really good plug in is a plug in its redirect in.

53
00:03:36,370 --> 00:03:41,500
And it'll basically allow you to redirect the browser to any web page you want.

54
00:03:41,530 --> 00:03:46,420
This could be very useful because you can use it to redirect the target person and tell them that they're

55
00:03:46,420 --> 00:03:46,660
going to.

56
00:03:46,690 --> 00:03:51,150
They need to download an update and instead of giving them an update, give them a backdoor.

57
00:03:51,160 --> 00:03:53,260
You can redirect them to a fake login page.

58
00:03:53,260 --> 00:03:58,630
For example, for Facebook, you can really do anything you want with this, so you can set the website

59
00:03:58,630 --> 00:04:00,910
that you want the target to be redirected to.

60
00:04:00,910 --> 00:04:04,350
And we're going to redirect them to Beef Project in this example.

61
00:04:04,360 --> 00:04:06,250
And once you hit execute.

62
00:04:08,880 --> 00:04:11,700
As you can see here, the Redirected to Beef Project.

63
00:04:13,050 --> 00:04:16,950
These are some of the basic modules that are that you can use.

64
00:04:16,980 --> 00:04:22,950
Again, you can experiment with these, go over them and see what would be useful in your particular

65
00:04:22,950 --> 00:04:23,820
situation.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,610 --> 00:00:07,540
Now in this lecture, I want to show you a social engineering command that we can use with beef to display

2
00:00:07,540 --> 00:00:09,820
the Microsoft Clippy icon.

3
00:00:09,820 --> 00:00:15,340
And then we're going to tell the target person that their browser is out of date and they can download

4
00:00:15,340 --> 00:00:16,270
the new version.

5
00:00:16,570 --> 00:00:23,200
When they download the new version, they'll be downloading a file that we choose so we can give them

6
00:00:23,200 --> 00:00:27,970
a backdoor, a keylogger, a virus, or any file that we want.

7
00:00:28,360 --> 00:00:30,970
In this example, I'm going to give them a Trojan.

8
00:00:30,970 --> 00:00:38,200
So a file that looks and functions like an actual update to Firefox, but at the same time it's going

9
00:00:38,200 --> 00:00:43,870
to execute code in the background to give me full control over the computer.

10
00:00:44,710 --> 00:00:48,670
Now don't worry about how to create evil files and malware.

11
00:00:48,670 --> 00:00:54,700
I will show you how to program your own backdoors, Keyloggers and Trojans later on in the course.

12
00:00:54,700 --> 00:01:02,140
So in this lecture, let's focus on how we can deliver files to a target using beef, using this smart

13
00:01:02,140 --> 00:01:03,970
social engineering technique.

14
00:01:04,710 --> 00:01:11,790
Now the file or the Trojan that I want to deliver is actually stored in here and my work w w w html

15
00:01:11,790 --> 00:01:15,900
evil files and it's called firefox dot x e.

16
00:01:16,720 --> 00:01:23,260
Now, the beef command that I'm going to use in this lecture works against all operating systems, like

17
00:01:23,260 --> 00:01:25,350
we said, because it uses JavaScript.

18
00:01:25,360 --> 00:01:32,200
So you just want to make sure that you select a backdoor that will work on your target operating system.

19
00:01:33,070 --> 00:01:38,740
Now there is a number of social engineering plugins that you can use in here and a lot of them are actually

20
00:01:38,740 --> 00:01:39,340
useful.

21
00:01:39,370 --> 00:01:42,700
I highly recommend you spend some time exploring them.

22
00:01:42,820 --> 00:01:45,550
So you have you can show a fake flash update.

23
00:01:45,550 --> 00:01:50,800
You can show a fake notification bar that shows on top, or you can show the Clippy.

24
00:01:51,010 --> 00:01:57,610
I'm choosing to use the Clippy in this example because it is something that Microsoft users are used

25
00:01:57,610 --> 00:01:59,410
to see and familiar with.

26
00:01:59,740 --> 00:02:05,950
Most people are also familiar with the flash update and notification bar, so you can experiment using

27
00:02:05,950 --> 00:02:06,940
these as well.

28
00:02:07,180 --> 00:02:12,430
Using them is exactly the same, so I'm only going to show you how to use the Clippy plugin.

29
00:02:13,320 --> 00:02:16,080
Now configuring the plugin is very, very simple.

30
00:02:16,500 --> 00:02:20,970
First of all, it's asking you for the location where the clip image is stored.

31
00:02:21,000 --> 00:02:25,100
Now, beef already has that image, so you don't really need to download anything.

32
00:02:25,110 --> 00:02:27,690
You just want to put your IP address in here.

33
00:02:27,840 --> 00:02:30,660
So mine is ten 2014 to 13.

34
00:02:32,070 --> 00:02:35,520
Then you can set the text that's going to appear to the target person.

35
00:02:35,520 --> 00:02:41,490
So the text is already kind of set to a nice text which is going to say Your Firefox is out of date,

36
00:02:41,520 --> 00:02:43,080
do you want to update it?

37
00:02:43,710 --> 00:02:49,770
Then once they say yes, they're going to download an executable and you want to put the link to your

38
00:02:49,770 --> 00:02:51,090
executable here.

39
00:02:51,690 --> 00:02:56,130
So my executable is at ten 2014 to 13.

40
00:02:57,340 --> 00:02:59,050
And it's an evil philes.

41
00:03:02,230 --> 00:03:04,210
And I named it Firefox.

42
00:03:07,140 --> 00:03:13,830
This is a delay until clippy shows up and this is the message that the target person is going to see

43
00:03:13,830 --> 00:03:15,180
after they download.

44
00:03:15,180 --> 00:03:18,870
And it's just going to say thank you for updating your browser.

45
00:03:19,670 --> 00:03:21,320
So the plugin is very simple.

46
00:03:21,320 --> 00:03:22,520
It's in social engineering.

47
00:03:22,520 --> 00:03:23,100
Clippy.

48
00:03:23,120 --> 00:03:25,520
We configured our configuration here.

49
00:03:25,520 --> 00:03:31,700
We set the IPS correctly and we set the location where our evil file is stored.

50
00:03:32,810 --> 00:03:34,400
I'm going to click on Execute.

51
00:03:35,400 --> 00:03:37,170
And go to the target machine.

52
00:03:38,410 --> 00:03:45,160
Now, as you can see, we see clips showing up in here and it's saying that my browser is out of date.

53
00:03:45,190 --> 00:03:46,810
Do I want to update it?

54
00:03:47,350 --> 00:03:48,400
I'm going to say yes.

55
00:03:48,400 --> 00:03:50,020
Please update it for me.

56
00:03:50,140 --> 00:03:52,840
We're going to get an installer for Firefox.

57
00:03:53,140 --> 00:03:54,550
We're going to save it.

58
00:03:55,480 --> 00:04:02,500
We're going to go to our downloads and we can see a file that looks exactly like Firefox.

59
00:04:02,590 --> 00:04:04,780
It has a Firefox icon.

60
00:04:04,810 --> 00:04:06,850
If we double click the file.

61
00:04:10,340 --> 00:04:16,370
We'll actually get an installer for Firefox so you can actually use this to install it and if their

62
00:04:16,370 --> 00:04:19,310
browser is out of date, it will actually update it for them.

63
00:04:19,310 --> 00:04:22,110
So you'll actually be doing the target person a favor.

64
00:04:22,130 --> 00:04:28,760
But at the same time, my evil code will get executed in the background, giving me full control over

65
00:04:28,760 --> 00:04:29,750
this computer.

66
00:04:30,500 --> 00:04:36,290
Now, like I said, don't worry about how you're going to be controlling this computer, how you're

67
00:04:36,290 --> 00:04:41,810
going to be creating the Trojan, the file that looks and functions like a Firefox update program like

68
00:04:41,810 --> 00:04:45,470
you see in here, and how to create a backdoor or any evil file.

69
00:04:45,500 --> 00:04:48,710
We will cover all of that in details later on.

70
00:04:48,710 --> 00:04:57,080
So for now, I just want to show you how to serve evil files to a target computer by tricking them into

71
00:04:57,080 --> 00:04:58,670
downloading an update.

72
00:04:59,030 --> 00:05:03,710
And keep in mind, all of this is happening without the user having to do anything.

73
00:05:03,710 --> 00:05:08,980
So we didn't really send any links or didn't really interact with the user directly.

74
00:05:08,990 --> 00:05:13,850
We're injecting beef's code automatically on any page that the user is loading.

75
00:05:13,880 --> 00:05:20,660
This is hooking the target browser to beef, and then we're showing the update notification using beef

76
00:05:20,660 --> 00:05:23,290
directly into the user's browser.

77
00:05:23,300 --> 00:05:28,390
So they might be reading a news article or browsing a web page that they actually trust.

78
00:05:28,400 --> 00:05:32,220
They're going to get an update telling them that their browser is out of date.

79
00:05:32,240 --> 00:05:33,800
They're going to download the updater.

80
00:05:33,800 --> 00:05:40,280
The updater will actually update their browser, but at the same time it will execute evil code in the

81
00:05:40,280 --> 00:05:41,030
background.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# Section 10: Bypassing HTTPS


1
00:00:01,050 --> 00:00:01,410
Now.

2
00:00:01,410 --> 00:00:05,100
So far we learned how to become the man in the middle.

3
00:00:05,100 --> 00:00:10,580
And once we are the man in the middle, we learned how we can read all the data that's being sent.

4
00:00:10,590 --> 00:00:16,660
So we were able to read usernames, passwords, URLs, images or anything that the people browses.

5
00:00:16,680 --> 00:00:23,610
We were also able to modify this data, so we were able to inject JavaScript code or any type of code

6
00:00:23,610 --> 00:00:28,470
that we want in the target browser when the target browses websites.

7
00:00:28,680 --> 00:00:33,690
This also allowed us to replace downloads and do so many cool attacks.

8
00:00:34,230 --> 00:00:40,140
Now everything that we did so far will only work against HTTP pages.

9
00:00:40,800 --> 00:00:48,870
The reason why it works against HTTP because as we see in the data and HTTP is sent as plain text.

10
00:00:48,870 --> 00:00:53,490
So it's text that humans like us can read and understand.

11
00:00:53,970 --> 00:00:57,570
That's why when we're the man in the middle, we're able to read this text.

12
00:00:57,570 --> 00:01:01,830
And if we wanted, we're able to modify this text as we wish.

13
00:01:02,250 --> 00:01:08,550
Now, this is obviously a problem, and this problem was fixed in https.

14
00:01:09,390 --> 00:01:16,590
So as you know, most websites these days use HTTPS and all good websites.

15
00:01:16,590 --> 00:01:19,620
Famous websites use HTTPS.

16
00:01:20,010 --> 00:01:24,420
The reason why, like I said, because it's a more secure version of HTTP.

17
00:01:24,720 --> 00:01:31,050
And basically the way it works is it keeps the implementation of HTTP exactly the same.

18
00:01:31,050 --> 00:01:35,190
So all the headers, all the way that the data is sent is kept the same.

19
00:01:35,190 --> 00:01:44,190
So HTTP is not modified, but it adds an extra layer over http, which is where the TSS comes from.

20
00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:53,930
So it's a secure HTTP protocol and this extra layer will encrypt the plaintext data that HTTP sends.

21
00:01:53,940 --> 00:01:59,640
So if a person manages to become the man in the middle, they will be able to read this data.

22
00:01:59,640 --> 00:02:01,530
But the data will be gibberish.

23
00:02:01,530 --> 00:02:06,540
It will not be readable to the person intercepting the connection.

24
00:02:06,540 --> 00:02:13,980
It's only going to make sense to the user and to the server because they both have the key and are able

25
00:02:13,980 --> 00:02:16,560
to decrypt the encrypted data.

26
00:02:17,220 --> 00:02:21,360
So let's have an example of what this would look like.

27
00:02:22,050 --> 00:02:29,580
Now in general, when we become the man in the middle using ARP spoofing or any other method, as we

28
00:02:29,580 --> 00:02:32,040
know, the requests will flow through the hacker.

29
00:02:32,040 --> 00:02:37,110
The hacker will forward this to the internet or to the access point, then the Internet, and then the

30
00:02:37,110 --> 00:02:42,840
responses will flow through the hacker computer and down to the victim.

31
00:02:43,530 --> 00:02:52,470
Now, usually, if a person or a user tries to go to a website and let's say they try to go to BBC.com,

32
00:02:53,310 --> 00:02:54,710
they will send the request.

33
00:02:54,720 --> 00:03:03,060
Now, this request, this initial request will not be sent over https unless the user types https before

34
00:03:03,060 --> 00:03:03,920
the website.

35
00:03:03,930 --> 00:03:10,350
And we know most users, including myself, we don't really type https, we just type the name of the

36
00:03:10,350 --> 00:03:10,830
website.

37
00:03:10,830 --> 00:03:15,330
So if they want to go to BBC.com, they'll just type BBC.com.

38
00:03:15,330 --> 00:03:19,260
So the initial request is going to be sent over HTTP.

39
00:03:19,680 --> 00:03:24,570
The hacker being the man in the middle is going to receive this and forwarded to the internet.

40
00:03:25,550 --> 00:03:32,670
BBC.com, on the other hand, is going to respond by saying, Hey, why don't you talk to me over https?

41
00:03:32,690 --> 00:03:39,280
It's more secure now that the target knows that BBC can communicate over https.

42
00:03:39,290 --> 00:03:44,630
Then they're going to communicate with BBC using HTTPS from now on.

43
00:03:44,630 --> 00:03:51,380
So all the requests that will follow after the initial request will be sent over https.

44
00:03:51,890 --> 00:03:57,490
The hacker will receive this forwarded to the internet again as HTTPS.

45
00:03:57,500 --> 00:04:04,700
The server will respond with https and as you can see now, all the communication, all the requests

46
00:04:04,700 --> 00:04:08,690
and the responses are sent over https.

47
00:04:08,960 --> 00:04:15,950
Now, like I said, HTTPS adds an extra encryption layer to the HTTP protocol.

48
00:04:16,460 --> 00:04:23,750
This will mean that the plaintext data that we usually read in HTTP will be encrypted and it will be

49
00:04:23,750 --> 00:04:25,270
not useful for us.

50
00:04:25,280 --> 00:04:30,270
So now that we are in the middle, we're able to see all this data, we're able to read it.

51
00:04:30,290 --> 00:04:32,890
But this data will be gibberish.

52
00:04:32,900 --> 00:04:38,570
It will not be readable, and therefore we won't be able to see the usernames, the passwords, the

53
00:04:38,570 --> 00:04:45,530
URLs or the HTML code so we won't be able to inject code or modify these packets because we can't even

54
00:04:45,530 --> 00:04:47,480
read them in the first place.

55
00:04:48,140 --> 00:04:53,810
Now, this is really bad for us as a hacker because we did all the hard work of trying to become the

56
00:04:53,810 --> 00:04:54,590
man in the middle.

57
00:04:54,590 --> 00:05:00,650
And once we're the man in the middle, were not able to make use of this very good position.

58
00:05:01,370 --> 00:05:08,240
Luckily, there is a smart guy called Moxy who has been defeating SSL for a very long time, and one

59
00:05:08,240 --> 00:05:16,070
of the ways that this guy came up with allows us to bypass HTTPS and downgrade it to HTTP.

60
00:05:16,640 --> 00:05:19,670
Now, Moxy didn't only discover this attack.

61
00:05:19,670 --> 00:05:23,120
He also wrote a great tool that will do this for us.

62
00:05:23,600 --> 00:05:28,370
So the way this will work is let's have a look again on the initial request.

63
00:05:28,370 --> 00:05:35,420
And like we said, usually people don't type https when they request a web page, so they'll type,

64
00:05:35,420 --> 00:05:37,370
for example, BBC.COM.

65
00:05:37,610 --> 00:05:43,700
This will go through the hacker computer, who is who is already the man in the middle and is also running

66
00:05:43,700 --> 00:05:49,130
a tool called SQL Strip, which is the tool that implements Marks's attack.

67
00:05:49,940 --> 00:05:54,100
Now, the tool is going to see that this is a HTTP request, so it's not going to play with it.

68
00:05:54,110 --> 00:05:58,370
This is good for us as a hacker, and it will just forward it to the Internet.

69
00:05:58,850 --> 00:06:06,200
Next, BBC.com is going to respond by saying, Hey, why don't you communicate with me over https?

70
00:06:06,650 --> 00:06:09,430
Now, SSL strip is going to detect this.

71
00:06:09,440 --> 00:06:16,430
And by the way, when I say BBC is going to say, Hey, why don't you communicate with me over https?

72
00:06:16,610 --> 00:06:21,800
The process is actually a little bit more complicated than this, but I'm just trying to keep it as

73
00:06:21,800 --> 00:06:23,090
simple as possible.

74
00:06:23,840 --> 00:06:32,210
So SSL strip is going to detect that the server is trying to tell the targets to communicate over https

75
00:06:32,210 --> 00:06:36,770
and it's going to remove or strip all the fields and all the data.

76
00:06:36,800 --> 00:06:41,690
That's basically saying let's upgrade this connection to a HTTPS connection.

77
00:06:42,110 --> 00:06:49,580
So the response is going to come as a HTTPS response, but Sstl strip is going to strip that and only

78
00:06:49,580 --> 00:06:55,550
forward the parts that look like a normal HTTP response to the victim.

79
00:06:56,090 --> 00:07:02,210
Now, so far, the victim doesn't know that the server can communicate over https, therefore it will

80
00:07:02,210 --> 00:07:06,350
send its next request as a normal HTTP request.

81
00:07:06,830 --> 00:07:13,790
Now here is where SSL strip is smart because it's going to keep track of all the websites that have

82
00:07:13,790 --> 00:07:16,160
sent a HTTP response.

83
00:07:16,310 --> 00:07:23,600
So even though the victim is going to send the HTTP request when it's communicating with BBC.com, SSL

84
00:07:23,600 --> 00:07:28,910
script knows that BBC.com likes to communicate over https.

85
00:07:29,000 --> 00:07:36,080
So what it's going to do is once it gets the HTTP request from the victim or the target, it will actually

86
00:07:36,080 --> 00:07:40,190
upgrade that connection to a HTTPS connection.

87
00:07:40,190 --> 00:07:48,230
So as far as BBC.com is concerned, it told the target that you should communicate with me over HTTPS,

88
00:07:48,230 --> 00:07:52,790
and the target responded by starting communicating over https.

89
00:07:52,970 --> 00:07:57,740
So this is perfect for BBC.com and for the victim.

90
00:07:57,770 --> 00:07:59,320
As far as they're concerned.

91
00:07:59,330 --> 00:08:06,710
They don't think BBC.com can communicate over https because every time they send the HTTP request,

92
00:08:06,710 --> 00:08:09,290
they are getting a HTTP response.

93
00:08:09,290 --> 00:08:16,970
So they don't think that BBC can communicate over https because if it can, then it will respond to

94
00:08:16,970 --> 00:08:20,450
the victim saying Communicate with me over https.

95
00:08:21,320 --> 00:08:25,040
Now this configuration is perfect now because us.

96
00:08:25,100 --> 00:08:33,470
As a hacker were able to read the requests and the responses in plaintext, and nobody knows that we

97
00:08:33,470 --> 00:08:35,410
are playing around with the connection.

98
00:08:35,420 --> 00:08:42,650
The target thinks that the website that they're trying to access only supports HTTP and the web server

99
00:08:42,650 --> 00:08:50,750
is happy also because it's communicating over https with what they think is their client, but who they're

100
00:08:50,750 --> 00:08:53,300
connecting with is actually us.

101
00:08:53,960 --> 00:09:00,950
Now we're able in here, when we get HTTP responses from the Internet, we're actually able to read

102
00:09:00,950 --> 00:09:05,960
them because we are the ones initiating the HTTP requests.

103
00:09:05,960 --> 00:09:14,660
So you can see the HTTPS cycle is actually only between us and the internet or the web server, and

104
00:09:14,660 --> 00:09:18,380
it's not between the victim and the web server.

105
00:09:19,640 --> 00:09:26,180
Now, another thing that Sstl strip will do is when it loads a web page, it will actually convert all

106
00:09:26,180 --> 00:09:30,770
the links in the page from HTTPS to a normal HTTP.

107
00:09:31,190 --> 00:09:39,920
And this way, when SSL strip is working, all the HTTPS connections will be downgraded to HTTP.

108
00:09:40,520 --> 00:09:45,650
Now, since the connections are going to be downgraded to HTTP, that means the data is going to be

109
00:09:45,650 --> 00:09:52,070
sent in plaintext, which means that we'll be able to read and modify this data just like we usually

110
00:09:52,070 --> 00:09:53,750
do with HTTP.

111
00:09:54,620 --> 00:10:04,280
Now there is one exception to this whole rule, which is websites that use HST as now HTTPS is used

112
00:10:04,280 --> 00:10:07,730
by Facebook, PayPal and Gmail, for example.

113
00:10:07,730 --> 00:10:15,470
And basically the way this works is the browser at the victim at the target comes up with a hardcoded

114
00:10:15,470 --> 00:10:20,750
list of websites that it should only load as https.

115
00:10:20,870 --> 00:10:28,100
So even if we try everything that we can and try to downgrade this connection to a HTTP, the browser

116
00:10:28,100 --> 00:10:35,390
will just refuse to load this page because it knows that this page has to load over https.

117
00:10:35,870 --> 00:10:40,560
Now, so far, there isn't a practical way of bypassing hosts.

118
00:10:40,790 --> 00:10:42,920
There was, but that got fixed.

119
00:10:42,920 --> 00:10:47,960
But I will update you as soon as there is a new way to bypass it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,330 --> 00:00:04,740
Now that we understand how cell strip works, let's put it in practice.

2
00:00:05,230 --> 00:00:11,340
The cell strip itself is actually written in Python, and at this stage you have all the necessary skills

3
00:00:11,340 --> 00:00:18,510
to implement a tool like that because you already know how to intercept data, analyze it and edit it

4
00:00:18,510 --> 00:00:19,410
on the fly.

5
00:00:19,440 --> 00:00:26,790
However, to implement a tool like SQL Strip, we're going to have to dive really deep into networking

6
00:00:26,790 --> 00:00:30,420
and how HTTPS works, which could take some time.

7
00:00:30,420 --> 00:00:36,210
Therefore, in this section I'm going to show you how to use all of the programs that we created so

8
00:00:36,210 --> 00:00:43,920
far with an implementation of SQL Strip so that you can use them with websites that use HTTPS.

9
00:00:44,010 --> 00:00:50,190
So right here I have my KDE terminal and the first program that we're going to test this with is the

10
00:00:50,190 --> 00:00:52,350
packet sniffer because it's the simplest.

11
00:00:52,350 --> 00:00:57,090
And then we're going to move on to the file interceptor and code injector.

12
00:00:57,270 --> 00:01:02,160
So let's run the packet sniffer simply by type in Python three packet sniffer.

13
00:01:02,160 --> 00:01:08,340
I'm running the Python three version and we're also going to run our ERPs buffer with Python three to

14
00:01:08,340 --> 00:01:14,190
intercept data and place us in the middle of the connection in here so that data flow through us.

15
00:01:14,790 --> 00:01:21,810
Now for us to be able to downgrade HTTP to HTTP, we're going to have to run SQL Strip on our computer.

16
00:01:21,810 --> 00:01:23,550
So let's go ahead and do that.

17
00:01:23,970 --> 00:01:30,270
Now, we're actually not going to run the original SQL Strip as it has not been maintained since it

18
00:01:30,270 --> 00:01:31,080
was made.

19
00:01:31,200 --> 00:01:35,940
We're going to use the better implementation of it, which works very, very well.

20
00:01:36,300 --> 00:01:42,480
So all you have to do is simply type better cap, which is the name of a network penetration testing

21
00:01:42,510 --> 00:01:43,080
tool.

22
00:01:43,140 --> 00:01:48,870
We're going to give it the interface using the IFS argument, and our interface is at zero.

23
00:01:48,870 --> 00:01:54,750
And then we're going to do the couplet to run a plug in or a couplet, as Buttercup calls it, that

24
00:01:54,750 --> 00:02:01,170
implements the same functionality as SQL Strip, but better that works against more websites.

25
00:02:01,350 --> 00:02:08,100
So the name of this plug in or couplet is HST is hijack, and we're going to have to type forward slash

26
00:02:08,100 --> 00:02:08,550
H.

27
00:02:08,550 --> 00:02:09,180
S T.

28
00:02:09,180 --> 00:02:14,760
S hijack because it's actually stored inside a directory called HDS hijack.

29
00:02:14,760 --> 00:02:16,680
That's why we have to type it twice.

30
00:02:16,860 --> 00:02:22,530
So I'm going to hit enter here and if you get no errors, it means everything is running as expected.

31
00:02:22,530 --> 00:02:24,600
So let's go and test this out.

32
00:02:24,750 --> 00:02:29,700
So first of all, it's a really good idea to delete all browsing data, make sure you select everything

33
00:02:29,700 --> 00:02:30,930
and you tick all the boxes.

34
00:02:30,930 --> 00:02:33,690
When you're testing, we're going to click on okay.

35
00:02:33,690 --> 00:02:38,430
And let's go to a website that uses HTTPS such as LinkedIn.

36
00:02:40,120 --> 00:02:45,300
And as you can see, the website is not loading over https, which is great.

37
00:02:45,310 --> 00:02:49,830
So we already know we will probably be able to see the username and the password.

38
00:02:49,840 --> 00:02:52,750
So let's go ahead and input test email.

39
00:02:52,750 --> 00:03:01,150
So let's say Z at test dot com and we'll put the password as one, two, three, four, five, six.

40
00:03:01,150 --> 00:03:03,460
Now note the box that you see in here.

41
00:03:03,460 --> 00:03:10,150
This box will always be displayed by Firefox whenever you enter information on HTTP pages.

42
00:03:10,150 --> 00:03:15,670
It's not because Firefox is able to detect the attack that we are running at the moment.

43
00:03:15,670 --> 00:03:22,540
It always displays this box when the user enters data on HTTP pages, so it's not very suspicious.

44
00:03:22,540 --> 00:03:24,520
We're going to click on sign in.

45
00:03:24,520 --> 00:03:31,900
And if we go to the attacker machine, as you can see, we were first of all able to sniff all of the

46
00:03:31,900 --> 00:03:38,050
links as usual, as if it's going through a HTTP page because it was going through HTTP.

47
00:03:38,080 --> 00:03:43,720
And you can also see that we have a possible username and password and you can see that the username

48
00:03:43,720 --> 00:03:49,270
was Z at test dot com and the password was one, two, three, four, five, six.

49
00:03:50,150 --> 00:03:57,320
So perfect, we managed to downgrade HTTPS to HTTP and therefore we didn't actually have to modify our

50
00:03:57,320 --> 00:03:58,190
sniffer at all.

51
00:03:58,220 --> 00:04:04,400
It was able to sniff everything by default because it can sniff everything that goes over http.

52
00:04:04,700 --> 00:04:08,180
Let's go ahead and test this against another HTTPS website.

53
00:04:08,210 --> 00:04:11,060
So let's go to Stack Overflow Dotcom.

54
00:04:12,760 --> 00:04:14,170
And let's log in.

55
00:04:14,650 --> 00:04:19,080
And again, if you look here on the top, you can see that it's not loading over https.

56
00:04:19,090 --> 00:04:21,520
So we already know this will probably work.

57
00:04:21,520 --> 00:04:23,680
But let's get let's give it a test again.

58
00:04:23,680 --> 00:04:31,060
So we're going to say Z at test two dotcom this time and let's set the password to six, five, four,

59
00:04:31,060 --> 00:04:36,790
three, two, one hit enter to log in and go back to the hacker machine.

60
00:04:36,790 --> 00:04:42,430
You can see again the URLs are being sniffed in here, all of the URLs that the target is accessing.

61
00:04:42,430 --> 00:04:45,670
And then we have a possible username and password.

62
00:04:45,670 --> 00:04:51,820
And if you look closely, you will see that there isn't actually a username and a password in this particular

63
00:04:51,820 --> 00:04:52,390
line.

64
00:04:52,630 --> 00:04:58,360
But if we go down more, you'll see another one and you can see that we have the username here again

65
00:04:58,360 --> 00:05:00,430
Z at test two dot com.

66
00:05:00,430 --> 00:05:05,350
And then you can also see the password which is six five, four, three, two, one.

67
00:05:06,070 --> 00:05:13,080
So again, as you can see, the packet sniffer now works perfectly because we're able to downgrade HTTPS

68
00:05:13,120 --> 00:05:20,080
to HTTP thanks to the WSL strip implementation and better cap and therefore we're able to capture everything

69
00:05:20,080 --> 00:05:21,340
using our sniffer.

70
00:05:21,520 --> 00:05:26,830
Now the same methodology can be used with all of the other programs that you're going to program that

71
00:05:26,830 --> 00:05:33,310
intercept data, but you might have to slightly modify them, and I'm going to cover that in the next

72
00:05:33,310 --> 00:05:38,920
lectures when we test the code injector and the file interceptor.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,390 --> 00:00:07,440
Now that we know how to downgrade HTTP to HTTP using better cap and we learned how to use it with a

2
00:00:07,440 --> 00:00:09,900
simple sniffer in this lecture.

3
00:00:09,900 --> 00:00:16,320
And the next lecture, I'm going to show you how to use the same technique with the more complex applications

4
00:00:16,320 --> 00:00:21,810
that actually modify data such as the replace downloads and the code injector.

5
00:00:21,930 --> 00:00:23,670
So let's go to Kelly.

6
00:00:23,670 --> 00:00:28,920
And first of all, as you can see in here, I'm going to run my ARP spoof or to put me in the middle

7
00:00:28,920 --> 00:00:33,210
of the connection and redirect the flow of packets to go through my computer.

8
00:00:33,720 --> 00:00:40,950
Then I'm going to run Buttercup using the exact same command as the last lecture to downgrade HTTP to

9
00:00:40,950 --> 00:00:41,940
https.

10
00:00:41,940 --> 00:00:48,680
So we're specifying the host as hijack couplet, which is the plugin that actually downgrades http to

11
00:00:48,690 --> 00:00:54,330
http because as we mentioned, Buttercup can be used to do many things and we're running it with the

12
00:00:54,330 --> 00:00:57,750
interface that is connected to the target network.

13
00:00:57,870 --> 00:01:00,930
So I'm going to hit Enter at that runs with no issues.

14
00:01:00,930 --> 00:01:02,280
So that's perfect.

15
00:01:02,580 --> 00:01:06,000
And finally, we're going to run the replace downloads in here.

16
00:01:06,000 --> 00:01:12,420
But as you remember, for us to use the replace downloads, we have to use IP table rules to redirect

17
00:01:12,420 --> 00:01:16,830
packets to the Q that the replace downloads program uses.

18
00:01:16,830 --> 00:01:23,310
And usually if we're running this against a remote computer, not our local machine, we would use the

19
00:01:23,310 --> 00:01:24,240
forward rule.

20
00:01:24,480 --> 00:01:27,180
So and here we would say forward.

21
00:01:27,950 --> 00:01:35,000
But because we're running it this time with better cap and better cap is actually going to be modifying

22
00:01:35,000 --> 00:01:38,540
data and downgrading HTTPS to HTTP.

23
00:01:38,570 --> 00:01:44,810
We're actually going to be using the input and output rules because we're going to want this program

24
00:01:44,810 --> 00:01:49,810
to be modifying the data that is entering and leaving our computer.

25
00:01:49,820 --> 00:01:54,350
So it's similar to when we were testing on the local computer.

26
00:01:54,590 --> 00:01:57,470
So we're going to run the input rule first.

27
00:01:58,420 --> 00:02:00,880
And then run the output rule.

28
00:02:01,660 --> 00:02:08,050
And finally again, because we are using better cap and we're using the replace downloads with better

29
00:02:08,050 --> 00:02:14,350
cap data is going to go to better cap and better cap is going to be downgrading HTTPS to HTTP.

30
00:02:14,740 --> 00:02:20,560
It's going to do this using a proxy that runs on port 80, 80 by default.

31
00:02:20,560 --> 00:02:24,970
And therefore, we're going to have to modify the code for the replace downloads.

32
00:02:24,970 --> 00:02:31,750
And when we used to check for requests, we used to check if they're coming on Port 80, but this time

33
00:02:31,750 --> 00:02:37,660
we're going to change it to 80, 80 because that's the port used by better caps proxy.

34
00:02:37,660 --> 00:02:43,450
And we want to modify data that is entering and leaving that proxy because that's the proxy that's going

35
00:02:43,450 --> 00:02:47,380
to do all the magic downgrading HTTPS to HTTP.

36
00:02:47,680 --> 00:02:53,290
So we're going to do the same with the if statement that is checking for the responses for the source

37
00:02:53,290 --> 00:02:53,740
port.

38
00:02:53,740 --> 00:03:00,640
And again, instead of 80, we're going to set it to 80, 80 and last but not least, before running

39
00:03:00,640 --> 00:03:07,000
the actual program, there is actually a bug in this program that I only discovered once I started testing

40
00:03:07,000 --> 00:03:10,630
it with more websites when I was bypassing HTTPS.

41
00:03:10,720 --> 00:03:17,230
And that bug is if you actually look at the code in here, we're checking if the request contains dot

42
00:03:17,260 --> 00:03:23,140
x e, we're going to detect that it is a request and we're going to replace that file that is being

43
00:03:23,140 --> 00:03:27,670
downloaded with the file specified in this particular URL.

44
00:03:28,090 --> 00:03:34,180
Now, this URL includes an executable file with a dot x extension.

45
00:03:34,180 --> 00:03:41,680
Therefore, when the target requests this file, this if statement is going to be true again, and therefore

46
00:03:41,680 --> 00:03:44,350
we're going to replace it again with this file.

47
00:03:44,350 --> 00:03:50,920
And this will continue in an infinite loop and dependent on what the Target website is and how it's

48
00:03:50,920 --> 00:03:52,480
serving the executable.

49
00:03:52,630 --> 00:03:58,450
The user might get stuck in an infinite loop and they will never get the file that we want them to get.

50
00:03:58,660 --> 00:04:04,330
Therefore, we're going to modify this if statement and say if the request contains a dot x.

51
00:04:04,480 --> 00:04:11,410
So if it's a download request for an executable and if my current IP address.

52
00:04:11,410 --> 00:04:15,220
So the IP address that we are serving this one right here.

53
00:04:15,670 --> 00:04:23,470
So we're going to copy and paste it and we're going to see if that is not in this copy packet.

54
00:04:24,660 --> 00:04:27,420
KP dot raw don't load.

55
00:04:27,420 --> 00:04:34,830
So if it's not in the load of the RO packet and because we're doing this with Python three, similar

56
00:04:34,830 --> 00:04:41,670
to what we did with the string in here, we're going to have to put a B before it to indicate that this

57
00:04:41,670 --> 00:04:44,160
is a byte string and we're done.

58
00:04:44,460 --> 00:04:48,520
So we've only made two modifications to the application.

59
00:04:48,540 --> 00:04:53,940
The first one is we changed the destination port and the source port to 8080.

60
00:04:53,940 --> 00:05:00,630
And we did that because we're going to use this program with better cap and Better Cap works by redirecting

61
00:05:00,630 --> 00:05:06,450
data to its own proxy to strip HTTPS and its own proxy runs on 8080.

62
00:05:06,480 --> 00:05:12,750
That's why we're setting the destination port and the source port to 88 so we can catch the requests

63
00:05:12,750 --> 00:05:18,540
and the responses after HTTPS has been stripped and downgraded to HTTP.

64
00:05:19,020 --> 00:05:23,670
And the next change that we made was to fix a bug in the program.

65
00:05:23,670 --> 00:05:29,400
And the main problem of the bug was that we are, first of all, checking if the user is requesting

66
00:05:29,400 --> 00:05:30,710
an executable.

67
00:05:30,720 --> 00:05:33,820
If they are, we're serving them an executable.

68
00:05:33,840 --> 00:05:39,000
Then this check is going to be true again and we're going to serve them an executable and we're going

69
00:05:39,000 --> 00:05:42,870
to continue doing this infinitely, which might break the download.

70
00:05:42,990 --> 00:05:50,040
Therefore, we added another condition to our check so that we only intercept the download if it's an

71
00:05:50,040 --> 00:05:51,120
executable.

72
00:05:51,120 --> 00:05:59,760
And if this IP is not in the download and this IP is the IP that is serving the evil file, therefore

73
00:05:59,760 --> 00:06:06,090
this if statement will always be false when we're serving the evil file and therefore we will not be

74
00:06:06,090 --> 00:06:07,290
stuck in the loop.

75
00:06:08,010 --> 00:06:14,610
So now that we're happy with everything, let's go down and let's run the replace downloads.

76
00:06:15,000 --> 00:06:21,300
So we're going to hit Enter and let's go ahead and download files from HTTPS websites.

77
00:06:21,300 --> 00:06:26,490
So I'm going to remove all my browsing data as usual and let's start with WinZip.

78
00:06:27,530 --> 00:06:34,640
Now WinZip uses https by default, but as you can see straight away it's been stripped and it is being

79
00:06:34,640 --> 00:06:36,830
loaded over normal http.

80
00:06:37,040 --> 00:06:39,260
So let's go ahead and try to download it.

81
00:06:39,290 --> 00:06:44,480
Click here to download and usually you should get a download for WinZip.

82
00:06:45,080 --> 00:06:52,340
And as you can see, instead of getting the WinZip installer, we're being served a file called Evil

83
00:06:52,610 --> 00:06:57,710
X, which is the file that our replace download script is serving to the user.

84
00:06:57,980 --> 00:07:04,490
Now obviously you can serve this with a proper name icon and even possibly uninstaller as a Trojan.

85
00:07:04,490 --> 00:07:07,760
And we're going to cover creating Trojans in this course later on.

86
00:07:07,760 --> 00:07:09,170
So don't worry about that.

87
00:07:09,200 --> 00:07:16,280
The goal of this lecture is to bypass HTTPS, intercept downloads and serve a malicious file when the

88
00:07:16,280 --> 00:07:18,230
user requests a download.

89
00:07:18,890 --> 00:07:22,430
So let's test this now with another download.

90
00:07:22,520 --> 00:07:24,770
So let's go to seven zip.

91
00:07:27,560 --> 00:07:31,010
And let's download any of the executables.

92
00:07:31,220 --> 00:07:37,040
And perfect again, as you can see, instead of getting the seven zip installer, that download request

93
00:07:37,040 --> 00:07:43,790
is being intercepted by our program and the user is getting a malicious file even though seven zip and

94
00:07:43,790 --> 00:07:51,020
winzip, both of them usually use https, but we're able to first of all downgrade https to HTTP using

95
00:07:51,020 --> 00:07:56,300
better cap and then intercept the download and replace it using our place downloads.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,510 --> 00:00:09,210
Now that we know how to use better cap to downgrade HTTP to HTTP with our more complex applications

2
00:00:09,210 --> 00:00:15,030
that also modify packets such as the replace downloads and this lecture, I want to show you how to

3
00:00:15,030 --> 00:00:18,540
use the same technique with the code injector.

4
00:00:18,780 --> 00:00:24,960
As you know, both the code injector and the replace downloads use the same idea and actually rely on

5
00:00:24,960 --> 00:00:27,230
the same library and the same methodology.

6
00:00:27,240 --> 00:00:32,320
Therefore, using these programs with SQL Strip is going to be pretty similar.

7
00:00:32,340 --> 00:00:35,560
There is only going to be one small change that I want to highlight.

8
00:00:35,580 --> 00:00:39,390
So as I mentioned earlier, what we do is very generic.

9
00:00:39,390 --> 00:00:45,480
Therefore, any other programs that you're going to build that relies on net filter Q To intercept data

10
00:00:45,480 --> 00:00:53,880
and modify it will still work with better caps hosts hijack couplet to downgrade http to http exactly

11
00:00:53,880 --> 00:00:56,520
the same way that I'm showing you in this section.

12
00:00:56,730 --> 00:00:59,670
So let's go back here to our Kelly Machine.

13
00:00:59,670 --> 00:01:05,550
And as you can see, I'm already running my ARP spoof to intercept data and put myself in the middle

14
00:01:05,550 --> 00:01:06,530
of the connection.

15
00:01:06,540 --> 00:01:13,110
I am already running better cap in here with the hosts hijack couplet using exactly the same command

16
00:01:13,110 --> 00:01:16,020
as we have been using throughout this whole section.

17
00:01:16,020 --> 00:01:21,610
And in here I'm going to run my code injector now before running the code injector.

18
00:01:21,630 --> 00:01:27,900
As mentioned earlier, you're going to have to run the input and the output IP table rules in order

19
00:01:27,900 --> 00:01:35,610
to redirect data within this computer to the code injector so that it can read the data and modify it.

20
00:01:35,610 --> 00:01:44,550
And as I mentioned earlier, Buttercup relies on a proxy that runs on Port 1880 to downgrade HTTP to

21
00:01:44,550 --> 00:01:45,570
HTTP.

22
00:01:45,720 --> 00:01:52,710
Therefore, we're going to need to modify the code for our code injector and change these if statements

23
00:01:52,710 --> 00:01:55,860
that check for destination port and source port.

24
00:01:55,890 --> 00:02:02,070
They check if these ports are 80 and we're going to have to modify them to 80, 80, and that's pretty

25
00:02:02,070 --> 00:02:02,600
much it.

26
00:02:02,610 --> 00:02:06,460
So we haven't really done anything that we haven't explained earlier.

27
00:02:06,480 --> 00:02:13,050
We're running ARP spoofing here, better cap with the Hdz hijack couplet here and two IP table rules

28
00:02:13,050 --> 00:02:17,700
in here, input and output to redirect data within the current computer.

29
00:02:17,700 --> 00:02:24,780
Usually when we use our code injector or file interceptor on a remote computer within the network,

30
00:02:24,780 --> 00:02:26,490
we would use the forward rule.

31
00:02:26,490 --> 00:02:31,800
But as mentioned in the previous lecture, since we are doing this with better cap and better cap is

32
00:02:31,800 --> 00:02:36,030
actually the program that is downgrading http http.

33
00:02:36,030 --> 00:02:41,550
We only want to see the data after it's downgraded and therefore we're using the output and the input

34
00:02:41,550 --> 00:02:48,660
rules because the data is already in our local computer and has already been modified using better cap.

35
00:02:49,020 --> 00:02:51,570
So let's run the code injector.

36
00:02:51,570 --> 00:02:59,430
We're going to do Python three code injector and let's go to the Windows machine and let's start loading

37
00:02:59,430 --> 00:03:02,700
some websites that actually use HTTPS.

38
00:03:02,910 --> 00:03:05,730
So let's start with Stack Overflow.

39
00:03:07,140 --> 00:03:12,780
And as you can see, the website loads, but our code does not get injected.

40
00:03:12,930 --> 00:03:20,730
Now as you know, if we go up, our code looks for the slash body tag and replaces it with another slash

41
00:03:20,730 --> 00:03:23,220
body with the injection code.

42
00:03:23,460 --> 00:03:27,840
So let's look at the source code of this page and see what happened in there.

43
00:03:29,100 --> 00:03:32,850
And we're going to do control F and look for the slash body.

44
00:03:33,360 --> 00:03:38,010
And as you can see, that part of the page does not exist.

45
00:03:38,340 --> 00:03:44,220
Now, let's scroll down all the way down to the end of the page, and you'll notice that the page did

46
00:03:44,220 --> 00:03:45,800
not actually finish loading.

47
00:03:45,810 --> 00:03:49,470
So this is not a normal end of a HTML page.

48
00:03:49,470 --> 00:03:52,950
This is a normal tag that seems like it's incomplete.

49
00:03:53,130 --> 00:04:00,540
So something went wrong and therefore the page did not load fully and the slash body did not get loaded

50
00:04:00,540 --> 00:04:03,810
and therefore our code did not get injected.

51
00:04:04,530 --> 00:04:08,220
So let's go back and let's go to our terminal.

52
00:04:08,220 --> 00:04:14,280
We're going to close this, clear the screen and in the code just to get more information, let's just

53
00:04:14,280 --> 00:04:20,430
print this copy packet show as usual, when we're not sure what's happening and we want to get more

54
00:04:20,430 --> 00:04:24,060
information, let's try to get more information and see what's happening.

55
00:04:24,060 --> 00:04:26,160
Why is the page breaking?

56
00:04:26,370 --> 00:04:34,290
So let's run the code again and we're going to delete all browsing data and we'll load Stack Overflow

57
00:04:34,290 --> 00:04:34,920
again.

58
00:04:35,550 --> 00:04:37,950
Now again, our code does not get injected.

59
00:04:37,950 --> 00:04:39,660
It's not loading, we know that.

60
00:04:39,660 --> 00:04:41,700
But let's go in here.

61
00:04:41,880 --> 00:04:44,100
We're going to do control C to stop it.

62
00:04:44,100 --> 00:04:47,070
And let's look at the requests that we got.

63
00:04:48,290 --> 00:04:55,790
And if you look closely in here, you'll see that this particular page uses HTTP 1.1.

64
00:04:56,210 --> 00:05:01,160
Now, we know from our previous experience, and you can obviously test this with a number of websites

65
00:05:01,160 --> 00:05:04,450
to see, it's actually going to work with one web.

66
00:05:04,460 --> 00:05:08,360
And you should notice that one web uses 1.0.

67
00:05:08,450 --> 00:05:13,130
So that should give you an indication to what might be causing this problem.

68
00:05:13,160 --> 00:05:18,950
Our code works with HTTP 1.0, but it doesn't work with HTTP 1.1.

69
00:05:19,070 --> 00:05:28,520
If you dig further, you will notice that HTTP 1.1 supports chunked data transfer with chunked transfer.

70
00:05:28,550 --> 00:05:31,100
The data is sent into chunks.

71
00:05:31,130 --> 00:05:38,450
Each chunk has the same size, and therefore when we modify one of these chunks in here, we're going

72
00:05:38,450 --> 00:05:44,810
to mess up the size, but we're not going to be able to recalculate it because with chunked encoding,

73
00:05:44,810 --> 00:05:47,480
the content length is not specified.

74
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:55,310
Therefore, what we can do is we can downgrade this request from HTTP 1.1 to 1.0.

75
00:05:55,340 --> 00:06:01,790
This way the content length will be specified and we'll be able to recalculate it in here in the code

76
00:06:01,790 --> 00:06:03,980
that we have already implemented.

77
00:06:05,090 --> 00:06:09,400
Downgrade in HTTP 1.1 to 1.0 is very simple.

78
00:06:09,410 --> 00:06:15,260
It's a simple string replacement similar to the way that we're modifying the requests in here.

79
00:06:15,530 --> 00:06:25,100
So all we have to do is simply say load is equal to load, dot replace, and we want to replace this

80
00:06:25,100 --> 00:06:27,110
specific string right here.

81
00:06:28,770 --> 00:06:35,430
With the same string, but this time we're going to say 1.0 instead of 1.1.

82
00:06:35,730 --> 00:06:37,020
So very, very simple.

83
00:06:37,020 --> 00:06:41,610
We're simply replacing HTTP 1.1 with HTTP 1.0.

84
00:06:42,450 --> 00:06:48,210
Let's remove the print in here, save and clear the screen and run the code.

85
00:06:48,810 --> 00:06:51,420
And let's go ahead and test it with some more websites.

86
00:06:51,420 --> 00:06:56,700
So we're going to remove all browsing data and let's test it with Stack Overflow first.

87
00:06:56,970 --> 00:07:03,630
And perfect as you can see, the page loads normally and we get the script injected and therefore we're

88
00:07:03,630 --> 00:07:06,570
seeing the alert message in here that is saying test.

89
00:07:07,380 --> 00:07:09,000
Let's test with more websites.

90
00:07:09,000 --> 00:07:12,240
So let's go to LinkedIn dot com.

91
00:07:13,140 --> 00:07:19,260
And again, perfect code works as expected and we see the warning being injected in here.

92
00:07:19,890 --> 00:07:24,090
Now let's go to another website, for example, BBC.com.

93
00:07:25,330 --> 00:07:31,050
And again, you get the idea our script is getting injected in every single page that the target loads,

94
00:07:31,060 --> 00:07:39,100
even if that page is used in https thanks to the use of the BTS hijack couplet that comes with better

95
00:07:39,100 --> 00:07:39,640
cap.

96
00:07:40,360 --> 00:07:43,450
So like I said, pretty much the idea is always the same.

97
00:07:43,450 --> 00:07:49,570
You want to become the man in the middle run better cap with the hosts hijack couplet and you're good

98
00:07:49,570 --> 00:07:50,320
to go.

99
00:07:50,800 --> 00:07:56,590
If your code is going to intercept data and modify it, you want to make sure you intercept data on

100
00:07:56,590 --> 00:08:02,470
the power that better cap uses, which is 80, 80 instead of 80 as usual.

101
00:08:02,470 --> 00:08:08,470
And because this time we're getting better cap to modify data instead of running the forward rule,

102
00:08:08,470 --> 00:08:15,250
