you're going to have to run the input and output rules to modify data within our local computer.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 11: Writing an ARP Spoof Detector

1
00:00:00,660 --> 00:00:01,470
So far.

2
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:08,760
We spoke about a number of powerful man in the middle attacks that allowed us to read usernames, passwords,

3
00:00:08,760 --> 00:00:15,060
cut the Internet connection, modify packets, do DNS spoofing and all that cool stuff.

4
00:00:15,090 --> 00:00:21,000
And we were able to do all of that because we were able to become the man in the middle.

5
00:00:21,150 --> 00:00:26,820
Now there are a number of ways to become the man in the middle, but the way that we're focusing on

6
00:00:26,820 --> 00:00:29,790
in this course is using ARP spoofing.

7
00:00:30,420 --> 00:00:36,750
Now, this is a very reliable way because it exploits the way the ARP protocol works.

8
00:00:37,410 --> 00:00:44,580
Now, in the next lecture, I want to show you how to build a program that can detect ARP spoofing attacks.

9
00:00:44,580 --> 00:00:50,760
Now, detecting an ARP spoofing attack will allow us to detect all of the other man in the middle attacks

10
00:00:50,760 --> 00:00:51,870
that we were running.

11
00:00:51,870 --> 00:00:58,980
Because in order to run any of these attacks, we had to first become the man in the middle using ARP,

12
00:00:58,980 --> 00:01:05,250
spoofing using the ARP underscore spoof script that we built earlier in the course.

13
00:01:06,090 --> 00:01:12,090
Now, before we dive into how this program is going to work, we're going to need to run an ARP spoofing

14
00:01:12,090 --> 00:01:14,790
attack from the Windows Machine.

15
00:01:14,790 --> 00:01:20,520
So in the next lecture, I'm going to be using the Windows machine as the attacker machine and the machine

16
00:01:20,520 --> 00:01:22,350
is going to be the victim machine.

17
00:01:23,080 --> 00:01:29,470
The reason for this is because we usually write programs in Linux in Cali, and this time we're writing

18
00:01:29,470 --> 00:01:31,990
a program that will detect an attack.

19
00:01:32,020 --> 00:01:34,570
Therefore, we can't run the attack from Cali.

20
00:01:34,600 --> 00:01:40,450
We're going to be running the attack from Windows and then we'll be writing the program that detects

21
00:01:40,450 --> 00:01:42,460
the attack in Linux.

22
00:01:43,000 --> 00:01:49,150
So in order for us to use the Windows machine as the attacker machine, we're going to need to run the

23
00:01:49,150 --> 00:01:57,100
ARP underscore spoof script that we built previously in the course, which is this one right here.

24
00:01:57,730 --> 00:01:59,620
Now we know this is a Python script.

25
00:01:59,620 --> 00:02:02,740
We built it ourselves and we know exactly how it works.

26
00:02:02,830 --> 00:02:09,640
And it works on Linux without the need to install any programs because Linux come with its own Python

27
00:02:09,640 --> 00:02:10,600
interpreter.

28
00:02:11,200 --> 00:02:17,680
Now, if we want to run this on Windows, we're going to have to manually install a Python interpreter

29
00:02:17,680 --> 00:02:18,790
on Windows.

30
00:02:19,130 --> 00:02:25,840
Doing that will allow our Windows operating system to run any Python script, so we'll be able to run

31
00:02:25,840 --> 00:02:28,780
pretty much all the programs that we wrote so far.

32
00:02:29,260 --> 00:02:36,100
Now, you want to keep in mind that we're installing a Python interpreter, not a Linux emulator, so

33
00:02:36,100 --> 00:02:43,120
you'll be able to run Python programs that use Windows libraries or Python libraries that are supported

34
00:02:43,120 --> 00:02:43,960
on Windows.

35
00:02:44,320 --> 00:02:50,440
You won't be able to use libraries that are made for Linux only, and you won't be able to use programs

36
00:02:50,440 --> 00:02:53,680
that use Linux commands or Linux functions.

37
00:02:53,680 --> 00:02:59,500
For example, the Mac changer program that we built before that uses Linux commands.

38
00:02:59,500 --> 00:03:06,250
So even if you install a Python interpreter on Windows, the mac changer will still not work because

39
00:03:06,250 --> 00:03:08,470
it relies on Linux commands.

40
00:03:09,380 --> 00:03:12,410
So let's go ahead and install the Python interpreter.

41
00:03:12,440 --> 00:03:13,440
It's very easy.

42
00:03:13,460 --> 00:03:19,790
All you have to do is Google Python 2.7 for Windows and you'll get the latest.

43
00:03:19,790 --> 00:03:22,940
2.7 version is 2.7. 14.

44
00:03:23,210 --> 00:03:29,960
So I'm just going to scroll down and I'm going to download the x 86 MSI installer.

45
00:03:31,950 --> 00:03:34,470
This is going to go in my downloads directory.

46
00:03:35,820 --> 00:03:38,430
And I'm just going to double click it in here.

47
00:03:41,620 --> 00:03:43,680
And install it for all users.

48
00:03:43,690 --> 00:03:46,690
Click on next next next.

49
00:03:46,690 --> 00:03:48,250
And that will install it.

50
00:03:48,250 --> 00:03:49,900
I'm going to say yes.

51
00:03:50,020 --> 00:03:54,550
And just like any other Windows Installer, it's very easy to install and it's just going to do everything

52
00:03:54,550 --> 00:03:55,240
for you.

53
00:03:56,400 --> 00:03:56,810
Okay.

54
00:03:56,820 --> 00:03:57,690
Now this is done.

55
00:03:57,690 --> 00:04:02,250
I'm going to click on Finish and this would have installed it in.

56
00:04:02,250 --> 00:04:09,270
If we go to this PC and go to see you will see it in here and Python 27.

57
00:04:10,260 --> 00:04:16,500
Now you can see that there is a file called Python dot XY and this is the file that you need to call

58
00:04:16,530 --> 00:04:19,380
every time you want to run a Python program.

59
00:04:19,380 --> 00:04:22,950
So it's similar to the way that we type Python in Linux.

60
00:04:22,980 --> 00:04:28,320
Within Windows, you're going to have to type the location of this file, followed by the Python file

61
00:04:28,320 --> 00:04:29,490
that you want to run.

62
00:04:30,210 --> 00:04:38,670
Now, you probably noticed in my downloads I have already downloaded my ARP spoof dot py program, but

63
00:04:38,670 --> 00:04:39,720
it's very easy.

64
00:04:39,720 --> 00:04:43,320
You can transfer this to here and many ways you can send it by email.

65
00:04:43,320 --> 00:04:47,370
You can host it on your web server in Apache and Kali.

66
00:04:47,880 --> 00:04:51,720
Once you have this downloaded, we're going to go to our command prompt.

67
00:04:51,720 --> 00:04:55,620
So I'm just going to click on the windows and type CMD.

68
00:04:56,570 --> 00:04:59,510
This will open my command prompt for me in windows.

69
00:05:00,570 --> 00:05:09,210
And I'm already in C Z, so I just need to do CD downloads to go to my downloads directory.

70
00:05:09,210 --> 00:05:16,410
And if I do there to list all files and directories, you'll see I have my ARP spoof dot p in here.

71
00:05:17,220 --> 00:05:22,980
Now to run this, like I said, we're going to have to call the Python program that we just installed.

72
00:05:23,340 --> 00:05:30,420
So we're going to say this program is stored in C and the name of the directory is Python 27, just

73
00:05:30,420 --> 00:05:33,240
like I showed you in here in my PC.

74
00:05:33,840 --> 00:05:35,400
This directory right here.

75
00:05:36,370 --> 00:05:40,770
And then the file that we want to call is called Python Dot XY.

76
00:05:42,050 --> 00:05:45,650
And then we'll type the name of the script that we want to run.

77
00:05:45,650 --> 00:05:49,400
And my script is called ARP Spoof Dot PY.

78
00:05:49,880 --> 00:05:54,860
Now again, you can use the tabs here the same way that you use it in Linux in the terminal.

79
00:05:55,010 --> 00:06:01,580
And basically what I'm doing right now, you can think of all of this right here as if running the Python

80
00:06:01,580 --> 00:06:02,480
command in Linux.

81
00:06:02,480 --> 00:06:04,910
So in the next all we have to do is just type Python.

82
00:06:04,920 --> 00:06:08,240
It will know that we want to run a Python program in Windows.

83
00:06:08,240 --> 00:06:12,080
You have to give it the executable of the Python interpreter.

84
00:06:12,080 --> 00:06:17,720
So this is the program that's able to interpret the code and execute it on the system.

85
00:06:17,840 --> 00:06:22,620
And then we're giving it the name of our Python program, which is a spoof dot.

86
00:06:23,900 --> 00:06:27,470
Now, if I hit enter, I'm actually going to get an error.

87
00:06:27,620 --> 00:06:32,660
And as you can see, the error is telling us that Skippy is an unknown module.

88
00:06:32,780 --> 00:06:37,010
So this system right now doesn't know what Skippy is.

89
00:06:37,010 --> 00:06:45,080
And as we know, our ARP spoof program relies on Skippy now in Linux to install unknown libraries.

90
00:06:45,080 --> 00:06:50,900
All we have to do is do pip install and type the library name.

91
00:06:50,900 --> 00:06:56,750
So in our case we want to install Skippy, but this is a little bit different in windows.

92
00:06:56,750 --> 00:06:58,910
The PIP command is still the same.

93
00:06:58,910 --> 00:07:05,210
But what we want to do is you need to, first of all, call the Python interpreter right here.

94
00:07:06,580 --> 00:07:10,000
And pass all of this command as an argument.

95
00:07:11,200 --> 00:07:16,540
So basically what we're going to do is we're going to I'm going to paste this here and do that.

96
00:07:17,440 --> 00:07:21,880
And then we're saying, I want to use Pip to install Skype.

97
00:07:22,420 --> 00:07:29,860
So you call the interpreter first, followed by Dash, followed by the same PIP command that you use

98
00:07:29,860 --> 00:07:30,640
on Linux.

99
00:07:31,420 --> 00:07:32,740
I'm going to hit enter.

100
00:07:33,500 --> 00:07:36,020
And this is going to install CP for me.

101
00:07:37,000 --> 00:07:43,390
Now, as you can see, this is saying that Skippy has been successfully installed and now I can run

102
00:07:43,390 --> 00:07:50,620
my ARP spoof program by simply doing python dot exe followed by the program name.

103
00:07:51,900 --> 00:07:54,730
And as you can see, this is running the program for me.

104
00:07:54,750 --> 00:07:59,340
It's displaying a very similar output than what we usually get.

105
00:07:59,580 --> 00:08:06,750
And I've actually already modified the code in this program to spoof the machine.

106
00:08:06,900 --> 00:08:13,380
So if I just show you the code here real quick, you can see that I've set the target IP to the IP of

107
00:08:13,380 --> 00:08:16,020
the machine, which is ten 0 to 16.

108
00:08:16,320 --> 00:08:23,910
And right now, if I go on the machine and do RPA, you'll see that the router is Mac address is referring

109
00:08:23,910 --> 00:08:31,650
to the Mac address of the Windows machine, which means that our ARP spoof program is running properly.

110
00:08:32,440 --> 00:08:35,500
So this is the Mac address right here of the Gateway.

111
00:08:35,830 --> 00:08:40,360
And if the Windows machine in here, if I just open another terminal.

112
00:08:42,730 --> 00:08:46,510
And do IP config all.

113
00:08:49,150 --> 00:08:51,070
You'll see that the Mac address in here.

114
00:08:51,070 --> 00:08:52,660
This is the Windows Machine.

115
00:08:52,660 --> 00:08:57,820
Mac address is what's showing up as the Router's Mac address in here.

116
00:08:58,150 --> 00:09:05,350
So my ARP spoof program is working properly at the Windows Machine, spoofing the machine into thinking

117
00:09:05,350 --> 00:09:07,480
the Windows machine is the router.

118
00:09:09,170 --> 00:09:10,900
So that's it for this lecture.

119
00:09:10,910 --> 00:09:16,790
I just wanted to show you how to run Python programs on Windows because it's actually a very handy skill

120
00:09:16,790 --> 00:09:22,190
to learn when testing, because sometimes you find yourself in a situation where you need to run the

121
00:09:22,190 --> 00:09:23,690
program from Windows.

122
00:09:23,990 --> 00:09:29,930
Also, maybe in some scenario you only had access to Windows Machine and you wanted to run a Python

123
00:09:29,930 --> 00:09:31,820
program that you know how to do that.

124
00:09:31,910 --> 00:09:37,040
But I still recommend that you use Linux for all the programs that we're going to build and that we

125
00:09:37,040 --> 00:09:43,760
built so far and always use Linux as the operating system when you're testing or writing pen testing

126
00:09:43,760 --> 00:09:44,540
programs.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,170 --> 00:00:01,640
Okay.

2
00:00:01,650 --> 00:00:08,010
Now that we're able to run Python programs from Windows, I want to use the ARP spoof program that I

3
00:00:08,010 --> 00:00:12,360
wrote previously to Target The Killing Machine in here.

4
00:00:12,450 --> 00:00:19,260
And then in here we're going to write a program that can detect ARP spoofing attacks and show us a warning.

5
00:00:19,650 --> 00:00:24,340
Now, there are a number of ways that we can detect ARP spoofing attacks.

6
00:00:24,360 --> 00:00:27,540
The easiest is to watch the ARP table.

7
00:00:27,840 --> 00:00:36,750
So right now, if I do RPA, you'll see that I get the content in my ARP table and we can see the Mac

8
00:00:36,750 --> 00:00:38,430
address for my gateway.

9
00:00:39,060 --> 00:00:45,330
Now you can write a program that as soon as it gets executed, it stores the value for the Mac address

10
00:00:45,330 --> 00:00:46,560
for the router.

11
00:00:46,680 --> 00:00:51,460
And then keep an eye on that every 10 seconds or every 50 seconds.

12
00:00:51,480 --> 00:00:56,640
Run the same command again, run RPA again and check if the router is Mac.

13
00:00:56,640 --> 00:00:59,430
Address got changed to another Mac address.

14
00:00:59,430 --> 00:01:03,450
If it did, then the attacker is the person with the new Mac address.

15
00:01:04,020 --> 00:01:07,770
Now, this is a very good way of doing it and there is nothing wrong with it.

16
00:01:08,130 --> 00:01:14,610
The only problem is this way relies on the fact that when you run the program, you're not under an

17
00:01:14,610 --> 00:01:16,170
ARP spoofing attack.

18
00:01:16,170 --> 00:01:22,230
Because if you run the program while the attack is being executed, then you're going to start the first

19
00:01:22,230 --> 00:01:27,060
Mac address that you get as the proper actual Mac address of the router.

20
00:01:27,330 --> 00:01:30,510
So in this case, the attack got executed.

21
00:01:30,510 --> 00:01:34,950
Before you start this, Mac, and when you run your program, your program is going to think this Mac

22
00:01:34,950 --> 00:01:42,810
address is the MAC address for the router, where in fact it's not a better way of doing this is to

23
00:01:42,810 --> 00:01:46,260
look at the way our ARP spoofing attack works.

24
00:01:46,950 --> 00:01:54,060
So if we look at the ARP spoof programming here, and if you remember the spoof function is the function

25
00:01:54,060 --> 00:02:02,850
that sends the ARP responses that will poison the ARP table of the target person and change it so that

26
00:02:02,850 --> 00:02:06,810
the Routers Mac address is changed to the Mac address of the attacker.

27
00:02:07,770 --> 00:02:15,120
Now, the way this is done is using this line right here, which creates an ARP response of type is

28
00:02:15,120 --> 00:02:15,420
AD.

29
00:02:15,420 --> 00:02:20,370
So the opcode for that is two and we spoke about all of that in details before.

30
00:02:20,400 --> 00:02:26,340
That's why I'm going very quick right now and we set the destination to the target IP.

31
00:02:26,580 --> 00:02:35,100
We set the hardware destination to the MAC address of the target and we set the IP of the source to

32
00:02:35,100 --> 00:02:37,590
the IP of the router.

33
00:02:38,040 --> 00:02:44,130
So the source IP is the IP of the router and the source Mac, we're not doing anything with it.

34
00:02:44,130 --> 00:02:49,230
So it's being set by default, which is going to be the mac of the attacker.

35
00:02:49,830 --> 00:02:54,540
Now, just looking at this function should give you a good idea of detecting these attacks.

36
00:02:54,540 --> 00:02:58,650
We can wait for IP packets of type is at.

37
00:02:58,650 --> 00:03:06,900
So when the op field is equal to two and we're going to check if the source IP is the IP of the router.

38
00:03:08,320 --> 00:03:14,500
Now in order to capture packets, we need to use the snip function and we've already implemented a program

39
00:03:14,500 --> 00:03:17,830
that sniffs packets and that's the packet sniffer.

40
00:03:17,920 --> 00:03:20,140
So I'm just going to copy this program.

41
00:03:20,620 --> 00:03:25,030
I'm going to paste it in my new program, which is called ARP Spoof Detector.

42
00:03:26,150 --> 00:03:29,120
And I'm actually going to remove a lot of the functions in here.

43
00:03:29,120 --> 00:03:35,960
So I don't need to get URL and I don't need to get login info because these functions allow me to get

44
00:03:35,960 --> 00:03:38,360
the URL and the login information.

45
00:03:38,480 --> 00:03:42,050
If someone logs to a service, but that's not our goal.

46
00:03:42,620 --> 00:03:45,800
You can see we're also using the HTTP module in here.

47
00:03:45,800 --> 00:03:52,460
So I don't need that and I don't need any of the code in here in the process packet function.

48
00:03:52,460 --> 00:03:54,380
So I'm going to remove all of that.

49
00:03:55,560 --> 00:04:02,040
Now this cause, this nip function here and the sniff function will cause skippy sniff function which

50
00:04:02,040 --> 00:04:08,100
will call my callback function which is the process sniff packet and it will pass the packet for me

51
00:04:08,100 --> 00:04:09,990
as a variable called packet.

52
00:04:10,770 --> 00:04:13,950
Now the first thing that I want to check if this packet.

53
00:04:14,970 --> 00:04:15,990
Has a layer.

54
00:04:15,990 --> 00:04:17,190
So has layer.

55
00:04:18,590 --> 00:04:21,950
Of KP dot arp.

56
00:04:22,520 --> 00:04:25,050
So we use this a lot when we're checking for layers.

57
00:04:25,050 --> 00:04:26,660
So I'm just doing this very quickly.

58
00:04:26,960 --> 00:04:35,060
Now, if the packet has a Skippy ARP layer, we want to check if this packet is a response and if the

59
00:04:35,060 --> 00:04:39,290
OP field, so this field right here is set to two.

60
00:04:39,860 --> 00:04:47,900
So we're going to say and the packet and we want to access the ARP layer of the packet so we do scape

61
00:04:47,930 --> 00:04:49,250
dot arp.

62
00:04:49,580 --> 00:04:51,620
So again you can see the pattern.

63
00:04:51,620 --> 00:04:59,450
We usually do skip to raw to access the raw skippy, the TCP to access the TCP IP dot ARP, to access

64
00:04:59,450 --> 00:05:00,260
the ARP.

65
00:05:00,650 --> 00:05:04,040
And then the field name that we're interested in is the op field.

66
00:05:04,040 --> 00:05:05,960
So we do dot op.

67
00:05:07,160 --> 00:05:12,320
And we want to check if this is equal to two, then let's print.

68
00:05:13,050 --> 00:05:15,930
The packet dot show.

69
00:05:16,890 --> 00:05:23,520
So basically all we're doing is we're checking if the packet has an ARP layer and if this ARP layer

70
00:05:23,520 --> 00:05:25,920
is a response of type is AD.

71
00:05:25,920 --> 00:05:28,170
So if the OP is equal to two.

72
00:05:29,360 --> 00:05:30,020
Now.

73
00:05:30,020 --> 00:05:31,160
Let's go ahead.

74
00:05:31,190 --> 00:05:38,390
I'm already in the working directory of my Python program, so I'm just going to do python a spoof detector

75
00:05:38,390 --> 00:05:39,590
dot py.

76
00:05:40,720 --> 00:05:47,360
Now, as you can see, nothing's going to happen on screen because there are no IP responses being sent.

77
00:05:47,380 --> 00:05:55,300
So we're going to go to the Windows Machine and we're going to use our program, the ARP spoof Dot Pi

78
00:05:55,330 --> 00:05:56,080
program.

79
00:05:56,590 --> 00:05:59,740
Now, as soon as we run this, if we go back to Kelly.

80
00:06:00,980 --> 00:06:04,520
You'll see that we'll start receiving IRP responses.

81
00:06:04,700 --> 00:06:09,380
So I'm just going to do Control C and I'm going to go all the way to the top.

82
00:06:10,100 --> 00:06:13,850
And you can see all of these are AARP responses.

83
00:06:14,390 --> 00:06:15,800
So that's perfect.

84
00:06:15,800 --> 00:06:24,320
For now, we're able to filter AARP responses and display detailed information about them on screen.

85
00:06:24,800 --> 00:06:32,090
And the next lecture, we're going to first analyze these responses, learn how we can use the information

86
00:06:32,090 --> 00:06:35,180
in it to detect AARP spoofing attacks.

87
00:06:35,180 --> 00:06:42,290
And then we'll implement this in our program so it can automatically detect AARP spoofing attacks.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,590 --> 00:00:07,070
So our program now captures ARP responses and print them on screen for us.

2
00:00:07,950 --> 00:00:15,030
In this lecture, I want to show you how to analyze these responses and modify our program so that it

3
00:00:15,030 --> 00:00:22,140
can automatically detect a ARP spoofing attacks based on the information in these responses.

4
00:00:23,030 --> 00:00:29,450
So if we scroll down a little bit, you'll see we have an ARP response in here.

5
00:00:29,630 --> 00:00:36,830
So it's an is at as you can see, the OP and this AARP response is coming from the Windows machine.

6
00:00:36,830 --> 00:00:44,840
So this is its MAC address, but it's pretending or it's saying that the source IP is the Router's IP.

7
00:00:45,380 --> 00:00:50,840
Now, if you scroll down again, you'll see we have another packet like this one.

8
00:00:50,960 --> 00:00:59,360
Again, the ops is at the IP sources, the router, but the MAC address is the Windows Mac address.

9
00:00:59,720 --> 00:01:08,660
Now for each IP response that we're listing in here, if we can verify that this IP has this MAC address,

10
00:01:08,660 --> 00:01:14,060
then we'll know that this packet is a legitimate packet and we shouldn't worry about it.

11
00:01:14,510 --> 00:01:21,710
But if we check, if we get the MAC address of this IP and we get a different Mac address, then we'll

12
00:01:21,710 --> 00:01:27,920
know that this person right here is pretending to have this IP address.

13
00:01:28,460 --> 00:01:35,690
Now, luckily, we have a function that we can give it an IP address and it will give us the MAC address.

14
00:01:35,840 --> 00:01:41,300
So let's go up and go to my ARP spoof program again.

15
00:01:41,690 --> 00:01:45,530
And as you remember, we have a function in here called Get Mac.

16
00:01:46,070 --> 00:01:51,320
So I'm just going to copy this function and we're going to paste it in here and our code.

17
00:01:52,850 --> 00:01:59,090
And as you remember, the way this function works is you give it the IP address and it returns the MAC

18
00:01:59,090 --> 00:01:59,780
address.

19
00:02:00,350 --> 00:02:06,500
And this is where you see the beauty of refactoring and splitting your code into smaller functions.

20
00:02:06,740 --> 00:02:09,850
Because right now I don't need to worry about how this works.

21
00:02:09,860 --> 00:02:11,870
In fact, I forgot how this works.

22
00:02:12,050 --> 00:02:18,080
But all I know is if I give this get my function an IP, it will give me the MAC address.

23
00:02:18,830 --> 00:02:25,880
So in my code in here, once I check that this is an ARP response, I'm going to say Real Mac.

24
00:02:27,440 --> 00:02:29,840
This equals to get Mac.

25
00:02:30,830 --> 00:02:36,830
And I need to pass the IP to this function and we can get the IP by doing packet.

26
00:02:37,920 --> 00:02:39,360
And we want to access.

27
00:02:39,360 --> 00:02:42,480
Let's see which layer we need to access to get the IP from.

28
00:02:42,510 --> 00:02:54,540
It's in the ARP layer and it's in the P, s or C, so we're going to access scapegoat ARP and the IP

29
00:02:54,540 --> 00:02:55,310
is the P.

30
00:02:55,350 --> 00:02:56,730
S or C.

31
00:02:57,930 --> 00:03:04,020
Now this will get me the real mark and then I'm going to use another variable and we'll call this the

32
00:03:04,020 --> 00:03:04,980
response mark.

33
00:03:04,980 --> 00:03:12,030
So it's the mark that we got in the response and we can get that from packet escapee Dot.

34
00:03:12,040 --> 00:03:22,890
R.P. And if we look in here, it's the h w as RC, so it's thought h w as RC.

35
00:03:23,920 --> 00:03:30,010
And then I'm going to use an if statement to check if the real Mac is the same as the Mac.

36
00:03:30,010 --> 00:03:32,020
That is in the response Mac.

37
00:03:32,860 --> 00:03:34,960
If it is, then everything is fine.

38
00:03:34,960 --> 00:03:39,970
So what I should actually do is check if the real Mac is not the response.

39
00:03:39,970 --> 00:03:40,540
Mac.

40
00:03:42,690 --> 00:03:48,180
If it's not, we're going to print on screen a message saying you're under attack.

41
00:03:50,440 --> 00:03:58,660
So basically all we're doing right now is we're checking if the Mac address that's in the W RC is the

42
00:03:58,660 --> 00:04:04,960
actual MAC address that we get if we send this IP to our get Mac function.

43
00:04:05,620 --> 00:04:09,400
So right now, my program is already running.

44
00:04:09,670 --> 00:04:16,810
So if we use the first way of implementing the ARP spoof detector that I spoke about, then this will

45
00:04:16,810 --> 00:04:17,470
not work.

46
00:04:17,470 --> 00:04:20,080
We will not be able to detect an attack.

47
00:04:20,080 --> 00:04:21,960
But let's try it right now.

48
00:04:21,970 --> 00:04:25,690
Actually, I'm going to clear the screen and run my program.

49
00:04:27,940 --> 00:04:29,470
And we got an error.

50
00:04:30,040 --> 00:04:35,040
Now, this error is coming from the get mark function in here.

51
00:04:35,050 --> 00:04:43,930
And I think the reason for this is the test field in some of the packets was set to the IP of this machine.

52
00:04:44,500 --> 00:04:50,110
Therefore, the get mark was breaking because it wasn't able to get the Mac address.

53
00:04:50,140 --> 00:04:58,300
But what we can do is we can just put a try, except we can just wrap this with a try to do all of this

54
00:04:58,300 --> 00:04:59,140
code.

55
00:05:00,150 --> 00:05:01,470
And then use.

56
00:05:01,470 --> 00:05:05,040
Except now we covered this before as well.

57
00:05:06,310 --> 00:05:10,030
And we're going to accept this error, which is an index error.

58
00:05:12,750 --> 00:05:16,040
And if we get this error, we're just going to do nothing.

59
00:05:16,050 --> 00:05:17,610
So we're going to do pass.

60
00:05:18,180 --> 00:05:21,600
So basically what we're saying is try to do this for me.

61
00:05:21,630 --> 00:05:24,360
If you get an index error, then don't do anything.

62
00:05:24,360 --> 00:05:27,000
Just pass and continue your execution.

63
00:05:27,930 --> 00:05:30,330
Let's go down and run the code again.

64
00:05:31,750 --> 00:05:38,080
And as you can see, it's given us an alert telling us that we are under attack.

65
00:05:39,060 --> 00:05:45,840
Now, let me stop this and let me go to the Windows Machine and I'll stop this as well.

66
00:05:46,990 --> 00:05:54,820
And I'll clear the screen and let's run this again and see if it'll still give us a false positive,

67
00:05:54,820 --> 00:05:58,900
if it will tell us that we are under attack when we are not under attack.

68
00:06:00,230 --> 00:06:03,840
But you can see that it's not given us any false positives.

69
00:06:03,840 --> 00:06:09,000
And let's try, for example, just go bing google.com.

70
00:06:10,260 --> 00:06:15,150
And you can see that we have Internet access, we're sending packets and we're receiving responses.

71
00:06:15,150 --> 00:06:16,700
So it's all fine.

72
00:06:16,710 --> 00:06:22,320
And then if someone goes ahead and runs an ARP spoofing attack against us.

73
00:06:23,110 --> 00:06:25,900
Our script should tell us that we are under attack.

74
00:06:26,110 --> 00:06:27,320
And here we go.

75
00:06:27,340 --> 00:06:32,590
As you can see, the script is given us an alert that we are being attacked.

76
00:06:33,450 --> 00:06:40,740
So now this script works if you're on the script before or while the attack is being executed, and

77
00:06:40,740 --> 00:06:46,350
therefore it's a very good script that's able to detect ARP spoofing attacks.

78
00:06:47,310 --> 00:06:53,970
Now you can actually add code to your script now to, for example, play a warning like an MP three

79
00:06:53,970 --> 00:06:57,330
or send an email saying that you're under attack.

80
00:06:57,690 --> 00:07:03,540
I'll cover sending emails from Python later on in the course and if you want to add a warning, then

81
00:07:03,540 --> 00:07:05,540
that's something you can explore on your own.

82
00:07:05,550 --> 00:07:10,920
It's actually very easy, so I don't want to make this course longer covering stuff that's easy for

83
00:07:10,920 --> 00:07:11,970
you to figure out.

84
00:07:11,970 --> 00:07:14,010
I want to focus on the main things.

85
00:07:14,580 --> 00:07:17,970
So that's it for this program, a very simple program.

86
00:07:17,970 --> 00:07:21,510
And we actually pretty much used everything that we learned so far.

87
00:07:21,510 --> 00:07:23,970
We didn't really implement anything new.

88
00:07:23,970 --> 00:07:29,550
We reused a lot of our functions and a lot of the concepts that we learned so far.

89
00:07:29,850 --> 00:07:37,500
And literally just building this very small program shows how good it is to always write your programs

90
00:07:37,500 --> 00:07:44,820
as module programs and divide them into small functions so that in the future, if you need any of these

91
00:07:44,820 --> 00:07:48,480
functions, you can go ahead and reuse them very easily.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

# Section 12: Writing Malware

1
00:00:01,170 --> 00:00:02,610
This is going to be fun.

2
00:00:02,640 --> 00:00:06,060
In this section, we're going to start programming malware.

3
00:00:06,870 --> 00:00:15,510
If you think of it, keyloggers viruses and backdoors are just programs that carry out a specific task.

4
00:00:15,630 --> 00:00:23,640
For example, a backdoor is a program that solves the problem of allowing you to gain access to a target

5
00:00:23,640 --> 00:00:24,360
system.

6
00:00:24,540 --> 00:00:30,330
So right in malware is really just writing evil programs in the section.

7
00:00:30,330 --> 00:00:37,200
We're going to start by writing simple but powerful evil files, files that will allow you to download

8
00:00:37,200 --> 00:00:42,810
and execute code, execute and report the result of code and so on.

9
00:00:43,080 --> 00:00:49,350
You're going to learn how to combine all of these files together to run powerful attacks and gain access

10
00:00:49,350 --> 00:00:50,310
to systems.

11
00:00:50,550 --> 00:00:55,560
Once done with that, we're going to move to writing more complex, evil programs.

12
00:00:55,680 --> 00:01:02,460
We're going to write a program that can record every key strike entered by the target and send it to

13
00:01:02,460 --> 00:01:03,810
our own email.

14
00:01:03,810 --> 00:01:09,360
This is known as a keylogger, so we're going to write our own remote keylogger.

15
00:01:10,080 --> 00:01:16,860
We'll also write a program that, when executed on a target system, it will give us full control over

16
00:01:16,860 --> 00:01:17,790
that system.

17
00:01:18,060 --> 00:01:24,210
This is known as a backdoor, and you guessed it, in this section we're going to build our own remote

18
00:01:24,210 --> 00:01:24,930
backdoor.

19
00:01:25,590 --> 00:01:31,530
Not only that, but by the end of the section, I'm going to show you how to convert all of these evil

20
00:01:31,530 --> 00:01:38,640
files into Trojans so that they look and function just like any other file type, like an image or a

21
00:01:38,640 --> 00:01:39,480
PDF.

22
00:01:41,280 --> 00:01:47,460
All of the evil programs that we're going to write in the section will work against all operating systems,

23
00:01:47,460 --> 00:01:50,040
against Windows, OSX and Linux.

24
00:01:50,130 --> 00:01:57,000
Not only that, but they'll also bypass antivirus programs because they'll be custom made by us.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,090 --> 00:00:01,610
Okay.

2
00:00:01,630 --> 00:00:06,310
So I want to start the section with a very simple evil file.

3
00:00:06,520 --> 00:00:08,230
We'll use that as a warmup.

4
00:00:08,230 --> 00:00:14,020
So you get used to the idea of all of the files that we're going to build on how I'm going to be transferring

5
00:00:14,020 --> 00:00:15,340
them and testing them.

6
00:00:16,060 --> 00:00:23,020
So the file that I want to build is going to allow me to execute system commands on the system that

7
00:00:23,020 --> 00:00:24,880
it gets executed on.

8
00:00:25,390 --> 00:00:33,640
So this file will work against all operating systems as long as you use commands that work on that operating

9
00:00:33,640 --> 00:00:34,200
system.

10
00:00:34,210 --> 00:00:39,150
So if you put a Windows Command, this Windows Command will work on Windows Computers.

11
00:00:39,160 --> 00:00:45,670
If you put a Unix command, then it will work on Mac OS, X, Android and even Linux.

12
00:00:46,690 --> 00:00:51,940
Now for this lecture and all the coming lectures, we're going to be using the programs or the evil

13
00:00:51,940 --> 00:00:55,120
files that we build as is as Python scripts.

14
00:00:55,120 --> 00:01:01,600
And at the end, I'll show you how we can package these programs as executables for Windows, for Linux

15
00:01:01,600 --> 00:01:03,400
or for OS X.

16
00:01:04,030 --> 00:01:10,810
So I already have my pie chart running here and I've already created a new project and a new file called

17
00:01:10,810 --> 00:01:12,100
Execute Command.

18
00:01:12,580 --> 00:01:14,980
And this file is going to be very, very simple.

19
00:01:14,980 --> 00:01:16,480
It's going to take a variable.

20
00:01:16,480 --> 00:01:23,740
So we'll call this variable command, and this is going to be the command that I want to run and then

21
00:01:23,740 --> 00:01:25,930
we're going to use sub process.

22
00:01:25,930 --> 00:01:28,330
So I'll do import.

23
00:01:29,270 --> 00:01:30,470
So process.

24
00:01:30,470 --> 00:01:37,190
So this is a module that we've seen before and used in a number of programs and we're going to say some

25
00:01:37,190 --> 00:01:38,420
process.

26
00:01:39,770 --> 00:01:42,110
Dot P open.

27
00:01:42,320 --> 00:01:48,680
Now, p open is one of the functions that you can use with sub process in order to execute system commands.

28
00:01:48,890 --> 00:01:56,060
The cool thing about the P open is that it executes the command that you give it and continues the program

29
00:01:56,060 --> 00:02:00,020
execution so it doesn't wait for the command to finish execution.

30
00:02:00,920 --> 00:02:06,440
So the command that we're going to execute is going to be the command stored in the command variable.

31
00:02:06,800 --> 00:02:09,950
And we're going to set Shell to.

32
00:02:09,950 --> 00:02:10,700
True.

33
00:02:12,340 --> 00:02:12,960
And that's it.

34
00:02:12,970 --> 00:02:15,070
Our script is done very simple.

35
00:02:15,070 --> 00:02:16,530
It takes a command variable.

36
00:02:16,540 --> 00:02:21,250
It executes the command using the p open function of the sub process module.

37
00:02:22,030 --> 00:02:27,870
Now, like I said, the command here can be any command you want for any operating system.

38
00:02:27,880 --> 00:02:29,950
So you can put Windows commands here.

39
00:02:29,950 --> 00:02:36,280
If your target is Windows, you can put UNIX commands if your target is going to be Linux or OS X.

40
00:02:37,530 --> 00:02:40,860
Now for today's lecture, because I want to keep this very simple.

41
00:02:40,890 --> 00:02:44,250
We're going to be using a very simple Windows Command.

42
00:02:44,580 --> 00:02:51,930
So if I just go to the Windows Machine here and open my command prompt so I'll type CMD.

43
00:02:53,590 --> 00:02:58,770
And if we just type MSG followed by a star, followed by a message.

44
00:02:58,780 --> 00:03:03,250
So let's say you have been hacked.

45

You'll see that we'll get a message saying you have been hacked.

46
00:03:08,260 --> 00:03:14,200
Now, obviously, this is nothing useful, but it just works for our example because I want to show

47
00:03:14,200 --> 00:03:18,550
you that this script will execute any system command that you want.

48
00:03:19,420 --> 00:03:24,380
So in the command, we're just going to use the exact same command that we used right now.

49
00:03:24,400 --> 00:03:31,180
So we're going to say MSG, followed by a star, followed by You have been hacked.

50
00:03:31,630 --> 00:03:38,390
Now I've saved this and this is going to be saved in the project directory for py charm.

51
00:03:38,890 --> 00:03:42,520
So I configured that to be in my home directory.

52
00:03:43,310 --> 00:03:46,160
In a directory called Pie Chart Project.

53
00:03:47,540 --> 00:03:51,560
And the name of this project is Execute Command.

54
00:03:51,950 --> 00:03:54,110
And this is the file right here.

55
00:03:54,920 --> 00:03:58,220
Now, I want to transfer this file to my Windows machine.

56
00:03:58,490 --> 00:04:00,260
So I'm going to copy this file.

57
00:04:01,870 --> 00:04:10,120
And I'm going to put it in my vaa ww w html and in here I've actually manually made the directory called

58
00:04:10,120 --> 00:04:16,960
Evil Files so you can right click and create a new folder and I'm actually going to paste this inside

59
00:04:16,960 --> 00:04:23,350
this directory, so I'm just going to do control V And as you can see, this is the file that we want.

60
00:04:23,950 --> 00:04:28,180
Now, as you probably know by now, Kelly comes with a web server.

61
00:04:28,180 --> 00:04:38,110
So if I just go to my terminal and do service Apache to start this will start my web server and I'll

62

be able to access this web server and all of its files using the IP of Caleb.

63
00:04:42,940 --> 00:04:45,160
So if I do if config here.

64
00:04:46,020 --> 00:04:51,210
You'll see that the IP 480 is ten zero 216.

65
00:04:51,810 --> 00:04:57,780
So if I go to my Windows machine, go to my browser and go to ten zero 216.

66

I'll be able to access my web server, which has an index saying, ha ha ha.

67
00:05:04,020 --> 00:05:11,430
And then if I put a forward slash eval files, I'll be able to access all of the files stored in the

68
00:05:11,430 --> 00:05:12,840
evil files directory.

69
00:05:13,440 --> 00:05:19,050
Now, the file that I want to download right now is called Execute Command Dot Pie.

70
00:05:19,560 --> 00:05:21,030

71

Save it.

72

And this will go into my downloads directory, as you can see in here.

73
00:05:28,810 --> 00:05:34,120
Now I can run this using my python interpreter just like I showed you before when we were running the

74
00:05:34,120 --> 00:05:35,830
ARP spoof API.

75
00:05:36,370 --> 00:05:41,770
So from my command prompt, I'm going to do see the downloads.

76

And then I'm going to put the location where my python interpreter is installed on.

77
00:05:47,320 --> 00:05:53,740
So it's installed in my C Python 27 and it's called Python dot x.

78
00:05:55,120 --> 00:06:01,570
And I'm going to follow this with the name of the file that I want to run, which is called Execute

79

Command Dot py.

80
00:06:03,430 --> 00:06:10,630
So again, this is exactly the same the way that we used when we were running the ARP spoof Python script.

81
00:06:10,630 --> 00:06:15,920
And it's the same way that you want to use when you want to run any Python script on Windows.

82
00:06:15,940 --> 00:06:18,940
After installing the Python interpreter.

83
00:06:19,750 --> 00:06:24,970
Now I know this is not the best way of running evil files, especially when you're targeting a person

84
00:06:24,970 --> 00:06:28,390
and you're using social engineering to get that person to run your file.

85
00:06:28,390 --> 00:06:32,410
You can't tell them to install Python first and then run it this way.

86
00:06:32,440 --> 00:06:38,110
But we're just testing right now, and later on I'll show you how you can package this into a normal

87
00:06:38,140 --> 00:06:43,570
X or even make it look like an image, a PDF or any other file type.

88
00:06:44,020 --> 00:06:48,400
So for now, we're still in the testing stage and all the programs that we're going to build.

89
00:06:48,400 --> 00:06:52,360
They're going to have a Python extension and we're going to test them like so.

90
00:06:52,630 --> 00:06:54,910
So for now, I'm going to hit enter to run this.

91
00:06:56,980 --> 00:06:58,660
And we got an error.

92
00:06:58,810 --> 00:07:05,010
Now, we actually wasn't sure why I got this error because we tested the message command and it works,

93
00:07:05,020 --> 00:07:06,610
so I had to Google it.

94
00:07:06,760 --> 00:07:13,300
And in the result, I got a result from Stack Overflow, which is a really good website for you to debug

95
00:07:13,300 --> 00:07:14,920
when you get errors like this.

96
00:07:15,370 --> 00:07:18,100
And the first result explains it very well.

97
00:07:18,400 --> 00:07:24,670
Basically, the message command that I use is only available for 64 bit.

98
00:07:24,820 --> 00:07:29,650
So when I'm running the command prompt in here, it's running in 64 bit.

99
00:07:29,800 --> 00:07:33,070
And therefore when I use the message command, it works.

100
00:07:33,370 --> 00:07:39,700
But if you remember when I installed Python, I installed the 32 bit version of the interpreter.

101
00:07:39,910 --> 00:07:44,740
Therefore, when Python tries to call this command, this command doesn't exist.

102
00:07:45,220 --> 00:07:51,340
So if you're calling it from a 32 bit architecture, you're going to have to use the full syntax for

103
00:07:51,340 --> 00:07:57,940
the location for this command for the 64 bit, because it's only available for the 64 bit, which is

104
00:07:57,940 --> 00:07:59,200
this one right here.

105
00:07:59,620 --> 00:08:04,630
So instead of just saying message, you're going to have to use this full command instead of the message.

106
00:08:05,050 --> 00:08:07,330
Now I'm just going to modify the script locally.

107
00:08:07,510 --> 00:08:15,790
So if I just go to my start menu and I've actually installed a text editor called Sublime Text, I'll

108

link it in the resources.
