110
00:08:20,320 --> 00:08:26,350
Instead of me going back to Linux, editing the script there and coming back and I'm just going to open

111
00:08:26,350 --> 00:08:31,750
the script, which is in downloads and it's called Execute Command.

112
00:08:32,800 --> 00:08:38,860
And I'm going to replace the message here with the full path, as shown in Stack Overflow.

113
00:08:38,890 --> 00:08:40,480
I'm going to save this.

114
00:08:40,480 --> 00:08:41,440
Quit it.

115
00:08:43,390 --> 00:08:45,700
And run my command again.

116
00:08:47,550 --> 00:08:50,250
And as you can see, the message box works.

117
00:08:50,250 --> 00:08:58,200
And basically the code that we used inside our script right here got executed.

118
00:08:59,150 --> 00:08:59,750
Now.

119
00:08:59,750 --> 00:09:01,410
This is a very simple script.

120
00:09:01,430 --> 00:09:06,710
The main reason why I made a full lecture on the script because I want you to get comfortable with the

121
00:09:06,710 --> 00:09:11,060
way I'm going to be transferring files between Linux and the target operating system.

122
00:09:11,060 --> 00:09:17,570
So in the future, I'm not going to show you how am I going to copy this to the HTML and then download

123
00:09:17,570 --> 00:09:19,100
it from the target computer?

124
00:09:19,220 --> 00:09:24,260
I'm going to cut that part of the video and you'll just see me writing the code in here and then tested

125
00:09:24,260 --> 00:09:29,060
it in the Windows Machine or the target machine, whether it's Windows or Linux or OS X.

126
00:09:29,360 --> 00:09:34,490
And I also wanted to just get you comfortable with the way that we're writing simple scripts that will

127
00:09:34,490 --> 00:09:39,770
execute commands or do evil stuff on the target that are useful to us.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,530 --> 00:00:07,250
The script that we built so far is actually very useful because it can be used to execute any system

2
00:00:07,250 --> 00:00:09,050
command on any system.

3
00:00:09,140 --> 00:00:16,910
So if your target is UNIX based like Linux and OS X, then you'll be able to run any UNIX based command.

4
00:00:16,910 --> 00:00:21,230
And these commands allow you to pretty much gain full control over the system.

5
00:00:21,830 --> 00:00:25,940
If your target is Windows, then you can run any Windows Command.

6
00:00:25,940 --> 00:00:32,060
Plus you have all the PowerShell commands that you can run on Windows ten and Windows eight, which

7
00:00:32,060 --> 00:00:35,810
basically again allow you to gain full control over the system.

8
00:00:36,470 --> 00:00:42,950
In this lecture, I want to take this one step further and we're going to extend the script so that

9
00:00:42,950 --> 00:00:49,850
it's, first of all, execute the command and then send us the result of that command on our email.

10
00:00:50,000 --> 00:00:56,810
So in many cases, we're not interested into executing the command itself, but we're interested in

11
00:00:56,810 --> 00:00:59,480
the result that this command returns.

12
00:01:00,290 --> 00:01:01,880
Let me show you an example.

13
00:01:02,150 --> 00:01:05,390
So, first of all, I'm just going to go to my windows machine right here.

14
00:01:05,600 --> 00:01:16,160
So if we just type net, S-H, lan show profile, this will list all the wi fi networks that this computer

15
00:01:16,160 --> 00:01:17,360
has connected to.

16
00:01:17,960 --> 00:01:23,780
So you can see that this computer connected to two wi fi networks previously, it doesn't have to be

17
00:01:23,780 --> 00:01:25,180
connected to them right now.

18
00:01:25,190 --> 00:01:31,820
As you can see, I'm not connected to wi fi, but previously it was connected to these two networks

19
00:01:31,820 --> 00:01:33,020
at some stage.

20
00:01:33,830 --> 00:01:40,880
Now we can also list all of the information about a specific network by putting the network name after

21
00:01:40,880 --> 00:01:42,620
or at the end of the command.

22
00:01:42,980 --> 00:01:48,830
So if I just paste this here and hit enter, you'll see.

23
00:01:48,830 --> 00:01:52,040
I'll get all of the information about this network.

24
00:01:53,720 --> 00:02:01,250
And if I run the exact same command again and at key equals clear, which means I want you to show me

25
00:02:01,250 --> 00:02:03,320
the key in clear text.

26
00:02:04,340 --> 00:02:05,090
You'll see.

27
00:02:05,090 --> 00:02:10,760
I'll even see the wi fi password for this network, which is this one right here.

28
00:02:11,710 --> 00:02:16,290
So this is an example of a command that we're interested in its result.

29
00:02:16,300 --> 00:02:22,000
So when this command is executed on the system, it doesn't really do anything useful to us unless we

30
00:02:22,000 --> 00:02:23,770
can see the result.

31
00:02:23,770 --> 00:02:27,640
And this is where the execute and the report scripts are useful.

32
00:02:28,180 --> 00:02:33,850
So basically the script that I want to build should be able to, first of all, execute this command

33
00:02:34,570 --> 00:02:42,010
and then send all of this result to my email so that I can read it and see the wi fi password.

34
00:02:42,970 --> 00:02:49,090
Again, this is just an example of one command that returns useful results, but you can extend this

35
00:02:49,090 --> 00:02:50,050
to any scenario.

36
00:02:50,080 --> 00:02:56,800
You can even run standalone programs that, for example, steal passwords and then email you the passwords.

37
00:02:57,400 --> 00:02:59,080
So let's see how we can do that.

38
00:03:00,000 --> 00:03:02,910
So first of all, it's important sub process.

39
00:03:03,030 --> 00:03:08,850
And I'm also going to import another library that allow me to send emails from my python code.

40
00:03:08,850 --> 00:03:12,540
And the name of this library is SMTP lib.

41
00:03:13,940 --> 00:03:20,870
Then I'm going to write a function called send mail that will allow me to send emails from my python

42
00:03:20,870 --> 00:03:21,500
code.

43
00:03:21,950 --> 00:03:25,820
So I'm going to start by saying def send mail.

44
00:03:27,320 --> 00:03:29,900
And this function is going to take three arguments.

45
00:03:29,930 --> 00:03:32,120
First of all, the email address.

46
00:03:32,660 --> 00:03:34,580
Second is the password.

47
00:03:35,330 --> 00:03:37,250
And third is the message.

48
00:03:37,250 --> 00:03:40,280
So it's the content of the message that we want to send.

49
00:03:41,060 --> 00:03:47,630
Now, the first thing that we need to do is create an instance of an SMTP server, and we're going to

50
00:03:47,630 --> 00:03:50,360
use the SMTP library to do that.

51
00:03:50,990 --> 00:03:58,640
So I'm going to say server is equal to SMTP, lib dot SMTP.

52
00:03:58,670 --> 00:04:03,200
So this is going to create an SMTP server instance for me.

53
00:04:04,250 --> 00:04:08,300
When I create this object, I need to give the server that I want to use.

54
00:04:08,300 --> 00:04:14,450
And we're going to use Google's SMTP server because Google allow people to use their own server to send

55
00:04:14,450 --> 00:04:18,980
emails and the address for their server is SMTP.

56
00:04:19,550 --> 00:04:21,320
Dot gmail.

57
00:04:21,320 --> 00:04:22,370
Dot com.

58
00:04:23,730 --> 00:04:30,720
And the second argument in here is going to be the port and Google runs their server on port five,

59
00:04:30,720 --> 00:04:31,610
eight, seven.

60
00:04:31,620 --> 00:04:37,350
So I'm using this port and I'm using the server because this is Google Server and this is the port that

61
00:04:37,350 --> 00:04:39,360
Google run their server on.

62
00:04:39,990 --> 00:04:45,570
And this full line right here will create an instance of an SMTP server.

63
00:04:45,600 --> 00:04:52,320
It's going to start that in my server variable so that I can use this in order to send my email.

64
00:04:52,950 --> 00:04:59,520
The next thing that I'm going to need to do is initiate a TLS connection using the server that I just

65
00:04:59,520 --> 00:05:00,390
created.

66
00:05:00,690 --> 00:05:04,080
So I'm just going to do server 30 less.

67
00:05:05,890 --> 00:05:10,510
Next, we're going to need to log in to our email so that we can send emails.

68
00:05:10,510 --> 00:05:16,300
So this is similar to when you go to gmail.com and log in using username and password.

69
00:05:16,900 --> 00:05:18,700
So we're going to say server.

70
00:05:19,550 --> 00:05:20,750
Dot login.

71
00:05:21,930 --> 00:05:28,110
And we're going to log in with the email and password that will be passed to this function.

72
00:05:28,440 --> 00:05:32,130
So we're going to log in with email and password.

73
00:05:33,160 --> 00:05:39,160
And finally, we're going to send out an email by saying server that sent mail.

74
00:05:41,310 --> 00:05:47,970
And the way this function works is the first argument is the from the second argument is the two and

75
00:05:47,970 --> 00:05:51,060
the third argument is the content of the email.

76
00:05:51,480 --> 00:05:54,320
So we're going to send this email from our own email.

77
00:05:54,330 --> 00:05:55,980
So this is going to be email.

78
00:05:55,980 --> 00:05:58,500
So it's the email variable that we get in here.

79
00:05:58,800 --> 00:06:03,600
We're going to send this to our email as well because we don't want to send this to another person.

80
00:06:04,500 --> 00:06:10,350
And the content of the message is going to be the message that, again, we got as an input to this

81
00:06:10,350 --> 00:06:11,040
function.

82
00:06:11,910 --> 00:06:18,670
Now, once we're done with using the SMTP server, we're going to have to close it by doing server dot

83
00:06:18,690 --> 00:06:19,320
quit.

84
00:06:20,950 --> 00:06:28,480
So now we have a generic function called send mail that we can use to send an email with a specific

85
00:06:28,480 --> 00:06:29,170
message.

86
00:06:29,530 --> 00:06:35,440
Now, the nice thing about this is that we can use this function into any other code that we might need

87
00:06:35,440 --> 00:06:37,150
in order to send an email.

88
00:06:37,150 --> 00:06:39,640
We might want to just send an email to a person.

89
00:06:39,640 --> 00:06:46,210
We might want to send that report from our own computer, or we can use it for evil stuff like the way

90
00:06:46,210 --> 00:06:47,860
that we're going to use it right now.

91
00:06:48,550 --> 00:06:54,760
So basically the way that this function works is it takes an email, it takes a password and a message.

92
00:06:55,480 --> 00:07:04,750
It starts by creating a server variable that is an instance of an SMTP server using the SMTP library.

93
00:07:04,960 --> 00:07:09,510
And when you create this object, you have to specify the server that you want to use.

94
00:07:09,520 --> 00:07:16,660
And we're using Google's SMTP server and we're specifying the port that Google server works on.

95
00:07:17,410 --> 00:07:20,200
We're initiating a TLS connection here.

96
00:07:20,350 --> 00:07:23,530
We log in using our username and password.

97
00:07:23,650 --> 00:07:31,060
We send the email from our email to our email and we send the message variable that's passed this function

98
00:07:31,210 --> 00:07:32,980
and then we quit the server.

99
00:07:33,790 --> 00:07:39,820
So now in our code in here, first of all, we need to change this command and we're going to set this

100
00:07:39,820 --> 00:07:42,940
to the command that we want to execute on the system.

101
00:07:43,180 --> 00:07:47,110
And looking at this and here, this is going to be this command.

102
00:07:48,010 --> 00:07:52,120
So it's going to be net's h lan.

103
00:07:53,210 --> 00:07:54,710
Show profile.

104
00:07:55,490 --> 00:07:58,400
And then we're going to put the network name that we want to get.

105
00:07:58,400 --> 00:07:59,350
It's password.

106
00:07:59,360 --> 00:08:05,750
So in my case, it's UPC 723762.

107
00:08:06,530 --> 00:08:10,490
And we're going to say we want to see the key in plain text.

108
00:08:10,490 --> 00:08:14,930
So we spoke about this command and we've seen exactly how it works.

109
00:08:15,500 --> 00:08:18,440
Then we're using set process to run this command.

110
00:08:18,440 --> 00:08:22,730
But instead of using P open, I'm going to use check output.

111
00:08:23,280 --> 00:08:30,210
So this is a function in a process that executes a command and return its result.

112
00:08:30,360 --> 00:08:34,380
And we actually had a detailed look on it before in this course.

113
00:08:34,590 --> 00:08:40,740
So the result that will be returned is going to be the result that we see in here on the command prompt.

114
00:08:41,070 --> 00:08:44,520
So I'm going to capture that in a variable called result.

115
00:08:46,060 --> 00:08:54,310
And then I'm going to use my sentinel function to send an email from my own email, which is John Wick

116
00:08:54,640 --> 00:08:59,500
70 at Gmail dot com.

117
00:09:00,300 --> 00:09:01,890
I'm going to set the password.

118
00:09:02,890 --> 00:09:08,740
Which is abc1, two, three, a, b, c, one, two.

119
00:09:09,740 --> 00:09:14,060
And then the content of this message is going to be the result of the command.

120
00:09:16,390 --> 00:09:17,590
And we're done.

121
00:09:18,520 --> 00:09:23,420
So basically, I'm going to minimize this because we don't need to worry about how S.M. works.

122
00:09:23,470 --> 00:09:27,250
We wrote that in a function, and then we forget about it as usual.

123
00:09:27,580 --> 00:09:34,570
And basically what we're doing right now is we're setting a command that will return a result that contains

124
00:09:34,570 --> 00:09:35,950
the wi fi password.

125
00:09:36,100 --> 00:09:42,580
We're capturing this result using a result variable because that captures what's a process, the check

126
00:09:42,580 --> 00:09:43,840
output returns.

127
00:09:44,140 --> 00:09:50,440
And then we're going to send an email to John 70 gmail.com with this password.

128
00:09:50,440 --> 00:09:57,850
And we're given the result of the command as the message body that will be sent in the email.

129
00:09:59,080 --> 00:10:07,630
Now the account password that you usually use to log in to your Gmail account cannot be used from programs

130
00:10:07,720 --> 00:10:11,830
such as this program right here for security reasons.

131
00:10:12,280 --> 00:10:18,940
Therefore, we have to create a special app password to be used with this application.

132
00:10:19,360 --> 00:10:21,700
Doing this is very, very simple.

133
00:10:21,730 --> 00:10:30,190
All you have to do is simply go to my account dot google.com, go to the security section on the left

134
00:10:30,460 --> 00:10:33,940
and scroll down to the signing into Google section.

135
00:10:34,120 --> 00:10:40,030
You want to make sure that two step verification is enabled, and you should see another option that

136
00:10:40,030 --> 00:10:42,250
is called app passwords in here.

137
00:10:42,280 --> 00:10:48,550
As you can see, it's saying none because I don't have an app password created to create one, you simply

138
00:10:48,550 --> 00:10:49,840
need to click in here.

139
00:10:50,880 --> 00:10:53,250
Login again with your normal password.

140
00:10:55,160 --> 00:10:58,490
Select the application that will be used in the password.

141
00:10:58,490 --> 00:11:02,960
And our case we're going to see other because we're creating our own application.

142
00:11:03,050 --> 00:11:05,210
You can call it anything you want.

143
00:11:05,240 --> 00:11:09,140
I like to use meaningful names, so I'm going to call it a reporter.

144
00:11:10,090 --> 00:11:11,620
And click on Generate.

145
00:11:12,910 --> 00:11:16,240
Right now, as you can see, it's given us a new password.

146
00:11:16,540 --> 00:11:23,710
And this will be the password that we will use to authenticate with our Google account from our application.

147
00:11:23,890 --> 00:11:25,990
So we're simply going to copy this.

148
00:11:27,110 --> 00:11:30,650
And paste it in our program in the password field.

149
00:11:31,460 --> 00:11:34,070
Save the program and you're good to go.

150
00:11:34,770 --> 00:11:40,420
Now I'm going to copy this and put it in my webroot and then download it in the Windows machine.

151
00:11:40,440 --> 00:11:43,800
I showed you how to do that, so I'm going to cut this part of the video.

152
00:11:44,960 --> 00:11:50,180
So I have that here in my downloads directory and I'm going to run it in here.

153
00:11:50,180 --> 00:11:52,160
First of all, I'm going to clear the screen.

154
00:11:54,360 --> 00:11:59,910
And then I'm going to run it from here as usual, by, first of all, specifying the location of the

155
00:11:59,910 --> 00:12:06,150
interpreter, followed by the location of my file, which is execute and report.

156
00:12:06,630 --> 00:12:08,310
I'm going to hit enter.

157
00:12:09,320 --> 00:12:11,330
This will run once it's run.

158
00:12:11,360 --> 00:12:12,950
It'll just not print anything.

159
00:12:12,950 --> 00:12:17,030
But it should have sent me an email now to my Gmail account.

160
00:12:17,720 --> 00:12:24,770
And now if I look at my Gmail account and refresh this, you'll see that I got a new message.

161
00:12:25,460 --> 00:12:29,810
And if I look at this message, it was sent 0 minutes ago.

162
00:12:29,810 --> 00:12:36,020
And as you can see now, if we go down, we have all of the information about the wi fi network.

163
00:12:37,140 --> 00:12:41,940
Plus, we have the key or the password for this network in here.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,100 --> 00:00:01,570
Okay.

2
00:00:01,580 --> 00:00:08,420
Now the script that we created is actually very useful because it uses the central function, which

3
00:00:08,420 --> 00:00:14,750
is a generic function that we can use to send emails from our python code in many, many scenarios.

4
00:00:14,750 --> 00:00:21,230
And it does its job well because it executes a command and it sends the result to an email.

5
00:00:22,280 --> 00:00:28,430
But I don't think this script is useful in a real life scenario, because if you look at the command

6
00:00:28,430 --> 00:00:35,540
that we're running, you'll notice that in the command I'm specifying the network name that I want to

7
00:00:35,540 --> 00:00:36,860
get its password.

8
00:00:37,490 --> 00:00:44,120
So in order for me to use this in a real life scenario, I need to know the name of a network that the

9
00:00:44,120 --> 00:00:47,900
target has connected to so that I can get its key.

10
00:00:48,620 --> 00:00:55,010
A better script would be one that can list all the networks that the target computer has connected to,

11
00:00:55,250 --> 00:01:00,170
and then get me the password for each of these networks.

12
00:01:00,560 --> 00:01:03,350
So let's try to implement a script like this.

13
00:01:03,860 --> 00:01:09,500
Now, in order to write the script, I'm actually going to modify the execute and report, but I'm going

14
00:01:09,500 --> 00:01:11,750
to do that on the Windows Machine.

15
00:01:12,350 --> 00:01:18,860
Usually I like to write my programs in Carly because in real life you'll probably always be using Carly

16
00:01:18,860 --> 00:01:20,180
when you're doing a pen test.

17
00:01:20,180 --> 00:01:23,540
So it's just I'm just more comfortable with that.

18
00:01:23,540 --> 00:01:27,260
And for you, it's better to be more comfortable programming from Carly.

19
00:01:27,620 --> 00:01:33,440
But when it comes to testing and when it comes to making this lecture, I think I can explain it better

20
00:01:33,440 --> 00:01:39,140
if I actually modify the script in here in Windows, because I want to show you the output in steps

21
00:01:39,140 --> 00:01:40,970
before I send it by email.

22
00:01:41,570 --> 00:01:47,330
So this will also be a good example for you if you want to modify scripts from Windows.

23
00:01:47,330 --> 00:01:52,130
If you weren't sure about the steps that you're going to take or what the output is going to look like,

24
00:01:52,850 --> 00:01:59,030
so you can actually install pip charm on windows, or you can just install a lightweight text editor

25
00:01:59,030 --> 00:02:04,160
called Sublime Text, like I said before, and I'm going to include the link for that in the resources.

26
00:02:04,160 --> 00:02:08,780
All you have to do is just download it, click on next, next, next, next and install it.

27
00:02:09,200 --> 00:02:15,290
Once it's installed you'll be able to see it in your start menu and I have it in here called Sublime

28
00:02:15,290 --> 00:02:23,570
Text three and I'm going to open my report and execute script, which is in my downloads and it's right

29
00:02:23,570 --> 00:02:23,870
here.

30
00:02:23,870 --> 00:02:25,730
It's execute and report.

31
00:02:27,680 --> 00:02:34,010
Now the goal in our script is, first of all, to list all the networks or profiles that the target

32
00:02:34,010 --> 00:02:35,690
computer connected to.

33
00:02:35,690 --> 00:02:39,950
And we can do that as we seen before by doing that message.

34
00:02:39,950 --> 00:02:43,070
Learn, show, profile.

35
00:02:44,380 --> 00:02:48,670
So the first command that we're going to run is going to be this command.

36
00:02:49,690 --> 00:02:51,400
I'm just going to paste it in here.

37
00:02:53,560 --> 00:03:00,580
Now this command is going to be executed at this line and this will store all of this output in the

38
00:03:00,580 --> 00:03:01,870
result variable.

39
00:03:02,470 --> 00:03:08,950
Now I'm going to rename this variable to networks because right now this is not the final result that

40
00:03:08,950 --> 00:03:09,580
we want.

41
00:03:09,610 --> 00:03:11,650
This is the network names.

42
00:03:12,580 --> 00:03:19,090
Now, the next thing that we need to do is be able to access this network name right here.

43
00:03:19,420 --> 00:03:21,370
And this network name right here.

44
00:03:21,370 --> 00:03:24,340
And if there are more, obviously access all of them.

45
00:03:24,340 --> 00:03:27,410
So we're not interested into the whole output in here.

46
00:03:27,430 --> 00:03:30,160
We're only interested in the network names.

47
00:03:30,790 --> 00:03:33,660
Now, you probably already know what I'm going to use.

48
00:03:33,670 --> 00:03:36,550
I'm going to use regex to access these values.

49
00:03:37,030 --> 00:03:40,290
And to do that, first of all, I'm going to import re.

50
00:03:40,300 --> 00:03:43,420
So I'm going to put a comma here and put three.

51
00:03:43,960 --> 00:03:47,750
And notice I'm actually using multiple imports in the same line.

52
00:03:47,770 --> 00:03:53,150
Usually we use import module name and then on the next line we do import module name.

53
00:03:53,170 --> 00:03:58,120
Right now I'm just importing them all on the same line and I'm separating them by commas.

54
00:03:59,130 --> 00:04:02,160
And once I have reimported I can use it.

55
00:04:02,160 --> 00:04:05,730
So I'm going to use it in here after I get the networks.

56
00:04:06,540 --> 00:04:14,880
And I'm going to say my network names is going to be equal to re dot search.

57
00:04:16,400 --> 00:04:19,460
And I'm going to put my rejects in the first argument.

58
00:04:19,790 --> 00:04:24,470
And we want to search for anything that starts with profile.

59
00:04:24,620 --> 00:04:27,920
So the first thing we're going to do is literally type profile.

60
00:04:29,700 --> 00:04:34,470
And then we can expect any number of blank spaces.

61
00:04:34,830 --> 00:04:42,360
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to put backward slash s to account for one blank space and I'm

62
00:04:42,360 --> 00:04:46,860
going to put a star to say that I can accept any number of blank spaces.

63
00:04:47,670 --> 00:04:53,610
This will be followed by a column and it will be followed by another blank space.

64
00:04:54,270 --> 00:04:58,830
Now usually I go to PI text and show you how how I come up with this regex.

65
00:04:58,830 --> 00:05:04,380
But I think we've done that a lot and it's actually simple enough and we should be able to come up with

66
00:05:04,380 --> 00:05:07,410
this straight away without even the need to use Python.

67
00:05:08,580 --> 00:05:13,530
So the regular expression that I'm using right now is going to match profile, followed by any number

68
00:05:13,530 --> 00:05:18,410
of blank space, followed by a colon and followed by one more blank space.

69
00:05:18,420 --> 00:05:21,480
So it's going to match all of this so far.

70
00:05:22,500 --> 00:05:25,230
After this, we won't expect any character.

71
00:05:25,920 --> 00:05:28,500
So we can do this by the dot star.

72
00:05:28,560 --> 00:05:33,120
And before I do that, I'm going to separate this in two groups.

73
00:05:33,120 --> 00:05:35,160
So this is going to be my first group.

74
00:05:36,630 --> 00:05:43,560
And I'm going to create another group by putting two brackets and inside it I want to match any character.

75
00:05:44,380 --> 00:05:46,690
And I want to do that any number of times.

76
00:05:46,690 --> 00:05:50,050
So this will literally match all of this right here.

77
00:05:50,740 --> 00:05:55,770
Now, if I print what this regular expression will match, let me just show you.

78
00:05:55,780 --> 00:05:59,230
So I'm just going to print network names.

79
00:05:59,800 --> 00:06:01,510
Dot group zero.

80
00:06:02,420 --> 00:06:04,010
And I'll just run it.

81
00:06:05,780 --> 00:06:09,620
Oh, and I forgot to give the string that I want to search for in my regex.

82
00:06:09,620 --> 00:06:12,200
And that string is networks.

83
00:06:13,790 --> 00:06:19,280
And I should comment the send mail because we're not sending the email right now and I'll just clear

84
00:06:19,280 --> 00:06:21,680
this and run it again.

85
00:06:22,910 --> 00:06:27,500
And as you can see right now, it's matching all of the outputs.

86
00:06:27,500 --> 00:06:31,880
So it's matching profile and the network name.

87
00:06:32,600 --> 00:06:38,690
Now we tackle this issue before by using a non capturing group, and that's actually the main reason

88
00:06:38,690 --> 00:06:41,470
why I separated my regex into groups.

89
00:06:41,480 --> 00:06:47,090
So I have the second group, which is the one that I'm interested in, and I have the first group which

90
00:06:47,090 --> 00:06:52,580
is only used as a keyword in order to match my interesting text.

91
00:06:53,240 --> 00:06:59,300
So I'm going to mark the first group as a non capturing group so that it's not included in the result.

92
00:06:59,420 --> 00:07:06,620
And to do that, we just have to type question mark Colon at the start of the group that we don't want

93
00:07:06,620 --> 00:07:10,940
to include, which is the non capturing group which is the profile and here.

94
00:07:12,010 --> 00:07:14,320
So now if I run my code again.

95
00:07:15,570 --> 00:07:19,380
And actually access group one instead of group zero in here.

96
00:07:21,350 --> 00:07:25,490
You'll see, I'm able to access my network name.

97
00:07:26,150 --> 00:07:32,330
But if you remember the original output that we're getting, we actually have two networks in that output,

98
00:07:32,330 --> 00:07:36,710
not only one network, and this is where we're actually going to use something new.

99
00:07:36,710 --> 00:07:37,430
This time.

100
00:07:37,640 --> 00:07:43,360
We see that reader search to search for something, and we've seen that much before.

101
00:07:43,370 --> 00:07:52,340
And right now we're going to use really dot find all and basically this is a function that again uses

102
00:07:52,340 --> 00:07:53,060
regex.

103
00:07:53,060 --> 00:08:00,260
But instead of only finding the first text that matches the regular expression, it will actually look

104
00:08:00,260 --> 00:08:05,750
for text that matches our regular expression and the whole string.

105
00:08:05,750 --> 00:08:11,720
So if there is more than one piece in the string that we're passing in the networks in here that can

106
00:08:11,720 --> 00:08:17,960
match this regular expression, it will match them all and it will put them in a list.

107
00:08:18,470 --> 00:08:21,860
So we actually won't even need to use the groups in here.

108
00:08:21,950 --> 00:08:24,860
It will automatically put everything in a list.

109
00:08:25,730 --> 00:08:31,040
So now I'm just going to print the network names as is, and it's actually going to print it as a list

110
00:08:31,040 --> 00:08:31,760
for me.

111
00:08:32,630 --> 00:08:41,090
And as you can see, the result now is a list in which each element is a piece of string that matches

112
00:08:41,090 --> 00:08:42,530
the regular expression.

113
00:08:43,160 --> 00:08:49,790
So the first element in this list is the first network, and the second element in this list is the

114
00:08:49,790 --> 00:08:50,990
second network.

115
00:08:51,530 --> 00:08:54,560
You can already see how powerful the regex is.

116
00:08:54,560 --> 00:09:00,680
Looking at the number of times that we used it throughout the programs that we wrote and final is actually

117
00:09:00,680 --> 00:09:05,780
a very, very useful function because it has different hues than what we see so far.

118
00:09:05,930 --> 00:09:14,240
And it's a nice way of capturing useful data if it occurs multiple times in a single string like our

119
00:09:14,240 --> 00:09:15,410
example in here.

120
00:09:15,830 --> 00:09:23,150
So if we had ten networks stored on this computer, the command that we're running in here will return

121
00:09:23,150 --> 00:09:24,380
these ten networks.

122
00:09:24,470 --> 00:09:31,580
And this function defined all function will automatically create a list of size ten in which each element

123
00:09:31,580 --> 00:09:35,150
is a network name, just like we have in here.

124
00:09:35,480 --> 00:09:37,940
Now, I don't want to make this lecture too long.

125
00:09:38,030 --> 00:09:44,480
In the next lecture, we'll see how we can iterate over this list and then run a specific command for

126
00:09:44,480 --> 00:09:45,470
each element.

127
00:09:45,470 --> 00:09:54,050
Combine the result of these commands and send it as a single email so that we have a file that when

128
00:09:54,050 --> 00:10:01,220
executed on a target system, it will send all the wi fi passwords that are stored in that system and

129
00:10:01,220 --> 00:10:06,440
not a single password like we're doing in the script that we wrote in the previous lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,050 --> 00:00:01,390
Okay.

2
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:08,310
Now that we have a variable called network names, that includes a list of all the networks that are

3
00:00:08,310 --> 00:00:10,380
stored on the target computer.

4
00:00:10,380 --> 00:00:12,330
And we actually printed the list.

5
00:00:12,330 --> 00:00:16,140
So we have a visual representation of this list right here.

6
00:00:16,740 --> 00:00:23,610
Let's see how we can iterate over this list and then get the password for each network and send it to

7
00:00:23,610 --> 00:00:24,540
our email.

8
00:00:25,170 --> 00:00:30,300
So first of all, I'm actually going to rename this variable so that it makes more sense for us.

9
00:00:30,300 --> 00:00:36,570
So it's called network names and we're going to call the Network Names List so that we know this is

10
00:00:36,570 --> 00:00:37,210
a list.

11
00:00:37,230 --> 00:00:39,360
This actually makes the code more readable.

12
00:00:39,360 --> 00:00:44,610
So in the future, if we come back and we want to understand how this works, it's very simple and it's

13
00:00:44,610 --> 00:00:45,690
very intuitive.

14
00:00:46,960 --> 00:00:54,040
I'm going to remove this print statement here and I'm going to use a for loop to iterate over this list.

15
00:00:54,820 --> 00:00:59,110
So basically what I'm going to say is for each network name.

16
00:01:00,320 --> 00:01:00,890
In.

17
00:01:01,880 --> 00:01:03,500
Network names list.

18
00:01:04,440 --> 00:01:10,950
And I'm going to just print the new variable that we just created, which is the network name.

19
00:01:11,490 --> 00:01:17,730
Basically, I'm using a for loop and in this for loop I'm creating a new variable and I'm calling this

20
00:01:17,730 --> 00:01:19,440
new variable network name.

21
00:01:19,740 --> 00:01:26,730
Python will automatically know that this is a list and that this variable should represent an element

22
00:01:26,730 --> 00:01:28,590
each time the loop runs.

23
00:01:29,280 --> 00:01:32,550
So basically we're saying for each element in the list.

24
00:01:32,550 --> 00:01:38,130
So for each network name in my network names list, I want to print the network name.

25
00:01:38,400 --> 00:01:42,180
So what this code will do is it will go to the first element in here.

26
00:01:42,420 --> 00:01:45,970
It will store that in my new variable, which is network name.

27
00:01:45,990 --> 00:01:47,580
It will print it on screen.

28
00:01:47,700 --> 00:01:53,970
Then in the next iteration of the list, it will go up again and it will set network name to the next

29
00:01:53,970 --> 00:01:55,260
element in the list.

30
00:01:55,440 --> 00:02:01,050
And then we're printing that on screen, and this will keep going until the end of the list.

31
00:02:01,770 --> 00:02:07,390
So just to show, what's this going to look like if I run the code again, you'll see the network names

32
00:02:07,390 --> 00:02:13,860
that are being printed in separate lines, which means that the print statement is being executed twice

33
00:02:13,860 --> 00:02:17,130
each time for one element in the list.

34
00:02:18,090 --> 00:02:19,370
Now, this is very good.

35
00:02:19,380 --> 00:02:25,950
We know the network name variable now is going to contain the value for each network and the network

36
00:02:25,950 --> 00:02:26,970
names list.

37
00:02:27,840 --> 00:02:34,650
The next thing that we need to do is run the command that we ran in the previous lecture and the basic

38
00:02:34,650 --> 00:02:36,510
report and execute script.

39
00:02:37,020 --> 00:02:38,550
So the command was.

40
00:02:39,560 --> 00:02:42,200
And I can show it to you here if I just go up.

41
00:02:43,130 --> 00:02:48,050
So this is an example of the command when we were running it against the second network.

42
00:02:48,350 --> 00:02:58,850
So basically the command is going to be net s hage lan show profile and that will be followed by the

43
00:02:58,850 --> 00:02:59,900
network name.

44
00:03:00,080 --> 00:03:08,130
So we'll just put a plus, put the network name variable and then put another plus open my quote.

45
00:03:08,160 --> 00:03:10,860
So we're just appending a variable to a string.

46
00:03:10,880 --> 00:03:17,960
We showed that multiple times before, and then we're going to append string to this variable now and

47
00:03:17,960 --> 00:03:23,420
then we're going to append more string, which is the rest of the command where we say keys equal to

48
00:03:23,420 --> 00:03:24,050
clear.

49
00:03:24,470 --> 00:03:30,200
So we're going to say key is equal to clear and that's it.

50
00:03:30,320 --> 00:03:37,340
So basically we're just doing the normal command Nettleton show profile and instead of the network name,

51
00:03:37,340 --> 00:03:40,790
we're putting the variable that's going to contain the network name.

52
00:03:41,530 --> 00:03:46,050
The next line is going to execute this command for me and give me the result.

53
00:03:46,060 --> 00:03:53,140
So we're going to create a variable, we'll call this variable current result, and this is going to

54
00:03:53,140 --> 00:03:56,500
be the result of executing the above command.

55
00:03:56,890 --> 00:04:01,360
So it's going to be sub process that check output.

56
00:04:01,360 --> 00:04:03,700
Again, we spoke about this before.

57
00:04:03,700 --> 00:04:08,200
We're going to give this the command and we're going to set Shell to true.

58
00:04:10,040 --> 00:04:18,200
And finally, we'll need a variable that will add text to it so that we send it at the end in here at

59
00:04:18,200 --> 00:04:19,010
this line.

60
00:04:19,760 --> 00:04:27,740
So for each time this loop runs, we are getting a result that will contain a password for only one

61
00:04:27,740 --> 00:04:28,220
network.

62
00:04:28,220 --> 00:04:33,880
Because remember, we were iterating over each element in each iteration of the loop.

63
00:04:33,890 --> 00:04:37,450
So the first time we'll run the loop, the network name is going to be this.

64
00:04:37,460 --> 00:04:40,160
The next time the network name is going to be this.

65
00:04:40,790 --> 00:04:46,490
So the first time the loop is going to run, it's going to get us the password for the first element

66
00:04:46,490 --> 00:04:48,500
in the list for the first network.

67
00:04:48,920 --> 00:04:54,290
Then when the loop runs again, it's going to get us the password for the second element.

68
00:04:54,290 --> 00:05:01,250
So the value and the current result will actually be removed and it will contain a new value for the

69
00:05:01,250 --> 00:05:03,980
password for the second network.

70
00:05:04,100 --> 00:05:11,030
So every time the loop runs, these variables get created again and their value get created again,

71
00:05:11,540 --> 00:05:17,840
therefore, so that we don't lose the value for the password for the first element in here.

72
00:05:18,200 --> 00:05:23,270
We're going to have to store that in a variable that is created outside the loop.

73
00:05:23,720 --> 00:05:29,240
So outside I'm going to create a variable called the result, and I'm going to set that to an empty

74
00:05:29,240 --> 00:05:29,780
string.

75
00:05:29,810 --> 00:05:30,710
It's nothing.

76
00:05:30,920 --> 00:05:37,940
Once we get the value or the password for the first network, I'm going to say the result.

77
00:05:38,650 --> 00:05:45,160
Is equal to the result, so it's equal to nothing plus the current result.

78
00:05:45,400 --> 00:05:48,250
So plus the password for the first element.

79
00:05:48,870 --> 00:05:51,010
Then the loop is going to go up.

80
00:05:51,030 --> 00:05:53,380
It's going to get the second network name.

81
00:05:53,400 --> 00:05:56,520
It's going to get the password for the second network.

82
00:05:56,670 --> 00:06:05,460
Then the result, which already contains the password for the first network, is going to be equal to

83
00:06:05,460 --> 00:06:10,080
itself, plus the current result, which is the password for the second network.

84
00:06:10,740 --> 00:06:16,980
So now by the end of this line, result is going to contain both the password for the first network

85
00:06:16,980 --> 00:06:19,200
and the second network.

86
00:06:19,770 --> 00:06:22,090
Now, let's assume there is a third network.

87
00:06:22,110 --> 00:06:24,940
We're going to get its name in here at the start of the loop.

88
00:06:24,960 --> 00:06:26,900
We're going to execute this command.

89
00:06:26,910 --> 00:06:33,960
Now, a result contains the password for the first, and the second network is going to equal to itself

90
00:06:33,960 --> 00:06:40,080
again, which is the password for the first and second network, plus the current result, which is

91
00:06:40,080 --> 00:06:43,400
the password for the third network and so on.

92
00:06:43,410 --> 00:06:50,070
So this way, by instantiating the variable outside the loop the first time and then making the variable

93
00:06:50,070 --> 00:06:57,270
equal to itself plus the current value, we're going to be able to keep appending the current result

94
00:06:57,270 --> 00:07:03,900
or the current value to the original variable so that by the end of the loop result is going to contain

95
00:07:03,900 --> 00:07:08,760
all the values that the current result contained throughout the whole loop.

96
00:07:09,570 --> 00:07:16,800
Therefore, when we send this in here in this line, this will send me the passwords for all the networks

97
00:07:16,800 --> 00:07:20,820
in the machine that this code gets executed on.

98
00:07:21,570 --> 00:07:27,030
Now, the structure of this loop is actually something that is very useful and that you will use a lot

99
00:07:27,030 --> 00:07:28,770
again in many, many scenarios.

100
00:07:28,770 --> 00:07:34,380
So this is just one example, but you should be able to adapt it in so many other programs.

101
00:07:35,130 --> 00:07:41,640
Now let's run our code and see if this actually send us an email of all the passwords.

102
00:07:43,860 --> 00:07:46,110
Now this got executed with no errors.

103
00:07:46,110 --> 00:07:47,520
So that's a good sign.

104
00:07:48,100 --> 00:07:50,040
And let's go to Cali.

105
00:07:50,640 --> 00:07:53,710
And in my email, I'm going to go to my inbox.

106
00:07:53,730 --> 00:07:55,020
Refresh this.

107
00:07:55,680 --> 00:07:58,140
And we have a new email that's good stuff.

108
00:07:58,560 --> 00:08:02,550
And we can see that we have the first network here, which is the air network.

109
00:08:03,340 --> 00:08:08,050
And if we scroll down, we have the password for this network.

110
00:08:09,510 --> 00:08:12,810
And next we have the second network.

111
00:08:13,320 --> 00:08:19,080
And again, if we scroll down, we have the password for the second network.

112
00:08:20,380 --> 00:08:26,290
Now, you could have also actually print the result in here before you send it by email if you were

113
00:08:26,290 --> 00:08:30,100
testing and you weren't sure about if you built this loop properly.

114
00:08:30,100 --> 00:08:32,200
But I was sure that I built it properly.

115
00:08:32,200 --> 00:08:34,650
And to be honest, I forgot to print it and show you.

116
00:08:34,660 --> 00:08:39,940
But as you can see, it's being sent properly and it's working as expected.

117
00:08:41,440 --> 00:08:45,020
So that's it for the execute and report script.

118
00:08:45,040 --> 00:08:50,290
I just wanted to spend a little bit more time to show you how you can actually use your programming

119
00:08:50,290 --> 00:08:52,720
skills to do whatever you want.

120
00:08:52,720 --> 00:08:55,900
Really, like you're only limited by your imagination.

121
00:08:55,900 --> 00:09:01,570
You can do so many things because you can execute Python code, which allows you to do so many things,

122
00:09:01,630 --> 00:09:04,240
and we can also execute system commands.

123
00:09:04,240 --> 00:09:06,880
So you have so much power with this.

124
00:09:07,060 --> 00:09:12,430
And I know we're still running this as a script and the target still needs to have Python installed

125
00:09:12,430 --> 00:09:16,030
because we're using we're running it using the Python interpreter.

126
00:09:16,030 --> 00:09:22,360
But like I said, at the end of the section, I'll show you how to convert all of these files into normal

127
00:09:22,360 --> 00:09:28,540
executables so that they run on any computer without the need to the interpreter And I'll even show

128
00:09:28,540 --> 00:09:33,880
you how to make these files look and function like images, PDFs or any other file type.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,870 --> 00:00:05,190
So far, we built some really simple but useful, evil scripts.

2
00:00:05,610 --> 00:00:12,000
We built the execute command script, which basically executes any system command on any computer.

3
00:00:12,420 --> 00:00:18,840
And then on top of that, we built the execute and report, which basically executes a command first

4
00:00:18,840 --> 00:00:20,640
and reports the result.

5
00:00:20,640 --> 00:00:26,820
When the command returns, good results to be used in cases where the command that you're executing

6
00:00:26,850 --> 00:00:29,580
is not useful unless you see the result.

7
00:00:30,600 --> 00:00:37,620
Now basically the execute and report was a combination of the execute script with a send mail function

8
00:00:37,620 --> 00:00:42,220
that basically sends the result of the command to a specific email.

9
00:00:42,240 --> 00:00:48,720
So basically the send malfunction was a helper function that can be used in so many useful scenarios.

10
00:00:49,770 --> 00:00:54,570
Another helpful simple function is a download file function.

11
00:00:55,050 --> 00:01:00,900
As the name suggests, this function will basically download the file on the target computer on the

12
00:01:00,900 --> 00:01:03,600
computer that executes this program.

13
00:01:04,380 --> 00:01:09,210
Now, why this is very useful is because, first of all, once we package it and I'm going to cover

14
00:01:09,210 --> 00:01:12,690
that later on, it's going to work against all operating systems.

15
00:01:13,350 --> 00:01:19,920
And this is very useful because we can use it with the other simple scripts that we built and that we

16
00:01:19,920 --> 00:01:21,540
will build in the future.

17
00:01:22,380 --> 00:01:29,340
So basically, like I said, the execute and report was basically the execute program with the central

18
00:01:29,340 --> 00:01:30,030
function.

19
00:01:30,450 --> 00:01:35,580
Now what we can do is we can use the download file function that we're going to build with the execute

20
00:01:35,580 --> 00:01:37,380
command that we built before.

21
00:01:37,380 --> 00:01:43,740
And that will give us a brand new program which will download the file and execute it on the system.

22
00:01:43,740 --> 00:01:51,000
So you can use that to download the keylogger virus, anything that you can think of really, and execute

23
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:52,710
it on the target computer.

24
00:01:53,550 --> 00:02:00,180
Another really handy use, which will actually have an example of later on, is downloading a file which

25
00:02:00,180 --> 00:02:02,670
might be a credential harvester, for example.

26
00:02:02,970 --> 00:02:04,770
Then execute this file.

27
00:02:04,770 --> 00:02:06,570
So we run this file on the computer.

28
00:02:06,570 --> 00:02:13,470
Still all the passwords saved on that computer and then use the report function or the sentinel function

29
00:02:13,470 --> 00:02:16,320
to send the result to our email.

30
00:02:16,530 --> 00:02:21,480
So the result of this combination is going to be download, execute and report.

31
00:02:22,310 --> 00:02:24,940
So you can see the pattern that I'm trying to build in here.

32
00:02:24,950 --> 00:02:32,390
We're going to be building small functions and you'll be able to mix them and come up with very useful

33
00:02:32,390 --> 00:02:35,960
programs or evil files that you can use every few lectures.

34
00:02:35,960 --> 00:02:40,360
I'm going to be showing you some examples, but the possibilities are endless.

35
00:02:40,370 --> 00:02:44,720
You can build any type of script that you want or any type of evil file.

36
00:02:45,470 --> 00:02:46,930
So enough talking.

37
00:02:46,940 --> 00:02:50,030
Let's go and build this download file function.

38
00:02:50,900 --> 00:02:52,490
Now in my machine.

39
00:02:52,490 --> 00:02:58,970
I've already created a new project called Download File and I've already created a new Python file called

40
00:02:58,970 --> 00:03:00,440
Download Dot Pie.

41
00:03:01,250 --> 00:03:07,670
So the first thing that I'm going to do is import a library that allows me to send requests to the Internet.

42
00:03:07,790 --> 00:03:11,270
So the name of this library is requests.

43
00:03:12,820 --> 00:03:17,680
I'm going to include the link for this library in the resources of this lecture for you to have more

44
00:03:17,680 --> 00:03:18,790
details about it.

45
00:03:19,390 --> 00:03:22,000
And then we're going to create our function.

46
00:03:22,000 --> 00:03:27,850
So I'm going to define a new function and I'm going to call it Download, and I'm going to make this

47
00:03:27,850 --> 00:03:34,810
function take a variable called URL, which is the link for the file that this function will download.

48
00:03:35,500 --> 00:03:41,530
Then inside this function, I'm going to create a new variable and I'm going to call this get request

49
00:03:42,340 --> 00:03:49,330
and I'm going to make this equal to requests dot get my new URL.

50
00:03:49,690 --> 00:03:55,120
So basically the library that I just imported the requests, it has a function called get.

51
00:03:55,180 --> 00:04:00,820
And basically what this function does, it sends a guest request for the URL that we pass, which is

52
00:04:00,820 --> 00:04:08,140
the URL that the function takes and it returns the result of the get request to my variable, which

53
00:04:08,140 --> 00:04:09,640
is called get request.

54
00:04:10,390 --> 00:04:17,260
Now, if this if I pass a valid URL to this, the get request will actually have the response.

55
00:04:17,260 --> 00:04:22,480
And then before we actually use this response, I'm just going to print it on screen.

56
00:04:22,480 --> 00:04:29,740
So I'm going to print get request just to see what this response looks like.

57
00:04:30,730 --> 00:04:35,650
Now I've created the function, but I haven't called the function from my code.

58
00:04:35,650 --> 00:04:37,360
So we're going to call it in here.

59
00:04:37,360 --> 00:04:44,050
We're going to call my download function and I'm going to give it a URL that I want to download so you

60
00:04:44,050 --> 00:04:45,730
can use any file that you want.

61
00:04:45,730 --> 00:04:49,480
You just want to make sure that you give a direct link to this file.

62
00:04:49,870 --> 00:04:53,170
So what I mean by direct link is I'm going to show you an example.

63
00:04:53,440 --> 00:04:55,270
So I have my web browser here.

64
00:04:55,270 --> 00:05:03,070
I've already looked for my favorite car, which is a Nissan GT-R, and if I just copy the image location

65
00:05:03,910 --> 00:05:11,590
and paste it in a new tab, you'll see that the end of this link is the name of the actual image and

66
00:05:11,590 --> 00:05:14,800
when I load this I'll see the image on its own.

67
00:05:14,800 --> 00:05:17,560
There is no ads, no HTML page, nothing.

68
00:05:17,560 --> 00:05:20,080
This is a direct link to the image.

69
00:05:20,740 --> 00:05:26,140
So this applies to whatever file you want to download, whether you want to download a PDF, an XY,

70
00:05:26,170 --> 00:05:28,870
a zip, or an image like I'm doing right here.

71
00:05:29,740 --> 00:05:34,600
So I'm going to go to my script and I'm literally just going to paste this in here.

72
00:05:34,960 --> 00:05:41,440
And basically what this will do, it will call my download function with this URL, the get request

73
00:05:41,440 --> 00:05:48,010
now is going to call the get function from the request library with the URL that we just passed.

74
00:05:48,010 --> 00:05:54,640
And then we're going to print the get request, which is going to contain the response of this request,

75
00:05:55,120 --> 00:06:00,550
which actually makes me think I should call this get response, not request, because this is going

76
00:06:00,550 --> 00:06:01,900
to be the response.

77
00:06:02,200 --> 00:06:04,810
So this will just make more sense.

78
00:06:07,780 --> 00:06:10,510
And let's go and run our program.

79
00:06:10,720 --> 00:06:16,270
Now, I'm already in the working directory of my program, so I'm just going to do python.

80
00:06:16,870 --> 00:06:18,610
Download dot pi.

81
00:06:20,000 --> 00:06:25,310
And as you can see, it's printing a response for me on screen and it's shown me the response code,

82
00:06:25,310 --> 00:06:26,450
which is 200.

83
00:06:26,450 --> 00:06:33,620
And if you remember from previous lectures, the 200 HTTP response means that it's OC.

84
00:06:33,650 --> 00:06:36,410
The server was able to send me the response.

85
00:06:36,650 --> 00:06:45,590
But what we see in here is the object that was returned by the request dot get if we actually want to

86
00:06:45,590 --> 00:06:48,800
see the actual content of this response.

87
00:06:48,950 --> 00:06:55,400
So the stuff that we used to see in the raw header when we were using Skype, then we just have to do

88
00:06:55,400 --> 00:06:57,050
that content.

89
00:06:57,050 --> 00:07:00,440
So we're saying I want the get response dot content.

90
00:07:01,100 --> 00:07:03,440
Now if we print this again.

91
00:07:04,480 --> 00:07:10,240
As you can see, we see all the gibberish that we used to see in the raw header, and this is basically

92
00:07:10,240 --> 00:07:13,480
the binary content of the file itself.

93
00:07:13,960 --> 00:07:19,900
So now we're able to send the request and we're able to get the binary content of the file.

94
00:07:19,960 --> 00:07:26,620
Now the next step is to just write this content to a file so that we'll have the file downloaded in

95
00:07:26,620 --> 00:07:27,130
here.

96
00:07:27,880 --> 00:07:32,020
Keep in mind, this is the working directory of the download pie file.

97
00:07:33,080 --> 00:07:34,740
So that's perfect for now.

98
00:07:34,760 --> 00:07:40,730
In the next lecture, I'm going to show you how we can write files in general using Python, and then

99
00:07:40,730 --> 00:07:46,340
we'll see how we can use that to write the response that we just got so that we'll be able to download

100
00:07:46,340 --> 00:07:48,800
the file and write it on our system.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,780 --> 00:00:06,240
And this lecture, first of all, I want to teach you something that we never spoke about, which is

2
00:00:06,240 --> 00:00:12,870
writing files using Python, and then we'll see how we can use that in order to write a binary file

3
00:00:12,870 --> 00:00:18,420
and write the content that we're getting here on screen in a file, so that we'll be able to download

4
00:00:18,420 --> 00:00:21,060
any file that we want from the Internet.

5
00:00:21,900 --> 00:00:24,270
So we're going back to our code.

6
00:00:24,360 --> 00:00:29,750
And as a start, I'm just going to write a simple file, a simple text file, and I'm going to write

7
00:00:29,760 --> 00:00:31,530
test inside this file.

8
00:00:32,100 --> 00:00:37,170
So in Python, when dealing with files, we use the with keyword.

9
00:00:38,190 --> 00:00:42,810
Then what we want to do is, first of all, we need to open this file.

10
00:00:42,810 --> 00:00:48,240
So you need to do this whether you want to write stuff on the system or even read stuff.

11
00:00:48,240 --> 00:00:51,480
And we'll talk about reading stuff later on in this section.

12
00:00:51,960 --> 00:00:54,420
So we're going to say, I want to open.

13
00:00:55,480 --> 00:00:59,710
And then we need to specify the name of the file as the first argument.

14
00:00:59,740 --> 00:01:04,100
Now, since we're going to be creating a file, this is a file that does not exist.

15
00:01:04,120 --> 00:01:06,460
We can call it anything we want.

16
00:01:06,970 --> 00:01:13,300
So for this very simple example, I'm going to call it sample dot text.

17
00:01:14,610 --> 00:01:20,970
The second argument that we need to pass to the open is the mode of opening this file.

18
00:01:21,240 --> 00:01:26,610
For example, if you want to read this file, then you just pass or for read.

19
00:01:27,060 --> 00:01:29,220
If you want to write, you pass.

20
00:01:29,220 --> 00:01:34,110
W if you want to read and write, you can do all w.

21
00:01:35,050 --> 00:01:39,640
Now I'm going to include the link in the resources that covers all the different modes that you can

22
00:01:39,640 --> 00:01:40,240
use.

23
00:01:40,390 --> 00:01:45,790
But for this example, we want to write, so I'm just going to type W in the mode.

24
00:01:46,510 --> 00:01:54,340
Next, we need to specify a variable name that can be used to deal with the file that we just opened.

25
00:01:54,730 --> 00:02:03,010
So we're saying with open sample text to write us and we can use any variable name in here.

26
00:02:03,160 --> 00:02:05,890
And I'm just going to call this out file.

27
00:02:07,110 --> 00:02:09,180
Because it's going to be an output file.

28
00:02:09,810 --> 00:02:16,440
And then we put a call on and once I press enter, you'll see that the code will be automatically indented.

29
00:02:16,740 --> 00:02:24,000
And basically any code I write right now inside this block is going to be executed while the file is

30
00:02:24,000 --> 00:02:24,660
open.

31
00:02:24,900 --> 00:02:30,750
Once we get out of this block, once we go back in here and start writing stuff here, this will be

32
00:02:30,750 --> 00:02:34,020
executed after the file is closed.

33
00:02:35,050 --> 00:02:41,380
So any code that you want to execute while the file is open is going to have to be in here within the

34
00:02:41,380 --> 00:02:43,750
intention of this block.

35
00:02:44,020 --> 00:02:52,390
And what we want to do is we want to use the out file and we want to write to this out file, and then

36
00:02:52,390 --> 00:02:54,880
we can put the text that we want to write in here.

37
00:02:55,090 --> 00:02:58,660
And let's say this is a test.

38
00:02:59,900 --> 00:03:07,280
So basically once we execute this code, this should open a file named sample txt.

39
00:03:07,640 --> 00:03:09,980
And we're saying the mode is w.

40
00:03:09,980 --> 00:03:16,790
So if the file doesn't exist, this will create a new file called sample dot txt and the same working

41
00:03:16,790 --> 00:03:21,050
directory as the one that we're executing this program from.

42
00:03:21,140 --> 00:03:25,880
And we're saying that we want to open this file as out file.

43
00:03:25,880 --> 00:03:33,140
So basically we can reference this file that we just opened using the keyword out file throughout this

44
00:03:33,140 --> 00:03:34,220
block in here.

45
00:03:35,010 --> 00:03:42,240
Then inside our block, while the file is open, we're going to say, I want to write to my out file.

46
00:03:42,510 --> 00:03:44,940
And this is the text that I want to write.

47
00:03:45,810 --> 00:03:47,910
So it's very, very simple stuff.

48
00:03:48,000 --> 00:03:52,800
Now, if I save this, go down and let me clear the screen.

49
00:03:54,360 --> 00:03:56,000
And run my code again.

50
00:03:56,010 --> 00:04:00,240
Now, notice, as soon as I write this code, a new file will be created here.

51
00:04:00,240 --> 00:04:06,690
This is the working directory of download file and when we open this file it should contain the text

52
00:04:06,690 --> 00:04:09,270
that we put in the right method.

53
00:04:09,920 --> 00:04:12,260
So let's run this code.

54
00:04:13,990 --> 00:04:18,520
And as you can see, it created a new file called sample dot txt.

55
00:04:18,550 --> 00:04:25,690
So it's called the name that we specified in here as the first argument and the content of this.

56
00:04:26,810 --> 00:04:29,210
Says this is a test.

57
00:04:29,210 --> 00:04:34,310
So it basically says what we passed to the right method.

58
00:04:35,310 --> 00:04:42,510
Now this is the general way of writing files in Python, so you can use this way in any program when

59
00:04:42,510 --> 00:04:47,400
you want to write files, whether you're writing results, whether you're writing logs or any other

60
00:04:47,400 --> 00:04:48,000
reason.

61
00:04:48,600 --> 00:04:54,450
Now, the reason why we're writing this right now, we want to be able to write all of this gibberish

62
00:04:54,450 --> 00:04:58,080
to a file so that we actually have a proper image.

63
00:04:59,110 --> 00:05:04,550
So the file that we're writing is not a normal text, it's a binary file.

64
00:05:04,570 --> 00:05:11,200
So we have to tell Python in the mode here that I want you to open this file for writing, but this

65
00:05:11,200 --> 00:05:17,050
is going to be a binary file and hence we're passing be in here in the mode.

66
00:05:18,010 --> 00:05:24,850
The next thing that we need to do is write the content so the gibberish that we're getting in here in

67
00:05:24,850 --> 00:05:25,480
this file.

68
00:05:25,480 --> 00:05:29,500
So we need to pass that in here instead of this is a test.

69
00:05:30,100 --> 00:05:35,440
So we know that all of this gibberish is stored in get response content.

70
00:05:35,620 --> 00:05:37,300
So I'm going to cut this.

71
00:05:38,660 --> 00:05:42,740
And paste it here, and I'm going to remove this print statement.

72
00:05:44,100 --> 00:05:47,860
Now, the last thing that we need to modify is the file name.

73
00:05:47,880 --> 00:05:54,690
You can see that every time this function is going to be executed, it will store the file that we're

74
00:05:54,690 --> 00:05:58,610
downloading in a file called sample dot txt.

75
00:05:59,070 --> 00:06:05,520
But this is not right because for example, the URL that we're using right now is an image and it ends

76
00:06:05,520 --> 00:06:06,930
with a dot jpg.

77
00:06:07,710 --> 00:06:11,670
Maybe in the future we'll use it to download a PDF or an X.

78
00:06:12,150 --> 00:06:16,380
So the way that this is hardcoded in here is not good.

79
00:06:17,340 --> 00:06:25,410
A better way of naming the file is to take the last part of the URL and use that as the file name.

80
00:06:26,130 --> 00:06:32,550
Now we can use our knowledge of string manipulation in order to capture this value and store it in a

81
00:06:32,550 --> 00:06:33,270
variable.

82
00:06:33,750 --> 00:06:38,010
Now you can use regex to get this value, which is a very good way of doing it.

83
00:06:38,280 --> 00:06:41,550
Or you can use another way, which is what I want to show you.

84
00:06:41,550 --> 00:06:47,610
I just like to show you as many ways as possible of doing the same thing so that you have a number of

85
00:06:47,610 --> 00:06:51,610
ways to do things in case one method doesn't work in your scenario.

86
00:06:51,630 --> 00:06:53,910
Then you'll be able to use the other way.

87
00:06:54,420 --> 00:06:58,440
This also allows me to cover more techniques when it comes to programming.

88
00:06:59,100 --> 00:07:02,880
So instead of using regex, I'm actually going to use split this time.

89
00:07:03,390 --> 00:07:11,430
So I'm going to say the file name is equal to the URL, so it's going to be equal to all of this.

90
00:07:12,330 --> 00:07:14,280
And then I'm going to split this.

91
00:07:15,890 --> 00:07:19,880
And I'm going to split it based on the forward slash.

92
00:07:20,270 --> 00:07:23,780
So basically what this will do is it will create a list.

93
00:07:24,050 --> 00:07:30,700
And each element in this list is the text that you see between two forward slashes.

94
00:07:30,710 --> 00:07:35,510
So this will be the first element, this will be the second element and so on.

95
00:07:36,680 --> 00:07:39,140
Let me actually show you what this is going to look like.

96
00:07:39,140 --> 00:07:41,780
So if I do print file name.

97
00:07:44,450 --> 00:07:46,810
And clear this.

98
00:07:46,820 --> 00:07:48,260
Run the code.

99
00:07:48,920 --> 00:07:51,060
You'll see that it'll print a list for me.

100
00:07:51,080 --> 00:07:54,260
Split based on the forward slash.

101
00:07:54,260 --> 00:08:00,530
So each element in here is text that you can find before or after a forward slash.

102
00:08:01,370 --> 00:08:07,550
Now you can see the last element of this list is the actual file name that I want to use.

103
00:08:08,300 --> 00:08:15,650
So we know to access the first element of the list, you usually do zero to access the second element

104
00:08:15,650 --> 00:08:18,410
of the list you do one and so on.

105
00:08:18,860 --> 00:08:26,330
Now, if we want to access the list from the end, so if we want to access the last element of the list,

106
00:08:26,360 --> 00:08:29,270
then we can just do minus one.

107
00:08:29,870 --> 00:08:32,720
This will print the last element of my list.

108
00:08:33,640 --> 00:08:35,620
So let's run this again.

109
00:08:36,570 --> 00:08:42,420
And as you can see, this is printing the last element of the list, which is this one right here,

110
00:08:42,420 --> 00:08:45,690
which is the actual file name that I want to use.

111
00:08:46,770 --> 00:08:55,050
So all I have to do now is remove this print statement and instead of using sample text to include my

112
00:08:55,050 --> 00:09:00,180
file, I'm going to remove this and use my file name variable.

113
00:09:01,170 --> 00:09:01,880
And that's it.

114
00:09:01,890 --> 00:09:03,000
My code is done.

115
00:09:03,150 --> 00:09:09,090
I have a really simple download function that allows me to download any file from the internet.

116
00:09:09,810 --> 00:09:11,990
Let's have a quick look on how this works.

117
00:09:12,000 --> 00:09:14,310
So we're calling the function here.

118
00:09:14,310 --> 00:09:16,410
We're giving it a URL string.

119
00:09:16,890 --> 00:09:20,910
First of all, we're getting the response using this line right here.

120
00:09:21,790 --> 00:09:24,280
And then we're getting the file name.

121
00:09:24,280 --> 00:09:28,780
And the file name is equal to the last element of the URL.

122
00:09:28,990 --> 00:09:36,270
After splitting this URL based on the forward slash, then we're opening a new file.

123
00:09:36,280 --> 00:09:40,570
We're using the file name variable, which is going to be this part right here.

124
00:09:40,600 --> 00:09:46,540
We're going to be writing to this file, and this file is going to be a binary file, hence the be in

125
00:09:46,540 --> 00:09:47,140
here.

126
00:09:47,500 --> 00:09:50,380
The reference for this file is called out file.

127
00:09:50,380 --> 00:09:56,710
So this is the variable that we're going to use to refer to this file and we're going to write to this

128
00:09:56,710 --> 00:09:59,710
file the content of the response.

129
00:10:00,680 --> 00:10:02,330
Now let's see this in action.

130
00:10:06,030 --> 00:10:08,520
As you can see, we have a new file in here.

131
00:10:08,550 --> 00:10:10,020
It has a JPEG.

132
00:10:10,050 --> 00:10:14,160
It has the name that we expect, which is the last part of the URL.

133
00:10:14,160 --> 00:10:18,900
And if we double click this file, you can see it allows the image.

134
00:10:18,900 --> 00:10:23,580
The image works perfectly, which means that our code works perfectly.

135
00:10:24,560 --> 00:10:31,190
So as you can see, we have a very, very simple function that works well, which is the idea that you

136
00:10:31,190 --> 00:10:33,440
should always use when you're writing programs.

137
00:10:33,440 --> 00:10:40,430
You split your program into small, useful functions that do one job but do their job very, very well.

138
00:10:40,820 --> 00:10:46,640
Now we can use this function, like I said at the start, with any other function, and this will add

139
00:10:46,640 --> 00:10:50,870
the ability to download the file so we can use it with the execute Python script.

140
00:10:50,870 --> 00:10:52,180
To download and execute.

141
00:10:52,190 --> 00:10:57,740
We can use it with the execute and report to download, execute and report and we'll have an example

142
00:10:57,740 --> 00:10:58,430
of that.

143
00:10:58,430 --> 00:11:00,860
And the possibilities are endless really.

144
00:11:00,860 --> 00:11:06,230
You can use it in any code that you want where you need to download the file now.

145
00:11:06,230 --> 00:11:13,130
Also, while we built this very simple script, we learned more about strings splitting them and accessing

146
00:11:13,130 --> 00:11:13,760
lists.

147
00:11:13,760 --> 00:11:20,780
We learned how to create our HTTP request and receive the response, and we learned how to write normal

148
00:11:20,780 --> 00:11:22,940
files and binary files.

and receiving requests and responses.

Now, as you know, this is a programming and hacking course at the same time.

00:00:29,030 --> 00:00:35,300
So we learned the programming part and the hacking side is kind of self explanatory because like I said,

8

it's a very simple function that can work as an add on or a helper function with larger or other scripts.

9
00:00:42,620 --> 00:00:48,050
So with the scripts that we built before or with the scripts and programs that we will build in this

10

section, so we can literally just skip this and you'll see us using it later on in the course.

11

But I think it wouldn't hurt to spend a lecture or two showing you this in action in a scenario where

12
00:01:01,100 --> 00:01:02,360
this can be useful.

13
00:01:02,450 --> 00:01:08,300
So this is just one simple example of using the function, but you can use it in any other program that

14
00:01:08,300 --> 00:01:11,960
you want, so you're only limited by your imagination, really.

15
00:01:12,410 --> 00:01:18,170
Now, the example that I want to show you is going to be built up on the execute and report script.

00:01:18,170 --> 00:01:22,820

In this example, we're going to be using a program called Lesson and you actually see me using it before

18
00:01:29,990 --> 00:01:36,260
in the course when I was executing and executable on the Windows machine and I was getting the results

19
00:01:36,260 --> 00:01:38,990
of the saved passwords on my email.

20
00:01:39,710 --> 00:01:47,420
So the way this works is we have a program called Lesson and this program, when executed on a system,

21
00:01:47,420 --> 00:01:50,930
it shows all the saved passwords in this system.

22
00:01:51,530 --> 00:01:59,090
Now the program is designed as a post exploitation program, so you need to have access to the computer

23
00:01:59,090 --> 00:02:02,960
that you want to see its passwords before you can use it.

24
00:02:03,380 --> 00:02:10,190
But with our coding skills, we can create a script that will first download the program on the system.

25
00:02:10,190 --> 00:02:17,750
Second, execute the program, wait for the result, and third, it'll report the result to our email,

26
00:02:17,750 --> 00:02:23,960
thus allowing us to use this program remotely to steal all stored passwords from a computer.

27
00:02:24,650 --> 00:02:30,440
So in this lecture, I'm going to show you a quick overview of the lesson of the tools that will actually

28
00:02:30,440 --> 00:02:32,000
steal the passwords for us.

And in the next lecture, we're going to build the download Execute and report script, which will be

30
00:02:37,430 --> 00:02:42,470
based on our download function and the report and execute script that we built before.

31
00:02:42,470 --> 00:02:43,940
We'll see how we can use that.

32
00:02:43,940 --> 00:02:48,230
We'll do a few modifications and you'll see how useful that will be.

33
00:02:49,010 --> 00:02:53,040
So first of all, this lecture is not going to be a programming lecture.

34
00:02:53,060 --> 00:02:57,170
It's going to be a lecture where I'm going to be showing you an overview of lesson.

35
00:02:57,290 --> 00:03:02,300
So the first thing that I want to show you is lessons website or their git repo.

36
00:03:02,300 --> 00:03:05,270
And I'm going to include this in the resources of this lecture.

37
00:03:05,600 --> 00:03:12,620
Now on the releases, they have releases for Windows, Linux and OS X, but right now we want to use

38
00:03:12,620 --> 00:03:13,760
the Windows version.

39
00:03:13,760 --> 00:03:16,640
So I'm just going to download it if I just click here.

40
00:03:17,310 --> 00:03:19,260
That'll download it for me.

41
00:03:20,980 --> 00:03:22,930
I have it here in my downloads.

42
00:03:23,570 --> 00:03:29,270
Now this is a zip file, so you'll need winrar or winzip in order to extract this file.

43
00:03:29,270 --> 00:03:36,140
And we can right click it, click on extract here and we will see the contents of the file.

44
00:03:36,140 --> 00:03:39,800
And if we go inside this, you'll see that we have two versions.

45
00:03:39,800 --> 00:03:43,640
We have the 32 bit version and the 64 bit version.

46
00:03:43,640 --> 00:03:47,750
Now the 32 bit will always work against 32 and 64.

47
00:03:47,750 --> 00:03:53,510
So if you're not sure about the architecture of your target, then you can just use the 32 bit version.

48
00:03:54,350 --> 00:03:59,990
Now I'm going to cut this and just put it in my download so I can access it easily.

49
00:04:00,350 --> 00:04:06,230
And I'm just going to delete these two files that we downloaded and we have the program now and here.

50
00:04:07,460 --> 00:04:11,110
Now go into my terminal or to my command prompt.

51
00:04:11,120 --> 00:04:15,410
I'm already in the downloads so I can use listen straight away.

52
00:04:15,440 --> 00:04:20,390
I'm just going to make this a little bit bigger and we can just do lesson.

53
00:04:21,340 --> 00:04:25,030
And since we don't know how this works, I'm just going to type help after it.

54
00:04:26,710 --> 00:04:32,020
And this will show you the help menu of the tool, showing you exactly how to use the tool.

55
00:04:32,710 --> 00:04:35,290
Now, using the tool is very, very simple.

56
00:04:35,320 --> 00:04:40,360
Basically, you type the tool name followed by the module that you want to run.

57
00:04:40,960 --> 00:04:46,810
Now, each one of these modules will get you specific passwords and you can see the description of the

58
00:04:46,810 --> 00:04:47,950
module in here.

59
00:04:48,610 --> 00:04:55,780
For example, the males module will get you usernames and passwords stored on this computer for email

60
00:04:55,780 --> 00:04:56,650
accounts.

61
00:04:56,950 --> 00:05:03,790
We have the browsers module that will get you emails and password stored on browsers.

62
00:05:03,790 --> 00:05:09,670
So basically the user have to first of all log into their account and you know, when you log in, you

63
00:05:09,670 --> 00:05:14,110
get the pop up in here telling you if you want to store this password.

64
00:05:14,110 --> 00:05:16,870
So the user have to actually click on Save.

65
00:05:17,170 --> 00:05:25,480
For example, now if I just use a fake login here, let's say they get and put a password, which is

66
00:05:25,480 --> 00:05:29,590
one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight and log in.

67
00:05:29,770 --> 00:05:33,130
This password does not stored in this browser.

68
00:05:33,160 --> 00:05:39,910
The user has to actually click on save for this to be stored on the browser and now less than will be

69
00:05:39,910 --> 00:05:44,650
able to get you all of the saved passwords such as the one that we just saved.

70
00:05:45,520 --> 00:05:51,160
Now, if you want to use a specific module, then you can just type the module name after the tool name.

71
00:05:51,160 --> 00:05:54,790
So we have the tool name, which is lesson followed by the module name.

72
00:05:54,790 --> 00:06:01,240
And in my example, let's say I want to use the browser and then you can first of all do dash dash help

73
00:06:01,240 --> 00:06:05,950
to get further information on how to use this specific module.

74
00:06:06,310 --> 00:06:09,490
Or you can just remove this and run it as is.

75
00:06:09,550 --> 00:06:16,270
And again, as you can see now, it's showing us the password stored on my browser on Firefox.

76
00:06:16,270 --> 00:06:20,800
And as you can see, I have a password for Z at security dot org, which is one, two, three, four,

77
00:06:20,800 --> 00:06:21,760
five, six.

78
00:06:22,090 --> 00:06:28,570
And I have a password for GitHub dotcom, which is a git and the password right here.

79
00:06:29,440 --> 00:06:35,470
Now another cool thing that you can do, you can actually use all to run all the modules.

80
00:06:36,200 --> 00:06:39,230
So if I just do listen, all.

81
00:06:40,700 --> 00:06:46,250
This will run all of the modules that come with lasagne and it will show me all the passwords that lasagne

82
00:06:46,250 --> 00:06:48,020
can get on screen.

83
00:06:49,640 --> 00:06:50,990
So that's it for lasagne.

00:06:51,020 --> 00:06:52,560

of these evil files.

For now we know how to use lesson and we're able to produce all of this output by doing less than dot

93
00:07:33,950 --> 00:07:36,290
x e, followed by all.

94
00:07:36,980 --> 00:07:43,940
The only problem right now is we have to have access to the target computer so that we can see this

95
00:07:43,940 --> 00:07:45,710
result on our screen.

96
00:07:46,190 --> 00:07:53,120
So in a remote scenario where you want to get all this information from a target computer, you can't

97
00:07:53,120 --> 00:07:54,380
really use lesson.

So in the next lecture, we'll see how we can build a download Execute and report program that will

Second, execute this command right here and then send us all of this result to our email, allowing

101

I've already created a new program called Download Execute and Report.
