00:00:37,730 --> 00:00:45,020
So the first thing that I want to do in this program is download a file and in our example we want to

11
00:00:45,020 --> 00:00:46,280
download lesson.

12
00:00:46,580 --> 00:00:49,010
So I'm going to go to my download script.

13
00:00:49,280 --> 00:00:53,840
I'm going to copy everything and I'm going to paste it in here.

14
00:00:55,090 --> 00:00:55,780
Now.

15
00:00:55,780 --> 00:01:01,090
First of all, I don't care about how the download function works because we, like I said, we usually

16
00:01:01,090 --> 00:01:05,260
build that once we make sure we build it well and we forget about it.

17
00:01:05,950 --> 00:01:13,780
So what I care about right now is the download where I'm calling it in here and I'm passing a link to

18
00:01:13,780 --> 00:01:14,560
an image.

19
00:01:15,340 --> 00:01:20,470
Now in my example, I want to download lesson in the target computer.

20
00:01:20,830 --> 00:01:28,660
So I need to have listen uploaded on a web server and it needs to have a direct download link.

21
00:01:28,660 --> 00:01:34,930
So something like this where when the person clicks on it, it will download the file directly, it

22
00:01:34,930 --> 00:01:39,460
won't show HTML or timers or any of that.

23
00:01:40,270 --> 00:01:43,570
So I'm using my web server in Kali.

24
00:01:43,570 --> 00:01:51,570
So if I if you see my WW HTML level files, you'll see I have a file in here called Lesson Dot XY,

25
00:01:52,300 --> 00:01:59,140
which means that if we go to the Windows Machine just real quick and go to my evil files right here.

26
00:01:59,140 --> 00:02:07,510
You can see that I have a file called listen dot x right here so we can download this file from the

27
00:02:07,510 --> 00:02:13,300
link that you can see at the bottom of the browser or by going to ten zero 216, which is the IP of

28
00:02:13,300 --> 00:02:18,820
Kali, followed by Evil Files, which is the name of my directory, followed by the name of the file,

29
00:02:18,820 --> 00:02:21,540
which is less than dot x e.

30
00:02:22,090 --> 00:02:25,660
So this is going to be my download link, which I'm going to use in here.

31
00:02:26,880 --> 00:02:31,350
So it's going to be http ten zero 216.

32
00:02:31,800 --> 00:02:33,090
Evil files.

33
00:02:34,060 --> 00:02:38,650
And the file name is La Zone Dot E.

34
00:02:39,700 --> 00:02:41,370
Now that's the first step done.

35
00:02:41,380 --> 00:02:45,510
This will download listen to my target computer.

36
00:02:46,710 --> 00:02:50,430
Now the next two steps, we've actually already implemented them.

37
00:02:50,430 --> 00:02:58,050
So we need to first of all, execute lasagne and then wait for the result and send this result as an

38
00:02:58,050 --> 00:03:00,500
email to my own email address.

39
00:03:00,510 --> 00:03:07,560
And if you think of this functionality, we've already implemented it in our Execute and report program.

40
00:03:08,040 --> 00:03:16,020
So if I just click on that and copy everything in here, I'm going to come here and I'll paste that

41
00:03:16,020 --> 00:03:16,620
here.

42
00:03:17,400 --> 00:03:18,990
I'm just going to do a few fixes.

43
00:03:18,990 --> 00:03:24,360
So first of all, I'm going to get all of this import and have one import statement at the top.

44
00:03:27,290 --> 00:03:29,200
So I'll delete this.

45
00:03:29,210 --> 00:03:31,310
I'll delete these trailing lines.

46
00:03:31,670 --> 00:03:36,110
And after that, I'm going to paste the send mail function.

47
00:03:36,110 --> 00:03:39,530
And again, I'm going to minimize it because I don't care how it works.

48
00:03:39,530 --> 00:03:40,310
I know it works.

49
00:03:40,310 --> 00:03:41,090
And that's it.

50
00:03:42,120 --> 00:03:45,900
Then we have the code that executes our report and executes.

51
00:03:45,930 --> 00:03:47,370
I'm just going to bring that up.

52
00:03:47,610 --> 00:03:51,420
And we already implemented this and spent a few lectures implementing it.

53
00:03:51,420 --> 00:03:53,580
So I'm really not going to talk about it much.

54
00:03:53,850 --> 00:03:56,520
First of all, we're downloading our file here.

55
00:03:56,550 --> 00:04:00,990
Next is the command variable that executes our command.

56
00:04:00,990 --> 00:04:03,150
And our command is going to be very simple.

57
00:04:03,150 --> 00:04:09,420
So I'm actually going to remove this variable and I'm just going to write my command in here as a string.

58
00:04:10,140 --> 00:04:14,850
So my command is going to be executing the file that we download, which is liaison.

59
00:04:14,850 --> 00:04:19,040
So it's going to do less than dot xy.

60
00:04:20,100 --> 00:04:21,450
Followed by all.

61
00:04:21,750 --> 00:04:26,910
So it's exactly what we were doing in here in my terminal.

62
00:04:29,650 --> 00:04:35,710
Now the result of this command is going to be captured in the result variable and then the result variable

63
00:04:35,710 --> 00:04:41,590
is going to send this result to my own email where I'm going to see the results.

64
00:04:42,560 --> 00:04:47,380
So as you can see, we actually didn't program anything in this lecture.

65
00:04:47,390 --> 00:04:54,980
All we did is we used a very, very important skill that programmers have, which is reusing code.

66
00:04:55,040 --> 00:05:01,610
So we use the download code which we already implemented before we use the email function again, we

67
00:05:01,610 --> 00:05:03,290
already implemented that before.

68
00:05:03,290 --> 00:05:10,100
And even the code in the main program in here or in the main script, we implemented all of this before.

69
00:05:10,790 --> 00:05:15,410
So all we did is just copy paste code from code that we already implemented.

70
00:05:15,410 --> 00:05:20,330
And we're using two helper functions that can be used in so many scenarios.

71
00:05:20,330 --> 00:05:22,240
And this is just one example.

72
00:05:22,250 --> 00:05:27,980
Like I said, you can mix these functions together with everything that we learned so far and with the

73
00:05:28,000 --> 00:05:29,360
stuff that we will learn.

74
00:05:29,360 --> 00:05:33,590
So you can use them in so many scenarios to achieve different goals.

75
00:05:34,530 --> 00:05:39,300
So I have this saved and I'm going to transfer this to my Windows machine again.

76
00:05:39,300 --> 00:05:44,190
I'm going to use my web server and I put it I'm going to put it in my evil files and then download it

77
00:05:44,190 --> 00:05:45,120
from Windows.

78
00:05:45,120 --> 00:05:49,890
But I'm going to cut this from the lecture because I don't want the lecture to be boring as I covered

79
00:05:49,890 --> 00:05:50,820
this before.

80
00:05:51,560 --> 00:05:57,380
Now I have the file here, the download Execute and the report program and I'm actually going to remove

81
00:05:57,380 --> 00:06:02,390
this than the one that we manually downloaded and I'll remove this as well.

82
00:06:02,810 --> 00:06:06,410
And let's see, let's run the program and see what happens.

83
00:06:06,410 --> 00:06:12,500
So I'm going to make this smaller and I'm going to clear the screen.

84
00:06:13,990 --> 00:06:17,710
And then I'm going to run the program again using Python as usual.

85
00:06:17,740 --> 00:06:25,300
We'll talk about packaging later and the name of my script is Download, Execute and Report Dot Pie.

86
00:06:25,390 --> 00:06:28,960
I'm going to hit enter and notice what's going to happen in here.

87
00:06:33,290 --> 00:06:35,840
Now the script got executed.

88
00:06:35,840 --> 00:06:38,900
And as you can see, Lozano was downloaded in here.

89
00:06:38,960 --> 00:06:46,190
And in theory, this should have executed lasagne, got the result and sent it to my own email.

90
00:06:46,310 --> 00:06:52,370
So let's go to Cali and I've already logged into my email and as you can see, I got a new message.

91
00:06:52,700 --> 00:06:59,480
If I click on this, you'll see I have the report that we usually get by lasagne and I have the password

92
00:06:59,480 --> 00:07:02,300
stored in Firefox on that system.

93
00:07:02,300 --> 00:07:07,670
And if there were any other password stored on the target system, then I'd be able to get them.

94
00:07:08,120 --> 00:07:15,500
So now using the download execute and report or using the functions that we built so far, we're able

95
00:07:15,500 --> 00:07:22,640
to download an external program on the target system, execute the program, get the result, and get

96
00:07:22,640 --> 00:07:24,500
the result on our email.

97
00:07:25,160 --> 00:07:33,230
And we manage to do all of this by building a download execute and report script using a smaller helper

98
00:07:33,230 --> 00:07:35,930
functions that we already built before.

99
00:07:36,740 --> 00:07:38,750
Now there is one problem with our script.

100
00:07:38,750 --> 00:07:44,360
If you look at it in here, it downloaded lesson and the same working directory.

101
00:07:44,360 --> 00:07:50,870
So if this was a normal user and they executed our file, they will see that there is a new file showing

102
00:07:50,870 --> 00:07:54,380
up on their directory and they don't know what this file is.

103
00:07:55,160 --> 00:07:58,070
Now in the next lecture we'll address this issue.

104
00:07:58,070 --> 00:08:02,510
And as we do that, you're going to learn some some more useful python tricks.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,630 --> 00:00:07,740
Now in the previous lecture, we seen an example of using smaller functions that we already programmed

2
00:00:07,740 --> 00:00:14,670
to build a script that will download the file, execute it, and send the result to our email.

3
00:00:14,670 --> 00:00:21,570
And as an example, we used a program called Lesson to get all the passwords stored on the target computer

4
00:00:21,570 --> 00:00:22,890
to our email.

5
00:00:23,040 --> 00:00:29,670
This is actually very, very handy because lasagne is a post exploitation tool, so it can't be used

6
00:00:29,670 --> 00:00:31,860
unless you have access to the program.

7
00:00:31,860 --> 00:00:38,040
And using our programming skills, we were able to use it remotely without having access to the target

8
00:00:38,040 --> 00:00:38,850
computer.

9
00:00:39,510 --> 00:00:46,680
Now, the main problem with our script is once it's executed, it downloads the file in the same working

10
00:00:46,680 --> 00:00:47,430
directory.

11
00:00:47,430 --> 00:00:53,970
So the user will see that there is a new file on their system that they didn't download themselves that

12
00:00:53,970 --> 00:00:57,660
just appeared magically in their file system.

13
00:00:58,170 --> 00:01:06,090
So we can actually enhance our program by deleting this file at the end of our program.

14
00:01:06,630 --> 00:01:12,420
So to do that or to delete any file in Python, again, everything I do is just examples.

15
00:01:12,420 --> 00:01:17,850
So when I show you how to delete a file, it's the same whether you want to do it in an evil file or

16
00:01:17,850 --> 00:01:20,100
if you're doing it in a just normal program.

17
00:01:20,460 --> 00:01:23,850
To do that, we're going to use a library called OHS.

18
00:01:24,120 --> 00:01:31,800
And basically this module allows us to run a number of operating system tasks, but they will work on

19
00:01:31,800 --> 00:01:33,150
all operating systems.

20
00:01:33,150 --> 00:01:35,640
So basically it's cross-platform.

21
00:01:35,880 --> 00:01:43,470
So instead of using OS, you could have just come in here and use set process, dot call and use a Windows

22
00:01:43,470 --> 00:01:45,640
Command to delete the file.

23
00:01:45,660 --> 00:01:49,230
That will work, but that will only work on Windows.

24
00:01:49,350 --> 00:01:51,780
But our script is so cool right now.

25
00:01:51,780 --> 00:01:57,930
It works against all operating systems because the download function is cross platform, the sentinel

26
00:01:57,930 --> 00:02:03,690
is cross platform and the sub process, the check output is cross platform.

27
00:02:03,690 --> 00:02:08,100
So this program will work against all operating systems.

28
00:02:08,100 --> 00:02:14,910
That's why it will be nice if we can maintain this compatibility when we add new features to it.

29
00:02:15,540 --> 00:02:21,630
So instead of just doing some process, don't call and then call in a Windows Command to delete a file.

30
00:02:21,630 --> 00:02:28,500
We can just use OS doh to remove and put the file that we want to remove.

31
00:02:28,500 --> 00:02:32,300
And in our case the file is called Zone.

32
00:02:34,320 --> 00:02:34,620
Now.

33
00:02:34,620 --> 00:02:35,430
That's very cool.

34
00:02:35,430 --> 00:02:41,850
So now our file is going to be first downloaded, executed, and once we're done with executing the

35
00:02:41,850 --> 00:02:47,880
file, it will be removed from the working directory so that it won't be very suspicious.

36
00:02:48,180 --> 00:02:50,160
But we still have a problem.

37
00:02:50,280 --> 00:02:57,570
This file is going to exist in the working directory as long as lesson is being executed.

38
00:02:57,930 --> 00:03:05,880
And as you've seen before later that takes about five to up to 60 seconds depending on the resources

39
00:03:05,880 --> 00:03:07,050
on the system.

40
00:03:07,470 --> 00:03:14,550
So this file is going to exist on the system for at least 5 seconds in most operating systems.

41
00:03:15,330 --> 00:03:21,810
Therefore, when this script is executed and you can test it on your own time, you'll see the lasagne

42
00:03:21,870 --> 00:03:24,390
file or the file that's supposed to be downloaded.

43
00:03:24,390 --> 00:03:30,690
You'll see it here for a few seconds and then you'll see it disappearing, which again will be even

44
00:03:30,690 --> 00:03:37,170
more suspicious if the user is looking at the working directory, which chances are they will be looking

45
00:03:37,170 --> 00:03:37,650
at it.

46
00:03:38,580 --> 00:03:46,440
So a better solution would be to download this file into somewhere that the user is not looking at somewhere

47
00:03:46,440 --> 00:03:52,140
like the temp directory, execute it and when done even remove it from the temp.

48
00:03:52,140 --> 00:03:53,940
So there are no traces.

49
00:03:54,690 --> 00:04:01,230
Now again you can use the sub process module to navigate to the temp directory in here.

50
00:04:01,230 --> 00:04:08,610
So you can literally do a process call and do KD temp to go to temp and then this will automatically

51
00:04:08,610 --> 00:04:12,060
download this into temp and the user will not see it.

52
00:04:12,210 --> 00:04:17,400
But like I said, it will be nice to keep this program cross-platform.

53
00:04:17,400 --> 00:04:22,470
So we're going to use Python functions and not rely on system commands.

54
00:04:22,860 --> 00:04:30,690
So what we want to do right now is first of all, go to the temp directory before we execute this download

55
00:04:30,690 --> 00:04:35,850
function, because this download function will download the file to the working directory.

56
00:04:35,850 --> 00:04:42,060
So if we can move to another directory, then lasagne will be downloaded to the directory that we move

57
00:04:42,060 --> 00:04:42,600
to.

58
00:04:43,500 --> 00:04:50,010
Now, first of all, we don't know where the temp directory is because it's different depending on the

59
00:04:50,010 --> 00:04:51,210
operating system.

60
00:04:51,870 --> 00:04:57,870
So in order to get the temp directory, we can use a module called Temp File.

61
00:04:58,290 --> 00:05:00,840
So again, we're going to import that in here.

62
00:05:03,260 --> 00:05:07,670
And then we're going to say temp directory.

63
00:05:08,520 --> 00:05:13,200
Is equal to the module that I just imported, which is temp file.

64
00:05:14,980 --> 00:05:20,350
Dot get temp there, which basically returns the temp directory.

65
00:05:21,140 --> 00:05:27,710
Then I'm going to use a function in the OS module that we already imported in here, and this function

66
00:05:27,710 --> 00:05:30,710
allows us to change the working directory.

67
00:05:30,710 --> 00:05:36,440
So again, you can use this function any time you want to move within the system and change your working

68
00:05:36,440 --> 00:05:37,220
directory.

69
00:05:37,340 --> 00:05:47,840
So we can just do ose dot c h d r and give the directory that we want to go to or to change our working

70
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:48,980
directory to.

71
00:05:49,040 --> 00:05:52,820
And in our example, we want to move to the temp directory.

72
00:05:54,350 --> 00:06:01,880
And this will move us to whatever directory is stored in the temp directory variable which will contain

73
00:06:01,880 --> 00:06:04,580
whatever is returned from temp file.

74
00:06:04,820 --> 00:06:07,820
Get temp directory which returns the temp directory.

75
00:06:08,210 --> 00:06:14,810
So we're first of all getting the location of the temp directory and this is a cross-platform way to

76
00:06:14,810 --> 00:06:16,820
get the temporary directory.

77
00:06:17,270 --> 00:06:20,540
Then we're going to change our working directory to temp.

78
00:06:21,230 --> 00:06:24,140
Then we're going to download our eval file.

79
00:06:24,170 --> 00:06:28,780
We're going to execute the evil file and wait for its result.

80
00:06:28,790 --> 00:06:30,920
We're going to report its result.

81
00:06:30,920 --> 00:06:34,130
And then we're going to remove this evil file.

82
00:06:35,250 --> 00:06:43,230
Now the OIS module is very useful and as I said, you can use it to do so many related tasks and everything

83
00:06:43,230 --> 00:06:45,870
that you do with it will be cross-platform.

84
00:06:45,960 --> 00:06:51,390
I will add link to its page in the resources so that you can spend more time reading through it and

85
00:06:51,390 --> 00:06:53,120
seeing the things that you can do with it.

86
00:06:53,130 --> 00:06:58,290
But this is a very good way of showing it in practice, in a useful example.

87
00:06:58,770 --> 00:07:02,970
So I'm going to start this and transfer this to my Windows machine.

88
00:07:04,390 --> 00:07:07,480
And now I have the file here downloaded.

89
00:07:08,050 --> 00:07:15,100
So I'm actually going to remove this and this is just the file that will be executing the download execute

90
00:07:15,100 --> 00:07:16,120
and report.

91
00:07:16,630 --> 00:07:18,400
So let's run this.

92
00:07:21,700 --> 00:07:25,180
And as you can see, we didn't get anything in here.

93
00:07:25,180 --> 00:07:27,250
There was no files downloaded in here.

94
00:07:27,250 --> 00:07:29,260
Nothing showed up and disappeared.

95
00:07:29,260 --> 00:07:32,050
So it's not suspicious at all.

96
00:07:32,440 --> 00:07:37,150
And if we go to our email, go to the inbox, you'll see.

97
00:07:37,150 --> 00:07:39,910
We have a new email with our report.

98
00:07:41,220 --> 00:07:47,460
The reason why we didn't see anything in here because the file was downloaded to the temp directory

99
00:07:47,460 --> 00:07:50,880
and not to the directory where the script is stored.

100
00:07:51,690 --> 00:07:54,390
Now, let me just show you this so you have a better idea.

101
00:07:54,390 --> 00:08:00,900
I'm going to go to my temp directory, which is in C Z, APPDATA local temp.

102
00:08:02,260 --> 00:08:04,540
And right now you don't even see the file.

103
00:08:04,540 --> 00:08:08,990
You don't see lasagne because it gets removed at the end of our script.

104
00:08:09,010 --> 00:08:10,250
But watch this.

105
00:08:10,270 --> 00:08:16,720
If I run the script, you'll see that the file will show up in here for a very short period of time.

106
00:08:16,720 --> 00:08:20,830
And this is the time while the script is being executed.

107
00:08:20,840 --> 00:08:29,080
And this is the reason why I added extra code at the start in here so that we change the working directory

108
00:08:29,110 --> 00:08:31,450
before executing the file.

109
00:08:32,470 --> 00:08:37,240
So let's just run it very quick and keep an eye on what's going to happen in here.

110
00:08:39,150 --> 00:08:45,060
As you can see, we have the lasagne here for a split second and then it's gone because it was executed

111
00:08:45,060 --> 00:08:46,980
and it sent me the result.

112
00:08:47,520 --> 00:08:53,010
Now, this is not very suspicious because chances are the user is not going to be watching the temp

113
00:08:53,010 --> 00:08:58,120
directory when they run our file, when we package the file and send it to the user.

114
00:08:58,140 --> 00:09:04,080
They will be looking at the directory where they executed the file from, which will be this directory.

115
00:09:04,080 --> 00:09:11,040
So the file is only going to exist on the system for a short period of time and it will only exist in

116
00:09:11,040 --> 00:09:15,060
a directory that the user is not looking at in the temp directory.

117
00:09:16,190 --> 00:09:17,540
So that's it for now.

118
00:09:17,570 --> 00:09:21,770
I just wanted to make sure that when we build something, it's actually useful.

119
00:09:21,770 --> 00:09:24,380
And that's why I spent one extra lecture.

120
00:09:24,830 --> 00:09:31,160
With this extra lecture, we actually learned about how to use AWS to remove to change the working directory,

121
00:09:31,160 --> 00:09:33,820
and we learned how to get the temp directory.

122
00:09:33,830 --> 00:09:39,980
Everything we learned is cross-platform and like I said, it can be used in any scenario.

123
00:09:39,980 --> 00:09:42,530
It doesn't have to be used in this script.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Now in the next lecture, we're going to start writing our own remote.

2
00:00:05,280 --> 00:00:12,720
Keylogger a Keylogger is a program that records all keystrokes entered on the keyboard.

3
00:00:12,750 --> 00:00:19,290
Now, from this description, you can probably see how useful this can be because it's going to register

4
00:00:19,290 --> 00:00:21,980
everything that the user type on their keyboard.

5
00:00:21,990 --> 00:00:27,990
So you're going to be able to see the usernames, the passwords, chat messages, emails, anything

6
00:00:27,990 --> 00:00:29,010
they type, really.

7
00:00:30,240 --> 00:00:32,490
Now there are two main types of keyloggers.

8
00:00:32,490 --> 00:00:34,750
We have local keyloggers.

9
00:00:34,770 --> 00:00:41,850
These are Keyloggers that will store the entered keys in a specific location on the target computer.

10
00:00:42,180 --> 00:00:49,620
And we have remote keyloggers and these are more useful in my opinion, because they will send you reports

11
00:00:49,620 --> 00:00:54,810
of the entered keys to your own email account or to your own server.

12
00:00:56,230 --> 00:00:59,230
So we're going to be building a remote key logger.

13
00:00:59,230 --> 00:01:05,770
And as we do this, I'm going to teach you about recursive functions, threading and object oriented

14
00:01:05,770 --> 00:01:06,580
programming.

15
00:01:07,300 --> 00:01:11,930
Right now, I just want to show you a teaser of the program that we're going to build.

16
00:01:11,950 --> 00:01:15,480
So, as usual, don't worry about how this is working.

17
00:01:15,490 --> 00:01:19,600
I will cover how this works in details in the next lectures.

18
00:01:19,600 --> 00:01:25,300
For now, I just want you to see what we're going to build and get an idea of our goal.

19
00:01:27,050 --> 00:01:30,390
So right here, I have the target windows machine.

20
00:01:30,410 --> 00:01:33,400
This is the machine that we want to spy on.

21
00:01:33,410 --> 00:01:37,250
And I have the backdoor right here called Z Logger.

22
00:01:37,460 --> 00:01:42,730
Now, I know this still looks a little bit suspicious, but don't worry about this.

23
00:01:42,740 --> 00:01:49,940
We will cover how to convert this file and similar files to Trojans so that they look and function like

24
00:01:49,940 --> 00:01:52,870
any other file types, like images and books.

25
00:01:52,880 --> 00:01:57,650
But for now, I just want to give you a teaser on how this keylogger will work.

26
00:01:58,400 --> 00:02:04,760
So I'm going to run it right now and we're getting a warning message that you always get in windows

27
00:02:04,760 --> 00:02:08,110
when you run X is with an unknown publisher.

28
00:02:08,120 --> 00:02:09,470
So this is normal.

29
00:02:09,470 --> 00:02:11,510
We're just going to say run anyway.

30
00:02:12,390 --> 00:02:15,780
And this should run the backdoor for me in the background.

31
00:02:16,260 --> 00:02:20,760
Logging every keystroke entered by the user.

32
00:02:21,180 --> 00:02:28,230
So literally any time they use the keyboard, the key strikes will be logged by my program and they

33
00:02:28,230 --> 00:02:30,750
will be sent to me by email.

34
00:02:31,810 --> 00:02:35,650
So let's go ahead and try to type some key strikes and test it.

35
00:02:36,190 --> 00:02:37,840
So let's go to Facebook.

36
00:02:40,830 --> 00:02:49,380
Let's put a username, Azadeh, and let's put a password as password for Facebook.

37
00:02:50,850 --> 00:02:51,500
Okay.

38
00:02:51,510 --> 00:02:52,220
We'll hit enter.

39
00:02:52,260 --> 00:02:53,280
Doesn't really matter.

40
00:02:54,200 --> 00:02:58,400
Now let's go to my email and see if the keylogger worked.

41
00:02:58,610 --> 00:03:04,820
And as you can see, we got the first email which basically telling us that the keylogger started and

42
00:03:04,820 --> 00:03:11,330
then we will start receiving reports based on the time interval that we set in the keylogger.

43
00:03:12,850 --> 00:03:13,540
And perfect.

44
00:03:13,540 --> 00:03:15,970
As you can see, we got a report here.

45
00:03:16,270 --> 00:03:23,350
If we open it, you'll see that the user typed Facebook.com, hit enter, put a username which was Z,

46
00:03:23,350 --> 00:03:26,980
and then they put a password, which was password for Facebook.

47
00:03:27,700 --> 00:03:29,410
Now this is just an example.

48
00:03:29,410 --> 00:03:34,390
Like I said, this will record anything that the user types using their keyboard.

49
00:03:34,840 --> 00:03:38,050
And again, don't worry about how this works.

50
00:03:38,050 --> 00:03:41,350
We will cover this in details in this section.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:01,450
Now.

2
00:00:01,450 --> 00:00:06,670
So far we built a number of nice but simple evil files.

3
00:00:06,880 --> 00:00:10,040
We built stuff like the download and execute.

4
00:00:10,060 --> 00:00:14,140
Download the report and execute, execute and report.

5
00:00:14,140 --> 00:00:18,910
And all of these programs are useful and can be used in so many scenarios.

6
00:00:18,910 --> 00:00:24,760
And the idea was to cover a small thing in each lecture, and then you'll be able to use these small

7
00:00:24,760 --> 00:00:32,020
functions as is, or you can mix them together to build up programs that will achieve your goal depending

8
00:00:32,020 --> 00:00:33,250
on your scenario.

9
00:00:34,080 --> 00:00:38,010
Now I want to start doing stuff that is a little bit more complex.

10
00:00:38,220 --> 00:00:42,180
And the first thing that I want to do is build a keylogger.

11
00:00:43,030 --> 00:00:49,870
Now a keylogger is basically a program that records all keystrokes entered on the keyboard.

12
00:00:50,620 --> 00:00:56,680
From this description, you can see that this program can be very useful in so many scenarios and it's

13
00:00:56,680 --> 00:01:03,850
used a lot throughout penetration testing because if you manage to plant this program on a system using

14
00:01:03,850 --> 00:01:10,630
social engineering or by physically transferring this file to a computer and executing it, once this

15
00:01:10,630 --> 00:01:15,990
program works, it's going to record everything that the user enter on their keyboard.

16
00:01:16,000 --> 00:01:22,360
So this will include usernames, passwords, chat messages or anything they literally do.

17
00:01:23,170 --> 00:01:28,930
Now there are already a number of keyloggers that are ready to use and you can download and use yourself

18
00:01:29,050 --> 00:01:34,960
and the common features that you'll see in these keyloggers are some of them will store the key strikes

19
00:01:34,960 --> 00:01:38,830
on the local machine and hence the name local keyloggers.

20
00:01:38,830 --> 00:01:45,310
And then there are Keyloggers that will send you the logs or the key strikes to an email or to your

21
00:01:45,310 --> 00:01:46,150
own server.

22
00:01:46,150 --> 00:01:48,250
And these are called remote keyloggers.

23
00:01:48,250 --> 00:01:55,000
And these are actually more useful in my opinion, because in many scenarios you're going to be able

24
00:01:55,000 --> 00:02:01,330
to see the key strikes remotely without having to get back to the computer where the keylogger is installed.

25
00:02:01,960 --> 00:02:07,450
Some of them will also take screenshots and start with this with the system start up.

26
00:02:07,690 --> 00:02:13,480
But for now, let's forget about all of these features and let's focus on the main goal.

27
00:02:13,480 --> 00:02:18,100
And once we get the main goal, as you see, the main features are just going to be small functions

28
00:02:18,100 --> 00:02:20,560
that will that will be easy to implement.

29
00:02:20,560 --> 00:02:24,430
And you probably already know how to implement some of these small features.

30
00:02:25,120 --> 00:02:31,600
So the main goal is to write a program that can record keys entered through the keyboard.

31
00:02:32,440 --> 00:02:35,910
Now to do this with Python, it's actually very, very easy.

32
00:02:35,920 --> 00:02:39,220
We're going to use a library called Pie Input.

33
00:02:39,400 --> 00:02:44,620
I'm going to include a link in the resources for the documentation of this library if you want to learn

34
00:02:44,620 --> 00:02:45,430
more about it.

35
00:02:45,430 --> 00:02:49,360
But I'm going to cover it in this lecture and the next few lectures anyway.

36
00:02:49,960 --> 00:02:55,630
So the first thing that you need to do is install this library because it doesn't come preinstalled

37
00:02:55,630 --> 00:02:56,560
with Kali.

38
00:02:56,920 --> 00:03:04,510
So as usual, when we want to install a Python module, all we do is pip and install followed by the

39
00:03:04,510 --> 00:03:09,310
module or the library name and in my case it's called PY and put.

40
00:03:10,700 --> 00:03:13,680
Now it's telling me that this is already installed for me.

41
00:03:13,700 --> 00:03:14,480
For you?

42
00:03:14,480 --> 00:03:16,370
It should install it with no errors.

43
00:03:16,370 --> 00:03:19,160
There is no need to configure anything.

44
00:03:19,340 --> 00:03:22,490
And once it's installed, we are ready to write our program.

45
00:03:22,490 --> 00:03:25,910
And we'll start as usual by importing this library.

46
00:03:25,910 --> 00:03:29,960
So we're going to do import PY input.

47
00:03:30,770 --> 00:03:36,980
Now this library allows us to monitor mouse and keyboard and it also allows us to control mouse and

48
00:03:36,980 --> 00:03:43,310
keyboard so we can literally send key strikes and mouse clicks, but we're only interested into recording

49
00:03:43,310 --> 00:03:48,440
the keyboard, so we're only going to import input the keyboard.

50
00:03:50,460 --> 00:03:56,700
Next, we're going to create a keyboard listener object that will listen for key strikes entered on

51
00:03:56,700 --> 00:03:57,540
the keyboard.

52
00:03:57,780 --> 00:04:01,020
So I'm going to create a variable called keyboard listener.

53
00:04:02,380 --> 00:04:08,230
And I'm going to make this equal to the library that I just downloaded, which is pie input.

54
00:04:09,350 --> 00:04:14,960
I want to use the keyboard and I want to create a listener.

55
00:04:16,470 --> 00:04:22,320
So basically what we're doing is we're creating an instance of a listener object that we are taking

56
00:04:22,320 --> 00:04:28,260
from the PY input keyboard which we import it in here and what we want to do in here.

57
00:04:28,260 --> 00:04:35,370
We want to specify a callback function to be executed every time a key strike is entered.

58
00:04:35,400 --> 00:04:42,540
So this is something similar to the snip function with KP when we specified a callback function in the

59
00:04:42,540 --> 00:04:44,040
PR and argument.

60
00:04:44,040 --> 00:04:48,360
So that callback function was executed every time we received the packet.

61
00:04:48,600 --> 00:04:55,710
This time we're going to say on press equal to the name of the callback function that we want to be

62
00:04:55,710 --> 00:05:01,800
executed every time a key strike or the user presses a key on the keyboard.

63
00:05:01,920 --> 00:05:06,060
So I'm going to call this function process key press.

64
00:05:07,920 --> 00:05:11,010
And then I'm going to implement this function in here.

65
00:05:11,010 --> 00:05:18,690
So I'm going to define a new function and the function name is going to be called process key press.

66
00:05:19,810 --> 00:05:22,240
And this function is going to take a variable.

67
00:05:22,270 --> 00:05:26,230
This variable is going to be the key that the user has pressed.

68
00:05:26,590 --> 00:05:30,940
And all I'm going to do inside this function for now is print the key.

69
00:05:32,440 --> 00:05:36,310
So this is actually very similar to what we were doing with Skippy.

70
00:05:36,340 --> 00:05:42,790
We're specifying a callback function to be executed every time a user presses a key on the keyboard.

71
00:05:42,880 --> 00:05:48,580
The name of this callback function can be anything, and I'm choosing to call it process key press.

72
00:05:48,790 --> 00:05:52,820
Then I implemented the process key press just like any other function.

73
00:05:52,840 --> 00:05:57,100
It takes a variable called key, and then I'm printing the key on screen.

74
00:05:57,970 --> 00:06:04,120
Now, going back to our code, we created the keyboard listener, but we didn't start this listener.

75
00:06:04,120 --> 00:06:09,880
So we have a variable that holds an instance of the keyboard listener, but we haven't done anything

76
00:06:09,880 --> 00:06:10,630
with this.

77
00:06:11,410 --> 00:06:15,820
Now, to start this, first of all, we're going to use the width keyboard.

78
00:06:16,060 --> 00:06:21,230
Now you see an example of using this when we were opening files to read or to write.

79
00:06:21,250 --> 00:06:26,770
But here we have a different example of using it to interact with the keyboard listener.

80
00:06:27,650 --> 00:06:34,130
Basically the with keyboard and python is used to interact with unmanaged streams of data.

81
00:06:34,130 --> 00:06:39,410
So something like opening a file or using a listener like the one that we're going to use right now.

82
00:06:40,190 --> 00:06:48,740
So we're going to say with keyboard listener, I want to start this listener by doing keyboard listener,

83
00:06:48,740 --> 00:06:50,270
dot join.

84
00:06:51,820 --> 00:06:52,480
And that's it.

85
00:06:52,510 --> 00:06:53,140
We're done.

86
00:06:53,500 --> 00:06:59,140
Now, keep in mind, I've actually read through the documentation of PI input, which I'm going to include

87
00:06:59,140 --> 00:07:00,760
in the resources of this lecture.

88
00:07:00,760 --> 00:07:04,960
And this is usually the same with everything that I show or teach in here.

89
00:07:05,380 --> 00:07:07,570
I don't know everything about Python.

90
00:07:07,570 --> 00:07:08,620
I know the basics.

91
00:07:08,620 --> 00:07:13,810
And then when I want to use a module, I go, I read the documentation and I learn about how to use

92
00:07:13,810 --> 00:07:15,120
it and then use it.

93
00:07:15,130 --> 00:07:20,650
So for example, I didn't know that there was a joint method that I can use with the keyboard listener

94
00:07:20,650 --> 00:07:21,930
in order to start it.

95
00:07:21,940 --> 00:07:26,140
But reading through the documentation, I knew that I have to do it.

96
00:07:26,440 --> 00:07:33,400
Another thing to mention, for example, I didn't know that the callback function has to be passed to

97
00:07:33,400 --> 00:07:34,360
on press.

98
00:07:34,600 --> 00:07:40,690
For example, with KP, we had to pass it to the PRN in here we're passing it to on price.

99
00:07:40,960 --> 00:07:46,540
Again, reading the documentation will cover all of this and will allow you to know all of all of these

100
00:07:46,540 --> 00:07:49,390
things so you can't just learn them by default.

101
00:07:50,020 --> 00:07:55,360
Now we have our very basic keylogger done, and if you look at the code, it's actually very, very

102
00:07:55,360 --> 00:07:57,160
simple, but it works well.

103
00:07:57,460 --> 00:08:03,880
First of all, we're creating a variable which is going to hold an instance of the listener object of

104
00:08:03,880 --> 00:08:06,490
the API input library, which we imported here.

105
00:08:06,670 --> 00:08:12,490
And basically what we're saying is, for every time a key is pressed on the keyboard, I want you to

106
00:08:12,490 --> 00:08:19,150
call a function called process key press the process key press function will take a variable called

107
00:08:19,150 --> 00:08:21,700
key and we'll print the key on the screen.

108
00:08:22,330 --> 00:08:28,020
And then I'm interacting with my listener and I'm starting the listener by putting the listener name

109
00:08:28,150 --> 00:08:28,900
or join.

110
00:08:29,710 --> 00:08:36,040
Now let's go to our terminal and I'm already in the working directory of my project of my key logger,

111
00:08:36,040 --> 00:08:38,080
so I'm just going to do Python.

112
00:08:39,340 --> 00:08:41,140
Keylogger dot pi.

113
00:08:41,770 --> 00:08:42,960
I'm going to hit enter.

114
00:08:42,970 --> 00:08:45,130
This will start the keylogger for me.

115
00:08:45,310 --> 00:08:50,890
And just as a test, I'm going to go to this second terminal window and I'm going to start typing some

116
00:08:50,890 --> 00:08:51,730
characters.

117
00:08:51,730 --> 00:08:57,430
And you'll see as soon as I start typing stuff on my keyboard, it's going to be logged and displayed

118
00:08:57,430 --> 00:08:58,750
on screen in here.

119
00:08:59,140 --> 00:09:00,610
So let's just type.

120
00:09:00,610 --> 00:09:02,890
This is a test.

121
00:09:03,610 --> 00:09:10,330
And as you can see, we have all the characters being logged in here and you can even see the space

122
00:09:10,330 --> 00:09:12,820
key is being logged as a space key.

123
00:09:12,850 --> 00:09:16,030
Now, if I press tab, you'll see that we have key tab.

124
00:09:16,030 --> 00:09:21,370
If you press any of the special keys, like a down arrow or an up arrow, everything is going to be

125
00:09:21,370 --> 00:09:24,670
displayed on screen and logged through our program.

126
00:09:25,150 --> 00:09:31,420
Now, if we want to kill this, I'm just going to do a kill all python, and that will kill all Python

127
00:09:31,420 --> 00:09:34,210
programs, including the one that's running right now.

128
00:09:35,620 --> 00:09:41,350
Now we have a basic keylogger and we managed to solve the main issue with building a keylogger.

129
00:09:41,350 --> 00:09:47,650
So the main purpose of a keylogger program is to be able to record keystrokes.

130
00:09:47,650 --> 00:09:53,890
So now we're able to record the key strikes and everything that comes after here is going to be polish

131
00:09:53,890 --> 00:09:58,540
in the code and implementing the features that make the keylogger useful.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,180 --> 00:00:07,300
Okay, now that we have the basic functionality of a keylogger, so we're able to record keystrokes

2
00:00:07,300 --> 00:00:08,920
entered on the keyboard.

3
00:00:09,220 --> 00:00:17,740
Let's work more on making this a more useful program, because keep in mind the goal of this is to send

4
00:00:17,740 --> 00:00:22,630
this to as an executable to the target person so that they run it.

5
00:00:22,630 --> 00:00:26,350
It runs in the background and it'll send us all key strikes.

6
00:00:26,680 --> 00:00:31,120
Right now, as you can see, it's printing the key strikes on the terminal.

7
00:00:31,120 --> 00:00:37,330
So when you send this to a user, the user is going to see all the key strikes they enter on their screen.

8
00:00:37,330 --> 00:00:40,480
But this is not useful at all for us as hackers.

9
00:00:40,870 --> 00:00:45,400
So the first thing that comes into mind right now is logging.

10
00:00:45,400 --> 00:00:51,610
We need to be able to log this data, the key strikes that the user is entering somewhere.

11
00:00:52,330 --> 00:00:55,990
So going back to my slides, we said that there are two types.

12
00:00:55,990 --> 00:01:01,930
There are the local keyloggers, the ones that would store the data locally and there is the remote

13
00:01:01,930 --> 00:01:09,520
ones that would send the data to a server or an email allowing us to monitor the key strikes remotely

14
00:01:09,520 --> 00:01:12,130
and hence the name Remote Keyloggers.

15
00:01:12,760 --> 00:01:18,370
Now if we go up, we know that the characters are being printed in here.

16
00:01:18,370 --> 00:01:24,190
So the key variable contains the value for each character that the user enters.

17
00:01:25,030 --> 00:01:31,870
So all we have to do now is implement a logging mechanism in here so you can, for example, open a

18
00:01:31,870 --> 00:01:34,420
file and write the data to the file.

19
00:01:34,420 --> 00:01:37,150
And we covered opening a writing files before.

20
00:01:37,150 --> 00:01:40,450
So this will be the exact same as we did it before.

21
00:01:41,230 --> 00:01:46,930
Or you can send an email with the key with the key variable as the content.

22
00:01:46,930 --> 00:01:49,720
And again, we covered send in emails before.

23
00:01:50,290 --> 00:01:57,670
The only problem with this is you will be opening a file and writing to it or sending an email.

24
00:01:57,790 --> 00:02:00,700
Each time the user enters a key strike.

25
00:02:00,790 --> 00:02:07,960
So for example, if the user is writing a sentence which contains 20 letters, then you'll end up receiving

26
00:02:07,990 --> 00:02:15,820
20 emails, or you'd be opening a file 20 times and each time writing a single character.

27
00:02:16,720 --> 00:02:18,910
In both cases, this is not smart.

28
00:02:19,060 --> 00:02:24,580
A better implementation is to use a variable, and let's call this variable log.

29
00:02:26,240 --> 00:02:30,190
And append keys to this variable as the user enter them.

30
00:02:30,200 --> 00:02:32,030
But don't send this log.

31
00:02:32,030 --> 00:02:38,240
Every time the user enters a key, we'll send it every one minute, 2 minutes, or even every one hour,

32
00:02:38,240 --> 00:02:40,250
depending on what we want.

33
00:02:40,820 --> 00:02:47,480
Now, this is a good idea, but the problem is creating a variable in here, and the process key price

34
00:02:47,510 --> 00:02:54,590
is not a good idea because as you know, this is a callback function and this function gets executed

35
00:02:54,590 --> 00:02:57,230
every time the user enters a key.

36
00:02:57,290 --> 00:03:03,890
So if you create a new variable in here, this variable will be created every time the user enters a

37
00:03:03,890 --> 00:03:08,630
new key and overwrites whatever that was stored in this previously.

38
00:03:08,630 --> 00:03:11,390
So it won't really work for our purpose.

39
00:03:12,530 --> 00:03:18,590
A better implementation would be to create the variable outside all of the functions.

40
00:03:18,590 --> 00:03:23,600
So we're going to create a variable in here and we're going to call this variable log.

41
00:03:24,850 --> 00:03:28,360
And at the start of the program, the log is going to be empty.

42
00:03:29,310 --> 00:03:36,180
And then inside our callback function, every time the user enters a key, the key variable is going

43
00:03:36,180 --> 00:03:38,320
to contain the value for that key.

44
00:03:38,340 --> 00:03:46,060
So we're going to say the log is equal to whatever that's already in the log, plus the key.

45
00:03:46,080 --> 00:03:48,810
So plus whatever the user has entered.

46
00:03:49,830 --> 00:03:53,640
And then since we're still testing, let's just print the log.

47
00:03:56,500 --> 00:03:59,680
Now there is one more thing that we need to do in our code.

48
00:03:59,710 --> 00:04:05,440
You can see that we defined the log variable, this time in an unusual place.

49
00:04:05,440 --> 00:04:13,420
We defined it outside all of the functions right after the import, and then we're using the log inside

50
00:04:13,420 --> 00:04:16,720
one of our functions inside the callback function.

51
00:04:17,350 --> 00:04:22,600
So we need to tell the callback function that this is a global variable.

52
00:04:22,600 --> 00:04:25,240
So we're going to say global log.

53
00:04:26,080 --> 00:04:31,480
Now, we've never spoken about global variables, so this is a great opportunity for me to talk about

54
00:04:31,480 --> 00:04:34,150
it and to show it to you in practice.

55
00:04:34,810 --> 00:04:41,140
Basically a global variable is a variable like the log that we have in here that we declare outside

56
00:04:41,140 --> 00:04:43,150
all of the functions in our code.

57
00:04:43,150 --> 00:04:49,930
So you can see that it's not indented, it has zero indent, zero spaces before it, but then we use

58
00:04:49,930 --> 00:04:56,500
this variable inside a number of functions in our code, and you'll see now as we build our keylogger,

59
00:04:56,500 --> 00:05:02,800
we'll actually going to be using it, accessing it and modifying it in a number of functions inside

60
00:05:02,800 --> 00:05:03,820
my program.

61
00:05:03,820 --> 00:05:10,450
And every time I access it inside the function, I'm going to have to say global, followed by the variable

62
00:05:10,450 --> 00:05:11,020
name.

63
00:05:12,090 --> 00:05:18,690
Now global variables are useful in some scenarios, as you can see in this scenario, but it's not a

64
00:05:18,690 --> 00:05:21,330
very good idea to use a global variable.

65
00:05:21,360 --> 00:05:29,040
The reason being is when your code gets big and you have one variable and you're modifying this variable

66
00:05:29,040 --> 00:05:34,560
in a number of functions, it gets hard to track what's happening to this variable where it's getting

67
00:05:34,560 --> 00:05:40,500
modified, where it's getting, where it's being read, and if things go wrong, it gets very hard to

68
00:05:40,500 --> 00:05:41,190
track.

69
00:05:41,670 --> 00:05:43,800
For now, we're going to use it like this.

70
00:05:43,800 --> 00:05:49,080
But as we go through the course, I'm actually going to show you a better implementation of all of this

71
00:05:49,080 --> 00:05:51,640
program, but stay tuned for that.

72
00:05:51,660 --> 00:05:57,510
For now, let's use this as a global variable, and we're going to go down to my code.

73
00:05:57,600 --> 00:06:02,700
I'm going to clear the screen and I'm going to run Python Key Logger.

74
00:06:03,770 --> 00:06:06,700
And then let's try to write some text in here.

75
00:06:06,710 --> 00:06:08,540
So let's say this.

76
00:06:08,660 --> 00:06:12,710
Oh, and we got an error right away.

77
00:06:13,130 --> 00:06:18,320
And looking at the error as usual, we said looking at the error usually is a good idea.

78
00:06:18,320 --> 00:06:24,470
And as you can see, it's telling us, first of all, the error is at line 14 and it's telling us that

79
00:06:24,470 --> 00:06:27,950
it cannot contaminate a string with a key code.

80
00:06:28,670 --> 00:06:33,890
So this is something that we've seen before, and it's when we want to combine a string with another

81
00:06:33,890 --> 00:06:38,000
object or when we want to print an object as a string.

82
00:06:38,030 --> 00:06:45,650
So basically the key and here is an object of type key and we can just add it to a string because log,

83
00:06:45,680 --> 00:06:47,360
as you can see, it's a string.

84
00:06:47,630 --> 00:06:52,490
So we have to tell Python that I want you to treat this object as a string.

85
00:06:54,360 --> 00:07:00,390
Let's go back to our code and write some more stuff so you can see its log in the back spaces already

86
00:07:00,390 --> 00:07:03,960
when I'm deleting this and if I just type this.

87
00:07:04,720 --> 00:07:07,210
Is a test.

88
00:07:07,540 --> 00:07:14,500
You can see that it's log in all of this in one variable so you can see that this is the this that I

89
00:07:14,500 --> 00:07:19,930
typed and this is this is we have a space.

90
00:07:19,930 --> 00:07:23,590
We have another space and we have test.

91
00:07:24,630 --> 00:07:31,550
So basically each line in here that you see on screen is stored in my log variable.

92
00:07:31,560 --> 00:07:38,370
So if I just send the log now, if I write the log to a file or send it to an email, I can just send

93
00:07:38,370 --> 00:07:38,940
the log.

94
00:07:38,940 --> 00:07:44,550
I can just send this variable in here and it will send all of the stuff that we wrote so far.

95
00:07:44,550 --> 00:07:52,170
So it will send all of this in one single email, or we can write all of this to the file in one single

96
00:07:52,170 --> 00:07:58,770
open operation instead of having to open the file 20 or 30 times, depending on how many characters

97
00:07:58,770 --> 00:07:59,670
were pressed.

98
00:08:00,570 --> 00:08:06,330
Now, again, if you want to kill this, we're just going to do kill all python and it'll kill it for

99
00:08:06,330 --> 00:08:06,780
me.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:01,550
Okay.

2
00:00:01,750 --> 00:00:08,230
Now that we are able to record the key strikes and store all the key strikes in one variable so we can

3
00:00:08,230 --> 00:00:15,010
report it using one operation, whether we want to send it in as an email or write it to a file, I

4
00:00:15,010 --> 00:00:21,580
want to cover one more thing before I cover the actual implementation of our report function.

5
00:00:22,180 --> 00:00:28,810
Now, as you can see, when we when stuff is being logged, you can see before each character you see

6
00:00:28,810 --> 00:00:29,340
you.

7
00:00:29,350 --> 00:00:35,670
So when I was typing this, you can see that I have the T in here, then I have you, then I have the

8
00:00:35,670 --> 00:00:37,860
H, which is a character that I entered.

9
00:00:37,870 --> 00:00:43,360
Then I have you again and then I have I, then I have you again and so on.

10
00:00:43,870 --> 00:00:50,860
And if I just go up when we were printing stuff once at a line, you can see that for each character

11
00:00:50,860 --> 00:00:53,740
that's printed, there is a you before it.

12
00:00:54,280 --> 00:01:00,070
Now this is due to the way that we are printing the key variable in here.

13
00:01:00,610 --> 00:01:09,610
So what we could do instead of printing the key as is, we can print the key dot C, h, a r, which

14
00:01:09,610 --> 00:01:10,720
is character.

15
00:01:11,320 --> 00:01:16,350
Now again, this is covered in the documentation of this module.

16
00:01:16,360 --> 00:01:17,920
So that's how I knew it.

17
00:01:17,980 --> 00:01:27,340
And now if we go back and run our code, so let me clear it first and run it and let's type some code.

18
00:01:27,340 --> 00:01:29,380
So let's type Z.

19
00:01:31,320 --> 00:01:34,800
As you can see now, it looks much clearer than what it was.

20
00:01:34,800 --> 00:01:41,880
So it's shown me the characters as they are being entered on the keyboard properly without the view

21
00:01:41,880 --> 00:01:44,310
that was shown before each character.

22
00:01:44,700 --> 00:01:46,020
So that's perfect.

23
00:01:46,020 --> 00:01:47,740
But now we have a problem.

24
00:01:47,760 --> 00:01:48,300
Watch.

25
00:01:48,300 --> 00:01:53,100
Now what happens when I press a special key on my keyboard like the space?

26
00:01:54,360 --> 00:01:56,160
As you can see, we get an error.

27
00:01:56,430 --> 00:02:03,030
And the problem is it's telling us that the key object has no attribute as char or character.

28
00:02:03,750 --> 00:02:10,620
Now, basically what Python is saying right now, because I am saying I want to print the key character,

29
00:02:10,740 --> 00:02:14,580
it's telling me that the space does not contain any character.

30
00:02:14,610 --> 00:02:15,410
So what?

31
00:02:15,480 --> 00:02:17,010
I can't print you anything.

32
00:02:17,040 --> 00:02:20,840
Same goes when you press a tab or when you press up or down.

33
00:02:20,850 --> 00:02:23,280
Python is not going to know what to print.

34
00:02:23,640 --> 00:02:27,830
Therefore, we're going to wrap this in a try and accept.

35
00:02:27,840 --> 00:02:35,310
So first of all, I'm going to say I want you to try to set the log to be equal to all of this.

36
00:02:36,500 --> 00:02:38,660
And I want you to accept.

37
00:02:39,890 --> 00:02:42,710
And let's go down and see what kind of error we need to accept.

38
00:02:42,710 --> 00:02:45,470
We need to accept an attribute error.

39
00:02:48,270 --> 00:02:58,130
And once this happens, I want the log to be equal to the log, plus the star of the key.

40
00:02:58,140 --> 00:03:03,630
So this is exactly what we have previously were literally printing the key as is.

41
00:03:04,990 --> 00:03:06,490
Let's go and test this now.

42
00:03:08,320 --> 00:03:10,090
So let's type.

43
00:03:10,270 --> 00:03:12,700
This is a test.

44
00:03:13,060 --> 00:03:15,670
And as you can see, this is working perfectly.

45
00:03:15,670 --> 00:03:19,210
It's not giving me any errors when I press a special key.

46
00:03:19,210 --> 00:03:25,630
So it's typing this this properly and then it's telling me that there is key space pressed, then is

47
00:03:25,630 --> 00:03:28,450
then key space pressed and so on.

48
00:03:29,320 --> 00:03:35,380
Now same happens if I press a tab, if I press up or down and so on.

49
00:03:36,010 --> 00:03:37,300
Now there is one problem.

50
00:03:37,300 --> 00:03:39,850
You can see that there is no spaces between them.

51
00:03:39,850 --> 00:03:40,270
So.

52
00:03:40,270 --> 00:03:46,720
So you can see that every time a special key is being logged, it's being logged directly after the

53
00:03:46,720 --> 00:03:48,220
keys that I was entering.

54
00:03:48,970 --> 00:03:55,810
So it will be nice if this was spaced out so that it's easier to read and this is very easy to implement.

55
00:03:55,810 --> 00:03:57,400
So I'm going to kill my python.

56
00:03:57,400 --> 00:04:01,570
And if we go up and just add a space in here.

57
00:04:03,090 --> 00:04:07,740
And append it to my string and then add the space after it.

58
00:04:10,160 --> 00:04:12,500
Now if we go down and test this.

59
00:04:15,570 --> 00:04:16,920
And let's type some stuff.

60
00:04:16,920 --> 00:04:17,940
So let's type.

61
00:04:18,090 --> 00:04:20,670
This is a test.

62
00:04:20,850 --> 00:04:29,280
Now, this is very nice because you can see that even if I press now a few tabs, spaces, backspace,

63
00:04:29,280 --> 00:04:35,160
you can see that every time there is a special character, it puts a space and then it starts log in

64
00:04:35,160 --> 00:04:36,030
after that.

65
00:04:36,030 --> 00:04:38,160
So it's much more readable.

66
00:04:39,030 --> 00:04:46,170
Another thing that would improve readability is if instead of printing key space, when the space is

67
00:04:46,170 --> 00:04:48,960
logged, let's just print a normal space.

68
00:04:49,710 --> 00:04:52,200
So again, let's kill the Python program.

69
00:04:52,620 --> 00:05:00,180
And in here, because we know whenever the user enters or presses as special key, like the space,

70
00:05:00,180 --> 00:05:02,460
the exception will be raised in here.

71
00:05:02,610 --> 00:05:09,510
And what we're going to say is we're going to say if the key is equal to and I don't know what to make

72
00:05:09,510 --> 00:05:10,360
it equal to.

73
00:05:10,380 --> 00:05:15,660
So I'm going to go down and I'm going to look at what the space is being logged as.

74
00:05:15,660 --> 00:05:18,900
And you can see that the space is being logged as this.

75
00:05:19,320 --> 00:05:24,990
So we're going to go up and say if the key is equal to key, the space.

76
00:05:25,730 --> 00:05:28,040
Then I want my log.

77
00:05:29,260 --> 00:05:34,030
To be equal to log plus a space.

78
00:05:35,830 --> 00:05:37,480
And then we're going to say else.

79
00:05:37,480 --> 00:05:45,580
So if the press key is not a space, then just do the normal, which is just lock the key as is.

80
00:05:46,120 --> 00:05:48,010
So let's go down again.

81
00:05:48,010 --> 00:05:51,460
Let's clear the screen and run the code.

82
00:05:52,450 --> 00:05:54,520
And let's type some stuff in here.

83
00:05:54,520 --> 00:05:55,720
So let's type.

84
00:05:55,900 --> 00:05:58,690
This is a test.

85
00:05:59,230 --> 00:06:06,610
And as you can see now, it's much nicer because instead of seeing a key space for every time a space

86
00:06:06,610 --> 00:06:10,170
is entered, we can see a normal space between our text.

87
00:06:10,180 --> 00:06:16,630
So you can imagine your user type in normal text and you know, normal text contains a lot of spaces.

88
00:06:16,630 --> 00:06:22,270
Then this will be much more readable, especially if your log login chat messages or typing an email.

89
00:06:22,570 --> 00:06:29,170
Then if the user enters any special characters like tabs or enter, you can see that these will still

90
00:06:29,170 --> 00:06:32,530
be recorded and it's still very readable.

91
00:06:32,650 --> 00:06:38,980
Everything is being recorded in one variable, which is my log variable, and therefore now we can report

92
00:06:38,980 --> 00:06:44,530
this using one operation, whether it's a send operation, send email or a write operation to write

93
00:06:44,530 --> 00:06:50,620
on disk, and the result that's going to be logged is going to be readable and easy to analyze.

94
00:06:51,460 --> 00:06:56,380
Now, in the next lecture, we're going to cover the report function and see how we're going to report

95
00:06:56,380 --> 00:06:56,860
this.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:01,500
Okay.

2
00:00:01,510 --> 00:00:07,230
Now, our key logger is 100% ready to start sending us the reports.

3
00:00:07,240 --> 00:00:10,330
It's storing all of the logs in one variable.

4
00:00:10,360 --> 00:00:19,120
The variable contains the letters and the special keys, and it's very readable and easy to analyze.

5
00:00:19,720 --> 00:00:27,370
So in this lecture, I want to program a method that will report whatever is being stored in the log

6
00:00:27,370 --> 00:00:28,120
variable.

7
00:00:28,600 --> 00:00:31,900
Now the method of reporting is irrelevant.

8
00:00:31,900 --> 00:00:35,830
We covered how to send emails and we covered how to write stuff to disk.

9
00:00:35,830 --> 00:00:44,710
What's really important to me is to implement a way of triggering the report function because as you

10
00:00:44,710 --> 00:00:50,440
know, the whole goal of this program is so that we sent it to a user, the user is going to execute

11
00:00:50,440 --> 00:00:56,890
it and this program will keep working for as long as the user is using their computer, recording the

12
00:00:56,890 --> 00:00:59,710
key strikes and sending me reports.

13
00:01:00,340 --> 00:01:08,710
Now, if we implement our reporting function in here within the process key press, this function is

14
00:01:08,710 --> 00:01:12,160
going to be executed every time the user enters a key.

15
00:01:12,160 --> 00:01:18,370
So if my report function sends an email, then an email will be sent every time a user presses a key.

16
00:01:18,370 --> 00:01:25,270
Or if the function writes to a file, then the function is going to open a file, write to it and close

17
00:01:25,270 --> 00:01:27,550
it every time the user enters a key.

18
00:01:27,550 --> 00:01:30,880
And you can imagine that this is very, very excessive.

19
00:01:32,280 --> 00:01:36,180
A good idea of reporting logs is to rely on time.

20
00:01:36,180 --> 00:01:43,140
So every minute, 10 minutes or one hour, send me a report of all the keystrokes that the user entered

21
00:01:43,140 --> 00:01:45,030
within this period of time.

22
00:01:45,660 --> 00:01:51,840
Now, if you look at the code in here, you'll see that there is no good place for us to implement such

23
00:01:51,840 --> 00:01:52,500
method.

24
00:01:52,980 --> 00:01:59,280
Like I said, if you implement it in here, it will be executed every time a key is pressed and then

25
00:01:59,280 --> 00:02:03,000
everything else within the code gets executed once.

26
00:02:03,420 --> 00:02:08,850
Now you can argue the fact that you can create a function and call it report.

27
00:02:10,520 --> 00:02:13,520
And then within this function, use a while.

28
00:02:13,550 --> 00:02:20,450
True, which is something that we've seen before, a loop that will run infinitely and within this loop

29
00:02:20,480 --> 00:02:27,170
execute, send an email, let's say send email and then sleep, which is something that we covered as

30
00:02:27,170 --> 00:02:30,470
well before for for example, 60 seconds.

31
00:02:31,280 --> 00:02:34,580
Then you could call this function from here.

32
00:02:36,810 --> 00:02:40,290
And this function will be called before the listener starts.

33
00:02:40,320 --> 00:02:46,980
It's going to be trapped within this while loop and it will send an email, wait for 60 seconds, go

34
00:02:46,980 --> 00:02:47,520
up again.

35
00:02:47,520 --> 00:02:48,810
Send an email again.

36
00:02:49,560 --> 00:02:56,430
The problem is the while loop in here is going to run before the listener starts.

37
00:02:57,330 --> 00:03:03,000
Therefore, the code will be trapped within this while loop and the listener will never start.

38
00:03:04,060 --> 00:03:11,890
Alternatively, if you put the report in here, the program will be trapped in the listener and the

39
00:03:11,890 --> 00:03:14,950
report function will never get executed.

40
00:03:15,790 --> 00:03:24,100
That's why in order to implement a function that will be executed every certain number of seconds without

41
00:03:24,100 --> 00:03:28,960
interrupting the execution of my program, we're going to have to use threading.

42
00:03:29,620 --> 00:03:32,140
Now, this is something that we never spoke about.

43
00:03:32,140 --> 00:03:37,450
And since you're going to learn it using an example, you should be able to understand it very, very

44
00:03:37,450 --> 00:03:38,110
easily.

45
00:03:38,710 --> 00:03:46,000
So basically what we mean by threading is consider the yellow line in here and this is the main program.

46
00:03:46,390 --> 00:03:48,850
So we're going to execute our main program.

47
00:03:48,880 --> 00:03:50,950
It's going to go all the way down.

48
00:03:50,950 --> 00:03:58,420
And then this timeline or this thread where the main program is running is going to be split in two.

49
00:03:58,690 --> 00:04:05,170
We have the yellow line continue to execute, which is where my keylogger listener is going to work

50
00:04:05,170 --> 00:04:08,080
and store all the keys in my log variable.

51
00:04:08,530 --> 00:04:16,390
And I'll also have another thread or another timeline running simultaneously at the same time as the

52
00:04:16,390 --> 00:04:17,250
main thread.

53
00:04:17,260 --> 00:04:19,300
And this is going to be my timer.

54
00:04:19,750 --> 00:04:23,980
The timer is going to wait for a specific period of time that I'm going to choose.

55
00:04:23,980 --> 00:04:30,520
And then whenever that period of time is reached, it's going to execute a specific code again that

56
00:04:30,520 --> 00:04:34,480
I'm going to choose and then reset the timer and go back again.

57
00:04:35,140 --> 00:04:38,720
This way the same program is going to run on more than one thread.

58
00:04:38,740 --> 00:04:46,270
One of them is running my keylogger and the other one is literally a timer, triggering my log function.

59
00:04:47,800 --> 00:04:51,910
Let me implement this and you'll see how easy it is to understand and use.

60
00:04:52,840 --> 00:04:55,480
So I'm going to put my report in here.

61
00:04:55,480 --> 00:04:59,320
So I'm going to call my report before we start the listener.

62
00:04:59,320 --> 00:05:06,100
But this is fine because the report is going to run on a separate thread like I showed you in the diagram,

63
00:05:06,940 --> 00:05:13,990
and I'm actually going to import a library called Threading and in my report function, I'm going to

64
00:05:13,990 --> 00:05:14,830
remove all of this.

65
00:05:14,830 --> 00:05:16,510
We're not even going to use a loop.

66
00:05:17,410 --> 00:05:22,330
The first thing that I'm going to do is I'm going to say global log.

67
00:05:22,330 --> 00:05:26,360
And I explained why we have to say this when we're using a global variable.

68
00:05:26,380 --> 00:05:33,220
Before then, I'm going to print my log, so I'm going to print whatever I have so far.

69
00:05:33,850 --> 00:05:40,980
So this would actually be the line where you would report so you could write to disk or send it to email.

70
00:05:40,990 --> 00:05:43,420
But for now, we're just going to print it on screen.

71
00:05:44,200 --> 00:05:50,590
Then once you actually send the log, you want to reset the lock variable to an empty string because

72
00:05:50,590 --> 00:05:53,600
you've already logged this to a file or sent it to your email.

73
00:05:53,620 --> 00:05:57,970
So you want to start logging fresh with an empty string.

74
00:05:58,150 --> 00:06:06,250
So we're going to set log equals to an empty string and then we're going to set our timer.

75
00:06:06,250 --> 00:06:11,530
So I'm going to create a variable called timer and this is going to be equal to my threading.

76
00:06:11,530 --> 00:06:18,190
So this is the threading module that we just imported, and we're going to use a class called timer

77
00:06:18,190 --> 00:06:19,540
in this module.

78
00:06:20,350 --> 00:06:23,140
And first of all, we're going to have to set an interval.

79
00:06:23,140 --> 00:06:26,050
So a number of seconds for this timer.

80
00:06:26,050 --> 00:06:31,180
And for in this example, let's set it to 5 seconds, which is very short, but it will be easy for

81
00:06:31,180 --> 00:06:32,380
us when we're testing.

82
00:06:33,040 --> 00:06:40,210
And what we want to do is we're saying basically after the timer starts, wait for 5 seconds and then

83
00:06:40,210 --> 00:06:43,120
call a function that I'm going to give you in here.

84
00:06:43,360 --> 00:06:50,440
And the function that I'm going to give is the report function, which is the same function that we're

85
00:06:50,440 --> 00:06:51,580
in right now.

86
00:06:52,480 --> 00:06:56,440
And finally, we're going to say, I want to start my timer.

87
00:06:56,440 --> 00:07:00,940
So we're doing timer start and we are done.

88
00:07:01,780 --> 00:07:05,500
So let's have a quick look on what this function is doing.

89
00:07:06,140 --> 00:07:13,670
First of all, if you look at the let's minimize this and if you look at the main programming here,

90
00:07:14,000 --> 00:07:17,000
first of all, we're creating our keyboard listener.

91
00:07:17,120 --> 00:07:22,760
Then before we even start the listener, we are calling our report.

92
00:07:22,940 --> 00:07:25,920
The report is going to print the log, which is empty.

93
00:07:25,940 --> 00:07:28,670
Then it's going to set it to nothing, which is fine.

94
00:07:28,820 --> 00:07:34,120
And then we're creating a timer which will run on a separate thread.

95
00:07:34,130 --> 00:07:39,050
So this timer right here, you can think of the program is here.

96
00:07:39,050 --> 00:07:44,390
And once we create the timer, the timer is going to run here on the blue line and then the program

97
00:07:44,390 --> 00:07:47,390
will continue execution on the yellow line.

98
00:07:47,960 --> 00:07:49,760
So we're setting a timer.

99
00:07:49,760 --> 00:07:56,240
And what we're saying basically once we do timer, dot, start, I want you to wait for 5 seconds and

100
00:07:56,240 --> 00:07:57,980
then call the report again.

101
00:07:58,640 --> 00:08:04,250
Now, across these 5 seconds, the user is going to be using their keyboard, typing stuff on the keyboard,

102
00:08:04,430 --> 00:08:07,250
and that stuff is going to be logged in the log.

103
00:08:07,670 --> 00:08:13,280
After 5 seconds, the timer, which is running on a separate thread, will call the report function

104
00:08:13,280 --> 00:08:17,330
again, which is the same function that we're in again.

105
00:08:17,330 --> 00:08:18,630
We're going to print the log.

106
00:08:18,650 --> 00:08:25,730
Set it to nothing and do the same, which is basically going to say, wait for 5 seconds and run the

107
00:08:25,730 --> 00:08:26,810
report again.

108
00:08:27,620 --> 00:08:34,430
And because this is running on a separate thread, it will not block or interrupt the execution of my

109
00:08:34,430 --> 00:08:35,590
main program.

110
00:08:35,600 --> 00:08:42,890
We will have the timer execute in my report function and my listener working at the same time without

111
00:08:42,890 --> 00:08:44,270
blocking each other.

112
00:08:45,020 --> 00:08:50,660
Now you can see that in my function, in the report function, we are calling the report.

113
00:08:50,660 --> 00:08:54,290
So basically we're calling the report function from itself.

114
00:08:54,290 --> 00:08:58,450
And this is an example of a recursive function in programming.

115
00:08:58,460 --> 00:09:03,680
So any function that calls itself is called a recursive function.

116
00:09:04,490 --> 00:09:06,950
Now let's go and see this in practice.

117
00:09:08,030 --> 00:09:09,980
So I'm going to clear my screen.

118
00:09:11,000 --> 00:09:12,500
I'm going to run my program.

119
00:09:13,460 --> 00:09:20,450
And I'm going to start writing stuff in here and you'll see that every 5 seconds the log will print

120
00:09:20,450 --> 00:09:26,810
a new fresh data so it won't be appending the same data like it was doing before.

121
00:09:27,080 --> 00:09:28,220
So let's see.

122
00:09:28,220 --> 00:09:31,100
So I'm going to say this is a test.

123
00:09:32,620 --> 00:09:34,300
Oh, I have a problem.

124
00:09:34,960 --> 00:09:38,740
Because I didn't remove the print statement in here.

125
00:09:38,740 --> 00:09:42,340
That's why we were seeing stuff printed straight away.

126
00:09:43,120 --> 00:09:45,250
So now we're good.

127
00:09:46,040 --> 00:09:53,030
Let's go clear the screen and run it again and let's type stuff.

128
00:09:53,030 --> 00:09:55,370
So let's say this is a test.

129
00:09:57,060 --> 00:10:03,240
You'll see this is a test being printed, but then after that nothing is going to be printed because

130
00:10:03,240 --> 00:10:10,320
this has been logged, either saved to a file or sent to an email, and then the log variable is set

131
00:10:10,320 --> 00:10:11,460
to nothing again.

132
00:10:11,790 --> 00:10:14,490
So now if we go and type stuff again.

133
00:10:14,490 --> 00:10:17,550
So let's say this is another test.

134
00:10:19,320 --> 00:10:26,320
Again, you can see this is another test being logged in the log variable and then reset.

135
00:10:26,340 --> 00:10:33,480
So basically now at this line in here when we're printing, you can implement the log function whether

136
00:10:33,480 --> 00:10:38,550
you want it to send this stuff to an email or to store it on file.

137
00:10:39,300 --> 00:10:43,770
Now keep in mind, I'm using 5 seconds, which is a very short period of time.

138
00:10:43,770 --> 00:10:50,010
So in a real life scenario, you want to set this to at least 5 minutes so that a good bit of data has

139
00:10:50,010 --> 00:10:51,810
been collected and sent.

140
00:10:52,640 --> 00:10:54,050
So that's it for this lecture.

141
00:10:54,050 --> 00:10:55,670
We actually cover two new things.

142
00:10:55,670 --> 00:10:58,880
We cover the recursion and we cover threading.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:07,170
Now throughout the previous lectures we were building a keylogger and we tested it in the previous lecture

2
00:00:07,170 --> 00:00:09,030
and it was working perfectly.

3
00:00:09,120 --> 00:00:15,210
Now all we have to do is just implement a send mail or a method to write the logs to our file in here.

4
00:00:15,210 --> 00:00:19,620
And instead of printing the logs on screen and the code is done.

5
00:00:20,280 --> 00:00:23,350
The problem is, as you can see, the code is very ugly.

6
00:00:23,370 --> 00:00:29,910
We're using a global variable called log and then if we use the send mail in here, we're going to have

7
00:00:29,910 --> 00:00:36,320
to define two new global variables, which is the username and password for the email.

8
00:00:36,330 --> 00:00:42,510
Because as you remember, if I go to my send email code, you can see that when we call the function,

9
00:00:42,510 --> 00:00:50,580
we have to pass a username and password now because of the way the listener works and all of that similar

10
00:00:50,580 --> 00:00:55,920
to the reason why we have to use a log in here, a log variable, we'll have to define the username

11
00:00:55,920 --> 00:00:57,210
and password in here.

12
00:00:58,030 --> 00:01:05,590
Furthermore, if we wanted to set the time interval to a variable instead of keeping it hardcoded in

13
00:01:05,590 --> 00:01:10,120
our code in here, we'll have to add another variable in here on top.

14
00:01:10,390 --> 00:01:14,960
So this is all not really good practices when it comes to programming.

15
00:01:14,980 --> 00:01:22,510
And it also gives me a great opportunity to introduce you to object oriented programming and to Python

16
00:01:22,510 --> 00:01:23,350
classes.

17
00:01:23,800 --> 00:01:29,890
Now, the reasons that I just mentioned are not the only reasons that why you might want to use classes.

18
00:01:29,890 --> 00:01:36,910
But this program is a really good example to show you classes in practice instead of me just talking

19
00:01:36,910 --> 00:01:41,350
about classes and show you a useless program that uses classes.

20
00:01:42,100 --> 00:01:48,220
So before we start writing code, let's talk about classes really, really quick.

21
00:01:49,090 --> 00:01:53,950
So basically classes in Python allow us to write object oriented code.

22
00:01:53,950 --> 00:01:58,420
And this is where the whole idea of object oriented programming comes from.

23
00:01:58,660 --> 00:02:02,530
It's a method of modeling or writing your code.

24
00:02:02,830 --> 00:02:08,980
All the programs that we wrote before, we can write them as classes, as objects.

25
00:02:09,280 --> 00:02:14,590
The reason why I didn't do it, because they were simple programs and even the ones that were a little

26
00:02:14,590 --> 00:02:19,450
bit more complicated, it just made sense to use them simply as functions.

27
00:02:20,260 --> 00:02:25,690
But sometimes in usually when the code gets more complex or in some instances like the instance that

28
00:02:25,690 --> 00:02:26,770
we have right now.

29
00:02:26,800 --> 00:02:31,420
It really makes sense to divide the code or write it as a class.

30
00:02:32,020 --> 00:02:36,400
So it's like a blueprint of how the code is going to be used.

31
00:02:36,970 --> 00:02:41,770
Now you'll see once we put this in practice, our code is going to be more readable.

32
00:02:41,800 --> 00:02:43,660
It's going to be more reusable.

33
00:02:43,660 --> 00:02:47,440
So it's similar to the way functions make our code more usable.

34
00:02:47,470 --> 00:02:50,320
Classes will make it even more usable.

35
00:02:51,220 --> 00:02:54,280
It's going to separate the implementation from the usage.

36
00:02:54,280 --> 00:02:59,680
So we'll be right in the class and then forget about how it works and then just use it from the program

37
00:02:59,680 --> 00:03:01,360
and you'll see how that's going to work.

38
00:03:01,690 --> 00:03:06,490
Now, because of all of these good things, because of the way it works, because it's going to be more

39
00:03:06,490 --> 00:03:08,200
readable and more reusable.

40
00:03:08,470 --> 00:03:10,660
Our code is going to be easier to extend.

41
00:03:10,660 --> 00:03:17,170
So it will be easier to build up upon and it will be easier to maintain and fix issues if there were

42
00:03:17,170 --> 00:03:19,150
any issues discovered later on.

43
00:03:20,050 --> 00:03:25,840
So enough with the token, let's go and put this in practice and I think you'll get a better idea of

44
00:03:25,840 --> 00:03:27,070
what I'm talking about.

45
00:03:27,970 --> 00:03:32,560
Now, I'm actually going to convert this program right here into a class.

46
00:03:33,340 --> 00:03:37,760
The first class that I'm going to write throughout this lecture is actually not going to be great.

47
00:03:37,780 --> 00:03:41,020
It's going to be a bad, really bad class.

48
00:03:41,290 --> 00:03:47,710
But I just want to show you the transition between writing a code like the way we were writing it and

49
00:03:47,710 --> 00:03:49,360
how to just convert it to a class.

50
00:03:49,360 --> 00:03:51,580
You'll see it's actually very, very similar.

51
00:03:52,670 --> 00:03:58,580
So the first thing that you need to do when you're defining a class so you know, in order to define

52
00:03:58,580 --> 00:04:02,450
a function, we say def followed by the function name.

53
00:04:02,930 --> 00:04:09,110
Now if you want to define a class, you're going to say class followed by the class name.

54
00:04:09,920 --> 00:04:17,240
And as a common practice in Python, when we define functions and variables, we always use small letters.

55
00:04:17,240 --> 00:04:22,700
And if we're using more than one word in the variable or function name, we use underscores.

56
00:04:23,240 --> 00:04:29,120
This is different when it comes to classes and it's just a way so that people know when they're programming,

57
00:04:29,120 --> 00:04:32,900
whether they're dealing with a class or with a function or a variable.

58
00:04:33,350 --> 00:04:39,410
So you can actually call the class anything that you want, but if you want to follow the common practice,

59
00:04:39,410 --> 00:04:43,730
then you should call it something that starts with a capital letter.

60
00:04:44,000 --> 00:04:49,490
Now, since we're building a key logger, we're going to call it key logger with a capital K.

61
00:04:50,590 --> 00:04:51,640
Now that's done.

62
00:04:51,670 --> 00:04:59,290
Next thing is we're going to put a call on and then if you hit enter, you'll see that python will automatically

63
00:04:59,290 --> 00:05:00,820
indent my cursor.

64
00:05:00,820 --> 00:05:05,770
And then anything that I'm going to write in here is going to be part of my class.

65
00:05:05,770 --> 00:05:07,360
That is called Keylogger.

66
00:05:08,380 --> 00:05:14,380
So it's similar to the way we define blocks of codes and functions and if statements and so on.

67
00:05:15,040 --> 00:05:19,960
Now everything that I wrote so far, I want it to be part of my class.

68
00:05:19,990 --> 00:05:27,280
So I'm going to highlight all of this and just press tab from my keyboard and that's going to make all

69
00:05:27,280 --> 00:05:30,550
of this part of my keylogger class.

70
00:05:31,300 --> 00:05:38,680
Now you can see that we're using normal functions inside this class, but functions inside classes are

71
00:05:38,680 --> 00:05:42,160
