called methods, so it's exactly the same concept.

72
00:05:42,160 --> 00:05:47,740
But we call this now a method, since it's a function inside a class.

73
00:05:48,460 --> 00:05:54,310
The reason why they're called methods is because we invoke this method on the class.

74
00:05:54,310 --> 00:06:01,690
Once we create an object of this class, and this will become clearer once we actually go ahead and

75
00:06:01,690 --> 00:06:04,840
create and use an object of this class.

76
00:06:06,230 --> 00:06:06,560
Now.

77
00:06:06,560 --> 00:06:11,990
I've actually been using objects a lot throughout throughout our code and something that I just noticed

78
00:06:11,990 --> 00:06:12,710
right here.

79
00:06:12,740 --> 00:06:14,830
We're using an object in here.

80
00:06:14,840 --> 00:06:22,400
So we're basically saying the timer, which is a variable, is of type timer with a capital T.

81
00:06:22,520 --> 00:06:25,250
So you can see that this is a name of a class.

82
00:06:25,250 --> 00:06:31,490
Because remember, like I said, when in Python, when we create classes, we call them a name that

83
00:06:31,490 --> 00:06:33,170
starts with a capital letter.

84
00:06:33,350 --> 00:06:38,570
So basically what we're saying is my timer is a variable of type timer.

85
00:06:38,570 --> 00:06:45,020
So timer here is an object that is defined in a class called timer.

86
00:06:45,710 --> 00:06:48,740
And then we're calling the start method.

87
00:06:48,740 --> 00:06:55,610
So we're saying timer don't start because this is just a method inside the timer class.

88
00:06:55,820 --> 00:06:58,730
So it's similar to the way we have the process key here.

89
00:06:58,730 --> 00:07:06,680
This is a method the report here is another method that can be invoked on our keylogger class or on

90
00:07:06,680 --> 00:07:09,770
the keylogger object once we actually created.

91
00:07:09,770 --> 00:07:12,050
And a similar syntax like this one.

92
00:07:13,010 --> 00:07:19,400
Now because these methods like the report and the process key will be invoked on an object.

93
00:07:19,400 --> 00:07:27,050
Once the object is created, we're going to have to put self as the first argument in this method.

94
00:07:27,110 --> 00:07:34,370
So every method that we define inside the class, the first argument has to be self because this method

95
00:07:34,370 --> 00:07:37,820
will be called on an instance of this object.

96
00:07:37,880 --> 00:07:43,040
And I will revisit all of this once we actually create an object of type keylogger.

97
00:07:43,700 --> 00:07:46,580
So I have to do the same with my report method.

98
00:07:46,580 --> 00:07:54,230
I have to put self as an argument and I also want to put all of the code in here because remember this

99
00:07:54,230 --> 00:08:00,710
code was just put so that it actually uses the above two methods and starts my keylogger.

100
00:08:01,190 --> 00:08:08,060
Now, because this is going to be a class or an object, this should be put inside the method.

101
00:08:08,210 --> 00:08:11,360
So we're going to create a method called Start.

102
00:08:12,050 --> 00:08:18,620
And again, as you can see, once I put once I open a bracket py charm automatically puts self inside

103
00:08:18,620 --> 00:08:19,520
this bracket.

104
00:08:20,770 --> 00:08:27,850
And then I'm just going to tap all of this code so that I can do keylogger start and that will start

105
00:08:27,850 --> 00:08:28,900
my keylogger.

106
00:08:31,030 --> 00:08:39,070
Now another note when using classes, every time you need to use a variable or a method that is defined

107
00:08:39,070 --> 00:08:45,460
within this class, within the class that we're using right now, you have to put self before the method

108
00:08:45,460 --> 00:08:46,060
name.

109
00:08:46,540 --> 00:08:51,520
For example, in here we call the report, which is this function right here.

110
00:08:51,760 --> 00:08:56,200
Now usually we just type report and this will know that this will call that.

111
00:08:56,500 --> 00:09:03,460
But since this is in a class and we're calling a method that is defined within our current class, we're

112
00:09:03,460 --> 00:09:07,150
going to have to say I want to call self dot report.

113
00:09:07,150 --> 00:09:15,700
So I want to call the report method that is defined within this class and I misspelled self in here,

114
00:09:15,700 --> 00:09:23,950
hence the error and I need to do this the same everywhere that I call a method that is defined in my

115
00:09:23,950 --> 00:09:24,610
class.

116
00:09:24,610 --> 00:09:27,430
So I need to add self before this.

117
00:09:29,460 --> 00:09:32,940
And I also need to add it in here before the report.

118
00:09:32,940 --> 00:09:39,090
So we're saying I want to call self doubt report, which is again a method that's defined within this

119
00:09:39,090 --> 00:09:39,840
class.

120
00:09:41,260 --> 00:09:43,420
So now everything is done.

121
00:09:43,450 --> 00:09:49,690
All we did is we created a class by saying class followed by the class name, first letter, capital

122
00:09:49,690 --> 00:09:50,300
letter.

123
00:09:50,320 --> 00:09:55,540
We had to pass self to each method that is used in our class.

124
00:09:55,690 --> 00:10:01,300
Each function now is called the method, not a function because it gets invoked on our class.

125
00:10:01,720 --> 00:10:09,190
And every time we use a method that is defined within our class, we had to type self before the name

126
00:10:09,190 --> 00:10:10,300
of that method.

127
00:10:11,080 --> 00:10:14,680
Now we're done and let's use this class.

128
00:10:14,680 --> 00:10:20,560
Now, we created this class right now and even the start method or the way that we use to start our

129
00:10:20,560 --> 00:10:25,260
program, which was this piece of code in here, we put that inside the method.

130
00:10:25,270 --> 00:10:31,420
So now if we literally just call keylogger dot pi, nothing is going to happen.

131
00:10:31,420 --> 00:10:32,650
So let me show you.

132
00:10:32,650 --> 00:10:40,720
So if we just do python keylogger dot pi, you'll see that nothing happens because we're creating a

133
00:10:40,720 --> 00:10:41,320
class.

134
00:10:41,320 --> 00:10:49,390
We're creating a blueprint of the keylogger class, of something of an object that is called the keylogger.

135
00:10:49,390 --> 00:10:54,130
But we never actually used this blueprint to build the object.

136
00:10:54,580 --> 00:10:59,230
And this is where you see the first really cool thing about using classes.

137
00:10:59,500 --> 00:11:08,080
So in order to use this class, we have to create an object that uses this blueprint or uses this code

138
00:11:08,080 --> 00:11:09,040
to work.

139
00:11:10,010 --> 00:11:13,880
Now you can do that in here, but that's not a good practice.

140
00:11:13,910 --> 00:11:17,870
A better way of doing it is creating another Python file.

141
00:11:19,200 --> 00:11:25,320
And this file is actually going to be the file that uses this object and runs our key logger.

142
00:11:25,320 --> 00:11:29,400
So this is the file that you'll name as your program name.

143
00:11:29,400 --> 00:11:33,900
So for example, I'm going to call this Z logger dot pi.

144
00:11:35,960 --> 00:11:44,660
And then in here, first of all, I'm going to add the shebang and then we're going to say import the

145
00:11:44,660 --> 00:11:48,140
file name that we have in here where we define our class.

146
00:11:48,140 --> 00:11:55,340
So it's called key logger with a small K, and then we can say, my key logger.

147
00:11:57,280 --> 00:12:04,810
Is equal to keylogger, which is the name of our file where our class is defined dot keylogger with

148
00:12:04,810 --> 00:12:05,970
a capital K.

149
00:12:05,980 --> 00:12:14,380
So this is going to create my object based on the class that I defined in here because I'm calling this

150
00:12:14,380 --> 00:12:15,580
class right here.

151
00:12:16,330 --> 00:12:20,200
So what I'm doing in here, I'm basically creating a new object.

152
00:12:20,200 --> 00:12:27,220
I'm storing that in a variable called my keylogger, and this object is going to work using the code

153
00:12:27,220 --> 00:12:31,330
that is defined in my class, which is called keylogger.

154
00:12:32,140 --> 00:12:37,960
So now in order to start my keylogger, all we're going to do is my keylogger.

155
00:12:37,990 --> 00:12:39,190
Don't start.

156
00:12:40,550 --> 00:12:42,030
And this is what I meant.

157
00:12:42,050 --> 00:12:43,880
Why we call these methods.

158
00:12:43,880 --> 00:12:49,490
Because right now, if you remember when we used to call functions, we used to just call the function

159
00:12:49,490 --> 00:12:50,090
like so.

160
00:12:50,090 --> 00:12:53,060
So we just type the function name like this.

161
00:12:53,480 --> 00:13:01,220
But because this is a method defined inside the class and we created an instance of this class first

162
00:13:01,220 --> 00:13:03,110
and then we're calling start.

163
00:13:03,110 --> 00:13:09,740
So basically we're saying that we want to invoke the START method on my object that I just created,

164
00:13:10,160 --> 00:13:17,390
which will derive its code or its code is going to be based on the blueprint that is designed or written

165
00:13:17,390 --> 00:13:18,260
in here.

166
00:13:19,200 --> 00:13:25,860
So if you look at the code in here, you can see that whenever someone needs to use the keylogger class

167
00:13:25,860 --> 00:13:31,080
in the future, they don't need to know the code used inside this class.

168
00:13:31,080 --> 00:13:34,350
They don't need to know how the programmer wrote this class.

169
00:13:34,350 --> 00:13:40,020
They can just import keylogger and start the keylogger by saying, My keylogger don't start.

170
00:13:40,050 --> 00:13:41,800
That's all they need to do.

171
00:13:41,820 --> 00:13:44,100
The programmer might come in the future.

172
00:13:44,100 --> 00:13:48,810
Update the code in here, change the code, modify it, add more features.

173
00:13:48,810 --> 00:13:51,720
Whoever is using this and here doesn't really matter.

174
00:13:51,720 --> 00:13:56,820
They don't care about the implementation of the code, they are just using it in here and then they

175
00:13:56,820 --> 00:13:59,850
can focus on the code that they are writing.

176
00:14:00,840 --> 00:14:07,410
So this is one of many advantages of using object oriented programming or using classes.

177
00:14:08,560 --> 00:14:09,220
Again.

178
00:14:09,220 --> 00:14:16,060
The code in here is still not very pretty and still has a number of bad practices that will fix in the

179
00:14:16,060 --> 00:14:16,720
future.

180
00:14:16,720 --> 00:14:20,860
But for now, let's just test this and make sure that it still works.

181
00:14:21,160 --> 00:14:28,270
So if I just go Python Z Logger Dot Pi and start writing some stuff in here.

182
00:14:28,270 --> 00:14:35,020
So let's say this is a test and you can see that it recorded this is a and test should come up in the

183
00:14:35,020 --> 00:14:37,000
next iteration as you can see.

184
00:14:37,000 --> 00:14:40,960
Let's write something else again.

185
00:14:40,960 --> 00:14:46,270
Something else comes up and then everything is going to be cleared on the next one and perfect.

186
00:14:46,270 --> 00:14:51,550
So it's working perfectly exactly the same way that our previous code was working.

187
00:14:51,940 --> 00:14:57,340
And in the next lecture, I'll show you how we can actually improve this code, because like I said,

188
00:14:57,340 --> 00:15:03,010
there is still a lot of bad practices in here and there's still a few things that I want to cover when

189
00:15:03,010 --> 00:15:06,370
it comes to classes and object oriented programming.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,640 --> 00:00:01,150
Okay.

2
00:00:01,150 --> 00:00:08,530
Now that we know what objects are, how to write classes and use them, I want to talk about constructor

3
00:00:08,530 --> 00:00:10,120
methods in this lecture.

4
00:00:10,660 --> 00:00:13,990
This is something that will be very useful in our code.

5
00:00:14,080 --> 00:00:21,850
And basically a constructor method is a normal method that gets executed automatically every time we

6
00:00:21,850 --> 00:00:23,110
create an object.

7
00:00:23,560 --> 00:00:31,420
So for example, in here you can see that on this line we're creating a new object and we're storing

8
00:00:31,420 --> 00:00:34,570
its reference in a variable called my key logger.

9
00:00:35,140 --> 00:00:44,620
Now, if we are using a constructor method in our code in our class, then this method will be executed

10
00:00:44,620 --> 00:00:46,870
when we create the object.

11
00:00:47,650 --> 00:00:56,320
As you remember, if I comment this line and go down and run my program, you'll see that nothing happens

12
00:00:56,320 --> 00:00:57,260
at all.

13
00:00:57,280 --> 00:01:05,530
But if we implemented a constructor method, then the code in that constructor method will be executed

14
00:01:05,530 --> 00:01:08,110
automatically without us calling it.

15
00:01:08,140 --> 00:01:12,400
So you can see we had to call the start in order to call the start method.

16
00:01:12,880 --> 00:01:19,600
But if we have a constructor, the constructor will be called automatically when we create the object

17
00:01:19,600 --> 00:01:21,370
that's based on this class.

18
00:01:22,330 --> 00:01:28,780
Now, in order to create a constructor method, we have to define it using the def keyword just like

19
00:01:28,780 --> 00:01:29,950
any other method.

20
00:01:30,100 --> 00:01:34,240
And then we're going to type the name of the constructor method.

21
00:01:34,330 --> 00:01:41,620
And all constructor methods should be called the same name, which is underscore, underscore, init,

22
00:01:41,620 --> 00:01:43,270
underscore, underscore.

23
00:01:43,270 --> 00:01:48,040
And you can see that py charm is automatically suggesting that for me.

24
00:01:48,040 --> 00:01:55,840
So if I hit enter it will autofill for me and then any code that I'm going to put inside this method

25
00:01:55,840 --> 00:02:00,460
will be executed automatically when we create the object.

26
00:02:00,880 --> 00:02:07,690
So just as a quick example, if I just print, we are in constructor method.

27
00:02:08,620 --> 00:02:14,890
Now, if I save this and keep in mind the code in here is still the same, all we're doing is literally

28
00:02:14,890 --> 00:02:16,660
just creating the object.

29
00:02:16,900 --> 00:02:25,570
But if I go down and run my program, you'll see that our print statement is getting executed automatically,

30
00:02:25,990 --> 00:02:33,820
which means that whatever code that I'm going to put in here inside the init will be executed automatically

31
00:02:33,820 --> 00:02:36,520
whenever the object is created.

32
00:02:37,850 --> 00:02:44,660
So this is a great place for us to define variables that we're going to use throughout the program.

33
00:02:44,660 --> 00:02:48,650
So something similar to the log variable that we're using in here.

34
00:02:49,430 --> 00:02:57,710
So I'm going to cut this variable and I'm going to define it in here inside my constructor, and I'm

35
00:02:57,710 --> 00:03:03,290
going to say self dot log is equal to nothing.

36
00:03:03,830 --> 00:03:10,010
So what I'm doing right here is you can think of this something similar to a variable, but it's actually

37
00:03:10,010 --> 00:03:16,700
going to be called an attribute because it's a variable that we can use within this whole class and

38
00:03:16,700 --> 00:03:19,640
it's actually going to be used in multiple methods.

39
00:03:20,180 --> 00:03:28,130
Note The self in here is used so that we're saying that we're actually using a variable that is defined

40
00:03:28,130 --> 00:03:32,450
and going to be used within the current key logger class.

41
00:03:33,230 --> 00:03:39,200
Now, because this variable is an attribute of this class, we're actually not going to need to use

42
00:03:39,200 --> 00:03:43,880
the global keyword every time we need to use this variable.

43
00:03:43,880 --> 00:03:46,190
So I'm going to remove that from here.

44
00:03:46,190 --> 00:03:48,260
I'm going to remove that from here.

45
00:03:49,160 --> 00:03:56,180
And what I'm actually going to do is you can see that every time we're using the log, we're only appending

46
00:03:56,180 --> 00:03:58,610
or adding stuff to this variable.

47
00:03:59,390 --> 00:04:02,870
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to create a new method.

48
00:04:03,900 --> 00:04:08,940
And let's call this append to log.

49
00:04:10,140 --> 00:04:14,520
And again, you can see that pie chart automatically fills the self for me.

50
00:04:14,520 --> 00:04:21,810
And the next thing that I want to take as an argument is the string that we want to append and what

51
00:04:21,810 --> 00:04:23,160
this method is going to do.

52
00:04:23,190 --> 00:04:25,320
It's going to do self dot log.

53
00:04:25,320 --> 00:04:32,880
So again, I'm accessing the attribute, the self log variable that's used within this whole class and

54
00:04:32,880 --> 00:04:40,140
I'm going to make this equal to self dot log plus the string that has been passed.

55
00:04:40,920 --> 00:04:47,220
The reason why I created this method is because most of the time when we're using the log, all we're

56
00:04:47,220 --> 00:04:50,460
doing is we're appending stuff to this variable.

57
00:04:51,240 --> 00:04:56,070
So in here our try instead of doing log is equal to log.

58
00:04:56,070 --> 00:05:03,000
Plus this character, all we're going to say is self dot append to log.

59
00:05:03,000 --> 00:05:09,780
And what we're going to pass is the string of the key dot character.

60
00:05:10,860 --> 00:05:13,710
So it's exactly what we're doing in here.

61
00:05:13,710 --> 00:05:15,480
But the nicely.

62
00:05:17,410 --> 00:05:22,030
And then what I'm going to do in here, I'm actually going to use a local variable.

63
00:05:22,030 --> 00:05:25,210
So it's a variable that's only used within this method.

64
00:05:25,330 --> 00:05:28,810
And I'm going to call this current key.

65
00:05:30,090 --> 00:05:34,340
And we're going to make that equal to the key to the character that has been pressed.

66
00:05:34,380 --> 00:05:38,210
And then we're going to do this here when there is a special character.

67
00:05:38,220 --> 00:05:42,140
Again, we're going to say the current key is equal to space.

68
00:05:42,150 --> 00:05:48,270
If that key is a space and then if it's a special key, then the current key is going to be a space

69
00:05:48,270 --> 00:05:49,890
plus the special key.

70
00:05:50,760 --> 00:05:57,170
And then at the end of this method we're going to say self dot append to log.

71
00:05:57,180 --> 00:06:01,410
And what we want to append is the current key.

72
00:06:02,920 --> 00:06:08,500
And finally in my reporting here, I'm resetting the value of the log.

73
00:06:08,590 --> 00:06:16,090
So I can't just say the log is equal to nothing because that's basically what defining a new variable

74
00:06:16,090 --> 00:06:19,870
called log, and we're setting it to equal to an empty string.

75
00:06:20,140 --> 00:06:30,300
If I want to reset the attribute log that we defined in here, I have to say self log is equal to nothing

76
00:06:30,310 --> 00:06:34,270
and when I'm printing it, I have to print the self log.

77
00:06:36,250 --> 00:06:36,610
Now.

78
00:06:36,610 --> 00:06:37,420
This is it.

79
00:06:37,600 --> 00:06:41,320
As you can see, we're not using any global variables anymore.

80
00:06:41,530 --> 00:06:47,650
And instead we're using a constructor that gets executed every time we create a new object.

81
00:06:47,740 --> 00:06:54,520
Inside this constructor, we're creating an attribute called Log, which is a variable that will be

82
00:06:54,520 --> 00:06:56,590
used throughout our program.

83
00:06:56,590 --> 00:07:00,940
And every time we need to use it, we're going to refer to it as self log.

84
00:07:01,690 --> 00:07:07,720
Other than that, our code pretty much stayed the same other than some refactoring in here that just

85
00:07:07,720 --> 00:07:11,290
makes the code prettier and easier to understand.

86
00:07:12,120 --> 00:07:12,480
Now.

87
00:07:12,480 --> 00:07:13,980
Let's go and run a quick test.

88
00:07:15,480 --> 00:07:22,050
Oh, and I've commented, I'm actually going to remove this print statement and I've commented the line

89
00:07:22,050 --> 00:07:26,250
that starts my code or that starts my keylogger.

90
00:07:26,460 --> 00:07:28,780
So we're going to run it like this.

91
00:07:28,800 --> 00:07:30,960
As you can see, there is nothing right now.

92
00:07:30,960 --> 00:07:34,440
And if we type, this is a test.

93
00:07:35,620 --> 00:07:36,790
That is being printed.

94
00:07:36,790 --> 00:07:37,980
So everything is fine.

95
00:07:37,990 --> 00:07:41,350
The next print should be empty and perfect.

96
00:07:41,350 --> 00:07:44,380
And now let's do another test.

97
00:07:46,110 --> 00:07:47,280
That's perfect.

98
00:07:47,280 --> 00:07:53,460
Everything is working as expected, and now our code is much better than it used to be.

99
00:07:54,510 --> 00:08:00,570
Now in the next lecture, we're going to polish this code a little bit more and we'll add the Sandman

100
00:08:00,570 --> 00:08:06,000
function to it so that it's a complete and usable keylogger.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,710 --> 00:00:05,270
Right now that we have a nice code for keylogger that works perfectly.

2
00:00:05,450 --> 00:00:12,440
Let's extend this code and polish it more to have a good program that we actually can use in a real

3
00:00:12,440 --> 00:00:13,430
life scenario.

4
00:00:14,360 --> 00:00:22,100
So the first bad thing that we have in our code is this number right here, which is the interval that

5
00:00:22,100 --> 00:00:24,680
is triggering our report function.

6
00:00:24,860 --> 00:00:29,810
So if we set this to a minute, the report function will be executed every minute.

7
00:00:29,840 --> 00:00:34,680
If we set this to an hour, the report function will be executed for every hour.

8
00:00:34,700 --> 00:00:41,870
So it will be nice if we actually use a variable in here instead of hardcoded in it like this in the

9
00:00:41,870 --> 00:00:42,470
code.

10
00:00:42,980 --> 00:00:49,760
Now using the way that we initially wrote the program, we would have had to use a constant on a or

11
00:00:49,760 --> 00:00:51,470
a variable here at the top.

12
00:00:51,950 --> 00:00:58,700
In our case, since we have a better implementation right now, we can actually use an attribute in

13
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:00,770
the constructor in here.

14
00:01:01,430 --> 00:01:06,470
Now, like I said, you can think of a constructor just like any other method.

15
00:01:06,500 --> 00:01:12,410
The only difference is it gets executed automatically when the object is created.

16
00:01:12,950 --> 00:01:20,420
So this, just like any other method, can take an input, an argument, and we're going to make it

17
00:01:20,420 --> 00:01:22,820
take the interval.

18
00:01:25,440 --> 00:01:33,210
And we're going to say self doubt interval is equal to the time interval.

19
00:01:33,810 --> 00:01:40,260
So basically what we're saying is now when someone wants to create my object, when they want to use

20
00:01:40,260 --> 00:01:47,130
my keylogger class, they have to pass a variable or they have to pass a value in here.

21
00:01:47,370 --> 00:01:54,930
So for example, let's set this value to four this time and this value, the value that we're passing

22
00:01:54,930 --> 00:02:03,330
in here is going to be stored in an attribute called interval, and then I'll be able to access this

23
00:02:03,330 --> 00:02:06,090
attribute throughout my class.

24
00:02:06,750 --> 00:02:14,550
So this is a very common way of allowing the attributes to be set when the object is created.

25
00:02:15,470 --> 00:02:22,010
So now when we want to use this value, all we have to do is just type self dot interval.

26
00:02:22,340 --> 00:02:30,440
And now my report function or my report method will be triggered depending on the time that is passed

27
00:02:30,440 --> 00:02:33,500
to us when the object is created.

28
00:02:34,100 --> 00:02:36,710
So in here we're passing number four.

29
00:02:36,740 --> 00:02:38,980
This will create a new object.

30
00:02:38,990 --> 00:02:41,360
It's going to get the time interval in here.

31
00:02:41,360 --> 00:02:49,040
As for this will set self dot interval as four and then we can access self dot interval throughout the

32
00:02:49,040 --> 00:02:55,610
program and its value is always going to be four unless we modify it in our code.

33
00:02:56,940 --> 00:03:02,850
Now, the next thing that I want to add in this lecture is instead of the printing here, like I said,

34
00:03:02,850 --> 00:03:09,000
you can either write the logs to a file or send them to an email or to a server.

35
00:03:09,690 --> 00:03:15,960
Now I think sending it to an email is more practical because that will allow us to use the keylogger

36
00:03:15,960 --> 00:03:19,320
as a remote keylogger and send it to our target.

37
00:03:19,320 --> 00:03:26,790
And then the keylogger will send us reports of every keystroke entered by our target to our email for

38
00:03:26,790 --> 00:03:30,330
every interval that we set when we create the object.

39
00:03:30,840 --> 00:03:37,650
So instead of just printing in here, I'm actually going to get the send mail function that we programmed

40
00:03:37,650 --> 00:03:38,430
before.

41
00:03:38,940 --> 00:03:44,600
I'm going to copy it and paste it in my keylogger as a method.

42
00:03:44,610 --> 00:03:49,870
So I'm just going to minimize these two and minimize this as well.

43
00:03:49,890 --> 00:03:52,380
And I'm just going to paste it in here.

44
00:03:54,010 --> 00:03:59,860
And obviously, like we said, because this is now a method, not a function, the first argument has

45
00:03:59,860 --> 00:04:00,940
to be self.

46
00:04:01,690 --> 00:04:05,830
Now I'm going to minimize this as well because we don't care about how this works.

47
00:04:05,830 --> 00:04:08,740
We wrote it, we know it works and we forget about it.

48
00:04:09,190 --> 00:04:13,690
All we have to do is call it in here instead of printing the logs on screen.

49
00:04:13,690 --> 00:04:17,890
So we're going to call self dot send mail.

50
00:04:18,790 --> 00:04:25,390
And as you know, this method takes an email, a password and the message to send.

51
00:04:25,810 --> 00:04:27,760
Now, the message to send is easy.

52
00:04:27,760 --> 00:04:29,440
That's going to be the self dot log.

53
00:04:29,440 --> 00:04:35,320
So it's exactly what we're printing in here, but we don't know the email and the password.

54
00:04:35,320 --> 00:04:41,230
And again, if we are writing this program, using the initial program that we wrote, the one that

55
00:04:41,230 --> 00:04:47,680
did not use a class, we would have had to create variables in here, call them username and password,

56
00:04:47,680 --> 00:04:52,180
and then use them in our method, which is not a good way of doing it.

57
00:04:52,300 --> 00:04:58,600
A better way is what we're going to do right now, which we're going to take more arguments in our initial

58
00:04:58,670 --> 00:05:00,760
user or in our constructor.

59
00:05:00,760 --> 00:05:06,880
And the first one is going to be the email and the second one is going to be the password.

60
00:05:07,300 --> 00:05:16,390
And then we're going to store these values into attributes by saying self dot email is equal to the

61
00:05:16,390 --> 00:05:24,070
email that we're going to get as an input and we're going to say self, the password is going to be

62
00:05:24,070 --> 00:05:27,670
equal to the password that we get as an input.

63
00:05:28,210 --> 00:05:33,040
So again, it's something similar to what we're doing with the interval.

64
00:05:33,760 --> 00:05:43,600
And then when I'm calling my send email, I'm going to give it the self dot email and the self dot password

65
00:05:43,600 --> 00:05:45,430
as the username and password.

66
00:05:45,430 --> 00:05:51,130
And finally, I'm going to give it the message to send, which is the self log.

67
00:05:53,010 --> 00:05:57,750
I'm going to remove this print statement and we are good to go.

68
00:05:58,430 --> 00:06:02,670
The only thing is we need to modify the way we're creating the object.

69
00:06:02,690 --> 00:06:07,720
Now, first of all, we have to give it the interval and I'm setting it now to four.

70
00:06:07,730 --> 00:06:13,100
So this is going to be for every 4 seconds we're going to get an email with the logs that the person

71
00:06:13,100 --> 00:06:13,740
entered.

72
00:06:13,760 --> 00:06:17,960
This is not good because it's a very short period of time.

73
00:06:17,960 --> 00:06:23,600
There will not be a lot of text written using the keyboard and we're going to get too many emails on

74
00:06:23,600 --> 00:06:24,470
our email.

75
00:06:24,860 --> 00:06:33,560
Instead, I'm going to set this to 120 seconds, which means every 2 minutes I want you to send me reports

76
00:06:33,560 --> 00:06:36,500
to my email of what the user has entered.

77
00:06:37,100 --> 00:06:42,800
Now, in a real life scenario, you want to set this to an even larger number, but 2 minutes is good

78
00:06:42,800 --> 00:06:43,550
for now.

79
00:06:44,210 --> 00:06:51,140
We also need to pass the username and the password because as you can see here now, the initial user

80
00:06:51,140 --> 00:06:54,920
takes the time and table takes an email and takes the password.

81
00:06:55,650 --> 00:07:01,710
So I need to give it the username and password and because I'm lazy I'm just going to copy them from

82
00:07:01,710 --> 00:07:05,450
here and I'm going to paste them.

83
00:07:05,460 --> 00:07:08,820
And now our program is ready to be used.

84
00:07:09,750 --> 00:07:13,590
Now I'm going to go down and I'm going to run my program.

85
00:07:14,830 --> 00:07:21,790
Oh, and we're getting an error because I forgot to import the SMTP lib, which is a library that is

86
00:07:21,790 --> 00:07:24,970
used in our central method in here.

87
00:07:25,060 --> 00:07:30,280
So I'm just going to do import SMTP lib.

88
00:07:31,090 --> 00:07:36,260
And also I want to modify one thing before sending the log.

89
00:07:36,280 --> 00:07:40,660
I'm actually going to append to slash ends.

90
00:07:40,870 --> 00:07:44,650
So two new line characters before we send it.

91
00:07:44,650 --> 00:07:52,000
And the reason for this is sometimes when the log is sent as is, and if it doesn't contain any new

92
00:07:52,000 --> 00:07:56,980
line characters before it, it'll all be included in the subject of the message.

93
00:07:56,980 --> 00:08:00,700
So it will still work, but the text is going to be sent in the subject.

94
00:08:00,730 --> 00:08:07,300
When I do this, we're going to skip the headers and it will be placed inside the content of the message.

95
00:08:08,390 --> 00:08:15,080
Another thing is when creating the log, the first thing that I'm going to set this to, I'm going to

96
00:08:15,080 --> 00:08:18,920
set it to Key Logger started.

97
00:08:19,460 --> 00:08:25,250
So the first time the report method is sent, it's actually going to be something like a notification

98
00:08:25,250 --> 00:08:28,490
telling us that the key logger has started on the system.

99
00:08:29,300 --> 00:08:34,670
So I'm going to save this and run it again and this is working.

100
00:08:34,670 --> 00:08:35,870
So that's perfect.

101
00:08:35,870 --> 00:08:42,260
And if we go down here we go, you can see that we got an email straight away telling us that the key

102
00:08:42,260 --> 00:08:43,520
logger has started.

103
00:08:43,520 --> 00:08:49,310
So we know we're on track and we should be receiving logs from our user every 2 minutes.

104
00:08:49,490 --> 00:08:54,200
So let's, for example, go to a website like Facebook.com.

105
00:08:55,670 --> 00:08:57,580
I spelled that really, really badly.

106
00:09:00,390 --> 00:09:08,370
Then I'm going to put a username and let's call it for example Z at Facebook dot com and put a password

107
00:09:08,370 --> 00:09:15,690
which is one, two, three, eight, one, two, three and notice that this website is being served

108
00:09:15,690 --> 00:09:17,400
over https.

109
00:09:17,400 --> 00:09:24,450
So it's being served over the latest security measurements or standards that's known for now.

110
00:09:24,930 --> 00:09:30,990
So this should have been reported and everything that I'm going to type in my keyboard is going to be

111
00:09:30,990 --> 00:09:38,430
reported regardless of what type of security is used on this application, because we're literally recording

112
00:09:38,430 --> 00:09:40,950
everything that's entered on the keyboard.

113
00:09:40,980 --> 00:09:47,310
We're not really hacking any security that's implemented on the server side or using an application

114
00:09:47,310 --> 00:09:49,620
that's being used by the user.

115
00:09:50,430 --> 00:09:55,860
Now I'm going to pause this video so that you don't have to wait for the full 2 minutes until I get

116
00:09:55,860 --> 00:10:00,960
the report and I'll resume once we actually get the report on my email.

117
00:10:02,210 --> 00:10:03,370
And here you go.

118
00:10:03,380 --> 00:10:04,730
We got an email.

119
00:10:04,760 --> 00:10:09,980
And if we click on this email, you can see all the key strikes that were entered.

120
00:10:10,010 --> 00:10:14,660
This is when I was trying to type Facebook and I typed it really, really badly.

121
00:10:14,690 --> 00:10:22,460
Then this is when I typed it properly, I hit enter, I put the username Z and then I type shift to

122
00:10:22,490 --> 00:10:24,080
to type the add character.

123
00:10:24,080 --> 00:10:31,370
And then it's at Facebook.com and I press tab to go to the password field and I put my password which

124
00:10:31,370 --> 00:10:33,950
is one, two, three, Z one, two, three.

125
00:10:34,910 --> 00:10:38,750
So as you can see, the keylogger is working perfectly.

126
00:10:38,750 --> 00:10:44,900
It sending in the reports on my email and the code that's used to do that is actually a really good

127
00:10:44,900 --> 00:10:48,560
code that uses object oriented programming.

128
00:10:48,590 --> 00:10:55,550
We can set the interval, the username and the password using variables and we don't really need to

129
00:10:55,550 --> 00:10:57,760
worry about how the keylogger works.

130
00:10:57,770 --> 00:11:01,880
All we have to do is literally import the keylogger and start it.

131
00:11:02,390 --> 00:11:04,880
So this is a very modular way of doing it.

132
00:11:04,880 --> 00:11:08,660
It even allows us to use it with other programs.

133
00:11:10,600 --> 00:11:13,140
Now I know what you're thinking right now.

134
00:11:13,150 --> 00:11:19,170
Our program consists of two files, the keylogger and the actual keylogger class.

135
00:11:19,180 --> 00:11:25,360
So how are you going to target a person and send them to files and convince them to run them?

136
00:11:25,570 --> 00:11:33,730
Not only that, but also these files are dot pie, so they'll only work if the system has a python interpreter,

137
00:11:33,730 --> 00:11:38,560
which we know windows does not come with one and it has to be manually installed.

138
00:11:39,400 --> 00:11:45,100
Now my answer to this is the same as what I've been saying for now, which is, don't worry about this.

139
00:11:45,100 --> 00:11:50,830
We'll talk about this when we get to packaging and you'll see how we can combine this into one executable

140
00:11:50,830 --> 00:11:57,610
and make that look like any other executable or like an image or a PDF or any other file type.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================


# Section 14: Writing Malware - Backdoors

1
00:00:01,220 --> 00:00:01,630
Okay.

2
00:00:01,700 --> 00:00:07,010
Now, in the next lecture, I want to start building our own backdoor.

3
00:00:07,880 --> 00:00:14,930
A backdoor is a program that, when executed on a system, would allow us to gain full control over

4
00:00:14,930 --> 00:00:15,830
that system.

5
00:00:16,790 --> 00:00:21,980
We're going to implement this using sockets and you're going to learn how to establish a connection

6
00:00:21,980 --> 00:00:26,390
between two devices and transfer data between them.

7
00:00:27,140 --> 00:00:32,570
This is very useful because it can be used in so many other scenarios other than hacking.

8
00:00:32,570 --> 00:00:37,430
So you can use it to create a web server, a chat program and so on.

9
00:00:38,400 --> 00:00:44,430
Then we're going to build more cool features in this back door so that we can use it to execute system

10
00:00:44,430 --> 00:00:51,750
commands remotely on the target computer, download and upload files and maintain our access even if

11
00:00:51,750 --> 00:00:53,130
the system restarts.

12
00:00:53,610 --> 00:00:57,960
Now I'm going to cover how to do all of this in details in the next lectures.

13
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:04,680
But before we start programming as usual, I'm going to give you a quick teaser on how this backdoor

14
00:01:04,680 --> 00:01:07,200
will work by the end of this section.

15
00:01:07,290 --> 00:01:10,170
So don't worry about how this is working.

16
00:01:10,170 --> 00:01:12,150
I will cover that in details.

17
00:01:12,150 --> 00:01:18,660
But for now, I just want to show you how this backdoor will work so that you have a general idea of

18
00:01:18,660 --> 00:01:21,900
what I'm trying to achieve by the end of the section.

19
00:01:23,290 --> 00:01:23,700
Okay.

20
00:01:23,740 --> 00:01:27,580
So right now I'm in my attacking machine, the carry machine.

21
00:01:27,940 --> 00:01:31,980
And as you can see, it's already waiting for incoming connections.

22
00:01:31,990 --> 00:01:34,660
This is using my Listnr program again.

23
00:01:34,660 --> 00:01:41,890
I'll show you how to do that, how to program that later on and on my windows machine, I have the backdoor

24
00:01:41,920 --> 00:01:44,500
already converted to an x e again.

25
00:01:44,500 --> 00:01:46,000
We'll cover that later on.

26
00:01:46,000 --> 00:01:49,330
And again, it looks suspicious, but don't worry about that.

27
00:01:49,330 --> 00:01:55,360
We're going to focus on getting a backdoor that works and we'll talk about making this look less suspicious

28
00:01:55,360 --> 00:01:59,410
and how to make it look like any other file later on in the course.

29
00:02:00,160 --> 00:02:03,670
So right now, I'm just going to execute this by double clicking it.

30
00:02:04,090 --> 00:02:10,270
This will execute the evil code, which will establish the connection between this computer and the

31
00:02:10,270 --> 00:02:11,440
hacker computer.

32
00:02:11,440 --> 00:02:15,400
And as you can see, it's telling me right now that I get a connection.

33
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:20,480
So now I can use all the features that the backdoor allow me to do.

34
00:02:20,480 --> 00:02:26,570
And I'm just going to show you a quick overview, just a teaser on what we'll be able to do with this

35
00:02:26,570 --> 00:02:27,290
backdoor.

36
00:02:27,800 --> 00:02:30,620
So first of all, I have file system access.

37
00:02:30,620 --> 00:02:35,810
So if I do seed, it will show me my current working directory.

38
00:02:35,810 --> 00:02:39,620
And as you can see, I'm in Users Z downloads.

39
00:02:40,070 --> 00:02:44,420
That's basically because the backdoor is actually stored in this location.

40
00:02:45,410 --> 00:02:53,750
So I can do c d dot dot to go back one directory and if I do CD now to see where am I?

41
00:02:53,780 --> 00:02:59,930
You can see I'm in Z and we can also execute all system commands.

42
00:03:00,050 --> 00:03:04,310
And keep in mind this factor works on all operating systems.

43
00:03:04,310 --> 00:03:10,880
It works on Linux, Windows and OS X, so you can use the system commands of your target and they will

44
00:03:10,880 --> 00:03:12,140
work by default.

45
00:03:12,530 --> 00:03:14,570
So right now my target is Windows.

46
00:03:14,570 --> 00:03:18,170
So if I wanted to list the directories I can do there.

47
00:03:18,530 --> 00:03:24,590
And as you can see, I get a list of all the files and directories in the current working directory.

48
00:03:25,370 --> 00:03:29,840
Now I can go back in downloads by doing see the downloads.

49
00:03:31,040 --> 00:03:37,100
And if I list again in here, you'll see the files that we have in the downloads, which are the back

50
00:03:37,100 --> 00:03:41,330
door and the GT-R image which I have right here.

51
00:03:43,620 --> 00:03:49,200
Now we also want to highlight a feature that we're going to program, which is the ability to download

52
00:03:49,200 --> 00:03:50,010
files.

53
00:03:50,940 --> 00:03:57,450
So I'm just going to do a download and I'm going to follow it by the file name that I want to download

54
00:03:57,450 --> 00:04:01,680
and it's the image, so it's GDT or the JPG.

55
00:04:02,860 --> 00:04:07,000
And as you can see, I'll see it automatically being downloaded in here.

56
00:04:07,000 --> 00:04:12,580
And if we double click this, we have the image intact and we're able to open it.

57
00:04:13,720 --> 00:04:19,090
Now I'm downloading the image just as an example, but it just goes to show you that you'll be able

58
00:04:19,090 --> 00:04:22,390
to download any file from the target system.

59
00:04:23,450 --> 00:04:29,210
You can also upload files, which is really, really useful because you'll be able to upload evil files,

60
00:04:29,210 --> 00:04:31,670
viruses, keyloggers and so on.

61
00:04:31,880 --> 00:04:40,760
And as an example, I'm just going to rename this image to GTR two dot JPG and I'm going to upload it.

62
00:04:42,900 --> 00:04:48,390
So I'm going to do upload GTX or two dot jpg.

63
00:04:50,100 --> 00:04:57,000
Now it's tell me the upload is successful and if we go here again, you can see we have the new image.

64
00:04:57,880 --> 00:05:03,880
Now, if this was an evil file because you're able to execute system commands from the back door, you'll

65
00:05:03,880 --> 00:05:09,550
be able to just call it from here and execute it remotely on the target computer.

66
00:05:10,420 --> 00:05:13,750
Now I'm also going to show you how to make this back door persistent.

67
00:05:13,750 --> 00:05:20,470
So even if the system restarts, it will automatically connect back to us when the system boots.

68
00:05:21,930 --> 00:05:27,000
Now these are a lot of features and you're actually going to learn a lot of programming concepts as

69
00:05:27,000 --> 00:05:28,640
we write this back door.

70
00:05:28,650 --> 00:05:31,020
So don't worry about how this is working.

71
00:05:31,020 --> 00:05:36,960
I know it sounds a little bit complicated, but we're going to break it down as usual and explain it

72
00:05:36,960 --> 00:05:42,720
simply so that it will make sense to you and you'll be able to use the concepts that you learn here

73
00:05:42,720 --> 00:05:46,200
in any other scenario and in any other program.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,210 --> 00:00:08,140
So far, we created a number of evil files that can be used in so many scenarios, and we know that

2
00:00:08,140 --> 00:00:12,610
we can combine them to come up with even more files and even more attacks.

3
00:00:13,420 --> 00:00:20,020
Now I'd like to spend this lecture and the next few lectures talking about generating backdoors.

4
00:00:20,620 --> 00:00:28,690
Now, these evil files are very useful, but in most cases the ultimate goal is to be able to control

5
00:00:28,690 --> 00:00:34,600
the target system and be able to execute commands on it and see the result.

6
00:00:35,460 --> 00:00:41,040
So I'd like to spend this lecture and the next few lectures talking about backdoors.

7
00:00:41,070 --> 00:00:48,630
Now, a backdoor is just a program that allows us to control the target system and do whatever the normal

8
00:00:48,630 --> 00:00:51,450
users are able to do on that system.

9
00:00:51,720 --> 00:00:57,750
Usually we'd communicate with this backdoor using the command line, and there is a large number of

10
00:00:57,750 --> 00:01:00,010
backdoors that you can already get and used.

11
00:01:00,030 --> 00:01:01,830
You don't have to program your own.

12
00:01:01,980 --> 00:01:08,280
I actually cover creating different types of backdoors in my social engineering course and in my general

13
00:01:08,280 --> 00:01:09,470
ethical hacking course.

14
00:01:09,480 --> 00:01:13,690
So if you're interested in that, then go and check out these courses.

15
00:01:13,710 --> 00:01:19,740
But since this is a programming course, we're going to focus on creating our own backdoors.

16
00:01:20,490 --> 00:01:28,160
Now, the most basic feature that pretty much all backdoors offer is the ability to execute system commands.

17
00:01:28,170 --> 00:01:32,840
So if your target is Windows, you'll be able to execute Windows commands remotely.

18
00:01:32,850 --> 00:01:38,190
If your target is Linux, you'll be able to execute Linux commands remotely and so on.

19
00:01:39,030 --> 00:01:44,400
Other backdoors, such as the ones that I cover in my other courses, will allow you to also upload

20
00:01:44,400 --> 00:01:51,180
download files, run keyloggers, open the webcam, record the mic and do a lot of cool stuff.

21
00:01:51,180 --> 00:01:53,510
But these are all extra features.

22
00:01:53,520 --> 00:01:58,680
The very basic feature is to be able to execute system commands.

23
00:01:59,250 --> 00:02:03,180
Now when we talk about a backdoor, it has to be interactive.

24
00:02:03,360 --> 00:02:07,600
So basically we have the hacker machine and we have the victim machine.

25
00:02:07,620 --> 00:02:10,980
The backdoor gets executed on the victim machine.

26
00:02:11,220 --> 00:02:14,850
So it's just a program that's going to run on the victim machine.

27
00:02:15,030 --> 00:02:21,750
But when it does that, it's going to establish some kind of a connection between the victim and the

28
00:02:21,750 --> 00:02:22,380
hacker.

29
00:02:22,380 --> 00:02:25,620
And you can see this is represented in the yellow line.

30
00:02:26,520 --> 00:02:34,560
So you can think of this as a pipe or a connection that allows data to flow between the hacker and the

31
00:02:34,560 --> 00:02:35,280
victim.

32
00:02:36,030 --> 00:02:39,150
And then the hacker is going to send system commands.

33
00:02:39,150 --> 00:02:45,810
The system commands are going to get executed on the victim machine and the result is going to be sent

34
00:02:45,810 --> 00:02:47,460
back to the hacker.

35
00:02:48,210 --> 00:02:56,370
All of this is possible using this yellow line, which you can think of as a pipe passing data between

36
00:02:56,370 --> 00:02:57,720
the two computers.

37
00:02:58,470 --> 00:03:05,820
So now if you think of it, even before being able to run system commands, we need to be able to establish

38
00:03:05,820 --> 00:03:10,050
some sort of connection between the hacker and the victim.

39
00:03:10,470 --> 00:03:15,750
Once the connection is established, running the system commands is easy because we know we can do that

40
00:03:15,750 --> 00:03:17,860
using the sub process module.

41
00:03:17,880 --> 00:03:20,790
So that's going to be very easy to achieve.

42
00:03:20,820 --> 00:03:27,450
The main challenge right now is to be able to establish some kind of a connection between the hacker

43
00:03:27,450 --> 00:03:33,150
and the victim so that we can pass data, we can send the commands and receive the results.

44
00:03:33,180 --> 00:03:35,100
All of this is just normal data.

45
00:03:35,100 --> 00:03:38,430
So once we have the connection, everything else is easy.

46
00:03:39,330 --> 00:03:45,330
Now, this actually gives me a great opportunity to introduce you to sockets in Python, which can be

47
00:03:45,330 --> 00:03:48,360
used to establish connections and pass data.

48
00:03:48,390 --> 00:03:54,390
You don't have to use them to create backdoors, but we're going to use it to create backdoors because

49
00:03:54,390 --> 00:03:55,920
this is a hacking course.

50
00:03:56,400 --> 00:04:01,560
Now there are a number of ways to establish a connection between two computers.

51
00:04:01,710 --> 00:04:07,140
The two most common ways is to use a direct connection or a bind connection.

52
00:04:07,140 --> 00:04:12,810
And you'll actually see this even with backdoors generated using other programs like Metasploit and

53
00:04:12,810 --> 00:04:13,320
Empire.

54
00:04:13,320 --> 00:04:16,050
So I even talk about this in my other courses.

55
00:04:16,880 --> 00:04:24,410
Now a bind or a direct connection means the initial connection is going to go from the hacker to the

56
00:04:24,410 --> 00:04:25,640
victim machine.

57
00:04:25,940 --> 00:04:33,050
So the victim computer needs to be listening or waiting for incoming connections on a specific port,

58
00:04:33,050 --> 00:04:37,400
and then the hacker is going to connect on that port.

59
00:04:37,820 --> 00:04:45,050
Now, this is not great because for the victim machine to be able to receive the connection, the backdoor

60
00:04:45,050 --> 00:04:48,440
will have to open a specific port in this machine.

61
00:04:48,440 --> 00:04:54,740
And when you open a port on a machine that has a firewall, most of the time the firewall will give

62
00:04:54,740 --> 00:05:01,530
a warning saying here there is an unknown port that has been opened on your computer by an unknown program.

63
00:05:01,550 --> 00:05:02,640
Be careful.

64
00:05:02,660 --> 00:05:05,930
So most people or most systems will detect this.

65
00:05:05,930 --> 00:05:10,040
So it's not a very good way of exploiting a system.

66
00:05:11,340 --> 00:05:15,300
A better way of doing this is to use a reverse connection.

67
00:05:15,840 --> 00:05:23,370
In this case, when the backdoor is executed on the victim computer, it will connect back to the hacker.

68
00:05:23,930 --> 00:05:30,860
So what happens is the hacker will open a port on their computer and they're going to listen for incoming

69
00:05:30,860 --> 00:05:31,760
connections.

70
00:05:31,790 --> 00:05:36,670
Now, the hacker firewall might say here, be careful, there is an open port on your computer.

71
00:05:36,680 --> 00:05:42,980
But me as a hacker, I'm not going to worry about this because I'm going to open this port myself.

72
00:05:42,980 --> 00:05:49,400
So I know that this port is going to be used for something that is useful to me, so I can even disable

73
00:05:49,400 --> 00:05:51,680
my firewall before I do this.

74
00:05:52,190 --> 00:05:58,730
Then we're going to execute the backdoor on the victim computer and the backdoor is going to connect

75
00:05:58,730 --> 00:06:02,420

76
00:06:03,050 --> 00:06:08,720
This connection is very similar to any other connection that the victim does every day.

77
00:06:08,720 --> 00:06:14,390
Similar to when a person opens a website, they connect to the website when they open a website.

78
00:06:14,750 --> 00:06:22,880
Therefore, most firewalls will not trigger this as something that is suspicious, especially if the

79
00:06:22,880 --> 00:06:30,260
port that's used that's opened on the hacker computer is a port that's commonly known such as Port 80

80
00:06:30,260 --> 00:06:35,510
or Port 88, which is a port that's usually used by web servers.

81
00:06:35,540 --> 00:06:42,080
Therefore, as far as the firewall is concerned down here, this computer is making a connection to

82
00:06:42,080 --> 00:06:47,430
a port that's usually used by Web servers, and therefore this is not suspicious at all.

83
00:06:47,450 --> 00:06:54,530
There are no open ports on this computer and there are no suspicious processes opening ports on the

84
00:06:54,530 --> 00:06:55,250

85
00:06:55,910 --> 00:07:02,840
So in all of my other courses, and even in this course, I always use a reverse connection in my back

86
00:07:02,840 --> 00:07:04,610
doors and in my payloads.

And this is what we're going to do when we start building our back door in the next lecture.

88
00:07:10,790 --> 00:07:16,730
For now, I just wanted to make sure that you understand what a backdoor is the most common ways of

89

90
00:07:20,720 --> 00:07:26,180

92

Now.

On the Linux machine.

00:01:59,300 --> 00:02:03,140

00:02:23,560 --> 00:02:24,380

I call that connection.

00:02:36,250 --> 00:02:46,810
First of all, the family which is going to be socket dot af iiNet and the socket type and this is going

00:02:50,770 --> 00:02:56,850
Now again, this is identical to what we did when we were building our backdoor identical to this line.

38

And basically this will create my socket for me.

39
00:03:00,730 --> 00:03:07,540
Now the next thing that I'm going to do is change an option in my socket object and my listener.

40
00:03:07,540 --> 00:03:13,210
So this is going to be an example of changing any option that you want to do that.

41
00:03:13,210 --> 00:03:17,710
First of all, we're going to type the name of the variable that's holding the socket object.

42

Then the method that we can use to change any option is set up, which is short for set option.

43
00:03:27,790 --> 00:03:32,410

44
00:03:32,410 --> 00:03:33,250
So.

45
00:03:33,940 --> 00:03:34,860
Suck it.

46
00:03:35,620 --> 00:03:40,750
And the option that we want to modify is the socket dot.

47
00:03:41,170 --> 00:03:49,390
So we use other, which is a short for address and we want to set the value of this to one.

48
00:03:50,480 --> 00:03:57,560
So basically what I'm trying to do is to enable an option that will allow us to re-use sockets.

49
00:03:57,560 --> 00:04:05,300
So in case the connection drops or we lose connection, then the socket that we created can be reused

50
00:04:05,300 --> 00:04:07,280

51
00:04:07,970 --> 00:04:11,210
So to do that, we're calling the socket object.

52

00:04:14,000 --> 00:04:16,100
Opt to set a socket option.

54
00:04:16,280 --> 00:04:22,820
We're specifying the level, we're specifying the attribute or the option that we want to modify.

55
00:04:22,820 --> 00:04:27,560

56

Now the next thing that we usually do is connect.

So if we go back to our diagram, we want to create something like this socket so that we are waiting

60

00:05:01,800 --> 00:05:04,060

64
00:05:04,080 --> 00:05:10,020
On my machine again, we're creating a circuit that's going to wait for income and connections on our

65
00:05:10,020 --> 00:05:11,160

66
00:05:11,160 --> 00:05:14,060
So it's going to be ten, 0 to 16.

67

69
00:05:23,590 --> 00:05:24,010
Again.

You can use any port you want as long as it's not already being used by your system.

71
00:05:30,580 --> 00:05:33,070
And this is by not being.

Now, once done with this, we're going to say listener.

73
00:05:39,510 --> 00:05:43,290
Not listen so that we can listen for income and connections.

74
00:05:43,440 --> 00:05:47,640
And we need to specify a backlog to this method.

75
00:05:48,340 --> 00:05:55,930
Basically a backlog is the number of connections that can be queued before the system starts refusing

76
00:05:55,930 --> 00:05:56,830

77
00:05:57,580 --> 00:06:00,290
Now, this is not very important right now.

00:06:03,010 --> 00:06:10,840
And basically right now, if we execute our code, this will create something similar to the socket

80
00:06:10,840 --> 00:06:18,400
that's going to bind on my computer and listen for income and connections on the part that we specified

81
00:06:18,400 --> 00:06:19,180
in here.

82
00:06:19,900 --> 00:06:26,110
Now, we also need to tell my computer to accept connections that come to this port.

83
00:06:26,560 --> 00:06:31,150
And to do that, we're going to say listener dot accept.

84
00:06:32,260 --> 00:06:33,280
And that's it.

85
00:06:33,400 --> 00:06:35,970
So let's have a quick look on our code.

86
00:06:35,980 --> 00:06:38,860
And if you think of it, it's actually very, very simple.

00:06:39,040 --> 00:06:43,390
First of all, we're creating a socket object exactly like we did before.

The only difference is we're calling a listener this time instead of connection.

89

90
00:06:53,970 --> 00:07:01,440
Then instead of connecting to a destination, we're actually binding our socket to our computer so that

91
00:07:01,440 --> 00:07:05,610
we listen for income and connections on port four, four, four, four.

92
00:07:06,540 --> 00:07:12,930
We're setting the number of connections that can be queued before connections start getting refused.

93
00:07:13,170 --> 00:07:17,910
And then we're saying that if you get a connection, I want you to accept it.

94
00:07:18,510 --> 00:07:22,140
Now, once the connection is accepted, let's print.

95
00:07:23,280 --> 00:07:25,560
Got a connection.

96
00:07:26,360 --> 00:07:31,910
And before we get a connection, before anything gets accepted, let's print.

97
00:07:33,440 --> 00:07:35,720
Waiting for income and connections.

98
00:07:36,960 --> 00:07:37,590
Okay.

99
00:07:37,680 --> 00:07:40,260
And let's go ahead and test our code.

100
00:07:40,260 --> 00:07:46,710
So I'm already in my working directory and I'm just going to do python listener dot py.

101
00:07:47,760 --> 00:07:52,500
As you can see, it's telling us that it is waiting for income and connections.

102
00:07:52,500 --> 00:08:01,200
So basically we're at this line right here and we proceeded to this line and basically this line paused

103
00:08:01,200 --> 00:08:08,760
the program and it's waiting for a connection to come before executing any code that comes after this.

104
00:08:08,880 --> 00:08:12,330
Hence, we still didn't see this print message right here.

105
00:08:13,890 --> 00:08:21,960
Now, if I go to the Windows machine and run my back door now I'm actually going to remove this line

106
00:08:21,960 --> 00:08:28,440
which sends connection established because we're printing that in the listener and I'm just going to

107
00:08:28,440 --> 00:08:31,260
go and run the back door as usual.

108
00:08:32,630 --> 00:08:41,180
And if we go here, you'll see that the next line got executed and it's telling us that we got a connection.

109
00:08:41,720 --> 00:08:48,560
So we basically proceeded to this line and then our program quit because we don't have anything in here.

110
00:08:49,430 --> 00:08:56,480
But basically right now we're able to create a listener that can listen on a specific port and receive

111
00:08:56,480 --> 00:08:57,440
a connection.

112
00:08:58,070 --> 00:09:04,250
Now, in the next lecture, we'll see how we can write code so that we can communicate with our backdoor,

113
00:09:04,280 --> 00:09:07,580
send it commands and receive responses.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,040 --> 00:00:08,060
So our listener now is able to receive a connection, but we're not able to use it to send or receive

2
00:00:08,060 --> 00:00:08,750
data.

3
00:00:08,810 --> 00:00:15,590
So as you can see, as soon as we receive the connection, we accepted the connection and the program

4
00:00:15,590 --> 00:00:20,810
just terminated because we didn't really have any code to send or receive data.

5
00:00:21,590 --> 00:00:26,840
Now, the listener did accept method according to the documentation.

6
00:00:26,900 --> 00:00:29,270
It returns two values.

7
00:00:29,630 --> 00:00:37,670
The first value is a socket object that represents the connection that we can use to send or receive

8
00:00:37,670 --> 00:00:38,360
data.

9
00:00:38,630 --> 00:00:41,510
So we're going to call this connection.

10
00:00:42,530 --> 00:00:51,590
And the second object or the second value returned by this method is the address that is bound to this

11
00:00:51,590 --> 00:00:52,460
connection.

12
00:00:52,610 --> 00:00:55,130
So we'll call this address.

13
00:00:56,010 --> 00:01:01,440
And we'll make these variables equal to the values returned from the listener.

14
00:01:01,710 --> 00:01:02,460
Except.

15
00:01:03,600 --> 00:01:10,050
Now, since the address represents the address bound to this connection, we can improve this print

16
00:01:10,050 --> 00:01:12,870
statement by saying, Got a connection.

17
00:01:13,560 --> 00:01:20,040
From and will convert the address to a string by saying string address.

18
00:01:20,550 --> 00:01:27,960
And basically now when we get a connection, we're going to say, got a connection from the IP, which

19
00:01:27,960 --> 00:01:29,730
we got the connection from.

20
00:01:30,840 --> 00:01:37,710
Now the other value that we're capturing is the connection, and this is the socket object that we can

21
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:41,340
use in order to send and receive data.

22
00:01:41,370 --> 00:01:46,350
So it's something similar to the connection that we are creating here.

23
00:01:46,500 --> 00:01:53,850
So we can say connection to receive in order to receive data and we can say connection to send to send

24
00:01:53,850 --> 00:01:54,510
data.

25
00:01:55,320 --> 00:02:02,370
Now we're actually going to be using a loop very similar to this one so that we can send commands and

26
00:02:02,370 --> 00:02:03,720
receive the results.

27
00:02:04,440 --> 00:02:06,780
So I'm going to say, while true.

28
00:02:07,610 --> 00:02:11,120
First of all, we're going to ask the user to enter a command.

29
00:02:11,270 --> 00:02:15,980
We're going to do this using RO input, which is a function that I covered before.

30
00:02:16,040 --> 00:02:20,600
You can go revise it if you don't remember how this works, but it's very simple.

31
00:02:20,630 --> 00:02:27,230
First of all, we're going to say my command is equal to the result that's going to be returned from

32
00:02:27,230 --> 00:02:35,210
RO input and I want the user to see these two arrows for them to enter their command.

33
00:02:35,660 --> 00:02:41,960
Keep in mind, if you're using Python three, you're supposed to use input instead of raw input.

34
00:02:41,990 --> 00:02:44,150
That's something that I covered before.

35
00:02:44,810 --> 00:02:49,640
Now the command variable is going to hold the value entered by the user.

36
00:02:50,120 --> 00:02:54,080
So the next thing that we're going to do, we're going to do connection.

37
00:02:54,770 --> 00:02:58,750
The sense of something very similar to what we did before.

38
00:02:58,760 --> 00:03:03,440
And we want to send the command as the data that we want to send.

39
00:03:04,100 --> 00:03:09,110
Then our client is going to receive this command using this line.

40
00:03:09,110 --> 00:03:14,330
It's going to execute the command and it's going to return the result for me.

41
00:03:15,020 --> 00:03:21,500
Now, once the back door or the client returns, the result, what I'm going to do is I'm going to say

42
00:03:21,500 --> 00:03:22,610
the result.

43
00:03:23,700 --> 00:03:33,000
Is equal to connection, dot, receive, receive and we're going to receive the result in batches of

44
00:03:33,000 --> 00:03:35,550
1024 bytes.

45
00:03:36,060 --> 00:03:40,500
And finally, we're going to print the result.

46
00:03:42,200 --> 00:03:43,130
And we're done.

47
00:03:43,910 --> 00:03:51,470
So basically all we did is we captured two values that get returned by our listener except method.

48
00:03:51,770 --> 00:03:55,910
The first one is a new socket object that represents the connection.

49
00:03:55,910 --> 00:03:59,530
So it's the one that we're using to send and receive data.

50
00:03:59,540 --> 00:04:04,820
And the second one is the address that is bound to the socket.

51
00:04:05,120 --> 00:04:10,940
We're printing an appropriate message to tell the user that we received the connection from this address.

52
00:04:11,390 --> 00:04:15,170
Then we're trapping the user in an infinite wild loop.

53
00:04:15,260 --> 00:04:20,240
First of all, we're asking the user to enter a command using RO input.

54
00:04:20,270 --> 00:04:23,270
The value is stored in a variable called command.

55
00:04:23,540 --> 00:04:30,440
We're sending the command to the backdoor and then we're waiting for the backdoor to give us the result.

56
00:04:30,530 --> 00:04:35,270
So basically the program is going to pause waiting for the result.

57
00:04:35,660 --> 00:04:41,150
Once it gets the result, it's going to store it in a variable called the result, and then we're going

58
00:04:41,150 --> 00:04:42,470
to print it on screen.

59
00:04:43,040 --> 00:04:45,230
So let's see how this is going to work.

60
00:04:45,410 --> 00:04:47,780
I'm going to listen for income and connections.

61
00:04:47,780 --> 00:04:51,350
As you can see, it's telling me I'm waiting for income and connections.

62
00:04:51,560 --> 00:04:53,570
We're going to go to the back door.

63
00:04:53,600 --> 00:04:54,950
We're going to run it.

64
00:04:56,450 --> 00:05:03,170
And if we come here, you'll see that we got a connection from the IP that initiated the connection,

65
00:05:03,170 --> 00:05:05,360
which is the IP of the Windows machine.

66
00:05:05,600 --> 00:05:12,370
And then we're seeing the two little arrows, which is what we specified in here and our input.

67
00:05:12,380 --> 00:05:18,050
So you can put any text that you want to be displayed to the user to tell them to enter a command.

68
00:05:18,770 --> 00:05:23,300
So we're basically at this line waiting for a command to be given.

69
00:05:23,570 --> 00:05:26,750
And once the user puts a command like the are.

70
00:05:27,990 --> 00:05:30,060
As you can see, we're going to see the result.

71
00:05:30,090 --> 00:05:34,930
This is because this command is being sent and we're waiting for the result.

72
00:05:34,950 --> 00:05:40,710
The back door is executing the command, returning the result to us, and the result is being printed

73
00:05:40,710 --> 00:05:41,670
on screen.

74
00:05:42,480 --> 00:05:46,480
Keep in mind, we actually did modify the code in the backdoor at all.

75
00:05:46,500 --> 00:05:50,310
It's just working without the need to modify anything.

76
00:05:51,120 --> 00:05:57,600
So right now we're able to build our own listener, which is able to communicate with our backdoor.

77
00:05:57,810 --> 00:06:06,090
And now we're able to basically hack the target computer and execute system commands using a backdoor

78
00:06:06,090 --> 00:06:08,730
and a listener that we wrote from scratch.

79
00:06:09,450 --> 00:06:16,710
Now, the method that I showed you so far can be created to create a connection and transfer data between

80
00:06:16,710 --> 00:06:19,680
any two computers for any reason.

81
00:06:19,710 --> 00:06:21,190
It doesn't have to be a backdoor.

82
00:06:21,210 --> 00:06:27,390
I know I said this a lot, but I just want to get this idea across that you can use sockets to transfer

83
00:06:27,390 --> 00:06:29,590
data between two computers.

84
00:06:29,610 --> 00:06:36,420
Now we created a server and the client, so we're able to transfer data between them regardless of the

85
00:06:36,420 --> 00:06:40,740
reason that you want to do that, for using it to create a backdoor.

86
00:06:40,770 --> 00:06:45,750
It's just one of many examples or uses that you can use sockets for.

87

Now, since we have our own listener now that works perfectly.

In the next lecture, we're going to start implementing more features in our backdoor so that it's more

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,840 --> 00:00:08,880
So far we learned how to use circuits in Python in order to connect to computers and transfer data between

2
00:00:08,880 --> 00:00:09,330
them.

3
00:00:09,720 --> 00:00:16,110
We wrote the code needed for this from scratch, so we wrote a code for a client or a back door and

4
00:00:16,110 --> 00:00:18,690
a code for a listener or a server.

5
00:00:18,690 --> 00:00:21,840
And we seen how we can transfer data between them.

6
00:00:21,840 --> 00:00:28,200
And I already mentioned that this method can be used in any scenario where you need to transfer data.

7
00:00:29,100 --> 00:00:35,560
As an example, we built a backdoor when installed or executed on a target computer.

8
00:00:35,580 --> 00:00:41,190
It allows us to execute system commands remotely on that computer.

9
00:00:41,940 --> 00:00:48,420
Now, when I spoke about backdoors, we said the most basic functionality is to allow the hacker to

10
00:00:48,420 --> 00:00:50,730
execute system commands remotely.

11
00:00:51,240 --> 00:00:55,310
But a lot of backdoors allow you to do much more than this.

12
00:00:55,320 --> 00:00:59,120
For example, they'll allow you to upload or download files.

13
00:00:59,130 --> 00:01:03,060
They allow you to access the file system and so on.

14
00:01:03,570 --> 00:01:09,480
So in the next lectures, I'd like to start implementing more features in our backdoor so that it's

15
00:01:09,480 --> 00:01:10,470
more useful.

16
00:01:10,470 --> 00:01:16,830
And also, as we do this, you're going to learn more about socket programming and about Python in general.

17
00:01:17,610 --> 00:01:23,310
But before we do that, because we're going to start implementing more features, writing more code,

18
00:01:23,370 --> 00:01:30,360
it will be a good idea to convert our code, which is right now is just a simple script into an object

19
00:01:30,360 --> 00:01:32,790
oriented code that use classes.

20
00:01:33,420 --> 00:01:40,320
We spoke about classes before, so this will be a good revision and another example on using classes.

21
00:01:41,010 --> 00:01:45,500
So the first part of my program is the server or the listener.

22
00:01:45,510 --> 00:01:50,840
And in order to convert this to a class, we have to create a new class.

23
00:01:50,850 --> 00:01:58,050
So as we shown before, we have to type class to define a class followed by the class name.

24
00:01:58,050 --> 00:02:00,030
And this is going to be our listener.

25
00:02:00,030 --> 00:02:01,830
So we're going to call it listener.

26
00:02:02,960 --> 00:02:06,100
Note the capital L at the start of the word.

27
00:02:06,110 --> 00:02:13,250
This is a convention when you're writing programs in Python to capitalize the first letter in the name

28
00:02:13,250 --> 00:02:14,510
of the class.

29
00:02:15,200 --> 00:02:19,430
Now, the next thing that I'm going to do is I'm going to create a constructor.

30
00:02:19,430 --> 00:02:26,000
So this is a method that gets executed as soon as the class is created.

31
00:02:26,000 --> 00:02:28,970
So it's something that initializes the class.

32
00:02:29,660 --> 00:02:32,930
So to create a constructor, we do def.

33
00:02:33,730 --> 00:02:40,990
Underscore underscore in it, and that's going to take self and then we implement the code that's going

34
00:02:40,990 --> 00:02:43,120
to be executed in this method.

35
00:02:43,750 --> 00:02:52,650
Now I want to establish a connection and listening on a specific port whenever the object is created.

36
00:02:52,660 --> 00:02:59,350
So I want this to be executed automatically whenever the listener object is created, and therefore

37
00:02:59,350 --> 00:03:04,660
I'm going to copy all of this code that we usually execute at the start of our script.

38
00:03:05,680 --> 00:03:07,210
We're going to put it in here.

39
00:03:08,250 --> 00:03:12,060
Now looking at the code, you can see that this takes two variables.

40
00:03:12,070 --> 00:03:15,330
It takes the port and the IP to listen to.

41
00:03:15,960 --> 00:03:22,200
So I'm going to add two arguments to my constructor, which are IP and port.

42
00:03:23,270 --> 00:03:25,160
And I'm going to use them in here.

43
00:03:29,640 --> 00:03:35,760
This will allow the user to use different IPS and different ports whenever they create the listener

44
00:03:35,760 --> 00:03:36,510
object.

45
00:03:37,930 --> 00:03:43,240
Now, another thing that we need to keep track of is the connection.

46
00:03:43,480 --> 00:03:49,240
Now you can see that this variable right here gets used later on in our program.

47
00:03:49,390 --> 00:03:56,020
So I'm going to convert this to an attribute of my object by saying self.

48
00:03:56,920 --> 00:04:03,850
That connection is going to be equal to the first value returned by the listener dot.

49
00:04:03,910 --> 00:04:12,640
Except by doing so I'll be able to access the self that connection from anywhere within my code.

50
00:04:12,670 --> 00:04:16,240
As long as that code is part of the class.

51
00:04:16,930 --> 00:04:23,410
So this means that we need to include all of this code, which is the code that takes a user input,

52
00:04:23,410 --> 00:04:26,920
sends it to the backdoor and get the result and print it.

53
00:04:27,040 --> 00:04:32,920
This needs to be part of the class now in order to be able to access the connection attribute.

54
00:04:33,130 --> 00:04:34,570
So I'm going to cut it.

55
00:04:36,220 --> 00:04:41,560
I'm going to create a method here and we'll call this method run.

56
00:04:43,200 --> 00:04:50,700
And in here I'm just going to paste all of my code and whenever I use the connection variable now we're

57
00:04:50,700 --> 00:04:52,290
going to call it self.

58
00:04:53,970 --> 00:04:55,350
Dot connection.

59
00:04:55,530 --> 00:04:57,060
And same here.

60
00:04:58,800 --> 00:05:05,880
We're doing this so that any time we're trying to use the connection object, we're actually using the

61
00:05:05,880 --> 00:05:09,810
attribute of our class, of our listener class.

62
00:05:10,960 --> 00:05:17,350
Now I'd also like to put this code right here, which is the code that sends the command and receive

63
00:05:17,350 --> 00:05:19,540
the result into a method.

64
00:05:19,960 --> 00:05:24,910
So I'm going to cut this and I'm going to create a method here.

65
00:05:26,360 --> 00:05:29,270
I'm going to call this execute remotely.

66
00:05:31,420 --> 00:05:34,240
And this will take a command.

67
00:05:35,820 --> 00:05:42,900
And in here, we're going to send the command through our connection and we're going to return.

68
00:05:43,650 --> 00:05:54,570
Whatever we receive and in here and our run method, we're going to say the result is going to be equal

69
00:05:54,870 --> 00:05:59,160
to myself that execute remotely.

70
00:06:00,230 --> 00:06:02,750
And this is going to pass the command.

71
00:06:04,100 --> 00:06:04,820
This way.

72
00:06:04,820 --> 00:06:10,910
Every time I need to implement a new command, I'm going to start implementing them and processing them

73
00:06:10,910 --> 00:06:11,660
in here.

74
00:06:12,910 --> 00:06:15,340
So now I think the code is very neat.

75
00:06:15,370 --> 00:06:23,800
It's using a class and we are able to forget about how this class works and only remember the methods

76
00:06:23,800 --> 00:06:25,180
to use the class.

77
00:06:26,020 --> 00:06:32,950
Now you can import this listener class in a different file and use it from that file just like I showed

78
00:06:32,950 --> 00:06:37,420
you when we converted our keylogger code to use classes.

79
00:06:38,080 --> 00:06:44,470
Alternatively, you can actually create an instance of this class and use it within this file.

80
00:06:44,920 --> 00:06:50,500
So I'm just going to show you this in here as an example, but using the class from a separate file

81
00:06:50,500 --> 00:06:51,820
is actually better.

82
00:06:52,510 --> 00:06:58,870
Now, in order to create an instance of this class, as usual, we're going to create a variable and

83
00:06:58,870 --> 00:07:00,280
we'll call it my listener.

84
00:07:00,880 --> 00:07:04,270
And this is going to be equal to listener.

85
00:07:05,240 --> 00:07:12,800
We'll have to pass it the IPI, which is ten 0 to 16, this is the IP of the local machine followed

86
00:07:12,800 --> 00:07:17,360
by the port to listen on and then to run this class.

87
00:07:17,360 --> 00:07:20,540
We're going to say my listener dot run.

88
00:07:22,150 --> 00:07:23,170
And that's it.

89
00:07:23,770 --> 00:07:29,500
Now, let's go ahead and test this and make sure that our listener still works as expected and we didn't

90
00:07:29,500 --> 00:07:30,520
break anything.

91
00:07:30,730 --> 00:07:38,770
So if I do python listener dot py and go to my windows machine and run the backdoor.

92
00:07:40,050 --> 00:07:42,970
As you can see, we got a connection, so that's perfect.

93
00:07:42,990 --> 00:07:46,680
Now let's try to execute some commands, so let's do there.

94
00:07:47,610 --> 00:07:48,420
That's perfect.

95
00:07:48,420 --> 00:07:49,630
We're getting the result.

96
00:07:49,650 --> 00:07:51,330
Let's do another command.

97
00:07:51,720 --> 00:07:54,290
And this is working as expected.

98
00:07:54,300 --> 00:08:01,860
So right now, we didn't really add any functionality to our back door or to our listener, but we converted

99
00:08:01,860 --> 00:08:05,290

100
00:08:05,310 --> 00:08:12,180
This will allow us to easily extend this code and add more features as we go along the course.

1
00:00:00,540 --> 00:00:01,100
Okay.

00:00:01,110 --> 00:00:06,870
Now that we converted our listener into a class, I want to do the same with my backdoor.

00:00:07,350 --> 00:00:10,230
So this is going to be similar to what we did.

4
00:00:11,070 --> 00:00:17,970

call Backdoor.

00:00:21,040 --> 00:00:29,530
