in it, underscore, underscore.

8
00:00:32,980 --> 00:00:40,780
And this is going to take self as an argument and similar to the listener, I'm going to make it take

9
00:00:40,780 --> 00:00:44,860
the IP and the port as arguments.

10
00:00:45,800 --> 00:00:52,700
Now, like we said before in the constructor, we put the code that we want to be automatically executed

11
00:00:52,700 --> 00:00:55,910
when an instance of this class is created.

12
00:00:56,630 --> 00:01:03,950
So in my example, I want to execute these two lines, which will create an instance of a circuit which

13
00:01:03,950 --> 00:01:06,980
we were executing at the start of our script.

14
00:01:07,040 --> 00:01:10,640
So I'm going to cut them and put it in here.

15
00:01:11,440 --> 00:01:13,870
I'd make sure it's indented properly.

16
00:01:14,560 --> 00:01:20,530
And basically, as we know, these two lines, the first line will create an instance of the connection

17
00:01:20,530 --> 00:01:26,590
of a socket, and then the second one will connect to a specific IP and port.

18
00:01:27,310 --> 00:01:33,010
So and here I'm going to use the IP that I'm taking as an argument and the port.

19
00:01:34,680 --> 00:01:41,070
And also, if you look at the rest of the code, you can see that our code uses the connection.

20
00:01:41,730 --> 00:01:46,190
So we need to be able to access this within our class.

21
00:01:46,200 --> 00:01:54,150
Therefore we're going to add self before the variable name so that this will be an attribute of the

22
00:01:54,150 --> 00:01:54,870
class.

23
00:01:54,870 --> 00:02:01,620
And this means that we'll be able to access this instance or this variable throughout the whole class,

24
00:02:01,620 --> 00:02:04,560
throughout any method in our class.

25
00:02:05,310 --> 00:02:14,640
So when we want to use this, we will always have to say self dot connection and this will apply here.

26
00:02:16,330 --> 00:02:17,380
And here.

27
00:02:19,300 --> 00:02:19,930
Now.

28
00:02:19,930 --> 00:02:26,590
I already have a function right here that I programmed before, and all we have to do is just tap it

29
00:02:26,890 --> 00:02:35,620
so that it's part of my class and I'm going to make it take self as the first argument so that it's

30
00:02:35,620 --> 00:02:37,720
a proper method of the class.

31
00:02:38,720 --> 00:02:45,320
And finally, I'll have to put the rest of the code, which is this code right here into a method.

32
00:02:46,100 --> 00:02:53,060
Now, this code is the part of the program that will run the infinite loop, where the program is going

33
00:02:53,060 --> 00:03:00,650
to take the user input, execute it as a system command, and return it back to the hacker or to the

34
00:03:00,650 --> 00:03:01,280
listener.

35
00:03:01,940 --> 00:03:09,620
So I'm going to define a function, we'll call this run and it will take self as an input.

36
00:03:10,950 --> 00:03:17,550
I'm just going to highlight all of this and press tab twice so that it's part of this method.

37
00:03:18,260 --> 00:03:20,630
And now our program is done.

38
00:03:20,960 --> 00:03:23,040
So like we said before.

39
00:03:23,060 --> 00:03:27,640
Now we can create another file and call this from that other file.

40
00:03:27,650 --> 00:03:29,330
And this is perfectly fine.

41
00:03:29,330 --> 00:03:36,020
When we get to packaging, you'll be able to package the two files as one file, or you can do what

42
00:03:36,020 --> 00:03:42,010
I did with the listener and just create an instance of this class at the bottom of the class.

43
00:03:42,020 --> 00:03:48,950
So we can literally just fold this whole class right here and forget about it, and we can create an

44
00:03:48,950 --> 00:03:51,490
instance of it by typing a variable name.

45
00:03:51,500 --> 00:03:53,540
So let's call this my backdoor.

46
00:03:54,710 --> 00:03:58,100
This is going to equal to a backdoor class.

47
00:03:58,100 --> 00:04:02,590
So this will create the object for me and it'll pass.

48
00:04:02,600 --> 00:04:11,420
First of all, the IP, which is ten 0 to 16 and the port that we want to connect to, which is four,

49
00:04:11,420 --> 00:04:12,680
four, four, four.

50
00:04:13,160 --> 00:04:19,790
And then we're going to say, I want to do my backdoor dot run to run the backdoor.

51
00:04:20,330 --> 00:04:24,320
Now let's go and test this and make sure that we didn't break anything.

52
00:04:24,590 --> 00:04:33,470
So I'm going to go down, run the listener and we're going to go on my command, prompt here and run

53
00:04:33,470 --> 00:04:34,460
the backdoor.

54
00:04:35,090 --> 00:04:36,740
Looks like it's running fine.

55
00:04:36,740 --> 00:04:41,180
We get the connection, let's execute some commands, so let's do there.

56
00:04:41,840 --> 00:04:43,760
And this is not working.

57
00:04:43,760 --> 00:04:48,950
So something is not being passed or used properly.

58
00:04:49,640 --> 00:04:52,640
So let's go back and see if we made any mistakes.

59
00:04:53,510 --> 00:04:55,670
And yes, we did.

60
00:04:56,150 --> 00:05:00,860
Throughout our code, we're calling a function called execute system command.

61
00:05:01,040 --> 00:05:04,760
But as we learned before, when we do this.

62
00:05:05,840 --> 00:05:08,030
So it's in here.

63
00:05:08,840 --> 00:05:16,880
We need to say self don't execute system command because now we're calling a method that is programmed

64
00:05:16,880 --> 00:05:18,680
within our current class.

65
00:05:18,680 --> 00:05:22,460
So we have to put self before the name of the method.

66
00:05:23,330 --> 00:05:26,150
So now this should get it working for us.

67
00:05:26,150 --> 00:05:28,400
So let's do the same again.

68
00:05:28,400 --> 00:05:30,580
We'll I'll do control seen here.

69
00:05:30,590 --> 00:05:32,780
Listen for income and connections.

70
00:05:35,430 --> 00:05:39,930
And run the backdoor and let's do a command.

71
00:05:41,700 --> 00:05:46,320
And as you can see, the command is getting executed properly.

72
00:05:46,860 --> 00:05:49,110
So let's try another command.

73
00:05:50,330 --> 00:05:53,960
And again, everything is working as expected.

74
00:05:54,320 --> 00:05:59,280
Now, like I said before, we didn't really implement any new features.

75
00:05:59,300 --> 00:06:06,170
All we did right now is we converted the code for the listener and for the backdoor so that it's object

76
00:06:06,170 --> 00:06:08,870
oriented, so that it uses classes.

77
00:06:08,870 --> 00:06:15,260
And this will make the code neater and easier to extend so that in the future, in the next lectures,

78
00:06:15,260 --> 00:06:22,220
we'll start to build on our backdoor, we'll make it more reliable, and we'll start adding more features

79
00:06:22,220 --> 00:06:22,730
to it.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,130 --> 00:00:01,750
Okay.

2
00:00:01,760 --> 00:00:08,630
Now that we converted our backdoor and listener to use classes so that they're easier to extend, we

3
00:00:08,630 --> 00:00:13,430
are ready to start implementing new features in our backdoor.

4
00:00:13,850 --> 00:00:20,960
But before we do that, I want to spend some time talking about a common issue that you might face when

5
00:00:20,960 --> 00:00:23,680
transferring data over TCP.

6
00:00:24,890 --> 00:00:31,520
Now, as you remember, when we created our sockets in the back door and in the listener, we set the

7
00:00:31,520 --> 00:00:39,170
connection type to SOC stream, which basically means that we want to create a TCP connection.

8
00:00:39,560 --> 00:00:45,530
So the way that TCP works is it creates a connection between two computers.

9
00:00:45,710 --> 00:00:53,240
And you can think of this connection as a pipe where the data is water flowing through this pipe.

10
00:00:53,420 --> 00:01:02,090
So the way the data is transferred between these two computers is as a stream, not as messages.

11
00:01:02,090 --> 00:01:06,800
So it's a stream based protocol, not a message based protocol.

12
00:01:07,280 --> 00:01:10,670
Now, you might be asking, so what are you trying to say?

13
00:01:10,700 --> 00:01:17,690
Basically, what I'm trying to say is think of this as a pipe where every time the client wants to send

14
00:01:17,690 --> 00:01:23,810
something to the server, they add more data to this pipe or they add more water to this pipe.

15
00:01:24,290 --> 00:01:27,710
So the data is all mixed up in this pipe.

16
00:01:27,710 --> 00:01:34,310
And when it's sent to the server, the server is able to receive this data or this water, but it doesn't

17
00:01:34,310 --> 00:01:37,610
know where the end of each message is.

18
00:01:38,090 --> 00:01:39,950
So let me show you an example.

19
00:01:39,980 --> 00:01:46,460
Let's say that the client wants to send some long data as a message to the server.

20
00:01:47,060 --> 00:01:54,980
Now, as you remember in the server, when we receive data, we call the connection DOT or HCV, and

21
00:01:54,980 --> 00:02:00,950
we're specifying that I want to receive 1024 bytes of data.

22
00:02:01,600 --> 00:02:11,290
So even before receiving the data here, we are saying that I want to receive only 1024 bytes, so hence

23
00:02:11,290 --> 00:02:16,930
the small red box that we're already creating before we even receive the message.

24
00:02:17,650 --> 00:02:23,770
Then when the client sent this on the stream, as you can see, the whole data gets pushed in the TCP

25
00:02:23,770 --> 00:02:24,340
stream.

26
00:02:24,340 --> 00:02:26,470
So that's all fine so far.

27
00:02:26,770 --> 00:02:34,840
But the problem happens is once the data arrives to the server, once the server starts to drawing data

28
00:02:34,840 --> 00:02:36,310
out of the stream.

29
00:02:37,010 --> 00:02:40,610
So we're going to take only 1024 bytes.

30
00:02:40,610 --> 00:02:44,810
So we're only going to take enough data to fill our box.

31
00:02:45,260 --> 00:02:51,710
Now, the server thinks that it receives the data, but there is still a lot of data left in the pipe.

32
00:02:52,190 --> 00:02:58,130
Now, the reason why the server doesn't know that, because like I said, every time the client wants

33
00:02:58,130 --> 00:03:02,280
to send something, they just push it in this pipe in the stream.

34
00:03:02,300 --> 00:03:08,210
There is no way for the server to know where the start and the end of each message.

35
00:03:09,280 --> 00:03:09,790
Again.

36
00:03:09,790 --> 00:03:13,860
The main reason for this is because we're sending this stuff over TCP.

37
00:03:13,960 --> 00:03:18,640
And TCP is a stream based protocol, not a message based.

38
00:03:19,250 --> 00:03:21,680
So everything is mixed up in here.

39
00:03:22,860 --> 00:03:26,490
Now let me show you how this issue will happen in practice.

40
00:03:27,120 --> 00:03:30,780
So right here, I already have a connection from my back door.

41
00:03:31,050 --> 00:03:38,760
And if I just do it there, I'm in Z Python projects and you can see that I have a file called sample

42
00:03:38,760 --> 00:03:39,830
dot txt.

43
00:03:40,530 --> 00:03:46,620
Now in windows we can use the more command to read the contents of a file.

44
00:03:47,220 --> 00:03:51,090
So let's say I want to read the contents of sample text.

45
00:03:51,900 --> 00:03:57,180
Now if I hit enter, you'll see that I get the contents, which is perfect.

46
00:03:57,330 --> 00:04:01,170
But this is not the full content of the file.

47
00:04:01,800 --> 00:04:03,300
So let me show you.

48
00:04:03,300 --> 00:04:06,360
If I go to my Windows Machine and open the file.

49
00:04:07,550 --> 00:04:11,660
You'll see that we're only reading up to this paragraph right here.

50
00:04:12,110 --> 00:04:18,710
So basically what's happening is the client, which is the back door in here, is placing all of this

51
00:04:18,710 --> 00:04:21,320
data on the TCP stream.

52
00:04:21,890 --> 00:04:28,940
But when this data arrives at the back door, the backdoor is only reading 1024 bytes.

53
00:04:29,300 --> 00:04:31,970
So it's only reading up to this point.

54
00:04:31,970 --> 00:04:34,970
And then it doesn't know that there is more data.

55
00:04:35,570 --> 00:04:42,410
Now, what's going to happen if I had enter my rack v method will be called again and we'll actually

56
00:04:42,410 --> 00:04:43,480
receive more data.

57
00:04:43,490 --> 00:04:48,740
So you'll see, we'll get the rest of the file and if I hit it again, I'll get the rest of the file.

58
00:04:48,770 --> 00:04:50,810
But now I broke the connection.

59
00:04:50,810 --> 00:04:53,090
So if I had enter, I'll get nothing.

60
00:04:53,090 --> 00:04:57,620
And if you wait for a little bit, you'll get a broken pipe exception.

61
00:04:59,240 --> 00:05:06,770
Now, this is actually a very important thing to fix because regardless of why you have a socket connection,

62
00:05:06,770 --> 00:05:12,230
let's say maybe you have that for any other reason other than using a backdoor, you're going to face

63
00:05:12,230 --> 00:05:21,260
this issue every time you send data that is larger than the buffer size, which is 1024 in our program.

64
00:05:21,380 --> 00:05:26,810
Now you're going to get this issue, whether you're sending stuff from the server to the client or from

65
00:05:26,810 --> 00:05:28,250
the client to the server.

66
00:05:28,970 --> 00:05:32,960
And this issue will obviously get bigger when you try to implement more features.

67
00:05:32,960 --> 00:05:38,840
For example, we want to be able to upload and download files and a lot of these files will be bigger

68
00:05:38,840 --> 00:05:42,530
than 1024, which again will cause us issues.

69
00:05:42,800 --> 00:05:49,220
And if you're writing a larger application, something other than a backdoor, then you'll definitely

70
00:05:49,220 --> 00:05:53,930
face this issue when you're transferring data between your client and your server.

71
00:05:54,350 --> 00:06:01,700
So before we dive into implementing new features in our backdoor, I want to address this issue so that

72
00:06:01,700 --> 00:06:08,210
you know how to properly and reliably send data between two clients over sockets.

73
00:06:08,360 --> 00:06:13,550
I'm going to keep this as generic as possible and we're going to forget that we have a backdoor and

74
00:06:13,730 --> 00:06:20,660
a listener so that you can use this way regardless of what application you're writing, as long as your

75
00:06:20,660 --> 00:06:22,580
application uses sockets.

76
00:06:23,590 --> 00:06:27,010
Now there are a number of solutions to fix this.

77
00:06:27,160 --> 00:06:33,460
Basically, the idea that you want to keep in mind is you need to make sure that the message is well

78
00:06:33,460 --> 00:06:41,320
defined so that the device that's receiving this message knows exactly where this message ends.

79
00:06:42,010 --> 00:06:48,340
This way, even if it receives half of it or part of it, it knows that there is more to this message,

80
00:06:48,340 --> 00:06:54,460
and it will call the received message again and again until it actually receives the whole data.

81
00:06:56,290 --> 00:06:58,360
So ideas of doing this.

82
00:06:58,360 --> 00:07:04,330
You can, for example, measure the size of the message before you actually send it at the client,

83
00:07:04,480 --> 00:07:10,450
add the size to the start of the message, and then send it when the server receives it.

84
00:07:10,450 --> 00:07:11,840
It can read the size.

85
00:07:11,860 --> 00:07:18,640
It knows exactly how big this message should be, and it will keep receiving until the size of the received

86
00:07:18,640 --> 00:07:21,730
message is equal to the size of the sent message.

87
00:07:22,780 --> 00:07:30,070
The second way of doing this, you could append something to the end of the message so that when this

88
00:07:30,070 --> 00:07:36,340
guy is receiving stuff, it will keep receiving the message or or removing stuff from the connection

89
00:07:36,340 --> 00:07:39,460
until it reaches this special end of message.

90
00:07:39,460 --> 00:07:41,680
Mark that you're going to specify yourself.

91
00:07:42,100 --> 00:07:45,430
Again, you'll be adding this at the client before you send it.

92
00:07:45,430 --> 00:07:51,280
And once received, you're going to have to make sure that you read the stuff up to this end and then

93
00:07:51,280 --> 00:07:54,940
remove this end so that you don't interfere with the message.

94
00:07:55,780 --> 00:08:02,140
And finally, which is the method that I want to use and implement is using serialization.

95
00:08:02,680 --> 00:08:09,340
Now, serialization is very commonly used in programming, not only in Python, when we want to transfer

96
00:08:09,340 --> 00:08:11,650
data between two computers.

97
00:08:12,130 --> 00:08:18,100
The reason why this is very widely used is because, first of all, it fixes the issue that we have

98
00:08:18,100 --> 00:08:21,670
right now, which is our messages are not well defined.

99
00:08:21,670 --> 00:08:28,600
So using serialization, our message is going to be well defined and the receiver will know exactly

100
00:08:28,600 --> 00:08:30,040
where the message ends.

101
00:08:30,580 --> 00:08:36,400
It also has an added benefit, which it basically allows us to send data structures.

102
00:08:36,400 --> 00:08:42,970
So it allows us to send lists or dictionaries through our connection, which is something that we can't

103
00:08:42,970 --> 00:08:43,930
do right now.

104
00:08:43,930 --> 00:08:47,560
We can only send text, we can only send strings.

105
00:08:48,340 --> 00:08:55,840
Now, the way that serialization works is basically the client converts the object or the message to

106
00:08:55,840 --> 00:08:59,200
be sent to a stream of well defined bytes.

107
00:08:59,680 --> 00:09:08,140
It puts that on the TCP stream, the server is going to keep receiving stuff from the TCP stream, and

108
00:09:08,140 --> 00:09:13,720
once it receives everything because it's well defined, it's going to know what it has everything.

109
00:09:13,780 --> 00:09:18,620
And it's going to convert this back to the object that it was.

110
00:09:18,640 --> 00:09:22,600
So if we send the list here, we'll be able to receive a list here.

111
00:09:22,600 --> 00:09:27,670
If we send a dictionary, we'll be able to receive a dictionary here and so on.

112
00:09:28,560 --> 00:09:34,880
So basically the way that this will work is going back to the same example that we were talking about.

113
00:09:34,890 --> 00:09:42,000
So let's say that the client wants to send a message that says sample long data to send over TCP stream.

114
00:09:42,390 --> 00:09:44,670
It's going to put all of this message.

115
00:09:44,670 --> 00:09:46,680
So this could be a normal string.

116
00:09:46,680 --> 00:09:50,100
This could be a list, this could be a dictionary.

117
00:09:50,100 --> 00:09:56,310
Regardless of what it is, it's going to put it inside a box so you can think of this as a package or

118
00:09:56,310 --> 00:09:57,060
a box.

119
00:09:57,540 --> 00:10:03,930
Then it's going to close this box and put this box in my TCP stream.

120
00:10:04,870 --> 00:10:11,710
Then because this is a box, because this is well defined, the server is going to receive data and

121
00:10:11,710 --> 00:10:16,270
it will only stop receiving data until this box is complete.

122
00:10:16,450 --> 00:10:19,190
We still don't know what's inside this box.

123
00:10:19,210 --> 00:10:21,820
We just know that this box is complete.

124
00:10:22,480 --> 00:10:29,530
Then the server is going to unbox this box and it will be able to retrieve whatever that was inside

125
00:10:29,530 --> 00:10:37,000
this box, whether it's a message or a string or a list or an object or any other data type.

126
00:10:37,570 --> 00:10:43,930
So you can see that this is a very simple and useful way of transferring data between a client and a

127
00:10:43,930 --> 00:10:44,560
server.

128
00:10:44,860 --> 00:10:52,300
It allows us to make sure that the message is sent and received properly, and it also allows us to

129
00:10:52,300 --> 00:10:59,500
send data structures like lists and dictionaries, which is very, very important when we want to do

130
00:10:59,500 --> 00:11:02,440
more complex stuff in our program.

131
00:11:03,190 --> 00:11:09,070
So in the next lecture, we'll see how we can put this in practice, and then we'll start using it in

132
00:11:09,070 --> 00:11:12,970
order to extend our backdoor and implement more features.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,100 --> 00:00:01,440
Okay.

2
00:00:01,520 --> 00:00:09,140
Now that we know what serialization is and why we might want to use it, let's talk about how we can

3
00:00:09,140 --> 00:00:10,970
implement this in Python.

4
00:00:11,630 --> 00:00:14,170
Now, there are a number of ways that you could do this.

5
00:00:14,180 --> 00:00:17,150
You could even write your own class that does this.

6
00:00:17,150 --> 00:00:22,790
But a very common ways of doing this in Python is to use JSON or pickle.

7
00:00:23,300 --> 00:00:30,440
Now I'm going to use JSON because it's implemented in a number of programming languages.

8
00:00:30,440 --> 00:00:35,780
So getting comfortable with it will actually help you in general as a programmer.

9
00:00:36,500 --> 00:00:40,550
Now JSON stands for JavaScript object notation.

10
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:48,680
It's basically a way of converting data structures such as dictionaries, lists and objects into a string

11
00:00:48,680 --> 00:00:49,640
or a text.

12
00:00:50,090 --> 00:00:54,060
Then we'll be able to send this text over the TCP stream.

13
00:00:54,080 --> 00:01:02,360
Once it's received again, we'll use JSON again to convert this text back into its original data structure,

14
00:01:02,360 --> 00:01:08,090
whether it was an object, a dictionary or a list or any other data type.

15
00:01:08,720 --> 00:01:11,920
Now, like I said, JSON is very commonly used.

16
00:01:11,930 --> 00:01:17,800
It's actually used in a lot of apps where you have an app that communicates with a server on the cloud.

17
00:01:17,810 --> 00:01:25,340
So the way that the app communicates with the server and receive data from the server is using JSON.

18
00:01:25,340 --> 00:01:32,450
So it wraps all of its data in a JSON object, it sends it to the cloud, and then the response comes

19
00:01:32,450 --> 00:01:35,990
back as a JSON object which gets unwrapped in the app.

20
00:01:35,990 --> 00:01:40,910
And then you see the beautiful app with all the information and the services that offer.

21
00:01:40,910 --> 00:01:46,760
But usually a lot of this data is actually transferred as JSON strings.

22
00:01:47,420 --> 00:01:51,980
So let's go and put this in practice and you'll see that this is very, very easy.

23
00:01:52,550 --> 00:01:56,090
The first thing that I'm going to do is I'm going to define a method.

24
00:01:57,380 --> 00:01:59,840
And I'm going to call it reliable send.

25
00:02:01,070 --> 00:02:07,550
And we'll use this method instead of the socket send method every time we need to send data.

26
00:02:08,360 --> 00:02:13,190
Now this method is going to take the data to be sent as an input.

27
00:02:13,980 --> 00:02:20,760
And then the first thing that it's going to do is convert this data into a JSON object.

28
00:02:21,450 --> 00:02:25,020
To do this, we're going to need to import the JSON library.

29
00:02:26,500 --> 00:02:32,830
And then all we're going to do is I'm going to say JSON data, which is the name of my variable, is

30
00:02:32,830 --> 00:02:37,240
going to be equal to JSON dot dumps.

31
00:02:37,330 --> 00:02:46,300
So this is the method that will convert my data into a JSON object and then I'm going to call the normal

32
00:02:46,300 --> 00:02:48,070
send method that we always use.

33
00:02:48,070 --> 00:02:56,140
So I'm going to do self dot connection, dot send and we're going to send the JSON data.

34
00:02:57,640 --> 00:02:58,330
And that's it.

35
00:02:58,330 --> 00:02:59,530
This method is done.

36
00:02:59,980 --> 00:03:02,110
So as you can see, it's very simple.

37
00:03:02,140 --> 00:03:08,740
All we're doing is we're calling a method called dumps, which is implemented in the JSON class, which

38
00:03:08,740 --> 00:03:13,300
is we're importing in here, we're passing the data to it.

39
00:03:13,450 --> 00:03:20,740
So all of this is going to convert the data that we are getting as an input into a JSON object.

40
00:03:21,250 --> 00:03:27,790
We're starting this in a variable called JSON data, and then we're sending this variable using the

41
00:03:27,790 --> 00:03:30,430
normal send method that we always use.

42
00:03:31,270 --> 00:03:38,710
Now because we're sending this data as a JSON object, our receiver method needs to be able to expect

43
00:03:38,710 --> 00:03:42,880
this and unwrap this to read the data that's inside it.

44
00:03:43,510 --> 00:03:46,800
So it's very similar to what we're doing here.

45
00:03:46,810 --> 00:03:53,200
First of all, we put all of the data in our package or in our JSON object.

46
00:03:53,440 --> 00:03:57,340
We're going to transfer this object through the TCP stream.

47
00:03:57,640 --> 00:04:00,640
And this will be received in our received method.

48
00:04:00,640 --> 00:04:07,840
So we need to program this method in a way that it's able to open this package and read whatever data

49
00:04:07,840 --> 00:04:09,160
that's inside it.

50
00:04:10,350 --> 00:04:13,740
So we'll call this method reliable receive.

51
00:04:15,340 --> 00:04:21,940
And the first thing that this method will do is it's going to try to receive the object itself before

52
00:04:21,940 --> 00:04:23,170
even unwrapping it.

53
00:04:23,380 --> 00:04:26,770
So we're going to use the normal socket receive method.

54
00:04:27,310 --> 00:04:30,670
So I'm going to say my JSON data, this is still JSON.

55
00:04:30,670 --> 00:04:32,950
We still don't know what's inside it.

56
00:04:33,040 --> 00:04:40,120
This is going to be equal to my self, that connection that I receive.

57
00:04:40,690 --> 00:04:44,620
And we're only going to receive 1024 bytes.

58
00:04:45,430 --> 00:04:53,380
And once we receive this, we're going to be able to convert it into whatever object that it was by

59
00:04:53,380 --> 00:04:56,860
calling the load SX method in the JSON class.

60
00:04:57,070 --> 00:05:00,940
And we'll pass the JSON data to it.

61
00:05:02,040 --> 00:05:08,400
And this will basically unwrap whatever data that's sent as the JSON object and return it to me.

62
00:05:08,430 --> 00:05:14,970
And since this is a received method, I'm going to return this when the method finishes execution.

63
00:05:15,840 --> 00:05:19,080
So again, a very simple two line method.

64
00:05:19,110 --> 00:05:25,110
All we're doing is, first of all, we're receiving the data and we're storing it in a variable called

65
00:05:25,110 --> 00:05:26,340
JSON data.

66
00:05:26,760 --> 00:05:31,860
But this data is still in JSON format because we sent it in JSON format.

67
00:05:32,040 --> 00:05:37,950
So we have to unwrap this and we're doing this by calling the load SE method, which is implemented

68
00:05:37,950 --> 00:05:43,710
in the JSON class, which we imported here and we're passing the JSON data to it.

69
00:05:43,740 --> 00:05:47,010
This will return the original data.

70
00:05:47,010 --> 00:05:53,010
So this will basically do the packaging opening in this package and giving us whatever data that's in

71
00:05:53,010 --> 00:05:53,400
it.

72
00:05:54,180 --> 00:05:54,930
Now that's it.

73
00:05:54,930 --> 00:06:02,220
The methods are done and all we have to do right now is use them wherever we send or receive data in

74
00:06:02,220 --> 00:06:05,160
our listener and in our backdoor.

75
00:06:05,670 --> 00:06:08,370
So we usually send data in here.

76
00:06:08,370 --> 00:06:11,520
So this is where we call self that connection send.

77
00:06:11,640 --> 00:06:17,220
And instead of doing this, we're going to call self dot reliable send.

78
00:06:18,570 --> 00:06:24,450
And when we receive data in here, we're going to call self dot reliable receive.

79
00:06:25,550 --> 00:06:33,020
And we don't need to put this anymore because the reliable receiver don't take any input and that's

80
00:06:33,020 --> 00:06:33,150
it.

81
00:06:33,170 --> 00:06:39,200
Now our listener is ready to use our reliable send and receive methods.

82
00:06:40,130 --> 00:06:45,220
The next thing that we need to do is use this in our backdoor as well.

83
00:06:45,230 --> 00:06:51,200
So I'm going to copy both of these methods and I'm going to paste them in my backdoor code.

84
00:06:53,270 --> 00:06:58,730
And we need to make sure, again, to use these methods whenever we're sending and receiving data.

85
00:06:58,790 --> 00:07:07,880
So I usually receive data in here, so I'm going to call self dot, reliable receive and again remove

86
00:07:07,880 --> 00:07:09,110
this from here.

87
00:07:09,380 --> 00:07:14,390
And when we send data, we're going to call self dot reliable send.

88
00:07:15,440 --> 00:07:17,450
One more thing we need to do.

89
00:07:17,540 --> 00:07:23,000
As you know, these methods use the JSON library, so we need to import it in here.

90
00:07:23,330 --> 00:07:29,510
So I'm going to do import JSON and we're all set.

91
00:07:29,840 --> 00:07:35,690
Now, there is still one issue with this code, but I don't want to show it to you now.

92
00:07:35,960 --> 00:07:40,460
I want to highlight this issue once we go and test this code.

93
00:07:40,700 --> 00:07:47,690
So in the next lecture we'll test these reliable send and receive methods, fix any issues with them,

94
00:07:47,690 --> 00:07:54,710
and make sure they can be used to transfer large amounts of data and data structures reliably.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,180 --> 00:00:07,110
So now that we implemented reliable send and receive methods, let's test them and see if they work

2
00:00:07,110 --> 00:00:12,510
properly and allow us to send and receive large amounts of data.

3
00:00:12,690 --> 00:00:17,460
So I'm going to go down and run the listener.

4
00:00:18,460 --> 00:00:23,320
And I'm going to come here and run my back door.

5
00:00:23,710 --> 00:00:27,760
Looks like we actually have a proper connection, so that's good.

6
00:00:27,910 --> 00:00:31,440
Now, let's try to run a normal command that used to work before.

7
00:00:31,450 --> 00:00:35,350
So something that doesn't send or receive a big message.

8
00:00:35,350 --> 00:00:38,530
So something like the dare that works.

9
00:00:38,530 --> 00:00:39,070
Perfect.

10
00:00:39,070 --> 00:00:39,940
That's fine.

11
00:00:40,150 --> 00:00:48,670
Now let's go ahead and try to read a big file like this, sample the text and see if we're able to read

12
00:00:48,670 --> 00:00:50,140
the full file this time.

13
00:00:50,590 --> 00:00:55,330
So again, we're going to do more sample text.

14
00:00:56,500 --> 00:00:58,720
And our code crashed.

15
00:00:59,230 --> 00:01:04,600
Now, if we look at the error code, the main error that we're getting is a value error.

16
00:01:05,290 --> 00:01:13,810
And basically what it's telling us that the message or whatever that we are receiving is an terminated

17
00:01:13,810 --> 00:01:14,540
string.

18
00:01:14,560 --> 00:01:21,460
So basically when we were receiving the package in here, we were still at this point, we couldn't

19
00:01:21,460 --> 00:01:23,320
receive the full package.

20
00:01:23,320 --> 00:01:26,710
We only received part of the package.

21
00:01:26,830 --> 00:01:34,990
And therefore you can see that it's telling us this is happening on line 21 where we are trying to unwrap

22
00:01:34,990 --> 00:01:40,660
whatever that we received and converting it back to its original text.

23
00:01:40,960 --> 00:01:47,020
So basically what's happening is we still don't have the full package, but we are trying to open it

24
00:01:47,020 --> 00:01:48,910
and read what's inside it.

25
00:01:49,030 --> 00:01:51,730
That's why we're getting an error here.

26
00:01:51,730 --> 00:01:54,640
It's telling us that this is a value error.

27
00:01:55,430 --> 00:02:02,900
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to go to the line that is telling us, which is line 21, which

28
00:02:02,900 --> 00:02:04,010
is right here.

29
00:02:04,010 --> 00:02:11,360
And again, this is where we are trying to unwrap the JSON data, which is whatever we are receiving.

30
00:02:11,990 --> 00:02:20,450
Now, since what we're receiving is larger than 1024 bytes, the JSON data is still not complete.

31
00:02:20,450 --> 00:02:28,040
And because this is a well defined way of transferring data, when we're calling the load as method,

32
00:02:28,130 --> 00:02:34,550
it knows that this data, whatever we're passing to it, whatever we're passing in here is still not

33
00:02:34,550 --> 00:02:43,340
complete and therefore it's not able to convert it back to its original message or to its original structure.

34
00:02:43,940 --> 00:02:53,690
So what we need to do is we need to keep receiving data until the load method is able to convert this

35
00:02:53,690 --> 00:02:57,770
message or this data back to its original structure.

36
00:02:58,640 --> 00:03:04,040
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to create an infinite loop by doing while true.

37
00:03:05,430 --> 00:03:10,170
And in this infinite loop, I want to try.

38
00:03:11,150 --> 00:03:12,920
To receive data.

39
00:03:13,130 --> 00:03:20,180
Once I receive the data, I want to convert this JSON data back to its original structure using this

40
00:03:20,180 --> 00:03:20,750
line.

41
00:03:21,260 --> 00:03:27,790
And if I get an error, so I'm going to accept a value error, which is the error that we were getting.

42
00:03:27,800 --> 00:03:32,280
If we get this error, that means that we need to receive more data.

43
00:03:32,300 --> 00:03:34,280
So I'm going to do continue.

44
00:03:34,610 --> 00:03:41,270
This will bring me back to the top of the loop where we're going to try to receive more JSON data.

45
00:03:41,910 --> 00:03:48,900
Now I also need to modify this because the way that this loophole works, every time we enter this loop,

46
00:03:48,900 --> 00:03:54,180
we're going to overwrite whatever is stored in the JSON data variable.

47
00:03:54,690 --> 00:04:02,070
So all I'm going to say is I'm going to say the JSON data is equal to whatever is currently stored in

48
00:04:02,070 --> 00:04:03,330
the JSON data.

49
00:04:03,690 --> 00:04:12,840
Plus add 1024 bytes for me from the TCP stream to my existing JSON data.

50
00:04:13,890 --> 00:04:18,940
Then I want you to try to convert this back to its original structure.

51
00:04:18,960 --> 00:04:22,620
If you get an error, then go back to the start of the loop.

52
00:04:23,190 --> 00:04:29,340
Now, since this is an infinite loop and we're using this variable inside it and we're making this variable

53
00:04:29,340 --> 00:04:36,330
is equal to itself, we have to initialize this variable to some value for the first time the loop runs.

54
00:04:36,690 --> 00:04:42,330
So I'm just going to say my JSON data is equal to nothing at the start.

55
00:04:42,960 --> 00:04:51,960
Then every time the loop runs, it's going to add 1024 bytes from the TCP stream to the JSON data,

56
00:04:51,960 --> 00:04:54,840
try to convert it to its original structure.

57
00:04:54,900 --> 00:04:57,060
If this works, we're going to return it.

58
00:04:57,090 --> 00:04:59,790
Otherwise, we'll add more data to it.

59
00:05:00,780 --> 00:05:01,260
Now.

60
00:05:01,260 --> 00:05:06,390
I'll also need to use this code inside my backdoor and the reliable receiver.

61
00:05:06,420 --> 00:05:12,330
So I'm going to copy this and we'll paste it in here.

62
00:05:13,440 --> 00:05:16,020
And now this should all be good.

63
00:05:16,020 --> 00:05:18,060
Let's go and test the code again.

64
00:05:18,060 --> 00:05:20,280
So I'll clear this.

65
00:05:20,970 --> 00:05:29,860
Run my program and run the backdoor and let's try to run a normal command like there.

66
00:05:29,880 --> 00:05:31,920
And you can see that this is working.

67
00:05:31,920 --> 00:05:37,470
And let's run more sample text to read a large file.

68
00:05:37,680 --> 00:05:39,930
Hit enter and perfect.

69
00:05:39,930 --> 00:05:43,650
As you can see, now we're able to read the full file.

70
00:05:44,430 --> 00:05:52,260
So now we have a reliable way to transfer data between the server and the client, between the backdoor

71
00:05:52,260 --> 00:05:53,340
and the listener.

72
00:05:53,340 --> 00:05:58,770
And this will allow us to reliably extend and build on our backdoor.

73
00:05:59,130 --> 00:06:03,870
Now, again, you'll actually have to implement this or something similar to this.

74
00:06:03,870 --> 00:06:10,710
Whenever you want to transfer larger amounts of data between your client and your server, regardless

75
00:06:10,710 --> 00:06:12,520
of what program you're writing.

76
00:06:12,540 --> 00:06:19,200
As long as your program is using sockets and TCP streams in order to transfer data.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:01,320
Okay.

2
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:07,950
Now that we built a strong base for our backdoor and we know that we can use it to send and receive

3
00:00:07,950 --> 00:00:09,360
data reliably.

4
00:00:09,450 --> 00:00:15,210
Let's go ahead and start implementing cool and useful features in this backdoor.

5
00:00:16,110 --> 00:00:21,930
Now, right now, the backdoor already allows us to execute system commands, and what we want to be

6
00:00:21,930 --> 00:00:25,950
able to do is, first of all, access the file systems.

7
00:00:25,950 --> 00:00:30,270
So navigate between the directories of the computer.

8
00:00:30,510 --> 00:00:34,890
I also want to be able to upload files and download files.

9
00:00:35,490 --> 00:00:39,990
Now, it's always a good idea to think before we start programming.

10
00:00:40,020 --> 00:00:47,700
So if you look at all of the commands that we want to implement, they all take some sort of an argument

11
00:00:47,700 --> 00:00:48,730
or option.

12
00:00:48,780 --> 00:00:52,260
So when we want to use the seed, we're given the directory name.

13
00:00:52,260 --> 00:00:55,320
When we want to upload the file, we're given the file name.

14
00:00:55,320 --> 00:00:59,190
And when we want to download the file, we're downloading the file name.

15
00:00:59,820 --> 00:01:06,810
Also, probably if you want to add more features, chances are you'll want your features or your commands

16
00:01:06,810 --> 00:01:11,400
to be able to take arguments or to send arguments to the target.

17
00:01:11,940 --> 00:01:18,660
Right now we send everything as a string, but it will actually make sense to split the string or the

18
00:01:18,660 --> 00:01:26,250
command that the user inputs into a list so that the first element of the list is going to be the command.

19
00:01:26,250 --> 00:01:30,390
And the second element is going to be the first argument.

20
00:01:30,390 --> 00:01:35,550
The third element, if there is a third element, will be the third argument and so on.

21
00:01:36,360 --> 00:01:43,170
And since we built a strong base, since our send and receive methods, use JSON, we can actually send

22
00:01:43,170 --> 00:01:46,170
lists to and from the backdoor.

23
00:01:46,950 --> 00:01:54,030
So what I want to do in here, I'm taking the command from my raw input and my command right now is

24
00:01:54,030 --> 00:01:55,470
literally a string.

25
00:01:56,040 --> 00:02:04,920
So what I'm going to do, I'm going to say my command is equal to the command dot split and I'm going

26
00:02:04,920 --> 00:02:07,350
to split this based on spaces.

27
00:02:07,350 --> 00:02:15,630
So the string is going to be converted into a list in which each element is a word in this string.

28
00:02:16,200 --> 00:02:17,730
So let me show you an example.

29
00:02:17,730 --> 00:02:20,550
Before we actually use this, I'm going to do a print command.

30
00:02:21,960 --> 00:02:24,650
And we actually won't send or receive anything.

31
00:02:24,660 --> 00:02:27,540
Let's just see what's this going to look like.

32
00:02:27,720 --> 00:02:32,100
So I'm just going to comment this and I'm going to run my backdoor.

33
00:02:33,030 --> 00:02:35,370
And let's run the backdoor from here.

34
00:02:36,470 --> 00:02:38,060
We have a connection now.

35
00:02:38,060 --> 00:02:43,100
The commands that I'm going to run will not get executed because I commented the code that sends and

36
00:02:43,100 --> 00:02:44,300
receive the data.

37
00:02:44,450 --> 00:02:48,330
But what I want to do is just show you what the commands are going to look like.

38
00:02:48,350 --> 00:02:52,400
So let's say, for example, I'm doing see the test.

39
00:02:53,120 --> 00:02:58,130
As you can see, CD is the first element and test is the second element.

40
00:02:58,700 --> 00:03:00,050
So let's do another command.

41
00:03:00,050 --> 00:03:01,070
Let's just run.

42
00:03:01,070 --> 00:03:03,710
This is a sample.

43
00:03:04,830 --> 00:03:12,510
And again, as you can see, this is being separated into a list in which each word is an element.

44
00:03:12,510 --> 00:03:18,990
So we can access the first element or the first word by typing command square bracket zero.

45
00:03:19,320 --> 00:03:24,030
Second element is command square bracket one and so on.

46
00:03:24,720 --> 00:03:29,330
Now, we spoke about lists before, so I don't want to spend too much time on this.

47
00:03:29,340 --> 00:03:36,090
And basically the main reason I'm doing this so that I can separate my command from its arguments.

48
00:03:36,870 --> 00:03:44,490
So as an example, let's implement a very simple but important command, which is an exit command that

49
00:03:44,490 --> 00:03:47,640
will allow us to properly quit the backdoor.

50
00:03:47,880 --> 00:03:52,110
You can see right now, when I quit the backdoor, I type control C in here.

51
00:03:52,170 --> 00:03:55,050
This sometimes leaves the backdoor open in here.

52
00:03:55,050 --> 00:04:00,450
As you can see, the socket is still open and we have to come here and do control C as well.

53
00:04:00,870 --> 00:04:06,780
So it will be a good idea to actually implement a command that if I type it, it will properly close

54
00:04:06,780 --> 00:04:11,880
the socket both at the listener and at the client or at the backdoor.

55
00:04:12,720 --> 00:04:17,580
So what I'm going to do in here, we don't need to print the command anymore.

56
00:04:17,580 --> 00:04:21,210
So we're going to remove that and I'm going to uncomment these lines.

57
00:04:22,330 --> 00:04:25,990
And what I'm going to do in my execute remotely method.

58
00:04:26,720 --> 00:04:28,910
This has already taken the command.

59
00:04:29,360 --> 00:04:36,410
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to check if the command square brackets zero.

60
00:04:36,410 --> 00:04:45,740
So we're checking if the first element, which is this element of the command, is equal to exit.

61
00:04:47,110 --> 00:04:51,400
Then we want to, first of all, close our connection.

62
00:04:51,400 --> 00:04:55,300
So we're going to do solve that connection.

63

1
00:00:00,760 --> 00:00:07,690
Now the next feature that I'd like to add to my back door is to be able to download files from the hacked

2
00:00:07,690 --> 00:00:10,570
computer into my own computer.

3
00:00:11,200 --> 00:00:18,280
Now again, the method that I'm going to show you can be used to transfer files between two computers

4
00:00:18,280 --> 00:00:20,740
using circuits using Python.

5
00:00:20,770 --> 00:00:27,310
It doesn't have to be a backdoor, so you can use it regardless of what your program is, as long as

6
00:00:27,310 --> 00:00:30,580
you need to transfer files between two computers.

7
00:00:31,300 --> 00:00:38,800
So keeping this generic, let's try to think what happens when a file is transferred on a system.

8
00:00:38,800 --> 00:00:41,910
So let's say you went to a file on your own computer.

9
00:00:41,920 --> 00:00:47,570
You press right click copy, and then you go to a different place and press right click paste.

10
00:00:47,590 --> 00:00:48,880
What happens?

11
00:00:49,090 --> 00:00:53,860
Basically, there is no such thing on the system as copy and paste.

12
00:00:54,550 --> 00:01:02,710
What happens is your system is going to read the file as a sequence of characters each file, even if

13
00:01:02,710 --> 00:01:07,870
it's an image or a song, it is basically a sequence of characters.

14
00:01:08,590 --> 00:01:16,240
So your system is going to read this file, and then when you press paste on your target location on

15
00:01:16,240 --> 00:01:23,140
the next place where you want to paste this file, it's going to create an empty file and then put the

16
00:01:23,140 --> 00:01:26,470
sequence of characters inside this new file.

17
00:01:27,100 --> 00:01:34,840
So if we want to implement a way to download the file from this Windows machine, then we need to,

18
00:01:34,840 --> 00:01:42,310
first of all, read this file in here through our backdoor, send it through our reliable send method,

19
00:01:42,310 --> 00:01:49,150
receive it in here, and then create an empty file and put the contents inside our new file.

20
00:01:49,990 --> 00:01:55,810
Now, this might sound a little bit long and complicated, but you'll see that this is very, very easy,

21
00:01:55,810 --> 00:02:00,160
especially that we already structured our program really, really well.

22
00:02:01,120 --> 00:02:06,910
So the first step is going to be reading the file from our backdoor.

00:02:07,530 --> 00:02:13,350
So I'm going to implement a method and I'm going to call this method read file.

24
00:02:14,820 --> 00:02:20,160
And this will take self and the file path to read as inputs.

00:02:20,940 --> 00:02:28,680
And I actually showed you before how to write a file in the download and execute payload, but we never

26
00:02:28,680 --> 00:02:30,600

So the first thing that we need to do is open this file.

00:02:40,440 --> 00:02:47,310
And just like we did in the download and execute, we can use the with keyword in order to open this

30
00:02:47,310 --> 00:02:47,940
file.

31
00:02:48,690 --> 00:02:56,640
So I'm going to say with open and the first argument is going to be the path to the file to open.

32
00:02:56,880 --> 00:03:00,750
So this is going to be the path that we get as an input.

33
00:03:00,990 --> 00:03:06,480
And then we're going to specify the method that we want to open this file as.

34
00:03:06,600 --> 00:03:12,210
So for example, when we were writing, we specified that we want to write this file.

35
00:03:12,750 --> 00:03:16,320
Now what we want to do, we want to read this file.

36
00:03:16,320 --> 00:03:20,880
So we're going to put our as the method of opening this file.

37
00:03:21,330 --> 00:03:28,740
And I want to treat all the files as binary files, and therefore I'm going to add a B to the method.

38
00:03:29,160 --> 00:03:35,460
Now you can use just or if you just want to read normal text files, but if you add B, we'll be able

39
00:03:35,460 --> 00:03:37,830
to read files as binaries.

40
00:03:37,830 --> 00:03:45,120
So this will allow us to transfer normal files such as images and all of that and other text files.

41
00:03:46,540 --> 00:03:51,700
Then we're going to say we want to open this file as file.

42
00:03:51,850 --> 00:03:58,060
So this is the variable that we're going to use to reference the file that we opened.

43
00:03:59,080 --> 00:04:06,850
And everything that we need to do while this file is open needs to be implemented within this block

44
00:04:06,850 --> 00:04:08,170
of code in here.

45
00:04:08,680 --> 00:04:11,940
So we don't really need to do anything complicated.

46
00:04:11,950 --> 00:04:17,260
We just want to read this file so we can read the file by doing file.

47
00:04:17,350 --> 00:04:18,520
Dot read.

48
00:04:19,400 --> 00:04:19,930
File.

49
00:04:19,970 --> 00:04:26,540
Being the name that we use to reference the file that we just opened and then dot read is a method in

50
00:04:26,540 --> 00:04:28,970
order to read the contents of the file.

51
00:04:29,750 --> 00:04:35,440
And what I want to do is I want to actually return the contents of this file.

52
00:04:35,450 --> 00:04:38,510
So we're going to say, I want to return the file.

53
00:04:38,510 --> 00:04:39,380
Don't read.

54
00:04:39,890 --> 00:04:46,370
Now I've actually covered the width syntax in greater detail when we were programming the download and

55
00:04:46,370 --> 00:04:47,710

56

So if this is still a little bit vague for you, then please go back and revise it there.

00:00:56,030 --> 00:01:06,260
And the main issue is we're being told that Python or the UTF eight codec cannot decode a special character.

15
00:01:07,160 --> 00:01:11,390
Basically what's happening is we have the image right here.

16
00:01:11,720 --> 00:01:17,300
But if we open this image as text, which is what our program is trying to do.

00:01:17,660 --> 00:01:25,160

18
00:01:25,820 --> 00:01:30,170
So it's in C users.

19
00:01:30,940 --> 00:01:31,980
Z.

20
00:01:32,590 --> 00:01:35,440
Python projects.

21
00:01:35,860 --> 00:01:38,800
And we have to set this to all files.

22
00:01:39,650 --> 00:01:42,380

00:01:44,540 --> 00:01:45,350

24
00:01:45,350 --> 00:01:51,800
Like I said, it's just a series of characters, but a lot of these characters are gibberish.

25
00:01:52,400 --> 00:01:58,910
Now, the problem is, as you can see in here, it's telling us that one of these characters, we're

26
00:01:58,910 --> 00:02:02,090
not sure which one it is, cannot be decoded.

27
00:02:02,090 --> 00:02:08,270
So Python or JSON is not able to understand what this character is.

28
00:02:08,960 --> 00:02:17,420
So in order to solve this issue, I'm going to encode or encapsulate all of the stuff that I want to

29
00:02:17,420 --> 00:02:22,010
send using Bayes 64, which is a known encoding.

30
00:02:22,160 --> 00:02:30,140
And then when I receive it in here, I'm going to unwrap it again using the same encoding.

31
00:02:30,680 --> 00:02:39,770
Now, when characters are converted to base 64, Bayes 64 only use known characters and therefore it

32
00:02:39,770 --> 00:02:43,880
will not cause this exception or this issue right here.

33
00:02:44,330 --> 00:02:47,690
So let me put this in practice and you'll see what I mean.

34
00:02:48,620 --> 00:02:50,690
First of all, I'm going to do control.

35
00:02:50,690 --> 00:02:52,460
See in here to quit this.

36
00:02:54,040 --> 00:03:02,890
And because we want to use the base 64 library, we're going to have to import it by doing import base

37
00:03:02,890 --> 00:03:04,120
64.

38
00:03:04,900 --> 00:03:11,030
And then whenever we're reading the file, what I'm going to do instead of returning the file, don't

39
00:03:11,050 --> 00:03:11,680
read.

40
00:03:11,800 --> 00:03:25,210
I'm going to do base 64 dot B 64 in code and we're going to encode whatever the contents of the file.

41
00:03:26,050 --> 00:03:33,490
So if this content contains unknown characters, this function will actually convert all of these unknown

42
00:03:33,490 --> 00:03:36,310
characters to known characters.

43
00:03:36,910 --> 00:03:40,810
These characters then will be sent to my listener.

44
00:03:41,920 --> 00:03:49,510
But before I could write this, I will have to convert this back to these unknown characters so that

45
00:03:49,510 --> 00:03:52,840
the file would behave just like we expect.

46
00:03:53,530 --> 00:03:57,400
So I'm going to have to import base 64 here as well.

47
00:03:58,890 --> 00:04:07,260
And before we write the content to the file, we're going to call base 64 dot B 64.

48
00:04:07,290 --> 00:04:08,700
Decode this time.

49
00:04:08,700 --> 00:04:16,560
So the first time we encoded and the second time we're decoding the file or the contents before we write

50
00:04:16,560 --> 00:04:17,700
them to the file.

51
00:04:18,360 --> 00:04:19,230
And that's it.

52
00:04:19,230 --> 00:04:20,240
This should work.

53
00:04:20,250 --> 00:04:28,380
So basically all we're doing is we are using a base 64 encoding in here before we send the file.

54
00:04:28,830 --> 00:04:36,360
And then when we write it, we are decoding the file before we write the content to the file.

55
00:04:37,230 --> 00:04:44,490
This means that if the file contains any unknown characters, they will be encapsulated or converted

56
00:04:44,490 --> 00:04:52,020
to a base 64 to known characters, and then we'll convert it back to its original unknown characters

57
00:04:52,020 --> 00:04:58,560
before we write them to the file, so that when we go and double click this binary file, the file will

58

62

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,960 --> 00:00:01,380
Okay.

2
00:00:01,380 --> 00:00:06,720
Now, the actor that we wrote so far is really good and it's working as expected.

3
00:00:07,290 --> 00:00:15,600
The only problem is it might crash in some instances, but the main reason why it might crash is because

4
00:00:15,600 --> 00:00:16,670
of a misuse.

5
00:00:16,680 --> 00:00:24,900
So it's either because the user would input an incorrect command or they would use a command incorrectly.

6
00:00:25,170 --> 00:00:31,590
Now, usually when a program crashes, it actually gives us useful information why it crashed, and

7
00:00:31,590 --> 00:00:37,920
sometimes we use a try and accept block in order to do something when that exception happens, like

8
00:00:37,920 --> 00:00:39,120
I showed you before.

9
00:00:39,120 --> 00:00:45,300
And sometimes we ignore it because we want the user to see what happens or why their program crashed

10
00:00:45,300 --> 00:00:47,670
so that they can overcome the issue.

11
00:00:48,660 --> 00:00:55,500
Now, in our case, because we are establishing a connection between two computers when the listener

12
00:00:55,500 --> 00:01:02,400
or the back door crash, we will lose the connection, which is something that is really, really bad

13
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:06,960
because you might have done a lot of work in order to obtain this connection.

14
00:01:06,960 --> 00:01:13,350
And then you misspell a command or use a command incorrectly, and then you lose your connection and

15
00:01:13,350 --> 00:01:19,080
you won't be able to connect to your user again unless you social engineer them again or run your attack

16
00:01:19,080 --> 00:01:19,650
again.

17
00:01:20,340 --> 00:01:24,960
Now, let me show you an example of the user misusing the backdoor.

18
00:01:25,080 --> 00:01:30,120
So I'm going to start my backdoor as usual, and we'll start it from here.

19
00:01:31,560 --> 00:01:38,700

20
00:01:38,700 --> 00:01:40,410
put an extra orange here.

21
00:01:40,410 --> 00:01:43,020
So you type the command incorrectly.

22
00:01:43,290 --> 00:01:45,120
Now, if I had enter, you'll see.

23
00:01:45,120 --> 00:01:46,440
I'll get stuck here.

24
00:01:46,440 --> 00:01:48,990
And then if we look in here, you'll see.

25
00:01:48,990 --> 00:01:52,980
We get an exception that you use an incorrect command.

26
00:01:53,950 --> 00:01:55,900
Now let me show you another example.

27
00:01:55,900 --> 00:01:57,520
So let's run this again.

28
00:01:59,030 --> 00:02:06,170
And let's say you wanted to navigate into directory called Test 44.

29
00:02:06,770 --> 00:02:13,520
Now in this in Python projects directory, there is nothing that's called Test 44.

30
00:02:13,550 --> 00:02:17,390
Therefore now if I had enter again, we'll get stuck.

31
00:02:17,390 --> 00:02:23,360
And if we look in here, you'll say that the system is saying there is nothing called test 44.

32
00:02:23,600 --> 00:02:27,680
But the main issue is we are losing our connection.

33
00:02:27,950 --> 00:02:34,790
And the same would happen if you try to download or upload a non-existing file and the same would happen

34
00:02:34,790 --> 00:02:39,470
if you implement more features and use these features incorrectly.

35
00:02:40,400 --> 00:02:44,540
So what you could do is you could look at each exception we're getting.

36
00:02:44,540 --> 00:02:50,480
For example, right here, you're getting a Windows error and right here you're getting a sub processed

37
00:02:50,480 --> 00:02:52,670
dot called process error.

38
00:02:52,970 --> 00:02:57,410
And you can just come in here, go to the method that's causing this error.

39
00:02:57,410 --> 00:03:06,950
For example, for the execute system command, you would do try this and then you'd want to accept.

40
00:03:09,580 --> 00:03:13,570
This era, the sub process that called process error.

41
00:03:16,640 --> 00:03:23,150
And then when this happens, you may be able to return error during command execution.

42
00:03:24,370 --> 00:03:30,400
Then you can go ahead do the same with the download and with the upload again caused the error first.

43
00:03:30,400 --> 00:03:36,760
See what error you're getting in in the terminal, what exception you're getting, and then catch this

44
00:03:36,760 --> 00:03:41,140
exception with a try and accept and then print an appropriate error.

45
00:03:41,890 --> 00:03:48,520
Now this will be a great solution if you're writing a normal program because it will actually give you

46
00:03:48,520 --> 00:03:51,070
good information why the program crashed.

47
00:03:51,640 --> 00:03:58,840
The problem, if we use the solution in our backdoor is the main thing with our backdoor is that we

48
00:03:58,840 --> 00:04:02,950
want to maintain our connection regardless of what's happening.

49
00:04:03,250 --> 00:04:07,660
So this solution will allow us to catch the exceptions that we know.

50
00:04:07,660 --> 00:04:11,170
It'll allow us to catch an exception if the command is wrong.

51
00:04:11,200 --> 00:04:17,200
If a user tries to download the non-existing file, if the user tries to navigate to a non-existing

52
00:04:17,200 --> 00:04:22,780
location and that's it, it won't allow us to catch exceptions that we don't know.

53
00:04:22,780 --> 00:04:29,260
For example, maybe when you're using this in a real life scenario, you would get an error that I don't

54
00:04:29,260 --> 00:04:31,390
know that I haven't covered right here.

55
00:04:31,690 --> 00:04:34,390
This will cause your backdoor to crash.

56
00:04:34,390 --> 00:04:37,090
Maybe after that you'll know this error and fix it.

57
00:04:37,090 --> 00:04:40,360
But it will cause you to lose that connection.

58
00:04:41,050 --> 00:04:45,520
So an alternative method of catching all of these exceptions.

59
00:04:45,520 --> 00:04:47,710

00:04:49,120 --> 00:04:50,650

62
00:04:57,070 --> 00:05:02,420

63
00:05:02,420 --> 00:05:09,850

64

get.

So we're going to accept exception.

66
00:05:14,650 --> 00:05:20,830
So basically we're saying I want to accept any type of error, anything that could happen, anything

67
00:05:20,830 --> 00:05:23,880
that could be caused within this block of code.

68
00:05:23,890 --> 00:05:25,570
I want to accept that.

69
00:05:25,630 --> 00:05:28,330
And we're going to set the command result.

70
00:05:29,390 --> 00:05:34,580
Two error during command execution.

71
00:05:36,410 --> 00:05:41,480
So what's going to happen is now, first of all, the backdoor is going to get a command.

72
00:05:41,510 --> 00:05:45,890
It will pass this command and execute an appropriate function.

73
00:05:46,160 --> 00:05:53,150
If an error happens within this function, regardless of what error it is, this will be cut in here

74
00:05:53,150 --> 00:05:54,350
in this exception.

75
00:05:54,710 --> 00:06:00,200
We're going to set the command result to this message and then we'll send the command result to the

76
00:06:00,200 --> 00:06:00,860
listener.

77
00:06:01,460 --> 00:06:05,000
The listener is going to receive this command result.

78
00:06:05,600 --> 00:06:12,950
It's going to check if the user requested the download command, then it'll write this to a file, otherwise

79
00:06:12,950 --> 00:06:14,570
it will print this on screen.

80
00:06:15,140 --> 00:06:21,740
Now, if we received a result that says error, we actually don't want to write that to a file because

81
00:06:21,740 --> 00:06:24,350
this means that we could not download the file.

82
00:06:24,920 --> 00:06:27,950
So I'm going to add one more condition to this statement.

83
00:06:27,950 --> 00:06:39,950
So we're going to say if the command is download and error is not in my result, so I'm only going to

84
00:06:39,950 --> 00:06:46,970
write stuff to my disk if the command is download and error is not in my result.

85
00:06:47,090 --> 00:06:52,280
If error is in my result, I'm simply going to print this error on screen.

86
00:06:53,210 --> 00:07:00,350
Now, what I'm also going to do is I'm going to wrap all of this in a try.

87
00:07:00,500 --> 00:07:03,470
So I'm going to say, try to do all of this for me.

88
00:07:04,010 --> 00:07:09,710
Then if anything happens, I want you to accept exception.

89
00:07:11,140 --> 00:07:20,590
And if this happens, I'm going to set the result to again, error during command execution.

90
00:07:22,530 --> 00:07:29,010
Then the result will be printed on screen and the person using the listener, the hacker will be able

91
00:07:29,010 --> 00:07:31,980
to know that something went wrong with their command.

92
00:07:31,980 --> 00:07:37,350
And since this person is a hacker and they should know what they're doing, they should be able to know

93
00:07:37,350 --> 00:07:38,580
why this went wrong.

94
00:07:38,580 --> 00:07:44,250
So if they misspell the command or use the command incorrectly, they should be able to notice this

95
00:07:44,250 --> 00:07:49,920
and should not need the system to tell them that they misspelled the command or if they made a simple

96
00:07:49,920 --> 00:07:51,180
mistake like that.

97
00:07:52,110 --> 00:07:53,760
So let's test this out.

98
00:07:53,760 --> 00:07:55,770
So we're going to clear the screen.

99
00:07:55,920 --> 00:07:57,570
We're going to run the backdoor.

100
00:07:59,040 --> 00:08:01,200
And run it from here.

101
00:08:03,790 --> 00:08:06,250
So let's try a normal command like there.

102
00:08:07,670 --> 00:08:10,040
And as you can see, this is working fine.

103
00:08:10,070 --> 00:08:12,830
Now, let's try a command that doesn't exist.

104
00:08:14,130 --> 00:08:19,170
And as you can see, we're getting an error telling us that this command that there was an error during

105
00:08:19,170 --> 00:08:26,550
the command execution and I misspelled command there, but I did not lose my connection, which is very,

106
00:08:26,550 --> 00:08:27,560
very important.

107
00:08:27,570 --> 00:08:34,290
So now as a hacker, I should be able to know that I misspelled this command and fix it, or I can just

108
00:08:34,290 --> 00:08:35,250
try another command.

109
00:08:35,250 --> 00:08:42,900
So if I just do IP config, you can see that I still have my connection and I'm still able to run commands

110
00:08:42,900 --> 00:08:43,920
on the target.

111
00:08:44,730 --> 00:08:47,730
Now let's try to download the file that doesn't exist.

112
00:08:47,730 --> 00:08:51,720
So let's say I do download la la la.

113
00:08:52,470 --> 00:08:59,040
You'll see, I'll get an error, but I won't lose my connection again if I try to upload something that

114
00:08:59,040 --> 00:09:00,090
doesn't exist.

115
00:09:01,020 --> 00:09:01,860
Same thing.

116
00:09:01,860 --> 00:09:08,220
I'll get an error, but I'm still able to execute system commands and communicate with my back door

117
00:09:08,220 --> 00:09:10,500
and use all of its functions.

118
00:09:10,560 --> 00:09:16,440
Not only that, but now, even if there are any other errors that I couldn't think of and you can think

119
00:09:16,440 --> 00:09:23,280
of, even if they happen during the communication between your backdoor and your client, you will see

120
00:09:23,280 --> 00:09:28,290
an error coming up on screen, but you will not lose the connection the system will catch.

121
00:09:28,290 --> 00:09:34,530
The error will give you an error message, but it will resume the loop from the top of the loop, allowing

122
00:09:34,530 --> 00:09:37,050
you to execute more commands in the system.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,710 --> 00:00:06,500
Now that we have implemented most of the main features of the back door and we're happy with the way

2
00:00:06,500 --> 00:00:13,460
that they work, I want to spend this lecture highlighting one of the very important features in this

3
00:00:13,460 --> 00:00:18,500
back door and in all of the evil Python programs that we built so far.

4
00:00:18,950 --> 00:00:25,670
Now, all of these programs, including the backdoor, the keylogger, the download and execute, the

5
00:00:25,670 --> 00:00:29,060
download and report, the execute and report.

6
00:00:29,060 --> 00:00:33,470
All of these programs are written in pure python.

7
00:00:33,680 --> 00:00:38,780
Now, if you remember sometimes when we were, for example, writing the download code for the download

8
00:00:38,780 --> 00:00:45,530
and execute, I said it would be very easy to use the W get command to download the file, for example,

9
00:00:45,830 --> 00:00:49,220
but that would only work for Linux and OS X.

10
00:00:49,640 --> 00:00:57,680
Same would apply for many other concepts that we chose to implement using pure Python code.

11
00:00:58,370 --> 00:01:06,680
The advantage of this is that our code will work on any operating system that has Python installed on

12
00:01:06,680 --> 00:01:14,630
it, so it will work on Linux, OSX, Windows, Android, iOS, or even if someone invents a new computer

13
00:01:14,630 --> 00:01:21,860
or new platform tomorrow, this will work on it as long as this new platform supports Python.

14
00:01:22,610 --> 00:01:28,820
Not only that, but when we package this and we'll cover packaging later on, you'll see how we can

15
00:01:28,820 --> 00:01:34,460
transfer this python source code into a standalone or into an executable.

16
00:01:34,580 --> 00:01:42,410
And when this is done, our program will work on the target computer even if it doesn't have Python

17
00:01:42,410 --> 00:01:44,600
interpreter installed on it.

18
00:01:45,410 --> 00:01:49,050
So we're leaving the packaging for later on in this course.

19
00:01:49,070 --> 00:01:55,280
Right now, I just want to show you the first section of what I said, which is our code is going to

20
00:01:55,280 --> 00:01:59,360
work on any computer that has Python installed on it.

21
00:01:59,390 --> 00:02:02,420
We've already seen how this works with Windows.

22
00:02:02,420 --> 00:02:10,220
If we manually installed a Python interpreter on windows now OS X and Linux come by default from the

23
00:02:10,220 --> 00:02:14,060
factory with Linux interpreter installed on them.

24
00:02:14,060 --> 00:02:16,640
So we actually don't have to install anything.

25
00:02:16,640 --> 00:02:21,380
And all the programs that we wrote so far will work on them by default.

26
00:02:22,100 --> 00:02:23,450
So let me show you.

27
00:02:24,930 --> 00:02:25,560
Right here.

28
00:02:25,560 --> 00:02:28,410
I have a Ubuntu Linux machine.

29
00:02:28,920 --> 00:02:33,410
I've already downloaded my back door and as you can see, it's called Reverse Backdoor Dot.

30
00:02:34,290 --> 00:02:38,520
And right here I'm already in the working directory and downloads.

31
00:02:39,180 --> 00:02:42,660
And if I do ls we can see our backdoor right here.

32
00:02:43,290 --> 00:02:48,270
And before I run the backdoor, I just want to show you the IP of this machine.

33
00:02:48,270 --> 00:02:57,510
So if I do, if config, you'll see the IP is ten zero 217, which is on the same network as my machine.

34
00:02:57,510 --> 00:03:03,600
So if we do if config 88 zero, you'll see it's on ten zero 216.

35
00:03:04,260 --> 00:03:10,230
Now, as a rule of thumb, for any two computers to communicate, whether you're trying to get a reverse

36
00:03:10,230 --> 00:03:15,720
connection, whether you're trying to hack into a computer or to just test open ports or anything,

37
00:03:15,720 --> 00:03:23,070
really, if you can pick this IP or if you can communicate with this IP, then you can establish a connection

38
00:03:23,070 --> 00:03:24,930
between these two computers.

39
00:03:25,440 --> 00:03:29,990
So the backdoor that we wrote so far, it uses TCP sockets.

40
00:03:30,000 --> 00:03:37,200
Therefore it will work on the same network and it will work on the internet if the target is even in

41
00:03:37,200 --> 00:03:38,980
a completely different country.

42
00:03:39,000 --> 00:03:46,650
You just need to make sure that your computer is configured in a way that it can accept remote connections.

43
00:03:46,890 --> 00:03:52,950
Now I cover this configuration in details in my social engineering course and in my ethical hacking

44
00:03:52,950 --> 00:03:53,580
course.

45
00:03:53,880 --> 00:04:01,260
I won't be covering this in here because that's something that is purely dependent on ethical hacking,

46
00:04:01,260 --> 00:04:03,900
and it has nothing to do with programming, really.

47
00:04:04,260 --> 00:04:11,760
In this course, we're testing everything inside our local network because that reduces the factors

48
00:04:11,760 --> 00:04:15,690
or the things that can stop our program from working.

49
00:04:15,780 --> 00:04:21,600
So as a rule of thumb, whatever your programming or whatever you're trying to do, if you can do it

50
00:04:21,600 --> 00:04:27,390
on a local network between two computers, then you can do it across the Internet.

51
00:04:27,390 --> 00:04:33,690
You just need to configure your computer to be able to receive remote connections.

52
00:04:35,250 --> 00:04:38,970
Now, with that being said, we're ready to run our listener.

53
00:04:39,060 --> 00:04:44,460
So I'm just going to do python listener dot py hit enter.

54
00:04:44,490 --> 00:04:47,160
This will listen for income and connections.

55
00:04:47,310 --> 00:04:56,430
And if we go to the Linux machine and run the backdoor by again doing python reverse backdoor dot py.

56
00:04:56,790 --> 00:05:06,270

57
00:05:06,300 --> 00:05:11,580
Now since our target is Linux, then we're able to run any Linux commands.

58

00:05:24,660 --> 00:05:30,240

62
00:05:30,270 --> 00:05:32,160
CD dot dot to go back.

63
00:05:32,190 --> 00:05:35,600
If I do p wd again, I'm in z.

I can list the files by doing RLS.

I can go back to downloads.

Do P.W. de list and looks like everything is working perfect.

67
00:05:49,380 --> 00:05:56,490
Let's try to upload a file and let's upload the image because the image is the most complicated thing

68
00:05:56,490 --> 00:05:57,300
to upload.

And if it works, then chances are we can upload anything.

We get a message telling us the upload is successful.

Let's go ahead and see.

00:06:13,500 --> 00:06:15,810
As you can see, we have an image here.

Let's double click it.

75
00:06:17,700 --> 00:06:22,070
And as you can see, if we open it, we have the image working perfectly.

76
00:06:22,080 --> 00:06:27,650
That means the whole file was transmitted properly and it's working perfectly now.

77
00:06:27,660 --> 00:06:31,440
So again, we can use this to upload any files that we want.

78
00:06:31,440 --> 00:06:35,700
We can upload other backdoors, keyloggers, viruses and so on.

79

Now let's test the download feature.

So I'm going to close this image.

81
00:06:42,570 --> 00:06:45,510
And I'm going to delete the image from here.

And let's do download GTR the jpg.

83
00:06:51,550 --> 00:06:57,820
So we're literally downloading the image now from the Ubuntu machine, from the hacked machine to my

84
00:06:57,820 --> 00:06:58,660
machine.

85
00:06:59,170 --> 00:07:02,780
And as you can see it's telling us the download the successful.

86
00:07:02,800 --> 00:07:06,910
And if we double click the image we will get the image.

87
00:07:07,880 --> 00:07:12,470
So as you can see, we didn't have to modify the code for the backdoor at all.

88
00:07:12,500 --> 00:07:17,750
But the backdoor is working perfectly on a completely different operating system.

89
00:07:18,710 --> 00:07:20,180
So that's very cool.

90

Let me exit and close this backdoor and we're going to clear the screen.

And the next thing that I want to do is test the backdoor against a mac OS X machine that I have right

92
00:07:33,170 --> 00:07:33,740
here.

Now, again, you can see that I have the backdoor here in my downloads directory and if we do, if

94

config on this machine.

You will see that this machine's IP is 19216816.

96
00:07:49,510 --> 00:07:54,850
So basically this means that this computer is in a completely different network.

97
00:07:54,850 --> 00:08:02,350
And for me to hack this computer, I need to configure the machine to accept remote connections from

98

outside of my own network.

99
00:08:04,390 --> 00:08:11,020
Like I said, this involves a little bit of a configuration and if we do the configuration wrong, then

100

the attack will not work and we'll think that there is something wrong with our program.

101
00:08:15,910 --> 00:08:21,940
So one testing, it's actually better to make sure the two computers are on the same network.

102
00:08:22,360 --> 00:08:29,200

103
00:08:29,920 --> 00:08:36,190
So all I'm going to do now is just connect to the same network as the Mac OS X machine.

00:08:36,760 --> 00:08:46,260
And now if I do, if config h one, you'll see that my IP is 19216815.

105
00:08:46,270 --> 00:08:49,670
So basically the first three parts of the IP are the same.

106
00:08:49,690 --> 00:08:56,800
This means we are on the same subnet as the mac OS X machine, which means that we can communicate with

107
00:08:56,800 --> 00:08:57,190
it.

108
00:08:57,970 --> 00:09:00,400
Now, since my IP is different.

109
00:09:00,430 --> 00:09:03,370

So instead of ten 0 to 16, it's going to be 19216815.

111
00:09:10,820 --> 00:09:14,060
And we're going to run the listener again.

112
00:09:15,750 --> 00:09:23,040
Then at the AWS X machine, we also have to change the IP of the backdoor so that the reverse connection

113
00:09:23,040 --> 00:09:26,070
is sent to the correct IP.

114
00:09:26,310 --> 00:09:30,990
And as you can see, I've already changed that to the IP of the machine.

115
00:09:31,650 --> 00:09:38,580
So right now I'm in the working directory in downloads and if I do ls you can see I have my backdoor,

116
00:09:38,580 --> 00:09:45,420
which is called the reverse backdoor dot pie, so I can just run it by doing python reverse backdoor

117
00:09:45,420 --> 00:09:47,790
dot pie hit enter.

00:09:47,880 --> 00:09:56,340
And if we go to Carly again, as you can see, we have a connection now we can run system commands,

119
00:09:56,340 --> 00:10:01,500
which means that we can run all the commands that are normal user can run on their terminal, on their

120
00:10:01,500 --> 00:10:02,730
OS X machine.

121

So we can do ID.

122
00:10:04,830 --> 00:10:09,180
As you can see, it shows us the current user and the current machine and all of that.

00:10:09,180 --> 00:10:15,420
We can do p w DTC, the working directory, we can do ls to list all the files.

00:10:19,850 --> 00:10:23,600
So if we do see the dot dot, we can go back one directory.

00:10:23,630 --> 00:10:28,060

00:10:28,070 --> 00:10:31,700

Now if we go back to the downloads, we do see the downloads.

129
00:10:37,040 --> 00:10:43,160
We do allies and it's shown us that there is only one file, which is the reverse backdoor dot pie.

00:10:43,760 --> 00:10:46,640
Now let's test uploading something to this machine.

131
00:10:46,640 --> 00:10:50,780
So we're going to do upload the jpg.

132
00:10:52,530 --> 00:10:55,170
You can see still in us upload successful.

00:10:55,200 --> 00:11:01,380
If we do a slice, we can actually see there is a new file and let's go and run it and make sure it

134
00:11:01,380 --> 00:11:02,040
works.

135
00:11:02,340 --> 00:11:04,920
So if I just double click this.

136
00:11:06,480 --> 00:11:12,690
As you can see, the image is working perfectly, which means the file has been transmitted properly.

137
00:11:13,260 --> 00:11:20,460
Now let's close this and I'm going to delete the image from the machine to test the download ability.

138
00:11:21,440 --> 00:11:27,320
So we're going to say download GT-R the JRPG.

139
00:11:28,680 --> 00:11:30,860
And as we can see, we have the file here.

140
00:11:30,870 --> 00:11:33,810
Let's open it and perfect.

141
00:11:33,810 --> 00:11:36,330
As you can see, the file is working.

142
00:11:37,020 --> 00:11:44,490
So as you can see in this lecture, we didn't need to modify any of the code that we wrote, but the

143
00:11:44,490 --> 00:11:48,240
backdoor is working on Linux, OS, X and Windows.

144
00:11:48,600 --> 00:11:55,080
And this is a rule or an idea that you should keep in mind that when you write programs, if you write

145
00:11:55,080 --> 00:12:03,660
them using pure python code and not rely on a specific operating system operation, then your program

146
00:12:03,660 --> 00:12:09,420
is going to work on all operating systems as long as they have a Python interpreter.

147
00:12:09,600 --> 00:12:15,990
And as we know, OS X and Linux come with a Python interpreter pre-installed, you'll have to install

148
00:12:15,990 --> 00:12:23,400
one manually on Windows for testing, but once we actually get into packaging, your program will work

149
00:12:23,400 --> 00:12:30,150
on all of these operating systems, even on Windows and even if this Windows machine does not have a

150
00:12:30,150 --> 00:12:31,500
Python interpreter.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,540 --> 00:00:06,510
Now, as usual, now that we are happy with the way our code works with Python two, let's go ahead

2
00:00:06,510 --> 00:00:09,420
and try to get it to work with Python three.

3
00:00:09,630 --> 00:00:16,520
Now before we get into this, I just want to highlight one very important fact for this program, especially,

4
00:00:16,530 --> 00:00:23,010
it really doesn't matter what version of Python it works with because in the next section you're going

5
00:00:23,010 --> 00:00:31,380
to learn how to compile this code so that it runs on any machine, even if it does not have a Python

6
00:00:31,380 --> 00:00:32,310
interpreter.

7
00:00:32,310 --> 00:00:37,830
Because, you know, in a real life scenario, you can't really ask your target to go ahead and install

8
00:00:37,830 --> 00:00:40,080
Python before you run the program.

9
00:00:40,170 --> 00:00:46,620
Therefore, the Python version is irrelevant because at the end the program we have here will actually

10
00:00:46,620 --> 00:00:50,940
be converted to an executable that runs on any computer.
