# Section 15: Writing Malware - Packaging

1
00:00:01,040 --> 00:00:08,060
All the Python programs we wrote so far, except for the mark changer, do not rely on any operating

2
00:00:08,060 --> 00:00:09,920
system specific functions.

3
00:00:10,520 --> 00:00:17,600
This means that these programs will run on any operating system as long as this operating system has

4
00:00:17,600 --> 00:00:19,130
a Python interpreter.

5
00:00:19,580 --> 00:00:26,930
So the only limitation that we might face when we want to run our Python programs on targets is if the

6
00:00:26,930 --> 00:00:29,840
target does not have a Python interpreter.

7
00:00:31,010 --> 00:00:35,920
Another problem that we might face is we've seen all the programs we use so far.

8
00:00:35,930 --> 00:00:39,800
We had to run them from the terminal or from the command line.

9
00:00:40,160 --> 00:00:41,330
Now, this is fine.

10
00:00:41,330 --> 00:00:47,870
If your program is a normal program and the user is okay with running your program, but it gets hard

11
00:00:47,870 --> 00:00:53,030
when you're trying to send a back door or a keylogger and you're trying to social engineer your target

12
00:00:53,030 --> 00:00:54,770
and to run your evil file.

13
00:00:54,770 --> 00:00:58,340
And this file has to be executed from the command prompt.

14
00:00:58,340 --> 00:01:02,730
So you can't really tell the user to open a terminal and run the program from there.

15
00:01:02,750 --> 00:01:04,940
That will definitely make them suspicious.

16
00:01:05,980 --> 00:01:09,550
So in this section, we're going to talk about packaging.

17
00:01:09,760 --> 00:01:17,440
I'm going to show you how to convert any Python program to a binary executable so that it can run on

18
00:01:17,440 --> 00:01:23,710
any operating system, even if the target system does not have a Python interpreter.

19
00:01:24,070 --> 00:01:31,000
This executable will also be executed if the person double clicks the file so they don't need to open

20
00:01:31,000 --> 00:01:33,130
a terminal and run it from terminal.

21
00:01:34,150 --> 00:01:40,900
Not only that, but I'm also going to show you how to bypass anti-virus programs and convert our evil

22
00:01:40,900 --> 00:01:48,820
files to Trojans so they look and function just like any other file type, like an image or a PDF.

23
00:01:49,700 --> 00:01:56,810
Now I'm going to show you how to convert our Python programs to executables that work on any operating

24
00:01:56,810 --> 00:01:57,540
system.

25
00:01:57,590 --> 00:02:04,730
But one note to keep in mind is when you're trying to create an executable for Windows, then it's best

26
00:02:04,730 --> 00:02:08,580
to create this executable from a Windows computer.

27
00:02:08,600 --> 00:02:14,420
If you're trying to create it for Linux, then it's best to package your Python program from a Linux

28
00:02:14,420 --> 00:02:15,080
machine.

29
00:02:16,110 --> 00:02:23,220
So basically to avoid all issues, it's best to package your Python program from an operating system

30
00:02:23,220 --> 00:02:27,000
that is the same as the target operating system.

31
00:02:27,600 --> 00:02:31,230
I'm going to show you how to package from Linux to Windows and so on.

32
00:02:31,230 --> 00:02:39,300
But like I said, it's best to package from the same operating system as the target operating system.

33
00:02:40,050 --> 00:02:45,420
So let me show you a quick example of something that you're going to be able to achieve at the end of

34
00:02:45,420 --> 00:02:46,230
the section.

35
00:02:47,990 --> 00:02:48,650
Right here.

36
00:02:48,650 --> 00:02:56,090
I have a backdoor that we already know how to make, but as you can see, it looks just like a PDF and

37
00:02:56,090 --> 00:03:01,160
if we double click it it will actually show us something that the target is expecting.

38
00:03:01,310 --> 00:03:02,570
So a book.

39
00:03:02,960 --> 00:03:10,220
But if we go back to the Kelly machine, you'll see that at the same time our evil code get executed

40
00:03:10,220 --> 00:03:15,680
in the background and it's basically giving us the full access as we see before.

5

Now first of all, if we go to my C directory and I have Python installed in a directory called Python

7

27, and if we look at scripts, you'll see a number of programs that come with Python that we can use.

10

Then in order to install a module, we usually do dash m and then do pip install followed by the module

12

name.

M, followed by the PIP command.

X is.

So in order to do this, first of all, in my command prompt, you can see that I am already in the

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,720 --> 00:00:01,440
Okay.

2
00:00:01,470 --> 00:00:09,420
Now that we know the basic idea and the basic method of converting a Python program into an executable,

3
00:00:09,630 --> 00:00:14,220
I want to address a very common issue that we have right now.

4
00:00:14,880 --> 00:00:21,060
Now, when we double click this file, our backdoor is working perfectly on any computer, even if it

5
00:00:21,060 --> 00:00:22,490
doesn't have Python installed.

6
00:00:22,500 --> 00:00:23,970
So that's all good.

7
00:00:24,060 --> 00:00:30,810
But the problem is you will see a terminal or a command prompt window like this one popping up.

8
00:00:31,440 --> 00:00:38,670
This window will always be displayed until the hacker finishes their work in here and type exit.

9
00:00:38,910 --> 00:00:42,210
Once you do this, the window will disappear from here.

10
00:00:43,160 --> 00:00:48,950
Now, in some cases when you actually exit, you will even see an error message popping up in here.

11
00:00:49,850 --> 00:00:53,210
So in this lecture, I want to fix these two issues.

12
00:00:53,210 --> 00:00:57,560
I want to be able to, first of all, run the back door silently.

13
00:00:57,560 --> 00:01:00,830
So when the user double click it, they won't see a window.

14
00:01:01,130 --> 00:01:06,350
And also when the hacker exits, I don't want them to see any warning messages.

15
00:01:07,070 --> 00:01:14,210
Now, first of all, let's fix the simplest issue, which is the fact that the person might see a warning

16
00:01:14,210 --> 00:01:17,840
message when the hacker closes the connection, when they type exit.

17
00:01:18,230 --> 00:01:25,570
And the main reason for this is when we call exit in here we are calling it without importing the service

18
00:01:25,580 --> 00:01:31,190
library so we can solve this by simply importing this.

19
00:01:32,000 --> 00:01:37,430
And then when we call the exit, we'll just do this exit.

20
00:01:38,090 --> 00:01:41,150
This is a safe way of exit in a Python program.

21
00:01:41,150 --> 00:01:48,770
And even when it's converted to an executable, it will exit properly without showing any error messages.

22
00:01:49,640 --> 00:01:56,390
The next issue, which is the bigger issue, is the fact that when we run our executable, the user

23
00:01:56,390 --> 00:02:02,960
will see a command prompt screen and the screen will stay on for as long as the program is running.

24
00:02:03,470 --> 00:02:07,040
Now, this issue can be solved very, very easily.

25
00:02:07,400 --> 00:02:08,660
All you have to do.

26
00:02:08,660 --> 00:02:10,790
So I'm just going to clear the screen.

27
00:02:11,150 --> 00:02:15,800
And this is the command that we always use in order to package our backdoor.

28
00:02:16,430 --> 00:02:22,130
All we have to do is just add one more argument, which is called no console.

29
00:02:23,280 --> 00:02:24,450
Very, very simple.

30
00:02:24,450 --> 00:02:29,700
So the command stays the exact same and the argument is self explanatory.

31
00:02:29,730 --> 00:02:34,500
All it's saying is I want you to run the back door without a console.

32
00:02:35,040 --> 00:02:43,680
Now this will work for pretty much all Python programs, except the ones that interact with the standard

33
00:02:43,680 --> 00:02:45,750
input, output and error.

34
00:02:46,260 --> 00:02:53,550
So putting this in plain English, this will work on pretty much all programs except programs that use

35
00:02:53,550 --> 00:02:56,850
functions similar to the check output function.

36
00:02:57,540 --> 00:03:04,710
Now if you go to the resources and read about how this function works, you'll see that it actually

37
00:03:04,710 --> 00:03:09,030
interacts with the standard input output and standard error.

38
00:03:09,660 --> 00:03:16,980
So usually the information in these streams are handled through the console, so through the window

39
00:03:16,980 --> 00:03:18,390
that the user sees.

40
00:03:18,720 --> 00:03:26,520
But since we are saying I want to make this program so that it runs without a console, we have to manually

41
00:03:26,520 --> 00:03:32,370
specify or tell Python what to do with the information and the standard error.

42
00:03:32,370 --> 00:03:39,360
And in the standard input, the standard output will be automatically handled with the check output.

43
00:03:39,390 --> 00:03:46,620
So we just have to figure out a way of dealing with the standard error and the standard input.

44
00:03:47,400 --> 00:03:58,020
Now in Python three, you can just say I want to redirect the standard error to sub process dot dev

45
00:03:58,020 --> 00:04:03,000
null, which basically I want you to redirect it to nothing to null.

46
00:04:03,480 --> 00:04:13,410
And you also would redirect the standard input to sub process dot dev null.

47
00:04:14,190 --> 00:04:20,880
The solution will work perfectly if you installed python three on the computer where your package in

48
00:04:20,880 --> 00:04:22,050
the program.

49
00:04:22,530 --> 00:04:30,120
Now in my case I have python 2.7 installed as you know, and therefore we have to handle this differently.

50
00:04:30,840 --> 00:04:34,560
What we'll do is we're going to say def null.

51
00:04:35,390 --> 00:04:40,070
Is equal to open os dev null.

52
00:04:41,060 --> 00:04:46,760
And we're going to write to this and we're going to specify that this is a binary file.

53
00:04:47,540 --> 00:04:52,720
So what we're saying in here is, first of all, we're setting a variable.

54
00:04:52,730 --> 00:04:54,260
We're calling the dev null.

55
00:04:54,380 --> 00:04:59,060
We're saying that this variable is equal to a stream.

56
00:04:59,060 --> 00:05:04,430
And in Python, in order to open a stream, we use the open keyword.

57
00:05:05,210 --> 00:05:14,600
The stream that we're opening is the AWS dot dev null, which is a way to open the null location regardless

58
00:05:14,600 --> 00:05:17,750
of what operating system the program is running on.

59
00:05:17,780 --> 00:05:24,230
That's why we're using the AWS library and we're opening this as a binary file.

60
00:05:24,710 --> 00:05:32,510
Basically right now, the death knell contains a location for a file stream for the dev null, which

61
00:05:32,510 --> 00:05:39,080
is a location where we want to redirect the standard error and the standard input.

62
00:05:39,140 --> 00:05:45,860
All of this is going to be redirected to the dev null, which is basically a location that is set to

63
00:05:45,860 --> 00:05:47,120
null to nothing.

64
00:05:47,840 --> 00:05:53,300
So I'm just going to delete the sub process from here and the sub process from here.

65
00:05:54,590 --> 00:05:55,310
And that's it.

66
00:05:55,340 --> 00:05:57,050
Now our code is done.

67
00:05:57,320 --> 00:05:59,470
Now we're ready to compile this.

68
00:05:59,480 --> 00:06:06,050
And like I said, if we weren't using check output, you wouldn't have to do all of the stuff that I

69
00:06:06,050 --> 00:06:07,040
did so far.

70
00:06:08,120 --> 00:06:15,020
All you'd have to do is just add the no console argument to the command that we use to convert our file

71
00:06:15,020 --> 00:06:16,400
to an executable.

72
00:06:16,640 --> 00:06:18,140
We're going to hit enter.

73
00:06:19,510 --> 00:06:21,700
This will compile the file for me.

74
00:06:21,880 --> 00:06:26,170
And then now I'm going to start my listener here.

75
00:06:27,010 --> 00:06:30,880
And now we can test the file in here in the test location.

76
00:06:31,600 --> 00:06:36,160
Now, I'm going to transfer this to the computer that does not have Python installed.

77
00:06:36,950 --> 00:06:39,150
We have the executable in here.

78
00:06:39,170 --> 00:06:40,610
I'm going to run the file.

79
00:06:41,630 --> 00:06:47,180
And as you can see, we can see the mouse changing, which means that something is running in the background.

80
00:06:47,180 --> 00:06:49,400
But we don't see any new windows.

81
00:06:49,400 --> 00:06:54,710
We don't see a command prompt window, and we don't see anything here in the taskbar.

82
00:06:55,460 --> 00:07:00,140
But if we go to the machine, as you can see, we have a connection.

83
00:07:00,320 --> 00:07:07,280
And again, we can test it by running system commands, navigating and even doing the download and upload.

84
00:07:07,280 --> 00:07:10,340
And you'll see that everything is working perfectly.

85
00:07:11,000 --> 00:07:18,110
So now we manage to convert our Python program into an executable that runs in the background without

86
00:07:18,110 --> 00:07:22,460
showing any pop up windows and without even showing up in the taskbar.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,790 --> 00:00:01,230
Okay.

2
00:00:01,240 --> 00:00:08,290
Now that we know how to package and convert Python programs into Windows executables from a Windows

3
00:00:08,290 --> 00:00:14,350
computer and this lecture, I want to show you how to do that from a Linux machine.

4
00:00:14,800 --> 00:00:21,430
Now, this comes with the advantage of not having to have a virtual Windows machine as an attacker.

5
00:00:21,430 --> 00:00:27,850
So in some scenarios, you only have access to your carry machine and you want to be able to convert

6
00:00:27,850 --> 00:00:31,720
your Python program into a Windows executable.

7
00:00:31,870 --> 00:00:38,830
But like I said, for best results for more complex programs or if anything fails for you, then the

8
00:00:38,830 --> 00:00:45,880
best way to get the best results is to package your program from a computer that uses the same operating

9
00:00:45,880 --> 00:00:47,790
system as your target.

10
00:00:47,800 --> 00:00:51,590
So if you are targeting Windows, then package the program from Windows.

11
00:00:51,610 --> 00:00:53,110
Like I showed you before.

12
00:00:54,010 --> 00:01:00,100
With that being said, like I said, doing it from Linux can be handy in many situations and we're going

13
00:01:00,100 --> 00:01:02,200
to cover how to do that in this lecture.

14
00:01:02,710 --> 00:01:09,820
So in order to do this, we're going to, first of all, install a Windows Python interpreter for Linux.

15
00:01:10,060 --> 00:01:17,350
So we know that Linux comes with a Python interpreter, but we can only use that to create binaries

16
00:01:17,350 --> 00:01:18,180
for Linux.

17
00:01:18,190 --> 00:01:25,090
If we want to create binaries for Windows, we'll have to install the Windows Python interpreter inside

18
00:01:25,090 --> 00:01:25,870
Linux.

19
00:01:26,230 --> 00:01:31,870
Once we do that, we're going to use it exactly the same way like we did in the previous lecture.

20
00:01:31,870 --> 00:01:33,940
But we're going to do it from Linux.

21
00:01:34,510 --> 00:01:37,720
So let me show you how we're going to do it and it's going to become clearer.

22
00:01:38,110 --> 00:01:43,570
So first of all, we're going to need to download a Windows Python interpreter for Linux.

And the version that I'm going to download is Python 2.7. 14, the same version that we actually installed

on Windows.

32
00:02:25,450 --> 00:02:29,320
And as you know, this is a dot MSI file.

33
00:02:29,320 --> 00:02:35,080
So this is a Windows Installer that you cannot run on Linux by double clicking it.

34
00:02:35,590 --> 00:02:42,190
We will have to run it using a program called Wine, which is a program that allows us to run Windows

35
00:02:42,190 --> 00:02:46,540
Executables or Windows Files in general inside Linux.

36
00:02:47,170 --> 00:02:51,310
So first of all, from my terminal, I'm going to have to navigate to my downloads.

37
00:02:52,120 --> 00:02:56,290
Then if we do else we can see the Python installer.

38
00:02:57,430 --> 00:03:03,520
And to run this, we're going to do Wine, which is the name of the program that allows us to run Windows

39
00:03:03,520 --> 00:03:05,950
programs inside Linux.

40
00:03:06,280 --> 00:03:11,950
And as you can see, the file that we want to run has an MSI extension.

41
00:03:12,340 --> 00:03:15,830
So we're going to say we want to run an MSI x x.

42
00:03:15,870 --> 00:03:22,660
So the file type that we want to run is an MSI and we want to install this file.

43
00:03:23,260 --> 00:03:29,740
And the name of this file is Python 2.7. 14 MSI.

44
00:03:31,200 --> 00:03:37,920
So Wine is the name of the program that allows us to run Windows programs inside Linux.

45
00:03:38,370 --> 00:03:44,820
We're saying MSI X, E, c because we want to run a file with an MSI extension.

46
00:03:45,390 --> 00:03:51,330
We're saying that we want to install this file or this program and the name of the file or the program

47
00:03:51,330 --> 00:03:53,220
that we want to run is this.

48
00:03:53,850 --> 00:03:55,260
Now I'm going to hit enter.

49
00:03:56,430 --> 00:04:03,540
And as you can see, we get a normal Windows installer and using this is identical to using the installer

00:04:03,540 --> 00:04:06,480
in Windows and I showed how to do that before.

51
00:04:06,840 --> 00:04:10,920
So all we have to do is just click next here to install for all users.

00:04:14,040 --> 00:04:15,420

54

00:04:25,950 --> 00:04:28,560
And as you can see, very, very simple steps.

57
00:04:29,380 --> 00:04:36,940
Now, as I showed you, this should have been installed in C Python 27, but in Linux we don't have

58
00:04:36,940 --> 00:04:38,920
such thing as drive c.

59
00:04:39,310 --> 00:04:46,000
So like I said, we're using the program that's called line in order to run Windows programs inside

60
00:04:46,000 --> 00:04:46,750
Linux.

00:04:46,750 --> 00:04:55,420
So one creates a virtual location for C and that's going to be in a hidden directory in the home directory.

And as you can see right now, we can't see the hidden directories, but if you press control and H

from the keyboard, you will see all the hidden directories.

Now the one that we're interested in is the last one here dot wine inside this you'll see we have a

So if we double click this, you'll see we have our Python 27 directory which contains the Python interpreter

00:05:34,750 --> 00:05:39,220
and inside it we have the python executable.

in Windows.

00:05:47,710 --> 00:05:54,310
So from now on, the steps are going to be very, very similar to the steps that we follow on Windows

00:05:54,310 --> 00:05:59,260
to install PY installer and then convert the Python program into an X.

74
00:06:00,550 --> 00:06:06,130
So the first thing that we'll need to do in here is to go to this directory.

Now, if we list here, you can see we have the Python XY.

But since this is a Linux computer, it does not know how to run x is.

install py installer.

dot E because we're running this from a Linux computer and all we're doing is we're calling pip.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,990 --> 00:00:01,520
Okay.

2
00:00:01,530 --> 00:00:09,660
Now that we have a Windows Python interpreter and PI installer all installed inside my Linux machine,

3
00:00:09,660 --> 00:00:15,990
we can use this machine to convert Python programs to Windows executables.

4
00:00:16,770 --> 00:00:24,720
So in this lecture, just to change things up, I want to convert the keylogger into an executable.

5
00:00:25,350 --> 00:00:30,150
Now, as you know, the keylogger program that we wrote has two files.

6
00:00:30,150 --> 00:00:38,430
First, the keylogger class, which has all of the code that is used in order to record keys and report

7
00:00:38,430 --> 00:00:38,970
them.

8
00:00:39,150 --> 00:00:46,860
And then we have a file called Z Dagger dot P Y, and this is the file that actually uses the class

9
00:00:46,860 --> 00:00:49,110
and runs the program.

10
00:00:49,290 --> 00:00:54,800
So all of the code is in here, but the code never gets executed.

11
00:00:54,810 --> 00:00:59,010
It only gets called and executed from the Z logger file.

12
00:00:59,580 --> 00:01:06,180
Now, as you know, for this to work, you need both files to be in the same working directory, which

13
00:01:06,180 --> 00:01:07,490
is unrealistic.

14
00:01:07,500 --> 00:01:13,350
You can't really send two files to a person, tell him to put them both in in the same directory and

15
00:01:13,350 --> 00:01:15,250
run one of them from Python.

16
00:01:15,270 --> 00:01:17,010
They'll definitely not do that.

17
00:01:17,460 --> 00:01:25,110
So into this lecture, we'll see how we can combine both of these files into a single executable that

18
00:01:25,110 --> 00:01:31,200
runs on windows, even if it doesn't have a Python interpreter installed, and that will run in the

19
00:01:31,200 --> 00:01:34,380
background without a command prompt window.

20
00:01:35,190 --> 00:01:41,160
Now, before we start packaging this, and this is a rule that applies every time you're trying to package

21
00:01:41,160 --> 00:01:49,380
something, you need to make sure that all the libraries used in the program are installed on the computer

22
00:01:49,380 --> 00:01:51,090
that you're using to package.

23
00:01:51,390 --> 00:01:57,630
So, for example, we know that the key logger uses a library called PY input.

24
00:01:58,230 --> 00:02:04,140
Now this library does not come preinstalled and we see that we have to install it using PIP.

25
00:02:04,770 --> 00:02:11,070
So when you want to package a program that has a library that you had to install manually, you need

26
00:02:11,070 --> 00:02:17,220
to make sure that this library is actually installed on the current system, on the hacker system that

27
00:02:17,220 --> 00:02:19,350
you're using one, your packaging.

28
00:02:19,680 --> 00:02:25,920
This is very important and this is one of the main things that I want to highlight in this lecture plus

29
00:02:25,920 --> 00:02:29,310
package in a program that has to files.

30
00:02:30,120 --> 00:02:37,950
So we installed PI input in Kali, but we did not install it in the Windows Python interpreter.

31
00:02:38,640 --> 00:02:43,770
So we have to install that first before we start packaging the program.

32
00:02:44,010 --> 00:02:49,900
And to install this, we can do it exactly the same way that we install libraries in windows.

33
00:02:49,920 --> 00:02:57,780
Remember, we are dealing with the Windows Python interpreter Now we're not dealing with the Linux python

34
00:02:57,780 --> 00:03:05,640
interpreter So in order to install libraries on the Windows Python interpreter, we usually call the

35
00:03:05,640 --> 00:03:07,770
Python dot x first.

36
00:03:07,770 --> 00:03:15,240
So we have to do one followed by the full location where the python dot x is stored and we stored it

37
00:03:15,240 --> 00:03:28,770
in route dot line drive c, python 27, python dot x e then we want to use pip to install so we do dash

38
00:03:28,770 --> 00:03:35,820
m followed by pip install and then we put the library that we want to install.

39
00:03:35,820 --> 00:03:39,330
And in my case it's py and put.

40
00:03:40,710 --> 00:03:41,340
Okay.

41
00:03:41,370 --> 00:03:44,610
Very similar to the way that we'd install it in Windows.

42
00:03:44,670 --> 00:03:50,430
I'm going to hit Enter so that's all installed and ready for us.

43
00:03:50,520 --> 00:03:57,450
Now we are ready to package the keylogger into a single executable.

44
00:03:58,200 --> 00:04:05,160
So when it comes to packaging a program that uses multiple files, you want to make sure you package

45
00:04:05,160 --> 00:04:11,070
the file that calls everything, the file that you would call when you actually run the program.

46
00:04:11,490 --> 00:04:16,790
So if you remember, the zip logger file is the file that uses all the code.

47
00:04:16,800 --> 00:04:22,530
And when we want to run this from terminal, when we want to run our keylogger and test it from terminal,

48
00:04:22,530 --> 00:04:27,060
we used to run the zip logger dot pi and not the key logger.

49
00:04:27,510 --> 00:04:34,590
So that's why this will be the file that we will be converting to an x and PY installer will automatically

50
00:04:34,590 --> 00:04:40,470
know that this is using a class that's called keylogger and it will automatically package it within

51
00:04:40,470 --> 00:04:41,610
the same file.

52
00:04:43,660 --> 00:04:51,190
Now, as you know, in Windows in order to package a program into an x e, we use the py installer x

53
00:04:51,220 --> 00:04:59,050
e now we can call the pie installer x e from terminal, but since this is a Linux computer, we're going

54
00:04:59,050 --> 00:05:04,840
to have to type 1/1 and then we'll type the location of the pie installer.

55
00:05:05,530 --> 00:05:07,750
So it's stored in root.

56
00:05:08,760 --> 00:05:17,340
The Twine Drive C Python 27 Scripts and pie installer.

57
00:05:18,150 --> 00:05:24,660
Then we're going to type the file that we want to package and the name of the file is Z Logger Dot Pie.

58
00:05:25,230 --> 00:05:31,320
And we're going to want to put this in one file and we want to run it with no console.

59
00:05:32,150 --> 00:05:37,670
So if you think of the general idea of running the command, it's identical to the way that we do it

60
00:05:37,670 --> 00:05:38,480
in Windows.

61
00:05:38,510 --> 00:05:44,990
First of all, you have to type the location where you have pi installer dot x, followed by the file

62
00:05:44,990 --> 00:05:46,340
that you want to package.

63
00:05:46,370 --> 00:05:47,780
Followed by the arguments.

64
00:05:47,780 --> 00:05:52,730
And we're packaging everything in one file and we want to run it with no console.

65
00:05:53,060 --> 00:05:59,420
The only major difference is we had to type one before the command because we're running the command

66
00:05:59,420 --> 00:06:02,600
or we're running the program from a Linux machine.

67
00:06:03,340 --> 00:06:04,780
I'm going to hit enter.

68
00:06:04,810 --> 00:06:07,210
This will create the x e for me.

69
00:06:08,760 --> 00:06:14,190
And now we have the E inside this directory, just like we had it in windows.

70
00:06:14,760 --> 00:06:20,280
Now I'm going to transfer this to my windows machine and we'll see if it works.

71
00:06:21,120 --> 00:06:23,430
So I have the file here.

72
00:06:23,730 --> 00:06:29,220
As you know, this is a Windows machine with no Python interpreter.

73
00:06:29,220 --> 00:06:35,210
So by default, it doesn't have any libraries and it definitely doesn't have the API input library.

74
00:06:35,220 --> 00:06:38,790
Like I said, it doesn't matter if this computer doesn't have it.

75
00:06:38,790 --> 00:06:46,980
The main thing is the computer that generated this E has PI input and has a python installation for

76
00:06:46,980 --> 00:06:47,700
Windows.

77
00:06:48,540 --> 00:06:50,610
So we're going to double click this.

78
00:06:52,030 --> 00:06:57,910
And we're getting a warning message that you always get in windows when you run X is with an unknown

79
00:06:57,910 --> 00:06:58,740
publisher.

80
00:06:58,750 --> 00:07:00,100
So this is normal.

81
00:07:00,100 --> 00:07:02,140
We're just going to say run anyway.

82
00:07:03,040 --> 00:07:06,370
And this should run the backdoor for me in the background.

83
00:07:07,000 --> 00:07:10,840
So let's go ahead and try to type some key strikes and test it.

84
00:07:11,350 --> 00:07:13,000
So let's go to Facebook.

85
00:07:15,990 --> 00:07:24,570
Let's put a username, Azadeh, and let's put a password as password for Facebook.

86
00:07:26,010 --> 00:07:26,650
Okay.

87
00:07:26,700 --> 00:07:27,400
We'll hit enter.

88
00:07:27,420 --> 00:07:28,440
Doesn't really matter.

89
00:07:29,360 --> 00:07:33,590
Now let's go to my email and see if the keylogger worked.

90
00:07:33,770 --> 00:07:39,980
And as you can see, we got the first email which basically telling us that the keylogger started and

91
00:07:39,980 --> 00:07:46,490
then we will start receiving reports based on the time interval that we set in the keylogger.

92
00:07:48,000 --> 00:07:48,720
And perfect.

93
00:07:48,720 --> 00:07:51,150
As you can see, we got a report here.

94
00:07:51,390 --> 00:07:58,530
If we open it, you'll see that the user typed Facebook.com, hit enter, put a username which was Z,

95
00:07:58,530 --> 00:08:02,160
and then they put a password, which was password for Facebook.

96
00:08:02,700 --> 00:08:05,130
So the keylogger is working perfectly.

97
00:08:05,280 --> 00:08:12,060
We managed to get it working using a single executable that will work on any Windows computer, even

98
00:08:12,060 --> 00:08:18,270
if it doesn't have a Python interpreter installed on it and it will run in the background without showing

99
00:08:18,270 --> 00:08:19,800
any suspicious windows.

100
00:08:19,800 --> 00:08:26,310
And we did all of this from a Linux computer, so we programmed the program and we converted it to an

101
00:08:26,310 --> 00:08:32,700
X all from a Linux computer, and we didn't need to have access to a Windows machine.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,290 --> 00:00:08,280
Now so far we built a number of useful evil files and we covered a large number of concepts.

2
00:00:08,280 --> 00:00:14,730
So you can mix functions and you can even mix these evil files to come up with other evil files that

3
00:00:14,730 --> 00:00:16,350
will suit your situation.

4
00:00:16,350 --> 00:00:21,300
And you can use your Python programming knowledge to build something that I didn't even show you.

5
00:00:22,170 --> 00:00:30,090
Now, in all of these cases, it would be really useful if we are able to make these evil files persistent.

6
00:00:30,990 --> 00:00:38,760
What we mean by persistence is programming our program so that after the first time of execution, it

7
00:00:38,760 --> 00:00:46,680
plants itself in the target operating system so that even if the user restarts or turn off their computer

8
00:00:46,680 --> 00:00:54,390
and turn it back on, our program will get automatically executed when the user log in to their computer.

9
00:00:55,260 --> 00:01:00,510
This will allow us to execute our code, whatever that code is.

10
00:01:00,540 --> 00:01:02,730
Every time the computer starts.

11
00:01:02,760 --> 00:01:08,970
So if your program is a backdoor, you're going to maintain your access to that computer even after

12
00:01:08,970 --> 00:01:10,480
the computer restarts.

13
00:01:10,500 --> 00:01:16,710
If your program is a key logger, this will mean that your key logger will work with the startup again,

14
00:01:16,710 --> 00:01:22,390
allowing you to spy on that computer and get all the key strikes that the user enters.

15
00:01:22,410 --> 00:01:28,470
If your program is an execution report, something that runs than, for example, and gets you the saved

16
00:01:28,470 --> 00:01:34,800
passwords, then it will send you a report every time the system starts and it will send you all the

17
00:01:34,800 --> 00:01:36,000
saved passwords.

18
00:01:36,180 --> 00:01:38,400
And you can see where I'm going with this.

19
00:01:38,430 --> 00:01:43,710
Any evil file that you write will be more useful if you add persistence to it.

20
00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:50,130
So in this lecture I want to show you the concept or the idea of how we're going to achieve this, and

21
00:01:50,130 --> 00:01:53,310
in the next lecture we're going to implement it in code.

22
00:01:54,300 --> 00:02:01,200
So the way that I want to do this is by adding a value to the registry of the target computer.

23
00:02:01,830 --> 00:02:05,410
So let me show you a quick look over the registry.

24
00:02:05,430 --> 00:02:13,400
So if we just open the start menu and type drag edit, this will open the registry editor.

25
00:02:13,410 --> 00:02:15,330
So we're just going to say yes to this.

26
00:02:16,450 --> 00:02:22,480
Now, as you can see here, we have the main keys and the key that we're interested in, the one that

27
00:02:22,480 --> 00:02:25,120
I want to show you and the current user.

28
00:02:25,450 --> 00:02:27,220
We're going to go to software.

29
00:02:27,790 --> 00:02:29,710
We're going to look for Microsoft.

30
00:02:30,640 --> 00:02:32,740
And we're going to look for Windows.

31
00:02:33,610 --> 00:02:37,750
We're going to go to current version and run.

32
00:02:39,100 --> 00:02:45,370
Now, once we click on this, as you can see, it will show us the entries that we have.

33
00:02:46,240 --> 00:02:53,770
And you can probably guess this by now, the text or the string that we have in the data column.

34
00:02:53,770 --> 00:03:01,420
And here for each entry, it gets executed every time the system loads and you can see that we have

35
00:03:01,420 --> 00:03:09,760
an entry here by default added by Windows which will run a program called one drive dot XY in the background

36
00:03:09,910 --> 00:03:12,070
every time the system starts.

37
00:03:12,700 --> 00:03:20,560
Now, the reason why I'm showing you all of this is if we manage to add another entry in here and put

38
00:03:20,560 --> 00:03:28,390
the location of our backdoor or of our evil file in here, then this file that we're going to put in

39
00:03:28,390 --> 00:03:31,480
here will always work with the system.

40
00:03:31,480 --> 00:03:32,260
Start up.

41
00:03:32,940 --> 00:03:38,310
There is a number of ways of doing this and the easiest is using the command prompt.

42
00:03:38,850 --> 00:03:43,020
So let's open the command prompt, which is going to type CMD.

43
00:03:44,880 --> 00:03:50,190
And the command that we use to add registry entries is Reg.

44
00:03:50,670 --> 00:03:53,040
And what we want to do is add.

45
00:03:54,210 --> 00:04:00,330
And if you're actually not sure of how to use this, if you just put forward slash followed by a question

46
00:04:00,330 --> 00:04:08,370
mark, it will show you a very helpful description of all the options that you can use with this command.

47
00:04:08,790 --> 00:04:15,390
But since we know how to use it, I'm going to clear the screen and we're going to go back to do ADD.

48
00:04:15,780 --> 00:04:21,270
And the location that we want to add to is this location right here.

49
00:04:22,040 --> 00:04:27,890
So the start of it is going to be the initials, so it will be HC see you.

50
00:04:28,520 --> 00:04:33,800
And then the rest of the path is going to be identical to what you see in here.

51
00:04:34,220 --> 00:04:40,490
So it's going to be software, Microsoft Windows.

52
00:04:41,230 --> 00:04:42,490
Current version.

53
00:04:43,810 --> 00:04:44,560
Ron.

54
00:04:45,510 --> 00:04:51,960
Then we're going to put forward slash V to specify the name of this entry.

55
00:04:52,140 --> 00:04:54,900
So you can see that we have an entry called one drive.

56
00:04:54,930 --> 00:04:57,180
Here we have an entry called default.

57
00:04:57,180 --> 00:04:59,820
And let's call our entry test.

58
00:05:00,760 --> 00:05:06,880
Then we're going to use forward slash T to specify the type of this entry.

59
00:05:06,880 --> 00:05:12,100
And because this is going to be a string, because it's going to be a text, we're going to set it to

60
00:05:12,100 --> 00:05:16,330
B reg as Z, similar to the type that you see in here.

61
00:05:17,360 --> 00:05:25,130
And finally, we're going to do forward slash DX to specify the string that will be placed in data.

62
00:05:25,250 --> 00:05:30,020
And this is basically going to be the location for our eval file.

63
00:05:30,560 --> 00:05:37,190
So just as a test, let's just say this is in C test dot X.

64
00:05:38,660 --> 00:05:45,110

65
00:05:45,110 --> 00:05:47,930

66
00:05:48,800 --> 00:05:53,480
So the command might seem complicated, but it's actually very, very simple.

67
00:05:53,600 --> 00:05:59,870
Basically, Reg is the name of the command that allows us to add or edit registry values.

68
00:06:00,230 --> 00:06:02,810
We're saying we want to add a new entry.

69
00:06:03,410 --> 00:06:06,260
We're specifying the location for the entry.

70
00:06:06,260 --> 00:06:12,670
And this is the location where Windows puts the files that they want to run with the startup.

71
00:06:12,680 --> 00:06:14,540
So it's nothing that I came up with.

72
00:06:14,570 --> 00:06:16,730

73
00:06:17,840 --> 00:06:25,010
Then we're specifying the name of the entry after the forward slash V, we're specifying the type after

74
00:06:25,010 --> 00:06:31,040
the forward slash T and we're specifying the data after the forward slash dx.

75
00:06:31,910 --> 00:06:38,510
Now, if we hit enter, you'll see that this will tell us the operation got executed properly.

76
00:06:38,900 --> 00:06:44,930

77

00:06:49,040 --> 00:06:50,850

00:06:50,870 --> 00:06:55,670

81
00:06:56,330 --> 00:07:03,890

82

00:07:05,480 --> 00:07:13,340

Once that person opens this file, it will open the file that they expect.

7

8

9

So this evil code can be anything.

15
00:01:20,830 --> 00:01:21,970
many scenarios.

00:01:22,450 --> 00:01:27,670

17
00:01:27,670 --> 00:01:33,460

keylogger will start at the target system.

using their keyboard.

22
00:01:49,630 --> 00:01:55,530
This way you will get the saved passwords and the passwords that the person will input in the future.

23
00:01:55,540 --> 00:02:02,950
You can combine a backdoor lesson and the keylogger and the ideas goes on and on and on.

1
00:00:00,740 --> 00:00:07,670
In the previous lecture, we learned about Trojans and we seen how we can create a simple Trojan using

2
00:00:07,700 --> 00:00:12,860
a download and execute payload, so using something that we already learned before.

3
00:00:13,430 --> 00:00:20,270
Now, after watching this lecture and testing this, you probably noticed two main disadvantages about

4
00:00:20,270 --> 00:00:21,020
this method.

5
00:00:21,920 --> 00:00:26,000
The first one is because this is a download and execute payload.

6
00:00:26,000 --> 00:00:33,830
The target needs to have internet access when they execute the file because the file will have to download

7
00:00:33,830 --> 00:00:36,500
two files, the front file and the evil file.

8
00:00:36,500 --> 00:00:40,280
So without internet access, the method will not work.

9
00:00:40,850 --> 00:00:48,140
The second problem is that we need to have all the files uploaded on a hosting service that is accessible

10
00:00:48,140 --> 00:00:57,440
to the user and that allows the user to download the files using a direct URL without ads, without

11
00:00:57,680 --> 00:00:58,340
HTML.

12
00:00:58,340 --> 00:01:00,350
Literally save the file.

13
00:01:00,920 --> 00:01:06,560
Now, this is not hard to achieve, but you're going to have to go and sign up for a hosting service,

14
00:01:06,560 --> 00:01:15,020
or you need to use your own server an easier way to literally package the front file with the evil file.

15
00:01:15,020 --> 00:01:24,080
When we are converting the evil file to an x, then when the evil file is executed or the Trojan is

16
00:01:24,080 --> 00:01:31,070
executed, we're going to extract the front file, execute it and show it in the foreground, and then

17
00:01:31,070 --> 00:01:34,220
execute the evil code in the background.

18
00:01:35,060 --> 00:01:39,530
Now, this might sound a little bit complicated, but it's actually really, really easy.

19
00:01:39,650 --> 00:01:43,640
Let me show you how to do it in practice and you'll see how simple it is.

20
00:01:44,610 --> 00:01:50,820
So so far we know in order to package a program and let's say if we want to package the reverse back

21
00:01:50,820 --> 00:01:54,210
to the PI, we're going to have to use one.

22
00:01:54,450 --> 00:01:57,060
We're going to have to call pie installer.

23
00:01:57,060 --> 00:02:02,540
And we're going to have to say, I want this to be packaged as one file with no console.

24
00:02:02,550 --> 00:02:07,770
So we covered all of this and we covered exactly why we use each one of these options.

25
00:02:08,670 --> 00:02:15,330
Now, in order to add one more file, which is the file that we want the user to see when they execute

26
00:02:15,330 --> 00:02:20,100
our Trojan, all we have to do is add one more argument.

27
00:02:20,850 --> 00:02:27,990
So the argument that I'm going to add is called data and then we're going to give it the data that we

28
00:02:27,990 --> 00:02:30,570
need to add between two quotes.

29
00:02:31,500 --> 00:02:37,440
So the first thing that we need to write is the location of the file that we want to add that we want

30
00:02:37,440 --> 00:02:38,640
the user to see.

31
00:02:38,970 --> 00:02:46,260
And in my example, this time, instead of showing a car, I want to show the user a PDF file.

32
00:02:46,380 --> 00:02:51,530
So right here in my downloads, I have a file called sample PDF.

33
00:02:51,540 --> 00:02:55,530
And as you can see, if you execute it, you'll see a normal PDF.

34
00:02:56,070 --> 00:03:03,630
So this is what I want the user to see when they run my back door and I'm going to have to put its full

35
00:03:03,630 --> 00:03:08,460
location on my current system after the data argument.

36
00:03:08,670 --> 00:03:16,290
So it's in my route downloads and it's called sample dot PDF.

37
00:03:17,590 --> 00:03:25,450
Then I'm going to put a semicolon and after the semicolon you're supposed to put the location where

38
00:03:25,450 --> 00:03:29,680
you want to store this file once the backdoor is executed.

39
00:03:29,680 --> 00:03:34,630
So the location on the target system, where to store the front file.

40
00:03:35,260 --> 00:03:39,730
Now you can put this in the temp or somewhere hidden so that the user doesn't see it.

41
00:03:39,880 --> 00:03:45,460
By default, POI installer will actually put it in the temp of the updater.

42
00:03:45,460 --> 00:03:50,170
So it's in an unusual location that the user will probably not see.

43
00:03:50,590 --> 00:03:56,200
So I don't want to change that and I'm just going to put a dot to use that directory.

44
00:03:56,200 --> 00:04:05,140
The default directory that PY installer puts the files that we embed in our package and that's it.

45
00:04:05,140 --> 00:04:05,780
I'm done.

46
00:04:05,800 --> 00:04:09,760
So we're using the exact same command that we always use.

47
00:04:09,790 --> 00:04:14,140
The only difference is we added a new argument called data.

48
00:04:14,950 --> 00:04:21,700
In this argument, we specified the file that we want to show to the user when they execute our Trojan.

49
00:04:21,700 --> 00:04:29,380
And I put the full location to this file, I put a semicolon followed by a dot because I want to use

50
00:04:29,380 --> 00:04:36,520
the default location that PY installer usually use when they extract files at the target system.

51
00:04:37,420 --> 00:04:42,940
Then the rest of the arguments are still the same and we can compile the backdoor right now.

52
00:04:43,630 --> 00:04:49,660
Now what we are going to do once we compile it, we're going to package the front file with the evil

53
00:04:49,660 --> 00:04:50,310
file.

54
00:04:50,320 --> 00:04:56,320
When the evil file gets executed, it's going to extract the front file at runtime.

55
00:04:56,320 --> 00:04:57,880
So all of that is perfect.

56
00:04:57,970 --> 00:05:05,170
We need to do the third step, which is to run the front file from the evil code, because now when

57
00:05:05,170 --> 00:05:13,210
the person executes the reverse backdoor dot API, what's going to happen is it's going to run my backdoor

58
00:05:13,210 --> 00:05:18,910
according to this code, but nothing will execute the file that we just packaged.

59
00:05:19,450 --> 00:05:25,840

60
00:05:25,840 --> 00:05:28,870

61
00:05:29,620 --> 00:05:35,080
So first of all, I'm going to create a variable and I'm going to call this file name and this will

62
00:05:35,080 --> 00:05:40,540
hold the value for the location of the front file of the PDF.

63
00:05:40,960 --> 00:05:48,880
Now like I said, because we're only putting a dot in here, PY installer is going to place this file

64
00:05:48,880 --> 00:05:52,780
in a default location that PY installer always use.

65
00:05:53,290 --> 00:06:01,660
Now this default location can be accessed by doing this dot underscore my pass.

66
00:06:03,320 --> 00:06:06,380
And then we're going to follow this by the file name.

67
00:06:06,380 --> 00:06:09,860
And my file name is sample PDF.

68
00:06:11,200 --> 00:06:19,460
Now file name variable is going to contain the full location on the target system where the sample dot

69
00:06:19,480 --> 00:06:21,260
pdf is going to be stored.

70
00:06:21,280 --> 00:06:29,200
So this right here will be replaced by the default location where PI Installer places the files that

71
00:06:29,200 --> 00:06:31,270
we package with our program.

72
00:06:32,080 --> 00:06:37,210
Finally, we're just going to have to run the file name or the file that we have in here.

73
00:06:37,480 --> 00:06:45,130
So as you know, we usually use some process to run stuff and we're going to use P Open so that we don't

74
00:06:45,130 --> 00:06:47,560
block the execution of the program.

75
00:06:47,710 --> 00:06:50,530
And all we want to run is file name.

76
00:06:52,400 --> 00:06:53,900
And we're just going to set Shell to.

77
00:06:53,900 --> 00:06:54,470
True.

78
00:06:55,500 --> 00:06:56,170
And that's it.

79
00:06:56,190 --> 00:06:56,850
We're done.

80
00:06:58,180 --> 00:07:02,800
So I'm going to go down and I'm going to compile this.

81
00:07:02,800 --> 00:07:04,180
So I'm just going to hit enter.

00:07:05,580 --> 00:07:06,800
And this is all done.

00:07:06,810 --> 00:07:10,740
Now I'm going to transfer it to my windows machine.

84
00:07:12,340 --> 00:07:18,670
And I have the file here, but before I run it, I'm actually going to go and listen for income connections.

85
00:07:19,360 --> 00:07:24,970
So I'm just going to do Python listener dot py.

86
00:07:26,360 --> 00:07:28,520
Let's go ahead and run the file.

87
00:07:29,430 --> 00:07:31,200
We're going to run it anyway.

88
00:07:32,990 --> 00:07:39,740
And as you can see, we the user is going to see the PDF that we embedded with our backdoor.

you want.

00:08:11,140 --> 00:08:17,050
I'm using the backdoor just as an example, and as you can see, we can use this method to create a

96
00:08:17,050 --> 00:08:23,920
Trojan so that we can send the user a file and tell them that this is a PDF or an image or an MP three

97
00:08:23,920 --> 00:08:27,400
and when they run it they will get the file that they expect.

98
00:08:27,400 --> 00:08:32,500
But at the same time, our evil code is going to run in the background.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,070 --> 00:00:05,750
So far, we wrote a number of evil files and evil programs.

2
00:00:06,020 --> 00:00:12,500
And as we did this, we focused on the functionality and the implementation of these programs.

3
00:00:12,950 --> 00:00:20,360
So we didn't really care if these programs are being detected by antivirus programs as long as our evil

4
00:00:20,360 --> 00:00:23,330
code or evil program is doing its job.

5
00:00:24,250 --> 00:00:31,540
Now I think it's time to start talking about antivirus programs and how we can bypass them so that our

6
00:00:31,540 --> 00:00:34,600
code will not be detected as a virus.

7
00:00:35,470 --> 00:00:40,660
Now, in order to bypass antivirus programs, we need to understand how they work.

8
00:00:41,200 --> 00:00:45,730
Most antivirus programs work based on two main concepts.

9
00:00:45,760 --> 00:00:51,610
Now, some of them are much more complicated than this, and I'm going to include a link in the resources

10
00:00:51,610 --> 00:00:54,590
for more information and more details.

11
00:00:54,610 --> 00:01:00,250
But in general, most antivirus programs rely on two main factors.

12
00:01:00,790 --> 00:01:06,850
Understanding these two main factors will make bypassing them really, really easy, since you already

13
00:01:06,850 --> 00:01:08,590
have the program and skills.

14
00:01:09,340 --> 00:01:16,690
So I actually want to focus on the concepts and the ideas so that you can think on your own and be able

15
00:01:16,690 --> 00:01:20,530
to bypass antivirus programs using a unique way.

16
00:01:20,530 --> 00:01:26,170
Because, as what will explain now, being unique is very, very important.

17
00:01:26,860 --> 00:01:33,270
Now, the first main concept that antivirus programs rely on is the code of the file.

18
00:01:33,280 --> 00:01:35,950
So they analyze the code of the file.

19
00:01:35,950 --> 00:01:41,740
And the way they do this is they have a huge database of file signatures.

20
00:01:42,400 --> 00:01:48,880
These signatures correspond to harmful files that antivirus programs have already analyzed.

21
00:01:48,940 --> 00:01:55,000
And what they do is every time there is a new file, such as your evil file, they will compare this

22
00:01:55,000 --> 00:01:58,150
file to this huge database of signatures.

23
00:01:58,750 --> 00:02:02,830
If they see similarities, then they'll mark your file as harmful.

24
00:02:02,860 --> 00:02:05,230
Otherwise, your file will pass.

25
00:02:05,980 --> 00:02:12,760
So since you are a programmer now and you're writing your own files, it's highly unlikely that antivirus

26
00:02:12,760 --> 00:02:19,350
programs will even detect your file because your style of coding should be different than other people.

27
00:02:19,360 --> 00:02:23,520
Unless you're copying the code that I'm writing word for word, which is not bad.

28
00:02:23,530 --> 00:02:29,260
But as this course gets bigger, these evil files will probably be more detectable.

29
00:02:29,740 --> 00:02:36,520
Now, in order to bypass this, all you have to do is make your code more unique so you can add useless

30
00:02:36,520 --> 00:02:42,550
code, for example, useless operations, useless add operations, some equations, add some numbers,

31
00:02:42,550 --> 00:02:50,410
remove some numbers, loop over 0 to 1000, post the program using time, dot sleep and so on.

32
00:02:50,410 --> 00:02:56,110
Just literally make the code more unique, use different names, use different variable names and so

33
00:02:56,110 --> 00:02:56,560
on.

34
00:02:57,250 --> 00:03:03,280
You can also use obfuscation and there is programs that are literally just rename variables to weird

35
00:03:03,280 --> 00:03:07,000
names and all of that to make the code more unique.

36
00:03:07,330 --> 00:03:12,970
You can encode the code and you can even pack the code after it's converted to an X.

37
00:03:13,300 --> 00:03:15,280
And we'll talk about that in a minute.

38
00:03:16,060 --> 00:03:23,380
So basically, the main idea to bypass the first method that antivirus is used to detect your file is

39
00:03:23,380 --> 00:03:27,910
to make your file unique so you can use any way to modify your file.

40
00:03:27,910 --> 00:03:33,340
As long as you're going to generate a unique file, you will be able to bypass this method.

41
00:03:34,600 --> 00:03:41,890
The other main method that antivirus programs use in order to detect your files is the behavior of the

42
00:03:41,890 --> 00:03:42,560
file.

43
00:03:42,580 --> 00:03:50,560
So what they do is they run your file into a virtual environment or a sandbox and analyze how the file

44
00:03:50,560 --> 00:03:51,780
is going to work.

45
00:03:51,790 --> 00:03:55,160
So they're going to see is it going to modify registry values?

46
00:03:55,180 --> 00:03:57,280
Is it going to open a port?

47
00:03:57,310 --> 00:04:00,350
Is it going to remove some important files?

48
00:04:00,370 --> 00:04:07,250
So even if your code looks harmless, they're going to analyze what the file will do on the system.

49
00:04:07,270 --> 00:04:10,540
So this is a little bit harder to bypass.

50
00:04:10,540 --> 00:04:17,500
And in order to bypass this, you need to make sure your program or your evil code looks like it's going

51
00:04:17,500 --> 00:04:20,030
to do something harmless on the system.

52
00:04:20,050 --> 00:04:24,040
So you want to add some useless operations before the evil code.

53
00:04:24,070 --> 00:04:27,850
This will make some of these programs trust your program.

54
00:04:28,030 --> 00:04:34,810
You can also delay the execution of your evil code so you can delay it for a minute or two to make sure

55
00:04:34,810 --> 00:04:42,790
that the antivirus program will quit the execution of your program and think it's harmless before your

56
00:04:42,790 --> 00:04:45,520
program executes its evil part.

57
00:04:46,210 --> 00:04:51,790
Now, like I said, bypassing antivirus programs can be a very big topic, and I'm going to include

58
00:04:51,790 --> 00:04:54,290
links in the resources for further reading.

59
00:04:54,310 --> 00:05:00,850
And like I said, once you understand how antivirus programs work, bypassing them is going to be very

60
00:05:00,850 --> 00:05:05,620
easy because you already know programming and you're already writing your own files.

61
00:05:06,850 --> 00:05:14,590
So in the next lecture, we'll see how to scan our evil files to see how detectable they are and how

62
00:05:14,590 --> 00:05:15,550
to improve them.

63
00:05:15,550 --> 00:05:18,700
So they bypass antivirus programs.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,620 --> 00:00:08,360
So far, we learned how to make our evil files function like any other file type, like an image, a

2
00:00:08,360 --> 00:00:12,110
PDF or anything else that the user will trust.

3
00:00:12,770 --> 00:00:19,550
Now, in order to make a proper Trojan, we need our evil file to first function like any other file

4
00:00:19,550 --> 00:00:22,260
type and look like that file type.

5
00:00:22,280 --> 00:00:29,600
So if we are making our evil file function like a PDF, then it should have an icon that represents

6
00:00:29,600 --> 00:00:30,230
that.

7
00:00:30,290 --> 00:00:35,780
If we are pretending that this file is an image, then it should have an image icon.

8
00:00:36,440 --> 00:00:43,190
Now, changing the icon of evil files is actually easy, and the first thing that you need to do is

9
00:00:43,190 --> 00:00:44,510
download an icon.

10
00:00:45,050 --> 00:00:48,740
There is a number of websites that allow you to download icons.

11
00:00:48,860 --> 00:00:57,000
One website that I like you is in is called Icon Finder and we can search for icons by file type.

12
00:00:57,020 --> 00:01:01,460
So if I'm looking for a PDF icon, I'm going to type in PDF.

13
00:01:03,150 --> 00:01:09,630
And you want to make sure you set the price to free and set the quality to something high.

14
00:01:09,660 --> 00:01:13,500
So I'm setting it to a minimum of 128 pixels.

15
00:01:14,220 --> 00:01:20,520
Now you can pick any icon from here and an icon that I think would look perfect is this one.

16
00:01:21,610 --> 00:01:27,040
Now I'm going to set its quality to the highest possible, and I'm going to download it.

17
00:01:28,800 --> 00:01:30,420
I'm going to click on okay.

18
00:01:30,570 --> 00:01:33,870
And this will put it for me in my downloads directory.

19
00:01:33,870 --> 00:01:36,390
And as you can see, we have it in here.

20
00:01:36,990 --> 00:01:43,150
Now note this image has a P and G extension, so we can't use it as an icon.

21
00:01:43,170 --> 00:01:47,670
It needs to have an echo extension so that we can use it as an icon.

22
00:01:48,420 --> 00:01:51,990
Now there is a number of ways to convert PNG to echo.

23
00:01:52,200 --> 00:01:58,350
I have a handy website here that will do that for me and I'm going to include its link in the resources.

24
00:01:58,770 --> 00:02:04,170

25

Select the format and we wanted a microsoft icon select the size and we want it as high as possible.

26
00:02:13,560 --> 00:02:15,900
And we're going to click on Convert.

27
00:02:17,850 --> 00:02:21,810
And as you can see, it's allowing me to download the converted image.

28
00:02:21,930 --> 00:02:24,780
I'm just going to click on Saved, Save It.

29
00:02:25,320 --> 00:02:29,850
And if we go on downloads now, you'll see we have the image icon.

30
00:02:30,510 --> 00:02:35,850
Now I'm going to rename this image and I'm actually just going to call it PDF Dot Icon.

31
00:02:36,910 --> 00:02:38,290
Now we're all set.

32
00:02:38,500 --> 00:02:44,680
And all we have to do now is to use this icon as the icon of our evil file.

with no console.

38
00:03:14,560 --> 00:03:15,400
directory.

41
00:03:25,570 --> 00:03:29,680
And specify the full path to the icon that you want to use.

42
00:03:30,280 --> 00:03:34,360
Now, my icon is in the downloads here, so it's in route.

43
00:03:35,010 --> 00:03:40,260
Downloads and the name of the icon is pdf Eco.

44
00:03:41,260 --> 00:03:42,790
So very simple.

45
00:03:42,790 --> 00:03:47,920
We're using the exact same command that I showed you when we were creating the Trojan and combining

46
00:03:47,920 --> 00:03:49,180
it with a PDF.

47
00:03:49,660 --> 00:03:56,170
The only thing is we added a new argument called Icon and we specified the full path to the icon.

48
00:03:56,860 --> 00:03:58,180
I'm going to hit enter.

49
00:03:59,310 --> 00:04:01,470
This will generate the icon for me.

50
00:04:01,950 --> 00:04:07,710
And now we're ready to test this Trojan and see if it has the icon that we selected.

51
00:04:08,490 --> 00:04:13,890
Before I do that, I'm going to clear the screen and I'm going to start my listener.

52
00:04:14,160 --> 00:04:18,000
So I'm going to do Python listener dot py.

53
00:04:19,080 --> 00:04:23,220
And now I'm going to transfer the Trojan to the Windows machine.

54
00:04:24,430 --> 00:04:26,170
Now I have the backdoor here.

55
00:04:26,170 --> 00:04:29,710
And as you can see, it has the icon that we picked.

56
00:04:30,160 --> 00:04:33,580
And because we picked an icon with a high resolution.

57
00:04:33,670 --> 00:04:39,760
If we go to view and even go to extra large, you'll see that the file still doesn't look suspicious,

58
00:04:39,760 --> 00:04:42,340
still looks like a legitimate PDF.

59
00:04:43,300 --> 00:04:45,310
Now if we double click this file.

60
00:04:46,390 --> 00:04:48,460
And click on Run anyway.

61
00:04:50,030 --> 00:04:55,580
We will see the PDF that the user is expecting, so that's not suspicious at all.

62
00:04:55,610 --> 00:05:02,360
It has a proper icon that's not suspicious, but at the same time, our evil code got executed.

63
00:05:02,360 --> 00:05:10,430
And as you can see, we got a connection and now we are able to run any system command and use our backdoor

64
00:05:10,460 --> 00:05:12,800
the same way that we used it before.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,760 --> 00:00:08,260
Now that we know how to make our evil file function just like any other file type, and we are also

2
00:00:08,260 --> 00:00:13,150
able to change the icon to something that represents this file.

3
00:00:13,720 --> 00:00:19,300
So our Trojan so far is pretty good, but it still has a tiny problem.

4
00:00:19,720 --> 00:00:24,520
If you look at the extension here at the bottom, you'll see it says dot x.

5
00:00:25,210 --> 00:00:29,990
But if this was a PDF, it should say dot pdf.

6
00:00:30,010 --> 00:00:34,540
If it's an image, it should say an image type like a PNG or a jpg.

7
00:00:35,110 --> 00:00:42,190
Now if you use the method that I showed you to generate this Trojan, it has to have a dot x extension.

8
00:00:42,610 --> 00:00:46,880
The reason for this is our file is still an executable.

9
00:00:46,900 --> 00:00:54,670
We are relying on the fact that it is an executable to, first of all, run our evil code and also to

10
00:00:54,670 --> 00:00:57,760
run an open the file that the person sees.

11
00:00:58,600 --> 00:01:05,740
Now there are exploits that will allow you to actually inject evil code in a proper PDF or in a proper

12
00:01:05,740 --> 00:01:06,480
image.

13
00:01:06,490 --> 00:01:11,590
But when these exploits get discovered, they get patched very, very quickly.

14
00:01:11,590 --> 00:01:14,620
So you only have a day or two to use them.

15
00:01:14,620 --> 00:01:21,790
And then Microsoft or whatever company that is responsible for this file type, would release an update

16
00:01:21,790 --> 00:01:23,200
that fixed this issue.

17
00:01:23,920 --> 00:01:30,760
Whereas the method that I'm showing you right now will always work even on systems that are fully patched

18
00:01:30,760 --> 00:01:36,880
that do not contain any vulnerabilities because we're not really using any vulnerabilities.

19
00:01:36,880 --> 00:01:43,450
This is just the program that we are writing it in a way to run some code in the background and open

20
00:01:43,450 --> 00:01:44,950
a file in the foreground.

21
00:01:45,460 --> 00:01:49,930
Therefore, the file extension has to be a dot x e at the bottom.

22
00:01:51,100 --> 00:01:58,840
But this is not so bad because first of all, by default, Windows does not show the file extension.

23
00:01:59,350 --> 00:02:05,410
So by default, this option right here, which shows the name extension is unchecked.

24
00:02:05,410 --> 00:02:09,430
So the file would actually look like this without the extension.

25
00:02:09,430 --> 00:02:14,500
So the file would be convincing anyway without having to add anything to it.

26
00:02:15,250 --> 00:02:21,940
But if you know that your target probably has this checked and it's going to check the file extension,

27
00:02:21,940 --> 00:02:29,620
then I'm going to show you a trick to make the file extension look like it's not x e, but it will still

28
00:02:29,620 --> 00:02:31,870
be x e in reality.

29
00:02:32,620 --> 00:02:34,750
So let me show you what I'm trying to do.

30
00:02:34,870 --> 00:02:39,720
First of all, initially, I'm going to open the activity overview.

31
00:02:39,730 --> 00:02:45,280
You can actually access this from the dock from here, and I'm going to type characters.

32
00:02:48,050 --> 00:02:50,690
This will open the characters up for me.

33
00:02:51,050 --> 00:02:58,250
And what I'm going to search for in here is a character called Right to Left.

34
00:02:59,080 --> 00:03:00,880
So you can see it right here.

35
00:03:01,750 --> 00:03:04,240
Now, when we click it, we'll see this window.

36
00:03:04,270 --> 00:03:10,750
And if we click on copy character, this will actually copy the character for me in the clipboard.

37
00:03:11,670 --> 00:03:14,460
Now I'm going to open a text editor.

38
00:03:16,390 --> 00:03:22,900
And now I'm going to use this to type the name that I want to use for the file, just so that it's clearer

39
00:03:22,900 --> 00:03:25,720
to you how I'm going to use this character.

40
00:03:26,320 --> 00:03:37,750
So let's say I have a file called Z Dot XY and I want to make this x e have a PDF extension somehow.

41
00:03:38,440 --> 00:03:42,370
So what we're going to do, I'm just going to type it again here below it.

42
00:03:42,700 --> 00:03:46,990
We're going to type z dot xy, which is the same.

43
00:03:47,530 --> 00:03:56,350
And in here after the file name, I'm going to add Dash and then I'm going to put the extension that

44
00:03:56,350 --> 00:04:00,430
I want to spoof, the extension that I want to use.

45
00:04:00,550 --> 00:04:03,670
But I'm going to put it in a reversed order.

46
00:04:03,670 --> 00:04:06,040
I'm going to put it from right to left.

47
00:04:06,610 --> 00:04:11,560
So if I want this to say PDF, I'm going to type FDP.

48
00:04:12,510 --> 00:04:17,520
Now, the character that we have right here, it's called Right to Left Overwrite.

49
00:04:18,180 --> 00:04:26,520
So when I copy this and paste it in here, what's going to happen is our text is usually, as you know,

50
00:04:26,520 --> 00:04:28,440
is right from left to right.

51
00:04:28,740 --> 00:04:35,670
But once the computer reads this character, it will start reading text from right to left.

52
00:04:35,790 --> 00:04:41,190
So it's going to read Z Dash and then it will read this character.

53
00:04:41,190 --> 00:04:46,140
So it will read x e dot p d f.

54
00:04:47,030 --> 00:04:47,810
Let me show you.

55
00:04:47,810 --> 00:04:48,800
I'm going to paste it now.

56
00:04:48,810 --> 00:04:49,760
I'm going to press control.

57
00:04:49,760 --> 00:04:58,280
V And as you can see, the order of the letters is being read from right to left, and therefore whatever

58
00:04:58,280 --> 00:05:03,410
we had in here has been converted to xy dot pdf.

59
00:05:04,100 --> 00:05:07,580
Now keep in mind we are not changing the file name.

60
00:05:07,580 --> 00:05:12,440
We are changing the way the file name is displayed on screen.

61
00:05:12,470 --> 00:05:20,000
So if our file was an XY, it will still be an XY and it will still work as expected.

62
00:05:20,000 --> 00:05:22,790
It will just look like it is a PDF.

63
00:05:23,970 --> 00:05:26,460
Also note the x e in here.

64
00:05:26,460 --> 00:05:33,960
We can't get rid of this because the reason why we have this is because the file has an XY extension.

65
00:05:34,680 --> 00:05:42,150
So when you are renaming your backdoor or your evil file, make sure you pick a name that ends with

66
00:05:42,150 --> 00:05:44,850
an X and I'll show you why in a second.

67
00:05:45,120 --> 00:05:51,930
You can use online dictionaries and online tools to look up for appropriate names that end with an X.

68
00:05:52,290 --> 00:06:00,060
And for example, let's say since I'm sending a PDF, let's say I want to call this PDF Research on

69
00:06:00,060 --> 00:06:01,740
Human Reflexes.

70
00:06:03,150 --> 00:06:06,960
And as you know, this has to be a dot e x e.

71
00:06:07,710 --> 00:06:17,340
And for us to make this look like a PDF, we're going to put FDP and before the FDP, we're going to

72
00:06:17,340 --> 00:06:20,760
paste our right to left override like so.

73
00:06:21,720 --> 00:06:28,670
Now, the reason why I picked this name is because it ends with an ex anyway.

74
00:06:28,680 --> 00:06:35,280
So what I'm going to do is I'm going to put the cursor in here and I'm going to remove the X that I

75
00:06:35,280 --> 00:06:36,540
added myself.

76
00:06:37,170 --> 00:06:42,880
So what the file looks like now it's saying research on human refill.

77
00:06:42,900 --> 00:06:45,780
So this is the actual file name right here.

78
00:06:46,620 --> 00:06:54,480
The last x e is actually the extension, but it looks like it's part of the word and the person might

79
00:06:54,480 --> 00:06:58,110
think this e is a spelling mistake or a typo.

80
00:06:59,650 --> 00:07:01,810
Now we can just copy this name.

81
00:07:03,140 --> 00:07:05,510
And rename our backdoor here.

82
00:07:08,550 --> 00:07:14,010
And now let's send it to the target and see what it's going to look like on the Windows machine.

83
00:07:14,550 --> 00:07:22,620
One note to keep in mind is when this file is downloaded from a browser, the browser will automatically

84
00:07:22,620 --> 00:07:27,540
replace the right to left override with a character from their own.

85
00:07:27,660 --> 00:07:34,980
So if you're planning on getting your target to download this file from a link or to download it by

86
00:07:34,980 --> 00:07:43,590
email, make sure you actually put this file inside an archive so that the file name or the full file

87
00:07:43,590 --> 00:07:45,090
is actually protected.

88
00:07:45,090 --> 00:07:51,030
And when the person download this file, the file name will not be changed by the browser.

89
00:07:51,600 --> 00:07:56,760
Now I'm actually going to be sending this through my web server, so I'm going to compress this file

90
00:07:57,090 --> 00:07:58,020
to a zip.

91
00:07:58,170 --> 00:08:01,230
So it's already changing it to a zip for me.

92
00:08:01,230 --> 00:08:07,350
And let's just call it research on human reflex.

93
00:08:08,710 --> 00:08:11,140
And let's misspell it in here as well.

94
00:08:11,800 --> 00:08:13,570
So I'm going to create this.

95
00:08:13,720 --> 00:08:14,920
That's done.

96
00:08:14,950 --> 00:08:20,080
Now I'm going to put it in my var wwe w html eval files.

97
00:08:21,870 --> 00:08:26,250
And let's go ahead and download it from the Windows machine.

98
00:08:29,400 --> 00:08:31,240
So we have the file here.

99
00:08:31,260 --> 00:08:32,880
I'm going to download it.

100
00:08:32,910 --> 00:08:33,990
Save it.

101
00:08:35,020 --> 00:08:37,830
And we have it in the downloads here.

102
00:08:37,840 --> 00:08:41,590
I'm going to right click and extract all.

103
00:08:44,030 --> 00:08:47,180
And as you can see, let me make this bigger.

104
00:08:48,440 --> 00:08:52,980
As you can see now, our back door has a proper name.

105
00:08:53,000 --> 00:08:55,070
It ends with a dot PDF.

106
00:08:55,190 --> 00:08:57,260
It has a PDF icon.

107
00:08:57,650 --> 00:09:01,640
And before I run it, let me just listen for incoming connections.

108
00:09:03,620 --> 00:09:08,900
And if we run this back to right now, we will see a PDF.

109
00:09:09,170 --> 00:09:17,210
Ideally, it should be research on human reflex and at the same time our evil code is going to run in

110
00:09:17,210 --> 00:09:21,620
the background, allowing us to do whatever we want to do on the system.

111
00:09:22,700 --> 00:09:25,450
So now it's a perfect Trojan.

112
00:09:25,460 --> 00:09:32,750
And like I said, this will work even if the target system is updated because it does not rely on any

113
00:09:32,750 --> 00:09:33,890
vulnerabilities.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,950 --> 00:00:08,480
Now that we know how to package our evil files for Windows, I want to start talking about other operating

2
00:00:08,480 --> 00:00:09,260
systems.

3
00:00:09,980 --> 00:00:17,630
If you remember when we first talked about packaging, I said Since all of our programs use pure Python

4
00:00:17,630 --> 00:00:26,620
code and they do not rely on OS specific resources, these programs can be used on any operating system

5
00:00:26,630 --> 00:00:34,340
as long as we package them properly from an operating system that is similar to the target operating

6
00:00:34,340 --> 00:00:38,780
system and that contains all the needed Python libraries.

7
00:00:39,050 --> 00:00:43,940
So I said for best results, you need to use the same operating system as your target.

8
00:00:43,940 --> 00:00:49,030
So if your target is Windows, then package the program from Windows Computer with Python.

9
00:00:49,040 --> 00:00:56,030
If your target is OS X, then again package it from an OS x computer with a python installation.

10
00:00:57,260 --> 00:01:01,400
Now OS X comes with Python preinstalled in it.

11
00:01:01,790 --> 00:01:08,490
So in order to package stuff, we know the program that does all the magic is pie installer.

12
00:01:08,510 --> 00:01:12,980
So the first thing that we need to do is to install PI Installer.

13
00:01:13,580 --> 00:01:20,270
Now because OS X comes with Python preinstalled, it also comes with PIP pre-installed.

14
00:01:20,630 --> 00:01:27,110
So as you know, usually when we want to install a new library or a new tool for Python, all we do

15
00:01:27,110 --> 00:01:34,520
is pip install followed by the library name and in our case its PI installer.

16
00:01:35,180 --> 00:01:40,580
Now this way it's different from the way that we do it on Windows, but it's exactly the same way that

17
00:01:40,580 --> 00:01:42,800
we do it from a Linux computer.

18
00:01:43,790 --> 00:01:51,950
Now this should theoretically work, but the version of PIP that comes pre-installed with OS X is broken

19
00:01:51,950 --> 00:01:54,860
and we'll give you errors if you use it like this.

20
00:01:55,790 --> 00:02:02,390
Therefore, we're going to download a script that will download the latest version of PIP for us.

21
00:02:02,510 --> 00:02:06,930
We're going to download it from their own website, and this is the name of the script.

22
00:02:06,950 --> 00:02:12,230
I'm going to include it in the resources of this lecture and I'm just going to click on okay to save

23
00:02:12,230 --> 00:02:12,650
it.

24
00:02:13,390 --> 00:02:16,060
And that's now gone into my downloads.

25
00:02:16,060 --> 00:02:22,090
So I have it right here and now we can run this from my terminal.

26
00:02:22,120 --> 00:02:25,260
So first of all, you'd want to go to the downloads directory.

27
00:02:25,270 --> 00:02:28,170
I'm already in it, but I'm just going to show you what you'd need to do.

28
00:02:28,180 --> 00:02:30,220
You'd need to do CD downloads.

29
00:02:31,480 --> 00:02:31,810
Now.

30
00:02:31,810 --> 00:02:32,520
I'm already in it.

31
00:02:32,530 --> 00:02:34,220
That's why it's shown me an error.

32
00:02:34,240 --> 00:02:38,140
And once you're in the downloads, if we do ls you'll see.

33
00:02:38,140 --> 00:02:40,900
We have the script that we just downloaded in here.

34
00:02:42,260 --> 00:02:49,760
And to run this again, exactly the same way that you would run any Python file you do python followed

35
00:02:49,760 --> 00:02:53,210
by the file name which is get pip dot py.

36
00:02:54,940 --> 00:03:02,080
Now by default, when you log into Kali, you log in as root, which is the highest privileged user

37
00:03:02,080 --> 00:03:02,830
on the system.

38
00:03:02,830 --> 00:03:05,410
So you can think of it as the administrator.

39
00:03:06,190 --> 00:03:12,730
But on all other operating systems, including OS X, you never really log in as administrator, you

40
00:03:12,730 --> 00:03:14,380
log in as a normal user.

41
00:03:15,190 --> 00:03:21,580
Therefore, when you want to install stuff from the terminal, you have to run the commands as the root

42
00:03:21,580 --> 00:03:23,620
or as the administrator.

43
00:03:24,250 --> 00:03:28,270
And to do that you have to add sudo before the command.

44
00:03:29,300 --> 00:03:34,330
So now if I hit Enter, it's going to ask me for the administrator password.

45
00:03:34,340 --> 00:03:41,030
I'm going to type that and note you will not see the characters on screen, but it's being registered.

46
00:03:41,030 --> 00:03:43,340
This is just a security feature.

47
00:03:43,490 --> 00:03:45,020
And if I had enter.

48
00:03:45,980 --> 00:03:49,760
You'll see it will download PIP and install it for me.

49
00:03:50,660 --> 00:03:57,740
And once pip is installed we can install PY installer the same way that we do it on a linux computer.

50
00:03:58,040 --> 00:04:01,310
So we do pip install.

51
00:04:02,460 --> 00:04:03,930
Pie installer.

52
00:04:04,770 --> 00:04:10,470
Now, like I said, because we are trying to install something and we are not the administrators, we're

53
00:04:10,470 --> 00:04:14,460
going to do sudo before the command to run the command as the admin.

54
00:04:15,240 --> 00:04:21,330
I'm going to hit enter and this is telling me that py installer is already installed on my system,

55
00:04:21,330 --> 00:04:24,690
but for you it should go ahead and install it for you.

56
00:04:25,830 --> 00:04:29,370
Once you have this done, you are ready to start packaging.

57
00:04:30,370 --> 00:04:37,090
Now the program that I want to package is the download and execute script and I already have it here

58
00:04:37,090 --> 00:04:45,190
in my downloads and I've already modified it so that it downloads the sample pdf from my own web server.

59
00:04:45,550 --> 00:04:53,230
It's going to open it and note in os x you have to type open followed by the file name that you want

60
00:04:53,230 --> 00:04:54,020
to open.

61
00:04:54,040 --> 00:04:59,200
So in Windows we used to just specify the file name and here this is something different.

62
00:04:59,200 --> 00:05:07,390
In OS x you have to type open before the file name and then I'm downloading my reverse backdoor dot

63
00:05:07,390 --> 00:05:07,960
pie.

64
00:05:07,990 --> 00:05:14,560
So keep in mind I'm keeping it in its python form and then I'm executing it by doing Python, followed

65
00:05:14,560 --> 00:05:15,970
by the name of the script.

66
00:05:16,330 --> 00:05:21,790
Now you can actually compile this to an executable as well, using the method that I'm going to show

67
00:05:21,790 --> 00:05:25,810
you right now and then run it from here without having to type Python.

68
00:05:25,810 --> 00:05:30,340
But we know that OS X comes with Python preinstalled, so it doesn't really matter.

69
00:05:30,340 --> 00:05:37,090
You can run it this way and then we're going to remove this should be sampled that pdf.

70
00:05:39,390 --> 00:05:41,460
And then we'll remove the backdoor.

71
00:05:42,090 --> 00:05:43,840
So this script is still the same.

72
00:05:43,860 --> 00:05:45,390
Nothing changed here.

73
00:05:45,840 --> 00:05:52,770
I've also downloaded the icon here, as you can see, and I downloaded it from the same website that

74
00:05:52,770 --> 00:05:53,460
I showed you.

75
00:05:53,460 --> 00:06:00,690
I can find that dot com and I, instead of downloading the PNG, I just downloaded the icons, which

76
00:06:00,690 --> 00:06:04,290
is the icon type that's compatible with OS X.

77
00:06:04,710 --> 00:06:06,990
And as you can see, I have it here.

78
00:06:07,710 --> 00:06:13,230
So right now I'm actually ready for packaging and you'll see that the packaging command that we'll use

79
00:06:13,260 --> 00:06:16,830
is very similar to the command that we always use with Windows.

80
00:06:17,760 --> 00:06:20,430
So first of all, we have to call PY installer.

81
00:06:21,980 --> 00:06:24,710
Then we're going to say we want this to be one file.

82
00:06:25,590 --> 00:06:33,630
We wanted to run with no console and we want to give it an icon that is stored in the same working directory

83
00:06:33,630 --> 00:06:38,760
because we are in the downloads right now and the name of the icon is PDF.

84
00:06:39,150 --> 00:06:39,850
I see.

85
00:06:39,870 --> 00:06:40,800
And as.

86
00:06:42,080 --> 00:06:48,500
And the file that we want to compile is called download and Execute Dot Pi.

87
00:06:49,130 --> 00:06:55,880
So as you can see, the command is very, very similar to the command that we use with windows, except

88
00:06:55,880 --> 00:07:01,790
if we're running this from Linux, we had to type one before PI installers location and from Windows

89
00:07:01,790 --> 00:07:04,670
we just run the pi installer dot x e.

90
00:07:05,120 --> 00:07:10,520
The rest of the command is identical whether you're running this from Windows Linux or OS X.

91
00:07:11,330 --> 00:07:12,710
So I'm going to hit enter.

92
00:07:14,280 --> 00:07:17,230
And this will generate the binary for me.

93
00:07:17,250 --> 00:07:25,350
So if I go here and go to the list, you'll see I have my binary right here, the download and execute

94
00:07:25,350 --> 00:07:26,430
script.

95
00:07:26,520 --> 00:07:32,400
You can see it has the right icon, a PDF icon, and like I said, you can use any icon that you want.

96
00:07:32,880 --> 00:07:37,830
So before I run this, I'm going to listen for incoming connections on my machine.

97
00:07:38,760 --> 00:07:41,700
And let's double click this and see what happens.

98
00:07:43,450 --> 00:07:49,570
Now, as you can see, we get the PDF that we want the user to see so you can replace this with any

99
00:07:49,570 --> 00:07:51,340
sample PDF that you want.

100
00:07:51,760 --> 00:08:00,490
