And then if we go to Kali, you can see our evil code got executed and now we can run system commands

101
00:08:00,490 --> 00:08:01,390
on the target.

102
00:08:02,170 --> 00:08:06,120
Now, as you know, you can replace the evil file with any file that you want.

103
00:08:06,130 --> 00:08:11,110
You can replace the front file with any front file that you want, and you can replace the icon with

104
00:08:11,110 --> 00:08:12,700
any icon that you want.

105
00:08:13,970 --> 00:08:18,860
Also keep in mind in OS x, the file extension is not important.

106
00:08:18,860 --> 00:08:23,600
So you can see this file doesn't even have an extension and it still works perfectly.

107
00:08:24,500 --> 00:08:27,540
So this is really good when it comes to social engineering.

108
00:08:27,560 --> 00:08:32,300
You don't need to worry about your target getting suspicious about the file extension.

109
00:08:32,360 --> 00:08:39,260
As long as you give them a nice icon and a file to see when your evil code is getting executed in the

110
00:08:39,260 --> 00:08:40,010
background.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,770 --> 00:00:01,450
Okay.

2
00:00:01,460 --> 00:00:09,200
Now that we know how to package our Python programs for Windows and OS X, it's time to talk about Linux.

3
00:00:09,860 --> 00:00:16,490
Now the process of converting Python programs into Linux executables is very similar to the process

4
00:00:16,490 --> 00:00:23,840
of doing it for Windows and OS X, but I just want to spend one lecture for the sake of completion and

5
00:00:23,840 --> 00:00:25,550
to clarify some nodes.

6
00:00:26,380 --> 00:00:32,920
First of all, as mentioned before, Linux and OS X come with python preinstalled.

7
00:00:33,160 --> 00:00:40,900
Therefore, the only reason that you might want to convert a Python program into an executable is if

8
00:00:40,900 --> 00:00:46,830
you want to convince your target to run your file and make them think that this file is something else.

9
00:00:46,840 --> 00:00:53,920
So basically in order to create Trojans, because if your program just does something that is not evil

10
00:00:53,920 --> 00:00:59,440
or not bad, you can just give that program to the user and the user will run it because they want to

11
00:00:59,440 --> 00:01:00,550
use your program.

12
00:01:00,940 --> 00:01:07,180
Or if you already hack the computer and already have access to that computer, then you can run the

13
00:01:07,180 --> 00:01:10,140
Python program as is without converting it.

14
00:01:10,150 --> 00:01:14,770
Because like I said, Linux and X come with Python preinstalled.

15
00:01:15,310 --> 00:01:22,270
So the only reason that you might want to convert your program into an executable if your target is

16
00:01:22,270 --> 00:01:29,890
Linux or OSX is, if you want to create a Trojan now within for OS X, we can do that and that's really

17
00:01:29,890 --> 00:01:30,520
nice.

18
00:01:30,700 --> 00:01:38,920
The only problem with Linux is binaries or executables and Linux will not get executed if the user double

19
00:01:38,920 --> 00:01:39,670
click them.

20
00:01:39,910 --> 00:01:43,640
So I have an example here and this is my reverse backdoor.

21
00:01:43,660 --> 00:01:47,380
After packaging it and converting it to a Linux executable.

22
00:01:47,860 --> 00:01:52,990
If you double click this on a Linux computer, you'll see that it will tell you that the system does

23
00:01:52,990 --> 00:01:58,260
not know how to run this and it offers you to search for programs that can run this.

24
00:01:58,270 --> 00:02:04,180
But if you search for programs, you will get irrelevant stuff and they will not actually execute the

25
00:02:04,180 --> 00:02:05,620
executable for you.

26
00:02:06,280 --> 00:02:12,220
Therefore, if you are a social engineer and a person telling them this is an image or a PDF or a song,

27
00:02:12,220 --> 00:02:17,410
they will clearly know this is not because they can't double click it, and when they double click it

28
00:02:17,410 --> 00:02:20,950
they will get an error message and the program will not run.

29
00:02:21,460 --> 00:02:25,900
The only way to run executables from Linux is using the terminal.

30
00:02:26,680 --> 00:02:31,740
And as you know, people don't open images and PDFs from terminal.

31
00:02:31,750 --> 00:02:38,470
So if you really want to social engineer a Linux user, the best way to do it is to actually give them

32
00:02:38,470 --> 00:02:40,840
a program that does something useful.

33
00:02:40,840 --> 00:02:47,110
For example, a program like the one we did that detects ARP spoofing, or a program that will sort

34
00:02:47,110 --> 00:02:53,950
files or anything that you think the user will need and at the same time put some evil code in there

35
00:02:53,950 --> 00:02:56,170
that will do the thing that you want.

36
00:02:56,200 --> 00:03:03,940
But the main idea is you can't really send them a PDF or an image and run your evil code in the background.

37
00:03:04,810 --> 00:03:12,220
There is also a way of embedding evil code in proper legitimate Linux packages and they actually cover

38
00:03:12,220 --> 00:03:14,500
that in my social engineering course.

39
00:03:14,560 --> 00:03:20,620
So check that course out if you're interested in this, I will not be covering it here because it has

40
00:03:20,620 --> 00:03:22,150
nothing to do with programming.

41
00:03:23,000 --> 00:03:28,880
With that being said, let me show you how to convert a Python program to a Linux executable for the

42
00:03:28,880 --> 00:03:30,020
sake of completion.

43
00:03:30,020 --> 00:03:35,810
And if you want to use the method that I just said by creating a useful program with some evil code

44
00:03:35,810 --> 00:03:36,380
in it.

45
00:03:37,700 --> 00:03:43,680
So for this example, we're going to be converting the reverse backdoored IP into an executable.

46
00:03:43,700 --> 00:03:48,050
And as usual, the program that does that for us is PY installer.

47
00:03:48,260 --> 00:03:51,700
So the first thing that we need to do is install PY Installer.

48
00:03:51,710 --> 00:03:59,660
Since this is a Linux computer that comes with pip pre-installed, we can just do pip install py installer.

49
00:04:00,080 --> 00:04:03,800
So this is identical to what we did in os x.

50
00:04:03,800 --> 00:04:09,290
The only thing we needed to put sudo before the command when we were doing this on a mac because we

51
00:04:09,290 --> 00:04:11,090
were not the administrator.

52
00:04:11,090 --> 00:04:13,760
But with Kali we log in with root usually.

53
00:04:13,760 --> 00:04:17,990
So we are already the admin and we can run the command like this.

54
00:04:18,530 --> 00:04:23,660
And I already have py installer installed so you can see it's telling me that I don't need to install

55
00:04:23,660 --> 00:04:24,380
it again.

56
00:04:25,460 --> 00:04:26,650
Now that we're done.

57
00:04:26,660 --> 00:04:31,730
All we have to do is just package the program using the exact same command that we've been using in

58
00:04:31,730 --> 00:04:32,990
the previous lectures.

59
00:04:33,290 --> 00:04:38,720
So the first thing we need to put the name of the program that we need to use, which is PY installer,

60
00:04:39,170 --> 00:04:42,200
then we want this to be one file.

61
00:04:43,260 --> 00:04:45,780
We want this to run with no console.

62
00:04:47,400 --> 00:04:52,890
The file that we want to package is called reverse backdoor dot pie.

63
00:04:52,890 --> 00:04:56,040
And I'm already in the working directory of this file.

64
00:04:57,130 --> 00:04:59,140
So I'm going to hit enter.

65
00:04:59,170 --> 00:05:01,360
This will create the file for me.

66
00:05:01,360 --> 00:05:07,150
And if I list what I have in this, you'll see I have the file in here.

67
00:05:07,960 --> 00:05:14,380
And note that the file does not have any extension because like I said, in Linux and OSX, the file

68
00:05:14,380 --> 00:05:16,180
extension is not important.

69
00:05:16,870 --> 00:05:22,870
So this file is ready to be delivered to the target, and I've actually already created this file and

70
00:05:22,870 --> 00:05:23,680
moved it here.

71
00:05:23,680 --> 00:05:26,260
So this is identical to this file here.

72
00:05:26,410 --> 00:05:29,740
But like I said, double clicking it here will not work.

73
00:05:29,890 --> 00:05:34,000
You're going to have to, first of all, navigate to its working directory.

74
00:05:34,000 --> 00:05:36,850
So we have to do CD downloads.

75
00:05:37,770 --> 00:05:44,250
Now if we list, you'll see we have the file here and running the file right now will still not work

76
00:05:44,250 --> 00:05:48,600
because the file does not have executable permissions.

77
00:05:49,320 --> 00:05:56,100
So in order to change the permissions of this file to an executable, we have to do each mod, which

78
00:05:56,100 --> 00:05:59,820
is the command and Linux that's used to change permissions.

79
00:06:00,420 --> 00:06:06,690
We're going to say we want to change this to an executable by doing plus X and we're going to specify

80
00:06:06,690 --> 00:06:11,340
the file name that we want to change its permission, which is reverse backdoor.

81
00:06:12,150 --> 00:06:18,870
I'm going to hit enter and now if I do ls you'll see the file name in green, which means that this

82
00:06:18,870 --> 00:06:26,130
file is an executable now, so we can run it just like you run any other executable on Linux by doing

83
00:06:26,130 --> 00:06:31,140
dot forward slash followed by the file name, which is reverse backdoor.

84
00:06:32,010 --> 00:06:36,310
Now before I run this, I'm going to have to listen for income and connections here.

85
00:06:36,330 --> 00:06:41,130
So I'm going to clear the screen and run my listener.

86
00:06:42,270 --> 00:06:46,950
And now in here, I'm just going to hit enter, go back.

87
00:06:47,070 --> 00:06:53,520
And as you can see, I have a connection where I can run any Linux commands that I want and can upload

88
00:06:53,520 --> 00:06:54,810
and download files.

89
00:06:54,810 --> 00:06:58,140
Basically, my evil code now got executed.

90
00:06:58,980 --> 00:07:04,860
Like I said, you can use this if you want to hide the code because you have some good code with some

91
00:07:04,860 --> 00:07:10,200
evil code embedded in it, and you don't want to send the file as Python because the user will be able

92
00:07:10,200 --> 00:07:12,750
to see the code and know that you're doing something.

93
00:07:12,750 --> 00:07:20,340
But other than that, you can't really use this method in order to create Trojans like the ones that

94
00:07:20,340 --> 00:07:22,770
we were creating for OS x.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 16: Website  Web Application Hacking

1
00:00:00,680 --> 00:00:04,460
In this section, we're going to start talking about website hacking.

2
00:00:04,910 --> 00:00:10,520
Now, website hacking is a really big topic and I actually have a full course on that.

3
00:00:10,790 --> 00:00:16,460
But in this course, we're going to focus on the topics that are related to programming.

4
00:00:17,000 --> 00:00:20,030
So first of all, we're going to learn how websites work.

5
00:00:20,030 --> 00:00:26,660
The technologies used, the requests and the responses, and then we're going to learn how to communicate

6
00:00:26,660 --> 00:00:29,810
with websites from our Python program.

7
00:00:30,110 --> 00:00:35,120
Once done with that, we're going to start writing some useful information gathering tools.

8
00:00:35,240 --> 00:00:41,450
We're going to write programs to discover files, directories, subdomains, and a program that can

9
00:00:41,450 --> 00:00:47,540
be used to map the whole website and discover all the links within a Target website.

10
00:00:48,020 --> 00:00:55,010
We're also going to write a program that can be used to guess login information in any login page.

11
00:00:55,400 --> 00:01:03,020
As we do this, you're going to learn how to read the HTML code of any web page, analyze it, and extract

12
00:01:03,020 --> 00:01:04,760
the useful parts from it.

13
00:01:07,020 --> 00:01:11,310
Finally, we're going to build a generic vulnerability scanner.

14
00:01:11,520 --> 00:01:18,930
This is a program that takes a website as an input and scans the whole website for weaknesses and vulnerabilities.

15
00:01:19,200 --> 00:01:23,520
Once done, it's going to show us a report of all the discoveries.

16
00:01:23,520 --> 00:01:29,610
And we're going to write this program in a way so that it can discover any type of vulnerability.

17
00:01:30,240 --> 00:01:36,060
As we write this, you're going to learn more about object oriented programming and how to structure

18
00:01:36,060 --> 00:01:40,320
your program in a way so that it can be easily extended.

19
00:01:41,060 --> 00:01:46,760
Now, all of the programs that we're going to build in this section will work against real websites.

20
00:01:46,820 --> 00:01:51,660
And as a proof, I will be running some of them against my own website.

21
00:01:51,680 --> 00:01:57,440
But I do not give permission to anybody to run these programs against my own website.

22
00:01:57,470 --> 00:02:04,040
It is illegal to run it against other websites, so make sure that you only test it against websites

23
00:02:04,040 --> 00:02:07,550
that you own or websites that you have permission to test.

24
00:02:08,030 --> 00:02:13,490
If you don't have any websites that you own or have permission to test, then I'm going to show you

25
00:02:13,490 --> 00:02:19,550
how to install a vulnerable web application so that you have a target to test the programs that we're

26
00:02:19,550 --> 00:02:20,930
going to write against.

27
00:02:22,760 --> 00:02:30,230
Now we're actually going to install a virtual machine called Metasploit Table, which comes with a number

28
00:02:30,230 --> 00:02:36,680
of vulnerable web applications that you can use to practice hacking and practice everything that we're

29
00:02:36,680 --> 00:02:38,150
going to do in this section.

30
00:02:38,970 --> 00:02:43,620
So Metasploit table is another is another Linux machine.

31
00:02:43,740 --> 00:02:46,110
You can think of it as the opposite to Kali.

32
00:02:46,110 --> 00:02:50,580
So Kali is designed so that you can use it to hack into other devices.

33
00:02:51,240 --> 00:02:54,780
Metasploit firewall is designed so that you hack into it.

34
00:02:54,780 --> 00:02:58,020
So it's designed for people who want to learn penetration testing.

35
00:02:58,050 --> 00:03:05,460
It is designed so that it has a number of vulnerabilities and we're going to try to use Kali Linux in

36
00:03:05,460 --> 00:03:07,570
order to hack into Metasploit.

37
00:03:08,840 --> 00:03:11,030
You can download this from the following link.

38
00:03:11,240 --> 00:03:14,750
You will end up with a zip file like this one.

39
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:17,840
So sorry, not this one like this one.

40
00:03:17,840 --> 00:03:24,710
So once you compress it, you'll get this directory that will click it and you'll see the following

41
00:03:24,710 --> 00:03:25,430
files.

42
00:03:25,430 --> 00:03:31,110
So we're going to create a new machine here and virtual machine, and I'm going to call it Metasploit

43
00:03:31,130 --> 00:03:31,570
Double.

44
00:03:33,880 --> 00:03:36,160
And this is going to be a Linux machine.

45
00:03:40,310 --> 00:03:41,990
And I'm going to continue.

46
00:03:42,560 --> 00:03:45,230
And I'm only going to give it a gigabyte of RAM.

47
00:03:45,230 --> 00:03:46,100
Should be enough.

48
00:03:48,960 --> 00:03:54,140
And then I'm going to use remember with Kali Linux, we created a new virtual hard disk.

49
00:03:54,150 --> 00:03:56,850
With this we're going to use an existing one.

50
00:03:56,880 --> 00:04:04,050
The reason for that is because the image that we have right now is actually it's designed for VMware

51
00:04:04,050 --> 00:04:04,530
player.

52
00:04:04,770 --> 00:04:10,800
So what we're going to do is we're going to import the hard the hard disk file or the hard disk image

53
00:04:10,800 --> 00:04:16,790
in here so that we're going to have a ready installation without having to install it the way we installed

54
00:04:17,130 --> 00:04:17,640
Linux.

55
00:04:17,640 --> 00:04:21,150
So we're just going to use an existing virtual hard disk file.

56
00:04:22,140 --> 00:04:24,990
And I'm just going to navigate to select what it is.

57
00:04:24,990 --> 00:04:32,490
I'm going to go into the Metasploit directory and I'm going to select the dot VMD K file.

58
00:04:32,760 --> 00:04:34,680
So it's this file right here.

59
00:04:35,860 --> 00:04:37,180
I'm going to click on Open.

60
00:04:39,680 --> 00:04:40,580
And create.

61
00:04:42,520 --> 00:04:45,160
So we're going to start this machine right now.

62
00:04:48,340 --> 00:04:49,720
So the machine has run.

63
00:04:49,720 --> 00:04:50,980
Now it's completely installed.

64
00:04:50,980 --> 00:04:53,050
As I said, you don't really need to install anything.

65
00:04:53,050 --> 00:04:55,660
We just imported a ready installation already.

66
00:04:55,660 --> 00:04:58,390
Hard disk, so it's asking me for the username.

67
00:04:58,900 --> 00:05:02,920
And the username is m s f admin.

68
00:05:04,030 --> 00:05:05,470
And the password is the same.

69
00:05:05,470 --> 00:05:07,570
So it's MSFT admin as well.

70
00:05:07,600 --> 00:05:11,890
Now note when you type the password you will not see it on screen.

71
00:05:11,920 --> 00:05:16,030
This is a security feature so that people cannot see your password.

72
00:05:16,420 --> 00:05:20,890
So just make sure you type mss f admin similar to the username.

73
00:05:20,890 --> 00:05:23,170
Hit enter and it will let you in.

74
00:05:23,380 --> 00:05:28,090
Now this is only this machine only has a terminal as you can see right now.

75
00:05:28,630 --> 00:05:34,600
And it's given you a warning that you should never expose this machine to external internet connection

76
00:05:34,600 --> 00:05:36,580
because it is a vulnerable machine.

77
00:05:36,580 --> 00:05:38,110
It is designed to be vulnerable.

78
00:05:38,140 --> 00:05:41,140
Now, right now, it's only inside our lab.

79
00:05:41,140 --> 00:05:43,540
It's only installed as virtual machine.

80
00:05:43,540 --> 00:05:46,600
So literally nobody outside our lab can access it.

81
00:05:46,600 --> 00:05:49,120
So this is a really good way of using it.

82
00:05:49,960 --> 00:05:50,610
Now this is it.

83
00:05:50,620 --> 00:05:56,140
The machine is installed, as I said, in the future we're going to talk about how we're going to try

84
00:05:56,140 --> 00:05:57,820
and hack into this machine again.

85
00:05:57,820 --> 00:05:59,500
Don't be intimidated by the terminal.

86
00:05:59,530 --> 00:06:04,030
We're going to be using it a lot and we're going to learn how to use it step by step.

87
00:06:04,330 --> 00:06:09,550
So at the moment, just we have this installed and we're going to move on to the next step and we will

88
00:06:09,550 --> 00:06:12,760
talk about this once we actually need to use it in the future.

89
00:06:12,760 --> 00:06:16,510
So if you want to turn off this machine, all you have to do is just type in power off.

90
00:06:17,480 --> 00:06:18,560
And hit enter.

91
00:06:19,460 --> 00:06:21,190
And telling you that you need to be rude.

92
00:06:21,200 --> 00:06:24,340
So again, I'll talk about this later for now.

93
00:06:24,350 --> 00:06:25,460
So you do power off.

94
00:06:28,410 --> 00:06:30,270
So just run the command like this.

95
00:06:32,090 --> 00:06:34,310
And asking you for the admin password.

96
00:06:36,560 --> 00:06:37,240
And that's it.

97
00:06:37,250 --> 00:06:38,210
It's going off now.

98
00:06:39,730 --> 00:06:40,530
And here we go.

99
00:06:40,540 --> 00:06:41,460
The mission is off.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,470 --> 00:00:02,930
Web application penetration testing.

2
00:00:03,290 --> 00:00:08,330
Before we can start talking about that, we need to understand what is a website.

3
00:00:08,780 --> 00:00:14,150
And just like I said before, a website is just an application installed on a computer.

4
00:00:14,720 --> 00:00:21,740
That computer probably just has better specs than your computer, but it's fundamentally it works exactly

5
00:00:21,740 --> 00:00:23,180
just like another computer.

6
00:00:23,180 --> 00:00:31,070
So it has an operating system and it also has a number of applications to allow it to act as a web server.

7
00:00:31,190 --> 00:00:37,970
So the main two applications that it probably has is a web server and a database.

8
00:00:38,000 --> 00:00:43,010
A web server is like Apache and the database is like my SQL.

9
00:00:43,220 --> 00:00:48,830
And these are the web server basically understands and executes the web application.

10
00:00:48,830 --> 00:00:53,060
So your web application would be written in PHP or Python and any programming language.

11
00:00:53,060 --> 00:00:59,720
Really, the only thing is that the web server needs to be able to understand and execute this web application.

12
00:01:00,230 --> 00:01:07,460
The database contains the data used by the web application, so all of this is stored on a computer

13
00:01:07,460 --> 00:01:08,480
called the server.

14
00:01:08,480 --> 00:01:14,960
The computer is connected to the internet and it has a real IP address so anybody can access that computer

15
00:01:14,960 --> 00:01:15,830
and can ping it.

16
00:01:16,680 --> 00:01:19,470
So the web the web application.

17
00:01:20,300 --> 00:01:27,440
Is executed by the web server, which is installed by which is installed on your server, on your target.

18
00:01:27,470 --> 00:01:35,180
Therefore, any time you request the page or you run the web application, it's actually executed on

19
00:01:35,180 --> 00:01:38,720
the web server and it's not executed at the client's computer.

20
00:01:39,050 --> 00:01:46,940
It gets executed at the web server and then it sends a HTML page which is ready to read for the target

21
00:01:46,940 --> 00:01:49,100
person or for for the client.

22
00:01:50,100 --> 00:01:52,860
So let's have a look on an example.

23
00:01:52,860 --> 00:01:57,480
And let's say you're on a phone or on your computer and you want it to go to Facebook.com.

24
00:01:57,480 --> 00:02:00,120
So you type in Facebook.com into your URL.

25
00:02:01,020 --> 00:02:07,460
This will be translated using a DNS server to an IP address.

26
00:02:07,470 --> 00:02:15,180
So there is a server that translates every name dot com, dot edu or any website with a name with a

27
00:02:15,180 --> 00:02:15,720
domain name.

28
00:02:15,720 --> 00:02:19,500
So this is a domain name to its relevant IP address.

29
00:02:19,950 --> 00:02:23,790
So your request, facebook.com, the request goes to a DNS server.

30
00:02:23,820 --> 00:02:28,680
It translates facebook.com to the IP where Facebook is stored.

31
00:02:28,680 --> 00:02:34,560
And then you go it'll go to Facebook, to the IP address of Facebook.

32
00:02:34,560 --> 00:02:40,230
It will execute the page that you wanted using all of the applications that we spoke about and then

33
00:02:40,230 --> 00:02:41,970
just give you ready HTML.

34
00:02:42,120 --> 00:02:48,660
So what you get back is just a markup written in HTML, which is a markup language of the result of

35
00:02:48,660 --> 00:02:49,800
executing the program.

36
00:02:49,800 --> 00:02:54,540
So the program gets executed on the server and then you just only get the result.

37
00:02:54,810 --> 00:03:00,390
This is very important because in the future, if we wanted to get anything executed on the web server,

38
00:03:00,390 --> 00:03:06,180
if we wanted to get a shell or reverse shell or a virus executed on the target computer, then we need

39
00:03:06,180 --> 00:03:09,330
to send it into a language that the web server understands.

40
00:03:09,330 --> 00:03:16,290
So for example, PHP, and once you execute it there, it will be executed on that computer, not on

41
00:03:16,290 --> 00:03:17,190
your computer.

42
00:03:17,190 --> 00:03:23,430
So regardless of the person that accesses the page, the this the web shell that you're going to send

43
00:03:23,430 --> 00:03:28,500
if it's written in PHP in a language that the server understands, it's going to be executed on the

44
00:03:28,500 --> 00:03:30,540
server and not on your computer.

45
00:03:30,540 --> 00:03:36,690
Therefore, it will give you access to the server and not access to the person who accessed that server.

46
00:03:39,640 --> 00:03:40,390
JavaScript.

47
00:03:40,390 --> 00:03:46,330
On the other hand, there is some websites use JavaScript and JavaScript is a client side language.

48
00:03:46,330 --> 00:03:54,040
So if you manage to find a website that allow you to run JavaScript code, then the code will be executed

49
00:03:54,040 --> 00:03:55,090
by the client.

50
00:03:55,090 --> 00:04:00,580
So even though the code might be injected into the web server, it will be executed on the client side.

51
00:04:00,580 --> 00:04:02,080
And it will give you.

52
00:04:02,980 --> 00:04:07,210
It will allow you to do things to the client computer and not to the server.

53
00:04:07,480 --> 00:04:13,150
So it's very important to separate between a client side language and the server side language.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,650 --> 00:00:03,600
Now let's translate this into our example.

2
00:00:03,620 --> 00:00:07,790
So in our example, we're going to be targeting the Metasploit machine.

3
00:00:07,790 --> 00:00:12,890
So our web server is going to be the Metasploit table machine and we're not going to be using a DNS

4
00:00:12,890 --> 00:00:16,100
server, so we're not going to be using a name like Facebook.com.

5
00:00:16,100 --> 00:00:19,820
We'll be accessing the website directly using its IP address.

6
00:00:19,820 --> 00:00:24,410
So like we seen before, if we just go here to our Metasploit table machine and type in if config.

7
00:00:25,320 --> 00:00:32,880
You'll see that my IP is ten 2014 to 14 and if I go here to my Metasploit machine, to my Kali machine,

8
00:00:32,880 --> 00:00:38,190
you'll see that if I type in ten, 2014 to 14.

9
00:00:39,380 --> 00:00:41,990
You'll see that I can access my Metasploit machine.

10
00:00:41,990 --> 00:00:47,270
So the web server here is the Metasploit machine itself.

11
00:00:47,840 --> 00:00:54,080
The web applications are Matilda, PHP, my Admin d'Ivoire and all of these.

12
00:00:54,080 --> 00:00:56,390
So these are web applications.

13
00:00:56,390 --> 00:01:01,730
And when we click on them, you can see for example here it's written in PHP, so it's written in this

14
00:01:01,730 --> 00:01:02,840
programming language.

15
00:01:03,470 --> 00:01:08,530
It's also using my SQL Server, which can be accessed through PHP, my admin.

16
00:01:08,540 --> 00:01:15,440
So these are just technologies used on the web server, but the web server itself is the Metasploit

17
00:01:15,440 --> 00:01:16,520
table machine here.

18
00:01:17,630 --> 00:01:23,320
So when you put the IP address, when you put ten, 2014 to 14, we're actually accessing the Metasploit

19
00:01:23,480 --> 00:01:23,990
machine.

20
00:01:23,990 --> 00:01:29,750
And inside the Metasploit table machine, we have technologies, we have an interpreter that's running

21
00:01:30,020 --> 00:01:35,960
PHP code, we have a web server and a database and these are running the web application for us, which

22
00:01:35,960 --> 00:01:38,540
is Mortalité, DWI and all of them.

23
00:01:39,640 --> 00:01:44,290
So knowing these components leave us to knowing how we can hack a website.

24
00:01:44,290 --> 00:01:50,080
So there is more than one thing that we can exploit to gain control over a website.

25
00:01:50,110 --> 00:01:54,670
Now, the main thing, the obvious thing that you think of is the web application.

26
00:01:54,670 --> 00:01:58,250
So it's the thing that you always interact with, for example, in Facebook.

27
00:01:58,270 --> 00:02:03,030
It's where you search for stuff and where you upload pictures, where you write posts.

28
00:02:03,040 --> 00:02:07,870
All of these things are handled by the web application because it's the thing that you click, the thing

29
00:02:07,870 --> 00:02:08,980
that you interact with.

30
00:02:09,010 --> 00:02:15,730
So, for example, if I go here on a utility and I start browsing the web page, this is my web application.

31
00:02:15,730 --> 00:02:22,630
So every time I click on something, it's being handled and executed by the web application on the web

32
00:02:22,630 --> 00:02:23,170
server.

33
00:02:23,350 --> 00:02:29,620
So if I could exploit this web application in some way, if I could gain access to it, if I could connect

34
00:02:29,620 --> 00:02:34,690
to the database because obviously this web application connects to the database, then I'll be able

35
00:02:34,690 --> 00:02:41,530
to maybe gain control over the website and maybe even gain access to the web server and then get access

36
00:02:41,530 --> 00:02:43,240
to other websites on the same server.

37
00:02:43,240 --> 00:02:45,790
We'll talk about all of that later in the course.

38
00:02:46,960 --> 00:02:52,510
The other way of gaining access to websites is using the computer itself.

39
00:02:52,510 --> 00:02:57,580
So let's say you came, your target was TBWA and you couldn't get in.

40
00:02:57,610 --> 00:03:00,650
You couldn't find any exploit in the web application itself.

41
00:03:00,670 --> 00:03:01,720
You tried everything.

42
00:03:01,720 --> 00:03:05,560
You tried all the exploits that we're going to talk about and you just couldn't get in.

43
00:03:05,560 --> 00:03:10,510
Then your other option would be to exploit the programs installed on that computer.

44
00:03:10,510 --> 00:03:16,120
Because we said a web server, the website is installed on a normal computer just like your home computer.

45
00:03:16,980 --> 00:03:22,320
So if you couldn't get in using the web applications, what if there is one of the programs installed

46
00:03:22,320 --> 00:03:28,830
on the web computer and the computer itself has an exploit, has a buffer overflow or a remote execution

47
00:03:28,830 --> 00:03:29,460
exploit?

48
00:03:29,460 --> 00:03:35,130
What if the Web server itself or the database program, the program that's running the database itself

49
00:03:35,130 --> 00:03:41,070
had a remote root exploit that allows you to just gain access to the whole web server, including the

50
00:03:41,100 --> 00:03:42,440
all the websites inside it.

51
00:03:42,450 --> 00:03:44,880
So this will be really cool if you could get it.

52
00:03:45,750 --> 00:03:51,300
If you couldn't find anything wrong with the applications installed on the web server, on the operating

53
00:03:51,300 --> 00:03:56,640
system, then you could target the humans because we know websites are managed by humans.

54
00:03:56,640 --> 00:04:02,100
For example, Facebook, you can target mark or you can target the admins of Facebook, for example.

55
00:04:02,100 --> 00:04:08,190
These admins obviously have more privileges on that website than you, and then they might be able to

56
00:04:08,190 --> 00:04:13,980
upload sensitive files to that website and then you can control it and hack it by hacking into one of

57
00:04:13,980 --> 00:04:14,640
those people.

58
00:04:14,640 --> 00:04:19,830
So instead of maybe the website is very secure and the server is very secure and there is no way that

59
00:04:19,830 --> 00:04:25,500
you can get in, but you can always exploit the humans using social engineering attacks and client side

60
00:04:25,500 --> 00:04:30,900
attacks to gain control or hack one of the people that manage that target website and then maybe gain

61
00:04:30,900 --> 00:04:31,800
access to it.

62
00:04:33,430 --> 00:04:37,720
Now this course will be concerned with the Web application penetration testing.

63
00:04:37,720 --> 00:04:40,660
So it's concerned with the first step, with the first approach.

64
00:04:40,660 --> 00:04:46,900
So we're going to learn how to discover and exploit a large number of vulnerabilities that can be fine

65
00:04:46,900 --> 00:04:49,300
found in the Web application itself.

66
00:04:49,300 --> 00:04:51,850
We're not going to be talking about server side attacks.

67
00:04:51,850 --> 00:04:57,280
So the attacks that exploit the operating system and the applications installed on the operating system

68
00:04:57,280 --> 00:05:02,380
itself of the Web server, and we're not going to be talking about the client side attacks, about attacking

69
00:05:02,380 --> 00:05:06,940
humans and hacking their accounts and then gaining access to the website.

70
00:05:07,270 --> 00:05:13,120
All of these these two the last two sections are actually covered into my general ethical hacking course.

71
00:05:13,120 --> 00:05:18,490
So I have a course called Learn Ethical Hacking from Scratch, which which covers those two aspects.

72
00:05:18,490 --> 00:05:24,070
Therefore, in this course, we're going to be focusing on the Web application penetration testing site.

73
00:05:24,070 --> 00:05:29,620
So on hacking the websites based on the Web applications installed on that website.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 17: Website Hacking - Writing a Crawler

1
00:00:00,870 --> 00:00:08,640
Right now, you probably know when it comes to hacking any system, the first step is always to gather

2
00:00:08,640 --> 00:00:12,270
as much information as possible about the system.

3
00:00:12,780 --> 00:00:21,120
So website hacking is no different, and this tips gets more and more important as websites get bigger.

4
00:00:21,750 --> 00:00:29,790
So these days we have huge websites like Google that offer a number of services and host a number of

5
00:00:29,790 --> 00:00:34,230
different web applications in different locations on their website.

6
00:00:34,890 --> 00:00:42,420
So if you want to test the security of this website and hack into it, you want to be able to test all

7
00:00:42,420 --> 00:00:49,260
of these Web applications, test all of the functionality offered by these Web applications, and hopefully

8
00:00:49,260 --> 00:00:53,970
find a vulnerability that will allow you to gain access to the system.

9
00:00:54,450 --> 00:01:02,010
Sometimes something as small as a search box or a neglected page might contain a vulnerability that

10
00:01:02,010 --> 00:01:04,800
would allow you to hack a complete server.

11
00:01:05,690 --> 00:01:13,670
Therefore, it is very important to gather as much information as possible about this website its directories,

12
00:01:13,670 --> 00:01:16,880
its files, its subdomains and so on.

13
00:01:17,840 --> 00:01:23,990
Now, the list here contains the main things that you usually want to gather about the website, but

14
00:01:23,990 --> 00:01:28,340
you don't need to use any programs to gather any of these things.

15
00:01:28,340 --> 00:01:35,690
In white in here, I cover how to gather this kind of information in my website hacking course, but

16
00:01:35,690 --> 00:01:40,160
I'm not going to talk about it in this course because you don't need any programming.

17
00:01:40,220 --> 00:01:44,180
And since this is a programming course, we're not going to cover them.

18
00:01:44,870 --> 00:01:50,630
What I'm going to cover, though, how to discover files, subdomains and directories.

19
00:01:51,380 --> 00:01:55,580
So the first thing that I want to talk about is subdomains.

20
00:01:55,580 --> 00:02:03,260
And basically what we mean by a subdomain is a part or a smaller part of the main domain.

21
00:02:04,220 --> 00:02:07,770
So going back to the example of Google.com.

22
00:02:07,790 --> 00:02:10,310
Google.com is the main domain.

23
00:02:10,310 --> 00:02:16,580
And then we have a number of subdomains that would allow you to access different websites that belong

24
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:17,330
to Google.

25
00:02:17,990 --> 00:02:24,920
So we can go to mail dot google.com to access Google's email web application.

26
00:02:24,920 --> 00:02:33,680
We can go to plus the Google.com to access Google's social network and we can go to drive and analytics,

27
00:02:33,680 --> 00:02:40,880
dot google.com and so on, each allowing you to access a completely different web application.

28
00:02:41,000 --> 00:02:47,060
Therefore, if you're trying to hack into Google, you want to test all of these subdomains and you

29
00:02:47,060 --> 00:02:48,620
need to discover all of them.

30
00:02:48,740 --> 00:02:52,430
Maybe there is a hidden subdomain that nobody knows about.

31
00:02:53,090 --> 00:02:55,080
This is actually very important.

32
00:02:55,100 --> 00:03:00,770
A lot of companies have hidden subdomains that they don't announce to everybody, so they might have

33
00:03:00,770 --> 00:03:07,520
a subdomain for the people that work for that company, or they might have a subdomain for a beta version

34
00:03:07,520 --> 00:03:08,560
of the website.

35
00:03:08,570 --> 00:03:11,000
So something that they are still testing.

36
00:03:11,000 --> 00:03:17,900
And usually these are great to find vulnerabilities because since this is a testing subdomain or if

37
00:03:17,900 --> 00:03:23,930
it's something that's designed for people that work at the company, sometimes it is not programmed

38
00:03:23,930 --> 00:03:26,390
to be as secure as the main website.

39
00:03:26,900 --> 00:03:31,520
So this brings us back to how important information gathering is.

40
00:03:32,210 --> 00:03:38,630
So the first program that we want to write, as you might have guessed by now, is a program that will

41
00:03:38,630 --> 00:03:42,590
allow us to discover subdomains on the Target website.

42
00:03:43,670 --> 00:03:50,390
Now in order to do that, if you were just a human being trying to discover subdomains on website,

43
00:03:50,390 --> 00:03:56,570
what you would do is you'll probably just go to your web browser and try these subdomains.

44
00:03:56,570 --> 00:04:01,220
So for example, you'll try to go to mail dot Google dot com.

45
00:04:02,690 --> 00:04:11,870
Then maybe you'll try to go to plus the Google dot com and if you get a website like we're getting here,

46
00:04:11,870 --> 00:04:15,410
you're going to think, oh, this is a subdomain that I should test.

47
00:04:15,440 --> 00:04:21,260
Otherwise, if you put something that doesn't exist, for example, let's just put some gibberish and

48
00:04:21,260 --> 00:04:26,960
you put google.com, you're going to get an error and you'll know that this subdomain doesn't exist.

49
00:04:27,530 --> 00:04:30,500
So we're going to follow the same idea.

50
00:04:30,500 --> 00:04:33,140
We're going to try a number of names.

51
00:04:33,150 --> 00:04:39,950
If we get a positive response, we're going to say that this subdomain exists, otherwise we will just

52
00:04:39,950 --> 00:04:43,130
neglect it and go to the next subdomain.

53
00:04:44,070 --> 00:04:52,510
So in order to do this, we need to have a way of communicating with a website through our Python program.

54
00:04:52,530 --> 00:04:56,490
So we're using a browser here to actually access the domain.

55
00:04:56,490 --> 00:05:02,760
But since we want to do this using a Python program, we're going to have to implement a way to send

56
00:05:02,760 --> 00:05:06,030
requests to a website through Python.

57
00:05:06,630 --> 00:05:12,810
Now, we've actually done something like this before using the requests library and we use this in our

58
00:05:12,810 --> 00:05:14,010
download function.

59
00:05:14,130 --> 00:05:23,520
So we used to do request dot get and we pass this a URL in order to request a specific URL and then

60
00:05:23,520 --> 00:05:24,750
download the file.

61
00:05:25,410 --> 00:05:29,220
So we're actually going to use the exact same code in our program.

62
00:05:30,030 --> 00:05:33,750
So I've already created a program called Crawler Dot PY.

63
00:05:34,140 --> 00:05:38,910
And in this program, the first thing I'm going to do is import requests.

64
00:05:40,440 --> 00:05:44,850
Then I'm going to specify the URL that I want to request.

65
00:05:45,210 --> 00:05:50,640
So we're going to save the URL is equal to google.com.

66
00:05:51,920 --> 00:05:58,240
Then I'm going to paste the line that I copied from my download dot pie file.

67
00:05:58,250 --> 00:06:04,550
And basically what this will do, it will call a function called get, which will allow us to send a

68
00:06:04,550 --> 00:06:12,860
get request using the requests library and we're passing the URL variable, which is google.com.

69
00:06:13,640 --> 00:06:20,870
Now we actually have to append HTTP to this because as you know in the browser, when you try to go

70
00:06:20,870 --> 00:06:25,970
to a website, you either go to it over HTTP or https.

71
00:06:25,970 --> 00:06:28,280
So that's the protocol that's used.

72
00:06:29,300 --> 00:06:35,600
So all we did so far is something very similar to what we were doing in our download API.

73
00:06:35,630 --> 00:06:41,630
We imported the requests library that allow us to send requests over HTTP.

74
00:06:41,720 --> 00:06:48,740
We specify the URL and stored it in a URL variable and we're sending a get request using the request

75
00:06:48,860 --> 00:06:57,080
library to the URL that we created and we're storing the result returned by this function into a variable

76
00:06:57,080 --> 00:06:58,820
called get response.

77
00:06:59,710 --> 00:07:02,920
Now finally, let's print the get response.

78
00:07:04,530 --> 00:07:06,660
And let's test our code.

79
00:07:07,050 --> 00:07:14,250
So I'm already in the working directory of my crawler and all I have to do to run it is do python crawler

80
00:07:14,260 --> 00:07:15,390
dot y.

81
00:07:15,660 --> 00:07:23,550
And if I hit enter, as you can see, we get a response and the code for this response is 200.

82
00:07:24,460 --> 00:07:30,840
Now we spoke about HTTP responses before and we said 200 means it's okay.

83
00:07:30,850 --> 00:07:37,210
The web server responded and was able to complete my request with no issues.

84
00:07:37,210 --> 00:07:42,280
So basically the URL that we requested exists and can be loaded.

85
00:07:43,360 --> 00:07:46,090
Now let's test this with a subdomain.

86
00:07:46,090 --> 00:07:53,710
So let's do mail, dot google.com and run the code again and again.

87
00:07:53,710 --> 00:07:59,290
As you can see, we're getting the status code of 200, which means that the web server is able to deliver

88
00:07:59,290 --> 00:08:06,490
my request and means that mail dot google.com actually exists on google.com.

89
00:08:07,490 --> 00:08:09,650
Now let's try to send some gibberish.

90
00:08:10,700 --> 00:08:12,380
And run the code again.

91
00:08:13,880 --> 00:08:16,730
And as you can see, we got an exception.

92
00:08:17,120 --> 00:08:23,420
And the exception is requests that exceptions that connection error.

93
00:08:23,900 --> 00:08:32,000
So based on this now we can use a try in here to say I want to try.

94
00:08:33,290 --> 00:08:35,150
To send a get request.

95
00:08:36,180 --> 00:08:37,860
And print the response.

96
00:08:38,100 --> 00:08:43,110
But I want you to also accept this exception.

97
00:08:43,920 --> 00:08:47,350
And if this exception happens, I don't want to do anything.

98
00:08:47,370 --> 00:08:48,930
I just want to pass.

99
00:08:49,650 --> 00:08:53,910
So basically, what's going to happen now if I clear the screen?

100
00:08:54,690 --> 00:08:58,050
If we request a subdomain that exists.

101
00:08:58,050 --> 00:09:04,800
So let's try plus that google.com, we're going to see the result on screen.

102
00:09:05,310 --> 00:09:12,030
Otherwise, if you try something that does not exist, the try in here is going to raise the exception

103
00:09:12,030 --> 00:09:18,500
that we just see and it's going to be matched by this line and nothing will be printed on screen.

104
00:09:18,510 --> 00:09:24,600
So if I run my code again, as you can see, nothing is printed on screen.

105
00:09:25,690 --> 00:09:32,290
So now using our code, we're able to, first of all, communicate with websites so we can send or get

106
00:09:32,290 --> 00:09:33,280
requests.

107
00:09:33,520 --> 00:09:40,900
And we can also verify whether this request was complete or if it failed.

108
00:09:41,080 --> 00:09:48,310
Using this, we're able to test whether a certain subdomain exists on the website or not.

109
00:09:49,060 --> 00:09:55,750
Now, in the next lecture, we're going to build on this so that we can create a program that will automatically

110
00:09:55,750 --> 00:09:59,530
discover subdomains on a target website.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,160 --> 00:00:08,420
Now in the previous lecture, we learned how to use Python to send get requests and communicate with

2
00:00:08,420 --> 00:00:10,580
websites over HTTP.

3
00:00:11,480 --> 00:00:20,210
As an example, we built a very simple program that can request a certain domain, and if that domain

4
00:00:20,210 --> 00:00:23,390
exists, it's going to print the response.

5
00:00:23,600 --> 00:00:26,060
Otherwise, it won't show us anything.

6
00:00:27,020 --> 00:00:35,030
Now in this lecture, I want to build on this program so that it can discover all the subdomains on

7
00:00:35,030 --> 00:00:35,930
my target.

8
00:00:36,730 --> 00:00:43,300
Now, before I get into how I'm going to implement that, the first thing that I want to do is convert

9
00:00:43,300 --> 00:00:45,010
this into a function.

10
00:00:46,000 --> 00:00:49,060
So what I want to do is have a function.

11
00:00:49,060 --> 00:00:52,810
And for example, let's call it request.

12
00:00:54,140 --> 00:00:58,550
And this function will take a URL as an input.

13
00:00:59,360 --> 00:01:04,310
And all of this code that we wrote so far is going to be part of the function.

14
00:01:04,460 --> 00:01:07,550
So it's going to try to create this request.

15
00:01:07,880 --> 00:01:12,260
And if it succeeds, I don't want to print anything.

16
00:01:13,100 --> 00:01:16,840
I just want to return the result of this.

17
00:01:16,850 --> 00:01:21,470
So instead of storing the result in a variable, I'm just going to return it.

18
00:01:22,400 --> 00:01:24,800
And otherwise it will just pass.

19
00:01:24,800 --> 00:01:26,870
And it will not return anything.

20
00:01:27,820 --> 00:01:34,120
So in the future we can just call the request URL and not worry about how this works.

21
00:01:34,900 --> 00:01:37,870
And we don't need this URL variable anymore.

22
00:01:38,590 --> 00:01:45,910
Now, like I said, my goal is to use this program to discover all subdomains on my target.

23
00:01:46,570 --> 00:01:50,020
Now, to do this, we're going to use a word list.

24
00:01:51,420 --> 00:01:56,730
What we mean by a word list is it's a simple text file similar to this one.

25
00:01:57,690 --> 00:02:03,000
Basically, it contains a number of words, each at a specific line.

26
00:02:03,480 --> 00:02:11,610
And what we're going to do from our program is read this file one line at a time, each time trying

27
00:02:11,610 --> 00:02:16,980
this word as a subdomain and see if this subdomain exists.

28
00:02:18,000 --> 00:02:22,830
Now, this technique is actually used a lot when it comes to penetration testing.

29
00:02:22,860 --> 00:02:25,730
It's used with cracking keys.

30
00:02:25,740 --> 00:02:30,640
It's used with gaining access by guessing to accounts.

31
00:02:30,660 --> 00:02:33,930
It's used in discovering subdomains, as you can see.

32
00:02:33,930 --> 00:02:41,580
And the whole idea is you use a word list, which is a file with a lot of words that could answer your

33
00:02:41,580 --> 00:02:45,690
question and then try all of these possibilities.

34
00:02:46,170 --> 00:02:48,660
Now, what I have here is a very small word list.

35
00:02:48,660 --> 00:02:54,600
As you can see, it's only 35 lines, but there are huge word lists out there that you can use.

36
00:02:54,600 --> 00:02:57,720
And I'm going to include some in the resources of this lecture.

37
00:02:58,230 --> 00:03:01,500
But for now, for testing, this word list is perfect.

38
00:03:02,310 --> 00:03:08,910
Now, I have all of this stored in a file called subdomains dot list in my downloads.

39
00:03:09,480 --> 00:03:15,000
So the first thing that we need to do in our program is to open this file.

40
00:03:15,480 --> 00:03:25,740
So to open a file in Python, as we seen before, all we do is with open and we have to specify the

41
00:03:25,740 --> 00:03:28,010
file name as the first argument.

42
00:03:28,020 --> 00:03:37,980
And my file name is stored in route downloads and the file name is subdomains that list.

43
00:03:40,090 --> 00:03:42,970
And I want to open this for reading only.

44
00:03:42,970 --> 00:03:46,510
So I'm going to type or as the second argument.

45
00:03:47,740 --> 00:03:51,790
And I want to open this as world list file.

46
00:03:53,080 --> 00:03:55,090
Now I'm just going to resize this.

47
00:03:56,690 --> 00:04:03,590
Now that we have the file open such as this, we want to read this file one line at a time.

48
00:04:04,160 --> 00:04:16,220
So before when we used to read file, we used to say content is equal to word list file dot read, but

49
00:04:16,220 --> 00:04:20,210
this will read the whole file as one string.

50
00:04:20,750 --> 00:04:24,700
What I want to do now is to read one line at a time.

51
00:04:24,710 --> 00:04:32,900
So what I'm going to do is instead of this, I'm going to say four line in my word list file.

52
00:04:34,210 --> 00:04:37,210
Let's print the line.

53
00:04:37,780 --> 00:04:47,470
So now Python is going to iterate over each line and execute the block of code that we put in here on

54
00:04:47,470 --> 00:04:49,570
each line in this file.

55
00:04:50,380 --> 00:04:52,020
So let's see what this will do.

56
00:04:52,030 --> 00:04:53,500
Let's run the file again.

57
00:04:54,830 --> 00:05:00,950
And as you can see, it's printed in each word, in the file, in a separate line.

58
00:05:00,950 --> 00:05:10,490
So basically it's iterating over each line and executing the print statement in here once in every iteration.

59
00:05:11,600 --> 00:05:18,110
So now we can use these words to build our possible subdomains.

60
00:05:18,500 --> 00:05:23,630
So the first thing that we need to do is to have a target URL.

61
00:05:24,960 --> 00:05:27,200
And this is going to be the base URL.

62
00:05:27,210 --> 00:05:32,940
So for example, it's just Google dot com without any subdomains.

63
00:05:33,730 --> 00:05:35,780
Then we're opening the file.

64
00:05:35,800 --> 00:05:45,100
We're reading one line at a time, and then we're going to say the test URL is going to be equal to

65
00:05:45,130 --> 00:05:46,480
the current line.

66
00:05:46,480 --> 00:05:49,000
So this word that we see in here.

67
00:05:50,010 --> 00:05:52,620
Plus the target URL.

68
00:05:52,620 --> 00:05:58,110
So we're literally appending each word that we see here to the target URL.

69
00:05:58,800 --> 00:06:06,690
Then let's just print the test URL to make sure that it looks fine as a normal URL before we use our

70
00:06:06,690 --> 00:06:08,790
request to send the request.

71
00:06:09,330 --> 00:06:18,750
So I'm going to clear the screen, run my program, and it looks okay, but it's not perfect.

72
00:06:18,960 --> 00:06:26,400
As you can see again, we're reading one line at a time and we're printing, first of all, the line,

73
00:06:26,400 --> 00:06:34,290
which is the word that we're reading from our file followed by the target URL, which is google.com.

74
00:06:34,950 --> 00:06:39,600
The only problem is, first of all, we forgot to put a dot between them.

75
00:06:39,900 --> 00:06:45,600
And also there is a line, there is a new line character between them.

76
00:06:45,600 --> 00:06:49,050
So the two words are not on the same line.

77
00:06:49,680 --> 00:06:57,130
The reason behind this is when you look at this file in here, each one of these words has a new line

78
00:06:57,150 --> 00:06:58,560
character after it.

79
00:06:58,650 --> 00:07:02,640
So you can think of this file to Python, it looks like so.

80
00:07:02,640 --> 00:07:08,550
So there's a slash and here there is a slash and here there is a slash end here and so on.

81
00:07:09,970 --> 00:07:14,100
So we actually have to strip this out of each line.

82
00:07:14,110 --> 00:07:21,040
And to do that, we can use this strip method in Python, which will remove whitespace characters from

83
00:07:21,040 --> 00:07:23,350
any string that you want to use.

84
00:07:24,070 --> 00:07:33,130
So what I'm going to say in here before I use the line, I'm going to say my word is equal to the line.

85
00:07:33,250 --> 00:07:34,840
That strip.

86
00:07:36,090 --> 00:07:42,750
And then instead of using the line here, I'm going to use the word which is identical to line, but

87
00:07:42,810 --> 00:07:46,620
with the whitespace characters stripped out of it.

88
00:07:47,710 --> 00:07:56,450
I'm also going to put a dot between the word and the target URL and then we're printing the test URL.

89
00:07:56,470 --> 00:07:58,360
So let's see what's going to happen.

90
00:07:59,920 --> 00:08:00,790
And perfect.

91
00:08:00,820 --> 00:08:08,260
Now, what's happening is we're looping or iterating over the file, reading one line at a time, and

92
00:08:08,260 --> 00:08:16,420
then we're appending the word that we are reading to the Google.com, to the target website or the target

93
00:08:16,450 --> 00:08:17,230
URL.

94
00:08:18,920 --> 00:08:27,140
Now the next step is to test all of these possible domains and see if any of them actually exists on

95
00:08:27,140 --> 00:08:28,040
my target.

96
00:08:28,730 --> 00:08:36,890
Now, to do that test, we've already implemented a method that can send a get request and return the

97
00:08:36,890 --> 00:08:37,700
result.

98
00:08:38,210 --> 00:08:43,670
If the request fails, we will not get anything, so we will basically get none.

99
00:08:44,600 --> 00:08:53,150
So all we have to do is instead of printing the test URL in here, we can call our method, which is

100
00:08:53,150 --> 00:08:54,050
request.

101
00:08:55,290 --> 00:08:57,900
And we're passing the test URL to it.

102
00:08:58,290 --> 00:09:02,370
Then this method will run and return a result.

103
00:09:02,700 --> 00:09:08,340
Now we're going to capture this result by a variable and we'll call this variable response.

104
00:09:09,150 --> 00:09:17,430
And then we're going to test if we actually got a response, because as we see in here, if this method

105
00:09:17,430 --> 00:09:19,800
fails, it will not return anything.

106
00:09:19,800 --> 00:09:21,870
So response will contain none.

107
00:09:21,960 --> 00:09:30,540
So what we're saying is if we actually have a response, we're going to print on screen that we discovered

108
00:09:30,540 --> 00:09:31,740
a subdomain.

109
00:09:33,090 --> 00:09:37,710
And the value of this subdomain is the test URL.

110
00:09:38,840 --> 00:09:40,460
So very, very simple.

111
00:09:40,550 --> 00:09:46,790
All we're doing is we're calling our request method with the test URL that we built before.

112
00:09:47,090 --> 00:09:52,610
If we actually get a response, we're going to print the test URL on screen because that means this

113
00:09:52,610 --> 00:09:55,220
URL exists on my target.

114
00:09:55,250 --> 00:09:57,500
Otherwise we won't do anything.

115
00:09:58,160 --> 00:10:01,550
So let's clear the screen and test this.

116
00:10:02,920 --> 00:10:09,550
And as you can see now, the tool is working perfectly and it's printing the subdomains that actually

117
00:10:09,550 --> 00:10:12,190
exist on Google.com.

118
00:10:13,000 --> 00:10:21,280
So as you can see out of the 35 words that we have in our word list, only a few of them exist on the

119
00:10:21,280 --> 00:10:21,970
target.

120
00:10:21,970 --> 00:10:25,240
And all of these are valid subdomains.

121
00:10:26,330 --> 00:10:29,660
Now you can actually create a list in here.

122
00:10:29,660 --> 00:10:35,990
And then every time you discover a subdomain, you can append it to the list in here and then by the

123
00:10:35,990 --> 00:10:39,200
end you'll have a list of all the subdomains.

124
00:10:39,230 --> 00:10:42,840
You can also store this and write it to a file.

125
00:10:42,860 --> 00:10:46,700
But we covered all of this before, so I don't want to make this too long.

126
00:10:47,390 --> 00:10:54,170
The whole point of writing this program is to write a program that can be used to discover all possible

127
00:10:54,170 --> 00:10:56,060
subdomains on a target.

128
00:10:56,960 --> 00:11:03,500
As we did this, we learned how to read a file one line at a time because this is something we never

129
00:11:03,500 --> 00:11:04,580
covered before.

130
00:11:05,060 --> 00:11:12,740
We also learned how to interact with a website from our Python program using this request method.

131
00:11:13,730 --> 00:11:18,160
So in the future will actually be using this method to build other programs.

132
00:11:18,170 --> 00:11:25,240
And as I always say, everything that we covered so far can be used in so many other scenarios.

133
00:11:25,250 --> 00:11:32,540
So the way of reading the file one line at a time or the way of sending requests over HTTP using the

134
00:11:32,540 --> 00:11:36,380
request library can be used in so many other scenarios.

135
00:11:36,380 --> 00:11:43,160
So I'm just using this as an example to make it easier for you to understand these concepts.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,140 --> 00:00:01,840
Okay.

2
00:00:01,860 --> 00:00:09,600
Now, using the program that we built in the previous section, we're able to discover all the subdomains

3
00:00:09,600 --> 00:00:11,880
on a specific website.

4
00:00:12,660 --> 00:00:19,830
Now, each one of these subdomains might contain a number of directories and files.

5
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:22,850
Similar to subdomains.

6
00:00:22,850 --> 00:00:29,900
These directories might contain other web applications and these files might offer other functionality

7
00:00:29,900 --> 00:00:32,420
that's not offered on the main website.

8
00:00:32,420 --> 00:00:40,010
And therefore, if we want to hack or test the security of this website, we need to test the web applications

9
00:00:40,070 --> 00:00:45,920
in these directories and look for any sensitive files that they might contain.

10
00:00:46,550 --> 00:00:53,180
Now, when we talk about directory, if you look at any URL that you visit, anything that you see after

11
00:00:53,180 --> 00:01:01,370
the forward slash means this is a directory in the web root or in the main directory of the website.

12
00:01:01,760 --> 00:01:09,170
Now web admins can actually manipulate this, but the general rule is anything that you see after the

13
00:01:09,170 --> 00:01:11,750
forward slash is a directory name.

14
00:01:12,650 --> 00:01:15,830
So let me show you a quick example of what I mean.

15
00:01:16,010 --> 00:01:22,780
Now we know on Linux the web root is bar w w w HTML.

16
00:01:22,790 --> 00:01:29,360
So we have a web server that comes with Kali Linux and the main directory that contains the website

17
00:01:29,360 --> 00:01:32,870
files is var w w w h html.

18
00:01:33,580 --> 00:01:41,170
So if I go to my IP, which is ten zero 216, so you can think of this as the domain name.

19
00:01:41,290 --> 00:01:49,000
And then after the first forward slash, if I put any of the file names in here, I'll be able to access

20
00:01:49,000 --> 00:01:49,390
it.

21
00:01:49,420 --> 00:01:59,620
So for example, if I wanted to open this PDF, if I just put front that PDF, that'll load this file

22
00:01:59,620 --> 00:02:00,310
for me.

23
00:02:01,210 --> 00:02:05,160
Now we have a directory in here called eval files.

24
00:02:05,170 --> 00:02:13,930
So if I double click this, you can see I have all the files inside this directory to access this directory

25
00:02:13,930 --> 00:02:15,280
from a web browser.

26
00:02:15,280 --> 00:02:19,750
We have to put the directory name after the first forward slash.

27
00:02:19,870 --> 00:02:28,930
So if I type evil files, you'll see I have the directory here and I have all the files inside this

28
00:02:28,930 --> 00:02:29,710
directory.

29
00:02:30,490 --> 00:02:36,340
Now, again, in here, if I create a new directory and let's call this test.

30
00:02:37,520 --> 00:02:44,870
Then we can access this subdirectory inside the evil files by again appending another forward slash

31
00:02:44,870 --> 00:02:50,090
test which will load my test directory, which is open at the moment.

32
00:02:50,800 --> 00:03:00,100
So what I want to do now is to modify this program so that we can use it to discover directories and

33
00:03:00,100 --> 00:03:02,260
files on our target.

34
00:03:02,680 --> 00:03:09,670
Now to do this, I actually have a word list for common directories called common text.

35
00:03:09,700 --> 00:03:16,690
I've actually taken it from a program called Deb, which is made to discover directories on a Target

36
00:03:16,690 --> 00:03:17,470
website.

37
00:03:17,620 --> 00:03:19,990
So I'll include this in the resources.

38
00:03:19,990 --> 00:03:26,020
And as you can see, it's a text file with possible directory names on each line.

39
00:03:26,950 --> 00:03:32,980
So I'm going to go back to my code and the first thing that I'm going to do, instead of opening the

40
00:03:32,980 --> 00:03:39,070
subdomains dot list, I'm going to open common dot text.

41
00:03:39,850 --> 00:03:42,580
So so far we haven't done anything crazy.

42
00:03:42,880 --> 00:03:49,480
Then our program is going to open this file and it's going to iterate over it one line at a time.

43
00:03:49,780 --> 00:03:57,190
It's going to read the word in there and remove any white line characters and then it's going to create

44
00:03:57,190 --> 00:03:58,030
the test.

45
00:03:58,030 --> 00:04:05,500
You are l now because we were looking for subdomains, we were put in the word followed by a dot, followed

46
00:04:05,500 --> 00:04:08,800
by the target URL, which is google.com.

47
00:04:09,520 --> 00:04:13,090
In our example, we're looking for directories.

48
00:04:13,090 --> 00:04:21,280
So as we see in here, the directory is come after the domain name and they are preceded with a forward

49
00:04:21,280 --> 00:04:22,030
slash.

50
00:04:22,930 --> 00:04:25,840
So I'm going to remove all of this.

51
00:04:26,940 --> 00:04:35,190
And our test URL is going to be the target URL, which is google.com plus a forward slash.

52
00:04:35,970 --> 00:04:37,770
Plus the word.

53
00:04:38,490 --> 00:04:41,320
Then we're going to send this as a request.

54
00:04:41,340 --> 00:04:43,620
We're going to capture that in a response.

55
00:04:43,620 --> 00:04:51,690
And if we have a positive response, we're going to say, discovered your URL this time and that's it.

56
00:04:51,690 --> 00:04:52,980
Our program is done.

57
00:04:53,580 --> 00:04:57,030
So we actually didn't need to modify the program a lot.

58
00:04:57,150 --> 00:04:59,610
And we're using the same basic concept.

59
00:04:59,610 --> 00:05:04,440
We're sending a request of a directory that we think might exist.

60
00:05:04,620 --> 00:05:10,110
If the response is positive, then this URL exists, otherwise it doesn't.

61
00:05:10,980 --> 00:05:15,660
Again, you can store the results in here in a list or write them to a file.

62
00:05:15,660 --> 00:05:21,210
And we covered all of that previously, so I don't want to spend too much time on it now.

63
00:05:21,210 --> 00:05:25,830
One thing I want to modify, I don't want to run this against Google.com.

64
00:05:26,610 --> 00:05:33,170
I'm going to run this against a web application that comes preinstalled in Metasploit table.

65
00:05:33,840 --> 00:05:41,760
Now, like I said, Metasploit table is a vulnerable system that is designed so that we can use it to

66
00:05:41,760 --> 00:05:44,670
test vulnerabilities and hack into it.

67
00:05:45,210 --> 00:05:53,100
Metasploit table comes with a web server similar to Kali, and it has a number of web applications installed

68
00:05:53,100 --> 00:05:53,790
on it.

69
00:05:54,300 --> 00:06:00,450
So to access the web server of Metasploit, well, first of all, you need to make sure it's connected

70
00:06:00,450 --> 00:06:03,930
to the same knot network as the Kali machine.

71
00:06:04,410 --> 00:06:12,960
And then if I do, if config in here, you'll see it's IP right there, which is ten 0 to 20, therefore

72
00:06:12,960 --> 00:06:15,360
it is on the same subnet as Kali.

73
00:06:15,810 --> 00:06:21,150
So if we come here and just go to ten zero 220.

74
00:06:22,220 --> 00:06:28,940
You'll see we have a web server of Metasploit table and it actually has a number of web applications

75
00:06:28,940 --> 00:06:30,170
installed on it.

76
00:06:31,010 --> 00:06:35,440
The web application that I want to run this test against is Matilda.

77
00:06:35,450 --> 00:06:38,660
So if I click on it, you'll see a normal website.

78
00:06:38,660 --> 00:06:45,050
And by the way, even though this website is installed on a computer that is designed to be hacked,

79
00:06:45,380 --> 00:06:50,000
this website uses technologies that is used by normal websites.

80
00:06:50,000 --> 00:06:51,410
So it uses PHP.

81
00:06:51,440 --> 00:06:58,220
It uses a MySQL database, and therefore it acts and behaves just like a normal website.

82
00:06:58,790 --> 00:07:04,730
The main reason I'm using this website is because I don't want to run this test against websites that

83
00:07:04,730 --> 00:07:06,650
I don't have permission to test.

84
00:07:06,650 --> 00:07:10,410
And that's the main reason that websites like this one exists.

85
00:07:10,430 --> 00:07:14,030
It's for people like us who want to learn hacking legally.

86
00:07:14,900 --> 00:07:24,080
So I'm going to copy this and I'm going to set this as my target and I'm going to remove the HTTP because

87
00:07:24,080 --> 00:07:26,360
as you know, we're appending it in here.

88
00:07:27,470 --> 00:07:28,520
And we're done.

89
00:07:28,970 --> 00:07:33,470
So I'm going to clear the screen and I'm going to run the program.

90
00:07:35,310 --> 00:07:42,330
And as you can see, the program is running and it's able to discover directories on this website on

91
00:07:42,330 --> 00:07:43,890
the target that we gave it.

92
00:07:44,730 --> 00:07:47,590
So you can see some really useful stuff.

93
00:07:47,610 --> 00:07:50,800
For example, we managed to discover the login page.

94
00:07:50,820 --> 00:07:53,190
This could be the login for the admin.

95
00:07:53,340 --> 00:07:59,520
And this is actually very important when it comes to penetration testing in real life scenarios where

96
00:07:59,520 --> 00:08:06,510
I was doing pen tests, sometimes I was able to exploit the system and gain access to the database and

97
00:08:06,510 --> 00:08:09,390
get the username and password for the admin.

98
00:08:09,390 --> 00:08:12,480
But I couldn't find where to log in.

99
00:08:12,480 --> 00:08:20,340
So tools like this one that's able to discover files and directories are really, really useful because

100
00:08:20,340 --> 00:08:27,060
they're going to show you all the directories and files that exist on that website that you could use

101
00:08:27,060 --> 00:08:33,630
in order to log in or maybe discover hidden stuff that the admin does not want you to see.

102
00:08:34,260 --> 00:08:42,270
For example, we have a directory here called passwords, so if we copy this and access it here, you'll

103
00:08:42,270 --> 00:08:47,300
see it has a file called accounts attached with usernames and passwords.

104
00:08:47,310 --> 00:08:53,400
And trust me, maybe you don't see this a lot in real life scenarios, but it does happen.

105
00:08:54,480 --> 00:09:00,150
Another really useful file that we have here, and sometimes you see it in the wild and web admins forget

106
00:09:00,150 --> 00:09:02,730
it, which is the PHP info.

107
00:09:03,180 --> 00:09:11,790
Now this file displays comprehensive information about the web server, and sometimes web admins use

108
00:09:11,790 --> 00:09:16,920
this file to get information about their own server, but then they forget to delete it.

109
00:09:17,010 --> 00:09:23,310
So again, we're able to discover it and if we were doing a test, this is very useful to us because

110
00:09:23,310 --> 00:09:27,390
we'll be able to get so much information about our target.

111
00:09:28,910 --> 00:09:31,040
So that's it for this lecture.

112
00:09:31,250 --> 00:09:38,150
All we have to do is slightly modify our program in order to use it to discover all the directories

113
00:09:38,150 --> 00:09:40,880
and files on a Target website.

114
00:09:41,600 --> 00:09:46,790
And this just goes to show when I say everything that I show you is adaptable.

115
00:09:46,820 --> 00:09:54,020
It doesn't have to be used in this scenario, but I'm just showing you how to use it in an example so

116
00:09:54,020 --> 00:09:56,510
that it's easier for you to understand.

117
00:09:57,440 --> 00:10:04,040
Now, what I should note in here, you can see all of the directories and the files that we discovered

118
00:10:04,040 --> 00:10:06,710
are within the Matilda directory.

119
00:10:07,250 --> 00:10:14,210
Now, for example, the Styles directory right here might contain other files and directories.

120
00:10:14,660 --> 00:10:19,670
So what you could do in this case is use another loop.

121
00:10:19,670 --> 00:10:27,560
And here and again, loop over all of the entries of the word list passing the current test URL.

122
00:10:27,830 --> 00:10:32,990
This will allow you to discover all the files and directories within this URL.

123
00:10:33,470 --> 00:10:38,030
You could also combine all of this with the program that discovers the domains.

124
00:10:38,030 --> 00:10:43,970
So in that way you'll have a program that discovers domains, directories and files at the same time.

125
00:10:44,120 --> 00:10:50,300
So similar to everything that we do, really, you can combine this code and you can extend it and get

126
00:10:50,300 --> 00:10:53,780
it to do different things to suit your own scenario.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,720 --> 00:00:01,590
So far.

2
00:00:01,590 --> 00:00:10,170
We built a crawler that we can use to guess subdomains, directories and files on our target.

3
00:00:11,280 --> 00:00:19,740
Now this is great because it allows us to discover hidden paths or locations that the admin does not

4
00:00:19,740 --> 00:00:21,280
want us to know.

5
00:00:21,300 --> 00:00:28,440
So for example, we might be able to discover locations that's designed to test certain functionality.

6
00:00:28,440 --> 00:00:31,380
Locations of beta web applications.

7
00:00:31,620 --> 00:00:39,660
Locations of web applications that's only designed for the admin or for the employees of the company

8
00:00:39,660 --> 00:00:40,410
to use.

9
00:00:40,440 --> 00:00:47,070
Therefore, the admin might not advertise the existence of these locations, and we'll never be able

10
00:00:47,070 --> 00:00:49,260
to discover them without guessing.

11
00:00:50,310 --> 00:00:56,120
Now the disadvantage of this is because it relies on a word list or on guessing.

12
00:00:56,130 --> 00:00:59,640
It will not discover everything on the target.

13
00:01:00,090 --> 00:01:03,120
So the solution is to combine both.

14
00:01:03,120 --> 00:01:09,810
So first of all, discover the hidden paths so that if there are any hidden web applications or hidden

15
00:01:09,810 --> 00:01:11,940
files, we'll be able to see them.

16
00:01:12,060 --> 00:01:18,780
And then for each of these hidden paths and for the ones that are being advertised by the Web admin,

17
00:01:18,780 --> 00:01:23,190
we're going to analyze these paths and follow the links on them.

18
00:01:24,060 --> 00:01:29,610
For example, let's say I am trying to test the security of my own website.

19
00:01:29,610 --> 00:01:36,030
So when I load the website and when any user loads the website, they will get the normal index or the

20
00:01:36,030 --> 00:01:38,370
normal home page of this website.

21
00:01:38,910 --> 00:01:45,030
So if I wanted to analyse all the functionality in this website, I'm going to have to click on each

22
00:01:45,030 --> 00:01:52,590
of these links, see what page it takes me to, and try to exploit this page and see if I can hack the

23
00:01:52,590 --> 00:01:54,360
website through this page.

24
00:01:54,990 --> 00:02:00,840
So in order to discover all the paths in this web page, we have to click on all the links.

25
00:02:01,290 --> 00:02:07,800
Now, for us as humans, this is easy because we know when the mouse pointer changes to the finger pointer

26
00:02:07,800 --> 00:02:12,720
like this one, we know this is a link and we know that we can click it to go somewhere else.

27
00:02:13,320 --> 00:02:19,600
But to a computer, this website is not made out of images and colors.

28
00:02:19,620 --> 00:02:23,220
This website is literally HTML code.

29
00:02:23,880 --> 00:02:29,040
So if I right click this website and click on view page source.

30
00:02:29,770 --> 00:02:34,150
You will see the HTML code that makes up this website.

31
00:02:35,130 --> 00:02:42,360
So basically this whole website, the images, the videos, the links, the text, everything is literally

32
00:02:42,360 --> 00:02:47,880
found in here and it's organized in a markup language called HTML.

33
00:02:49,010 --> 00:02:54,020
So as an example, as you can see here, we have a link to one of the courses.

34
00:02:54,020 --> 00:02:58,280
So it's the Advanced Network Hacking course and you can see it in here.

35
00:02:58,280 --> 00:03:02,180
So it's literally the link that is displayed in here.

36
00:03:03,630 --> 00:03:10,560
So if we want to make a list of all the links on this page, the first thing that we need to do from

37
00:03:10,560 --> 00:03:17,340
our Python program is to load the source code or the HTML code that we are seeing in here.

38
00:03:18,230 --> 00:03:20,060
So let's go ahead and do that.

39
00:03:20,510 --> 00:03:27,800
Now, I've created a new project and I called it Spider, and right now it contains identical code to

40
00:03:27,800 --> 00:03:29,900
the code that we used in the crawler.

41
00:03:30,830 --> 00:03:36,560
So the first thing that I'm going to do is actually change my target URL and I'm going to set that to

42
00:03:36,560 --> 00:03:38,300
Z security dot org.

43
00:03:39,470 --> 00:03:43,910
The next thing that I'm going to do is I'm actually going to remove all of this because we don't want

44
00:03:43,910 --> 00:03:44,990
to open a word list.

45
00:03:44,990 --> 00:03:47,330
We don't want to rely on guessing.

46
00:03:47,900 --> 00:03:53,870
Now, once I have this URL, the first thing that I want to do is communicate with this website.

47
00:03:53,870 --> 00:03:58,370
And we already implemented a function that does that for us, which is the request function.

48
00:03:58,370 --> 00:04:01,810
And as usual, I don't really care how this works anymore.

49
00:04:01,820 --> 00:04:08,600
I implemented it before, so all I'm going to do now is use it and I'm going to say the response.

50
00:04:09,340 --> 00:04:14,860
Is going to be equal to the request and I'm going to pass it my target URL.

51
00:04:15,550 --> 00:04:21,460
And if we print the response, as you know, this will just show me the response object.

52
00:04:21,610 --> 00:04:27,370
So if I just run this real quick here and I'm already in the working directory of my spider.

53
00:04:29,670 --> 00:04:30,480
You will see.

54
00:04:30,480 --> 00:04:35,250
I'll get the response, which is 200 and this is literally just the object.

55
00:04:36,090 --> 00:04:41,940
Now, we've seen before how to see the content of the response when we were implementing the download

56
00:04:41,940 --> 00:04:42,540
method.

57
00:04:43,050 --> 00:04:50,610
So we've seen if we print the response content, we were able to get the binary code of the image or

58
00:04:50,610 --> 00:04:55,020
the X when we were downloading it, and then we wrote that to a file.

59
00:04:55,770 --> 00:05:04,770
Now in our case we are making a request to z security dot org, which is by default will load the default

60
00:05:05,190 --> 00:05:07,620
HTML page on this website.

61
00:05:08,340 --> 00:05:14,280
Therefore, this request will actually return a HTML website, which is the one that we're seeing in

62
00:05:14,280 --> 00:05:15,660
here and in here.

63
00:05:17,030 --> 00:05:21,650
So if I print the content now, I should get the HTML code.

64
00:05:22,820 --> 00:05:23,870
And perfect.

65
00:05:23,990 --> 00:05:29,720
As you can see, I have the HTML code that makes up this whole page.

66
00:05:29,720 --> 00:05:34,100
This page that gets loaded when we go to Z security dot org.

67
00:05:34,670 --> 00:05:43,400
So now I have all of this code stored in my response content variable as a normal string.

68
00:05:44,190 --> 00:05:49,080
This means that we can use all of the things that we learned about strengths with it.

69
00:05:49,080 --> 00:05:56,550
And the main important thing is we will be able to use regex like we've seen before to look for some

70
00:05:56,550 --> 00:05:58,740
strings within this big string.

71
00:05:58,740 --> 00:06:04,440
So we'll be able to extract any HTML element from this string.

72
00:06:04,590 --> 00:06:09,420
This will allow us to extract anything that's loaded on the page that the user sees.

73
00:06:09,420 --> 00:06:16,830
So we'll be able to extract images, links, scripts, or anything that might be useful in your scenario.

74
00:06:17,280 --> 00:06:24,300
In our example, we're interested in getting all the links in this page, and I'll show you how to do

75
00:06:24,300 --> 00:06:26,070
that in the next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,090 --> 00:00:07,950
Now that we know how to get the HTML code of any web page in this lecture, I want to show you how to

2
00:00:07,950 --> 00:00:12,420
parse this HTML code and extract useful data.

3
00:00:12,930 --> 00:00:20,310
Now, as you know in this example, we are trying to extract all the links in the target page, but

4
00:00:20,310 --> 00:00:26,520
you can use the same method to extract any element from the page, whether it's images, scripts or

5
00:00:26,520 --> 00:00:28,200
anything that you want, really.

6
00:00:28,650 --> 00:00:34,590
Now, the most common way to include links in a page is using the A tag.

7
00:00:35,130 --> 00:00:42,780
So in general, if you have a HTML page and you're not sure which tag is being used or what type of

8
00:00:42,780 --> 00:00:46,600
code is responsible for rendering a specific part.

9
00:00:46,620 --> 00:00:53,460
For example, if I wasn't sure how this is being displayed on screen, all you have to do is right click

10
00:00:53,460 --> 00:01:01,230
it, click on Inspect Element, and this will highlight the HTML part that's loading this.

11
00:01:01,230 --> 00:01:06,360
And as you can see, if I put the mouse here, it will literally highlight the image.

12
00:01:06,360 --> 00:01:11,850
So it's telling me this part of the code is responsible for loading the image.

13
