00:01:12,510 --> 00:01:18,390
And you can actually see we have a link to an image in here and if I hover over it, it will show me

14
00:01:18,390 --> 00:01:19,140
the image.

15
00:01:19,890 --> 00:01:22,260
Now, I'm not interested in the image.

16
00:01:22,260 --> 00:01:26,430
I'm actually interested in the link that this image would take me to.

17
00:01:26,730 --> 00:01:35,880
And if we look in here, you'll see we have an A tag, we have a ref and the ref is equal to the link

18
00:01:35,880 --> 00:01:37,230
that we will go to.

19
00:01:37,620 --> 00:01:43,200
So this is the most common way of linking elements to a certain link.

20
00:01:43,200 --> 00:01:51,180
You use the a tag in HTML and you use the ref to specify the link that the user should go to when they

21
00:01:51,180 --> 00:01:52,710
click on this element.

22
00:01:53,780 --> 00:02:02,210
So in our code, if we look for the hate trap in the HTML code, then we should be able to extract all

23
00:02:02,210 --> 00:02:03,710
the links in this page.

24
00:02:04,370 --> 00:02:11,140
Now we know how to look for stuff in a string in text and we usually use regex to do that.

25
00:02:11,150 --> 00:02:16,100
So we're going to use the same way that we always see and we're going to first import three.

26
00:02:16,520 --> 00:02:22,750
And then in my code, after I receive the response, I'm going to say, Hey, Trev.

27
00:02:23,510 --> 00:02:33,470
Links is equal to re dot find all because I want to find all of the sub strings that will match my regex,

28
00:02:33,470 --> 00:02:34,700
not only one.

29
00:02:35,240 --> 00:02:44,540
And the regex that I'm going to use is going to be followed by an equal, followed by a double quote.

30
00:02:44,720 --> 00:02:52,880
And inside this, I'm going to accept any type of characters all the way up to the next double quote.

31
00:02:52,910 --> 00:02:59,690
So basically what we're doing is let me just look for an example in here.

32
00:03:00,320 --> 00:03:05,150
So right here, we have a link to the courses page.

33
00:03:05,270 --> 00:03:11,840
And basically what we're saying is I'm looking for regex that starts with h ref equals double quote

34
00:03:11,840 --> 00:03:16,940
and I want to match everything up to the last double quote.

35
00:03:17,840 --> 00:03:18,440
Now.

36
00:03:18,440 --> 00:03:22,830
I don't want to see the hatred as part of my regex.

37
00:03:22,850 --> 00:03:27,440
So as I showed you before, I'm going to split this into two groups.

38
00:03:27,440 --> 00:03:34,220
So the first group is the stuff that's going to be used to locate my interesting text but will not be

39
00:03:34,220 --> 00:03:35,200
part of it.

40
00:03:35,210 --> 00:03:38,720
So that's the ref equals double quote.

41
00:03:38,990 --> 00:03:46,310
And the second part is the part that is interesting to me, which is everything that follows that and

42
00:03:46,310 --> 00:03:52,820
I'm going to mark the first group as non matching by putting a question mark followed by a colon.

43
00:03:53,690 --> 00:03:55,130
Now that's perfect.

44
00:03:55,130 --> 00:04:02,030
The only problem with this regex right now is it will first look for hatred of equals, double quote.

45
00:04:02,030 --> 00:04:09,920
So something similar to this and then it will match everything up to the last double quote that it sees.

46
00:04:09,920 --> 00:04:16,730
So it's basically going to keep going down all the way down to the end of the text until it sees the

47
00:04:16,730 --> 00:04:18,170
last double quote.

48
00:04:18,560 --> 00:04:26,630
But what I want to do is I want this to be non greedy and I want it to match everything up to the first

49
00:04:26,630 --> 00:04:28,250
double quote that it sees.

50
00:04:28,250 --> 00:04:35,180
So I wanted to match from the first double quote to the second, and I don't want it to keep going up

51
00:04:35,180 --> 00:04:36,920
to the last double quote.

52
00:04:37,490 --> 00:04:44,630
Therefore, I'm going to add a question mark to this group right here to say this group should be non

53
00:04:44,630 --> 00:04:45,320
greedy.

54
00:04:45,350 --> 00:04:49,100
It should match everything up to the first double quote.

55
00:04:51,040 --> 00:04:58,120
And because we're using find all, then it'll match all the sub strings that will match the string.

56
00:04:59,020 --> 00:05:04,150
Now, if you're not sure about how this is going to work, you can go ahead and use PI ethics and do

57
00:05:04,150 --> 00:05:05,570
your tests in there.

58
00:05:05,590 --> 00:05:11,020
I covered using Python X and creating regex with both of these concepts before.

59
00:05:11,020 --> 00:05:16,430
So we covered the non greedy part and we covered the non matching group part.

60
00:05:16,450 --> 00:05:19,000
That's why I don't want to make a big deal out of it.

61
00:05:19,000 --> 00:05:24,880
If you don't understand how this is working, please go back to the lectures that cover that and test

62
00:05:24,880 --> 00:05:26,530
them with Python x.

63
00:05:28,250 --> 00:05:35,180
Now we specified the regex, but we haven't specified the string that we want to look for this regex

64
00:05:35,180 --> 00:05:35,690
in.

65
00:05:36,680 --> 00:05:41,360
And obviously the strings that we're going to search is the response.

66
00:05:42,320 --> 00:05:43,490
Dot content.

67
00:05:44,060 --> 00:05:51,170
And finally, once I'm done with this, I'm going to print the links instead of the response content.

68
00:05:52,870 --> 00:05:53,410
And that's it.

69
00:05:53,410 --> 00:05:54,040
We're done.

70
00:05:54,940 --> 00:06:01,300
So basically all we're doing is we're searching the source code for all these strings that will match

71
00:06:01,300 --> 00:06:09,160
this rule, and this rule will match anything that starts with a Hey Trev equals double quote, and

72
00:06:09,160 --> 00:06:13,390
it will match everything inside that up to the next double quote.

73
00:06:14,830 --> 00:06:18,280
So let's clear the screen and run the code.

74
00:06:20,350 --> 00:06:21,370
On Perfect.

75
00:06:21,400 --> 00:06:28,780
As you can see, I have a list now in which each element is a link that's included in the current web

76

page.

77

Now, these links might not be to this website.

78

80
00:06:43,990 --> 00:06:44,710
page.

81
00:06:45,810 --> 00:06:53,050
And like I said, this same method can be used to extract any part that you want from any web page.

82
00:06:53,070 --> 00:06:56,610
So we're just using extract and links as an example.

83

84

element from the web page.

85
00:07:04,440 --> 00:07:08,790
But you can use the exact same method that I showed you to achieve that.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1

Now so far we built a crawler that we can give it a website.

But before we do that, I want to refactor this code because I think it's really, really ugly.

Now, the first thing that you'll notice is that we actually don't need to use this request method.

7
00:00:36,490 --> 00:00:44,560
This method basically calls the requests dot get and I can just call this directly in here in my code.

8
00:00:45,620 --> 00:00:48,200
And then I won't need to use this method.

9
00:00:48,350 --> 00:00:55,520
The only reason why I use this method before is because this code was originally written when we were

10

11

valid is by catching this exception.

00:01:12,020 --> 00:01:17,750

14

And the next thing that I want to do is create a function that I can pass in the URL and it will return

15
00:01:26,810 --> 00:01:30,770
me all the links that can be found in this URL.

16
00:01:31,550 --> 00:01:35,930
So this functionality is actually implemented in these two lines.

17
00:01:35,930 --> 00:01:42,020
So all I have to do is just create the function and let's call it extract length from.

18
00:01:43,760 --> 00:01:46,730
And this will take a URL as an input.

19
00:01:48,160 --> 00:01:55,840
And then it's going to get the response by calling requests don't get and it will pass the URL that

20
00:01:55,840 --> 00:02:01,150
it got as an input and then the result will be analyzed at this line.

21
00:02:01,150 --> 00:02:03,610
So we don't need to store it in a variable.

22

We're just going to return this.

23
00:02:06,710 --> 00:02:09,230
So it's the exact same code that we did before.

00:02:09,260 --> 00:02:11,780
It's just that it's much, much cleaner.

25

Then we can minimize this and forget how it works.

00:02:15,470 --> 00:02:25,700
And we can just say my hatred of links are equal to extract links from and we'll pass the target URL

00:02:25,730 --> 00:02:27,620
as the input to this method.

00:02:27,650 --> 00:02:35,270
This method will first submit a get request to this URL and then extract the links at this line and

29

return it.

30
00:02:36,160 --> 00:02:39,890
It will be captured in here and it will be printed on screen.

So let's clear this and make sure we didn't break anything and perfect.

00:02:45,650 --> 00:02:50,510
As you can see, the program is working and it's given us the same result that we were getting at the

33
00:02:50,510 --> 00:02:51,710
start of the lecture.

34
00:02:52,280 --> 00:02:56,950
And the next thing that I want to do now is actually start fixing these links.

35
00:02:56,960 --> 00:03:03,170
But before I do that, I need to show you these links and why I think this program needs improvement.

36
00:03:03,860 --> 00:03:08,510
So instead of printing the links like this, I want to print them one at a time.

37
00:03:08,510 --> 00:03:16,520
So I'm going to use a loop and we see how to do this before I'm going to say for each link in my links,

38
00:03:17,060 --> 00:03:20,030
I want to print the link.

39
00:03:20,540 --> 00:03:26,300
So basically we're iterating over each element in this list, which is the ref links.

40
00:03:26,420 --> 00:03:32,060
We're storing the value of each element in a variable called link, and then we're printing this variable

41
00:03:32,060 --> 00:03:32,900
on screen.

42
00:03:33,590 --> 00:03:36,530
So if we clear this and run it, you'll see.

43
00:03:36,530 --> 00:03:42,650
I'll get each link in a line so they won't be all placed inside one list.

44
00:03:44,230 --> 00:03:50,920
And as you can see, I have my links in here and I'm pretty sure you automatically can see a few things

45
00:03:50,920 --> 00:03:52,330
that need to be fixed.

46
00:03:53,320 --> 00:03:59,410
The first thing is you can see some of the URLs are not proper full URLs.

47
00:04:00,100 --> 00:04:02,430
Now this is normal in HTML.

48
00:04:02,440 --> 00:04:09,760
The developer can use relative URLs in their code and the browser will automatically know that these

49
00:04:09,760 --> 00:04:12,400
URLs exist in this website.

50
00:04:13,270 --> 00:04:19,990
But for us to use this in our code and to build upon this, we actually need to have the full URL for

51
00:04:19,990 --> 00:04:29,320
this, not the relative URL so we can convert this to a full URL easily by using a library called URL

52
00:04:29,320 --> 00:04:30,090
pass.

53
00:04:30,100 --> 00:04:32,170
So I'm going to import it first.

54
00:04:34,110 --> 00:04:43,360
And then in here I'm going to say my link is equal to your url pars dot u url.

55
00:04:43,380 --> 00:04:44,370
Join.

56
00:04:45,720 --> 00:04:52,170
And basically what I want to join is my target URL, which is the base URL if you want to think of it

57
00:04:52,170 --> 00:04:58,240
that way with the relative link with the small links that we're getting in here.

58
00:04:58,260 --> 00:05:00,240
So with the link.

59
00:05:01,360 --> 00:05:08,320
So basically what we're saying is we're calling the URL joint function that's in the URL library and

60
00:05:08,320 --> 00:05:15,220
we're giving it the base URL or the main URL and we're giving it each link that we are receiving.

61
00:05:15,220 --> 00:05:17,890
So we're basically giving it all the links in here.

62
00:05:18,400 --> 00:05:25,780
But this function will automatically know that these things in here are for links and it will not modify

63
00:05:25,780 --> 00:05:26,980
them at all.

64
00:05:27,670 --> 00:05:33,550
But when it reaches this link in here, for example, it will know that this link is a relative link.

65
00:05:33,550 --> 00:05:39,370
It's not a full URL and it will actually convert it to a proper full URL.

66
00:05:40,500 --> 00:05:42,600
So let's see this in action.

67
00:05:44,480 --> 00:05:45,500
And perfect.

68
00:05:45,500 --> 00:05:52,340
As you can see now, the small relative URL's have been converted to proper full earls.

69
00:05:53,210 --> 00:06:00,290
The next thing that I want to fix is the fact that some of the Earl's in here do not belong to my target

70
00:06:00,290 --> 00:06:01,100
website.

71
00:06:01,490 --> 00:06:06,230
They do exist in the web page and that's how we got them in here.

72
00:06:06,230 --> 00:06:08,980
But they're not part of my target.

73
00:06:08,990 --> 00:06:10,390
They're not in my scope.

74
00:06:10,400 --> 00:06:12,440
My target is at security.

75
00:06:12,440 --> 00:06:17,780
So I don't want any of these websites to be part of my target list.

76
00:06:18,950 --> 00:06:22,220
Now this can be fixed with a very simple if statement.

77
00:06:22,490 --> 00:06:27,980
So if I just go here and I can check if my target URL.

78
00:06:27,990 --> 00:06:34,400
So this URL in here is in my current link.

79
00:06:35,330 --> 00:06:38,450
If it is, then print it for me on screen.

80
00:06:38,480 --> 00:06:42,200
Otherwise this is an external link, so I don't want to see it.

81
00:06:43,200 --> 00:06:45,600
Now let's test this and see if it works.

82
00:06:46,910 --> 00:06:48,390
And that actually works.

fail because the links will actually have https in them.

85
00:07:01,220 --> 00:07:08,240
And as you can see, it's working perfectly and it removed the external links to other websites such

86
00:07:08,240 --> 00:07:09,770
as Facebook and LinkedIn.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,080 --> 00:00:09,000
In the previous lecture, we improved our spider program, so it converts relative lengths to full lengths,

2
00:00:09,420 --> 00:00:16,500
and we also improved it so that it ignores links that are not on the Target website.

3
00:00:16,860 --> 00:00:23,670
In this lecture, I want to continue improving the spider because we want to use this later on in our

4
00:00:23,670 --> 00:00:25,140
vulnerability scanner.

5
00:00:26,070 --> 00:00:32,580
So now, as you can see, when we run the program, it will show us all the links on the target page.

6
00:00:33,290 --> 00:00:40,160
Now, instead of printing everything on screen in here, it will be nice if we actually have a variable

7
00:00:40,160 --> 00:00:42,950
that contains all of my target links.

8
00:00:43,460 --> 00:00:48,380
So I'm going to create a list and I'm going to call it target links.

9
00:00:49,100 --> 00:00:52,580
And this is going to be equal to an empty list.

10
00:00:53,570 --> 00:00:59,360
And then every time we're printing something onscreen, so after doing all the checks that we did in

11
00:00:59,360 --> 00:01:03,740
this lecture, we're going to add this link to my target links.

12
00:01:04,070 --> 00:01:10,910
So we're going to do target links that append and we're going to add the link.

13
00:01:11,600 --> 00:01:18,650
So now when this program finishes execution, we're going to have a list called Target Links, which

14
00:01:18,650 --> 00:01:23,180
will contain all these links in here as elements.

15
00:01:23,900 --> 00:01:30,890
Now, we filtered these links and we made sure that these links are full links that are in our Target

16
00:01:30,890 --> 00:01:31,730
website.

17
00:01:32,000 --> 00:01:33,860
But there is only one problem.

18
00:01:34,160 --> 00:01:38,520
If you look through these links, you'll see that they are not unique.

19
00:01:38,540 --> 00:01:40,970
So some of the links are being repeated.

20
00:01:40,970 --> 00:01:44,600
For example, we can see the fact here and if we go down.

21
00:01:45,510 --> 00:01:48,240
We can see the fact again in here.

22
00:01:48,720 --> 00:01:50,050
So this is not good.

23
00:01:50,070 --> 00:01:57,570
I'd like to have a list in which each element is unique and is a target that is interesting to me.

24
00:01:58,290 --> 00:02:01,590
Now, again, fixing this is actually very, very simple.

25
00:02:01,620 --> 00:02:08,570
All we have to do is end this statement before we print and add our link to the target links.

26
00:02:08,580 --> 00:02:18,390
We can check so it can say if the target URL is in the link and the link is not already in my target

27
00:02:18,390 --> 00:02:19,110
links.

28
00:02:19,900 --> 00:02:26,350
This way, we're only going to append this link and add it to the target links if it does not exist

29
00:02:26,350 --> 00:02:27,010
in it.

30
00:02:27,190 --> 00:02:33,970
And we're only going to print it on screen again if this link has not been added to my target links

31
00:02:34,750 --> 00:02:38,530
so we can clear this and run it again.

32
00:02:40,450 --> 00:02:44,170
So now if you look at my links, you'll see that they are unique.

33
00:02:44,170 --> 00:02:50,770
There are no repeats, they are all full links and they are all directing to the target website.

34
00:02:50,770 --> 00:02:53,020
There is no external links in them.

35
00:02:53,980 --> 00:03:00,610
So now you might think that this program is perfect and it's ready to be used in the real world, and

36
00:03:00,610 --> 00:03:04,960
I'm sorry to break it to you, but there is one more thing that we still need to fix.

37
00:03:05,350 --> 00:03:07,510
Let's go back to my website.

38
00:03:07,990 --> 00:03:09,550
If we go to the shop.

39
00:03:10,940 --> 00:03:13,340
And let's click on any of the products.

40
00:03:14,900 --> 00:03:21,260
And if we go down and let's right click this and inspect this element.

41
00:03:23,460 --> 00:03:30,150
If you look at the graph in here on the link, you will see that this link starts with a hash.

42
00:03:30,810 --> 00:03:33,670
So basically, this is not a proper link.

43
00:03:33,690 --> 00:03:39,960
This is a way to load different parts of the same HTML page.

44
00:03:40,350 --> 00:03:47,070
So when we actually click on this link, you'll see that we won't go anywhere, but it will just load

45
00:03:47,070 --> 00:03:50,010
the part where it shows additional information.

46
00:03:51,090 --> 00:03:57,960
Now why I'm talking about this, because if we use this link in our program, so we're going to set

47
00:03:57,960 --> 00:04:05,460
that to the target and I'm going to clear the screen and try to get all the links in this page.

48
00:04:06,210 --> 00:04:12,900
Now, as you know, this is supposed to get me the unique links, so the links should not repeat themselves.

49
00:04:13,320 --> 00:04:19,920
But if you look in here, you'll see we have the same link, but it ends with the hash reviews.

50
00:04:20,070 --> 00:04:26,490
We have the same link here, but it ends with the hash tag description and we have the same link again

51
00:04:26,490 --> 00:04:28,710
with tab additional information.

52
00:04:29,430 --> 00:04:32,100
So this is actually the same link.

53
00:04:32,100 --> 00:04:35,970
It's all going to the same place and loading the exact same web page.

54
00:04:35,970 --> 00:04:40,410
So these three links in here are not unique links.

55
00:04:40,410 --> 00:04:45,360
And if the program was perfect, it should only display this link once.

56
00:04:46,450 --> 00:04:53,170
So what I want to do is add one more check to this to make sure that my program only shows unique links.

57
00:04:53,950 --> 00:04:57,010
And to do this, it's actually very, very easy.

58
00:04:57,280 --> 00:04:59,920
We have the link in here in this loop.

59
00:05:00,040 --> 00:05:10,180
So all I'm going to say is if a hash can be found in my link, then my link is going to be equal to

60
00:05:10,180 --> 00:05:11,200
itself.

61
00:05:11,350 --> 00:05:12,880
Dot split.

62
00:05:14,110 --> 00:05:17,320
And we're going to split it based on the hash.

63
00:05:17,560 --> 00:05:20,800
And I'm going to get the first element of this.

64
00:05:21,780 --> 00:05:27,600
So basically what we're saying is we're saying I want to split the link where the hash is.

65
00:05:28,020 --> 00:05:35,580
This will split my link into a list of two elements in which the first element is everything before

66
00:05:35,580 --> 00:05:40,590
the hash and the second element is everything after the hash.

67
00:05:41,580 --> 00:05:44,760
And I'm interested in everything before the hash.

68
00:05:44,790 --> 00:05:50,760
That's why I'm using square brackets zero to store the value of that in my link variable.

69
00:05:51,510 --> 00:05:55,860
Then we're doing our checks, which will make sure that our link is unique.

70
00:05:56,010 --> 00:06:04,140
And the result of this is the program is going to know that the hash is irrelevant and all of these

71
00:06:04,140 --> 00:06:05,580
three links are the same.

72
00:06:06,510 --> 00:06:08,430
So let's run the program again.

73
00:06:10,370 --> 00:06:11,500
And perfect.

74
00:06:11,510 --> 00:06:17,660
Now, as you can see, we have this link only being displayed once in my program.

75
00:06:18,350 --> 00:06:24,470
And again, as we've seen before, this program will only display links of the Target Web page.

76
00:06:24,470 --> 00:06:31,250
So it will not display the external links, and it will also convert the relative links to full links.

77
00:06:32,000 --> 00:06:38,390
Now, in the next lecture, I'm going to show you how to build on this program to map the whole website

78
00:06:38,390 --> 00:06:41,270
instead of a single page of the website.

00:00:01,060 --> 00:00:08,710

list contains unique links so that it does not contain any repeated elements.

So right now my program is actually doing most of the heavy work and most of this work is actually done

19
00:01:44,180 --> 00:01:45,200
in here.

20
00:01:45,200 --> 00:01:52,190
So the extract links from is literally just takes a URL and gives me all the links in that URL.

21
00:01:52,310 --> 00:01:58,730
And then we actually are looping over the links and filtering them all in here.

22
00:01:59,630 --> 00:02:04,490
So I want to create a function and I'm going to call this function crawl.

23
00:02:05,290 --> 00:02:13,690
And I want this function to take a URL and this will be the URL where we will start crawling from and

24
00:02:13,690 --> 00:02:18,190
then it will execute all of this code when I call this function.

25
00:02:18,910 --> 00:02:25,360
Now, instead of getting the URLs from the target URL, we're going to get it from the URL that this

26
00:02:25,360 --> 00:02:27,730
function will get as an input.

27
00:02:28,660 --> 00:02:34,930
And then I'm just going to call this function from my code and here by saying Crawl.

28
00:02:35,810 --> 00:02:37,190
With the target.

29
00:02:37,220 --> 00:02:38,210
You are l.

30
00:02:39,290 --> 00:02:42,260
So so far I haven't implemented anything new.

31
00:02:42,290 --> 00:02:47,570
All I did is I put the code that we wrote so far inside the function called Crawl.

00:02:48,110 --> 00:02:52,700
Now, as we know, the way that this function is going to work, it's going to take a link.

33

It's going to pass this link to my extract links from method, which will get me all the links inside

34

the page that can be accessed with this link.

35

All these links are going to be stored in my ref list.

And then for each link in this list, we're going to check if this link is not an external link.

37

00:03:19,250 --> 00:03:24,800
Now, once we reach this line, we know that this link is a full URL.

39
00:03:24,860 --> 00:03:25,820
It's unique.

40
00:03:25,820 --> 00:03:32,270
So we haven't seen this link in our code before, and it's a link to my target.

41
00:03:32,270 --> 00:03:34,040
It's not an external link.

42
00:03:34,520 --> 00:03:42,050
So this means that this link is relevant and that I actually want to open this link and get all the

43
00:03:42,050 --> 00:03:46,660
links that can be accessed from the web page that this link loads.

44
00:03:46,670 --> 00:03:52,100
So it's basically similar to when I go in here and click on one of these links.

45
00:03:53,080 --> 00:04:00,700
So all I'm going to do is I'm literally going to call the crawl function again and this time I'm going

46
00:04:00,700 --> 00:04:02,470
to pass it the current link.

47
00:04:03,500 --> 00:04:05,900
Now this will call the same function again.

48
00:04:05,900 --> 00:04:07,470
So it's going to call itself.

49
00:04:07,490 --> 00:04:12,420
So this method is now a recursive method, and it's something that we spoke about before.

50
00:04:12,440 --> 00:04:17,300
Basically any method that calls itself is called a recursive method.

51
00:04:18,110 --> 00:04:24,350
So now what this method is going to do is first thing is going to get all the links in the past URL.

52
00:04:24,770 --> 00:04:31,370
And then if this URL is not an external URL and if it is unique, so if it's something that we haven't

53
00:04:31,370 --> 00:04:38,990
seen before, then it's going to start this URL in my target lists and then it will extract all the

54
00:04:38,990 --> 00:04:40,700
links from this URL.

55
00:04:41,300 --> 00:04:48,410
So basically this method will keep calling itself until it properly maps the whole website.

56
00:04:49,280 --> 00:04:51,910
Now there is one thing that I forgot to change.

57
00:04:51,920 --> 00:04:59,390
I changed the target URL and here to the URL because this is taking the URL as an input and I need to

58
00:04:59,390 --> 00:05:01,610
change this to a URL as well.

59
00:05:02,300 --> 00:05:08,090
Because that's what my function is taken as an input and now my code is ready.

60
00:05:08,150 --> 00:05:13,730
So all we did today is basically just call the function this exact same function.

61
00:05:13,730 --> 00:05:20,420
Once we know that we have a link that is interesting to us, we're doing this because this function

62
00:05:20,420 --> 00:05:26,020
will extract any further links that might be found in this interesting link.

63
00:05:26,030 --> 00:05:30,620
And as it goes, it's going to add these links to my Target Links list.

64
00:05:30,830 --> 00:05:39,770
Therefore, by the end, I'm going to have a list of all the unique links in the Target website, not

65
00:05:39,770 --> 00:05:42,950
in the Target page and the full website.

66
00:05:43,760 --> 00:05:49,850
Now, obviously, right now I'm only passing it a specific page, so I'm going to remove all of this

67
00:05:49,850 --> 00:05:53,900
and make sure that we're passing it the web route of the website.

68
00:05:54,500 --> 00:05:57,800
And I'm going to be testing this against my own website.

69
00:05:57,800 --> 00:06:03,980
But please, when you're writing this program, do not test it on my website because if there are many

70
00:06:03,980 --> 00:06:10,040
students testing this against my website until this does my website and cause it to crash.

71
00:06:10,370 --> 00:06:16,310
So please, if you want to test this, you can test it against the Metasploit websites, for example,

72
00:06:16,310 --> 00:06:18,140
the utility website in here.

73
00:06:18,560 --> 00:06:24,080
Just make sure that Metasploit table is running when you do this or you can test it against your own

74
00:06:24,080 --> 00:06:28,010
website if you want, but do not test it against my own website.

75
00:06:28,010 --> 00:06:33,470
I'm doing it here because I want to show you that this will still work against real websites.

76
00:06:34,290 --> 00:06:36,240
So I'm going to run the code.

77
00:06:38,010 --> 00:06:45,510
And as you can see, every time it discovers a new directory or a new path, it's actually navigating

78
00:06:45,510 --> 00:06:46,380
inside that.

79
00:06:46,380 --> 00:06:52,470
So you can see, for example, when it discovered this link, it was able to go into this link and then

80
00:06:52,470 --> 00:06:58,080
discover another link inside it, which is the feed again in here.

81
00:06:58,080 --> 00:07:03,810
When it discovered the category, hacking and security, it was able to discover there is another link

82
00:07:03,810 --> 00:07:06,150
inside it, which is network hacking.

83
00:07:06,480 --> 00:07:11,880
Then it went inside network hacking and it was able to discover something called feed.

84
00:07:12,710 --> 00:07:13,790
Same in here.

85
00:07:13,790 --> 00:07:17,960
You can see it discovered it went back to hacking and security.

86
00:07:17,960 --> 00:07:22,340
So we were already in hacking and security, but we discovered network hacking.

87
00:07:22,370 --> 00:07:28,460
It discovered everything inside network hacking, but then it went back into hacking and security and

88
00:07:28,460 --> 00:07:29,990
it got me web hacking.

89
00:07:29,990 --> 00:07:33,950
And then inside Web hacking, it was able to discover feed.

90
00:07:34,550 --> 00:07:40,790
And this program is going to keep going on and on and on, entering every possible link in my website

91
00:07:40,790 --> 00:07:47,210
and extracting all links in that page and then go into each one of these links again.

92
00:07:47,210 --> 00:07:53,990
And it will keep doing this until it extracts all the links from all the pages in my website.

93
00:07:54,740 --> 00:08:02,030
And we manage to do all of this because we structured our function in a way so that it's recursive and

94
00:08:02,030 --> 00:08:07,640
so that it calls itself every time it sees an interesting link.

95
00:08:08,670 --> 00:08:12,980
Now I'm going to stop this because it's going to take some time to map my whole website.

96
00:08:12,990 --> 00:08:16,260
And if I run this against me till day.

97
00:08:17,200 --> 00:08:19,360
So this is where you should test it.

98
00:08:19,390 --> 00:08:25,330
Again, all of these websites are actually proper websites similar to any other website, and they work

99
00:08:25,330 --> 00:08:27,460
using the exact same functionality.

100
00:08:27,670 --> 00:08:34,570
The only reason why Matilda is much faster than my own website is because it is hosted on my own computer.

101
00:08:34,570 --> 00:08:40,660
So when I send a request, the response is received much faster than when you send it to the Internet.

102
00:08:41,710 --> 00:08:48,520
And again, as you can see, the program is working and it's able to extract all the links in my target

103
00:08:48,520 --> 00:08:49,270
website.

104
00:08:50,050 --> 00:08:51,850
Now, this is very, very important.

105
00:08:51,850 --> 00:08:58,030
It's very useful because in the future we're going to start trying to discover vulnerabilities in our

106
00:08:58,030 --> 00:08:58,780
target.

107
00:08:58,780 --> 00:09:05,680
But before we can discover vulnerabilities, we need to have a full map of the website so that we test

108
00:09:05,680 --> 00:09:09,490
every single possible page that could be vulnerable.

109
00:09:10,360 --> 00:09:14,860
Now, Crawler is such as this one have other uses than just hacking.

110
00:09:14,950 --> 00:09:20,890
So there are a number of programs that will do that for you in order to generate a sitemap, for example,

111
00:09:20,890 --> 00:09:22,180
for your own website.

112
00:09:22,210 --> 00:09:27,790
Google uses this to archive websites so that when people search for websites, they can find what they're

113
00:09:27,790 --> 00:09:28,740
looking for.

114
00:09:28,750 --> 00:09:34,840
And I'm pretty sure you can think of other examples, or maybe you might need to use it in a completely

115
00:09:34,840 --> 00:09:35,890
different manner.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,660 --> 00:00:07,170
Now, as usual, now that we're happy with our code running with Python two in this lecture, we're

2
00:00:07,170 --> 00:00:12,540
going to try to get it working with Python three, and you're going to see how easy it is to do that.

3
00:00:12,990 --> 00:00:17,730
So first of all, going back to our code, it still looks exactly the same.

4
00:00:17,730 --> 00:00:23,900
And what we're going to do, we're just going to clear the screen and run the code with Python three,

5
00:00:23,910 --> 00:00:26,220
see what breaks, and try to fix it.

6
00:00:26,640 --> 00:00:30,260
So we're going to hit enter and it breaks at line five.

7
00:00:30,270 --> 00:00:37,170
As you can see here, if we go back, it's actually breaking at the import line in here when we're trying

8
00:00:37,170 --> 00:00:39,090
to import u r ll pass.

9
00:00:39,390 --> 00:00:43,300
So clearly there is no such library for Python three.

10
00:00:43,320 --> 00:00:49,260
So the first thing that you should do in this case is try to install this library for Python three.

11
00:00:49,440 --> 00:00:57,000
So before I even Google or lock anything, I'm going to go to my terminal and I'm going to type PIP

12
00:00:57,180 --> 00:01:00,510
three because we want to install something for Python three.

13
00:01:00,510 --> 00:01:06,300
And what we want to install is a library called u r l pass.

14
00:01:08,080 --> 00:01:15,580
And as you can see, we get a404 error, meaning that this library does not or this page does not exist

15
00:01:15,580 --> 00:01:16,380
anymore.

16
00:01:16,390 --> 00:01:18,650
So something's clearly going wrong.

17
00:01:18,670 --> 00:01:24,730
So the next step, we're going to use the most important skill a hacker or a programmer should have,

18
00:01:24,730 --> 00:01:27,490
which is Google or researching stuff.

19
00:01:27,760 --> 00:01:33,070
So we're going to go to Google and we're just going to look for a URL, parse the name of the library

20
00:01:33,070 --> 00:01:35,690
that we want to use with Python three.

21
00:01:35,710 --> 00:01:38,380
So we're going to say URL paths, Python three.

22
00:01:39,930 --> 00:01:45,710
Now the first result is going to be from the official Python website, as you can see in here.

23
00:01:45,720 --> 00:01:49,560
And it's a library called URL lib dot pass.

24
00:01:49,650 --> 00:01:57,030
And if we click on it, you can read the description, but you'll see it's identical to the URL pass

25
00:01:57,120 --> 00:01:59,820
library that we were using with Python two.

26
00:02:00,360 --> 00:02:06,690
And the main method that we're actually interested in, if we go back up and look, we are interested

27
00:02:06,690 --> 00:02:08,880
in a method called URL join.

28
00:02:09,570 --> 00:02:15,900
So before even using this library, let's make sure that this method actually exists in this new library.

29
00:02:16,470 --> 00:02:21,600
So again, in here, just control F and we're going to look for URL join.

30
00:02:23,950 --> 00:02:24,640
And perfect.

31
00:02:24,640 --> 00:02:31,900
You can see that this method actually exists and it can be used exactly the same way as shown in Python

32
00:02:31,900 --> 00:02:32,470
two.

33
00:02:32,590 --> 00:02:37,500
So the only difference here really is the name of the library and the structure.

34
00:02:37,510 --> 00:02:39,670
It just got changed a little bit.

35
00:02:39,820 --> 00:02:41,800
So if we go up.

36
00:02:43,370 --> 00:02:45,440
You can see the name of the library now.

37
00:02:45,470 --> 00:02:50,030
You are a lib dot pass instead of simply URL pass.

38
00:02:50,540 --> 00:02:54,290
So we're just going to change our import line in here.

39
00:02:54,290 --> 00:03:00,290
And instead of saying import Earl pass, we're going to say import the new name of the library, which

40
00:03:00,290 --> 00:03:02,210
is the URL lib dot pass.

41
00:03:03,480 --> 00:03:07,050
And we're going to say we want to call this library in my program.

42
00:03:07,050 --> 00:03:11,340
I want to refer to it as u r l pass.

43
00:03:11,640 --> 00:03:14,550
This way I won't really have to change my code.

44
00:03:14,550 --> 00:03:20,880
So every time there is u r l pass in my code, it will be replaced with the URL lib dot parse and the

45
00:03:20,880 --> 00:03:23,460
method that we want to use is the URL.

46
00:03:23,460 --> 00:03:24,600
Join in here.

47
00:03:24,600 --> 00:03:28,140
And like I said, if we go down you will see the URL.

48
00:03:28,140 --> 00:03:33,990
Join can be used exactly the same way that we are doing it in our code.

49
00:03:34,440 --> 00:03:35,790
So that's perfect.

50
00:03:36,360 --> 00:03:38,400
Let's go ahead and run the code.

51
00:03:39,410 --> 00:03:40,820
With Python three.

52
00:03:42,020 --> 00:03:44,210
And now we get another error.

53
00:03:44,570 --> 00:03:48,410
So the error we're getting right now is online 13.

54
00:03:48,710 --> 00:03:55,820
And we can see that the error is the famous error that we get with Python three because of the distinction

55
00:03:55,820 --> 00:03:58,340
between strings and byte objects.

56
00:03:58,340 --> 00:04:04,940
So you can see the error saying there is a string pattern that is being used on a byte like object.

57
00:04:04,940 --> 00:04:06,830
It's on line 13.

58
00:04:06,830 --> 00:04:08,990
So let's go to line 13.

59
00:04:10,110 --> 00:04:10,890
Right here.

60
00:04:10,890 --> 00:04:18,840
And you can see it's in this regex method and they find all we are using a string regex, but we are

61
00:04:18,840 --> 00:04:24,800
comparing it to a bit like object because the error is already telling us that this is a byte like object.

62
00:04:24,810 --> 00:04:30,270
So all we have to do is simply convert this byte like object to a string.

63
00:04:30,480 --> 00:04:36,090
We can do this by casting it to a string like so as we seen before.

64
00:04:36,420 --> 00:04:40,230
Or we can just do response.

65
00:04:40,250 --> 00:04:41,280
Dot content.

66
00:04:41,280 --> 00:04:43,200
Dot decode.

67
00:04:44,060 --> 00:04:46,380
We're going to save this going down.

68
00:04:46,400 --> 00:04:47,510
Clear the screen.

69
00:04:47,510 --> 00:04:49,490
Run the code with Python three.

70
00:04:49,490 --> 00:04:55,760
And if you notice, first of all, it worked on the first line right now and then it crashed.

71
00:04:56,150 --> 00:04:57,560
So let's read the crash.

72
00:04:57,560 --> 00:04:58,970
Let's see the error again.

73
00:04:58,970 --> 00:05:02,060
They're saying it's a Unicode decode error.

74
00:05:02,120 --> 00:05:05,570
It was trying to decode this using UTF eight.

75
00:05:05,570 --> 00:05:11,450
And the problem is it encountered a character that it's not able to decode.

76
00:05:12,460 --> 00:05:19,930
Now this is normal because we are trying to pass HTML pages and a page could contain strange characters

77
00:05:19,930 --> 00:05:22,420
that Python will not be able to decode.

78
00:05:22,720 --> 00:05:29,290
These strange characters will not be relevant to us anyway because they can't be part of the URL.

79
00:05:29,290 --> 00:05:32,740
And all we need to do in this code is extract URLs.

80
00:05:32,830 --> 00:05:37,060
Therefore, in our decode method we're going to modify it a little bit.

81
00:05:37,060 --> 00:05:41,850
And this is good for you to know in the future when you're writing your own programs.

82
00:05:41,860 --> 00:05:50,080
If you want to ignore the decoding process of a character, all you have to do is use the errors flag.

83
00:05:50,080 --> 00:05:55,210
And we're going to say, if you get any errors, I want you to ignore them.

84
00:05:57,690 --> 00:06:00,960
So we're going to save this and run the code again.

85
00:06:01,650 --> 00:06:02,390
And perfect.

86
00:06:02,400 --> 00:06:06,690
Now our code is working perfectly with Python three.

87
00:06:07,380 --> 00:06:12,810
So the take on lesson from this lecture is we had to import a different library.

88
00:06:12,810 --> 00:06:18,780
Even though the library is used the same, we simply had to google the new library name for Python three

89
00:06:18,780 --> 00:06:20,100
and import it here.

90
00:06:20,100 --> 00:06:24,450
And then we converted one of our byte like objects to a string.

91
00:06:24,450 --> 00:06:26,970
So there is no conflict in here.

92
00:06:27,300 --> 00:06:30,390
There was a problem with decoding one of the characters.

93
00:06:30,390 --> 00:06:37,320
So in the future, if you do face this problem, you can use the errors equals ignore flag to tell python

94
00:06:37,320 --> 00:06:42,030
to ignore any errors you get while decoding abide to a string.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
