# Section 18: Writing a Program To Guess Login Information

1
00:00:01,000 --> 00:00:08,710
Now, throughout all of the programs that we wrote so far, we were communicating with the website using

2
00:00:08,740 --> 00:00:10,570
a method that's called Get.

3
00:00:11,450 --> 00:00:16,660
As I showed you before, this basically simulates us clicking on a link.

4
00:00:16,670 --> 00:00:23,960
So for example, when we were at Z Security, we were using this method to simulate a user clicking

5
00:00:23,960 --> 00:00:25,880
on any of these links.

6
00:00:26,720 --> 00:00:32,930
Now, as you know, clicking on a link is not the only way to navigate through a website.

7
00:00:33,530 --> 00:00:36,350
For example, let's go to Facebook.com.

8
00:00:37,210 --> 00:00:43,330
And as you can see, the first thing that we will see is two input boxes.

9
00:00:43,540 --> 00:00:51,130
And if we click on login, some kind of functionality is going to be executed on the target.

10
00:00:51,820 --> 00:00:59,720
Now using our code, we can't really communicate with this because with our code we can only send your

11
00:00:59,720 --> 00:01:05,950
URLs or links and our code will send a request to this link and get the response.

12
00:01:06,640 --> 00:01:13,990
But when it comes to input boxes, such as input boxes, where you enter username and password or input

13
00:01:13,990 --> 00:01:20,980
boxes for search boxes or any input box, really, we don't really know how to communicate with this

14
00:01:20,980 --> 00:01:23,770
box from our Python code.

15
00:01:23,980 --> 00:01:29,680
And it's actually very, very important to learn how to do that because if we learn how to do this,

16
00:01:29,680 --> 00:01:35,440
we'll be able to submit forms automatically from our Python program.

17
00:01:35,440 --> 00:01:40,620
So we'll be able to launch dictionary attacks to get usernames and passwords.

18
00:01:40,630 --> 00:01:46,360
We will be able to discover vulnerabilities in these input boxes and we'll see how to do that later

19
00:01:46,360 --> 00:01:46,870
on.

20
00:01:47,320 --> 00:01:54,040
So in this lecture, I want to show you how to communicate with input boxes using Python.

21
00:01:54,700 --> 00:01:58,000
Now, the example that I'm going to use is not Facebook.

22
00:01:58,000 --> 00:02:02,890
I'm actually going to use the example in div w a in Metasploit table.

23
00:02:03,130 --> 00:02:10,960
So I have the IP of Metasploit table here followed by DV A, and if you go to this location, you will

24
00:02:10,960 --> 00:02:14,350
be presented with a login screen similar to this one.

25
00:02:14,350 --> 00:02:19,000
And by the way, this uses the exact same functionality that Facebook uses.

26
00:02:19,000 --> 00:02:22,150
So I don't want to be testing this against Facebook.

27
00:02:22,150 --> 00:02:24,190
I want to test it against my own lab.

28
00:02:24,190 --> 00:02:26,320
So I'm sure I'm doing this legally.

29
00:02:27,130 --> 00:02:32,920
Now again, we have two input boxes similar to Facebook, and we have a button that will allow us to

30
00:02:32,920 --> 00:02:33,790
log in.

31
00:02:34,950 --> 00:02:40,380
Now input boxes are usually placed inside a form tag.

32
00:02:40,920 --> 00:02:45,300
So let me right click this and if we go to inspect element.

33
00:02:46,480 --> 00:02:48,670
You can see if I put my mouse in here.

34
00:02:48,670 --> 00:02:53,230
It highlights the element on screen that represents this code in here.

35
00:02:53,710 --> 00:02:57,070
So you can see that this is the input for the username.

36
00:02:57,100 --> 00:03:00,580
Its name is username and it's type is text.

37
00:03:00,580 --> 00:03:06,130
And we have another input box in here that is responsible for showing the password.

38
00:03:06,130 --> 00:03:07,210
Input box.

39
00:03:08,210 --> 00:03:08,780
In here.

40
00:03:08,780 --> 00:03:16,460
If I click on this small arrow, we have another input which is responsible for showing the login button

41
00:03:16,460 --> 00:03:18,200
and its type is submit.

42
00:03:18,200 --> 00:03:22,190
So when we click this, this whole form gets submitted.

43
00:03:22,940 --> 00:03:25,580
Now if I just make this a little bit bigger.

44
00:03:26,680 --> 00:03:35,020
You can see the combination of the two input boxes and the login button is all placed inside a form

45
00:03:35,020 --> 00:03:35,710
tag.

46
00:03:35,740 --> 00:03:41,890
So you can see I have a form in here and at the end I have a slash form to indicate the end of this

47
00:03:41,890 --> 00:03:42,490
tag.

48
00:03:42,580 --> 00:03:49,420
And inside it we have the input for the user name, the input for the password and the input for the

49
00:03:49,420 --> 00:03:50,470
submit button.

50
00:03:51,100 --> 00:03:59,260
Now usually input boxes are always placed inside a form, and when you click the submit or the login,

51
00:03:59,440 --> 00:04:07,600
the values you enter inside these input boxes will be sent to the location specified in the action.

52
00:04:07,600 --> 00:04:08,350
And here.

53
00:04:08,890 --> 00:04:16,240
So basically the username and password that I will put in here will be sent to a file called login dot

54
00:04:16,630 --> 00:04:17,260
php.

55
00:04:18,250 --> 00:04:25,300
So let's try to write code that can communicate with this form right here.

56
00:04:25,300 --> 00:04:32,680
Put a username, put a password and submit this form to a file that's called login dot PHP.

57
00:04:33,460 --> 00:04:38,440
So the first thing that we'll do is we're going to get the URL in here.

58
00:04:38,440 --> 00:04:47,860
Now in this example, we are actually at log in dot PHP so I can copy all of this, but sometimes you

59
00:04:47,860 --> 00:04:51,310
might actually see a completely different URL in here.

60
00:04:51,310 --> 00:04:57,430
So you want to make sure you copy the URL in here and you post the values to this URL.

61
00:04:58,450 --> 00:05:07,180
Now in my python code, I've already created a new file called Post Dot Pi and I'm going to import the

62
00:05:07,180 --> 00:05:08,680
requests library.

63
00:05:10,070 --> 00:05:17,960
Now, if you remember, we used to actually send a get request when we want to just open a normal URL

64
00:05:17,960 --> 00:05:21,470
similar to when you type google.com in your browser.

65
00:05:22,340 --> 00:05:29,900
But in this example, and if I actually go down, you can see in this form it's saying that the method

66
00:05:29,900 --> 00:05:33,320
of this form is post and it's not get.

67
00:05:33,890 --> 00:05:43,220
Therefore I'm going to say my response is equal to requests dot post instead of get.

68
00:05:43,890 --> 00:05:47,660
Now this will, first of all take a target URL.

69
00:05:47,670 --> 00:05:54,570
So I'm going to create a variable called Target your URL and we're going to set this to the URL that

70
00:05:54,570 --> 00:05:55,410
I copied.

71
00:05:56,130 --> 00:06:02,710
Again, if in the action you see a completely different URL, then make sure you use that URL.

72
00:06:02,730 --> 00:06:05,460
In my case, it's submitting it to log in.

73
00:06:05,460 --> 00:06:07,740
That's why I'm using it in here.

74
00:06:08,850 --> 00:06:14,340
The next thing that we need to specify is the data that we want to send.

75
00:06:14,640 --> 00:06:17,250
And this is going to be a dictionary.

76
00:06:17,730 --> 00:06:24,660
So first of all, I'm going to create a variable and I'm going to call it data, and this is going to

77
00:06:24,660 --> 00:06:25,560
be a dictionary.

78
00:06:25,560 --> 00:06:28,230
So we're going to use curly brackets to create it.

79
00:06:29,070 --> 00:06:36,810
And in this dictionary, the key for each element is going to be the name of the input.

80
00:06:36,810 --> 00:06:41,340
So you can see the first input in here is called user name.

81
00:06:41,790 --> 00:06:49,560
Now sometimes the name might be anything but make sure you copy whatever that you see in the name parameter

82
00:06:49,560 --> 00:06:50,310
in here.

83
00:06:50,700 --> 00:06:53,750
So the first input for me is called username.

84
00:06:53,760 --> 00:06:58,860
So I'm going to say username and the value for this.

85
00:06:58,860 --> 00:07:03,270
So this is the value that you want to input in the input box.

86
00:07:03,720 --> 00:07:08,840
So let me just put any random value, let's say blah blah.

87
00:07:08,880 --> 00:07:10,350
So that's the username.

88
00:07:10,980 --> 00:07:14,130
Then we're going to put a comma to give another value.

89
00:07:14,640 --> 00:07:19,770
And the next value again for the data is going to be the next input.

90
00:07:20,220 --> 00:07:23,430
Now the name of the next input is password.

91
00:07:23,910 --> 00:07:27,780
So again, we're going to set this as the key for this value.

92
00:07:29,040 --> 00:07:32,870
And the value you want to specify the value for the password.

93
00:07:32,880 --> 00:07:35,400
And again, I'm just going to put a random value.

94
00:07:36,580 --> 00:07:43,330
And finally, we're going to put the last input that we have, which is the button that submits the

95
00:07:43,330 --> 00:07:44,260
whole form.

96
00:07:44,920 --> 00:07:48,310
Now, again, the name of this is Login.

97
00:07:50,760 --> 00:07:55,110
And the value of this, we're going to set it to the type.

98
00:07:55,110 --> 00:07:57,570
So the type in here is submit.

99
00:08:00,050 --> 00:08:07,520
Now you might have a form that contains more than three inputs, so you want to put them all as values

100
00:08:07,520 --> 00:08:08,600
in this dictionary.

101
00:08:08,600 --> 00:08:15,800
So you put the name of the input as the key for the dictionary and then the value for this input.

102
00:08:15,800 --> 00:08:20,660
So basically whatever you want to type inside this box as the value.

103
00:08:21,470 --> 00:08:26,840
Now my form only contains three inputs the username, the password and the login.

104
00:08:26,840 --> 00:08:28,610
So that's what I have in there.

105
00:08:29,060 --> 00:08:34,940
And then we're going to call our request dot post to send a post request.

106
00:08:35,150 --> 00:08:41,150
We're going to give it the URL so the target URL and then we're going to tell it.

107
00:08:41,150 --> 00:08:50,330
The data is equal to my data variable, so maybe I should call this data dict so that you just don't

108
00:08:50,330 --> 00:08:51,530
get confused.

109
00:08:51,890 --> 00:08:54,200
And this will be data dict.

110
00:08:55,220 --> 00:09:03,200
So all we're doing is we're saying, I want to send a request of type post to the target URL with the

111
00:09:03,200 --> 00:09:06,260
data that I specified in the data variable.

112
00:09:07,160 --> 00:09:11,540
And then we can print the response similar to the way we always do.

113
00:09:11,570 --> 00:09:16,130
We can do print response, that content.

114
00:09:17,180 --> 00:09:25,160
To see the HTML code of the page that will be loaded after submitting this post request right here.

115
00:09:25,930 --> 00:09:33,970
Now we know that this should fail, because if I go down and close this and let's log in with a username

116
00:09:33,970 --> 00:09:40,090
that's called blah and a password that's called one, two, three, you will see that we won't be able

117
00:09:40,090 --> 00:09:43,750
to log in and we'll get a message saying login failed.

118
00:09:44,470 --> 00:09:49,090
So let's run our code here and see if we get the same.

119
00:09:50,200 --> 00:09:51,010
And perfect.

120
00:09:51,010 --> 00:09:54,160
As you can see, it's telling us the login has failed.

121
00:09:54,160 --> 00:10:01,120
So our Python program is able to communicate with the web page and submit the form with the values that

122
00:10:01,120 --> 00:10:02,200
we specified.

123
00:10:02,890 --> 00:10:09,880
Now the actual username in here is admin and the right password is password.

124
00:10:10,510 --> 00:10:18,640
So if I log in with this information, you'll see I'll get the default home page of this web application.

125
00:10:19,450 --> 00:10:21,970
So let's test this in our code.

126
00:10:21,970 --> 00:10:28,330
So we're going to give it this time a valid username, which is admin and a valid password.

127
00:10:28,900 --> 00:10:36,040
And note, like I said, the key of each element is the name of the input, and the value is the value

128
00:10:36,040 --> 00:10:40,480
that you literally type yourself when you're using the web application.

129
00:10:41,260 --> 00:10:43,240
So I'm going to save this.

130
00:10:43,360 --> 00:10:46,330
I'm going to clear the screen and run it.

131
00:10:47,850 --> 00:10:48,900
And perfect.

132
00:10:48,900 --> 00:10:55,380
As you can see, I'm actually getting the default HTML page that the user sees when they log in.

133
00:10:55,650 --> 00:11:01,350
And the proof is you can see in here is telling me that I am logged in as admin.

134
00:11:02,250 --> 00:11:03,540
Now that's perfect.

135
00:11:03,570 --> 00:11:11,490
Previously I showed you how to communicate with web applications or websites using GET, and right now

136
00:11:11,490 --> 00:11:15,660
you know how to communicate with the web applications using post.

137
00:11:15,870 --> 00:11:24,060
With this you're able to submit forms, so basically fill any input box that you can see on any website,

138
00:11:24,060 --> 00:11:30,120
put any value that you want, and literally click on the submit button to submit this form to the web

139
00:11:30,120 --> 00:11:30,720
page.

140
00:11:31,140 --> 00:11:38,220
Like I said, this is very important because you can use it in so many scenarios and a very useful one

141
00:11:38,220 --> 00:11:45,180
is writing a program that will brute force or run a dictionary attack against a certain login page or

142
00:11:45,180 --> 00:11:53,010
write program that will test these inputs for vulnerabilities like XSS, SQL injection and so on.

143
00:11:53,400 --> 00:11:58,860
We'll actually going to have examples of this in the future, so stay tuned for the future lectures.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,810 --> 00:00:08,730
Previously as I was explaining how to communicate with the website submit forms through post requests.

2
00:00:08,910 --> 00:00:16,410
I use this example where I submitted a username and a password in order to log in to a web service.

3
00:00:17,220 --> 00:00:23,820
Now I want to spend one more lecture on this because we can actually build a really, really cool program

4
00:00:23,820 --> 00:00:29,670
that we can use to guess the login password for any web application.

5
00:00:30,660 --> 00:00:37,950
Now, this is going to be similar to the way we created the program that guesses the subdomains or the

6
00:00:37,950 --> 00:00:38,930
directories.

7
00:00:38,970 --> 00:00:45,840
So the first thing that we need to do is to have a word list, which is a text file of possible passwords.

8
00:00:45,930 --> 00:00:50,150
Now I have a very short one in here of common passwords.

9
00:00:50,160 --> 00:00:53,400
I don't want to use a log one because I want to keep this short.

10
00:00:53,910 --> 00:00:57,960
And the last password is actually the right password, which is password.

11
00:00:58,050 --> 00:01:03,420
And I have that stored in my downloads directory and it's called password dot list.

12
00:01:04,410 --> 00:01:12,030
Now what we need to do is first of all, read this file one line at a time and we covered this in my

13
00:01:12,030 --> 00:01:13,920
crawler program in here.

14
00:01:14,400 --> 00:01:16,830
So I'm just going to copy the relevant lines.

15
00:01:16,830 --> 00:01:24,150
So only up to here where we strip the word and I'm going to paste it in my new program, which is called

16
00:01:24,150 --> 00:01:25,260
Guest Login Dot.

17
00:01:26,760 --> 00:01:32,250
Now the content of this program is identical to the contents of my post program.

18
00:01:32,280 --> 00:01:37,890
Like I said, I want to extend my post program to allow me to guess login passwords.

19
00:01:38,640 --> 00:01:40,950
So I'm going to paste this.

20
00:01:40,950 --> 00:01:47,270
And basically what this will do, it will open a file through downloads, common text.

21
00:01:47,460 --> 00:01:55,800
So I want to rename this to the file that contains my passwords, which is passwords the list.

22
00:01:56,820 --> 00:02:01,080
Then it's going to iterate for each line in this list.

23
00:02:01,290 --> 00:02:08,310
It's going to strip the new line character from this line so that the word variable contains the password

24
00:02:08,310 --> 00:02:12,780
or the word without any whitespace characters around it.

25
00:02:13,650 --> 00:02:21,120
And then what we need to do is to submit this word as a password to our target.

26
00:02:21,570 --> 00:02:24,060
So basically, I'll need to cut this line.

27
00:02:25,110 --> 00:02:26,610
And paste it here.

28
00:02:27,920 --> 00:02:36,950
And as we know, the value sent to the target URL in the post request is specified in the data dict.

29
00:02:37,430 --> 00:02:44,550
So the data dict is initialized in here with a username as admin and password.

30
00:02:44,570 --> 00:02:50,270
We're going to set this as empty because like I said, we want to try all the passwords in the list

31
00:02:50,270 --> 00:02:53,390
and hopefully one of them will be the right password.

32
00:02:54,020 --> 00:03:01,190
And then in here, before we actually submit the request, we're going to say my data dict.

33
00:03:02,020 --> 00:03:04,810
And I'm going to set the value for the password.

34
00:03:06,270 --> 00:03:08,430
This is going to be equal to the word.

35
00:03:08,430 --> 00:03:14,850
So the current word that we're reading from the file and then we're going to submit this request to

36
00:03:14,850 --> 00:03:17,220
our target URL at this line.

37
00:03:18,030 --> 00:03:25,350
Now we need a way of checking whether the word or the password that we tried did actually allow us to

38
00:03:25,350 --> 00:03:26,040
log in.

39
00:03:26,820 --> 00:03:32,670
So if we go down again and see you and we log in with something wrong, so I'm just putting a wrong

40
00:03:32,670 --> 00:03:36,690
password, we will get a message saying log in failed.

41
00:03:37,620 --> 00:03:47,670
So I'm going to copy this and I'm going to say if log in failed is not in my response that content.

42
00:03:47,940 --> 00:03:53,730
So if it's not part of my HTML code that I get, then I got the right password.

43
00:03:53,730 --> 00:03:58,440
So I'm going to display a message saying I got the password.

44
00:03:59,950 --> 00:04:07,120
And it is whatever word that allowed us to log in once we get the password.

45
00:04:07,120 --> 00:04:12,430
We don't really need to stay in this loop because we're actually going to keep trying every line.

46
00:04:12,430 --> 00:04:16,810
So for every word in the file, we're going to execute these lines in here.

47
00:04:16,810 --> 00:04:22,570
So if we actually get the right password, I'm just going to do Exit to quit my program.

48
00:04:23,950 --> 00:04:27,850
Now if we actually go through the whole word file.

49
00:04:27,850 --> 00:04:35,830
So if this for loop finishes execution, we will exit this loop at this line in here and then the file

50
00:04:35,830 --> 00:04:37,630
will be automatically closed.

51
00:04:37,630 --> 00:04:39,460
So we will end up in here.

52
00:04:40,270 --> 00:04:47,860
If we end up at this line, this means that the password could not be found inside the word list.

53
00:04:47,860 --> 00:04:52,870
So I'm just going to print a message saying reached end of line.

54
00:04:53,590 --> 00:04:59,770
Just so that we know that the program finished working properly, there was no errors and the only problem

55
00:04:59,770 --> 00:05:04,840
we couldn't get the password is because the password isn't actually in our word list.

56
00:05:05,680 --> 00:05:08,020
So let's have a quick look on our code.

57
00:05:08,320 --> 00:05:10,720
First of all, we're setting the target URL.

58
00:05:11,530 --> 00:05:16,480
We're set in the data that we want to send to this URL.

59
00:05:16,480 --> 00:05:21,730
So this is based on the form and the input, as I showed you in the previous lecture.

60
00:05:21,730 --> 00:05:27,820
So we are submitting a value of admin to an input called Username.

61
00:05:28,300 --> 00:05:35,560
We're also setting the value to an input called password to nothing, and the last input that we have

62
00:05:35,560 --> 00:05:40,660
is the login input and that's basically going to submit the form for me.

63
00:05:41,980 --> 00:05:45,070
Then I don't need to print this, so I'm going to remove this.

64
00:05:46,420 --> 00:05:55,390
And we're going to open a file that contains a number of passwords of possible passwords for read only

65
00:05:55,810 --> 00:05:57,550
as a word list file.

66
00:05:58,420 --> 00:06:02,170
We're going to iterate over each line in this file.

67
00:06:02,560 --> 00:06:08,720
We're going to strip this line of any whitespace characters, including new line characters.

68
00:06:08,740 --> 00:06:11,290
This will be stored in my word, variable.

69
00:06:12,220 --> 00:06:18,760
Then I'm going to set the value for the password in my data dict because as you can see, the value

70
00:06:18,760 --> 00:06:21,070
is set to nothing at the start in here.

71
00:06:21,670 --> 00:06:27,250
So we're going to set the value for the password to the current word, to whatever we're reading at

72
00:06:27,250 --> 00:06:28,360
the current line.

73
00:06:29,320 --> 00:06:36,940
Then we're going to send a post request to the target URL with the data that we specified, we're storing

74
00:06:36,940 --> 00:06:38,830
the result in the response.

75
00:06:39,130 --> 00:06:46,450
If login failed isn't part of the response, then this means that we actually managed to log in and

76
00:06:46,450 --> 00:06:50,230
our password is the word that allowed us to log in.

77
00:06:50,980 --> 00:06:55,090
Then we're going to exit so that we don't keep iterating over the file.

78
00:06:55,840 --> 00:07:02,110
And if we actually do iterate to the end of the file and reach this line, then that means we reach

79
00:07:02,110 --> 00:07:05,530
the end of the file and we could discover the password.

80
00:07:06,400 --> 00:07:08,230
So let's go ahead and test this code.

81
00:07:08,230 --> 00:07:11,500
I'm going to clear the screen and run the program.

82
00:07:13,470 --> 00:07:14,340
And perfect.

83
00:07:14,340 --> 00:07:19,190
As you can see, it actually tried all the passwords in my file.

84
00:07:19,200 --> 00:07:21,230
So like I said, I don't have a big file.

85
00:07:21,240 --> 00:07:22,800
It's only 60 passwords.

86
00:07:22,800 --> 00:07:24,810
But I tried all of them.

87
00:07:24,810 --> 00:07:31,620
And once it got a right password, which is called password, it was able to log in, it was able to

88
00:07:31,620 --> 00:07:38,160
know that it managed to log in and it told me that I got you the password, which is password.

89
00:07:39,090 --> 00:07:41,970
Now, let's make sure that this result is correct.

90
00:07:41,970 --> 00:07:45,750
So let me actually remove the password from the word list.

91
00:07:46,380 --> 00:07:51,840
And now if I run my code, it should actually tell me that it could not get the password.

92
00:07:51,840 --> 00:07:53,970
And I should see this line on screen.

93
00:07:54,940 --> 00:07:56,440
So let's test it.

94
00:07:58,230 --> 00:07:58,950
And perfect.

95
00:07:58,950 --> 00:08:04,980
As you can see, it's telling us that it reached the end of the line and it couldn't get the password.

96
00:08:06,250 --> 00:08:13,720
Now we have a program that we can use to guest login information to any website we want.

97
00:08:13,810 --> 00:08:15,160
So that's pretty cool.

98
00:08:15,160 --> 00:08:17,710
And as you can see, it's not a complicated program.

99
00:08:17,710 --> 00:08:19,600
It's actually very, very simple.

100
00:08:20,230 --> 00:08:26,590
So this just goes to show you how simple functions that you might think are irrelevant or not important

101
00:08:26,590 --> 00:08:29,500
can be used to do some really cool stuff.

102
00:08:30,070 --> 00:08:34,360
Now you can actually use this program against any login page you can think of.

103
00:08:34,630 --> 00:08:42,310
The only two problems that you might face is if the login page has a CAPTCHA, so you'll have to type

104
00:08:42,310 --> 00:08:45,850
in the CAPTCHA in an input box to let you log in.

105
00:08:46,330 --> 00:08:54,070
Or if the target has some kind of firewall that if you try, for example, ten failed attempts, it

106
00:08:54,070 --> 00:08:56,770
will prevent you from attempting anymore.

107
00:08:56,860 --> 00:09:02,950
And this way you're going to have to use some kind of a proxy or VPN to change your IP to a different

108
00:09:02,950 --> 00:09:04,660
IP and try again.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

==================================================================================

# Section 19: Writing a Vulnerability Scanner

1
00:00:01,370 --> 00:00:02,630
In the next lectures.

2
00:00:02,630 --> 00:00:09,560
I want to use everything that we learned and build so far to build a generic vulnerability scanner.

3
00:00:09,830 --> 00:00:17,150
This is a program that takes a Target website and first it maps the whole website, and then it scans

4
00:00:17,150 --> 00:00:20,900
this website for vulnerabilities and weaknesses.

5
00:00:20,990 --> 00:00:27,140
Once done, it's going to give you a report of all the vulnerabilities that it was able to find.

6
00:00:27,530 --> 00:00:33,920
The word generic is very important in this scanner because we're going to write it in a way that it's

7
00:00:33,920 --> 00:00:35,840
very, very easy to extend.

8
00:00:35,870 --> 00:00:42,050
So when it comes to discovering web application vulnerabilities, the process is pretty much the same

9
00:00:42,050 --> 00:00:45,020
regardless of the type of the vulnerability.

10
00:00:45,320 --> 00:00:48,020
Therefore, our program is going to be generic.

11
00:00:48,020 --> 00:00:54,310
So let's say in a month time from now, you did some reading and learned about a new type of vulnerabilities.

12
00:00:54,320 --> 00:01:00,620
Then all you're going to have to do is maybe write three or four more lines to our existing code to

13
00:01:00,620 --> 00:01:03,230
make it discover this new vulnerability.

14
00:01:04,510 --> 00:01:10,300
As we write this, you're going to learn more about object oriented programming and how to structure

15
00:01:10,300 --> 00:01:14,560
your program in a way so that it can be easily extended.

16
00:01:15,340 --> 00:01:21,370
Now, before we go into programming this vulnerability scanner, let me show it to you in action.

17
00:01:21,640 --> 00:01:23,440
Again, this is just a teaser.

18
00:01:23,470 --> 00:01:25,210
Don't worry about how it works.

19
00:01:25,210 --> 00:01:28,130
We will cover that in detail in the next lecture.

20
00:01:28,150 --> 00:01:34,240
So for now, I just want you to get an idea of the goal or the program that I want to build.

21
00:01:35,270 --> 00:01:42,170
So now I'm in my carry machine and I'm just going to run the scanner by doing Python vulnerability scanner

22
00:01:42,170 --> 00:01:43,100
dot pie.

23
00:01:44,950 --> 00:01:51,580
As you can see, the first thing that it's going to do is discover all the links and all the pages in

24
00:01:51,580 --> 00:01:53,080
the Target website.

25
00:01:53,110 --> 00:02:00,610
Then for each one of these pages, it's going to extract all the parameters and all the forms.

26
00:02:00,640 --> 00:02:08,770
And finally, for each one of these parameters and forms, it's going to submit code to discover vulnerabilities.

27
00:02:09,400 --> 00:02:13,240
Then once it's done, it's going to show me the results.

28
00:02:13,240 --> 00:02:18,460
And as you can see, it was able to discover an XSS vulnerability in here.

29
00:02:19,150 --> 00:02:25,360
Now, don't worry about what I mean by XSS and what I mean by vulnerability, and don't worry about

30
00:02:25,360 --> 00:02:26,320
how this works.

31
00:02:26,320 --> 00:02:29,290
We will talk about all of that in this section.

32
00:02:29,290 --> 00:02:35,170
So this is just a teaser to show you what I want to achieve by the end of this section.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,740 --> 00:00:07,730
Now when it comes to discovering vulnerabilities in web applications, the main concept or the main

2
00:00:07,730 --> 00:00:15,050
idea that I usually teach my students, even in my website hacking course, where we do everything manually

3
00:00:15,170 --> 00:00:23,360
is to go ahead and use the websites and access all the links that you gathered in the information gathering

4
00:00:23,360 --> 00:00:24,140
stage.

5
00:00:24,440 --> 00:00:32,210
And then every time you see a variable or an input that's being sent to the web application to the web

6
00:00:32,210 --> 00:00:40,010
server, try to manipulate this input and modify it to test for all the vulnerabilities that you learn.

7
00:00:40,640 --> 00:00:48,530
So the main idea is if we go back to the diagram of how a website works, you can see that the user

8
00:00:48,530 --> 00:00:50,090
usually make requests.

9
00:00:50,180 --> 00:00:57,620
The requests get executed by the web application on the web server, and the response is returned to

10
00:00:57,620 --> 00:00:58,400
the user.

11
00:00:58,970 --> 00:01:05,900
Now, some requests contain data that gets used by the web application.

12
00:01:06,800 --> 00:01:15,590
So what we usually do is we manipulate this data and try to send some malicious code or malicious payloads

13
00:01:15,590 --> 00:01:18,920
in order to exploit some kind of vulnerability.

14
00:01:19,700 --> 00:01:25,070
Now, usually data is sent to the web server using two main concepts.

15
00:01:25,790 --> 00:01:32,690
The first one is through the URL, and you usually see the file name that the data is being sent to,

16
00:01:32,720 --> 00:01:35,150
followed by a parameter.

17
00:01:35,150 --> 00:01:41,330
So you can think of this as a variable and followed by the value, which is one in this example.

18
00:01:41,990 --> 00:01:48,410
Sometimes you actually don't see the file name and you only see the value, which is one.

19
00:01:48,410 --> 00:01:51,800
And here we don't really care about the file name.

20
00:01:51,800 --> 00:01:59,030
If we see the value, then we can manipulate this data and instead of sending one, we can send payloads

21
00:01:59,030 --> 00:02:04,550
to test for SQL injection access and other vulnerabilities.

22
00:02:05,390 --> 00:02:13,100
The second form of sending data to a web server or to the web application is through input boxes.

23
00:02:13,100 --> 00:02:17,750
So we we've seen search boxes and check boxes before on websites.

24
00:02:17,750 --> 00:02:24,020
And every time you fill this with data, this data gets sent to the web application.

25
00:02:24,530 --> 00:02:31,340
Now, in most cases, in this example, the data will be sent through post and therefore you will not

26
00:02:31,340 --> 00:02:33,290
see the data in the URL.

27
00:02:33,740 --> 00:02:39,380
You only see the data in the URL if it's being sent using the get method.

28
00:02:40,430 --> 00:02:42,680
So let me show you a quick example.

29
00:02:43,100 --> 00:02:45,290
Right here, I have a page on Facebook.

30
00:02:45,290 --> 00:02:52,340
And if you look at the URL, you'll see that there is a parameter called ref type and the value for

31
00:02:52,340 --> 00:02:55,370
this parameter is registration form.

32
00:02:55,970 --> 00:03:05,180
So basically when you access this link your browser or you send to the Facebook Web application a value

33
00:03:05,180 --> 00:03:07,280
called registration form.

34
00:03:07,280 --> 00:03:13,430
Now, Facebook's web application is going to use this value somewhere in their code.

35
00:03:13,460 --> 00:03:19,010
So it's similar to the way when we write functions, for example, and they take a variable and then

36
00:03:19,010 --> 00:03:25,580
we use the variable somewhere in our code, this is exactly what Facebook's web application is doing.

37
00:03:25,610 --> 00:03:30,590
It's actually taking this value and it's using it somewhere in their code.

38
00:03:31,130 --> 00:03:39,560
So we as hackers usually try to instead of sending these values as is as the default values that the

39
00:03:39,560 --> 00:03:47,420
Web application expects, we usually manipulate this and send values that allow us to discover a number

40
00:03:47,420 --> 00:03:48,710
of vulnerabilities.

41
00:03:49,250 --> 00:03:55,940
Now, the values that we send depend on the vulnerability that we're trying to discover, but we always

42
00:03:55,940 --> 00:03:57,320
follow the same concept.

43
00:03:57,320 --> 00:04:04,370
We manipulate the inputs and analyze the output to see if the web application is vulnerable.

44
00:04:04,370 --> 00:04:09,590
And if it is, we will exploit it again using the same idea.

45
00:04:09,590 --> 00:04:16,490
So instead of sending code to discover the vulnerability, we will send code to exploit the vulnerability.

46
00:04:17,500 --> 00:04:24,520
Now, like I said, this is the first common way of sending data to the web application from the user,

47
00:04:24,520 --> 00:04:25,990
from the Web browser.

48
00:04:26,680 --> 00:04:31,020
The second way is, like I said, using text boxes.

49
00:04:31,030 --> 00:04:36,650
So right here, this is actually a vulnerable web page on my Metasploit table machine.

50
00:04:36,670 --> 00:04:40,540
I'm accessing it using the IP of the Metasploit table machine.

51
00:04:40,570 --> 00:04:50,410
And if you just go to forward slash Matilda and go to OWASP top ten cross-site scripting reflected and

52
00:04:50,410 --> 00:04:58,690
DNS lookup, you will actually get this page and this page actually does send some data over the URL

53
00:04:58,690 --> 00:05:04,480
using it, but at the same time it contains an input box.

54
00:05:05,230 --> 00:05:11,350
Now you can put any value that you want in this input box similar to the way you can manipulate this

55
00:05:11,350 --> 00:05:12,610
value in here.

56
00:05:13,480 --> 00:05:20,590
And therefore, when testing for vulnerabilities, you should always test these text boxes for the same

57
00:05:20,590 --> 00:05:26,560
vulnerabilities that you would test the parameters that you see in the URL.

58
00:05:27,490 --> 00:05:34,840
Now we've seen before how to discover all the URLs in a page and then using what we learned in Python,

59
00:05:34,840 --> 00:05:39,100
we can manipulate these URLs and send anything that we want.

60
00:05:39,100 --> 00:05:41,270
So we really have nothing to cover there.

61
00:05:41,290 --> 00:05:49,210
We will cover how to use that to automatically discover vulnerabilities and get requests or in parameters

62
00:05:49,210 --> 00:05:50,980
later on in the course.

63
00:05:51,700 --> 00:05:58,540
The main thing that we haven't covered now is how to actually discover the forms in a page.

64
00:05:58,780 --> 00:06:03,730
So we covered how to fill values for forms and submit them before.

65
00:06:03,880 --> 00:06:12,160
But using Python, if I just give a page or a link to my Python program, it's not able to discover

66
00:06:12,160 --> 00:06:15,190
whether this page contains any forms or not.

67
00:06:15,670 --> 00:06:22,660
And even if we manage to do that, we need to also discover all the inputs and the button so that we

68
00:06:22,660 --> 00:06:24,630
can actually submit this form.

69
00:06:24,640 --> 00:06:31,810
Because before, when I showed you how to submit a form, we manually filled up the names for the inputs

70
00:06:31,810 --> 00:06:34,870
and the location where the form should be submitted.

71
00:06:34,930 --> 00:06:42,520
So we can't really use that in an automatic program to automatically discover vulnerabilities throughout

72
00:06:42,520 --> 00:06:43,510
these forms.

73
00:06:44,470 --> 00:06:52,600
So in the next lecture, I'm going to show you how to extract forms from any HTML page and extract all

74
00:06:52,600 --> 00:06:59,950
the useful data from this form so that we can use it later on in our program to automatically discover

75
00:06:59,950 --> 00:07:05,260
vulnerabilities that can be exploited through these forms.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,040 --> 00:00:01,700
Okay.

2
00:00:01,700 --> 00:00:11,180
So now the main goal is to write a program or a function that takes the URL and returns all the forms

3
00:00:11,180 --> 00:00:18,320
in that URL with the inputs and the link which we can submit this form to.

4
00:00:18,800 --> 00:00:27,590
So previously I actually showed you a way of extracting a specific element from the HTML page, and

5
00:00:27,590 --> 00:00:33,950
we did this in our spider program and we did it using regex, using the find all method.

6
00:00:34,550 --> 00:00:41,000
Now, as you know, you can use a regex to find any substring within a bigger string using a regular

7
00:00:41,000 --> 00:00:41,960
expression.

8
00:00:41,960 --> 00:00:46,250
And as we seen before, regular expressions are really, really powerful.

9
00:00:46,280 --> 00:00:53,330
Hence we were able to use them to extract all the links from a HTML page.

10
00:00:54,200 --> 00:01:02,870
Now, as we seen in the guest login, in order to post form data, we have to first of all know the

11
00:01:02,870 --> 00:01:05,090
URL that we want to post to.

12
00:01:05,480 --> 00:01:13,490
We also need to find all the inputs, find the input names, as you see in here, put them in a dictionary

13
00:01:13,490 --> 00:01:17,180
and then submit this using the requests dot post.

14
00:01:17,990 --> 00:01:21,410
So you can do all of this using regex.

15
00:01:21,410 --> 00:01:28,760
But because this is a little bit more complex, I want to show you how to do it using a module called

16
00:01:28,760 --> 00:01:29,990
Beautiful Soup.

17
00:01:30,380 --> 00:01:38,990
And this module is actually really, really powerful to parse and extract useful data from HTML pages.

18
00:01:39,110 --> 00:01:44,990
So as usual, what I'm going to show you is just an example, but you can use this module to extract

19
00:01:44,990 --> 00:01:48,920
literally any part of any HTML page.

20
00:01:49,760 --> 00:01:55,760
So first of all, before we can use beautiful soup, we have to import it.

21
00:01:55,850 --> 00:01:59,330
So I'm going to do from beautiful soup.

22
00:02:00,230 --> 00:02:02,210
Import beautiful soup.

23
00:02:02,990 --> 00:02:11,270
Now, once we have this imported, the first thing that we need to do is to get the HTML code of the

24
00:02:11,270 --> 00:02:13,400
page that we want to parse.

25
00:02:13,820 --> 00:02:21,560
So we see how to do this before we're going to say response is equal to request.

26
00:02:22,970 --> 00:02:25,550
And we're going to give it the URL.

27
00:02:25,700 --> 00:02:33,920
So I'm going to say my target URL is equal to this URL right here.

28
00:02:35,520 --> 00:02:42,510
And we're just going to pass the target URL to my request function, which will call this function right

29
00:02:42,510 --> 00:02:43,080
here.

30
00:02:43,890 --> 00:02:50,190
And because I'm given the HTTP here in the URL, I actually don't need this in here because it just

31
00:02:50,190 --> 00:02:52,530
appends http to the URL.

32
00:02:53,430 --> 00:02:59,850
So now as we know, if I just print the response to that content.

33
00:03:01,160 --> 00:03:08,900
I will get the HTML code of this page right here, the page that can be accessed through this URL.

34
00:03:09,650 --> 00:03:12,230
So if we do this real quick in here.

35
00:03:15,620 --> 00:03:19,010
You can see I will get the HTML code.

36
00:03:19,990 --> 00:03:21,650
So this is all good.

37
00:03:21,670 --> 00:03:23,020
We've done this before.

38
00:03:23,020 --> 00:03:23,890
And response.

39
00:03:23,890 --> 00:03:28,390
That content contains the HTML code of my target page.

40
00:03:28,810 --> 00:03:30,920
So go into the fun part.

41
00:03:30,940 --> 00:03:36,850
Now we're going to pass this HTML code using beautiful soup.

42
00:03:37,180 --> 00:03:49,210
So we're going to say my first HTML is equal to beautiful soup and I'm going to give it the HTML code

43
00:03:49,210 --> 00:03:55,540
of my target web page, which we can access by saying response the content.

44
00:03:56,620 --> 00:04:01,180
So basically all we're doing right now is we're calling beautiful soup.

45
00:04:01,180 --> 00:04:08,200
We're giving it the HTML code and it's going to return an object which we're going to store in the past

46
00:04:08,440 --> 00:04:16,960
HTML variable and we'll be able to use that to access the different parts of the HTML page so you can

47
00:04:16,960 --> 00:04:20,710
use it, like I said, to access any interesting part to you.

48
00:04:20,710 --> 00:04:24,010
And I'm just using the forms as an example.

49
00:04:24,880 --> 00:04:32,710
So if we go to our target page, which is this one and right click the text box and click on Inspect

50
00:04:32,710 --> 00:04:33,550
Element.

51
00:04:34,580 --> 00:04:39,890
We will see the HTML code that's responsible for rendering this part of the page.

52
00:04:41,120 --> 00:04:45,260
And as you can see, when I'm over the input, it's highlighting it for me.

53
00:04:45,680 --> 00:04:55,970
Now, if I go up a little bit and close all of this, you'll see that I have my form in here and this

54
00:04:55,970 --> 00:04:57,910
is the form that I'm interested in.

55
00:04:57,920 --> 00:05:00,440
So it's literally the highlighted part.

56
00:05:00,620 --> 00:05:03,140
That's the part that I'm interested in.

57
00:05:03,470 --> 00:05:10,940
As you know, the most important parts for me is the action, which is the URL that this form will be

58
00:05:10,940 --> 00:05:15,950
submitted to and the inputs inside this form.

59
00:05:16,040 --> 00:05:24,050
So if we go down here, if we open the table, you'll see that I have only one input in here, which

60
00:05:24,050 --> 00:05:25,250
is the text box.

61
00:05:25,250 --> 00:05:31,880
And this is where I want to manipulate the value and use values that might allow me to discover exploits

62
00:05:31,880 --> 00:05:33,410
or vulnerabilities.

63
00:05:34,190 --> 00:05:38,720
And then we have another input, which is this one right here.

64
00:05:41,240 --> 00:05:45,410
And this input is the one that submits the form.

65
00:05:45,410 --> 00:05:52,490
So it's actually a button that when clicked, it will submit the value that we input in here.

66
00:05:53,180 --> 00:06:00,170
So like I said, you can use regex to extract all of this data, but because of the way this data is

67
00:06:00,170 --> 00:06:06,770
structured, the rejects that you need to use might be a little bit complex and it might not work against

68
00:06:06,770 --> 00:06:07,880
all websites.

69
00:06:08,450 --> 00:06:15,410
So using beautiful soup it will be much easier and we can actually use it to get all the forms in the

70
00:06:15,410 --> 00:06:17,330
page, not only one form.

71
00:06:18,020 --> 00:06:23,900
So to get all of the forms in this page, I'm going to say forms list.

72
00:06:25,450 --> 00:06:29,500
Is equal to my past HTML.

73
00:06:29,500 --> 00:06:32,440
So it's this variable right here that contains the past.

74
00:06:32,740 --> 00:06:37,360
TML And I'm going to say I want to find all.

75
00:06:38,660 --> 00:06:46,010
And in here you can specify any HTML tag or element that you want to find.

76
00:06:46,040 --> 00:06:53,330
So in our previous example, we were interested into getting the tags because these are the tags that

77
00:06:53,330 --> 00:06:54,800
contain the links.

78
00:06:54,800 --> 00:06:57,920
So all they have to do is literally type an A in here.

79
00:06:58,400 --> 00:07:02,360
In this example, we're interested in the forms on this page.

80
00:07:02,360 --> 00:07:07,010
So I'm telling it that I want you to find all the form elements.

81
00:07:07,010 --> 00:07:11,840
So basically all the elements that start and end with form.

82
00:07:12,560 --> 00:07:16,880
If you want to find the divs, then you would type div and so on.

83
00:07:17,540 --> 00:07:23,720
Now what this is going to do is it's going to return a list that's going to be stored in my form's list

84
00:07:24,170 --> 00:07:28,520
in which each element is a form on this page.

85
00:07:29,210 --> 00:07:33,470
So if I go ahead and print my forms list.

86
00:07:34,850 --> 00:07:37,160
And run this code in here.

87
00:07:38,860 --> 00:07:43,060
You will see that I have only one element in this list.

88
00:07:43,060 --> 00:07:45,010
So it's this one right here.

89
00:07:45,490 --> 00:07:50,860
And I only have the the code that's used inside this form.

90
00:07:50,860 --> 00:07:57,490
So I have the form, I have the action, I have the method of this form and you can see it's post and

91
00:07:57,490 --> 00:07:58,870
I have the inputs.

92
00:07:58,870 --> 00:08:03,670
So I have the first input in here and the second input which is the button.

93
00:08:04,210 --> 00:08:05,560
So that's perfect.

94
00:08:06,040 --> 00:08:13,690
And the next lecture, I want to show you how to further analyze this and extract the inputs, the method

95
00:08:13,690 --> 00:08:14,830
and the action.

96
00:08:14,830 --> 00:08:19,210
And then we'll see how we can use that with the post method that we used before.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:01,490 --> 00:00:10,910
So now that we learned how to use beautiful soup to extract specific elements from an HTML page, let's

2
00:00:10,910 --> 00:00:15,800
see how we can extract all the nested elements within this element.

3
00:00:16,580 --> 00:00:23,510
So basically in HTML, every time you create an element or a tag, for example, you have the form tag

4
00:00:23,510 --> 00:00:27,440
here, you close it with a forward slash form.

5
00:00:27,440 --> 00:00:28,850
So that's the end of it.

6
00:00:28,880 --> 00:00:34,520
And as you can see, when I put the mouse over here, it highlights the start and the end of the form

7
00:00:34,520 --> 00:00:36,710
in the actual HTML page.

8
00:00:37,460 --> 00:00:42,440
So this is the same if you look at the script in here, even though this is a JavaScript, it doesn't

9
00:00:42,440 --> 00:00:44,000
get displayed on the screen.

10
00:00:44,000 --> 00:00:49,670
But as you can see, it starts with the script and it ends with a forward slash script.

11
00:00:50,450 --> 00:00:57,260
Now going back to our example, which is the form, now this is what we extracted and as you can see

12
00:00:57,260 --> 00:00:58,700
inside this element.

13
00:00:58,700 --> 00:01:05,030
So it's similar to when we have a function and we have code inside the body of this function, or we

14
00:01:05,030 --> 00:01:12,980
have a loop and we have code inside the body of the loop in HTML between the start of the tag and the

15
00:01:12,980 --> 00:01:14,030
end of the tag.

16
00:01:14,060 --> 00:01:20,870
You can put HTML code and this code will be considered as part of this element.

17
00:01:21,560 --> 00:01:28,340
So inside this form you can see that we have a table and then inside this table we have rows.

18
00:01:28,340 --> 00:01:36,380
So each TR and here is actually a row and again it starts with a TR and with the forward slash tr and

19
00:01:36,380 --> 00:01:38,510
then we also have inputs.

20
00:01:39,580 --> 00:01:45,670
Now I'm talking a little bit too much about HTML, but I just want you to have a general idea of the

21
00:01:45,670 --> 00:01:47,650
syntax of HTML.

22
00:01:48,160 --> 00:01:56,800
Now going back to the result displayed by our program, you can see we have a list because like I said,

23
00:01:56,800 --> 00:02:01,450
the code we used will extract all the forms in that page.

24
00:02:01,450 --> 00:02:05,980
So if there were more forms, we'll actually see them as elements to this list.

25
00:02:07,120 --> 00:02:11,620
We only have one form in the page, so therefore we only have one element.

26
00:02:11,800 --> 00:02:18,160
And again, as you can see, we don't only have the start of the element, we have the element plus

27
00:02:18,160 --> 00:02:24,010
all of the nested elements inside it, which are actually very, very interesting to us.

28
00:02:24,790 --> 00:02:29,530
So the first interesting thing is the action.

29
00:02:29,740 --> 00:02:35,470
Like I said, this is the page that this form gets submitted to.

30
00:02:35,560 --> 00:02:43,420
So if we ever want to submit this form, we have to submit it to index dot PHP page equals DNS lookup

31
00:02:43,420 --> 00:02:44,530
dot php.

32
00:02:45,340 --> 00:02:51,880
Now if we go up and look at the URL and here this is the same as the URL and you might think that this

33
00:02:51,880 --> 00:02:58,660
is not very important because it's the same as the URL, but in many scenarios you will actually see

34
00:02:58,660 --> 00:03:02,410
a different URL in the action than the main URL.

35
00:03:02,830 --> 00:03:10,960
So it is very important for us to know the value for the action now in our code.

36
00:03:10,960 --> 00:03:18,160
Right now we are printing the forms list, which is basically a list in which each element is a form

37
00:03:18,160 --> 00:03:20,470
in the target HTML page.

38

So let's assume that this list contains more than one element, and I want to access all of these elements

39
00:03:28,000 --> 00:03:31,090
because like I said, we want to extract all the forms.

40
00:03:31,600 --> 00:03:36,210
Then what I do is I use a loop like we seen before.

41
00:03:36,220 --> 00:03:44,380
So all we have to do is we're just going to do for form in my forms list.

42
00:03:45,160 --> 00:03:48,310
I want to print the form.

43
00:03:49,750 --> 00:03:53,440
So all we're doing is we're creating a new variable called form.

44
00:03:53,470 --> 00:03:57,460
It's going to represent an element in this list.

45
00:03:57,460 --> 00:04:03,700
And then for each time we have an element, we're going to execute this code and we're just printing

46
00:04:03,700 --> 00:04:04,480
the form.

47
00:04:05,380 --> 00:04:12,220
So if I clear this and run it again, as you can see, I have my form and I don't have the list start

48
00:04:12,220 --> 00:04:17,680
and end, which means that I am actually accessing this one specific element.

49
00:04:17,680 --> 00:04:20,590
So I'm accessing one element at a time.

50
00:04:20,920 --> 00:04:23,200
And this is something that we've seen before.

51
00:04:23,200 --> 00:04:24,910
And we and we did a lot.

52
00:04:25,570 --> 00:04:32,980
Now, what I actually want to do is I want to start extracting the useful data from this form.

53
00:04:33,610 --> 00:04:37,120
So like I said, the first important thing is the action.

54
00:04:37,420 --> 00:04:44,620
And to extract any attribute, like the action in here or like the method in here, even if it is in

55
00:04:44,620 --> 00:04:53,320
any other HTML element, for example, this tile in here, all you have to do is put the name of the

56
00:04:53,320 --> 00:04:54,310
current element.

57
00:04:54,310 --> 00:04:59,230
So right now I'm in form and I want to get the action.

58
00:04:59,860 --> 00:05:05,680
Doesn't get any simpler than this and I'm going to store this in a variable called action.

59
00:05:07,160 --> 00:05:09,230
And I'm going to print this.

60
00:05:11,230 --> 00:05:16,300
So if I run this now, you will see I have the action right here.

61
00:05:18,550 --> 00:05:23,500
Now, the next important thing that I want to get is the method.

62
00:05:23,680 --> 00:05:27,430
And again, this is an attribute inside the form tag.

63
00:05:27,760 --> 00:05:39,490
So I can extract it by saying my method is equal to four dot get and I want to get the method.

64
00:05:40,330 --> 00:05:42,400
Then again, I can print this.

65
00:05:43,420 --> 00:05:48,010
And if I run my code, you'll see that my method is post.

66
00:05:48,580 --> 00:05:49,360
Perfect.

67
00:05:50,810 --> 00:05:56,880
The next thing that we need in order to post the data is the names of the inputs.

68
00:05:56,900 --> 00:06:06,080
So we have the input in here and we have another input in there, and the whole input is not really

69
00:06:06,080 --> 00:06:07,360
important to me.

70
00:06:07,370 --> 00:06:11,390
What's important to me is the name of the input.

71
00:06:11,390 --> 00:06:14,150
So the name attribute in the input.

72
00:06:14,360 --> 00:06:20,960
Remember we use that in the guest login and we set the name as the key of the dictionary and we set

73
00:06:20,960 --> 00:06:23,390
the value to whatever value that we want.

74
00:06:23,930 --> 00:06:29,960
So that's the main reason why I need it in here, so that I can build up my dictionary and then submit

75
00:06:29,960 --> 00:06:30,740
the form.

76
00:06:32,070 --> 00:06:40,080
So so far we know we can use the dot, get to get attributes and defined all to find actual elements.

77
00:06:40,470 --> 00:06:44,340
Now if you look at the input, the input is not an attribute.

78
00:06:44,340 --> 00:06:50,310
It starts with this sign and this means that it's an actual HTML element.

79
00:06:50,880 --> 00:07:00,030
So we're actually going to use the same syntax as this, and I'm going to say my inputs list is equal

80
00:07:00,060 --> 00:07:07,230
to my form because the inputs, if we go back to where we were printing the form, the inputs are actually

81
00:07:07,230 --> 00:07:08,640
part of this form.

82
00:07:08,640 --> 00:07:14,970
So all of this is the form and the inputs are in here and in here.

83
00:07:15,780 --> 00:07:22,680
So what I'm saying is my inputs list is equal to the form variable that contains all of the inputs.

84
00:07:22,680 --> 00:07:31,470
And I want to find all and with the element that I want to find all occurrences of it is my input.

85
00:07:32,220 --> 00:07:39,420
And then again, exactly similar to what we're doing here, my inputs list is going to contain a list

86
00:07:39,420 --> 00:07:49,020
in which each element is an input inside the form so I can iterate over it by doing for input in my

87
00:07:49,020 --> 00:07:50,100
input list.

88
00:07:51,150 --> 00:07:58,590
And then I can just print the input and this should print the inputs for me.

89
00:07:58,590 --> 00:08:00,900
And as you can see, I have them in there.

90
00:08:01,590 --> 00:08:05,610
But like I said, the input is not really important to me.

91
00:08:05,610 --> 00:08:10,080
And what's important to me is the name of the input.

92
00:08:10,740 --> 00:08:17,460
So again, as you can see, the name is an attribute to an actual HTML element.

93
00:08:17,460 --> 00:08:28,050
So to access attributes similar to what we're doing in here, we can say the input name is equal to

94
00:08:28,050 --> 00:08:36,120
input, get the name and then I can just print the input name in here.

95
00:08:38,120 --> 00:08:45,920
And if I run this, as you can see, I have the input names which are target host and the other name

96
00:08:45,920 --> 00:08:47,630
which we have in here.

97
00:08:49,760 --> 00:08:56,150
So going back to the top, we've actually kind of repeated ourself in this code, but I wanted to make

98
00:08:56,150 --> 00:08:57,740
sure that you get the idea.

99
00:08:58,220 --> 00:09:04,730
Basically all you have to do is, first of all, parse the HTML code using beautiful soup.

100
00:09:04,940 --> 00:09:06,860
This is stored in a variable.

101
00:09:07,250 --> 00:09:13,700
Then every time you need to find an element, you use the find all element on this variable.

102
00:09:13,730 --> 00:09:16,150
So we were looking for forms.

103
00:09:16,160 --> 00:09:17,870
We got a list of forms.

104
00:09:17,870 --> 00:09:21,230
We iterated over each element in this list.

105
00:09:21,530 --> 00:09:27,950
Every time we need to access an attribute so something similar to the class in here or similar to the

106
00:09:27,950 --> 00:09:31,760
name we do form the get.

107
00:09:32,180 --> 00:09:35,360
So we got the action, we got the method.

108
00:09:35,720 --> 00:09:43,040
And then when we wanted to get the inputs, these are actual HTML elements so we can't use the dot get

109
00:09:43,040 --> 00:09:43,900
to get them.

110
00:09:43,910 --> 00:09:53,360
But similar to the form, we use the find all method to find all the inputs in my current form.

111
00:09:54,140 --> 00:09:59,150
Then we iterated over them and we extracted the input names.

112
00:09:59,540 --> 00:10:04,610
So right now we have all the things that we need in order to submit this form.

113
00:10:04,610 --> 00:10:07,130
We already know how to post requests.

114
00:10:07,250 --> 00:10:14,630
We have the URL to post to because we extracted it from the action and we have the names of the inputs.

115
00:10:14,630 --> 00:10:21,080
So all we have to do now is use the same post method that I showed you before in order to post this

116
00:10:21,080 --> 00:10:21,710
form.

117
00:10:22,310 --> 00:10:28,580
You'll have to do a slight modifications before you do that, and I'll cover this in details in the
